Hate is strongly overblown for this game, while the characters and world and level design aren't as strong as DS1, and until the (included) DLC, the bosses don't experiment as memorably as DS1. However DS1's bosses often comprised of the same big stick swingy guy or abysmal unfettered ass regardless, when they experimented you got bed of chaos. Aside from that I think everything about DS2 feels better mechanically, movement feels very smooth and tight, being able to upgrade estus speed feels great, some of the areas are genuinely pretty like Majula or the (included) DLC areas. Rolling is smoother, movement is faster, you're actually able to handle ganks now, over time you get to clear out all the enemies on a runback and despawn them permanently giving you a feeling of progress even on failed boss runs, everything about DS2 feels quite nice other than the priorly mentioned things that are a bit worse than ds1, I think the rest feels nicer.

Posted: November 22
Not as bad as its reputation, but I understand why it's the most disliked game in the series. Almost all the small changes they made compared to DS1 are bad, and on top of that half these mechanics are broken or unpolished ( watch videos from MauLer and other youtubers like him, they show really well what's wrong with this game ).

Overall it's a rather easy game for a Souls, easier than the first one, except for a few areas and bosses. But I went through several areas without dying, and first tried several bosses. The DLCs on the other hand are extremely hard, the difficulty spike is insane even if I went in nearly at the end ( before the last boss ) at level 160. Enemies kill you in 2-3 hits and there are ganks everywhere.

But the level design is better than in the base game so that's a plus.

NG+ can go ♥♥♥♥ itself with all the new red enemies they added that kill you in 2 hits and are like invaders on crack. I quickly gave up, better things to do.

The worst might be that... it's not really interesting in terms of lore, world building and story. I forgot the names of the bosses as soon as I killed them, the NPCs are not memorable, and as usual their quests are so easy to miss that they might as well not exist. The story is generic and way too similar to the first one, but hundreds or maybe thousands of years later, since we are in an endless cycle of death and rebirth.

Anyway if you want more Dark Souls, play it, at least to see how superior the first game is. Now I'm going to tackle the third one.

Posted: November 22
I've played this game way back when I was just getting into the Souls series back on last gen Consoles, where the FPS being above a certain amount would start shafting the durability of your weapons. I was new to Dark Souls, and was unsurprisingly awful at the series for my 1st playthrough. And yet, despite literally playing what people say is the worst Souls game Fromsoft made, I ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ adore this game.

Yeah- Elden Ring exists, and I'm not gonna go so far as "Dark Souls 2 is actually the bestest game" but ♥♥♥♥ I'd be lying if DS2 wasn't worth the higher highs and lower lows. To put it simply, this game is like a rollarcoaster, for every good quality there's a middling or outright ass quality. For example, DS2 has the most build variety barring maybe Elden Ring, but playing a Hexer with a Dark Chaos Rapier is objectively the strongest build overall. Or how some of the sickest boss fights in the Souls Series (Like Sir Alonne or the Old Ivory King) is here in DS2, while also sporting the easiest bosses in the franchise (Looking at you Giant Lord.). Even so, I argue Dark Souls 2 has enough good to outweigh the bad.

A personal high point for me, which feels like it represents the game is the weapon type Halberd. It's my favorite version of the weapon type in the series, it has a sweet spot mechanic that forces the player to space good for the best damage, and it can carry you through the game if you treat it right. It's also an awful weapon type due to it's moveset, consisting of telegraphed overheads for it's 1 and 2 handed attacks by default and only getting sweeps on the running or follow-ups. And yet- it is my favorite ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ weapon (Mastadon Halberd my beloved.) with no substitutes. It represents this game perfectly to me, when it hits my God it's literally peak barring Elden Ring, but you gotta give it a chance.

Posted: November 19
If you are absolutely desperate for a souls game, maybe you can reluctantly give this a chance.
The best way to describe this game fast is: "Everything Dark souls 1 and 3 did wrong in one neat package".

I know this game has a die-hard fanbase and that I'll be eaten alive but lets go..

A quick breakdown of the most notable things (there are a lot more) I find to be bad about this game:
1. Too many high damage, high hp enemies for no reason (it's tedious to kill them over and over). People had been criticising the enemy placement and enemy density of Lords of the Fallen clearly havent played ds2.
2. Too often mechanics that make traversal unreasonably tedious like: Mobs that degrade (almost instantly) your equipment, many areas with water/whatever else that affect movement.
3. Extremely easy bosses (spend 2 hours moving around in a tedious place to fight a boss for a minute)
4. Atrocious hit boxes. Enjoy getting teleported to an enemy's grab attack (not uncommon)
5. Bad and clunky movement + poorly working lock on (Bonus: Lock on lingers like a second or two after target dies which is very fun when fighting 10 of them at a time!
6. Seriously lacking animations for attacks.
7. And finally since this matters the least out of the above to me, graphicly the game somehow managed to look worse that Dark souls 1 (which was looking very solid for its age)

Credit where credit is due:
The game has excellent atmosphere, with some beautiful sceneries (despite the graphical quality), almost as good as ds 1, it has a lot of interesting ideas, a lot of content (though quantity over quality) and some very cool areas, with my favourite being the Drangleic Castle.

TLDR:
This game takes a 5/10 from me. All the positive experiences that the game provided were poison by it's moment to moment clunky and poorly thought out gameplay.

Posted: November 18
I like the story, atmosphere, enemies are cool, weapons and armour are cool and I find almost every character really interesting to learn about.

But omg I hate how easy it is to get lost in this game when it barely tells you to begin with which is the right way. And it's not like Dark souls 1 lost since you can eventually find out where you're meant to go, but i cannot get anywhere without looking up a guide to find out where is the way to the end of the game. I remember I went to go talk to some npcs to figure out some clues on where i need to go and one literally just tells you to go east to fight something, as if you have any sense of direction to begin with (never mind the norths, souths, easts and wests).

I also find it quite frustrating how you can have certain areas where it feels like there are far too many enemies with the amount of damage you can take in this game. There's some points when you could be looking at over a dozen enemies at once all attacking you at the same time, or having multiple ranged enemies out of reach while you're trying to fight some ground enemies that are in your way.

There are also some trap areas that are impossible to dodge, so you have to hope that you can make it through with enough help and enough luck that you can either heal or just not run out of health before getting through the traps.

Haven't completed this game yet since I only just found out i had apparently been going the wrong way and didn't need to go where I'm at now. And let's just say I'm relieved because this area has a boss battle that has multiple enemies in it that'll fight you with the boss *UNLESS* you backtrack that entire section of the game again to find helpers that'll make the boss fight much easier than without them. It just feels like it's unnecessarily making the game longer than it needs to be.

If you're into dark souls as a whole, i think you'll like this. But I can't recommend it based on my experience as it can drag for way too long and be difficult in a way that I just find way too annoying. (I've considered giving up and moving onto dark souls 3 prematurely as i've heard it's more similar to dark souls 1).

Amazing game that I can fully recommend to everyone - do not skip it even though people might try to recommend that to you.

Game is different compared to DS1 and DS3 so you have to be prepared for it.
Lore centered much more around a player, the curse, npcs and their journey through drangleic for their own answer for the curse. Here the game is already different compared to the rest of trilogy - the myth of chosen undead/ashen one is replaced by much more personal story.

Gameplay is much better compared to its prequel - has its flaws but is still unbelieveably better. In the end - this game has inspired a lot of their future games, especially Elden ring.

Game was pretty ambitious by looking at what it supposed to be but the time restraints and limitations of (at the time) consoles did not really help the already problematic development.

Still, like I said, game is fun and if you are looking for great Souls experience this game is surely worth it.

Posted: November 18
I am still hesitant to give this game a thumbs up, it's more on the neutral side of things but you wouldn't go play souls-like games without experiencing the original Dark souls series

If you're new to this genre this game should be the last one you play or the one you skip altogether, there is nothing special about it, only torment and despair.

The biggest reason this game got the most hate in the series is the gameplay and level design.

For gameplay, they went for a slowish pace, animations are slower, iframes are shorter, stamina is scarce, and even when you level your character a lot you still need to manage your stamina and attacks. I personally like slow gameplay in games, I enjoyed it, but I agree that it didn't hit the mark, especially that the enemy doesn't have the same stamina management and it gets frustrating at times.

Level design has the most ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ going on you'll ever witness anywhere, the difficulty in this game isn't the boss, it's to reach the boss, every corner, every step has something that's gonna make you hate the game. If you played DS1, think Sen's fortress. Now there are ways to deal with all of it but till you figure it out, suffer...

Finally, the story, they tried to go for the same mysterious vibe DS1 had and they failed miserably, it's not a sequel, it's a cheap copy in that regard and not a single bit interesting.

If you're playing FromSoftware games you'll pass through this game, but if you're looking for a decent soulslike experience, that's not it.

Posted: November 18
At this point, I've played most Fromsoft games, and I feel like I can give a meaningful review. Maybe. Take it with a grain of salt: DS1, 2, 3, Bloodborne, and Elden Ring (minus the DLC).

This might be long, though I generally write as stream of consciousness. Forgive my rambling.

These games aren't about difficulty. Sure, it enhances the game, but if your sole reason for playing this game is the PVP or the bosses, fine, I guess? But really they're more philosophical if anything. Eat some hot sauce if that's your thing. Or touch grass. Anyways.

DS2 iterates on its predecessor in that it elaborates what it means to be human, and the purpose of existence itself. In my opinion.

In a sense, Dark Souls and Fromsoft games in general are concerned with religion, myth, and are our modern day version of the Illiad, or Odyssey, etc. That much is clear to me. But that does it a disservice, in a sense this is a form of art. The beauty of Dark Souls is it doesn't hold your hands, but it's fair. You have the choice to interact with the NPCs, to learn of the world. Or maybe you won't. You can do a strength build - a mage build, whatever. There are ways to make the game easier or harder based on that playstyle, and typically, it's the barrier to entry that makes it difficult.

Anyways, gameplay, because obligatory. When you understand the core basic mechanics of the genre, it becomes a lot easier - allocate stats, put on armour, upgrade armour, upgrade weapon, scale with highest stats. The mechanic people don't like, hollowing, can be avoided by just by putting more health to offset that gap. The game sells plenty of anti-hollowing stuff later, and it's really pushing a feeling on you. If there's a boss you're having trouble with, explore elsewhere. There is subtle telegraphing, and more often than not, if you're bashing your head against a wall, try a different angle. I think you can re-spec your character, or can learn to, 5 times? That's another thing, so you're encouraged to experiment. You can equip multiple spells to increase usage, and you can use consumables to use more spells as well - spell-casting is a lot more fun in this game than in a lot of others.

One of the spots I had most trouble with my play-through was because I did not explore the windmill and kept brute forcing the auto-health boss. I explored, discovered how to stop the auto-heal, and it was much easier. I'm keeping it vague because spoilers, but if you've played you know what I mean. That is precisely the feeling it's going for - hopelessness, and then triumph when stepping back. It tells its story through setting, environment, and aesthetics.

If this is your first game, come back once you've played a different entry to dip your toes in so to speak. Or dive in, either way, I can see on both sides why people find this divisive- it really boils down to how you interact with media, and therefore I cannot recommend this to most people, not out of an elitist mentality, but simply because Dark Souls doesn't explain itself and is sort of an anti-game, in the sense that most work with and for the player, where Dark Souls is actively challenging you perspective-wise.

Perhaps I missed the point. I can accept that. In any case, I finished the base game as of this review, but I definitely plan on trying the DLC at some point. (I did not do the ending because you restart from the beginning if you do that, and I want to do the ending once I've beaten the DLC. That's also a thing). Regardless, if you like Dark Souls 1 & 3, you might like Dark Souls 2, though maybe not.

Dark Souls 2 feels like it was actually the first attempt at Dark Souls, but has two major benefits of the developers not hating the player's guts yet and the game is actually finished.

Some design choices are a major step down when compared to Dark Souls 1, but in the end it is much better than people would have you believe.

Hard to recommend to newcomers of FromSoft or the Souls genre as most of the game's highlights are near end-game/at post-game, but it is a good time if you can stick it out.

The bad:

The legendary atmosphere the first game is just missing.

Much weaker environmental storytelling

Vendrick and the other major players aren't as clearly highlighted as Gwyn & his lords.

The combat feels less meaty, more flaily, than the first game.

There are tons of 'dead' level ups that you need to do in order to get utility points.

More throwaway bosses than in the previous title.

There are some difficulty spikes-here-and-there, but before the post-game, this entry is significantly significantly significantly easier than its predecessor.

The good:

The checkpoints are, for the most part, not hidden and are much more plentiful.

Still some issues figuring out what to do to advance, but not nearly as bad as its predecessor.

Most areas between the boss and the next checkpoint are rather short and easy.

Multi-boss fights have more openings to interact with opponents

Titanite slabs are much more abundant.

The post-game content is better than pretty much anything seen in DS1 (save the first half of the Artorias DLC in DS1).

Bosses' hitboxes and animations are much better aligned here than the previous title.

There is some BS, but nothing here is as terrible as Lost Izalith or the Tomb of Giants.

After beating the game you'll want to beat the ENTIRE game again, rather than in DS1 where you want to repeatedly just finish Anor Londo then restart.

Still beautiful, still Dark Souls.

Posted: November 14
This one may very well be the best.
People tend to forget how utterly broken the first Dark Souls game was (I myself have too), and since this game unfortunately still has massive problems (mostly hitbox related like in DS1, and the agility stat doesn't help it even though it is adjustable). This game is also less forgiving than DS1 and 3, i.e. you cannot just run to the boss gate and have the animation save your lazy bum (you kind of can in the original version, because it has less enemies), which I actually used to dislike myself, not anymore though. I still kind of enjoy playing it. Maybe I just like broken games.
One huge problem this game has is that it has items that can only be obtained through online play. The Ring of Thorns +2 is a great example, which requires the player to invade 1000 (!!!!) times and win. This game is dead (at least the official servers, not sure about the custom ones) so this is basically impossible, especially at higher levels (my main character is at SL262 with ~20M soul memory, so basically I am unofficially banned from playing online). The invisible weapon rings are just painful to get.
Not going to talk much about the level design as people know how dumb it is.
Another small problem is that the player character doesn't seem to be entirely ambidextrous (but is still ambidextrous, in DS1 and 3, holding an axe in your left hand will block with the axe, and holding a shield in your right hand will start attacking with the shield, which is completely dumb). For some enemy types (I only tested two, with slash damage) the left hand seems to deal less damage than the right hand. Blocking with shields takes the same amount of stamina with both hands, but holding a shield with the right hand seems to block less damage. This is a problem with inventory management, mostly.
Also, I hope the person who decided to show controller mappings on PC doesn't work on PC games anymore. I'm sure he was also the genius behind the double clicking idea as well, which has to be enabled and disabled each time you launch the game, because they refused to fix this bug, if you don't want input delay. You couldn't even do anything about it in the original DS2. Not to mention that you enable *disabling* double clicking just to confuse you. There's also this bug where it occasionally makes your character do a 360 spin and push you a *lot* to the left if you press the left strafe button while running (not sure if this happens if you try going to the right, never noticed that) which is extremely annoying near ledges and on bridges.
The camera being moved to the right when the inventory is open is an interesting concept, but makes walking while switching items annoying.
Balancing is actually very good (except for Iron Keep, which is a bit overkill to be honest), some buffs are are a little bit overpowered, there are still ways to one shot bosses, but it's still mostly balanced, especially PvP (or maybe I just didn't get enough minmax builds). This is also the only game which is beatable by only leveling strength and not using any weapons. It took me around 9 months but it was possible without armour as well. Beating the Asylum Demon with only fists was enough for me in DS1. Took 15 minutes each time I tried because I always messed up in the end. After Firelink Shrine, that's annoying. And yes, Shrine of Amana was as great as you can imagine.
Bonfire ascetics make replaying the game much much more enjoyable as they allow you to fight your favourite bosses without having to replay the entire game again. Doesn't restock shops, which sucks, but it's still better than nothing.
This game also looks the best. DS1 and 3 are very ugly in comparison. DS2 is just stunning. It is also quite colourful, unlike 1 and 3 which have an unrealistic limited colour palette.
It truly deserves to be called Best Souls II. It really doesn't deserve all the negativity it gets/got.

Recently played DS2 (SotFS), the only mainline FromSoftware game I hadn’t yet tried. After giving it a fair shot, I’m putting it aside. It lacks the appeal of Dark Souls or Elden Ring, and I don’t enjoy it enough as a standalone to keep playing.

Main issues:
- Disconnected, small levels feel like themed world checklists (pirate cove, castle, forest etc.). They're over as soon as they start and the lack of cohesion between them all makes them feel pointless.
- The hub-and-spoke world design isn't well implemented. Navigation and progression can be confusing. In DS1 and DS3, even if you're lost, there's always somewhere to go and you don't often hit dead-ends.
- Controls feel off; enemy hitboxes are weird and the lock-on mechanism only seems to lock the camera, not your aim. This can make combat awkward and frustrating.
- SotFS is supposedly harder due to non-stop (!) trollish enemy placement. It slows the pace and gets tedious checking corners and behind to find enemies. Maybe this is an attempt to compensate for the levels being so short? Misses the mark if so.
- Health penalties on death didn’t bother me much, but might turn others off. I tend to play glass cannon characters and I found this encouraged me to focus on stats that improved mobility and dodging.
- Torches are ineffective and clunky—forcing one-handed combat, wasting time relighting, and often leaving me running in darkness unnecessarily. They're so unusual that I suspect their mechanics are relics from early development.

Overall, DS2’s PvE fails to hold my interest due to these issues. I’ve heard PvP is good, so perhaps if you enjoy that, you might try it and enjoy the game if you don't expect much from the story or exploration.

Nota 78/100


Dark Souls II é curioso na minha jornada pelos Souls. Foi o primeiro que joguei, e dropei, e o último da trilogia que finalizei.
Desisti na época por achar muito difícil, mas anos depois voltei determinado a fechar a franquia e enfrentar um chefe que sempre me chamou atenção: Sir Alonne, cuja estética achei incrível e diferente da melancolia típica da série.

Recomecei com um personagem de destreza, meu estilo favorito, mas perdi meu save com mais de 40 horas após um problema no PC. Refazendo tudo do zero, aprendi a dominar o ritmo de combate, que é bem diferente dos outros Souls.


Pontos ruins

Estus lentíssimo. O personagem demora 354 anos pra curar, e só melhora com Adaptabilidade (que ainda teve o nome traduzido errado).

Excesso de inimigos. Muita área entupida de mobs sem sentido, principalmente nas DLCs. Torna a exploração cansativa e chata. Acaba forçando você tendo de agrar inimigos com arco de longe

Soul Memory. Péssima ideia: limita o co-op entre amigos ao invés de balancear automaticamente como nos outros jogos.

Frames e ações inconsistentes. Rolagens e ações básicas não garantem invencibilidade, o que gera muitos golpes “fantasmas”.

Debuffs confusos. O sistema de veneno e maldição é ninguém que eu conheço entende como funciona aquela barra esquisita que aparece lá pra medir o debuff.

Pontos bons

Ramos Perfumados. Itens limitados que criam boas escolhas.

Fogueira da Adversidade. Permite refazer áreas e bosses, ótima ideia para quem gosta de repetir desafios.

Empunhar duas armas iguais. Exclusividade dentre a trilogia é divertida que deixa o combate mais variado.

Magias - Tem uma variedade maior de magias no geral do que nos outros jogos.

Mesmo com todos os defeitos, Dark Souls II me divertiu mais que o primeiro. É um jogo imperfeito, mas cheio de identidade, e que merece ser jogado por qualquer fã da série Souls.

Dark Souls II is a game that evokes a lot of mixed feelings in me. DS2 is a game that does a lot of experimenting and big risks that ultimately put the experience somewhere between okay and downright awful. I've seen a lot of comments that the hate is overblown and it is actually a very good and maybe even the best DS game. While I agree the hate was overblown I can't help but think that we're swinging too far the other way with how much it's being praised. Since I am going to be pretty critical, let's start with the things I like.

The Good:

DS2 has a very addictive gameplay loop, despite the flaws I'll talk about later on in the review. The progression of weapons, armor, and skills feels really good and the pacing of getting stronger is genuinely really fun. This might be the primary reason why I'm able to mostly look past it's issues. Likewise, DS2's weapon and armor design is pretty good, I always looked forward to trying out new weapons and armor. Though I've only played through it once, it's easy to see that the flexibility and variety of builds far outstrips the original as well.

What DS2 does right, it does really well. NPCs are also a major highlight of the game for me. DS1's NPCS, while charming, always felt more mysterious and distant. DS2's NPCs feel more like real, living people and not like phantoms, which lends to DS2's overall atmosphere of a world that is beginning to die as opposed to DS1's atmosphere of a world that's been long dead. Majula in particular really enhances this, feeling like a growing community with each new NPC you discover. If you talk to certain NPCs enough, they'll give you gifts which is a nice touch. The NPCs you talk to have backgrounds, goals, and fears. While you could say that DS1's NPCs have these things, they're just more fleshed out in DS2. It just makes the hub and world feel a little more alive and connected.

The Bad:

Okay, now for the bad, which there is a lot of. The biggest, most glaring issue that trumps anything else is the design philosophy when it comes to making difficult encounters, which is: throw massive amounts of enemies and hazards at the player. DS2 is infamous for its ganks and to be completely honest, that is terrible game design. When comparing it to DS1, which had intentional and clever placement of enemies that were at a manageable number, DS2 will have giant groups of enemies rush you from across the map. Additionally, they will rarely break aggro and will chase you down if you try to run past, while attempting a boss for example. This forces you to fight these hordes of enemies every single time you're trying to beat a boss, which can get miserable quickly. Add onto the fact for some of the hardest bosses, like those in the DLCs, have brutal boss runs with not only hordes of enemies but also environmental hazards or enemies shooting magic at you from afar. It's not clever, it's not fun, it's just creating difficulty through volume as opposed to unique, quality move sets.

The hit boxes in this game are criminal. Everyone knows about the adaptability skill and how infuriatingly crucial it is to level it to reach a point that was just default in DS1 but beyond that, the hit boxes for enemies is just unfair. Enemies will be able to snatch you when you're multiple feet away, outside of their attack animations. Or they'll be able to damage you when their animations indicate they missed. I don't know if this was a design choice or an oversight but it's atrocious and very unfair. Another very poor gameplay decision was the constant loss of health for every death that you can only reverse with human effigies, a finite resource in the game. In a game series about constantly dying over and over, punishing the player for dying is abysmal design. It just sucks.

Finally, one of the biggest disappointments for me was the art design and atmosphere. Many people talk about how DS2 has the best atmosphere of the trilogy and that's just not true. DS1's atmosphere of darkness, mystery, and despair has been replaced in DS2 with an atmosphere that frankly feels cheaper in all respects. While some areas in DS2 have a good atmosphere and feel reminiscent of DS1 (Drangleic Castle, Majula) many others feel out of place and honestly like a theme park. Iron Keep and Huntsman's Copse make me feel like I'm playing WoW and that is the antithesis of the central visual themes of the dark souls series. This goes down to even how the undead look. In DS1, the undead's skin was stretched, dehydrated, withered. The undead in DS2 look like a stereotypical frankenstein costume, it's embarrassing. The boss designs, for the most part, do not help this. Many of the bosses in DS2 are generic, fantasy stereotypes as opposed to the unique twists on classic fantasy tropes that FromSoft is known for. Generic skeleton bosses, knight bosses, or demon bosses (some of which are very important to the story) make me feel like I'm playing a low quality MMO. Not to mention the reuse of bosses from DS1, there are a number of rehashes from DS1 that are just worst in every way (Ornstein, Bell Gargoyles). It's just lazy and uninspired. There are, of course, good boss designs but they are by far the exceptions and not the rule.

Overall, DS2 is a very flawed entry in FromSoft's catalogue and honestly if I hadn't played it with my friends, I don't know that I would have even finished the base game. My recommendation is that if you're souls crackhead like me and you see it on sale, pick it up and give it a try. It's far from the horrible game people made it out to be on launch but it's certainly not the brilliant, misunderstood underdog of the series that people today keep claiming it is.

Posted: November 5
There's some maddening nonsense and lack of polish that reveal themselves as you play. The default keyboard-mouse controls require you to double click to execute heavy attacks. This means there's an inevitable delay for registering light attacks. This control scheme has to be the invention of a troll, or a madman. Good thing you can disable double clicks. But! Here's the thing: Even though the game remembers this decision after you quit, and displays it as such in the settings, you still have to re-enable and re-disable this setting every time you start the game for it to actually work. These issues are so stupid, so obviously bad, so trivially easy to fix, and so loudly reported and ignored, that they feel almost intentional.

The continued refusal to include cloud saves is a lousy decision, even if you were to justify it with a willingness to prevent tampering with online features (ie the features I care the absolute least about in this franchise). What if I care about having a backup save of my single player game more than the possibility of being invaded by some rando?

Other stuff that bothers me: Some sound effects that should feel important are limp and unfinished. We're miles away from DS1's mystical roar that triggers when you rest near a bonfire. Boss corpses and particle effects can poof in and out of existence. Whenever you decide to interact with a chest, the contained item remembers to spawn in when you're halfway the chest opening animation. Fog walls look more like paper than fog, cutting through the ground like razor. Illusory walls that hide vital resources are opened by pressing the interact button instead of attacking them, and this arbitrary change in basic world interaction is not communicated to you anywhere. None of these problems exist in DS1. So what the hell happened here? I have no idea.

BUT.

The world is absolutely magical and full of whimsy. It is filled with unique, weird nonsense that are there seemingly for vibes' sake and little else. It is a capital-F Fantasy world with an identity all its own, which is something not many authors/teams get right. Once your stats get better and you find a weapon you're comfortable with, moving around is great. The main hub is perfect. It is the best hub in any game ever. The environments are gorgeous, and the sense of exploring a tangible, mysterious world is fantastic, which is the main appeal of these games for me. DS2 has the most pleasant vibes of any Souls games. It is, despite all its faults, currently my favorite Dark Souls game.

Is it worth buying? I don't know. It's a rough gem that feels abandoned. Its creators have moved on. Owning it doesn't even offer you the privilege of backing up your saves. It IS a great game with a lot of character though.

Well of course I recommend it. It's my favourite Soulsborne game. Initially playing it on Ps3 a ton, then Ps4 when SotFS released.

It has the a lot of variety for enemies. 3 has a few more and Elden Ring just copied enemies straight from 2 and 3 so it doesn't get any brownie points. This was the game that introduced Power stancing, an absolutely great ability. Every build is pretty 'viable' in this game, and everything feels pretty good.

Bosses are actually varied (Looking at Elden Ring) and usually unique. The story is very well written and presented in this game, plus the locations are grim and you can really see the corruption taking over. Except Majula.. beautiful Majula, the only place we need.

If you're worried about lack of weapons and armour, I wouldn't. 3 and ER probably have more, but the sheer amount of items in Ds2 is literally insane. The fashion is great too!

However even as a true Ds2 lover, and glazer. I recognise it's faults, and there are many.

First is obviously the most heard, Adaptability. Tying i-frames to a skill is a very non user-friendly choice. Second is the one I battled the most back in the day, Co-op being tied not to level.. but soul memory. If I decide to farm an enemy for some armour and my friend hadn't guess what? Can't summon until they get those souls too. Ring helps this but is still present.

The game is very clunky. It is also quite old, but it's still something I have to, and do recognise as someone who recommends this game. You are slower in this game, 3 and ER spoiled people with their anime movesets and all that bs. The hitboxes are also quite poor. You aren't abysmally slower but:
2 handed weapons feel like a 2 handed weapon. You're lugging a lot of weight. Rolling is still quite fast, but it's more imo realistic and nice feeling. Estus Drinking forces you to stop and is super slow, restricting panic heals. Looting is also quite slow.

Aside from these ^ the rest of the issues I would say are quite minimal. But most of the hate for this game, really is artificial. People on the Internet *told you* it was bad and you believed it. But people who actually played it, recognise it as a good game. So have an open mind with all I've said and give it a try. If you still don't like it, then it's fine it's not for you.

Elden Ring ruined the Franchise >:)

Posted: November 4
Dark Souls II: At the End of Ash, There Is Still Light

I still remember the first time I arrived at Majula.
That seaside ruin, swept by a damp wind, had almost nothing—
a bonfire, a windmill, a silent woman.
The sunlight was pale, the air was salty, and the music felt like a memory.
Back then, I didn’t yet understand that Dark Souls II wasn’t a game to “beat.”
It was a world meant to be felt—a world that had already fallen apart, but where people still refused to vanish.

This game never hurries to tell you anything.
It doesn’t throw its story into your face like most games do.
It simply stands there, silent, waiting to see if you can find meaning within the ruins.
Like those moments in life when you walk into an empty place—no one explains it to you, but you know it once mattered.

I

The map of Dark Souls II isn’t perfect.
Its world isn’t a miracle of interlocking design, but a crumbling labyrinth.
And yet, I love this imperfection.
It feels like reality itself: paths split, meanings blur, landscapes collapse into one another without logic.
You keep walking—through poison fogs, into castles, down into caverns—and everywhere there’s that mute sense of history.
The world feels like the corpse of the past, and we are wandering inside its bones.

I died in the same place over and over, until frustration turned into quiet concentration.
With every death, I memorized a stone’s edge, an enemy’s rhythm, a hidden shortcut.
The game, in punishing you, is also teaching you.
It’s teaching you what it means to endure—to find worth inside repetition.
Each time you rise from the ashes, you’re not just playing; you’re existing.

II

And the story? It barely speaks.
The NPCs mumble fragments, as if they’ve forgotten who they once were.
Yet that silence makes everything more human.
I began to imagine their pasts, to stitch meaning from scraps.
Each item description reads like a torn letter; each boss feels like a fallen king, a knight, or a mother whose memory curdled into wrath.

Dark Souls II never tells you who is good or evil.
It only shows how time erodes will.
You witness kingdoms decaying, ideals dissolving, the flame itself fading—
and still, you keep walking.
There’s no reason, no reward, only a journey you cannot stop taking.

III

I’ve always loved its light—not sunlight, but the trembling light of a torch.
When you walk into a cavern and raise that flickering flame,
the walls glisten with moisture, and the air itself feels rotten.
It’s terrifying, yet strangely peaceful.
The beauty of Dark Souls is not the kind that pleases the eyes;
it’s the beauty of order born from ruin.

A broken cathedral, a rusted suit of armor, a banner waving in a dead wind—
these things remind you the world isn’t dead,
it’s merely been forgotten.

IV

I think about the combat—it’s cruel, but honest.
It destroys arrogance, dismantles impatience.
It doesn’t care how skilled you are; it only demands sincerity.
When I finally defeated a boss, I didn’t feel triumph.
I felt something quieter—a breath after exhaustion.

You look at the fallen creature,
the message “Soul Acquired” appears,
and you wonder: What did I actually win?

Perhaps nothing. Perhaps only survival.
And maybe that’s enough.
Because life, too, rarely rewards effort with applause.
It simply asks us to keep breathing through the pain.

V

When the final door opened and the fire awaited, I didn’t feel joy.
I felt hollow—like I had witnessed the end of a world.
Yet within that hollowness, I began to understand what “soul” really means.

Dark Souls never glorifies strength; it glorifies the will to keep living beneath collapse.
It’s a tragic kind of romance—
the fire will die,
and still we choose to light it.

So when I lifted my head from the screen
and saw the first pale light of dawn outside my window,
I realized something simple:

Life itself is a long, dark soul.
You stumble through fog, awaken by the bonfire,
unsure of the goal—only knowing you must keep walking.

And when morning finally comes,
and you look back upon the ruins, you’ll understand—
you never conquered the darkness.
You merely learned how to walk within it.

After ~60 hours and making completing the game, it is clear that this game has been grotesquely underrated by the insipid copy/paste mainstream-gaming-media, followed by group-think YooHooToober "game reviewers". (I cannot imagine how much grief Tomohiro Shibuya and Yui Tanimura must have endured at the hands of these NPC game-reviewer dorks).

Pros:
- Locations are both wildly varied, stunning and oozing atmosphere
- Music, though sparse, is wonderful
- The pacing of the first 80% of the game is excellent and very satisfying
- Excellent variety of weapons
- NPCs well written and voiced
- Enemy variety broad and imaginative (though generally less intimidating than those of DS1)
- Excellent hub-world that doesn't feel like a cliche hub
- Difficulty ramps smoothly in first 80% of the game, making you feel ever-so-slightly behind the curve / under pressure at all times
- Full of pleasant surprises, such as some enemies fleeing when you're holding the blazing torch

Cons:
- Adaptability stat throws an unnecessary wrench into the game's mechanics
- Areas appear grand upon first encounter, but soon feel small once progress is made. Areas are more bite-sized than DS1
- Bosses are easier and less intimidating than in DS1
- Combat is clunkier than DS1, with large "I'll just wait here a moment while you cleave me open with your sword" windows of time for the enemy to punish you
- Locations, though beautiful, are less intimidating in their scale than in other DS games
- Parrying is less encouraged than in other SoulsBorne games, and when you do complete a parry your R1 attack is far far far less satisfying than in DS1
- Late game levelling becomes an absolute chore, with disappearing enemies, and difficult to find weapon upgrade materials. Making this section feel more like a job than a game.
- The pacing of the latter 20% of the game is a huge letdown. The final boss sits waiting for you in the mid-game area of Drangelic Castle, which robs the final confrontation of any sense-of-occasion. There's no hard-won battle to reach the boss, and therefore no gravitas. (The Throne should have been placed in a mountain-top castle, with a gruelling uphill gauntlet to fight through, filled with new enemies that train you to develop the skillset necessary to thwart the final boss, while building up a sense of intimidation via the perception of overwhelming odds, such as a lightning storm atop the castle that worsens as you approach, and eerie music full of diminished chords)
- DLC is resentfully cheap and difficult (I suspect the devs were upset about the game's initial reception and sought to prove themselves by way of difficulty-spiking the DLC with cheap lock-you-in-an-area gauntlets full of projectile-enemies that curse and mob you).

This one was a mixed bag for me.

I think that I was very much intrigued and motivated by beating the game than enjoying the actual moment to moment gameplay.

It often felt too easy to get lost and having barely any directions lead to me stumbling around the world not knowing what to do, not understanding whether I was progressing. The character gained levels, and with all of that wondering some areas felt like a breeze (more often toward the end of the game) while some felt like I am not meant to do those yet.

It is hard to clearly tell what is the best path in regards to challenge. Does a particular area try to teach perseverance or does it try to discourage an underleveled character for interacting with the area for too long? No idea. If you do choose to push on it feels like you can learn it and get over the hurdle, only to get showered with resources that make the next area less than what it was intended probably.

The effigies idea and the degrading health I didn't like at all. The need to interact with the item feels like it was a change from ds1 where I have used like zero humanities. Still, I feel like I have used like 5 to ten effigies and had around 50 sitting in my stash at the end.
Going down to 50% health (or 75% later with the ring) felt like being free of having to manage this at all - I'm at rock bottom, so nothing is wearing me down.
Looking through this section, this feels like a case of terrible game understanding but it just felt too natural to hoard a resource that feels scarce and too impactful. By the time I had enough to where I would not need to bother I got the 75% ring and discarded the idea of having to manage this at all.

The biggest gripes with the game that I have encountered was with the lock-on vs no lock-on.
When I started the game I played with a halberd as soon as I found it - I enjoyed the halberd from ds1 and wanted to continue with it.
The halberd was a mixed bag for some time. The hitbox of the light and heavy attacks felt needle-thin but lock on helped that a lot. The swipe attacks available when running never really worked well as the weapon hit some random tree or a wall or anything and got canceled. The windups for the attacks are also not that fast.
This all continued up to the fog forest with the ghost enemies. I just couldn't hit them. I have looked up a video on this and the video alleges that ds2 character movement and attacks work in 8 specific directions, so a long weapon with a narrow hit box has a lot of space to miss.
I decided to change for a mace for some time, and it worked great (I also had the stat requirements for it), but in the very next location I found a black knight halberd - the exact same one that I used in ds1. I also remember the moveset being different, so I decided to play that and get stat requirements for it (27 dex btw).
The damage was great but the moveset was the same. Game started having more multi enemy encounters, and the halberd didn't work for me.
I have changed to a straight sword at this point and all my problems magically went away:
1. The game has 8 directions for attacks? Doesn't matter, the sword strikes in a 150 degree arc
2. There are more enemies in encounteres? No problem the weapon strikes fast and commonly hits everything
3. Stats reqs are small
4. Upgrade for normal titanite unlike the black knight stuff.
Good that internet discourse on lock-on vs no lock-on is 50% get good, 50% why are you even trying <this>, whatever this actually is? Watch any video review and you can count how many times people praise the game and use a halberd on the hand of a quad amputee train accident victim - 0.

The game felt like such a relieve, but also did the game teach me this very well? Should I have persevered and reaped my reward or mastering the halberd? Or was the reward never there and the weapon fits the game poorly?

Agility as an idea I didn't like. The game uses an old frame-breakpoint idea, which means only increases over a set value will do anything. That "Do anything" that it does is extremely hard to see, particularly with dodge roll I-frames. How am I expected to tell appart 5 frames from 6 frames - the difference of 0.033 seconds of something that isn't even visible except for getting hit at that one particular frame? You start at 85 and the recommended values for agi when googled are 99 to 105. That is about 20-27 levelups which may as well feel like nothing. Why not dump this into vitality with 30hp a levelup you'll be sitting at a comfortable 1800 or something which is massive.
Agi needs to be googled. I am happy that I did.

For every interesting idea that the game proposed, there is a large baggage of conflicting choices that all amount to a sour taste in the mouth, though you can't quite pin down what it is exactly.

I have finished the game. I didn't interact with the dlc stuff to be done with it quicker. Looking back at the experience I feel like it wasn't half bad, but actually being the person who plays it was horrible.

I am a dog, but it is this game that made me feel this way. It is an interesting experience if you want to dive deeper into how the series developed and why choices have been made, but I can't recommend people go through this.

Would not recommend this to a friend.

Posted: October 31
Please stop listening to Youtubers.

The game is massive and fun. Unlike openworlds, the content in this game is very dense. Besides Frigid Outskirts, you don't end up running a big empty maps.

The game asks you to actually pay attention to your surrounding, not unlike tactical shooters. Check corners, be careful of chests and doors. Act like a Dark Souls 3 player just running and rolling their way to fog gates and you'll be, for good reason, punished. The game wants you to interact with its level design! This game has the most bosses in the series, but also has great levels between them. Running to fog gates is just turning the game into a mindless boss rush.

By the way! Find a level too difficult? Monsters only respawn up to twelve times! Everytime you fail reaching the boss or beating the boss, you progress towards making the game easier for you, so you will, eventually, be unstuck. The game also features healing consumables, so if you're low health after dealing with a lot of enemies, you can pop Lifegems to be fresh for the boss, without using any of your Estus Flasks.

You can level Agility through Attunement and Adaptability as a difficulty slider mechanic.

The weapons have the most varied movesets, and sets of weapons and sorceries in general. tons of builds possible, and tools to use depending on the situation.

Overall, it is my favorite game in the series. Stamina matters (unlike DS3), you have build variety (unlike DS1), the exploration is fun and rewarding (unlike Elden Ring).

Give this game a chance, and ignore souls sloptuber takes that they regurgigate for easy views and likes.

Posted: October 30
DS2 ended up being the last game of the trilogy for me, meaning that I unfortunately experienced a massive downgrade to what I felt were simply superior mechanics in not just DS3, but also DS1.

Having been aware of some of the oddities that exist in DS2, I avoided the pains caused by them by prioritising the acquisition of skills/items that help combat these issues. Even then, this did not help with my overall enjoyment of the game. The gameplay felt clunky, inconsistent, and unfair (ganks and ambushes my beloved). Level design felt claustrophobic, and uninspired. Apart from Majula and maybe Drangenleic, there are no awe-aspiring views in this game. No majorly impressive architecture, and disappointing boss rooms occupied by equally uninspired bosses.

The DLCs were rather... disappointing? They somehow manage to at times have even worse level design than the main game. The only worthy mention is The Crown of the Old King. The two major boss fights, if we ignore the copy & paste smelter demon, were quite fun and I felt that it was a glimpse at what combat in DS3 would become.

Unfortunately not much I can say in terms of positives. I do think DS2 is worth a play through as part of the trilogy, but I can't find any good reason to wholly recommend this game as it wouldn't stand on its own.

My Honest Review of Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin

The game starts off strong with a captivating cinematic and a mysterious new world to explore. You arrive in search of a cure for the undead curse, choose your name and class, and your journey begins.

From my experience so far, Dark Souls II is definitely challenging not just because of its difficulty, but largely due to its enemy placements, which can feel unfair at times. That said, you do start to master them with time and repetition.

One of the biggest hurdles is the Adaptability (ADP) stat, which affects your invincibility frames during rolls. It makes the early game feel clunky, but once you level it up, things smooth out. Another major difference is how crucial stamina management is in this game, Vigor and Endurance matter more than raw Strength, especially for survival and mobility.

If you're willing to dedicate yourself, learn enemy patterns, and embrace a fresh kind of challenge, Scholar of the First Sin is a rewarding experience. But if you're looking for more balanced enemy placement and no ADP stat to worry about, then Dark Souls III or even Dark Souls Remastered if you haven’t played it yet might be a better fit.

I've waited to finish all achievements before posting this review so I played long enough to form my opinion.
I do not recommend this game, especially for souls first timers. Not all is bad about this game though and I'll try my best to give it a fair review still.

With the positives, the game is long and even though if you buy this version you already have the DLCs, the DLC are quite long and complete.
The story is good, as a souls story is so you get a lot more of it by reading item descriptions and so on but very enjoyable story if you like Souls games. The atmosphere is amazing, the game is pretty in itself.
You have a good liberty to make interesting builds that you like and it's fine as long as you know how to use your build ofc but that's souls games anyway.
Crown of Ivory king is an amazing addition to that game, I really love that area, the story, the atmosphere, the boss you fight. (Except one very specific -optional- area)

And that's the negatives, I'll try to avoid spoiler but that one optional area I just talked about is horrendous. Not visually appealing, tons of enemies that pop out from nowhere, really nothing interesting going on at all.
For the rest, the game is full of ganks. Now ganks aren't inherently bad, but I'd argue most of the ganks in this game aren't well done. There are exceptions namely in the first area of the game especially in . But after these early game area which are really nice, the game takes a turn and start just popping tons of enemies in corridor, ambush all the time. The same trick over and over again. A single enemy walking alone then 4 others come in, all pretty tough. It gets boring. I'm a big fromsoftware fan and I've always been amazed by boss design and musics but there really isn't much memorable boss or music in this game. Then again, a few exceptions but I'm really disappointed on that regard too. The world building is a bit weird... I mean at some point you take an elevator at the top of a tower to end up... in a lava area? I read some people try to defend that but it's pretty sad that it's this way. I mean the elevator could have gone down below the ground instead, it'd make a lot more sense. But it's not like it's a bother in the game just a detail you notice only if you really pay attention to where you're going (if you're just here for bonk bonk, then it's whatever). And finally, the game feels so slow. Opening a chest, opening a door, entering a fog gate, healing... everything is just so slow and I get some people like that and I respect it, but it's not for me.

All in all, I wouldn't call it the worse game in the world or anything. It's a Souls game still, all things considered it's not THAT bad but I'm very disappointed in comparison to all the other fromsoftware games. I've had some fun but significantly less than in other games. It's quite a forgettable game I think and I'm pretty sad for that.

The most expansive dungeon crawling game from all fromsoftware games as of the time that I'm writing this review. It takes a loooong time to explore everything and all the levels have their own unique level design. It's amazing. Moment to moment it's about mastering the refined Dark Souls 1 combat mechanics, changing your stats to your preferred play style (do you want long distance dodging or more i frames with short distance dodging? for example.), and carefully observing EVERYTHING in the map to get treasure, identify traps, and not end up in situations that cost you your life. Buy it if what you want is 60+ hours of deep rich dungeon crawling gameplay.

Don't buy it if you only like 1 on 1 fights in clean large open space areas like in elden ring. This game is all about unfair fights and you have to master the raw mechanics for the most part to deal with them because there are no weapon arts to save you. Don't buy if you're not that into dwelling that deep into dark souls styled gameplay mechanics and experimentation. Also you have to be REALLY good at timing dodges too haha

Dark Souls 2 is also one of the most experimentatal soulsborne games! In dark souls 1 (mild spoilers) you could only do long distance dodging by having a certain ring equipped. In dark souls 2 you can dodge pretty far just depending on your equip load! There is also microstun lock and true stunlock in dark souls 2... The micro stunlock is brutal cause while you can still act... Every time an enemy touches you, you can barely move around. Making it waaaay harder to get out of tight spots. You have to prepare, observe everything, and think about all the mechanics carefully a lot! Dark Souls 2 is also when they introduced Power Stance for Dual Weilding! You just hold the 2 hand button while having a similar weapon on each hand. If you're strong enough you'll enter power stance.

Also, you may have heard that the hit boxes don't make sense in dark souls 2. What happens is that the handle of an enemy's weapon counts as a hit box too. It's a little annoying but if you avoid the enemy's handle as much as their blade you'll be fine. And don't expect grab animations to be seamless back then. Animations are hard. You can see your self get hit by the hit box but instead of the enemy turning to you, you teleport in front of the enemy when you get hit by the grab so that the grab animation can play properly... lol The HIT is valid, the teleport is just cause they didn't know how to make the animations perfect back then. Another caveat is that in order to not play online you have to temporarily disconnect your ethernet cable when you boot the game.

Have fun dwelling deep into a true a rich world full of so much content, adventure, and awesome game mechanics!

“Praise the Sun? More like Pray for Medals ☀️😩”

Coming straight from Dark Souls 1, I thought I knew what I was getting into. I was wrong. Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is like visiting a parallel universe where everything looks familiar but feels… off. The roll timing, the enemy placements, the way you accidentally fall off every ledge — it’s all part of the Drangleic experience.

That said, it’s still an awesome game once you adjust. The build variety is huge, dual wielding is fun, and power stancing makes you feel like a god. The bosses might not have the same magic as Ornstein & Smough or Artorias, but they’ve got their own charm (and by charm, I mean questionable hitboxes).

But the real boss isn’t any of them — it’s the Sunlight Medal grind.
If you’re going for achievements like Sunlight Spear, prepare for pain. Unless you’re super lucky, you’ll be farming falconers or grinding the Shrine of Amana until your eyes glaze over. Praise the sun? At this point, I’m just praying for more medal to drop.

Still, despite the grind and the quirks, DS2: SotFS has its own soul. It’s different, but it grows on you — like poison in Earthen Peak.
I genuinely enjoyed replaying it.

If you ever feel frustrated during run-backs, don’t worry — enemies despawn after a certain kill threshold, making reaching the boss easier over time.
On the other hand, if you’re farming and want enemies to stay, you can prevent them from despawning by joining the Covenant of The Company of Champions located to the right of the path leading to Heide's Tower of Flame.

Also, don’t forget to level up Adaptability — it increases Agility, which gives you more invincibility frames (i-frames) when rolling.
Important breakpoints to note are around Agility 92 / 96 / 99, and it also improves Estus usage speed, making a huge difference in survivability.

Don’t worry too much about spending levels here — leveling in DS2 is easier, allowing more build variety, and you can even reset your stats later by giving a Soul Vessel to one specific Firekeeper you meet early in the game.

New Game Plus (NG+) also adds fun surprises — new items in chests, extra red phantom invaders, and even new encounters, like one added on the path to The Duke’s Dear Freja. By NG+++, you can even buy a new spells from Chancellor Wellager, giving you more reasons to keep pushing forward.

If you want a peaceful playthrough and don’t want to deal with invasions, you can run the game in offline mode — just disable your internet connection or launch Steam in Offline Mode before starting the game. You’ll lose messages and bloodstains, but it’s a great way to play stress-free.

If you plan to play, I recommend tweaking your settings and controls — this video helped a ton:

Unfortunately, I had to tweak one keyboard setting to remove the input delay on mouse clicks every time I open the game, but after that, everything felt much smoother.

After tweaking controls, here are some useful combat inputs for keyboard players:
• Q or Hold Q to two-hand your left or right weapon
• Guard Break: press W + Attack (Left Click) at the same time
• Jump Attack: press W + Heavy Attack (Shift + Left Click) at the same time
• Guard/Block: hold Right Mouse Button (depends on your setup)

Here are some helpful keyboard shortcuts too:
• R for the Green A button (also handy for skipping dialogues —Bearer.. seek.. seek.. lest)
• Backspace for the Red B button (fast bonfire reset for mobs after sitting in a bonfire)
• E for the Blue X button (also lets you unequip attunements easily)
• Q for the Yellow button (for lighting torches near bonfires)

Lighting torches also really helps with visibility in dark areas, especially near water reflections and poison fog zones — and some enemies actually fear fire, which can make exploring dangerous places like No-Man’s Wharf a lot easier. It’s both practical and atmospheric.

8.5/10 — Praise the Sun, curse the drop rates. ☀️🙏

This review is about a decade late but here goes:

The first Souls game I ever played. The original version was Good, and Sotfs made it better. It is one of those games I always return to; just making a new character, playing through the early parts, and fighting the Pursuer makes me feel as though I've quaffed a healing potion.

If you are reading the reviews for DS2 you've probably heard about "adaptability" and you are nervous and have also heard many contradicting things, so I will put your mind at ease.

Adaptability, and to a lesser extent Attunement, are 2 stats you can level which effect your Agility. The main thing Agility does is determine the iframes in your roll. At 96 Agility you will have 11 iframes (like in DS1), and at 99 you will have 12 iframes. There's no reason to go above 99 Agility.

This is basically all you need to know. You will get many levels and there is genuinely no reason to fret over this. Just allocate your stats in whatever way is most amenable to you.

As much as I love DS1, DS3, and Elden Ring, none of them have captured what Dark Souls 2 did (and to that many people will say "Good"). But to me it is the atmosphere, the magic, the combat, the flavor. I can fully acknowledge it is not the best, but it is my favorite and I love it for what it is.

Posted: October 21
This game is wildly over-hated. The issues that many complain about in regards to this game, come from the simple fact that players refuse to use their brain to work within the constraints set for them by the developer. Dark Souls 2 experimented in a lot of ways; both good and bad. In terms of the good, we can see the now ubiquitous art direction, that FromSoftware is known for, come to form; the story telling is utterly incredible, building off of the foundation of Dark Souls 1; and much of the systems used to create added difficulty are well formed and sit effectively within the pacing of the game. For the bad, we have to mention the Adaptability stat, that is now no longer a part of the series. Adaptability in Dark Souls 2 is a stat that directly affects the amount of I-Frames you receive when rolling and the speed at which you can drink Estus. This stat is incredibly underwhelming and down right annoying. It would have fit better as a sub-stat that is related inversely to your Vitality stat, which is obvious; as this is the solution FromSoftware ended up switching to for Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring. Additionally for the bad, some of the boss fights for transitional areas of the game are quite lackluster.

Overall however, these negatives never seemed to bother me too much, as there was just so many amazing experiences to be had all the way through. If you are skeptical about this title, be assuaged this game is well worth the money. Another absolute BANGER in the SoulsBorne franchise.

There are only a few tips I have to give, in order for you to have a smoother experience:

1. You must level Adaptability, if you want to have a good time.
2. Do not run through areas. Several run-backs may have enemies, so learn to fight through them and zen out. This game was redesigned to be incredibly punishing for those who attempt to skip enemies. (unlimited agro range, no immunity in fog walls, relay agro, quit outs do not reset agro, more punishing poise damage). You will die a lot, if you do not kill enemies strategically, one at a time, on your way to the fog wall.
3. DON'T PULL THE LEVER!

Yk, I just realized this is the only Dark Souls game I didn't review.

Overall, I go from appreciating this game to liking it all the time. There's a number of things that I have issues with. I gotta say, maybe it's a me thing, but my biggest issue with this game is how easy it is to get lost. I was stuck after finishing The Lost Bastille and Heide's Tower of Flame for like 3 months. I refused to look up anything, only for me to give up and Google that I needed to talk to a specific NPC to progress. There's a portion of blame to put on me, but I still found the game kinda confusing to traverse sometimes. Eventually I did find my way of course. Also the default controls in keyboard and mouse are TERRIBLE. I know souls games are meant to be played on controller, but my Bluetooth has been ♥♥♥♥♥♥ forever now (code 43 whatever tf that means) and I haven't been able to fix it.

And of course, many runbacks and enemy placement/balancing are off.

That being said, I really love the level diversity, the armor, a lot of the music, a lot of the NPC's, and the main story. Like any Souls game, it takes a lot of effort from the player to understand what the story is through item descriptions, NPC dialogue, and world design. But that's what I love these games for. Not to mention this game genuinely did push the franchise in an interesting direction by questioning the cycle of relinking the flame and pushing the player to think more about what their role in this world really is.

It's a game that tests you both in your mechanical tolerance and conceptions of this world following the first. It's a mixed bag for sure, but with more light than dark if you catch my drift.

Posted: October 20
Recommended for FromSoftware enthusiasts, but nobody else lol.
---
It feels like a grand, dark fantasy epic. If you are sad, DS1 will probably somehow alleviate that. But DS2 will probably make it worse. It is a profoundly melancholy game.
---
For most of the game, the main challenge is the level more than the boss. This game has a lot of bosses, but they are the dessert. The meal is the level.
---
It's really fantastic, but you need to put effort into letting it convert you. It will not go out of its way to charm you. If you have already played other FromSoftware games, then you know how to patiently figure out the game and slowly snowball your character into a monstrous force.

Difficult, but not in a twitchy sense. It requires a lot of patience. 

If you absolutely hate fighting multiple enemies at once, DON'T GET THIS GAME. That is the primary way this game enforces difficulty. 

--
Do you NEED to play the first one? No, not really. DLC content and the ending become more poignant if you have played the first one. But even if you play both, you probably won't know what the hell is going on in the story.
---
Should you get THIS Scholar of the First Sin edition? I haven't played the original. But if you're interested in this game, this edition already comes with all of the DLC installed. The 3 DLC each feel like very distinct, huge areas and like their own grand adventure. You feel very accomplished for beating each one. They are really gorgeous, but are also very challenging.

My understanding is that SoFS has replaced the original version as the standard. When people talk about DS2, they are probably talking about THIS experience. The feeling I get is that the original is more "niche" and for like...historian people, lmao. If you adore being pedantic, then probably get the original version and separately buy the DLC for it.
-------

(+) Incredible amount of care and effort went into making this. Very lengthy game with plenty of side content. It is very difficult to separate the "skippable" stuff from the main branch stuff--sometimes MORE effort went into the "skippable" sections. 
----------

WARNING: You can't play this game on multiple devices. You can install it on different devices, but the save won't be uploaded to some cloud. 

If you are a tinkerer, there are workarounds. 

But it is not seamless like DS1. With DS1, you can freely go from device to device and keep playing the same character. It's not like that here.

This game really is better than DS1 and DS3, and im not even done with it yet.

If you played DS1 or DS3, this game does everything those 2 games does, but better in so many ways.

map design, music, bosses, difficulty spike is all 10/10 here. This is the game you've always wanted.

The map design is excellent here:

From the start as soon you meet the firekeeper you'll understand what i mean, breath taking visuals, expansive imagination and thought put into every scene.

The way the areas are set up with enemy placement remind me of Demon's Souls, where every encounter that isnt a boss is meant to challenge you, rather than something you run thru like DS1/3, save for the phantom boys that harass you thru the game.

This means you'll have much more action, and much more challenge navigating the corridors of some castles or cliffs. Leading you to do what i usually dont do in other souls games, which is using items.

Using items in this game is much more implored than other titles because of the dense maps, scarce estus charges (early) and boss design and boss frequency.

Another interesting mechanic is that enemies just.. stop respawning after awhile if youre not in the covenant of champions (hardmode) or use a bonfire ascetic.

This means you can make whole areas have 0 enemies premanently when resting at bonfire if you so desire a clean walk through.

This also means the act of JOLLY cooperation is very much desired and very rewarding to do in this game, because you cant just farm the same spot over and over again. so if anyone needs help anywhere lmk!

All and all a great souls game, maybe the best, and its not to say the other games aren't also great, but this game is so unique in its own way compared to all the other souls games that its become my favorite.

I came seeking adventure. I found despair wearing a nice hat. If Dark Souls is a tragic opera, Dark Souls II is the director’s experimental remix where every aria hides a pitfall and every NPC is just a little too cryptic to actually help.

The enemies are meaner, the hitboxes drunker, and gravity is the real final boss. The level design feels like it was built by a committee of sadists who loved spirals, elevators, and bad life choices. And yet, underneath the jank and the questionable geometry lies something profound: melancholy, mystery, and the strange comfort of dying slightly better each time. It’s not about victory, it’s about endurance, curiosity, and that exquisite moment when despair turns into laughter.

10/10 would get one-shot by a hip-high pig again. IYKYK.

Dark Souls II does not deserve the hate. There, I said it.

As a plucky 19 year old (as of writing this review), I grew up with this game on Xbox 360, the nostalgia runs through my vanes. Now, with that being said, I believe that the hate this game receives is almost exclusively from people with rose-tinted glasses for Dark Souls I. It's also a great game, don't get me wrong, but I firmly believe that the reason DS2 is hated is because it is ... different.

As I'm sure we all know, the production team of DS2 was quite different than that of DS1. They imposed their own ideas onto this game. Did it work? Evidently not, because DS3 went right on back to the teet of DS1.

I've heard people say to not play DS2 when playing the souls games. Like ... huh? What are you on? The DS1 fans successfully baby-raged hard enough for From Software to stuff their tail betwixt their legs and waddle on back to their roots. I guess we're just scared of change.

Now, I hear you saying: crimsonvailkry, you just said you loved DS2 out of nostalgia; what makes you any different? I'll tell you what: I'm in the minority, so I'm right. Suck on that, DS1-tard.

No but seriously, DS2 has some serious issues. Like- what the hell was that bossfight in the Sunken City that was just three guys? Brother, c'mon. Executioner's Chariot? Discount Elden Ring hero's grave but it's all jank and runs at 30 frames. Blue Smelter Demon? Yeah, okay. Oh, and the final boss? Might as well call her final thrust of my meaty d*ck because that's all it took for her to die.

Final comments: DS2 was good but different, people got angry. Not as good as DS3 or Elden Ring probably, but still fun. Make a remaster FromSoft, we want it. Final score: 8.5/10.

Oh, and Majula is the best hub.

I was never really invested in this kind of game challenging, dark fantasy RPGs weren’t really my thing. But years ago, a friend told me to buy Dark Souls 2 because it was just 5 euros. I thought, why not give it a try? And honestly, I fell in love with it.

What surprised me the most was how much this game pulled me in. The atmosphere, the world design, the challenge it all came together perfectly. I ended up buying Dark Souls 3 and later the remastered version of Dark Souls 2, but if I had to pick a favorite from the trilogy, it would definitely be Dark Souls 2.

I know a lot of people call Dark Souls 2 the “black sheep” of the series, criticizing its mechanics or story compared to the others. But for me, it has a unique charm and depth that the others don’t quite match. The world feels vast and mysterious, the combat is tough but fair, and the variety in builds and gameplay styles kept me hooked for hours.

In the end, Dark Souls 2 isn’t just a great game to me it’s the one that turned me into a fan of the series.

Requiring me to sink 20 levels into a stat that I need to play the game is such a bad idea. I know, grass is green but it also has such a subtle and terrible effect on the rest of your play-through as it creates a separation where you are both over-leveled and under-leveled for areas,

it creates scenarios where its basically: "I dont have enough stats to make it through and leveling up in general is hard because of the Soul Amount Gap created by leveling ADP"

Estus comes back, I can use it while dying often without worrying about running out unlike healing gems

I have tried to enjoy this game for over 50 hours across this version and the original, yet this trash experience taints the trilogy that is Dark Souls. I'll start with the positives.

The ambiance is good, possibly the lore too (this games' lore is disconnected from 1 and 3, as such I haven't delved too deep in it), artistic design is pretty decent, not as unique as DS1, then again neither is DS3, so I won't hold it against DS2. Sometimes the hitboxes are brilliant. Enemies like the Pursuer and some of the torch mechanics are interesting.

Now for the bad:
The gameplay is just utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Heavy weapons don't hit, ADP is a horrible design choice, encounters are usually just you against a horde, meaningful enemy placement has been forgotten,
The hitboxes while sometimes brilliant are often atrocious, with the garbage tracking of heavy weapons not helping at all.
Being punished for every death (to a certain point) is kind of counter-intuitive, considering DS1 and DS3 are mostly about trial and error, so punishing the player for even attempting is strange and seems like quite the overstep. (Granted, the same argument could be used for DeS, but as it is a different "series" I don't have the same expectations).
When I say overstep I think the devs focused too much on increasing the difficulty of the game rather than how the difficulty is implemented.
The controls are clunky and imprecise, which is completely counter to the gameplay of the first or third Dark Souls game (Even DeS, BB or Sekiro). To be precise, one aspect of the imprecission is the 8 "set-points" when using a controller. This reduces the overall control the player has and leads to a poor gameplay experience

Overall I do not recommend the game, it is a misguided attempt to reproduce the success of DS1. While it did succeed at certain aspects like ambiance, overall it did not succeed

After successfully completing the main story and slaying the big Nashy D, I can confidently say that Dark Souls 2 does some good things and some interesting things. First of all, I really enjoyed the story above all else. Deviating from the story of linking the fire and focusing more on the throne of want and the idea that there is this big power grab in a fallen kingdom really struck a cord with me. I also really enjoyed the levels for the most part. Yes, there are way too many enemies in small rooms, but you can avoid these obstacles altogether through clever thinking and puzzle solving. The undead crypt stood out to me in particular with the wraiths that come out of the statues (iykyk). I will say some levels felt pretty uninspired, like Shrine of Amana is a bunch of bs. Why purposefully put enemies that fire homing spells in deep water that slows the player, but not the enemies? Just some mechanics seemed pretty unfair. On another note I really enjoyed the leveling system because it is very easy to level your stats in this game and I know people have a lot to say about adaptability, but it becomes a non issue with how easy it is to level up. My one final complaint is while the boss and enemy designs are cool, other than a few exceptions, they are mostly very boring and easy fights, trivialized by end game stats and sluggish movement. I would argue that the bosses in DS1 are far more interesting mechanically. I cannot speak for the DLC bosses, but out of the main game they are kind of lame. Overall, 7.5/10 game, would play again.
