Dare I say Peak Souls? I think I do.

There is no denying that this game is rough around the edges at times, with many dreams and ideas that were never realised, but honestly to call this title a bad game and avoid it because of online false rhetoric is moronic, because so are many other souls titles at times suffer from time and development woes, as shown in areas like Lost Izalith. Dark Souls 2 however, is just where it is most apparent at times.
This title tries many new things, some that became staple features to the franchise, and some that unfortunately were left behind, however many complaints towards new systems like Adaptability or the Darkness mechanic are the epitome of skill issue. In some areas, the game could have done better explaining or planting the seed of an idea into the players head, but eventually once a player understands the new system(s), they should easily adapt and become used to it, being provided with many avenues they can take. Those that refuse to understand and reject these system(s) are at their own fault for struggling at the game.
Level design in this game is gorgeous, and the enemy placement is both brutal but smart. The game is infamously called Gank Souls 2, but in reality, every Souls game is a Gank Souls if you just run past all enemies and let them accumulate. 2 here however, makes it so that in some cases, this is not the way to progress, some areas even mechanically punish you for running past enemies (but that doesn't mean you can't).
The weapon variety in this game is incredible, with such a diverse set of movesets, the introduction of power stancing, and immense build variety that it took until Elden Ring to come around and offer a similar variety. The only downside is that durability in this game is quite harsh and low, with many skills destroying weapons quickly through maybe 10 uses. However, this is then remedied by having visual indicators on screen, items that repair your weapons on field and items that slow degredation, and being able to free repair your items by resting at a bonfire. And frankly, maybe you shouldn't be spamming your weapon skills left right and centre, maybe you should be instead saving them for opportune times in a boss fight. And if you want to be spamming your weapon skill (which at many times are very strong), maybe you need to sacrifice something to uphold that playstyle.
The music in this game is incredible, with the beautiful juxtaposition of Majula to the rest of Drangleic, where you take refuge in a town that almost seems normal, sheltered away from the perversions of the Undead Curse. Each boss have their own distinct theme (bar from maybe one or two), but each theme compliments the identity of the boss, adding more to their character beyond what you see. Each boss theme tells a part of their story, whether it's to reflect their past, present, and maybe even future, or to reflect their stance in the world, what power they hold, or once held, how they have shaped the land, what relationships they had with other characters. The music, despite no vocals, speaks a lot about the characters they accompany. Tracks like "Burnt Ivory King" speaks more words and portrays such a picture that no video essay or post online can begin to emphasise to its full extent.
The story and world of Drangleic is also a beautiful cycle about not only a journey on beating the Undead Curse, but also finding meaning, and finding yourself. You come across so many characters, past and present, that all seeked means to beating the Undead Curse, through their own means and ideologies, with many, through the process, losing sight of who they were, what they originally strived for. It is poetic how King Vendrick, on a crusade to eliminate the Undead, the non-living, brought about by his own hubris, began to employ golems and animated suits of armour, another form of his hubris, another form of the non-living, the Undead, to try and extinguish the curse that he only contributed to.
Now not all characters walked the same cycle, many had their own battles and own struggles, but through one way or another, they were all dragged into the overarching story, many unknowningly by the same root cause, one close to home, but also one from a distant kingdom.

The game is not without criticism however. Mechanics such as Soul Memory, an interesting new way to tackle online session match making, in attempt to make things fairer, falls short, as it can punish weaker players who die a lot by pairing them with players who are much better and have progressed further into the game, as they may have both respectively accumulated similar amount of souls. The de-spawning of enemies may seem harsh when attempting to grind for rare items. It helps promote players use bonfire aesthetics, an incredible feature lost in modern titles, however not all players may want the added challenge of NG+ imposed onto them. The game is also quite buggy at times, from dogey weapon tracking with certain weapons when upclose to enemies, to the infamouse Falchions that seemingly have stayed broken through several iterations of the game for the love of the game.

This game has the right to stand on its own pedestal, a brilliant game in an amazing trilogy. This title was the most experimental, when it came to player mechanics and boss fights, both gimmick and combo orientated, and would have been even more experimental, had time and development allowed. It's easy to mourn over what the game could have been, what had been lost, but it is important to see the game for what it became at the end of it all. Potentially, yet another analogy for the story of the game itself.

Proud Best Souls 2 Defender.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is the most controversial entry in the series – but also one of the most fascinating. It may not reach the atmospheric highs of Dark Souls I or the polish of Dark Souls III, but it makes up for it with sheer variety, dense world design, and deep mechanical complexity.

This version improves on the original with upgraded visuals, rebalanced enemies, and all the DLCs included. Enemy placement has been reworked, making the game feel fresh even for returning players. Some changes are brutal, though – expect ambushes where you least expect them.

Combat remains methodical and punishing. The hitboxes are tighter than in the original release, but still not perfect. Builds feel diverse, with tons of viable weapons, spells, and playstyles. PvP is active and brutal, as always.

What Scholar of the First Sin lacks in cohesion and flow, it makes up for with sheer content. It’s a longer, more experimental Souls game – not always elegant, but always interesting. If you can embrace its quirks, you’ll find a dark and rewarding journey well worth taking.

Rating: 8/10

After getting 100% completion on all 3 Dark Souls games, I can easily say that this game feels horrible in many, MANY ways...
Character Power and Controls
Okay so one of the first things you will realise is that your character is simply "weak".
- Drinking Estus and dodge rolling feel miserable without putting some levels in the new stat, ADP.
- This game doesn't use 360° controls, instead it uses 8 directions for some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ reason. Which means, no precise movement at all.
-This is Dark Souls, so you are going to die a lot, and it's fine as long as you learn from your mistakes and the difficulty feels fair. But guess what? You lose max HP when you die until you use a humanity. So you just cannot go through the same area or fight the boss and try to get better result, it's just going to be worse with every death. Very cool mechanic indeed!
-Armour does almost nothing compared to Dark Souls 1, so Fashion-Souls goes brrr.
-Early game difficulty is INSANE. Until you kill the first boss and get some levels, you simply can't do much.
-Enemy placement sucks. You usually fight so many of them in very narrow corridors, so you end up getting stuck and die.
-Enemy amount sucks. It's basically "Oh, you managed to kill 2 of these enemies at once? Now go fight 5 of them at once!"
-You end up running past all the enemies since you don't want to deal with this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and it's just not fun at all... This also causes you to get less souls in general. (However, the amount of souls you get from bosses is so high that you usually don't stay under-powered.)
-If you decide to farm an area, surprise surpirse! The enemies don't respawn after you kill them for a certain amount. And if you try to use this to clean the run-back to the boss, it just takes WAY too much time. So the kill count required to stop them from respawning is in the perfect spot for even more pain in the ass!
-You need some items (Pharros' Lockstone and Fragrant Branch of Yore) to unlock new areas and get new items. But they are limited and you need to use them carefully so you don't get soft-locked and have to go through an alternative map, or use Bonfire Ascetics, which take the area to NG+ for even more difficulty! This way you can get more lockstones and branches at the cost of your ascetics AND sanity.
-Map is completely disconnected. You get the ability to warp between bonfires, and this is actually nice. But this caused the developers to not care about the world design.
-The content is bloated. There are almost double the amount of bosses and areas compared to Dark Souls 1 (24 bosses vs 41 bosses)
-Visuals are great though, nice job there!
-Almost every single ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boss is a "Slash, slash, thrust" fight, nothing interesting or creative here.
-And that's literally everything I can say about bosses.
-Soul memory based matchmaking instead of level based matchmaking. So if you died and lost a large amount of souls, PvP is ruined for you. Well done FromSoft!
-Weapon variety and dual wielding is cool, I will give them credit here.
-Infusion mechanic is also better than different titanite types in my opinion.
-There are lots of upgrade materials, including slabs. So you can actually upgrade your gear without much doubt.
-To compensate this much of upgrade materials, they also made the armours upgradable. They are still useless though...
-Torch mechanic is kinda unnecessary, you can complete the entire game without ever using it for light. However, they tried something different and it doesn't affect the gameplay, so it's fine I guess.
-Equip load works a little different. So if you equip a stamina recovery shield, you will recover stamina even slower. Also physical block is GOD AWFUL on both stamina shields.

Overall, worst Souls game in my opinion. It just feels like a spin-off game with many failed aspects. Unless you want to torture yourself, stay away from this game, Dark Souls 1 and 3 are so much better and they are the true Souls games.

Also I cannot praise the Scholar of the first sins NPC invaders enough it is crazy how much personality they got into a NPC invader just by the cheesy ass builds they were using, the emotes they did, if they were aggressive or ran away to go find enemies and traps, it was amazing. I found myself having to look up the names to make sure they even were NPCs and not a cosplaying player. I tried not using too many NPC summon allies but the ones I did were so cool with how they would emote to show you secrets and suggest ways to take on encounters.....or betray you and actually kill you. Lastly the community for this game is so great. The game came out 10 years ago but I was still having such a good time with the social systems. Eventually i'll do a new game plus playthrough and see more of the actual scholar of the first sins changes but for now i'm happy to close the book on this one because I know that I will go back to it eventually since some of the most interesting mechanics didn't continue in the series and I still have more I want to try with them

Posted: October 1
Dark Souls 2 is truly an extraordinary experience, filled with rage, wonder, and irritation. Like many others, my first introduction to the Souls series was Elden Ring, an absolute masterclass of engineering and game design. I fell in love with its characters and story—so much so that I dove into the highly acclaimed Dark Souls next. I absolutely loved it. The story captivated me, and the gameplay felt fresh despite being years older. After defeating Manus, I decided to pursue the lands of Drangleic and conquer the so-called black sheep of the trilogy: Dark Souls 2. What I found was no small challenge—it was excruciatingly frustrating. Areas like the dreaded Iron Keep runback or the Shrine of Amana wizards truly made me rage. Yet, on the flip side, I discovered an amazing world filled with incredible characters. I loved seeing how each region reacted to the undead curse, each affected and diminished by their respective rulers. The world of Dark Souls 2 is messy, almost dreamlike, but it’s a dream I couldn’t get enough of. The build variety, replay value, and the New Game+ system—where each cycle adds new challenges and obstacles (up to a point)—kept me hooked.
Hot take: I believe Dark Souls 2 has the best level and enemy placement design. It forces you to meticulously plan your route to a boss while juggling item quantities. Should I use an Effigy? Is this a good run? I haven’t taken damage yet—should I infuse my weapon with lightning? These choices truly make you “git gud” in my opinion. While Dark Souls 1 has unmatched interconnectivity, I know every area in Dark Souls 2 like the back of my hand because I’ve been slaughtered there so many times. This makes you appreciate the world even more, as you’re forced to find every possible advantage.
I’d also argue that Dark Souls 2 has the best lore in the series. Seeing King Vendrick’s failures and the hidden plot to usurp the throne added such depth to the narrative.
Are some of the bosses bad? Yes.
Are some of the areas completely unfair? Absolutely!
Does the adaptability stat suck? YES!
But these flaws will never deter me from loving this incredible video game. It proudly sits at the top alongside Elden Ring and the other Souls titles

You start as a nobody, less than a nobody. You try desperately to push forward, but even the weakest skeleton, the smallest rat is enough to kill you. But you keep going, holding on to your last bit of humanity. Then you realise that you’re not alone. You meet many others on your path- some a shell of their former selves; others, like you, cling desperately to a faint hope. As everyone inevitably falls around you, you keep pushing on. You feel the pain after each death, the trembling hands after each victory. Through practice and persistence, you defeat all that stand in your way. Eventually, you begin to face godlike creatures of incredible proportions, and though they initially obliterate you, you beat them too. That's when you finally understand. The game doesn’t want to hurt you with its obstacles. It wants to give you the satisfaction of overcoming them. The game is not about killing all the bosses and reaching the ending, it’s about the journey you take to get there. Don’t let its difficulty discourage you. Keep pushing forward. Because how can the destination be so sweet without the hardships that precede it?

The best the Dark Souls series has to offer

This game is a work of art. Well-crafted, well-tought-out and severely underrated. Don't listen to all meme ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, just give it a try and you will discover a whole new world for yourself

Weapons here feel like unique items, not just another reskin of something you already have (I hitting you in the head my special boy Dark Souls: Prepare To Die™)

The bosses are another highlight, that tie strongly to previous take. Ingenuity in there on another level. Quantity over quality in some instances doesn't mean a worse experience. Almost every encounter in Dark Souls II is a joyride that requires a different approach to tackle. Just use your brain and you'll be fine, though

There is so much more to this game that simply must be experienced and not just absorbed through other sources like Steam reviews or petty YouTubers. So if you feel bold and have some spare money — most definitely give it a try

As a denizen with no credibility or following, this game is ROUGH. I've had to start 3 different playthroughs just to finish the game. In fact, I played dark souls 3 and remastered before i've played this game. While I now kind of regret overlooking ds2, I can absolutely understand why this game has been written off by the general census.

It's easy to discredit the game, the combat of the game damn near encourages dissent from haters and glazers alike. Even with gameplay mods the hit detection cannot be redeemed. The true sin here is not Gwyn creating his age of fire, it's the enemy placement, aggression, and number.

Aggression itself is not the issue per-se, it's how long enemies will chase after you. It's like Fortnite reload, no matter how long you run or hide, once that fog gate falls, enemies will ALWAYS be there. This game is so unfair with its hit detection it's almost anti-consumer.

Bosses like the Burnt Ivory king have annoying slash attacks that, even if perfectly dodged, will still hit you even if you are physically BEHIND the boss. While the combat is entirely deserving of the backlash, the world building and lore is great, it's almost criminal to lump it in with the garbage hollowing health penalty and ♥♥♥♥♥♥ hit detection.

Dark Souls 2 still has redeeming qualities, which I'm not really all that qualified to point out and analyze in a steam review anyways. I can't say it's the best starting point, as it's best suited to people like me who only played 1 and 3, looking for more Dark Souls. Be warned, strength builds can blaze through areas and bosses and can kinda skewer the experience as a whole.

Ds2 is a slow burn that border on a slog sometimes, but enhances the experience of the trilogy as a whole. I personally don't understand the extremity of the fanbase though, it's always "love it or hate it" in regards to ds2. I think its a good Dark Souls game with unfair hit detection. Not the best game in the trilogy, but a weak innovation with the right sentiment in mind.

A generally slower-paced and more leveling-intensive Dark Souls game.

Much of what you've heard is true; It's clunkier and way less fair than any other Souls game.

Key elements of frustration include
-Consistently mediocre boss design,
-Generally bad level design,
-The properties of your "reliable" Iframe dodge being effected by every level of Adaptability and every 5% of your Equip Load. Before you even consider touching any other stats, leveling ADP to 39 is kind of a must.

Despite these failings the game redeems itself through its unique mechanics (Bonfire Ascetics, Covenant of Champions, etc.), Stand-out DLC areas, and a visual style that stands out as extra vibrant and breath-taking in its own way.

So, to be short, this game is the unusual one of the Dark Souls trilogy. I will say there is no glaring problems with it so that if you just want an fantasy-adventure game, you will probably get some enjoyment out of this game. The real question is, would you get this one or the other Dark Souls games? This review will cover the key points that set this one apart from Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 3.
Ironically, this game has more in common with the newer Elden Ring than its cohorts. It has a vast set of levels with wildly different themes and settings, from a lava castle to a spooky foggy ruins to a spider-infested village. This is only expounded further with the inclusion of all 4 DLC to this version of Dark Souls 2. You definitely won't get bored, and it will definitely keep you interested for longer than the much more linear Dark Souls 3. Unlike Dark Souls 1, the overall theme of this game is much more focused on the individuals of this world rather than the world itself. The game is more focused on the deterioration and tragedy of the bosses and minions you fight, as well as your NPC allies. If DS1 felt like you were stumbling onto a painting of a time long past, then DS2 feels more akin to you flipping to the last pages of someone else's story. I won't go further than that due to spoilers; but overall, I felt the game's theme of determination against an ever present curse was more endearing than DS1 and DS3.
But, by far, the greatest distinction between DS2 and the others, is its core mechanics. The game encourages a wide variety of viable builds for your character, more so than DS1 and DS3; however, its movement is perhaps its most jarring quality. It has the slowest combat speed of the games; each swing/action you take can leave you vulnerable for a significant period of time. Stamina management is key, and dodging is much more unforgiving than the others. It's a slower paced game overall . I personally prefer DS3's fast paced combat, but ultimately, I found some gratification with DS2's more strategic pacing.
So, this game is for you if you like the Dark Souls challenge with a more expansive world layout, build variety, slower combat, and personal themes.
Multiplayer I won't comment on just due to a lack of experience, outside of killing the once-in-a-blue moon invader; but it should be said, that this game's community is very limited. So, I wouldn't buy this game if you're just interested in the multiplayer.

Terrible design. Why can't I just try the boss again after I die? Stop deducting my HP. What a stupid way to discourage trying again.

Update after completion
"All that glitters is not gold" can be used to perfectly describe this game. Apart from some of the beautiful maps, DS2 is boring and annoying.
The developers were trying to make a good game. You can tell it from the number of new consumables, armors, and weapons. However, the developers were just incompetent to make a good soul game.
Almost every good design that DS1 had, this game changed them in unpleasant directions to make players have a terrible experience, which makes me wonder if they actually played test the game.

1. Death penalty
As mentioned before, deducting HP after dying is such a terrible design to discourage players from trying again. In addition, the design is fundamentally against another core of a soul game: exploration. Usually, I am not afraid of dying because I know I have a chance to pick my souls back. Therefore, I can try to explore every corner of the map. For example, if I see some loot across the cliff, usually I would try to see if I can jump through it. However, with this permanent HP reduction penalty, I did much less during the playthrough, since the reward may not worthy, and I need my HP for the Boss. Not to mention effigy is not easy to get in the early stage.

2. Boring Boss and numbers of enemies in the map
More than half of bosses are just boring. The only way the developers can think of to increase the difficulty is having multiple enemies in the Boss arena for a gank fight.
Even when they finally have some good designs in the DLC, the runback just ruins everything. This game has the most ridiculous enemy setups in the map. Every corner you turn, every room you enter, you can expect more than one enemy are there. If you run pass them, enemies will chase you from one end of the map to the other like you just murdered their whole family in front of them one minute ago.

3. Invasion
In this game, you can be invaded while being hollow. Who think this is a good idea, so old players can keep harassing new players? The only way to stop this bullsh*t is to burn effigy in bonfire.
The important item to restore HP has to be used to keep myself some peace. I can not even go offline manually in the starting page, the stupid game automatically logs in as along as the Internet is connected. Basically, this forces me to play offline most of the time. Besides, the game intentionally adds NPC invasions in the areas where you are facing multiple enemies to make the gank fight even worse. The only good thing is when I am playing offline, the achievement did not pop. So I can get rid of this terrible once finishing new game and do not have to worry about trying 100% achievement.

4. Other ♥♥♥♥♥♥ designs
Who think put hundreds of poison statues in Black gulch is a good idea? These statues can even respawn after you die. If you try to break them, your weapon will break in the middle. Just a design to waste your time for no reason. Basically, the whole game is full of these nonsense designs and numerous enemies chasing you across the map
Fragrant Branch of Yore and Pharros' Lockstone are pathetic to show how lazy developers are. What can be unlocked can be a whole new map or some trash. There are even fake spots just to waste your keys.

In conclusion, the developers are incompetent and fundamentally do not understand how to make an appealing game. The only thought that developers had in mind while designing the game was to annoy the players. They had DS1 as a reference and made out this trash. This rubbish reflects what a masterpiece DS1 is and how great Miyazaki is.

Dark Souls II: The Unholy Stepchild of the Souls Series

I embarked on this journey with high hopes, but Dark Souls II swiftly shattered them. After over 6 hours, a level 50 character, and a handful of bosses defeated, I can confidently say this game is a travesty.

Combat: The combat feels like wading through molasses. Stiff animations, delayed responses, and hitboxes that seem to exist in an alternate dimension. It's as if the developers took everything that made the combat in the first game fluid and threw it out the window.

Graphics: Released in the same era as Bloodborne, yet it looks like a relic from the previous generation. Textures are muddy, lighting is flat, and the overall aesthetic is a far cry from the atmospheric beauty of its predecessors.

Balance: The game seems to have been designed by someone who hates players. Enemies swarm you in numbers, and bosses are either laughably easy or infuriatingly difficult. There's no middle ground.

Miyazaki's Absence: It's painfully obvious that Hidetaka Miyazaki had little to no involvement in this project. The soul of the series is missing, replaced by a soulless imitation that fails to capture the magic of the original.

In conclusion, Dark Souls II is a misguided attempt to cash in on a beloved franchise. If you're a fan of the series, do yourself a favor and skip this one. It's a stain on the Souls legacy.

Played DS1 and then this back to back. Overall, I would say this was an excellent sequel to the first. It feels and plays very different from the first in (mostly) good ways. There is certainly some jank with sliding enemies on ice skates and some randomly placed difficulty spikes (or maybe my build was just bad) that I feel like was not present in the first game, but I think this is a bigger game so I guess it makes sense.

The storyline is more presentable here. I was pretty confused what was going on in the first game and in this one I at least had a basic understanding. I'm not one who reads every item description to piece together the lore, so this game tried to be more upfront with the story.

Enemy and boss design was incredible. Very atmospheric. I got raided a bunch in this game, and the people who invade you have got some serious time on their hands to ragebait randoms on the internet lol. Luckily for me they weren't ready for my buckler parry build which saved me quite a few times in pvp.

My biggest gripe is the exact same issue I had with the first game. The last 20% is a total grind. Once again the game expects me to remember where several randomly placed doors and statues are in the course of my 30 hour playthrough and to go back and unlock them to beat the game. There is no indication when you come across these doors that they will be important later on. It is really lame to have to pick up the wiki in your last 3 hours of playing to know how to beat the game. I want to feel at the end of a game that I know exactly what I'm doing and it's building up to this final moment of tension. But because I'm chasing down Door #3 and Statue #4 it was a moment of relief that it is over when I did finally beat it which is not how I want to feel when finishing something as epic as this. I gave the first game the benefit of the doubt in this regard since it was their first title, but to see this repeated here again for DS2 brings this game from a 9/10 to a 7-8/10 for the ending alone. Posted: September 10
This game has SEVERE balance problems.

They crammed like 500 enemies into every little space and called it a day. The enemies have the ability to perfectly rotate to your character when attacking so there's no outplay only out scale. They added some attacks that are instant to enemies, and added toxic to enemies that drains your life in as little as five seconds.

What this results in is little bits of damage added up to the point where your character can no longer sustain itself. That's why I take healing wares as a starting gift. Otherwise I have no chance to keep going. The frustrating part is when you have killed all 500 of the enemies in the room and you died just at the end. Now you have to kill them again. The option to just run to your souls does not work because they have a bunch of oneshot chase combo attacks, groups that will surround your character, bleed or poison that will kill empty your characters full health bar.

You have to really go out of your way to optimize the controls for this game if you use mouse and keyboard. The reason being is there is a massive amount of input delay unless you map buttons to the keyboard keys instead of mouse buttons. This results in switching to a 3rd party service to map the mouse keys to like x mouse button.
Xmousebutton
[You have to map simulated key (during) to that mouse button or else it will flake out. It has How to simulate key strokes and to the right of it a drop down menu. Change it to 3 During (press on down, release on up) add the game profile and every time you boot up the game, the controls will work]

Despite all this, there is an atmosphere to this game that may make it worth to some who like art. There are also numerous paths to go instead of being funneled into one path. This does not mean you will enjoy going those ways because this game only likes to punish exploration. It has set optimal paths. It has set merchants. Figure out what you want. Get the stuff you need. Go the safer route until you get stronger. Otherwise there is a good chance you will be shredded by various things.

Posted: September 9
Maybe not a good sequel, but a great game.

Why

- You want a different but familiar take on Dark Souls
- You enjoy being thrown into ridiculous situations and fighting your way through
- You like power-stancing to the same unhealthy degree as my coworker



- The only reason you liked Dark Souls was the interconnectedness of the world



This one didn't pull me in to the same degree as the original; I took two long breaks to play other games (Ender Lillies/Magnolia, then Persona 5) and I think that's the biggest struggle for DS2. Living in the shadow of DS1's amazing world design and DS3's polish/fan service isn't easy, but if you can enjoy it on its own merits you'll discover a great game in the darkness.

To address one of the most common complaints about DS2, yes, the lack of interconnectivity is jarring coming from DS1. The Iron Keep is exactly as egregious as the memes claim (I knew about it and still couldn't believe where I was after the elevator), Majula is a true hub area unlike Firelink and you won't find any cool little shortcuts between areas here, just mostly linear paths radiating out from the Far Fire. Another letdown after DS1 is the characters, none even touched the charisma of Solaire or Siegmeyer and every non-Shanalotte character is pretty forgettable in the end. I think it was a missed opportunity to not have someone like Lautrec mess with Majula; it would've had more impact in this game since Majula feels more like home.

In terms of gameplay you more or less know what you're getting if you played the original, with the well-known caveats about reduced early iframes and hoolowing reducing your max HP. Honestly I wasn't bothered by either all that much, the first two souls games have slow enough enemies that you can often dodge with just good positioning, and it won't take long to get enough points in agility to not feel the difference anyway. The max-health reduction can be annoying at times, but as long as you're not popping a Human Effigy every time you die you'll have plenty to get you through and they can be farmed pretty reliably from midgame onward.

The level design holds up within areas despite them not being connected, with enough challenging encounters, creative shortcuts and 'You Died' moments to satsify any souls veteran. Drangleic castle stands out as making me absolutely terrified the whole way through, and Black Gulch was everything I'd been told Blightown was despite being a single 200m long ledge. They kept the variety of biomes from DS1 despite noticeably increasing the number of them and offer some truly spectacular vistas despite the ancient graphics. I was shocked to discover that despite the increased area count and much longer/more numerous DLCs my total playtime only clocked in at 8 hours more for DS2 than 1 thought.

Speaking of the DLCs they're good fun, each introduces a new 'thing' you need to do to progress and makes some creative choices with the smaller scope. The NPC invaders were definitely a highlight, much more personality than the base game. I'll leave some bullet point impressions on each below.



- Nice level design, more DS1-ish in its interconnectedness and being able to go everywhere you see
- Some interesting 'puzzles' to spice things up & trick enemies like the ghosty bois
- Absolutely egregious gank fight on an optional path that's not obviously optional
- Bonfire after the dinosaurs was an excellent fakeout, I was right there and turned around rather than fight them



- The intial arrival in the throne room and getting the soul really faked me out
- Maldron is great, him hiding to ambush near the chest then running like a lil ♥♥♥♥♥ when he gets hit was hilarious
- I also like that they signpost using the cogs as a platform to hit him without retaliation, and that he despawns after doing this for a while
- Fume knight is an excellent fight, Alonne as well though his runback in kinda annoying
- You can just run past the adds once you know the route though
- Interestingly, if you make it to the fog gate all the knights other than the archers fall back, so you can heal/become human



- Another fun invader, I thought he was the chest for sure
- Went completely the wrong way for a long time at the start, so long that I didn't remember about the large, obvious church until a few bonfires in
- Love that you can just drop into the final fight right at the start, very funny and good for challenge runs
- Do not love how useless the 4 knights are, making you go through a giant melee every time before you get to the Ivory King
- his fight is great, just so annoying to get there
- don't summon, won't help that much against him and he gets way too tanky
- better to take your lumps in the melee then actually do damage to him



A quality entry in the series and a great game, even if I wasn't drawn in as strongly as I was by the original. Well worth your money if you're a fan of the series, genre or just quality games (though I wish the series went on sale more often). You're missing out if you skip it to only play 1 & 3 as some suggest.

Posted: September 8
DS2 is known as the unloved middle child of the Dark Souls franchise. Much of the criticism against it is warranted; it is punishingly ganky, has many lackluster bosses, a few annoying mechanics and is possibly the most frustrating title in the franchise in terms of overall level design.

But I don't think the hatred directed towards it is justified. It's still my favorite Dark Souls of them all, and is a worthy successor to the first DS. It is a beautiful game, the level progression is very enjoyable with lots of cool areas to explore, and in spite of the lack of great bosses in the main game, the DLCs amend that by delivering some of the best boss fights in the trilogy (as well as introducing great levels and lore overall). People get frustrated with this game and compare it unfavorably with its predecessor, but like to forget DS1 had problems of its own, particularly the poorly baked latter half of the game. DS2 doesn't have that issue, and the amount and quality of content in it does clear DS1 by a mile in my opinion.

I'm also quite fond of DS2's lore and the immaculate, impeccable, godlike drip in this game which is easily the best in the franchise. I'm not sure I'd recommend it if you've never played a Dark Souls title before (in that case the prescription is always to go for DS1 first), but if you did, I say don't let the detractors discourage you from trying out DS2. I think you'll enjoy it in spite of its flaws.

Only after completing the game did I check to see if there was any heavy armor, and I discovered that heavy armor can only be obtained by dancing with tambourines. You can't farm armor sets in this game like you can in DS1 and DS3.

At first, I didn't really enjoy DS2, but the more I played and kept an open mind, the more I was rewarded.

DS2 is not perfect by any means, but if you can look past the quirks and treat it as a standalone game in the Souls series, there is a good experience waiting to be had.

The juice to this game is in the areas - they are like puzzles that require patience, persistence, clean execution, and a willingness to adapt to what the game is asking of you.

There are some solid boss fights and the DLCs also offer good content, but as a whole this is a different style of Souls and that's okay.

The camera/player movement differs from DS1/DS3. It feels "floaty" at first which can be jarring, but becomes a non-issue with a few hours of playing. There are a lot of experimental features and some are questionable, but just remember to keep an open mind.

There were only 2 optional areas that I didn't really enjoy - there was no real reward and it was just difficult for the sake of being difficult. It didn't feel like there was much thought behind them and the care for the player wasn't really there.

For the most part, this game delivers a good Souls experience with a solid standalone story (there isn't really any lore building upon games 1 and 3). I personally recommend you finish with this game in the series - you can appreciate it a little more when you have some Souls knowledge behind you.

If you go in expecting anything like DS1/DS3, you might be a little disheartened and end up putting this aside. If you give this game time to grow on you and are willing to adapt, you are honestly in for a good time.

In the end, I can say that , and I did enjoy my time playing. Don't miss out on a solid Souls experience just because this game is different.

Posted: September 5
I'll make this review brief given the amount of overwhelming negative sentiment surrounding this game. Remember when I said Dark Souls 1 was like taking care of your slow, arthritic grandpa? Dark Souls 2 is like taking care of your slow, arthritic, senile grandpa with dementia who berates and beats you with his cane as you try to get him to take his pills.

The same negative critiques for DS1 also exist here but were purposely amplified by the devs to punish you. Healing is as slow as molasses. Not only do you have to spend time sipping that syrup, you can't move while drinking and it replenishes your health over time. No more spam drinking to instantly save yourself from an undodgeable attack. The maps are now seemingly overpopulated with so many more enemies ready to ambush and apply debuffs who will also follow you to the ends of the earth. Boss runbacks are even more atrocious. As usual, the bonfire is placed a mile away with a whole army of cockblockers and snipers ready to whip your ass or pound your balls as you run the gauntlet. Arrows and magic track you midair, often curving from their trajectory like in the movie Wanted if you just so happen to slightly move to the side. Sometimes there will be doors in the way that take a whole 3 seconds to open (they close every time you attempt a rerun), same with entering the boss fog wall. Your dumb ass character takes his sweet time to carefully enter through the door as if not knowing what's on the other side (you've done this 10 times already), unaware of the hundred mouth breathers swarming your back. You are vulnerable during this interaction and you will die if you're hit out of the animation. The ABSOLUTE worst runback is Lud and Zallen where you have to wander through a snow storm while being harassed by Santa's roided up reindeer. Every time you die, you are afflicted with a temporary reduction in your health (similar to curse in DS1 but worse) that can drop to at most 50% of your health bar. You have to use human effigies, a fairly rare resource, to regain your humanity and your full health bar. If you run out, best you can do is equip the Ring of Binding and hide your hideous decaying corpse underneath your armor until you find/buy a human effigy. Most of the bosses are still slow and boring with some appearing as regular enemies on the map later on like the pursuer and ruin sentinels. Special bosses usually have some sort of gimmick that will often get you killed on the first attempt if you're unprepared or unaware of certain special mechanics such as: closing a gate, waiting for a room to fill with water, burning a windmill, destroying ashen idols, having 5 giant souls, etc. Some bosses are difficult because they either swarm you with trash enemies, have a massive health bar or perform attacks faster than your ability to replenish stamina or health (Fume, Alonne, Ivory King). Lastly, the devs added a new attribute called Adaptability (ADP) which increases your invincibility frames when you dodge. You start with a whopping 5 iframes and you'd have to dump dozens of levels into ADP to increase it. Some people go as high as 90-100 levels in ADP to have a comfortable roll. If you'd rather spend your souls elsewhere, be prepared to learn each boss's attack patterns.

There are only a few positives that I can recall. The first is the beautiful scenery and tranquility of Majula. I like the idea of bringing in people from around the map who would populate the town and act as a central hub for half of the game's vendors/blacksmiths. The best part is, everyone in the town survives all the way up to the end of the game unlike other souls game. The next positive, which saved my sanity countless times, was ability to purchase an unlimited amount of life gems from the old hag vendor when she moves to Majula. This is great considering that you start with 1 estus flask and you have to wander around looking for upgrade materials. Wouldn't be surprised if anyone playing this game developed a life gem addiction. There is a consummable called a bonfire ascetic that increases the map difficulty to NG+ and resets the bosses. This is great if you want to farm bosses or if you want to complete a questline but forgot to summon a certain npc. Lastly, there were so many opportunities to summon npcs like Lucatiel to help you in a fight. Although it causes the boss to massively increase its health, I indulge in the ecstasy of being able to attack its butt while my companion takes the abuse.

The slow and punishing nature of this game makes it seem like the devs want you to take your time to explore, enjoy the scenery and take out enemies one at a time. After about 10 hours into the game I developed the mindset that the moment you leave the bonfire, the entire map is an extension of the boss arena. As an average player, every soldier, trap and obstacle is just part of the procedure in order to kill the boss of that area, unless of course you know how to speedrun it. I'd rate this game a decent 7/10, just slightly lower than DS1. The rating would've plunged into the abyss with Manus had I not lobotomized my amygdala a few hours into the run. In this economy, I'm gonna move to Majula and chill out with Shanalotte and Rosabeth by the seashore.

Peak oil is the point when global oil production reaches its maximum rate, after which it will begin to decline irreversibly. The main concern is that global transportation relies heavily on gasoline and diesel. Adoption of electric vehicles, biofuels, or more efficient transport (like trains and waterways) could help reduce oil demand.

Peak oil relates closely to oil depletion; while petroleum reserves are finite, the key issue is the economic viability of extraction at current prices. Initially, it was believed that oil production would decline due to reserve depletion, but a new theory suggests that reduced oil demand could lower prices, affecting extraction costs. Demand may also decline due to persistent high prices.

Over the last century, many predictions of peak oil timing have been made, often later proven incorrect due to increased extraction rates. M. King Hubbert introduced comprehensive modeling of peak oil in a 1956 paper, predicting U.S. production would peak between 1965 and 1971, but his global peak oil predictions were premature because of improved drilling technology. Current forecasts for the year of peak oil range from 2028 to 2050. These estimates depend on future economic trends, technological advances, and efforts to mitigate climate change.

Oil, or petroleum, is a mixture of hydrocarbon substances. By its very nature, what "oil" is may vary. The geology of a region affects the type of oil underground. The types of hydrocarbons produced from an oilfield may also vary depending on the geology.

Crude oil generally comes in various 'grades,' commonly classified as "light," "medium," 'heavy," and "extra heavy." The exact definitions of these grades vary depending on the region from which the oil came. Grades of oil are also assessed by API gravity. Light oil flows naturally to the surface or can be extracted by simply pumping it from the ground. Heavy refers to oil that has higher density and lower API gravity. It does not flow as easily, and its consistency can be similar to that of molasses. While some of it can be produced using conventional techniques, recovery rates are better using unconventional methods.

Generally, especially with regard to peak oil, the primary concern regards what is called "crude oil" production (which may also be referred to as "crude and condensate" production in US EIA statistics), which is what is actually refined into the common fuels most people know such as gasoline and diesel fuel, in addition to other common fuels. Other oil production statistics may be named "total liquids production" or "petroleum and other liquids" in EIA statistics. This includes crude oil production in addition to other hydrocarbon liquids, such as natural-gas liquids (NGLs). These two production numbers are distinct and shouldn't be considered the same thing. Using "total liquids" production to refer to "crude oil" production is misleading. The extra liquids included in "total liquids" production do not refine into the same products. It can be misleading as it could be used to inflate the actual amount of crude oil being produced globally.

Where oil may come from is commonly divided into two categories, "conventional" oil sources and "unconventional" oil sources. The terms are not strictly defined and may vary within literature. As a result of the wide range of potential definitions, different oil production forecasts may vary based on which classes of liquids they choose to include or exclude. Some standard definitions for "conventional" and "unconventional" oil are detailed below.

Conventional oil is oil that is extracted using "traditional" techniques (i.e., in common use before 2000) techniques. Conventional oil commonly refers to onshore oilfields and shallow offshore oilfields that are "easy" to extract.

It has been recognized that conventional oil production has peaked around 2005–2006. What has prevented peak oil from then on is US tight oil production, which rapidly increased since the 2008 financial crisis. Additionally, but to a lesser extent, Canadian oil-sands production has helped increase oil supply since 2008.

In the same way, sources of natural gas production are usually divided into "conventional" and "unconventional".

Unlike conventional oil, unconventional oil refers to oil that is "difficult" to extract. The number of steps required translates into extremely high production costs. Common unconventional oil sources include:




Other less common unconventional oil sources include oil shale (see article).

Production of tight oil is mainly concentrated in the United States due to world-class geology and ease of borrowing (tight oil production is extremely expensive). Oil sands production is also concentrated in Canada for the same exact reasons (but different type of oil). There are also economic tight oil deposits in Argentina known as the Vaca Muerta Formation, but are less developed than tight oil in the US due to a lack of infrastructure and less capacity to borrow money.

In recent history, production of tight oil led to a resurgence of US production in the 2010s. US tight oil production initially peaked in March 2015 and fell by 12 per cent over the next 18 months; but then production rose again, and by September 2017 production had exceeded the old peak. As of 2024, US oil production, especially tight oil production, is higher than ever thanks to the Permian Basin.

Venezuela has oil sands deposits similar in size to those of Canada, and approximately equal to the world's reserves of conventional oil. Venezuela's Orinoco Belt tar sands are less viscous than Canada's Athabasca oil sands – meaning they can be produced by more conventional means – but they are buried too deep to be extracted by surface mining. Estimates of the recoverable reserves of the Orinoco Belt range from 100 billion barrels (16×109 m3) to 270 billion barrels (43×109 m3). In 2009, USGS updated this value to 513 billion barrels (8.16×1010 m3).

While not an actual source of unconventional oil, processes which convert other hydrocarbons are similar to unconventional oil in that they are 'unconventional' and very costly to produce. They include coal liquefaction or gas to liquids which produce synthetic fuels from coal or natural gas via the Fischer–Tropsch process, Bergius process, or Karrick process.

I've played ALL other souls games, and this is by far the worst one. And no, it's not because "it plays differently, just don't play it like the others" - it's absolutely horrid. It doesn't understand what difficulty means, it's not fun, and it doesn't even understand itself. Nothing is coherent.

Every single corner in this game has a very cheap "SEE?? ISN'T IT HARD BRO???" trope. It's not difficult at all, it's just tedious, exhausting, and simply not fun. The enemies have infinite aggro, there are a ton of ganks (ds2 fans justify this as luring them one by one with a bow. No thanks, non of the other souls game required me to do that. I'm not playing a turn based game, I think you might have forgotten).

The areas are completely disoriented, truly dull and boring, I couldn't get into the atmosphere at all. While Majula is a nice looking hub, it feels detached and unrelated to the game. The firekeeper is forgettable and seemingly has no dialogue variants, and the NPCs are not interesting and non interactive (for instance: blacksmith and his daughter). Their lore and quests are completely boring and feel like an afterthought.

In (edit: 35) hours of gameplay I could count on one hand the amount of times I felt like I encountered a fun challenge.

Some people say "it's the worst souls game, but it's still a good game." - I disagree. I genuinely don't think it's a good game; If this didn't have the dark souls naming brand, it would've been some random, disbanded and soulless game that doesn't understand itself, nor what difficulty or fun means. Genuinely disappointed, this is NOT fromsoftware, and you can tell.

Funnily enough, the worst part about this game is its fanbase.


I'll edit this as I finish some milestones, so far I'm before the last boss.

Now I've also beat the Sunken King DLC. The DLC was even worse than the base game. Throwing enemies at you everywhere, you have 0 time to do the so called puzzle they think they've created. Every single boss of course had goons, or the most horrible dragon fight I've ever seen in a fromsoft game. I seriously don't understand what the devs were thinking.

Another edit:
I've finished the old iron king dlc, didn't really care for it. The last boss was an alright fight for ds2 standards, I'll give them that. But nothing too special. No idea how people say ds2 has the best dlcs so far ngl, I didn't like them. Let's see if the third dlc can change my mind.


And the last edit:
I've now beaten the game, including the ivory king DLC. I actually somewhat liked this DLC, it felt meticulously crafted (minus the, you've guessed it, a gank fight final boss. Yes, even with freeing the knights, it's still a gank boss. Pity).

Anyways the final boss of the main game was an absolute disgrace and matches the laziness this game has presented throughout the gameplay. However, what they did with King Vendrick was very cool, I must admit.


So final words about this game from me: all in all, I would not recommend this game. I feel like it presents a very cheap and artificial sense of difficulty. There were very few moments I actually appreciated something in the game. I don't think it's a particularly good game, let alone a good souls game. But with that I have finished this game, and my souls game journey complete. Thanks for reading my thoughts lol

I shouldn't have listened to the ignorant people for so long. This game does't deserve the amount of hate it's gotten over the years. It does deserve some, however. Jank hitboxes, gank squads, and a slow clunky feeling are all issues this game faces... but the world design and sense of wonder I got from it made it all worth it. This game's world puts DS1 and DS3 to shame, and is completely reminiscent of Elden Ring. There's a vibrant range of colors in the environments, and areas that vary from grossly incandescent to abysmal and grimy. And though most of them were pretty easy, the bosses are generally amazing. Of course, there are some huge misses in that department... but each FromSoft game has some of those. The music has the quality of DS3 but still has some of the charm that DS1's soundtrack had, and the lore is just as intriguing as any FromSoft game. Then the DLCs take everything to the next level... including the jank and gank, but it's so worth it to explore the environments and overcome the challenges in your way. I've seen a few people call Elden Ring, "Dark Souls 2 2" and I'm inclined to agree because I definitely noticed some parallels between the two. Although, I think calling DS2, "Elden Ring 0.5" might be more accurate in a sense. If you are a true FromSoft fan, I guarantee you will find enjoyment in this game. Just make sure to level ADP!

*I had the Dark Souls 2 Lighting Engine installed during my playthrough. I highly recommend it as it's extremely easy to install, and massively improves the game's look while keeping the gameplay the same*

I beat DS1 right around the time DS3 had launched. I skipped DS2 Scholar and beat Dark Souls 3. Still craving more Dark Souls, I got Scholar of the First Sin. I was confused why the community was split on it. How you like it will be based on what you value.

For me, I loved the fashion souls. This game has so many more unique armors and weapons to play around with.
The level designs, especially of the dlc, are extraordinary.
unlike the previous Dark Souls, the side characters are very down to earth and relatable which makes their story lines a lot more memorable and emotional. The main story isn't any worse than the other two games.
You can use these things at bonfires that turn areas into their NG+ in case you want to fight a boss again or re-farm areas (if you kill an enemy a certain amount of times they'll disappear forever).

A few things I didn't like:
Characters have two extra stats and one of them is for I-frames in a roll. Inherently, this means level inflation so the meta level to beat DS2 is at lvl 125.
Somethings you kind of have to summon because they stack three large bosses against you in several fights. Weapon durability in this game is a piece of crap.
Some bosses even have debuffs that increase how fast your weapon durability evaporates. The stamina bar is unforgiving, if you reach zero, the stamina takes twice as long to restore.

I recommend this game not as a commendation of its quality, but as a fascinating example if you are interested in exploring game design. This game is full of so many strange quirks and decisions. I would say it is undoubtedly the worst Dark Souls game, but it's incredibly difficult to explain "the why of it" in a short amount of time. It's a game that feels like a "death by a thousand paper cuts". There are so many little things that irk the player over the course of a playthrough, and in the end lead to an experience that is conflicting at best.

Questionable decisions regarding player stats, healing, checkpoints, enemy placements, enemy designs, and so on. Weird jank and bugs regarding audio mixing, animations, and even something as simple and foundational as player movement. It's difficult, as all Souls games are, but this one feels like it aims to be "mean" more than any other title. All of it tickles the brain in the "how did this happen?" department. Was it misguided direction? Maliciousness? Incompetence?

It's well-known by now that this game had an incredibly troubled development, and it very much shows. And because of that, it's a fascinating game to study and think about. To explore, encounter a strange design decision, and think, "why did they do it like this?". A master class in game design, both good and bad. Yes, there is good to be found, especially in the DLCs.

Pros:
+The game has some lovely locations and atmosphere
+DLC areas feel very well-designed and are genuinely worth playing
+The raw mechanics in PvP feel much better than other Souls games

Cons:
-Good lord, pretty much everything else feels like a downgrade from DS1. Animations, sounds, boss designs, progression, etc. etc. etc. etc. etc.

Posted: August 25
Dark Souls 2, and especially Scholar of the First Sin is a little bit like the awkward middle child in the family.

Positive:

The world and atmosphere is great, it's the second best designed one out of the Dark Souls games in my opinion, worse than Dark Souls, but better than Dark Souls 3 as Dark Souls 3 always feels a little too linear to me after multiple playthroughs.

The lore is absolutely amazing but requires piecing together the puzzle from a huge amount of scattered sources just like every other From Software game.

This is specific to Scholar of the First Sin, if you only play the normal version of Dark Souls 2 this is not a thing. Scholar of the First Sin have the highest early game difficulty out of the Dark Souls games. If you play the base version, the difficulty scaling is better paced in many ways. But I enjoy the fact that it starts off more difficult and levels out later when your build comes together.

The theme of the Cursed One as a protagonist. And by that I mean when ever you die, you lose 5% of your maximum health up to a total of 50%. This works exactly like getting cursed in the game. It makes perfect sense from a lore standpoint. This may sound like it adds a lot of difficulty, but, that's not necessarily true, as you have the option to remove the effect with a certain item, and there is a ring which halves the effect. It may feel very bad in the early parts of the game to a new player, but once you progress a little bit deeper, it really doesn't matter as much as it first seems.

It is the only Dark Souls game where an archer playstyle is actually completely viable with the Dragonrider's Bow. It isn't fast or super high damage, but it does add another build type as an option. Bows are primarily a support secondary weapon in the other games. The issue is, there is only really one heavily scaling weapon option, even if the Black Bow of Pharris does look good on the surface, it struggles to put out anything even remotely comparable damage wise. However, it does add a ranged playstyle option to those who don't want to play magic, but do want to stay out of melee. And while I really hate ranged builds, it's a nice addition to at least have the option for those who wish to play it.


Weapons with the Striking Damage type are a bit too strong compared to everything else as so many enemies are weak to Striking Damage. Pretty much every heavily armored enemy in the game is weak to it, and about 80% of the enemies in the game are heavily armored.

Adaptability and Vitality always felt like wasted stats to me, I know some people see it as extra complexity and a good thing, but it's not added complexity having stats which only serve to increase the usefulness of your dodge in the case of Adaptability and only increases your carry weight and some minor resistances in the case of Vitality. You simply level them to a desired level and forget they exist, they add nothing to the game and only serve to feel like wasted skill points.

The amount of enemies is kind of excessive at times, and in a lot of cases, it doesn't necessarily add any difficulty, it simply make progressing an area take longer if you play it the normal way. Now after playing the game multiple times in both editions neither this nor the unnecessary stats really feel like an issue, but for a new player, it's something you simply have to learn to live with and adjust to if you play the game.


Dark Souls 2 in general is quite the divisive game, it's easy to see why some hate it and some love it and very few are in the neutral group in the middle. But I think those who hate it simply experienced the grueling early game and gave up due to being beaten around too much and never experienced the whole thing.

It's 100% worth buying, even at full price. But, the game will demand you adapt and learn early on, way more than the other From Software games excluding Sekiro, so keep that in mind.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is my favorite Souls game. It only needed more time—some areas feel empty—but what it gets right is brilliant.

Combat is different yet familiar, and the dual stance mode is fantastic (something removed after DS2 and later brought back in Elden Ring). Fashionsouls is on point—so many stylish armor sets. The game is long, packed with content, and Majula alone is worth the journey—majestic, serene, and unforgettable. The music? Perfect.

NPCs and side quests add depth, though some characters feel underdeveloped. Bosses are hit-or-miss—some are incredible, others disappointing—but the DLCs more than make up for it with tough fights and great content.

The Souls Memory system is punishing but clever: die a lot and your character level stays low, but your souls memory keeps growing. Invasions match by souls memory, not level, making encounters intense and strategic. Every lost soul counts.

The early version was darker and forced torches to light the way—a tense, atmospheric touch you can still enjoy today with torches or mods. Some mods even make the game look better than DS3.

Flawed? Yes. But full of ideas, challenge, and unforgettable moments.

This game is certainly something I'll tell you. Honestly I came into this game expecting to hate it considering everything I heard about this game, and well yeah there are a lot of parts I hate but not as much as I thought and definitely less when compared to the good things that I want to say.

Bad things first:
Boss battles: There are a lot of bosses, not many of them are challenging in the way you expect a boss fight to be especially if you just came from DS1 fresh off your fight with Artorias, Manus and Kalameet. There are bosses like them that are fun and challenging to fight but I'd argue thats only in the DLC's.

I say they are not challenging because for my very first play through of the game I relied on a spear and shield and turtled while simply rotating around them through most of the game until I ran into a boss that out DPS'd my stamina bar and changed up my strat. So yeah if thats not an indicator for how easy majority of the base game bosses are I dont know what is.

Enemy placement: This is ASS straight up there is no sugar coating and arguably the main thing that makes this game bad in the eyes of most, if there were people who enjoyed the first version of DS2 I would not be surprised if a lot of those people hated SOTFS. There are certain areas that you are better off summoning npc phantoms to help you get through so you dont feel so miserable when you do run backs on bosses. You can despawn them in this game which is good but only arguably a positive as it feels like its needed considering the amount and placement of enemies. You might as well just farm the enemies for souls and level yourself up than waste your time running to the boss at times.

Terrible Early game and unnecessarily punishing system:
Death is a little different here in DS2 than it was for DS1 which honestly gave me such a struggle at the start. If you die you lose max health as if you got the cursed status in the first game. The more you die the more your max health you lose that you cannot regenerate until you use an Human effigy which is this game's version of Humanity. It just feels like a redundant system, as you end up finding so many of them later in the game that you simply need to pace yourself as you go in your playthrough to the point where you wont even notice most of the time that your health is reduced when you die. As such it just feels unnecessarily punishing anyway especially when you first start out that you have to deal with this.

To be honest looking at the last two points I made, it feels as though this game was wholly designed upon the player relying on npc's to help with all the mobs, which is kind of annoying considering that the game is supposed to challenge you but npc's are everywhere and with the amount of enemies, its better to just let go of your ego and summon them to save you the misery.

now moving to good things

Build crafting, items, weapons, experimenting.
If you like to cook up sick builds or cool fashion, you will have fun in this game. The game constantly gives you different materials to upgrade your gear and test them out throughout your play through along with dozens of weapons to choose from many of which are just incredibly busted when you understand how to build. This to me is my best highlight of the game in that it encourages you to be creative and find new ways to fight enemies with the gear you get, maybe not on the first play through since you are getting your bearings but if you felt you wanted to play the game again and destroy all the bosses or the game itself, you will find no shortage of it in this game, which to me makes the mundane bosses you fight a little more bearable just to see them get deleted from the game in seconds.

World building and environment
Its Dark souls, what did you expect? Of course this was going to be good. No one ought to say that the did not do a good job in this category I mean look at how big this is. And yes regardless of some people saying that all the Dark souls games are separate and not in the same world and are just coincidentally have the same names like they are in different dimensions, you'd be surprised how much carries over and how interconnected everything is. If you are into the world like I am discovering more about the world and seeing the world after DS1 is something you will love.

PVP
I did not PVP all that much, but I had a lot of fun, I am a little thankful for the way PVP matchmaking works because if it wasnt for the way it did work I would have never tried PVP like dark souls 1. In this game PVP works of the number of souls acquired or soul memory not level like in DS1, good thing you can still match make with ppl on high level characters regardless of your level, bad thing you can get matched with someone who is max level and just kill you on the spot, regardless I do like this since it did still allow me to experience PVP in fact I actually one a few with my dumb strength build. I personally cant say it is the best PVP since I dont have much experience, but it is fun pvp thats for sure.

That being said to that one guy who invaded me at the start of my play through in forest of fallen giants while I was still level one and sniped me with dark magic from the tower for 3 invasions in a row. ♥♥♥♥ you in particular.

That would be all for me, ok technically I wrote more bad than good but I do feel that what is here is still worth dealing with all the bad. If you dont like what you are hearing but still want to play the game there is no problem with just playing the normal version of the game and just getting the DLC's there is no shame in it. That being said you do save more money for just buying this version of the game. I did put in 200-ish hours as of writing this review for the sake of achievements but playing through this game multiple times, and watching the videos on the story and how to make builds with weapons, really did boost my appreciation for what this game is and how it fits in the family. I do personally think these games need more time for people to play to understand what the game has to offer.

DARK SOULS 2: SCHOLAR OF THE FIRST SIN

Combat – 6.5/10
Combat in DS2 doesn’t feel drastically different from DS1, but the build freedom makes a big impact. You can experiment with more weapons, even dual-wielding two greatswords if you want, which gives you a lot of creative freedom. This is one area where DS2 shines compared to DS3, which limited build options, if a DS4 ever comes, bringing back this level of flexibility would be amazing.

Bosses – 5/10
Dark Souls 2 has a lot of variety in its bosses, which I’ve come to really appreciate. Some are weaker, and each fight feels different. At first, I didn’t enjoy DS2’s bosses, but after playing DS3, I realized there’s something special here that I actually miss. That said, DS2 also has some truly frustrating fights, bosses that feel overpowered or gimmicky to the point of being annoying. Variety is great, but the inconsistency holds it back.

World/Level Design – 9/10
One of my favorite worlds in the series. While it’s not as interconnected as DS1, the beauty and atmosphere make up for it. The intro area is incredible, the spawn bonfire is memorable, and the castle is easily the best-looking in the game. Some enemy placements can be annoying at times, but overall the world design is amazing. It’s a shame DS3 didn’t reference any of this world, because it really stands out.

Atmosphere – 8/10
The music and overall vibe are amazing. I could easily imagine living in this world, wandering along the coastline with my sword on my back. It’s beautiful and immersive, after finishing the game, I remember sitting and just looking out across the ocean. Dark Souls 2 has some of the best vibes in the series.

Difficulty – 4/10
This game can be really frustrating at times. Some bosses, like The Pursuer, are annoying if you don’t know which skills to level early on, and others can wipe you out with relentless combos. Sure, people might say “just get good,” and I did—I played all of these games with only a sword (using summons occasionally, but no magic). It’s tough in its own way, but the frustration can be overwhelming, even if you earn your stripes.

Replayability – 5/10
Replay value depends on the player. There’s more PvP and plenty of DLC content to explore, but I’m not sure I’d replay it as often as DS1. The main way I’d revisit it is through upgrading areas and the semi New Game Plus feature. It has potential, but it’s not as compelling for repeat playthroughs as some of the other entries.

Performance/Controls – 3/10
The controls feel similar to DS1, but the addition of a new skill makes things frustrating. It affects your “i-frames,” meaning you can get hit even when dodging or moving away, because technically your character is still within the enemy’s attack range. It works, but it can be really annoying in tight fights.

Overall Score – 5.8/10
Dark Souls 2 has some amazing moments, especially in world design, atmosphere, and build freedom. However, frustrating bosses, inconsistent difficulty, and clunky controls hold it back. It’s worth playing, especially for those who want more experimentation and a visually stunning world, but it doesn’t quite reach the heights of DS1 or DS3.
