Dark Souls II widely regarded as a disappointement and the worst game in the From Software Soulsborne anthology. While I understand where this vitriol coming from, I still can't help but respect this game. And, honestly, being the worst game in this series is still better than 90% of games. If we take DSII in vacuum, it's an amazing game in it's own right.

The game takes place in the kingdom of Drangleic. Our character, nameless person that was branded by the curse of the undead, destined to die and lose their soul over and over again until their mind is completely gone and they are nothing more than a hollow shell. Coming to Drangleic might be the only salvation for branded people, as the stories tell that somehow there is a way here to stop the curse, but the way for salvation will be long and painful.
The first thing that differs from the first game is how the game introduces itself in the first cutscene. While DS1 starts with an epic tale of war and beginning of a new age, the story of DS2 begins with a personal note. Our character is cursed and rapidly losing their memories, and false hope of reaching Drangleic is all they have.
Upon starting the game and leaving tutorial area, we reach the central hub of the game, a little village of Majula. Here we meet Emerald Herald, a person who will guide Bearer of the Curse on their journey, and will help us to reach that goal. The objective here becomes clear, find powerful souls to absorb their power to become stronger, and seek audience with the king Vendrick, who seemingly has the answer for how you can stop the curse.
The storytelling of DS2 remains largely the same, with the game giving general idea of what is going on and what you need to do, but, surprisingly, the scope feels different. In the first game there is no mention of your character until you get control of them in the undead asylum and only afterwards told of the prophecy of chosen undead. Here your character is a focal point of the story from the start and motivation feels mostly personal, until much later in the game when stakes rise higher. Not to mentione, there are very few obvious references to the first game, and the world of Drangleic feels largely disconnected from the Lordran. Only diving deeper into the lore and dialogue, you can piece together that there might be a connection, so the game doesn't really rely on the connection to it's past.

The graphics are a major improvement compared to the first game. While it's still not a high fidelity game in the traditional sense, the overall bump in the quality and resolution of the graphics is very noticable, especially compared to the original release of DS1. The game is also much more colorful and varied in it's palette. DS1 was mostly had darker and colder tones, while DS2 can use much more warmer tones. There is more variety in biomes and locations as well, not just castles and catacombes. And the most impressive achivement of the game visually is the lighting. in the promotional material, the lighting played a big part, especially with the introduction of dedicated torch, that was supposed to play a bigger part in the gameplay. While the lighting system isn't as impressive as was promised, it's still very much a big part of the game's visuals and atmosphere.

Now the gameplay changes is where it get's problematic. Dark Souls was already known to be a very difficult and abrasive game to it's players. There is a certain poijt you have to overcome, before these games will click with you, if you into them at all. But DS2 makes some controversial decisions that weren't welcome by most of the community.
i will start with my personal gripe, now the game punishes you for deaths by taking away a bit of health after each death, until you have 50%, at which point you get stuck with that amount. You can reverse this effect with an item and later in the game you will have it in abundance, but I still struggle to understand why was this change made in the first place. Dark Souls was already an extremely punishing game, where certain things you had to figure out by trial and error. The death wasn't the final point, the game actively gave you second chances to move a bit further with each attempt, and the only thing that could discourage you from moving on was yourself. But now the game actively punishes you for not playing it absolutely perfectly from one bonfire to another, discouraging experimentation and risk.
But this goes contrary to another decision, the enmy count and enemy agression is actually higher than original game, so even if you try to play it absolutely safe, you can and will get overwhelmed by the amount of enemies not only up close, but there frequently placed archers or magic users that will snipe you from far away, while you get ganked on by a group of 3-5 basic enemies, for which DS combat system is not made to handle, so you have to adjust for that with your build. Not to mention the usual Souls shenanigans wher the game places traps and tries to screw you over by placing bottomless pits and hidden enemies.
And thene there is introduction of the agility stat, whic dictates how fast you can drink your estus and how many invincibility frames you get during a dodgeroll. To improve that stat you have to invest either into attunement or adaptability stat, on top of having a light enough loadout to have a faster roll. The only way you can gues what this stat does if you look it up online, and thankfully the game was deconstructed to base and all of the numbers are already crunched for you, but without this stat, you basically playing a crippled version of the character, where you can get hit out of perfectly timed dodge or die because you took too long to heal up between hits.
DS2 is very hit or miss on boss fights. there are good and memorable fights for sure, but some of the feel like filler, with bosse with movesets that have giant blind spots, so they provide barely any challenge, or just a horde of basic enemies, that is presented as a bossfight with one big healthbar, so for each Smelter Demon or Mirror Knight you have Congregation or Covetous Demon.
Level design was changed from interconnected maze of different locations to more of cetral hub with branching paths. As mentioned before, Majula is your hub and base of operations. You will frequently return here to level up, upgrade your gear and maybe purchase some supplies. And from there you are basically free to choose which direction you want to go in to find a boss with a powerful soul. While this approach might give a bigger sense of personal choice and freedom, it also feels much linear in comparison, with each big bossfight ending with a dead end and bonfire that automatically teleports you back to Majula. So the world while varied, feels segmented and more linear. Not to mention how most of the locations in the late game take a nosedive in quality, with having more stupid traps and bad enemy placement. It's not as bad as DS1 Lost Izalith, but you can't help but notice that these zones wer probably rushed. And in the end I was really burned out on the game and wanted to rush to the finish quickly.

But despite all of this, I can't help but respect DS2 for trying to be different and not rest on the laurels. It definitely tried to move franchise further while doing it's own thing. It arguably has better atmosphere than the first game. The controls feel much more improved and smoother compared to the first game. There is much more build variety and more interesting weapons you will want to try out. Like I remember I could make somewhat viable build using fist weapons.

So yes, maybe DS2 overall feels worse than it's predecessor, but you can't deny that it is a quality game that, while having some controversial changes, overall had the courage to be different. And it's army of fans and defenders is a testament to the fact that this game deserves it's place in the Soulsborne anthology.

This game is completely over hated. I actually skipped it because of what everyone said online.When I played it, I had just as much fun with this as I did with DS1 and DS3. It seems like if you play slowly and carefully, you won't run into the same issues that people have online, especially with ganking. There are def a few bad ones, like in the Lost Bastille or Huntsman Copse, but I think the gankiness is vastly overstated.

I think there are also some for movement but I didn't mind being able to walk in a straight line easily.

Obviously it's not a flawless game, DS1 has better connected areas (in the first half) and DS3 has better boss fights & combat, poise is annoying, the crystal lizards are worse, frigid outskirts, there are no cloud saves, not as many good overhaul mods, durability can be annoying (but I like that it actually matters as a mechanic!! encourages diversity of weapons, also can be fixed with mods), etc... but none of this is bad enough to make this game not incredible.

it's completely worthy of being in the souls series and if you liked 1 or 3 I can't imagine not liking this, for me it scratches all the same itches and I enjoyed this so much. I regret skipping it. I've beaten it three times since I bought it, got all the achievements, and will def be back for more before long.

Good game, even though it has quite a few flaws.
It's my 2nd souls game (or 3rd, if you count Nightreign) as I decided to play them in order they were published. First time I tried playing it, was directly after finishing DS1, and let me tell you, it was not enjoyable.
Overall controls and hit detection are a huge downgrade compared to the first game. It was bothering me so much that I gave up on it.
After some time I decided to come back and I must admit, that was a good decision.
The game has a lot to offer. When it comes to the world, items, spells, secrets etc. there's just more of everything compared to the first one, and the quality is better as well.

Bosses are not that demanding, but if they were more complicated, I would argue that the game would be too hard. Why? Because half of the time I felt like my biggest enemy was not the abomination standing in front of me, but the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ controls, or the environment. Having said that, it still felt good to beat them, and the controls, well, you just get used to them.
Another thing I didn't like was ganking. At times there's just too much of it, it gets old and stale really fast. When you open the chest and suddenly an invisible enemy appears, just to shove a 3m long lance up yours ass, you no longer feel the the excitement related to the challenge or the anal penetration, you just roll your eyes and let out a long, sad sigh.

What was carrying the game for me, was the exploration. The map was well thought out, there were dozen places to visit and it was not holding your hand when it comes to directions. Usually it could be problematic (as you could wander the wrong way and end up next to the locked up entrance to Nito, like I did in the first one) but the game felt well balanced when it comes to power of enemies, so it wasn't that painful when you went the wrong way.

Overall I can understand both sides of the old DS2 love/hate conflict. The game is painful at times, but surprisingly, it has a lot to offer. If you're hesitant about playing it, like I was, don't be, it's worth your time, just don't push yourself to finish everything if it's not enjoyable (I'm looking at you, ♥♥♥♥♥ ass ice horses in 3rd DLC).

Pros:

Good story elements that keep you interested.

Well-written NPC dialogue.

Interesting concept with bonfire aesthetics.

Some cool design ideas.

Cons:

Overwhelming number of enemies.

Confusing and poorly thought-out level layout.

Badly designed bosses.

Poor NPC placement.

Poor enemy placement that feels unfair.

Lazy and unengaging item locations.

Overall:

Dark Souls 2 is kind of like Zach Snyder's DCEU where it absolutely has artistic vision it just isn't executed very well. One of the biggest things for me about why this doesn't work is animations and sound. The sound design to be frank is just garbage. Picking up an item cuts out all sound bar the item pickup sound then you get to hear the game mix in the rest of the sound again over the next 4 seconds and its just jarring and kills immersion. The impact noises of weapons are also just not good and make everything feel so weightless and pathetic. It's like everyone is getting hit with wet knuckles until all of a sudden there's a royalty free anvil sound because metal hit metal. The animations are so close to being good and feeling great but there's no acceleration or deceleration for a lot of the attacks so the entire animation plays at the same speed and it just feels gross. Swords lose their weight since it takes no effort to wind up same with axes and clubs and maces. ADP is just bad full stop. There's not much to be said about it other then it's a waste of time and souls and forces you to level 15-20 times before you can actually play the game. If enemy telegraphs were better and had starting points that were defined it wouldn't be an issue. The game keeps you very stamina starved so it's very clear the intent is to make you learn to sidestep or quickly sprint out of an attacks way instead of just spamming roll but it just doesn't work because the enemy animations are so poorly done which forces you to level ADP so you can spam roll. The best way to experience this game is unfortunately through a video essay.

It's often been said that games can't be art. Dark Souls 2 is the antithesis to that argument. Games occupy a unique niche where art is capable of causing genuine, tangible human suffering.

Dark Souls 2 reminds you, deep down, that you're going to die some day. With each death, you can feel the dulcet tones of the void in the bottom of your soul calling out to you. That maybe beating that boss really isn't worth the weapon or vague prestige, and that maybe you should focus on other things. Maybe try writing that book you've been talking about, stand up and go for a run, call your mom.

You attempt the Allone boss run "one last time", thinking maybe this will be it, knowing deep down that no reward could ever justify the time it's taken to even get this far. you just barely make it to Smelter Demon, eyes bloodshot, knowing full well that you're going to be doing that run again in five minutes.

Dark Souls 2 reminds us to value our lives, and maybe go outside and experience the breadth of what the world has to offer. I think it's really beautiful that From took the time to remind us all of that in the most painful ways imaginable, pioneering new and unique ways to waste your time and energy to encourage you to live up to your greatest potential.

Also the boss roster is pretty ok too I guess.

Normally you would expect a reasonably challenging area which leads to a challenging boss whereas this game starts with extremely challenging areas that lead to a pushover boss. For me bosses should be the most challenging and fun aspect of a souls game but in this game they feel like they barely add anything.

Apart from the DLC, most bosses are fairly easy to overcome. The sea of enemies and gank squads positioned to continuously overwhelm the player are the difficult aspect of the game. This coupled with the usual deaths from falling and the new genius mechanic to punish death by removing the players max HP by up to 50% from continuous deaths makes the early game extremely challenging for new players who are learning the game. This effect can only be removed by using a soul effigy(don't burn it in the bonfire) which are rarer in the early game.

Heide's tower and the Iron Keep have far too many enemies and it feels like a cheap way of increasing difficulty. As you get over this hill and level up and improve your skills; you now face a moderately challenging area with an even weaker boss.

The DLC's have fantastic bosses but are fairly short. They also have some of the worst boss run backs and argubably one of the worst regions i have ever experienced in gaming (beware of horse).

Apart from the gank squads and lack luster bosses there are several other annoyances with this game.
- Vitality is a new stat that specifically increases carry weight, SEPARATE to endurance (nice)
- The players iframes during a roll is now dependent on two other stats and the player starts with significantly less iframes than DS1 (dope)
- Stamina is severely lacking in this game, limiting number of dodges, sprint duration and weapon attacks
- You start with 1 estus and estus takes both a long time to use and a longer duration to heal you
- Combat mechanics like the lock on feature does not direct attacks towards said locked target - attacks are instead based of the direction the player moves in

I finished this game to tick it off the list of souls games, not because i enjoyed playing it. Its a massive step back from Dark souls one. 6/10 can't recommend to the average player, only for masochists such as myself.

Initially, I wasn't feeling this at all...I think coming directly from DS1, which I loved, and having that fresh on my mind made all the changes more noticeable. Particularly during Forest of Giants and No Man's Wharf (some of the first areas) I wasn't very enthused and No Man's Wharf especially I was pretty much over it, to the point of leaving a negative review and just going to put the game down. Despite that initial experience, DS2 has some twisted charm to it that had me give it a go one more time and I feel like the game really clicked for me after getting through Wharf.

Will list some pros, cons and neutrals of my experience after beating the base game to try and be concise.


PROS
+ The world of Dragleic is an almost jarring departure from DS1/DS3 but by the end I really have grown to appreciate the different direction they took the Souls universe. The story starts out very slow and barebones, not atypical for Souls games. As the plot thickens and universe of the game reveals it's secrets, I found it surprisingly fascinating to the point I would say I enjoyed this aspect more than 1 & 3! Like a more subdued Elden Ring of sorts.

+ I've gathered the changes to how stats work from the 1st game were not very well received overall? I probably felt the same at first but I actually really dig the newfound complexity stats like ADP add and how they made weight limit it's own stat. While there's respecs, it's pretty unforgiving going in blind so would recommend scouring the internet for questions about stats. Even with that knowledge I still enjoyed just puzzling out how to allocate everything and I would say I found it more interesting overall than the other Souls games besides Elden Ring.

+ The addition of "dark magic" is interesting. Between that, the other magic and ofc various melee type builds I find myself theory-crafting more than I did with DS1/DS3.

+ Each individual area is it's own puzzle and the world does less of that brilliant interconnected thing the first game did and moreso makes each area the "boss" compared to the actual area bosses. The devs were trollss and you will get ganked from the most random spots and you will like it, I was sort of rolling my eyes at that aspect at first but grew to dig it for what it is as it made strategizing the best path forward very satisfying in the end.

+ There's a ton of ♥♥♥♥ to do and I don't feel like I'm close to beating it even with seeing the ending.


NEUTRALS

+- I'm kind of torn on the way the movement and combat feels. I actually love how slow and deliberate everything feels, feels more tactical than something like DS3 especially. Critical hits are harder to get and require careful positioning which I think really makes DS2 feel like it's own beast. But the change of full 360 movement to 8 axis still feels kind of jank overall and combat just straight up feels bad if you don't level up your agility which is very obscure and not explained well at all. Overall, brilliant in many ways but some flaws from my perspective from being straight up positive.

+- Idk if I've just been getting better at Souls games and that's where this is coming from, but the bosses are almost hilariously easy for the most part. I think at most I took like, 4 or 5 tries on a boss? Usually 1 or 2? I put this as neutral as the epic scale of the boss fights was still sick and it was actually a relief to not struggle on them after getting through the pain in the ass it can be to actually reach them, but if you dug stuff like Pontiff in DS3 this aspect might be underwhelming.

+- The covenant grind is immense at this stage in the game's life with the "Soul Memory" mechanic that matches people based on how many souls they've gotten throughout the game. I find this both interesting but has been kind of frustrating at the same time, especially trying to do the boss co-op covenant as I didn't know any better and am higher SM than I'd like to be.


CONS

- 8 axis movement is jank which I already mentioned but feels especially noteworthy.

- Not a fan of how hard it is to try out different weapons on first playthrough, materials feel very limited even in endgame compared to the 1st game.

- While it adds a sort of charm to the DS2 vibe, there's this sort of "unfinished" feel to the game overall. A lot of NPCs don't have dialog progression at a certain point and sometimes areas just feel like they end abruptly. Most of the areas seem to be poison themed which is a bit strange. I can see how this would happen given the scope of 2 but overall the other games feel more complete.

- How convoluted it feels to acquire some items and the grind to do a lot of the covenants is a bit immense for better and for worse. Looking at you Falconers. And some endgame stuff.


& I'm tired of typing this so yeah, felt like I needed to revise my review and definitely recommend playing through this at least once if you're a Souls fan. Don't give up! DS2 has it's own wonderful charm that I highly recommend experiencing at least once and I'm really glad I stuck with it.

I’ve been on a Souls kick recently and am trying to play them all once. I just finished this one after about 70 hours, which was what it took me to kill every boss and explore every area. I did not mess around with NG+ or maxing out the (non-darkwalker) covenants. I don’t claim to be particularly good at Souls games, but I do enjoy them. I was nervous about this one because I know it’s kind of the redheaded stepchild of the Souls family, but I didn’t actually think it was a bad game at all. In fact I thought it was pretty fun.

I tend to write overly long drawn out reviews so I will give the short version up front: this game does some very frustrating things. Overall though I found it to be an enjoyable experience and not nearly as bad as many people say it is. It is a very demanding game that must be played in a specific way, and a significant part of enjoying this game is figuring out how the developers expect you to play it, because it is not the same as any other Souls game. If you don’t adjust to that playstyle you will not have a good time. That said, isn’t being challenging and uncompromising the reason we like Souls games?

Also, a ton of concepts from this game got a glow-up and made it into Elden Ring (enemies, locations, even simple stuff like some attacks). I played the games back to back and was constantly surprised when I saw something and recognized it from Elden Ring. It’s kind of a time capsule or a piece of Souls history in that way. I think that’s pretty cool.

Anyway, that’s the short version. Buckle in for the long version if you’re interested: I would like to spend a bit of time saying why I think this game gets an unfairly bad reputation. I don’t claim to have any special source for this information, it’s just what makes sense to me from what I saw while playing. In short, this game is different. The developers did a lot of things that no other Souls game does. Some of these mechanics are really cool (I love how it does elemental scaling) or thematic (going hollow and losing max health when you die, which is super thematic but can be annoying), and some are just half-baked (torches and lighting sconces around levels), but they are all deliberate. In fact “deliberate” is probably the key word I would use to describe this game and how it needs to be played. You can not rush in this game. You have to go slow, be methodical, and use the tools the game gives you.

Combat in general is much slower than modern Souls games. The enemies control the pace of the fight, not you. To some extent you have to play scared. I really had to adjust to that because I tend to be overly aggressive and trade damage with the enemies way more than I should. Sometimes an enemy would have an opening for me to attack and I would deliberately stand there doing nothing because my stamina was low and I needed to let it recharge. It felt really wrong at first, since newer games expect you to be constantly attacking, but stamina management of that kind is required in this game.

The enemy placement also requires a methodical approach. People often complain of getting swarmed by enemies in this game, but that is usually because they rushed in too fast. It is almost always possible to draw out a couple enemies at a time and have much more manageable fights. I will say that enemy detection ranges felt inconsistent to me (sometimes an enemy would see me from across the entire map, other times the same guy wouldn’t notice me if I tapped on its shoulder), and a couple were way too long, but overall the “this game is all gank squads” complaint mostly goes away if you are careful with managing your fights. Whether you find that fun is a personal decision. Also, 1v1 fights are pretty boring in this game anyway since you can do backstabs super easily.

Another part of this is just running through the enemies to get to the boss is not an option that works in this game. Going slowly and killing all the enemies is the expectation here (90% of the time, finding out what areas it is better to just bum-rush is part of the fun). Plus if you kill the enemies enough times they stop spawning, so if you are really stuck eventually you can just de-populate the level. I definitely had to do that a couple times when I was trying an area that was too high level for me.

Lastly, I get the feeling some of that enemy placement or bosses that 2v1 you is the developers really saying “you shouldn’t be playing this game solo”. More than any other Souls game I have played I think co-op and the NPC summons are an expected part of this game. There are several NPC summons that aren’t even close to a boss and are just there to help you through the level. Use them.

Let’s talk about the big one now, the hitboxes. There are a couple hitboxes that are too big or last too long, but that’s very much the exception. The majority are very faithful to what you see on screen. I think a lot of the complaints about this come from not understanding how the developers expect you to be playing. Newer Souls games have trained us to use dodging as a tool to get invulnerability frames, then roll through attacks and AOEs. You can not do that in this game. You are not supposed to do that in this game. You will have a bad time if you try to do that in this game. Raising agility lets you play a little more risky and maybe get more attacks in than you otherwise would, but it won’t change the fact that Dark Souls 2 is a game of positioning, not reflexes. You avoid damage not by rolling through attacks, but by learning where to stand relative to the enemy so you don’t get hit. Dodging is a positioning tool to get you into that place if you happen to be outside it when an attack starts.

I actually liked most of the bosses in this game, although this game is much more about the journey than the destination, so very few bosses felt incredibly great. A lot of them have gimmicks or make you have to play the game differently and I find that to be fun. A Souls game needs some “just get good” bosses, but if that’s all there is then I find it to get boring really quickly. Throwing a different challenge at me that makes me play the game differently is good. There are some stinkers, but there always are in early Souls games, and I didn’t find any of them to be Capra Demon or Centipede Demon levels of annoying/pointless.

One last thing I will say is that this game has some incredible little visual details no other game in the series does. Candle flames will flicker if you walk by them, your clothing flaps in the wind, footprints you make in the sand are different depending on how much equipment you are carrying and whether you have water on you or not. It’s a bunch of super immersive little details that you would never notice unless you are specifically looking for them. I also really liked the story and the general vibe of some of the areas was super compelling (even if that vibe is “this place is a miserable hellhole”, at least it does a good job of conveying that it is a miserable hellhole).

Anyway, thank you for reading this. If you made it this far then I hope I did an ok job of explaining how a lot of complaints about this game are features, not bugs. If you don’t like those decisions then that’s fine, but I think it is important to understand why this game plays the way it does rather than immediately assuming the developers were incompetent/sadistic.

I have very few good things to say about ds2.

pros:

-PvP boils down to who has the better lag switch
Whilst the game has the most content of the 3 games, most areas are full of pointless tedium that they often feel barren of engagement

-The worst example of asset and design reuse via the gutter, black gulch, the sunken city dlc, shrine of amana, and earthern peak. Of the listed areas, 4 of 5 are poison based areas, with all of the dlc being poison

Even still, it's definitely 'the weird one' in the Dark Souls trilogy. The bleak artistic and color direction of the first and third games in the series is largely absent here, replaced with a more vibrant palette and a more varied fantasy setting broken up into more disparate areas that aren't really 'connected' together quite as nicely or as tightly as the ruined lands of Lordran or Lothric.

In fact, the narrative and character connections between the characters in this game and the setting of Drangleic to the rest of the 'Dark Souls'-verse aren't readily apparent at first, feeling almost like an entirely separate game using the same engine. The connections -are- there, though, slowly revealing themselves through the same great environmental storytelling and item-description narrative that I adore from the other games.

Dark Souls 2 really tried to do new things with the gameplay mechanics and narrative, both. And sometimes it's a swing and a miss, but other times it actually hits upon interesting ideas. The concept of Aldia, Vendrick, the primal relationship between Fire and Dark, and how the Undead Curse plays into things are all neat ideas and it's kind of a pity that Dark Souls 3 didn't really try to look back on or build further upon those concepts.

It's a fun, interesting game, and definitely underrated in the trilogy.

You are probably looking at reviews to see if Dark Souls 2 is worth buying since the games launch its been ♥♥♥♥ on by so many people for so long (even including me for some time) but let me say most of the hate for this game is not justified at all, and while only have a very few mechanics that I actually agree.

Dark Souls 2 isn't like the other souls games, more so its not like Dark Souls 1 or 3. I would say in the trilogy its by far the most complex and different in the series. And this is where a-lot of people get stumped or decide to hate the game, because it challenges them in a different way with different mechanics. Its not like Dark Souls 1 where dying is more forgiving, and unlike Dark Souls 3 which IMO was made much easier because of the backlash of Dark Souls 2.

When you buy this game, and start playing, I'm not saying to forget everything you learned from Ds1 and Ds3, but keep in mind this games mechanics change so many things that if you aren't okay with adapting to, you will die over and over again. Don't try to rush into things and expect it to work. The game has its rules, and with that you do as you please. But if you decide to ignore the information given to you, and just try to brute force it, you are going to have a bad time.

There is quite alot of freedom in this game, I would say more than ds1 and ds3, but still, actually try to learn from mistakes, and use the information and items available to you. Lighting torches, using consumables, talking to NPCs, not killing every NPC you meet, reading item descriptions, using a bow (even on pure str builds), understanding how leveling up works and what each attribute gives you, level up ADP, exploring each new area, etc... In the other games you can go your entire play through without giving what you are doing much thought, this is coming from someone with many hours combined on Ds1 and Ds3.

In Dark Souls 2 it will ask alot from you, but if you simply learn how its world works, and learn to take things slower, it is an amazing experience filled with so much life, fantastic characters, fun PVE and PVP, customization, a New Game + that ACTUALLY matters, etc... Don't jump into this game expecting it to be similar to the other souls games, its the most unique, but to me, that's what makes it so much fun. Its an experience you won't forget. 9/10.

Dark Souls II might just be the best of the FromSoftware family. It experiments with new concepts, some of which fall short, but the gameplay changes feel like a passionate, almost desperate attempt to impress. As you play, it becomes clear that Dark Souls II is filled with the heartfelt effort of director Tomohiro Shibuya, not just to honor the legacy of Dark Souls, but to impress Hidetaka Miyazaki himself, like a child desperate for his parent's pride. Dark Souls 2 is unique and any fan of the genre should complete it to truly understand how it has shaped the future installments of the FromSoftware family.

Dark Souls II has long been known as the "black sheep" of the Souls series — often criticized, sometimes dismissed. But after giving it a real second chance, I can confidently say: this is one of my favorite entries in the franchise. It even surpassed Dark Souls III, which had been my top pick until now.

My first attempt at the game was years ago. I made it 30–40% through before getting completely lost. I wandered in circles, unsure where to go, and eventually quit. But recently, I returned — this time with patience and curiosity — and completed the game along with all three DLCs. And honestly? I'm glad I did.

Yes, the game introduces some controversial mechanics. Dodging tied to the ADP stat, lack of invincibility while walking through fog gates, and worst of all — the curse mechanic that lowers your max HP with every death. At first, these feel frustrating. But once you adapt, you start to see that DS2 plays by a different set of rules, emphasizing a slower, more methodical pace.

Where the game shines is in its world design. Majula — calm, melancholic, and beautifully atmospheric — serves as a hub that branches into multiple paths. You’re free to explore in various directions from the very start, which brings a strong sense of non-linear adventure.

It also offers a massive variety of weapons, the fantastic Power Stance system, and life gems, which make exploration smoother without constantly returning to bonfires. The game trusts the player — it gives you tools, but no hand-holding.

The DLCs are a standout feature. Tough, intricate, and often puzzle- or platform-focused, they add real depth to the base game and test your skill in creative ways.

If there's a major downside, it’s the boss fights. While many of them look great, they often fall flat in execution. Limited movesets, predictable behavior, and underwhelming difficulty. Often the real challenge is getting to the boss — not beating them.

Despite all that, Dark Souls II is an ambitious, bold, and highly underrated Souls experience. FromSoftware tried something different — and while not everything lands perfectly, the end result is still an incredibly rewarding journey. If you approach it with patience and an open mind, you might find a hidden gem here.

Rating: 8/10 – DS2 is Soulsborne through and through. Just… with a twist.

If you’re getting into the Souls series, this is a must-play form your own opinion and see where it stands for you.

I really enjoyed this game initially.
It just has a lot of flaws that actually kept me from even finishing the final boss.

It's got great environmental design, good music, fun enemies, characters and bosses. Plus this title is a little more freeform with letting you go where you want to go when compared to the first game. When I say flaws though, I don't think the base flaws of the game really keep it from being bad, it's some uh... other... flaws that really tick me off. This game has flaws like trying to limit enemy farming with souls by removing enemies after killing them too many times, except you can respawn them anytime you like by upping the area difficulty, plus the game actually ends up having an overflowing souls economy to an absurd extent. I had something like 35 in every stat except faith and pushed strength a little further than that by the time I reached the throne watcher and defender so it ends up being meaningless except for a reason I'll get back to later.
This game struggles with really informing you of where you need to go. It somewhat expresses it better than dark souls 1 did, but I really don't know where the dlc areas are or how to get to them which judging by the artorias dlc in ds1 I don't want to find out what arbitrary tasks you need to do. I just didn't really know what to do with the ashen mist, didn't know which kings door to open first, didn't know if I was on the right track or not and if a dead end was an actual dead end. So pretty much leaving those flaws from the first game out in the open. It has troubling boss design, which isn't new, but it swings from the pursuer with a cool attack moveset that I actually couldn't fight at my level when I faced him and you need to use the crossbows. Only to end up running through entire AREAS after where I go up and in a single swing clear out an entire third of the enemies on the way to a boss. Hell I remember going up to a boss fight only to see it was just this standard enemy I already faced on the path to the last boss fight and felt I had done it all out of order.
What really bothers me about this game though isn't any of that since the core gameplay is solid and I felt the non-existent but actually sortof there story that people don't want to admit is poorly implemented since you shouldn't be expected to only understand what the ♥♥♥♥ is happening by reading every item description and looking in corners. It was still kindof cool, and the enemy design could be cool when it wanted to be (but boring most of the time). What really bothered me was just the actual level design.

When I first played this game, I was expecting to not like it, and when I did play it, I found it frustrating and I stopped playing.

And then a while later, I played it again and realized it actually isn't that bad. The only part of the game I really hated was the DLC area . It's completely awful, and because its so awful, a lot of people tend to invade there, making it worse (can't blame them, really. I'd probably do the same if I tried invading in this game). The other two DLC are not as bad, but still definitely worse than the main game.

You can definitely see this game is a transition between DS1 and DS3. They had some really good ideas that they implemented later (like Power Stancing, where you can dual-wield certain weapons to get a unique move-set, which only returned in Elden Ring), and some bad ideas that everyone hates, like hollowing (where as you die, you lose a little bit of maximum health until you become human again, which can drop all the way to half that of when you're human, which sort-of appeared in a very lite form in DS3, and disappeared in Elden Ring).

Spoiler territory ahead btw



TL:DR Its still a good game, but probably not as good as DS1 and DS3. Its not the horrible trash people with the DS3 superiority mind-worm might tell you, but its not the amazing incredible god-send that people with the DS2 superiority mind-worm might tell you.

DS2 was my introduction to the soulsbornes games, and I played on Xbox 360. I didn't have any home-internet at the time, and I had the original non DLC version. It took me a whole year to finally beat the game. There were moments when I wanted to throw my screen out of the window, and I'm sure I threw a few ccontrollers. I'm glad this one was my first because it made me better at the others from the start.

I can see why people think this one is the worst: the area layouts don't make a lot of sense, it's slower, the hit boxes can be baffling, there's a finite number of collectable souls without increasing difficulty, and adaptability.

But it's still my favorite.

I love the desolate, dying world and its subtle lore. I love the characters slowly losing their minds and memories. I love dual weilding and how it unlocks different movesets.
I love how the bosses and their arenas all have different mechanics.

You should play it if you claim to be a fan of Dark Souls / Bloodborne / Elden Ring.

Ok, I wish to start with the bad things:
1) The game barely works well with mouse and keyboard. The menu is hard and unpleasant to navigate, the character does not respond to commands from time to time (despite having some stamina to spear) and configuring the controls is a nightmare.
2) The hitboxes are flawed, it happens quite often to get hit by some attacks even when you CLEARLY dodged them. Also, the weapons tend to not record hits when you clearly hit something.
3) The kind of difficulty this game gives us is purely unenjoyable sometimes. I remember defeating the Blue Smelter Demon and instead of feeling happy for defeating a challenge, I was happy for not having to do the runback ever again. The frigid Outskirts is just stupid so I won't waste my time on it. These are just two examples of areas that are difficult just to be difficult.
4) Throne Defender and Throne Warden. Worst boss in the game in my opinion...just stupid and absolutely a pain.
5) The hollowing mechanic made this whole experience unpleasant. Punishing a player for dying this badly is just bad. I used to die a lot in Elden Ring just to practice perfect parry, try new things and learn from my mistakes without being punished for it. This mechanic made me not wanting to try new things and learn...I just bonked stuff like a caveman because this is the safest way to go through this game.

...and now the reasons for liking this game:
1) Some boss fights were enjoyable, like Fume Knight, Queen Nashandra, Sir Allone and Aldia. Decently difficult and yet enjoyable. Unfortunately, all worse than Artorias, Manus and "Laurel and Hardy" form the previous game.
2) There was a godly amount of work being put into this game and you can definitely see that in the graphics, the map design, the areas, weapons and spells. Also, despite the hollowing mechanic, I was reticent to try anything new.
3) It is a Fromsoftware game.
4) Curiosity and exploring is well rewarded. I feels well finding nice stuff in the world , doing puzzles, finding shortcuts, etc.
5) It is easier to travel from bonfire to bonfire.
6) The despawn mechanic is a welcome introduction.
7) It has a lot of replayability. Even after 60 hours and finishing the main game and the 3 DLCs, there are still lots of things that I have yet to discover.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is a strange but captivating journey through the cursed land of Drangleic. It’s a game that doesn’t aim to win you over quickly — it tests your patience, slows you down, and at times confuses you, but always rewards persistence with a deep sense of accomplishment.

With reworked enemy placements, a new NPC (the Scholar), and all DLCs bundled in, this is the most complete version of the game. Its tone and atmosphere differ from the first and third entries, but that’s what makes it compelling — it dives into themes like the cycle of suffering, forgetting, and the passage of time.

Dark Souls II isn’t simply a “weaker” game — it’s a different one, and its uniqueness is its strength.

Dark Souls II is a really good game, offering a fascinating world, a challenging but fair combat system and, most importantly, an incredible gaming experience underlined by nervousness, panic and other pleasant emotions. It will drive you away a hundred times with constant frustration, but you will always return to it carefully, slowly, just to try it one more time. After all, it is just a giant waving its own torn claw, that would be so that you don't smash its head with an axe! And when it finally falls at your feet, the euphoria is almost indescribable. Even though a basic zombie will shamefully stab you around the next corner.

The Scholar of the First Sin special edition is named after one of the new bosses/NPCs. It combines a slightly modified base game with the latest extensive patch and three expansion DLCs with a jewelry theme – Crown of the Sunken King, Crown of the Old Iron King and Crown of the Ivory King.
The changes in the patch, which also apply to the normal version, are also entirely beneficial - they adjust the online game, balance and generally fix some shortcomings.
According to official statements, the version on PC and next-generation consoles has also received graphical and sound improvements, but this is not a revolution. You will notice a certain difference, but I think that very few people will stop to think about it. However, this does not matter too much - Dark Souls II may not be the most amazing game graphically, but you will still come across a lot of beautiful scenery and places, bursting with atmosphere.

A pleasant combination of a modified (and more difficult) version of Dark Souls II and excellent DLC. The brutal difficulty and fantastic atmosphere have not receded an inch, they are even more intense than before.

Dark Souls II is a worthy entry in the Souls series, offering a massive, mysterious world to explore, brutal yet rewarding combat, and the same sense of dread and triumph that defines the franchise. The game’s atmosphere is rich and oppressive in the best way, pulling you into the decaying kingdom of Drangleic with eerie visuals, cryptic NPCs, and haunting music.

One of the game’s strongest features is its build variety and replayability. With so many weapons, spells, and character paths, every playthrough can feel fresh and different. The combat retains that deliberate, punishing style Souls fans love, and boss fights—while not always as memorable as in other entries—still offer intense, strategic challenges.

That said, Dark Souls II isn’t without its flaws. The world design feels less interconnected than the first game, and some enemy placements can come off as unfair or artificial. The story, while intriguing, is more obscure and less emotionally gripping than in Dark Souls or Dark Souls III. Still, these issues don’t outweigh the game’s strengths.

Despite its rough edges, Dark Souls II delivers a deep and satisfying experience that stands tall on its own. It's a unique and ambitious chapter in the series, and for many players, it’s where they truly fell in love with the Souls formula.

Revisiting Dark Souls II years later, I’ve come to appreciate it far more, and aesthetically it’s become my favorite of the trilogy. My first playthrough left me frustrated with its clunky stat system, punishing health penalties, and awkward mechanics like needing to raise “adaptability” just to dodge properly. The hit boxes, repetitive knight-heavy enemies, and bosses with bloated health bars didn’t help either. Now, with fresh eyes, I can look past those flaws and enjoy what it does well. The haunting atmosphere, striking visuals, and eerie sense of place give it a unique identity that stands apart from the other games. While its world is more linear than the first game, it’s still full of moments that deliver that signature Souls satisfaction when you overcome its brutal challenges. It’s not the best-playing entry, but returning to it made me realize how much personality and aesthetic strength it has. For anyone who dismissed it early on, I’d say it’s worth another chance. Plus, it has the most DLC and all of it is a step above the base game.

While Dark Souls 2 has some interesting ideas and very cool small-scale levels, it rarely lets you enjoy them for more than a minute. It constantly throws its terrible design choices in your face, and when combined with the janky character controller, terrible hitboxes and ADP, it significantly sours an otherwise cool experience.

Throughout my time with Dark Souls 2, I regularly considered calling it quits. Not because I was stuck on a difficult boss or encounter, as most enemies and bosses are trivially easy. But because the game’s only tricks are to overwhelm or confuse. Often, the sanest way to play was to speed past everything to avoid frustration with obnoxious enemy placements and relentless spam.

The larger-scale level design was confusing and unintuitive. I rarely felt naturally guided along the right path.
Some small-scale level design was good, with clever secrets and shortcuts. I would have liked to explore them more.

Despite Dark Souls 2 containing some of FromSoft's worst design and very little of its best, I still recommend it to any Soulsborne fan. It's miles ahead of the competition, even if it is the weakest in the series.

After completing the first Dark Souls with all achievements, you know I had to play this one. And once again, what a game.

It felt really weird to play this one after the first game. Starting the game and reaching Majula, the "hub", I really thought something bad was going to happen, but no, this game just has beautiful zones and so much brighter than the first Dark Souls. But it doesn't mean that you don't get "dark" zones, this game has a beautiful mix of both.

Maybe it's because I jumped straight to this one after the first, but bosses felt a bit easier. So I can't really talk about the difficulty. But the game felt fair. To be completely honest, I do not think I've died once because of a bad hitbox or bug, it was my fault and only my fault every time. Do not listen to what people say, this game is not bugged.

I do not want to talk too much about the game itself because I truly think that Dark Souls is a must-play game. And don't forget that they are only hard if you challenge yourself too much. Level up, use the tools and items the game is literally giving to you, and most importantly, make a build that feels right for you, not the "meta" ones, it will make the game way more enjoyable.

To end on a funny note for people who already played the game:

♥♥♥♥ Shrine of Amana and Iron Keep and everything they stand for.

As beautiful as they are, whoever designed these zones deserves a special place in hell because Iron Keep itself wouldn't be cruel enough for this mf.

On that note, I'll go play Dark Souls 3.

Please give the Dark Souls games a chance. They are worth the time and effort.

You walk into an area. This area contains 1,000 enemies that will completely swarm you. Learn about these 1,000 enemies really well, since you'll now be walking through them for 6 km every time you die to one of this game's 40 boring bosses. Think you're doing badly? Don't worry—here's a random NPC invader, just so you're not bored of the enemies currently swarming you or the boss you're trying to reach for the 16th time.

After you beat a boss in Dark Souls 1, you feel accomplished and excited to see what new part of the world the boss was guarding. Here, you just kill a major boss, and it teleports you back to the most boring hub in any game. Don’t even get me started on how you have to teleport to this hub every time you want to level up and listen to “Bear… Seek… Seek… Lest.”

If this game didn’t have the Dark Souls franchise name, nobody would give it a chance, and it would be lost like many other poorly executed games of its era.

It’s not the worst game, but it’s too bad for having so many people defending it.

A great game that gets overhated imho. If you are searching for a more refined basic experience, play DS3. However, if you want something a bit more unique and with more depth, play DS2 (Of course im talking about the sotf edition, the og DS2 is different and another topic to discuss at some other point in time). This game has some of the greatest ideas and incorpations in the series (sadly this is rivaled with some of the worst ideas and incorporations). Some of these include, powerstancing (ER added this back but somehow made it worse than the system implemented in DS2), multiple movesets for a single weapon, hexes, ng+, upgrade system, covenants. Ontop of this DS2 had the best PvP in the entire series (sorry DS3 roll spammers, you actually can't do that in this game). Is this most likely the weakest game Fromsoft has ever made? Absolutely. Is it overhated? Absolutely. This game does deserve some of the flak it gets I know, but most of it is just nonsensical and obtuse. This is easily my favorite game of the franchise but I know others may not feel the same, however I do think this game is worth a try atleast. If your getting second thoughts, just buy the game when its on sale, it even costs the least amount being $20 at a 50% discount unlike DS3 being 30 at 50% (Which doesnt even cover the dlcs like DS2 does).

I've been playing this game for a few hours now and I just have to say that I *really* wanted to like it. As a long time souls fan, I find basically something to like in every game and generally speaking hold the whole franchise including fromsoft's other works on a pedestal of some of the finest game design put out onto the market.

However...

There's really nothing redeeming here to say.
I'm about 70% of my way through the bosses and some of them peak at being mid while the rest are actively terrible or forgettable. Pretty much every area I've been to has been boring, forgettable or so frustrating it makes me want to put down the controller.

Every new zone is filled with random death pits, gank fights where they cut off the exits and give enemies way too high poise even for my strength build or some other random bs gimmick to try and artificially add difficulty to an area that otherwise would have zero since none of the areas have really been thought through. I know they limited the amount of ganks compared to the original release but honestly, it's still the primary core gameplay loop.

I'll still keep working through this and beat it eventually because it's the only souls game I haven't yet completed, but honestly the game is beyond my ability to recommend it already. If you're going to play dark souls, pick either this game's predecessor or successor because those are both well designed, fun exploreable games with plenty of challenge, environmental intrigue and lore to sift through.

This game is just a janky mess held up by a cult following of strangely dedicated fans that are here for the vibe the game gives off. Which, fair enough - if this is your comfort space more power to you. But it's as far from a well put together experience as humanly possible. It still honestly *shocks* me just how this managed to even get released in this condition.

TL;DR: Overall, I recommend DS2. I think it's a good game. You don't have to read further if you are on the fence on trying it, give it a try, it's pretty good. While it has issues, most people I see around just refuse to engage with those issues and instead go "game bad". I think it's a bit unfair to do so.

============================================================

Dark Souls 2 is a pretty good game. Dark Souls 2 is also a really weird game. On one hand there are so many improvements over the first game, upgrade system is easier to engage with and wrap your head around, damage system is handled in a better way where split damage doesn't necessarily spell death sentence on otherwise pretty cool weapons or kept you away from engaging with infusions because they'd usually split your weapon's damage, which meant that you would do less damage a lot of the time due to defense calculations. It also lets you buff infused weapons. Upgrade materials are also made to be more available, including slabs, which lets you experiment with weapons a lot more. Powerstancing is very cool and fun. Attunement giving extra casts and cast speed (along with int/faith), most stats giving a tiny bit of health, equip load made to be a lot more linear instead of you having to learn about specific thresholds. Bonfire Ascetic is a really fun mechanic that makes certain items more accessible whereas in DS1 you would need to go into NG+ if you wanted a second copy of an item. There's a decent bit here that deserves a lot of appreciation. Invades happening regardless of whether you are human or not is nice.

However, this game, for some reason, has forgotten a lot of the intent behind design decisions from DS1. It feels almost as if the game wanted to talk to you about how hollowing makes you forget yourself that game started hollowing and forgot some of the things that made DS1 so good.

Friction from Estus charge management, which I found very enjoyable in DS1, trying to strategize so you can last it to boss, figuring out ways to run through certain encounters to save up on Estus are mostly removed in favor of Lifegems and their extremely cheap nigh infinite healing supply. DS2 souls currency is overall stronger in an economy standpoint, you have access to far more souls while things you would spend souls on are cheaper. Due to having this much healing and it being cheap enough and renewable, I'm sort of made to clear out an entire area before moving on, I know I'll make a profit of souls on top of replenishing what I lost.

Game is unnecessarily long. There's a ton of areas that don't really have much substance.

There are far more bosses in DS2, boss quality is mostly same but they are generally less interesting. In DS1 there's generally an intent as to why they placed certain bosses in certain places, whereas in DS2 they mostly feel like an elite enemy. They're also less memorable compared to less interesting looking (and less lore relevant) bosses like Taurus Demon. There just isn't much flavor in fighting these bosses.

Few of the areas ask you to juggle a bit too many things but sort of in an annoying way, like Shrine of Amana needing you to pay more attention to ground so you don't fall while exploring the place (it's hard to see due to water surface) but also you have to deal with these mages (you are also in water which means rushing them down isn't as effective), but also deal with these lurking melee enemies that will probably swarm you, unless you go at a snail's pace if you want to explore the area.

The very first achievement most people will get "This is Dark Souls" goes against the tone of DS1 in a really weird and off-putting way. Same with the Old Firekeeper cutscene before and after you create your character. It feels like what was attempted to be imparted by DS1 was taken, simplified to its most attention grabbing form (something fans of things tend to do) which resulted in this weird warped message that runs counter to game directly previous to this. These two things set the tone for the game and honestly, those cutscenes don't lie because there *are* situations where you probably will die because you simply weren't omnicient on your first time. In a game where death is a learning moment, it feels weird to just be killed as punishment for the act of playing the game. Losing maximum health, while I don't mind as much, kind of contributes to this attitude of the game. You're punished for making mistakes or falling for traps you had to be omnicient for, instead of rewarded for learning and persevering like before. More than anything game cheapens and insults itself with these.

I talked about agility here before, I think I changed my mind. While item use speed might have some merit as a stat, altering i-frames is an invisible enough mechanic that I believe it shouldn't be adjusted. THAT SAID, it's not that bad. You can invest to first softcaps on everything else before investing into agility. You get more than enough souls to put some points into it later on and early on it's not very relevant.

Enemies disappearing for good is a neat mechanic that can create some interesting moments but I don't like that most of the engagement you have with the mechanic is "there's enemies right next to this bonfire so I have to kill them over and over until they stop spawning so I can continue to play the game." (Lower Brightstone Cove).

Lock on is leagues better, however it decides to swap randomly or pick targets I'm not even close to sometimes. It can be very annoying to miss an attack you'd have hit because lock on decided to pick wrong target.

Belfry Luna is an insult to this game.

Aldia is a very cool character. If he has no fans I am no longer alive.

That's all I think. Overall I enjoyed this game, it was fun and I think it was good in spite of some of the more frustrating things. This is still a pretty good game and it does some pretty cool things. It feels like it kind of forgot what made Dark Souls 1 so good. That one achievement is a proof of this, Old Firekeeper cutscenes are proofs of this. Various design decisions throughout the game prove it over and over. It feels like game hollowed and forgot like how characters in the game hollow and forget. Leftovers got twisted while trying to cover the gap.

Fantastic, yet extremely flawed game. It's very good, I love it to death, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ coming back to it can be a chore.

This game exists in discussions as either a reviled bastard child, or some misunderstood gem that deserves miles more praise than it's given. I fall on the latter-side, but dip my toes into the former when the game gets egregious, and it does so often. The common complaints have been beaten into the ground, but a lot of them maintain their validity so I'll briefly mention a few I agree with: cramming as many enemies into one arena as they can (and then removing i-frames during opening/closing actions), bosses that hit like a truck but are otherwise extremely easy to beat since their moves are so telegraphed, constant recycling of boss fights or reusing concepts from Dark Souls 1 while executing them poorly, having 99 heals that you can restock for pocket change, adaptability, absolutely revolting netcode, and some aesthetic choices that aren't my favorite (this complaint isn't so bad, I just have some differing tastes).

The most frustrating thing to me about Dark Souls 2 is primarily it's fan-base. I don't doubt they love the game or think it's their favorite, but I do doubt that they *really* think some of these mechanics or areas are as well-designed as they're claimed to be, especially with how often this game will throw you at hundreds of enemies and force you to take them all out one by one if you want a chance at reaching a fog gate or switch. Spells like Yearn or items like the alluring skull do exist to counteract this, but you can't expect every person playing to know about them, have to constantly restock skulls (that don't even work on most enemies), or alter their build just to use a spell that makes running around an area more manageable. It starts to feel like they overcompensated for strategies players would implement in DS1 to avoid runbacks being such a hassle, without fully considering that even beyond runbacks, there are far too many enemies present. And yet, DS2 fans tend to ignore these kinds of complaints because it's apart of the "charm" or it's the "nature of Dark Souls" to be extremely difficult, which I feel like misses a lot of what makes Dark Souls special.

DS2 tends to take on the brunt of "git gud" discussion, with a popular example being "just level up ADP!" despite ADP never being properly explained by the game. Beyond this, DS2 also gets claimed as an "underrated" game which...it isn't? DS2 had a rocky start at launch, but it was still very favorable for the most part. Even and especially now, the game is talked about all the time, and I hear praise for it from every corner of the Souls community. Maybe they mean "underrated Souls game", then? But that isn't true either considering Demon's Souls is, without a doubt, the most underrated Souls game. I've heard tons of people say that this game was their favorite, whereas a significantly slim amount have ever discussed Demon's Souls being the top 1 Souls game. If anything, I hear more complaints that DeS hasn't aged well, or that it's mechanics are dated far more than I hear them about DS2.

I love this game a lot, but I loathe the discussion around it. The game is, constantly, taken between two extremes. It's either complete and utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, or the best game ever created and I feel like the lack of nuance really hurts the game's legacy, arguably making it more controversial as time goes on for none of the good reasons like the game having strong PVP/co-op elements. Although, there are certainly issues with that, like the aforementioned netcode, invaders being unable to interact with a large part of the map, removal of invaders being able to phase through enemies, soul memory, etc.

TL:DR Game is fantastic, but has a lot of caveats that keep it from being among the best souls games. Discourse surrounding it has homogenized all discussion and it's a good idea to brush up on some of the game's obscure mechanics, while avoiding the discourse and formulating your own opinion that isn't marred by discussion that's about as cyclical as the Souls game's own Age of Fire.

Being a newcomer to Soulslikes and choosing to play Dark Souls 2 first was certainly a decision of all time. But now that I've beaten all 41 bosses (plus rebeating a couple bosses in NG+) I can confidently say that this is one of my new favorite games of all time... the music, the environments, and the characters are all extremely well crafted and give this sense of awe that is hard to describe with words. After beating my last boss, I got genuinely emotional when I left Majula for the last time.

The gameplay in the earlygame definitely frustrated me, but it taught me to take my time and be careful in new areas, as to avoid getting jumped by a group of enemies that I'm ill prepared to defend myself against. The raw feeling of hope and security upon finding a bonfire after exploring for what felt like ages is truly something I hadn't felt before in a game. While I don't think the bosses in Dark Souls 2 are particularly hard (especially now that I have bested Margit, The Fell Omen in Elden Ring who would definitely rank among the hardest bosses in DS2), they are enjoyable fights and thematically / strictly based on story they are phenomenal for the most part. I will never relive the feeling of beating the Dragonrider for the first time.

All in all I can't recommend this game enough. Just don't forget to level your Adaptability.

Not my first dark souls game but the first dark souls game I've beaten.

Overall, it was an alright experience. I played a dark hexer build with a rapier and managed to plough through the base game content quite easily mostly with hexes (i.e. dark orb). Previously I tried playing the game with a more traditional sword and shield build (warrior?) and remember getting my ass handed to me by many of the ganks in the game, so having a mostly ranged build definitely made the game a lot easier.

Towards the end of the base game my feelings about the game definitely started to drag a bit. Outside of 2-3 bosses that I fought many of them were total pushovers, and the risk/reward feeling of other souls-like games I played was not really present. Bonfires are never that far apart and some bosses are reskins of the previous games, which felt a bit cheap and lazy. There was some interesting exploration and loads of secrets as you can find in any souls like games that kept things interesting but outside of that, I didn't feel a really big draw to revisit most areas as most of them didn't feel particularly unique. I haven't touched the DLC's yet and am not sure at what point I will, but I have heard they are excellent so I will try and get around to them at some point.

The build variety and the amount of weapons/spells you can acquire is massive in number and I could see myself returning to the game years from now to try another build or covenant. I know for a fact there's lots that I still haven't seen yet.

Posted: July 8
This is definitely a step down from the first game but still a pretty great experience in many ways.

First of all, use the lighting engine mod, it makes the game look 20 times better.

The sound design, animation and music are overall good to incredible so nothing bad to say here.

You no longer get the incredible interconnected world design that made Dark Souls so special, nor the same grand sense of place and cohesion. Even so, most individual areas in this game are still relatively well crafted, although the way they link together often feels awkward and poorly planned. The location of Iron Keep, for example, is a meme for a reason. Enemy placement is also a major issue, especially in the Scholar of the First Sin Edition. It is frustrating because it feels like the developers did not fully grasp what made the difficulty of Dark Souls 1 so compelling until they worked on the DLC. Instead of clever, progressively more tense encounters, DS2 is full of constant ambushes and areas that force you to fight large groups at once. This is irritating because DS2's gameplay just is not designed for that style of combat and it really highlights its clunky sides.

Despite these problems, the world is significantly larger than in Dark Souls 1, and the hidden paths and rewarding sense of exploration are still very much present. The art direction remains fantastic too (tho less than DS1 to me), especially in the enemy designs and the DLC areas, which stand out as a clear step up from the base game in pretty much every way (showing that those same developers that made the base game were very much capable of doing some good work!).

There are more than forty bosses, but sadly most of them are quite underwhelming. I would say about 25 percent are just bad, another 25 percent are passable, around 30 percent are okay, and only about 20 percent are genuinely good to excellent. And almost all the best bosses come from the DLC. The infamous runbacks are some of the worst in the series. While I did not find any of them particularly difficult except for the first Smelter Demon runback, they are still far too long and far too common.

Like the first game, this one can be extremely obscure, and its lack of accessibility is still a major problem. I highly recommend not hesitating to use a wiki to understand the mechanics, check the suggested game progression route if you get stuck, or look up specific weapons and builds that might suit your playstyle. If you find yourself struggling, I suggest trying either a rapier for high DPS or a greathammer or greatsword for heavy poise damage (heavy R2 splat attack is just so good). I personally went with a greathammer (Old Knight hammer then Dragon tooth) for both exploration and bosses, and it was excellent.

Look up how two handing weapons works. Also, if you have at least one and a half times the minimum required stats for a weapon, you can power stance it with another compatible weapon which meets the same reqsuirements (it can be the same weapon, or another). To do this, equip a weapon in each hand and hold the two-hand button until your stance changes (triangle or Y on controller). Your character will use both weapons together when you attack with R or L triggers I don't remember. The game litterally never explains this... Power stancing two greatswords/greathammers can be especially fun :)
However, the strongest thing you can probably do is simply two-hand your weapon of choice and buff it with a spell like Dark Weapon or Sunlight Blade. Also, make sure you understand how adaptability and agility work, since they affect your invincibility frames, how quick you can heal or use a shield etc. , and read up on how poise works too because it is very different from DS1 and armor is much more useless here.

Lastly, I think this game (and most Dark Souls titles) is overpriced for what it is today. It is quite old now and, as I mentioned earlier, can feel pretty clunky. Personally, I would wait for it to drop below 20 euros, I got it for 10 a while back, and that's the sweet spot to me. Then again, keep in mind that I am a total cheapskate when it comes to game prices.

Good luck and have fun. This game is really not as bad as many people claim. It definitely has its problems and in many ways is less memorable than the first game, but it offers even more character customization, it is still beautiful to look at, and it is genuinely fun to play. Just be prepared for some truly questionable hitboxes.

Please keep in mind I have over 300 hours in the original cop of the game during this review.

This game is certainly not bad, and I can still have fun playing it. The ethics of SotFS specifically, the movement, the hitboxes, and the level design are all up for legitimate criticism. To be honest, some of the failures in only ONE of these areas are justification enough to not recommend the game, and so I would not argue this game is 'underrated.' If anything, the current discussion seems to allow for little to no criticism of this game, and that you are just a hater if you disagree that the game is a looked-over masterpiece.

Regardless--I tried revisting this game after Elden ring came out. I went on a sort of binge--playing Sekiro and Bloodborne for the first time, and then replaying the souls series. This is the only game I did not finish. The movement feels weird, in a bad way. I've come to learn that the game employs snap points by default, and is only disabled by editing the INI files (sorry console players). The hitboxes are actually quite awful as well, and hits that should hit completely miss; hits that should miss somehow hit you; spells and effects go through walls at random. This is what broke the game for me, and led me to quit, to be completely honest. The issues are more than that too -- The level design and cohesion of the world are lacking, and the game loves to hard-code in aggression such that you are constantly backtracking to a doorway to manage the abscess of mobs the game throws at you. It often feels like that the game thinks that if it can't make it challenging in an interesting way, just add more mobs so they can call it difficult. It is not like this is the only game with this problem: Bloodborne and Elden Ring often throw a ton of mobs at you too. But this is the first game that made me feel punished for even trying to play.

If you are interested in a compilation of evidence that would make these arguments better than I could, please watch the video here. It is not that I am a mindless hater of this game. Again, it can be fun at times, and has some good moments. But this video truly puts into words what I was already feeling, and why Dark Souls 2 has remained unfinished in my souls replaythroughs -- /watch?v=HR3hbaKTVBs&t=1140

Posted: July 7
As just a video game? Truly an awful experience through and through. As a Dark Souls game? The most flattering way I could describe it as is abysmal and utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. This game somehow fails in every possible metric. Please do not fall into the typical trap of "Oh, well I played Dark Souls and maybe even Dark Souls III, Bloodborne, and others. I've heard bad things, but I like Soulsborne games, let me give this one a try!" I am someone who tends to disagree with popular opinion on games, and always gives them a try. I really wish I didn't with Dark Souls II.

Dark Souls II completely betrays the philosophy of its predecessor's genius game design. In the original Dark Souls, if you messed up, it was entirely your fault; entirely a skill issue, and you are made very well aware of that. You could have approached the situation better, prepared better, or balanced your hits and dodges better. The game had its moments, but it was rarely unfair. On the other hand, Dark Souls II's idea of difficulty is not in creating good enemies, but shoving multiple of them into tight spaces. In fact, the real difficulty actually comes from the controls, which (if you get them to even work in the first place) somehow feel jankier and clunkier than the previous game. I cannot stress enough how often this failed and flawed design choice is used. Every area does it at least five times, and I further cannot emphasize enough how many enemies it shoves at you. You will enter a tiny room with limited space to move, let alone dodge, and quite literally 15-20 enemies will be thrown at you. It is the absolute bottom-of-the-barrel game design.

This is just miniscule complaint, and I point it out because it constantly persists throughout the game. But it pales in comparison to the myriad of others you can find online. No apparent path to follow, confusing obscure things to do for progression, messed up stats, non-existent i-frames, enemy damage far too high, as much stamina taken for one roll as a greatsword swing (literally), secret bonfires, bosses that are far easier than the regular enemies... at EVERY. SINGLE. STEP. WITHOUT FAIL, Dark Souls II takes the exact opposite decision of what would have made sense. It makes the game frustrating and unplayable, because it is not your fault as the player. The game is simply broken and does not work.

It immediately feels almost as if this game was directed by an entirely different person from the first one, and that's because it literally was. Genuinely, I don't think there was a single area that was redeemable. The worst part was just simply navigating the game in general, as well as each specific area. If Hitler were still around, I can't help but feel he'd shed a tear and grow emotional, because even he could not have conceived of something so cruel and torturous. I find it hard to give this game any score, and if I were to stretch it, then perhaps I could give it something in the realm of a 3/10: broken, infuriating, and downright unenjoyable.
