-Fighting hordes of enemies while a houndred snipers are shooting you.
-Gameplay that is somehow even slower than DS1, and yet some enemies move faster than ninjas with magical input readings.
-Hit boxes are often very questionable.
-Every barrel you touch explodes in your face.
-Game often doesn't know the difference between challenging and annoying.
-Bosses are either too easy or too hard, there's very few that are in between.
-The game can't properly mesh locations together, sometimes making zero sense with their design and interconnectivity.
-Cringey characters and dialogue.
-Setting up the controls for keyboard and mouse was a nightmare, and remains buggy to this day.

Even after all these wonderful treats, I gotta say that I still like this game. Despite it's shortcomings and missteps, it still provides an enjoyable dark souls experience and lots of variety in playstyle. Is a fun time(mostly) 7/10, but...
It was also kind of hard to level up my character with Shanalotte with just one hand.

I appreciate that there is a lot to criticise. Boss design, level design, pointlessly added attribute(s).

Moving on, I have a LOT of fun playing through this game. To me, it is still one of the better games of the Souls-like genre, largely thanks to the DLCs. The combat can feel very slow, but aggressive play helps by giving zones a fresher and more temporary feel.
If you do get stuck dying in the same area, no matter which one of the many available, this will get very old very quickly, however. This is most clear when you're fighting bosses, as the run-backs are effectively ALWAYS long.

You WILL die if you run past too many without a clear way ahead. Most of the game-play that people post sh*tting on DS2 is due to running past enemies. There's a lot to critique, but this is self-inflicted, and punishes the player by design.

Finding bonfires feels very good, as they're quite rare. Killing the bosses means you get to move past whichever area has been frustrating you.

The bosses are my favourite part of Dark Souls 2, so I'm glad that there are so many. *But* I'd have preferred fewer bosses for higher quality experiences.

The best content in Dark Souls 2 comes with the DLCs. Easily the most creative maps, the best designed enemies, and genuinely challenging and memorable bosses in the game. It's a blast playing through them, all of them. It serves to highlight how and where the base game missed the mark.

Posted: July 4
I wouldn't recommend this game to anyone who's getting into Souls games. As in, if this is your first time playing a Souls game, DON'T start with Dark Souls 2.
If you are interested in the series, save DS2 for last. You wouldn't be missing out on anything because in terms of the story and the lore, Dark Souls 2 is almost entirely separated from the rest of the series and is overall very inconsequential to the in-game universe. Think of it like a side episode.

1) Visuals and sounds:
- More advanced engine comparing to Dark Souls 1, but very poor lighting, color and texture work, making everything looks flat and weird.
- Art design is also much worse.
- Almost no notable soundtrack.
- Sound effects sounds lacking.

2) Difficulty:
- Easiest game in the series if you know what to do but otherwise hardest for beginners playing through blind. This is due to the game having a lot of "gimmicks".

3) Movement and animations:
- Floaty and weightless (almost like they are moving in space or underwater swinging foam weapons).
- Even though actual animation speed is fine, every action looks like there is a weird delay, which is very annoying when you are not yet used to it.

4) Combat:
- Almost mandatory stat called Agility but not very well explained.
- Many misleading hitboxes that are frustrating to deal with if you are a new player.
- Enemy placement feels cheap and spammy, usually putting huge amount of enemies into small spaces. Other games mostly balance this out by giving them low poise, low health and/or low aggression. Dark Souls 2 does none of this, even goes one step further by reducing damage of melee attacks that hit multiple enemies.
- Enemy placement and level design also feels very artificial, like the developers are just trying to troll us without caring much about immersion. This is fine in moderation, even funny and charming sometimes in other games, but Dark Souls 2 just does it too much in my opinion
- Enemies movesets are very simple to deal with and their AI are very stupid, sometimes entirely broken and they just stand there not doing anything or running around then stopping aimlessly.
- Visual desgins are not very memorable.

5) Bosses:
- Bosses soundtracks sounds boring and forgettable.
- Some of them are just some normal enemies put together.
- Suffer from the same simple movesets and poor AI like normal enemies, making them too easy.
- Suffer from the same enemy spam problems sometimes.

6) Level design:
- Very linear with some optional paths.
- The main path to the boss is usually very short.
- The optional paths is also really short and simple.
- Not much to explore and very hard to get lost in this game.
- A lot of annoying traps like random holes in the ground, collapsing floor, poison spitting statues, curse barrels that can't be destroyed from afar and enemy spams in small rooms.
- A lot of rooms and levels feel empty and fake.

7) Story:
- Opening cutscene does not give any information about the world and the main figures in it.
- Very inconsequential story, does not add much to main lore and universe.

Posted: July 4
I wish I could give a mixed review instead of good vs bad.

Before you read this, I feel the need to provide a bit of context just so you know that this is not just unjustified hate:
I played this game in 2019. This was my first souls game, and it being my first, it was my favorite. I went on to play Ds3, Elden ring and finally I played and finished Ds1 a week ago. I still considered this as my favourite game. Then I played again Ds2.
End of context.

Dark souls 2 is missing a lot of from what made ds1 as good as it is. For example the item descriptions. Ds2 definetly has item descriptions but in Ds1 item descriptions gave you hints to what you had to do or where you had to go. It never told you stuff directly but you then remembered another piece of context that made it all click. "This is the ring of mr stupid it has the shape of a RIGHT buttcheek" and then you remember someone saying "Mr stupid is missing a right butcheek" and you go "HOLY SH*T" If you've played Ds1 you know what I mean.

Another piece of great game desing missing can be seen within the world. You've already heard about Ds1's map interconnection, a small open world(-like) map, full of shortcuts and alternate paths you could choose. The hub just happens to be a peaceful part of the world where everyone hangs out. In Ds2 majula (the hub) is in the middle of everything and every path you take is closed off from one another. Once you finish a zone, you have no need to go back, unless you need an npc but if your build doesn't require it, the zone could dissapear and it would make no difference.

The story is not bad. While Ds1's story was berserk-like ds2's is more game of thrones-like (I have never watched game of thrones but it gives me the same vibes). It is not bad it's just handicapped because of bad game design and poor or non-existent visual story telling. It feels like the game is separate from the story. You're just going from room to room killing guys.

That is not necessarily bad in a game but this is (supposedly) AN RPG. It doesn't work well.

I am aware of the development hell this game went through and I see the vision they had. This game could've been up there with Elden Ring if creative liberty was allowed within the company. That's the most heartbreaking fact. Who or what can we thank for this?? Good ol' capitalism, encouraging greediness over a good product.

Now for the last part I'll mention some of the things this game did RIGHT, because when things are done RIGHT godamn they are RIGHT RIGHT.

1rst atmosphere: "but Mr epic gamer didn't you say that this game has poor/non-existent visual story telling?? Isn't visual story telling supposed to go hand in hand with atmosphere??" Correct child. The thing is; visual story telling in this game is inconsistent. Sometimes is there and sometimes it just isn't. Sometimes is great and sometimes it just isn't.

Spoiler warning:







2nd thing: Fashion-souls
There's 2 types of armor in ds1: Armor that increases your poise and mask dropped by pinwheel combined with anything else.
Ds2 introduced fashionable armors to the franchise. Without this game is hard to imagine we would have them in future games.

3rd: Power stance
If you don't know what power stance is, don't worry it's a hidden mechanic (not explained anywhere in the game) that basically adds a whole new moveset as long as you're wielding 2 weapons with the same type of attack. This makes your damage go "bonkers" (XD). This is what inspired weapon arts/ashes of war in future games.

4rth: weapon variety
In ds1 weapons boiled down to: "look it's the same type, but bigger??!!"
In Ds2 on the other hand, it seems like they were just throwing ideas to see what stuck, and godamn a lot stuck. They went crazy with them.
Also what opened the doors in future games for the possibility for Goofy/unusual/creative weapons

A lot of the good (and some bad) things about this game either opened the doors or were used as inspiration for future games. There's a lot of other things I didn't mention. But you can tell, after playing other fromsoft games, we wouldn't have them without Ds2.

Like I said I see the vision they had. What they were trying to do. It just cuts a deep wound within my heart knowing artists were not allowed to complete their craft. It's not bad, but it could've been great.

In conclusion: The first Dark souls is a GREAT rpg while Dark souls 2 is just a somewhat good but unfinished souls-like. If you care about consistency, game design and visual story telling. This game is NOT for you. But if you only care for a challenging boss run type game, you might enjoy it.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is the black sheep of the Souls series—and I say that with love. It’s a strange, punishing, often frustrating journey that feels both familiar and completely offbeat compared to the rest of the franchise. But if you’re willing to embrace its weird charm, there’s a gem hidden here.

The world design is more sprawling and disjointed than the tight interconnected maps of DS1, but it somehow works in its own chaotic way. Every door feels like a dare, every new zone an invitation to suffer. The enemy placements in Scholar (compared to the original release) are way more brutal, but also make you rethink how you approach each area. You’ll die. A lot. But you’ll adapt.

The hitboxes are (mostly) fair, the power stance system is fun for experimenting with dual weapons, and the build variety is huge. Want to be a hexing glass cannon? A tank with a giant door for a shield? Go for it.

Story-wise, it hints at tragic pasts, and world-building that makes you feel like you’re piecing together the ruins of something long dead and forgotten.

Is it perfect? No. Some boss designs are unforgettable, it will test your patience more than your skill. But none of that takes away from the strangely addictive atmosphere and challenge loop.

If you’re already a Souls fan and haven’t given DS2 a real chance, it’s worth revisiting. And if you’re new… prepare to die—but in the best possible way.

8.5/10
Unpolished in places, but unapologetically itself. A flawed masterpiece that deserves more love.

Speaking as someone who loved the first one, I call this one a massive success. The changes take some time to get used to, but it's well worth the time I've spent on it. Even though I don't like all the changes they made, I love the ideas I can see they had in making those decisions. It all has a feeling that everything will one day disappear and be done, things will one day run out for good, and all that used to be taken for granted or put on a pedestal will go away and be forgotten. So, perfect for a Souls game!

Things I like:
The stat rebalancing, each level doesn't increase in cost so much, and every level increases something useful in multiple areas. (Leveling every single stat gives you an extra point or two of health as a bonus, so you aren't dumping stats and seeing no result until the required level. And Attunement also adds a cast speed bonus.) Strange decision to make it now via NPC, what if you accidentally attack her?

You can buy multiple copies of every spell and stack them up to large numbers.

The increased ring variety is so nice, I get so many new options of things to equip.

Teleportation between bonfires from day 1! And they come with a nice little thumbnail.

(There's more but I can't think of it right now)

Things that I didn't like as much:

Hollowing takes your health away, resources are limited, and each enemy will disappear if you kill it too many times. I both like and dislike this, as it always pushes you forwards where DS1 let you hang around at any place you want, which is really nice to see the variety in locations, however it also can leave you stranded if you aren't careful. It's worth checking out the Covenant of Champions for a harder mode, as it also allows infinite enemy respawns.

I'm invaded multiple times a day, sometimes the same person 5 times in a row. I know I can block them temporarily, but this seems to be a really high number.

Don't let the haters fool you: this game is a gem. People who dislike this game usually blame it for either failing to be a carbon copy of Dark Souls 1 or just because of annoying shenanigans which are indeed annoying but don't really ruin the overall experience as much as they make out to do.
My piece of advice is to just play the game for what it wants to be and not for what it isn't, and you'll see all the marvel it has got to show beyond its the rough edges.
Treat the game's bosses as encounters amidst your journey, each with its own gimmick, and not as if they should all be epic showdowns of might and power, and you'll eventually start to appreciate each one of them.
Try not to question too much the delusional geography of the world, but always be curious about what comes next as if you're exploring a continent yourself, and enjoy all the different locations the game has to offer, each one with its own atmosphere.
Give a shot to the main story and NPC quests, as they mostly speak about different approaches to life and death and are not as empty as people try to make them out to be just because they don't always finish in a dramatic epilogue.
And lastly, I beg of you to experiment with everything the game gives you without being peer-pressured into playing with a standard "veteran-hardcore" playstyle just to brag about how good you're at the game online. This title is full to the brim of unique weapons, incantations and consumables, each with its own unique use or area of expertise, and basically anything (maybe with the exceptions of whips) is a viable choice, making it a shame to just stick to the usual ultragreatsword-only no-armor no-shield no-spells no-summons no-fun as all those wannabe pro push you into doing.
And remember: don't give up, Skeleton!

Dark Souls 2 is quite overwhelming, and I would say that it is the most difficult game in the trilogy. The levels are densely packed with enemies and the boss runbacks are extremely long. Those runbacks can become even longer if you missed a bonfire. Recently, I have been playing a lot of soulslike games and this is the only one that made me rage quit several times. The combat is quite unintutive and the hit boxes are extremely weird, moreover there is a hidden character stat that governs your ability to roll and it is never well explained, which makes the game very challanging from the start.

The boss fights are generally not that interesting and they are not that unique. I had an impression to fight the same ennemy over and over again, sometimes with duo or even a trio variations. However, there were some really cool bosses like the dark lurker or the fume knight. The giant spider and the scorpion lady from the base game are also quite good. It was an interesting decision to give the players an extremely easy final boss fights, for the base game. It was like the dev team was telling you: "You have suffered a lot to get here, now it is time to relax, play the game a bit more and enjoy the final cutscene".

VIsually the environment feels like a dream or some sort of hallucination, for example the when you see Majula for the first time. Probably, a nightmarrish kind of dream. I believe that it is a survival horror game with some elements of rpg. In fact, the constant invasions contibute to this feeling.

If you have already played DS1 and you have extra 200h to spare, then go for it! Otherwise, I would not recommend this game. So... Did I enjoy the game? Absolutely, and after playing DS3 I started to appreciate this game much more.

Awesome and extremely over-hated game, most of the popular complaints on the internet are completely backwards. The only genuinely offensively bad feature was Soul Memory screwing you over when you want to PVP/Coop in lategame, but despite that the multiplayer holds up way better after a decade than DS1/DS3, its much more lively with more creative builds around.

If youre playing on keyboard+mouse, you need to turn on "Disable double clicking" every time you boot up the game if you dont want huge input delay

Its a very different deal than DS1 with its ups and downs compared to it, it doesnt have as great as a world (mostly branching paths instead of interconnection) and atmosphere and sense of mystique, but it has a lot more action, more tricky fights, more build variety, more side activities and all

People constantly complain about hitboxes, but it has the best hitboxes of the trilogy : I played with almost no points in ADP and it was so much more engaging to actually dodge the enemy attacks, have to roll in the correct direction, stuff like that. It feels like the game was actually designed around that and its easier than it sounds. There are exceptions like ancient dragon, but that goes for every game, just less so in this one. And if you dont like it, simply level ADP.
Just watch out for Frigid Outskirts because you cannot dodge these ice stallions without luck or i-frames.

People constantly claim it has the worst bosses of the series, but Fume Knight, Alonne, Smelter Demon, Ruin Sentinels, Sinh, Pursuer, Looking Glass Knight, Mytha (with the poison floor left on), among others, were all really neat, specially when they made me actually be mindful about the direction i roll when trying to evade attacks. Its a satisfying middleground that has more straightforward action than DS1's bosses, while still keeping interesting gimmicks and arenas, instead of being repetitive rollslop like DS3. And actually having to evade attacks by dodging the hitboxes is infinitely more engaging than, say, rolling forward against every single attack Sif throws out. Once again, if you dont like it, just level ADP

The powerstancing is amazing, left hand weapons having a full moveset is great, the jumping feels much better than DS1. parrying feels the best in this game compared to others, among other stuff, makes it have a great gamefeel. People call it clunky but i didnt find that in the slightest
I really like how it not only gives you much more upgrade materials, but also has much more oppressive enemy placements/ganks that encourage you to think more and have a wider arsenal, level up a lot of different weapons and experiment with stuff. So many more neat ways to play compared to DS1/DS3, so many more neat interactions like the effects of being drenched, more encouragement to interact with PVP/PvE, better ranged stuff, more interesting consumables, that parry with the ultra-greatswords and giga clubs, i like lifegems, i like the new hollowing mechanics, just so much neat stuff around!!1

Bonfire Ascetics and enemies running out of respawns are two wonderful features that i wish more games had copied

Also it has the coolest dragon form 🐉
good luck grinding those scales though..

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin isn't exactly holier than thou, itself.

Story
Set within a crumbling fantasy land known as Drangleic, players take on the role of an undead, a human cursed with a sort of immortality that saps away at both their physical and mental vitality until they are one day reduced to mindless, bloodthirsty husks seeking to devour the souls of anything they can manage to kill. Your character has come to Drangleic at the suggestion of a crone who describes it as a place where souls may mend the mind of the undead. From there, a small pack of yet more crones explaining that everyone who comes to this land has come to break the curse, and send you on along to the kingdom. Meeting another woman, she asks if you're to be the next monarch and then declares quite suddenly that she shall remain by your side and assist you in your endeavor to seek powerful souls that might help you. There after, much of the game's story revolves around trudging through the remnants of Drangleic and sometimes the remnants of yet older kingdoms that fell before it; learning bits and pieces of their histories here or there without ever really getting a full picture. For it's part, the Drangleic plot is at least a little more interesting. Just how it was founded is a little obscure and perhaps even contradictory to the state of the land itself, but it all centers on the King of the land and his findings on the essence of souls. There's a neat bit of eldritch drama at play, but also a few narrative shattering contradictions mostly related to the timing of things. By the end of it all, the game feels like it has raised for more questions than it answers about its own world. It has some interesting to ponder ideas, but many of them are mired in repetitive and sometimes contradictory writing.

Dark Souls II is a third person action RPG that emphasizes skill and timing as much as it does character build and gear selection. Not so much a difficult game as it is rigidly punishing to failure, DS demands a player's full attention as they carefully make their way past traps and deadly opponents. Some baddies are certainly more challenging than others, but at the end of the day, there are myriad of ways a player can improve their own odds if they come up against a wall.
Players can progress their characters in many classic directions: The massive strong man wielding an axe larger than he is. The dexterous fencer flowing around his enemies and prodding them to death. The all powerful mage obliterating opponents from afar. And that's to say nothing of archery, clerics with their god given miracles, flame wielding pyromancers, or even the many in between classes that harness multiple styles to overcome differing obstacles. Even darkness itself can be harnessed to cast powerful hexes. There are a multitude of weapons throughout the game with a massive variety of different move sets and even special powers or hidden effects. Armor ranges from delicate and dainty silks to stone plate several inches thick. A weight system helps mediate how quickly your character can dodge around enemy attacks while carrying all this gear; encouraging players to either build themselves into slow but sturdy tanks or light and nimble fighters whose entire goal is to remain untouched in combat. Or you can even be a combination of the two somewhere in between. Attack animations are often committed acts. Once you start swinging your sword, your character is going to follow through, so you want to make sure you pick your windows of attack wisely and avoid being greedy, lest you suffer an enemy's reprisal. Certain enemies can be stabbed in the back if you managed to get around them or have their attacks swatted aside for you to then hit them with brutal, high damage riposte attacks. A plethora of damage types helps keep players on their feet both in regard to incoming damage, and how resistant some enemies might be to the damage you're throwing out at them. The world of Dark Souls II is sprawling, varied, and feels unfinished more often than not. Exploration is a key means to finding new gear, upgrade materials, or even entire areas hidden from the main path, but each zone of the game is a largely linear affair that ends in sending you back to square one to travel off in a different direction. Some levels are certainly more interesting to explore than others, but never really escape a feeling of artificiality. The game world is populated with a huge cadre of enemies too numerous to describe. They come in every shape, size, and color imaginable, each with their own attacks, patterns, and AI tendencies. On top of that, there are a great many bosses in the game, perhaps too many. Rare is the opponent who feels like a genuinely unique and interesting creature to fight, with many standing out specifically because of how limiting or even obnoxious they are to combat. The game certainly hosts a few interesting surprises, but perhaps its greatest downfall is the execution of its mechanics. Everything possesses a strangely floaty jank, from moving through the environment to swinging most of the weapons. Combat isn't bad so much as it is often uninteresting, cumbersome, and repetitive.
The game also features a unique online element that helps build a sense of community and pushes back a little on the isolating atmosphere. The ghosts of other players on their own journey will briefly fade into and out of existence. Bloodstains on the ground can be touched to witness a player's final seconds of life, often leading either witnessing a comedic end or a warning of danger to come. Players can write messages both helpful and misleading using predefined words and phrases that will then appear spontaneously in the worlds of others. Summon signs can be set on the floor, allowing others to bring you into their world so that you may assist them in toppling a challenge that's giving them trouble. You can even be invaded by ne'er-do-well players seeking to kill you.

DSII is a never ending roller coaster ride of unappealing, half baked locales, boss designs, or narratives contrasted against some genuinely excellent scenes, songs, and ideas. Visually the game never manages to make the world feel like a cohesive place as you travel from one place to the next through meter long tunnels that inexplicably stretch miles, invisible elevators that lead up into previously invisible mountains above the clouds, or just using loading screens to teleport you to a destination, and for every level that feels like a truly completed whole, there are five that feel unfinished and uninspired to boot. It often feels more like a theme park than a real place. Almost every facet of the game suffers this same peak of quality moments standing above what is largely less than interesting content beneath it. For every incredible song there are several uninteresting ones. For every unique character or creature design, there are tons of less wonderful ideas littered about. As with the gameplay and story, it's never outright bad, but it's never consistently or even constantly excellent either.

i can recommend this game only as a dark souls fan.

dark souls 2 is weird as hell and beneath everything is a game in crisis. why is there a vague nordic aesthetic going on? why is this chariot boss the worst thing to exist in the souls series? why does stone trader chloanne look like that???

and yet, i kind of love it. combat is at its most meticulous in the series here, focused far more on spacing and baiting rather than dodging. torches and dynamic lights are an incredibly cool mechanic, and environments in this game feel unusually immersive and atmospheric (Forest of the Fallen Giants and Heide's Tower of Flame in particular-- really, why do these locations have such a strong hold over me?).
this game, so lacking in an ego, so desperate to follow in the footsteps of its predecessor, ends up creating an entirely new, unique identity for itself.

it's certainly not the best in the series. i don't even know if it's good. i haven't finished it yet, but i still want to recommend it simply for it being an odd experience. this game lulls me into a strange, feverish, meditative peace, such that all its flaws become a naturalistic part of the experience.

Posted: June 25
Things I liked:
-the story
-the gameplay
-bonfire ascetics

Things I didn't like:
-the lack of cloud saving
-Adaptability
-somethings need to be tweaked (visual bugs)
-lack of settings options
-certain areas suck

I'd give this game a 8/10. I think this game is very overhated. It definitely wasn't perfect and I felt like it overstayed its welcome sometimes, but I enjoyed my time with this game a lot. Dark Souls II has a reputation for being the "bad one" and out of the three, it probably is (I haven't started Dark Souls III yet). I feel like that reputation is well-known. I know people who are not into the souls-like games that know about that reputation. I think people shouldn't go into this game with that mindset. I personally had a great time with this game. I don't think there were any bosses as annoying as Bed of Chaos in this game, but there were a good bit of frustrating or stupid ones. Ancient Dragon and Fume Knight were very hard or frustrating. Another boss that stands out to me is the Demon of Song, but not for any good reasons. This boss fight isn't the hardest and it is pretty easy, but his gimmick makes him kinda annoying.

There were things I wish I knew about before my first playthrough:
1. Illusory walls activate by using the activate button, rather than activating after being attacked like in other souls games.

2. Adaptability: a new stat that was introduced in this game and hasn't returned since. It basically increases the amount of I-frames you get. The stat does other things, but that is the most important part.

3. Burn the windmills. In the Earthen Peak, there is a boss that has an arena in a poison pit. This pit will eat through your health at a decent pace, not unbearable but also not fun. This pit will also heal her. Thankfully she is pretty weak to fire and I have read that there is a summon sign for a character that is a pyromancer nearby. Thankfully you can burn the big windmill in the area to remove most of the poison from her arena. I did not learn about the windmill until I had beaten the final boss and started the DLC. I am slightly embarrassed about that, but you live and you learn

Weakest in the series. Has some nice game-play mechanics, like Torches, Statues, Secret gates etc. along with very frustrating ones like Adaptability and Weapon Degradation and god-forbid mob-style enemies. When you introduce so much enemies at ones, you should make it fluid like Bloodborne. But it is just the opposite. You feel like a slug getting crushed with even tiny animals in the Majula. The weapon, equipment and spell variety is rich but the game is so linear with no memorable quest-lines that it does not feel go to replay. Environments, nearly most of them, are magnificent and delight to explore. Use lightning mod and have a near next-gen experience in some scenes. Good game, could be much better with better enemy positioning, richer quest-lines and more non-linearity.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is a remastered and enhanced version of the original game that offers a tougher, more refined experience. It features improved graphics, reworked enemy placements, and all previously released DLC content, making the world feel more unpredictable and challenging. The changes add fresh tension even for returning players, forcing new strategies and approaches. While the story and mechanics can still feel less cohesive compared to other Souls titles, the overall package is rich with content and atmosphere. For both newcomers and veterans, Scholar of the First Sin is a demanding but deeply rewarding journey through a dark and mysterious world.

They changed bumper and trigger controls with no option to rebind in-game.

This game feels like a constant gotch-ya. Ambushes, Doors, and Runbacks are just some of the Intentional design decisions that hinder your gameplay. As well as that dying punishes the player with a constant max-health reduction.

The idea of DarkSouls for me is constantly running your head into something until you find the path that allows you to master a fight, to overcome something that seemed impossible earlier. The punishment on your deaths make these attempts progressively more difficult and requires you to use a consumable item (or an alter that isn't unlocked to mid-late game) to return to human form and get your maximum health back.

I want to like this game, but there were times I would get up and just leave my game running because I was astounded by "Why the heck is this set up the way it is."

Dark Souls 2 feels really different from DS1 and DS3. The world is much bigger and sometimes confusing. Some areas are horrible and some ideas are just bad, but there are also great parts that work really well. I liked the story a lot and it’s the first one in the series where I kind of understood the story on my first playthrough. Places like Majula are legendary with its theme song. The game can be unfair and unbalanced, and some of the design is really weird, but if you play it with an open mind, it’s a solid challenge and worth finishing.

I left this game on my first try but then came back, and understood how it should be played and it was really enjoyable and unique experience, so if you were struggling like me try to not rush some areas. Hardest fromsoftware game for me. 8,5/10

Posted: June 21
My first stop of my journey to play through and experience every dark souls game either again or for the first time, I started here as I had never played it before. And all I had heard was the most mixed bag of opinions when it comes to this game and if it is fun, or good. And I can say with full chest that the game is great fun!

Class Mechanics
I played through as a sorcerer, as I plan to for every souls game I play this time around. The spell diversity and the way that gear really played a role in making my build work felt really cool! Bonfire ascetics to get multiple stacks of the spells I liked to cast felt super intuitive and nice.

Bosses
The boss design in this game was a bit of a mixed bag, but every one of them felt fair to me, granted I used the NPC summons that this game gives you, and in a way it felt intended, so many quests were locked behind these summons, and I don't think it particularly hurt my experience in this game, and bosses without summons were still good fun! I think the standout fights of this game are Vendrick, Fume Knight, and Smelter Demon(s). They have a rhythm to them that feels rewarding to learn. I had to no hit Vendrick and that was very rewarding to learn and play with!

I beat my final boss Sir Alonne at SL 180, and stopped at 200 post game just to feel like I completed my build, I kept under 30% equip load and maxed out at 32 Vigor (Played most the game with it at 5 lol). 110 Agility made the dodging all feel very intuitive and great! I love this games stat system, it felt great to try and squeeze everything out of it, 80 int and 94 att made it feel like i got to trade some health for an insane diversity and power in my spells!

The zones between the bosses are some of the most challenging parts of this game, managing my spell counts, managing my Estus charges (Of which I had 7 at +4 at the end) all felt like each zone was a bit of a puzzle to get through, and while I had my fair share of teeth grindingly difficult zones most of them were very enjoyable! I struggled most as a baby sorcerer running through No-Man's Wharf trying to get to the vendor LOL.. Sometimes the enemy density felt insane, but I think being a ranged character made all the archers less cumbersome.

In all I REALLY loved Dark souls 2 and compared to Bloodborne and Elden Ring I think I would place this as tied for second favorite with Elden Ring. Give this game a try if you haven't already! Fromsoft really knows how to make a good game!!! Peak Souls 2!!

Posted: June 21
Completed 100%
Overall grade : 8.5/10

Dark Souls 2 is the ambitious sequel of the renown Dark Souls. It had innovated on quite a few mechanics of it's predecessor while also suffering from the colloquially dubbed "B-team syndrome". In other words it was being made alongside Bloodborne which was clearly the Director's focus. That being said the game is not by any means bad, just different.

PROS :
- World building : The world feels much vivid and colorful compared to the other dark souls games. The main hub Majula is famous for having a breathtaking horizon. The levels are all quite unique and different from one another, even when moving from one level to the next.
- Story : Rich and interesting story of a king who tried to stop this endless cycle of linking the fire. While you can read the item descriptions as usual there are lots of NPCs that will tell you the juicy details of the main plot.
- Equipment and builds : This game has tons of items. You can collect to your heart's content. When interacting with other players I have rarely seen 2 people using the same build. Fire, Magic, Dark, Lightning, weapons big and small, take your pick.
- Creative innovation : This game added tons of new things to the souls series, such as useful and interesting consumables, ability to "power stance" 2 weapons of the same type, the ability to send only 1 part of the map to the next new game cycle, open secret doors or create traps for invaders with a new item etc.
- 3 Cool DLC : Visit lost kingdoms from legends to collect the crowns of their deposed kings and forge a new destiny. In the usual DS fashion the DLCs have the most interesting bosses and items. However this time they added even more. Every DLC has a cool new mechanic, either solving puzzles with your bow, going through the same areas that have changed because of your actions or even interacting with intelligent NPC invaders

CONS :
- Overall clunkyness and lack of agility in your character that we are used to in modern games. You will fall off that narrow passage because of a bad roll. The larger enemies might hit you when you thought you dodged.
- Soul Memory : They added a feature that records all the souls you have collected on your journey. This has one use and that is to allow you to skip some main bosses. However it uses this instead of your level for matchmaking with other players. After finishing my platinum trophy I was having trouble finding people to play with or fight. It doesn't make sense to punish people for playing your game more.
- Enemy density : Some bosses and areas are infamous for having terrible, long run-backs with annoying enemies that are just as likely to end you as the boss is. Luckily if you defeat them 12 times they will disappear.

Minor Cons :
- Useless stats : There are stats for helping you dodge better and wearing more equipment which are part of your kit by default or part of other stats in other souls games. This makes some people disappointed at lower levels because of this forced investment.
- Hollowing : Dying makes you lose maximum health and turns your character into a zombie. In order to reset it you need a semi-rare consumable. Personally I like my character to look pretty.

I hope my review of this game will give it a better chance to receive some love from the fandom and newcomers alike. It has a bad reputation that I think is undeserved and is a valuable link in the evolution of the souls games.

Posted: June 18
Dark Souls II may very well be my favorite fromsoft game. However, it has its reputation as the worst modern one because reasons:

- Very slow and punishing combat, often times having terrible hitboxes that are much larger or last longer than they seem combined with terrible i-frames (thank you adaptability). This is carried from Dark Souls 1, which had similar issues, admittedly being overall worse in 2. Players will find themselves with less health than they think, as sometimes the game seems to take HP without the character flinching. In spite of that, the addition of powerstancing and the great variety of enemies and weapons can make the game very satisfying if worked with. In general, you cannot expect this game to play like Dark Souls 3 or Elden Ring, you can only panic roll four times in a row in the endgame and you won't recharge any until after half a second. It can really only make sense if you played Dark Souls 1 and liked it.

- Enemies are often placed deliberately to trap players in inescapable situations if they do not always proceed carefully. These enemies will pursue players to the ends of the earth. This is the primary element of the runbacks Dark Souls 2 is infamous for. In general, players shouldn't consider runbacks as "runbacks" and more like playing the level to get back there, as it is almost impossible to get back to Smelter Demon or Executioner's Chariot without fighting anybody. That being said, they may need to learn routing and movement techniques for some of the worse runbacks in the endgame (absolutely no one is fighting their way to Sir Alonne or Blue Smelter Demon. ts bs asl vro). It can be extremely frustrating, especially as the primary counterargument by blind DS2 defenders is to despawn the enemies. Fellas, don't waste your time killing the same enemy 12 times. It happens naturally. There's no defense for the placement. It just feels great to open shortcuts and outsmart enemy groups. Beware of Heide's Tower of Flame, Iron Keep, Shrine of Amana, etc.

- Bosses are boring. I dunno, that's just the way it worked. Fromsoft started going full boss encounter mode with Bloodborne and really turned up the heat in Dark Souls 3 with the faster gameplay. Dark Souls 1 and 2 are stuck with mild encounters by comparison. In addition, Dark Souls 2 has a few more dumb gank bosses, and is criticized for having mostly humanoid entities for bosses (the whole guy in armor thing, though some don't really have armor) which is true. I didn't find that particularly boring, but in general don't play this game expecting high-octane boss encounters.

- Players can be hit out of interactions. No excuse. It was experimental. You can get hit out of the fog wall or lever animations that are excessively dramatic. To my knowledge you can't be hit out of a riposte animation but you take significantly reduced damage. Still annoying.

- Not one interconnected world like Dark Souls 1. I don't really care, but indeed, the world kind of looks like a snowflake from above, with the center at Majula and each "final area" being the third in the path. Its not quite as interesting as 1, but its not like 3 did any better. That said, you do get a lot of good rewards for exploring the levels. There's a lot of unique interactions and illusory walls that you can use for shortcuts and loot. Also, you can burn the windmill.

- Soul memory is not really a good multiplayer system. The game does not matchmake you based on your weapon upgrades or level, it does it based on how many souls you have collected over the entire save file. This makes it hard to stay meaningfully with the otherwise fantastic multiplayer as you collect more souls than your peers. The game introduced a bandaid soul-ution with the Agape Ring, which makes you gain zero souls at all, available from Straid in the Lost Bastille (petrified dude blocking the door to a bonfire in a cell). Dark Souls 2 also shows you which places have active multiplayer in your soul memory range on the bonfire travel screen with orange borders.

- The story isn't directly connected to Dark Souls 1 or 3, not explicitly. It is true that Dark Souls 3 was Miyazaki's intended narrative for a sequel to 1, but this does not mean that Dark Souls 2 is meaningless and doesn't fit. For those that are confused, Dark Souls 1 takes place at the end of the beginning of time, while Dark Souls 3 takes place at the end (kinda). Dark Souls 2 takes place somewhere in the middle between those thousands of centuries and in a completely different place (no, Earthen Peak nor Heide are not Anor Londo), and primarily examines the impact of the Curse of the Undead upon normal human kingdoms and the role of the Abyss long after the defeat of Manus in Dark Souls 1. The update to Dark Souls 2 that brought the Scholar of the First Sin edition (with different placements of stuff, bundled with the DLC) introduced Aldia, the single best character in the Soulsbornering games, indisputable. Indeed, you won't really find any direct follow ups to events in Dark Souls 1 like Dark Souls 3 did, but 2 shares a different perspective with unique moments and themes. The connection from Aldia to Prince Lothric makes Dark Souls 3 way more interesting.

I think its pretty fun, enough to write all this.

Don't listen to Cartarian or the haters, Dark Souls 2 is a good game. You just have to take 20 years in every area, walking slowly and looking behind you, keeping eyes open at every waking moment to check for the 200 enemy ganks that may jump at you or get up from the ground. But don't worry you can deal with this by backtracking and slowly taking them out from afar one at a time. Don't you dare attempt to run to the boss area again if you die, as you may get railed from behind by those enemies at the start (yes i know you have invun towards the end of the animation :) ).

Jokes aside, I think this is not a bad game, it is simply the worst soulsborne game. And it is a game that forces it's players to play in very different ways than a lot of people may traditionally play these games. I do think the enemy placement is pretty egregious and it is the main 'difficulty' of this game and outright not fair a lot of times, but it is very fun to play this game slowly and very carefully. If lifegems were not a thing in this game I think I would have maybe quit this game many a time as I definitely needed those countless lifegems while exploring the areas of this game. The bosses are pretty uninspiring and boring and way too easy a lot of the times in comparison to the actual areas. Man in armour x 200 that you just strafe their attacks to the left/right for 90% of the time and counterattack. Makes me wonder if they actually tested their boss fights before releasing the game. A couple of noteworthy exceptions are Fume Knight from the DLC and sinh from the other DLC....which are actually the only really times I had exceptional fun in this game.

Controls are also very jank, 8 way targeting system results a lot of the time in missed backstabs, forward momentum swings going in between enemies when directly facing them and all sorts of stuff. Will happen a lot to you depending on what kind of weapon you decide to use.

It is sad really as this game has a lot of really cool mechanics that I wish they would bring back such as bonfire ascetics, an actual fun New game plus system, as well as powerstancing (which is somewhat brought back in elden ring but not to the same degree as DS2). That being said this game I firmly believe is a rushed mess and littered with annoying enemy placement to make the game 'difficult' for it's audiences as they seem to think that's what makes soulsborne games fun for people and not the amazing environments, epic boss battles, and interesting enemy design. Something that you can see they recognised and fixed in future games (The main teams result in Bloodborne shortly after, as well as Dark Souls 3 when it gets released later).

Games still fun just be prepared to die a lot to ganks, annoying enemy placements, environmental traps (looking at you Gutter) and all sorts of outright jank.

Just beat the game and all of the dlc's and i can't say i enjoyed it. The game is extremely clumsy. First of all, the hitbox on many enemie's attacks is very misleading and it is extremely notable on bosses. Secondly the heals-flasks on the game are extremely punishing to use because of how slow they are and how slowly they heal you. Thirdly, i think the fact that you need to level adaptability to increase the immunity frames on rolls is ridiculous considering that this is a game that should promote rolling to avoid attacks. Also, the fact that DYING lowers your maximum hp is insane. Lastly the most important thing that makes this game unplayable is the stamina bar. I do not understand why every single action you do whether that is rolling, attacking or even running consumes so much stamina. The recovery speed of stamina is abysmal even when you are using a ring that improves it and that makes fighting enemies in general and especially bosses extremely challenging since you always need to care about your stamina bar more than you should.

That being said DS2 has some very good aspects about it as well. Firstly, the scenery of every map and the map in itself are extremely beautiful and well made. There are smart puzzles and mazes in many areas which make the game more immersive and enjoyable as well as secret areas that make you want to explore every map all the way. There are a lot of items that range from healing to empowering yourself that can make the game easier when needed and there is an abundance of weapons and armor that you can use.

In conclusion, DS2 is a game that is more slow paced in comparison to its successors (namely elden ring) and a lot harder as well (other than the boss fights) that is more suited to players that enjoy and search for "unfair" difficulty. That being said considering that it is not the first game of the genre (DS1 exists as well) i find many of its flaws inexcusable.

I was given this game for free and paid $100 dollars for beating it, and I would still say I was ripped off.

I would like to preface this review with a few things of note:





Dark Souls 2 is a terrible, miserable, spiteful experience. The closest thing that I could compare it to is ‘hostile architecture’, a practice in which public spaces are designed to be unpleasant to remain in for long periods of time. So many design decisions, both intentional and accidental, come together to create a gameplay experience that comes across as malicious and hateful towards the player, practically begging them to put the controller down and stop playing. It truly astounds me how little fun I had in my nearly 40 hours of gameplay, and had there not been a monetary incentive to continue playing I would have quit very, very early into my playthrough.

Before I slam into this game with the visceral hatred that the developers of this game must have had for their fans, I will list a few positive things about this game.







These few points were the only parts of my playthrough that I can admit to having enjoyed or liked. I will now discuss the much, much longer list of things that I hated about this game.













Unless you are a die-hard From Software fan and want to experience everything they have released, stay far, far away from this game. There is almost no joy to be had playing this game.

Dark Souls 2 is the only game in the souls franchise that actually fits the stereotypes: counterintuitive and unfair.

All of the souls games are relatively easy once you understand the core gameplay loop and combat elements. Even DS2 is easy in hindsight, but its so draining while playing the game for the first time.

Exploration was not fun like DS1, runbacks are a pain in the butt (often harder than the actual boss fights), and when you finally get to the area boss (which is normally the highlight of the game) you are rewarded with either a boring damage tank or 2+ person fight that drags on for a long time.

You have to spec ADP for I frames. Bro you have to burn the windmill. Runbacks drain all patience before you even get to the boss room. Nice hitbox. Enemy agro distance.

THE END CREDITS ROLL THROUGH TWICE: ONE FOR SOTHS AND THE ORIGINAL GAME.

My least favorite souls game by far. Play DS3 instead.

Ah DS2 where do I even begin. Before I even touched a souls game I heard how terrible this game was and how you should never touch it and you should skip it. I came to the decision after playing Elden Ring to play all the souls games and that I wont skip any. So coming fresh off of DS1 Remastered I started playing DS2 ignoring the recommendation of skipping to DS3. After playing it all the way through I learned why so many people say its a terrible game and how many issue it has.

The hit boxes are crazy, ADP is stupid, way to many enemies in some areas, worst run backs OAT, random overtuned enemies, WORST invader NPC (I HATE Forlorn), Iron keep, and Shrine Amana. But it also has a lot of good things such as directional attack inputs, Mejula, the scenery, the diversity of weapons, the amazing armor variety, the despawing of enemies after killing them enough times, the lore, SOME of the bosses, bonfire ascetic, and area differences with NG+.

These are some of the good things I can name off the top of my head that makes me unable in good faith call DS2 a bad game. In conclusion DS2 isn't as bad as people say but it definitely isn't without its glaring issues. Don't listen to the people that say skip it, DS2 is a worth experience.

Dark souls 2 isn’t a bad game by any means, but it definitely feels like a downgrade compared to the first. The level design is often questionable, if you've played it, you'll probably know what I mean. Areas sometimes feel disconnected or confusing rather than interlinked like in DS1.

Enemy placement is another issue. There are frequently too many mobs crammed into tight spaces, which makes traversing the area more annoying and uninteresting than challenging. Unlike in DS1, where enemy placements were better planned, DS2 just feels like the game is spamming enemies and that's the only method the game actually becomes challenging.

Boss designs aren't terrible but are also kinda meh. Some fights are recycled, like the "old dragonslayer" which is literally just Ornstein but reskinned, or the belfry gargoyles, which are just copies of the bell gargoyles but their method of making it a challenging boss fight is by making you fight 6 of them, wow isn't that amazing, so creative of them!. These reused bosses feel lazy and not very original.

That said, the game does still have moments of nice atmosphere, and some satisfying combat at times. It’s just not as polished as DS1.

Overall not a bad game, could have some improvements done but still very enjoyable.

Dark souls 2 soft is a unique game a place where it doenst take the same land of the first game but on a distant land with the same curse while the king got tricked by a dark abyss wifu the i found this game amazing and always coming back to it by replaying the game or playing the game NG+ here are my pro and cons.
Pros
Huge amount of content: Tons of weapons, armor, areas, bosses, and secrets.
Flexible build options: More diverse character creation and leveling systems.
Better co-op tools: Soul memory system aside, multiplayer connectivity was improved over DS1.
NPC questlines and interactions: Some of the most complex and memorable NPCs in the franchise.
NG+ mechanics: Actually changes enemy placements and adds new items—unique to this game.

Cons
Inconsistent level design: Transitions between areas sometimes feel disconnected or illogical.
Enemy placement can feel cheap: Scholar edition adds more mobs in frustrating positions.
Hit detection and animation jank: Combat doesn't always feel as tight or weighty as other Souls titles.
Soul Memory: This system, which tracks total souls gained for matchmaking, can punish high-level players unfairly and complicates PvP balance.
Weaker boss designs (in parts): Some bosses feel repetitive or underwhelming compared to series standards.

Final Verdict: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (8.5/10)
Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin isn’t perfect, but it’s full of content, secrets, and replay value. The large world, flexible combat, make it worth trying. If you can look past some flaws, DS2 gives a unique and rewarding experience. It may not be everyone’s favorite, but it’s definitely one of the most interesting games in the series.

Having played ~100 hours of nonstop Nightreign over the last couple of days and meeting the Dukes Dear, Dear Freja for the first time in 10+ years I was hit with a wave of severe nostalgia.
Its time the unloved child of the Dark Souls-series gets a modern retelling that redeem the game in the eyes of the entire community!


What we all *should* want and need from this:

- Reimagined Ultimate Directors Cut Reimagined Ultra-gigachad version of Dark Souls 2.
Give Tomohiro Shibuya and Yui Tanimura free reign to expand their world with teachings taken from Elden Ring.


- Reimagine the Dark Souls 2 map, give us shortcuts and area transitions that make sense, Implement the DLC's seamlessly into the world and make lackluster areas like Shrine of Amana and Drangleic Castle into actual legacy dungeons worthy of their names. The thought of a bigger, better version of Brightstone Cove Tseldora unironically makes my eyes and mouth water.
(The wishful thinker in me would love to see a version of DS2 that connect the different areas that we know (and love) with a seamless overworld like in Elden Ring, based on this map right here;
)


- Rework the enemy placement, levelling experience, flow and mechanics of the game where needed.
Rethink the existance of the mechanics surrounding Bonfire Ascetics and the ability to exhaust enemy respawns after killing them 15 times, its not really needed since the FromSoft formula is all figured out at this point.
Remove the abysmal Adaptability stat.
But don't DARE touch the Hex system! Hexes being its own category of spells that scale with both INT and FTH was one of the best things DS2 did. Back in the day i was hoping FromSoft would have kept that system around for their future titles.


- Give us the improved controls and freedoms we've gotten used to from playing Elden Ring.
The ability to jump freely, crouch and stealth under the same control scheme as Elden Ring.
It's really really hard to go back to the prehistoric run-jumping mechanics of the older games, and lacking the ability to crouch for those sweet screenshots just makes your character feel stale.


- And one of the most important points:
Don't remove a single boss from the game! Not even the bosses that "everyone hates", instead rework the encounters with new mechanics and better boss arenas.
Give us versions of bosses like the Royal Rat Vanguard, Covetous Demon, Old Iron King and Skeleton Lords that prove the haters wrong!


- And make it all with Elden Rings graphics.


**Do all this and the lack of a Bloodborne PC release will be forgiven. <3**

_______________________________________________

Anyways, the review of 'Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin' as it stands right now? 7/10, It's ok.
It doesn't deserve all the hate it got. But I want more from it, which is why i want the reimagined Directors cut ultimate ultra gigachad version of the game.
Its the Majoras Mask to Dark Souls 1's Ocarina of Time.

Posted: June 5
Not as bad as I thought, but still not great.

Dark Souls is excellent at environment exploration, bosses and atmosphere. DS2 fails on at least two of these.

The bosses are absolutely abysmal. All of them are slow, clunky and unmemorable. The only one I liked was Looking Glass Knight, but even he suffered the same technical issues as all the others. On top of this, you have horrible controls where if you're moving a joystick whilst attacking, your character's tracking will go bananas and strike thin air.

Surely the world exploration made up for it?
Visually, usually yes. Gameplay-wise, no.
You get crowds of mobs who have way too much aggro time so they chase you practically through half the map. Yes, you adopt a more cautious playstyle, but if you wanted to run to the boss, it's not as simple as its predecessor, in turn making the game frustrating. You also often get stun-locked and overtaken by said crowd. If you enjoy being sane don't visit Shrine of Amana or the Black Gulch.

Lastly, the storytelling is pretty bad. It doesn't build up its main villain until the last third of the game. I also just generally don't like the art direction for some things, especially being a green zombie when hollow.

I appreciate the game's experimentation but this really is not a good entry of the franchise. Usually before reviewing, I like to complete the DLC, but I really don't have the motivation to.

Posted: June 3
Look I loved Sekiro. I loved Dark Souls III. This Souls game is just broken. Skip it...

This is an action RPG built to punish mistakes, but in Dark Souls II, much of that punishment feels unearned or poorly explained. During my one-hour session, the core systems didn’t feel challenging in a good way. They felt broken.

Dodging looked fine until I realized the iframe window was almost nonexistent. It turns out that dodging is tied to a stat called Adaptability. The game never mentions this. My Twitch chat had to explain it. As a result, dodging felt unreliable the entire time, and nothing in the game helped me understand why.

Stamina drained fast. Dodging alone could eat half the bar. Combined with the unreliable iFrames, this forced passive play from the start. Most of my Souls came from bosses anyway, so I stopped fighting optional enemies. There was no point practicing patterns when the systems didn’t feel responsive.

Bonfires were easy to miss. They weren’t hidden behind walls, just placed off the main path with no visual cues. I spent too much time running back through areas I'd already cleared, only to die again. That part’s on me, but in a game where you're dying constantly, it matters.

Visually, the game ran smoothly at 60 FPS with no technical issues.

I also joined a covenant early on, thinking it was required. It wasn’t. It quietly made the game harder, with no explanation. Only after chat stepped in did I realize it was a mistake. On top of that, the game shrinks your health bar with each death, down to 50 percent, without telling you why. That added pressure felt unnecessary and poorly timed for new players.

Final Rating: 4/10
I’ve played Sekiro and Dark Souls III. Both were brutal, but fair. This wasn’t. Dark Souls II is technically flawed at its core, and the systems fight against the player instead of challenging them. If you want a good Souls experience, skip this one.

Posted: June 3
Dark Souls 2 truly is the misunderstood game of the series. It feels less like the other Souls games, and more like one of the older FromSoft games. Evergrace, King's Field, this game is more like those than the traditional Souls mold.

Don't get me wrong, it's still a sequel to DS1, however, being made by B-Team, who worked on the pre-Miyazaki FromSoft games, it is much more influenced by them than the rest of the series. Sekiro was also made by B-Team but under direct Miyazaki supervision, although Sekiro started life as a follow-up to Tenchu Deadly Shadows.

Deadly Shadows is easily the best of the FromSoft made Tenchu games after Acquire bailed post-Wrath of Heaven (oddly, Tenchu roughly means "Wrath of Heaven") to make Way of the Samurai, a fever dream choose your own adventure hack and slash series, which has two spinoffs that are basically just Tenchu under a new IP, Kamiwaza is more unique and integrates mainline WotS ideas, and Shinobido, which is JUST Tenchu under a different name. Shinobido is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome BTW.

So, getting fairly sidetracked, but ever heard of Ninja Blade? You probably watched the Iron Pineapple video if you have. Well, after the failure of Ninja Blade, somehow, many of those devs went on to make the hidden gems Tenchu Z and Tenchu Deadly Shadows. Sekiro can actually be traced back to Ninja Blade. Although Ninja Blade isn't THAT bad, it's a clone of Ninja Gaiden Reboot 2 through and through, but like, I like Ninja Gaiden Reboot 2, and well the QTEs are more annoying than in NG2, but the base gameplay is very similar.

I also have to mention that Tenchu Z was a nuts idea, it's a 4-player co-op Tenchu game that's mission based. Tenchu with PSO missions, it's actually really good and no one knows about it. Interestingly, Aragami 2 is actually very similar to Tenchu Z, now there's a game that got undeserved hate at launch. Aragami 2 was hated for being different and for being "decaf Sekiro", but they were really trying to make Tenchu Z except on crack. Every character is detected separately in that game, and co-op Aragami 2 is utter fun chaos despite how it's a little buggy. I remember playing it on Game Pass when it came out and it was the beautiful kind of buggy, it's still pretty buggy because the studio shut down, but not crash-happy in my experience.

So, back on topic, Demon's Souls came out the same week as Ninja Blade, and Demon's Souls, contrary to the myth present in the Souls community, actually did well in Japan out of the gate. Same thing with Shadow Tower Abyss, another game Souls fans like to paint as a failure to fit their "Dark Souls saved FromSoft" narrative, which is largely false.

FromSoft were up-and-coming internationally at the time, although DeS was their first multinational hit. Armored Core, Evergrace (and Lost Kingdoms, called Evergrace 2 in Japan), the King's Field games, Echo Night 1-3, the Shadow Tower series, Kuon, ACE, Chromehounds, and their Tenchu games were all domestic hits.

Hell, Otogi had a cult following in the US, as well as Eternal Ring and Spriggan had a cult following in Japan. Otogi sold horribly but it got good reviews and would go on to become a "best game you've never played" piece darling, I still haven't played it despite wanting to, because it is OG XBOX exclusive and I don't have one of those.

Evergrace is mostly remembered for it's... strange soundtrack and weird technical problems that PCSX2 can work around, but it's a very interesting and unique game too. Just look up "Buying Goods At Palmira Evergrace OST", and you'll see what I mean. Then listen to some of the other songs and realize that some of them, feel like Grandia and Suikoden music except like, if you were crossfaded. I actually like "Sunbeams Streaming Through Leaves On The Hill", it's really ethereal and otherworldly sounding.

Evergrace is actually a major influence on Demon's Souls, as it was the first 3D stamina-based fantasy action RPG Fromsoft made. Spriggan, FromSoft's first action game, despite the name conjuring mental images of the tree women from The Elder Scrolls, is actually a sci-fi series, but it plays more like Shinobi Reboot, according to Wikipedia, Spriggan is not in English.

Dark Souls 2, was also an INSTANT hit worldwide, contrary to the community myths. Fans souring over the game is something that really only happened in the years after DS3 released. And I have to say, I do not like DS3 as much as DS2. DS2 has much better writing IMO, and also I find the more dangerous environments in this game to be far more interesting than the other mainline Souls games. Traversal actually feels like a challenge, enemies feel like real threats.

This game really is more brutal and unforgiving than the other games, and that's exactly why I love DS2 so much. It reminds me of older, more unrefined, more sadistic game design principles, and it makes every bit of progress feel like a real achievement. It is grindier, it is harder, and that's why I've come to love it.

SotFS actually makes enemy spawns happen in slightly trickier locations and adds more gank encounters. Some people like it, some people don't. To me, SotFS is the perfect version of the game. I also use SotFS' new CoC covenant all the time for extra CBT because I'm just a glutton for punishment. I am personally not fond of the anti-grinding in DS2, especially with how rare some drops are. The issue is not with the amount of souls you get, DS2 hands out souls like candy and you end the game at Lv 150+ in a game with 40 point softcaps. However item drops are low enough that I find CoC essential just for getting items like Faintstones, and even then I spent 2 hours getting ONE once.

Give DS2 a chance, it's the red-headed stepchild, but it's not bad for it, it's just very different. In ways that feel, almost like a return to older FromSoft traditions. While it feels clunky at first, once you level ADP a bit it gets better and feels just like DS1 with better balanced weapons and even tighter stamina management. If you like DS2, the Team Ninja soulslikes are closest in level design to DS2, although their combat is much slicker, which is hardly a bad thing, the Nioh combat is actually smoother than DS3 combat IMO, and combat smoothness was DS3's high point.

This game is a good game, it can also be a horrible game because some of its flaws. But what thing doesn't have flaws? The game has lots of good.

The Areas are some of the prettiest I've seen in gaming and this game came out 11 years ago. The freedoms this game gives you for story progression is also amazing and i see why some folks call this game the spiritual predecessor to Elden Ring. I went from the Forest of the Lost Giants straight to the Lost Bastille and Sinners Rise then i circled back around for No Man's Wharf. After that i went on to Harvest Valley, Earthen Peak, and Shaded Woods. All before the Gutter and Black Gulch which typically people do before most of the area's i did before these 2.

The game also has it's grievance's like, ADP stat and Enemy Placements that can be VERY frustrating at first, but they can be solved. This game is meant to be taken slower, if you have trouble with hordes of enemies slow down and try using ranged attacks to lure them out at smaller groups to a single enemy at a time. ADP and the Hitboxes are annoying, but leveling ADP to 20 is very rewarding. Yes it sucks that you have to spend levels on a stat many think shouldn't have existed, yes the enemy placements can be a pain, but we have the tools to make it easier for us as well. Enemies especially, if you kill an enemy 12 times in total they are de-spawned permanently unless you use a Bonfire Ascetic. If you have troubles with the runbacks you can take some time to slaughter all of the enemies in the area (i did this to Iron Keep because i hate those weeb katana dudes) to make it easier, plus you get so many souls that you can use for leveling or buying edible healing rocks.

Dark Souls 2 is a good/great game, it can also be a horrible game, but I've loved it as my first Souls game (Elden Ring is the true first I've played, but this is my first game of the trilogy). If you are on the fence about DS2 i recommend you try it, you are reading this on steam so you've got 2 hours to try the game and see how you feel. Personally this game called to me and that's why it was my first souls game in the trilogy, and i'm glad i chose it. A great game with a good and interesting story that has some banger quotes from the blob head dude named Aldi's.

Posted: June 2
I’ve wanted to play Dark Souls II for years, but every time I tried, I just couldn't do it. Compared to DS1 and DS3, the mechanics felt janky, sluggish, and just frustrating. The controls were awkward, the hitboxes felt off, and the infamous ADP stat made even basic dodging feel unreliable. On top of that, hearing that the game wasn’t “canon” because Miyazaki wasn’t directly involved pushed me away even more.

But eventually, curiosity pulled me back in. This time, I tweaked things to make the game more enjoyable: I changed the control scheme to mimic DS3, installed some graphics mods to make the visuals more appealing, and added the smooth roll mod—because I genuinely think ADP is a poorly designed stat.
Now, as of writing this, I’ve just made it past the Shrine of Amana (which, by the way, was awful), and I’m... actually having a great time.

Yes, DS2 has its flaws. Some areas are painfully frustrating, and it’s incredibly easy to get lost or stuck without a guide—so I’ve been using one, no shame there. But beneath the jank and weird design choices, there’s a uniquely atmospheric and often rewarding experience. The world feels mysterious in a way that’s different from the other games, and once you adjust to its quirks, it becomes surprisingly engaging.

I wouldn’t call it the best Souls game—but I also wouldn’t call it the worst. It’s an acquired taste, and if you’re willing to tweak a few things and go in with the right mindset, Dark Souls II has a lot to offer.

Best Souls 2 gets way too much forced hate. That's not to say this game is perfect- far from it. But speaking for myself, the flaws (mostly) add to the charm.

The world itself is colorful. Traversing through the world feels psychedelic and dreamlike. The first time I took an elevator from the top of a fort, upward to the base of an active volcano (???) I really began to love the game. When I took an elevator down to the Shrine of Amana, and looked up at the scenic beauty, I knew this would remain would of my personal favorites. So many zones are gorgeous and memorable. The scenery alone is with the experience- even being as dated as it now is.

The PVP and PvE builds are practically limitless. All viable, all fun, if you aren't an anxious minmax lunatic. You can cheese, you can tryhard, you can do whatever you please, and have great fun doing it. At some point you have as much fun with 2 shot cheese as you do attempting a boss naked with a broken dagger. The world PvP is a blast, and while a few dweebs take it too seriously, the players in general are all pvping with a sense of fun. The amount of ways to engage in tomfoolery is beyond extensive.

Unfortunately the same can't be said for the brotherhood of blood arena. Don't get me wrong, I've had some very fun PvP experiences in the arena. I'm not kidding through when I say at least half of the players who live in the arena are extremely angry minmax lunatics who, if need be, will hack. Classic examples: infinite health, killing you on command, or putting malicious items in your inventory that will crash your game. Hacking is out of control in this game. As in, if you want to hack, it's easy to do and there are basically no consequences in doing so. Even though most cheating isn't malicious (which is still common), and more like players giving themselves gear that takes a long time to farm, it still leaves a bad taste in my mouth.

The bosses, with a few exceptions, are all fun. Even the easy ones are fun, in that it's fun to see how fast or in what silly ways you can defeat them. It's as fun to take on Fume Knight in a serious manner as it is easily nuking Royal Rat Authority with pyromancy. Don't get me started on some of the DLC bosses though. While some are fun, some will drive you insane. As in, it's not challenging in the traditional sense. It just sucks to subject yourself to them. Most of these terrible bosses though are optional, so it doesn't ruin the overall experience.

Wish someone would care enough to end the hacking, but there are player made mods that mitigate the worst of it. Overall the game is great, is still active, and is worth playing to this day.

Just stay away from Iron Passage.

this game is beautifully magical in a manner i cannot possibly describe in words. some of my favorite locations in all of fromsoftware's work reside here, and so too does the greatest interwoven story in the series. nashandra is my personal favorite final villain in the trilogy in lore, and the immensely powerful realization of being able to abandon the cycle of light and dark feels much stronger here than it does in Dark Souls III. i increasingly enjoyed my time with this title, and felt far less fatigue when nearing the end of the game than the first or third entries in the trilogy as a result.

many of Dark Souls II's faults are often similar in impact to the rest of the souls games, and its most egregious pitfalls often end up being the most unique and special aspects of the entire experience. the drastic shift DS2 divisively placed upon the entire foundation of dark souls was the same universal change that made this game so special, memorable, and endlessly intriguing.

since DS2's release, a slew of fromsoftware titles have come and gone. many abandon the way of Dark Souls II in favor of legacy, nostalgia, or innovation. some choose to honor its more notable merits through brief references or lesser. be as it may, a decade has passed since the risky inception of something unmistakably wondrous that has only continued to shine as time passes it by - and it's something we may never witness again, an occurrence that may never repeat itself, in a magnificent analogy to the abandonment of the throne of want.

I now believe it might be the best of the trilogy, assuming I ignore the DLC for DS3. (But I'll admit that the DS3 bosses are much better overall, which is what I believe most people play these games for anyways.)

Bonfire ascetics, changes in NG+, powerstancing, lifegems, weapon/armor durability that actually mattered, invading players either while they are solo and/or while the boss in their world is dead, and equipment weight affecting roll distance and recovery time (beyond just fast, medium and slow rolling) are just some of the things added from DS1 but were unfortunately removed for DS3. A truly hollowing realization in my eyes.

All that said, go play Elden Ring instead.

This was the first Dark Souls game I ever played, or at least, the original DS2 was. SOTFS is definitely much harder than the original, yet I still believe it to be easier than the others in the FromSoft games (btw, easier than the others does not mean it is easy). Moral is, start with this game. You will die... over and over and over again. But you will continue to learn and become better. Especially in this Dark Souls game where it is, in my opinion, fairly easier compared to the others (and again, that does not mean this game is easy).
