So here’s the thing: I like Dark Souls. I respect Dark Souls. I tried with Dark Souls II. But this one? This one lost me. Fast. After reveling in the glorious chaos of Bloodborne and Dark Souls III, I thought, “Okay, let’s see what the black sheep of the family is all about.” I walked into Dark Souls II expecting moody ruins, cryptic NPCs, and some casual soul-crushing difficulty. What I got was a weirdly sunny beach town that felt less like the beginning of a doomed journey and more like the opening to a melancholic Animal Crossing. And then I met some depressed people who gave me vague advice, a talking cat who may or may not insulted me, and a world that just felt... off. The movement felt floaty. The hitboxes were questionable. The enemies? Weirdly aggressive but not in a fun way—more like “haha you stepped forward so here’s a sword in your spine and also your armor broke” kind of way. And the vibe? Hard to pin down. Like it wanted to be philosophical, but also wanted to drop you into a death maze with a giant hippo monster. I gave it about an hour. Explored a little. Died a few times. Tried to tell myself it would click eventually. It did not click. Maybe it gets better. Maybe I just wasn’t in the right headspace. But I bounced off Dark Souls II so hard I’m still airborne. I want to like it. I tried to like it. But something about the atmosphere, the mechanics, and the general vibe made me feel like I’d wandered into a sequel that forgot what made the first one special. Dark Souls II might have depth I never reached, but it lost me before it had the chance. I wandered, I rolled, I died, I shrugged—and then I quietly uninstalled. Maybe someday I’ll go back and try again. Or maybe I’ll just run back to Dark Souls III where everything makes sense and the enemies kill me politely.

"The Game That Filters Out The Frauds"

Dark Souls II isn’t for everyone. In fact, it’s not for most.

It’s not for the low-testosterone dopamine junkies looking to coast through life on hand-holding and nostalgia goggles. It’s not for those who rage when the mobs don’t wait their turn or cry about adaptability. It’s not for tourists. It’s not for goobermaxxing simps.

This is the brutal, misunderstood middle child of the Souls series. And it doesn’t care about your feelings.

I made the mistake of playing DS2 right after DS1—riding that high, overconfident, expecting it to bow to my muscle memory. It didn’t. It kicked my teeth in. I refunded.

But something about it stayed.

Over a year later, I came back—and that’s when it clicked.

DS2 is slower, heavier, more methodical. You don’t dance here—you fight with weight. Every roll, every swing, every decision is deliberate. The game doesn’t coddle you—it gives you full control and full consequences. If you’re getting smacked, it’s not the game. It’s you.

Majula isn’t just a hub. It’s a liminal home across dimensions. Lonely, sun-soaked, beautiful. It speaks to the weary soul between battles. And that’s DS2 in a nutshell—poetic brutality.

You’ll descend into The Gutter, a dripping hellhole that makes Blighttown look like a spa, and still pause to admire the artistic decay. This game is full of places that feel like death fell from the sky—towering dread, haunted architecture, cursed charm. It’s a fever dream carved into reality.

Yes, the mechanics are different. Yes, adaptability matters. So does discipline in real life. Life doesn’t hand you perfect iframes either. Grow up. Git aware. Git better.

DS2 is reality disguised as a fantasy RPG.
It punishes indecision. Rewards persistence. Humbles the arrogant. Raises the worthy.

If you can’t handle it, walk away.
If you can… you’ll know.
You’ll feel it. Deep in your bones.
You’ll be reborn from the rot.

This game doesn’t need your approval.
It needs your respect.

10/10 – For those with iron will and calloused souls.

peak souls \o/

my fromsoft play order is over the place; i first tried ds3 back in the day, struggled a lot and dropped it. then much later, elden ring released, and decided to give it a go. loved it, beat it two times and eventually felt more comfortable playing souls-likes, went back to ds3. due to the negative reputation of ds2, i skipped it... played ds1 and thought it would be the end of it. oh boy how wrong i was.

when people talked ♥♥♥♥ about shadow of the erdtree, i was confused. to me the dlc was perfectly fine. then it got me wondering what if ds2 wasn't all too bad... and it wasn't. playing this game was a blast, i had so much good time with it. i actually respect fromsoft for trying to spice up things and go more experimental. dlcs were good too.

most sticky situations you find yourself in can usually be managed easily (ex. in iron keep you can utilize traps etc. + if melee bows help a lot too) and agility can be leveled up fairly early on, so i don't get the criticism on these. sure starting out with that terrible roll made me feel bad too but after a couple sessions i had it leveled up just fine. i feel like ds2 prioritizes exploration and actually wants the player to go through the game without rushing it, hence the "frustrating" layouts/mob placements. im a slow player, i take my time a lot and do love exploring so ds2 was very enjoyable to me in that sense.

it surely is different to ds1 and ds3, and it does have its jank moments, but in no way it is a bad game as some make it out to be. i personally enjoyed it a lot, and would highly recommend first checking it out for yourself before being biased due to the negative vocal bunch.

Posted: May 25
By far the worst entry in the Souls series. The game isn't hard, as a matter of fact, it probably has the easiest bosses in the series because of how slow most of them are. The real issue is that it feels horrible and clunky to play. You can do one attack and one dodge roll before you're out of stamina, making every encounter just a long waiting game. The animations are stiff, and movement is janky. The graphics are flat, and the environment is filled with big, empty areas. I really wish I had something good to say about this game, but the level design is worse than DS1, and the gameplay is worse than DS3. The only reason you would buy this is out of morbid curiosity. Do not recommend.

Posted: May 25
Base game is fun. There are some serious hurdles to clear before you can enjoy it though. Look up how stats work before playing.

The DLC's suck and I will never play them again.

The base game was pretty easy once you figure out how stats work. The DLCs are a total slog. Enemies 2 shot you, while being huge damage sponges. I have fully maxed gear and every enemy is mortal threat. And there a TONS of enemies in these DLCs. Multiple times I just had to farm them until they stopped respawning. There was just no way to make the boss run with full health otherwise, and believe me, you need full health. Fume Knight almost had me give up.

This game has camera acceleration, which is always a bad thing IMO. It's dizzying at best and incomprehensible at worst. There is also input lag. Lots of it. There is even input ignore.

Sometime you press dodge, attack. Then he performs them in sequence. Sometimes he just doges. Sometimes you flask and he does nothing at all. Sometimes you get stun locked for a few seconds and he will perform whatever input you entered 8 bloody seconds ago when the stun ends. It is wildly inconsistent and if there is any rhyme or reason to it then I sure didn't figure it out. It really makes an otherwise fun game a very frustrating experience.

Despite its reputation as the “black sheep” of the trilogy, Dark Souls II surprised me in the best way! The game’s rough edges are real, with a strange pattern of hard levels but mostly easy bosses, but those tough roadblocks pushed me to experiment with weapons, builds and mechanics I probably never would’ve engaged with otherwise.
Overall, the haunting world of Drangleic is absolutely worth exploring, with a special shoutout to the amazing DLC that drastically improves on the base game. Seek strength, Bearer of the Curse… and maybe a few more points in ADP, just in case!

Posted: May 22
Personal Thoughts after Beating the Game

Though it doesn't have the same bombastic hype bosses, quick combat, or grim atmosphere of the other games, I find Dark Souls II to be honestly a really great souls game. It definitely overall is weaker than all the other ones, but it also just fills a different kind of niche.

The gameplay is much more slow-paced and thoughtful; healing happens over-time rather than immediately, stamina regenerates pretty slowly, enemies and rolls are fairly slow, and most creatures have large wind-ups that are clearly readable. All the slowness was actually really refreshing after the super-fast action combat of Elden Ring, which tends to rely a lot more on muscle memory and prayers than planning or thought. In DSII, you have to think very carefully about each move you are about to make- to attack, to heal, to dodge, or to wait and regenerate stamina- and because the game is slower, you actually have some time to think a little more about that (I'd say about 25-30% more time than in DS1 or DS3, although that's a very rough guess). Combat feels more methodical and resource-based because of that, and so it feels quite different to play compared to any of the other games, which isn't necessarily a bad thing. It's simply different.

Personally, I really appreciate that it plays differently; it makes it stand out, and so its not really like "DS1 but worse." The environments and areas are also very different from the others. While DS1 and 3 are very much dark fantasy and oriented around blood and darkness, DS2 paints a more mellow, bright light, and the world doesn't feel as decayed as the other games, although it clearly is crumbling. Where DS1 feels like a grimy and molding pile of rubble, ds2 feels like a moss-covered set of ruins with sunlight flowing across them (weird analogy I know but it's what came to mind). It definitely stands out visually, and the set-pieces are honestly probably my favorite in the series outside of Elden Ring.

However, the world also does not feel as tied-together as the others, because of its different design style. Most of the areas are very unique and have different tones, but that means you could be walking from a watery sea-side cliff tower, taking a short elevator going up, and then finding yourself in a fiery hellscape with lava stretching out in all directions (yes this actually happens in the game and it's really funny, although its probably the most extreme example of crossing levels). Some of the levels also just feel completely detached from others, so the kingdom of Drangleic (as it is called) doesn't feel as much like a 'kingdom' as it does a set of different lands loosely tied together. But in spite of that, I really adore most areas and the way they look, so I'm willing to look past that.



People whine a lot about ADP and how it's a waste of levels but genuinely I don't know what they're talking about. Though it is kind of annoying that you start out the game with 5 frames of dodge instead of 13 like in the others, I got it up to level 20 my firs few hours in the game and never touched it again and I was perfectly fine. Considering I passively went from level 12 to level 183 throughout the course of my playthrough, this still left ~95% of my levels to be used for all the other stats. It was barely even an inconvenience.


The healing is slow in this game. Like I mentioned before, the slow healing and dodging emphasizes a more thoughtful and planning-oriented approach to the game. I think many people thought it would be DS1 2.0 and were mad that the gameplay changed. The game also provides you with plenty of non-Estus healing throughout the game, which is a really neat feature especially for particularly long levels. In the end, you just need to go in knowing that it's a different way to play, and if you adapt then it works out just fine.


Basically, I had almost zero issues with ganks, even in the DLCs and the frigid outskirts, and I was really surprised by that. From how much people complain about getting set upon by 8 enemies at once out of nowhere, I expected it to be tough. But I never really experienced that- and I think it's because the game rewards patience. Just like how the slow gameplay asks you to be patient with your rolls and attacks and stamina, the areas ask you to be patient by clearing them out one or two enemies at a time, instead of rushing through an area like so many people apparently do. If you try rushing the level without paying attention to your surroundings or using the tools available at your disposal, you will certainly get mobbed- and it's typically your own fault. But even when it came to boss runbacks and the likes, I found that if I looped around enemies a couple times on the way to the boss, I could make it through without dying or having to kill a single enemy. Basically, how difficult ganks are depends on how patient you are, and how good you are at spacing if multiple dudes end up attacking you at once.


I know how a lot of people feel about summoning, and for the most part, I agree that generally Souls games are better experienced solo, one-on-one with the bosses and stuff, at least for the first run. However, in some specific circumstances the game provides some NPC summons for you to use in areas that are clearly tailored more for multiplayer enjoyment. There are only 2 or 3 areas in the whole game that are like this, which is what makes it fun and unique. I played solo for 95% of the game and summoned some guys in these couple of areas. I know many people hate summoning, but I would highly recommend summoning in these areas as it provides a new kind of gameplay that is really fun and refreshing, and makes their specific game sections a lot more enjoyable. Many people are so deadset against summons of any kind that they ruin parts of the game for themselves by stubbornly going into a group of enemies alone again and again, and I would just suggest that you use the tools the game provides for you.


The big one for me. Unfortunately, the complaints about the bosses in this game are very valid. While the bosses are often mechanically fun, they are just as often fairly boring and tepid in their movesets and designs. However, there are still a lot of really cool and fun boss fights that stand out to me even now- the roster is just more hit-or-miss than the other games (It also has twice as many bosses as DS1 or DS3). While I was able to beat most bosses on my first or second try, I still thoroughly enjoyed them most of the time as roadblocks or setpieces to cap off all the different areas. As per usual with FromSoft, the DLCs tended to have the higher-quality fun and memorable boss fights. I really liked those.


Overall, while I didn't find the gameplay as action-packed or the bosses as compelling as the other games, I think that Dark Souls II is absolutely a game worth getting (even in comparison to DS1/3/Elden Ring) and it captivated me for nearly 70 hours on my first playthrough, and I definitely feel motivated to play through again on NG+. I'm not sure how differently the Vanilla version plays compared to the Scholar version, but I really liked the features added in Scholar. However, I think either way to play will be a good experience.

Posted: May 22
Dark Souls II is slower, heavier, and more punishing than its predecessor - not because of bosses, but because of the sheer number of enemies thrown at you. Exploration demands effort; areas are dense, full of ambushes, and often test your patience more than your skill.

Bosses are less memorable, and the world is less cohesive than in DS1. But even with its flaws, it’s still a Souls game at heart: tough, atmospheric, and deeply rewarding once you push through.

Not the best, but far from bad. If you're into the series, you owe it to yourself to give it a shot.

What can I say? This is going to be extremely biased.
This was my first FromSoft game, and it will always be my favorite.

I understand that for a lot of people, DS2 is the black sheep. In some areas, I get that — adaptability sucks/ soul memory as well.

But there's something unique about DS2: powerstancing, bonfire ascetic, and it's the only game where NG+ actually feels different, with red phantoms ganking you.

There’s more to this game than I can put into words, just try it out.
At first, you’re going to hate it, but after a while, you’ll find yourself missing your time in Majula.

An absolute must. Way ahead of its time. Do not listen to the haters who say it is the worst Souls game. It has its own flavor and you are definitely missing out if you do not play this game. This game is like if someone took Dark Souls 1, microwaved it for 3 minutes, dropped it on the floor, stepped on it, and then said “yeah this is fine.”

The combat feels like trying to dodge punches in a swimming pool full of gravy. Every swing has a 3-second wind-up like I'm charging a spirit bomb just to bonk a zombie with arthritis. Hitboxes? More like guessboxes. I died to air more times than I’d like to admit. I’m convinced some enemies are just hallucinations from sleep deprivation.

The world design is actual spaghetti. You take an elevator and end up in a lava castle—what is this, Mario Kart Rainbow Road DLC? There’s no logic. It’s like someone built the map in Minecraft blindfolded.

NPCs speak entirely in riddles and depression. One guy literally said “fate is cruel” and then sat down and started moaning. Bro I came here to fight monsters, not share trauma.

Boss fights are just reskinned dudes with anger issues. One of them is literally named “The Lost Sinner” which is also what I call myself after playing this game for 8 hours straight thinking it would get better.

I rolled off a cliff, alt-tabbed, stared at my desktop, and questioned my life choices. That was the most emotionally impactful part of the game.

Final thoughts: If you want the Dark Souls experience, just stub your toe, pour Legos on your floor, and argue with your WiFi router.

Would rather 1v1 the Pursuer in real life with a Nerf sword than open this game again.

Dark Souls II: SOTFS is a respectable sequel to DS1, and certainly deserves more credit than it receives. While the world design is arguably less polished than that of the first, I appreciated the ambitions of the developers, particularly their turn to expansive environments, and reliance on lighting/darkness as a gameplay mechanic (better with a shader-texture mod). Some bosses appear to be much tamer in comparison to those of the first, enabling smoother progression through the storyline, albeit at the expense of trivialising some of the experience, particularly in the late game. But the enemy and item variety more than made up for the unfamiliar if different 'vibe' of DS2 that people so often complain about. Solid game, and rightly a classic. 8.5/10

Your dodges are also slow, so you cannot make a mistake. I believe the speed is tied to a stat? This shouldn't be the case. You can be punished for opening a chest, when in 1 and 3 you would have I-Frames. You take a very long time to use your Estus Flask, your healing item, something that is highly important, something that you can chug in 1 and 3 fast. Enemy placement is awful and the AI waits for you and chases you, so the game forces you to fight, so if you die? Fight, cant just pass by them. I can go on and on.

Due to the bad reputation this game gets from the community I was expecting it to be a nightmare!

I actually really really enjoyed it. DS2 has a lot of unique and progressive features. You can knock down trees to make bridges (like invisible walls), zip lines, oil on the ground that can be lit to make fires, honestly there are a lot of cool things in the game.

I also didn't mind that my health went lower with each death when I hollowed. You get plenty of human effigys throughout the game and from NPC invaders to remove that debuff. And once you finish the DLC's you can get an item for NG+ that stops you hollowing ;D So a great reward for doing the whole game.

The only thing I would have preferred myself was a way to disable invaders without being offline. Currently there is no way to do this so getting invaded by players in some yikes spots kind of sucked, that said when I REALLY didn't want to deal with it, I could just close the game to kick them out and reopen.

There are some really great boss fights in the DLC especially and boss arenas which are a lot of fun. And lore wise it does actually link back to DS1 even though this is a new kingdom and as I'm just starting DS3 now, I can see how the cycle has repeated again and it links to that too.

Highly recommend people pick this one up and ignore the negative feedback from Dark souls fans, I can see why it's a marmite game especially with the health decreasing on death mechanic and having to spec into adaptability but in the words of the roly poly lords:

Posted: May 17
Dark Souls 2 Scholar of the First Sin is the definitive and more well-crafted version of Dark Souls 2 and its haters don't know what they're talking about, and Dark Souls 2 in general is a hard-to-get but fun game and a creative playground for FromSoftware which led to a lot of great ideas and features carried onto later titles, that is after you struggle to make the game functional for around 30-40 minutes. Yes, this is why my review is negative, this is one of the most unacceptably shameless and incompetent PC ports ever made other than basic performance and they've changed nothing in 10 years! Negative thought was put behind it, not even basic QA or engine optimization. So TLDR: good game, even great game. For all its faults and many middling executions, I personally enjoy and appreciate it significantly more than Dark Souls 1. BUT!!! Hampered on PC by one of the worst ports ever made.

1. Right from the get-go the port doesn't have native aspect ratio detection and syncing and just opens as an awkward 720p window floating off center like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ flash game, meaning you have to set it to windowed and your desktop's resolution, go to the game directory and alter the App.Window.X/Y values in notepad and mess with them while rebooting the game over and over again to see if it fits neatly on your screen, and even then it just stretches the window and still has dead zones in the corners that are jarring to look at. Honestly I recommend playing this game with the lighting engine mod, not only does it offer native aspect ratio syncing it also makes the game look better. How something as basic as this got through in 2015, when aspect ratio detection was the bare minimum for a console to pc port, is beyond my mortal understanding. This reeks of either pure laziness, complete disregard for PC testing, no QA, pure incompetence, or all at once.

2. The mouse and keyboard controls are a thoughtless cluster****. There's an entire section of completely redundant mouse and keyboard combinations that overlap with other basic keyboard inputs, the fact that I had to unbind around 80% of them for the key mappings to even make sense says it all. You can also bind the attack functions to keys, which literally makes no sense, and you can't unbind them so I had to bind them to keys I don't use, which is totally not complete incompetence and a waste of time. The lock-on button or esc button doesn't actually exit the menu or interaction menus like npcs and bonfires (unlike in any other Soulsborne port) so you have to use right click and then manually select back. When the right hand weapon attack is bound to left click and blocking/left hand weapon attack/powerstanced attack to right click, the bow aiming is bound to left click, and shooting to right click, and that's totally not complete nonsense that's a result of complete lack of QA. The interact and confirm functions also have separate key binds (WHY??? HOW??? at least you can map them both to the same button but the fact that you even have to do that in the first place just shows how deep the incompetence and carelessness runs through this port).

3. Disastrous UI/UX feedback. You are pretty much obligated to change the mouse and keyboard controls unless you want to have a fever dream experience trying to play the game on them, right? well, guess what happens when you DON'T GET a basic "confirm the changes or something" button and press esc? nothing, the key binds just don't change and you're left bewildered. No, what you have to somehow figure out (I totally didn't google it) with zero UI visual feedback is that you have to manually hover over the key binds menu and left click on it once for the key binds to change before you can press esc. You have to do a similar thing to switch spells if I remember correctly. That is an UI design failure on every single level.

4. No tooltips for mouse and keyboard. Press X to... what X mother***** do I look like I'm holding an XBOX controller to you? shameless and lazy. "PC gamers are an afterthought and we didn't even test the port with mouse and keyboard... if we tested it in the first place" - the porting team probably.

5. and the worst offender of all, the strict memory limit, which was an avoidable legacy holdover necessary for PS3 but completely redundant and sometimes even broken for modern hardware, which makes it even more shameless that the SOTFS edition was advertised as a re-release for modern hardware when in reality no effort was taken in modernizing the engine or infrastructure of the game to make the most out of newer console and PC hardware. This makes the game infamously hard to mod, just take the recent randomizer mod, which casting the obvious aside, that it took such a long time to make, has lots of workarounds and limitations because of the game's strict memory limit, and even then can easily crash the game if the settings aren't tweaked carefully by the user. And even worse, one time, and I'm pretty sure because of the strict memory limit, my game's memory just freaked out seamlessly mid-session (with just the lighting engine mod, which only affects texture and lighting engine files) and started replacing every singular item pick-up with raw stones, even key items, which effectively bricked my save and forced me to uninstall the game and delete the save files folder then reinstall it. Absolutely broken, shameless and lazy beyond belief, if I didn't love this game I would have ragequit, uninstalled and never looked back.

Honestly, I believe horrific PC ports like this and Dark Souls 1's Prepare to Die edition led to conceptions like the inherent superiority of console gaming over PC gaming or controller over mouse and keyboard, specifically for Soulslikes, when the latter has a bunch of objective mechanical advantages like ergonomics, input stacking, camera control, lock-on target switching, input precision, instant digital inputs over analog inputs, hand and finger efficiency, no risk of stick drift, customization and muscle memory. Dark Souls Remastered, Dark Souls 3, Sekiro, and Elden Ring all have wonderful PC ports where I only had to tweak a few key binds more-so from personal preference.

Whatever team made this port I hope never got their hands on another port ever again.

Disclaimer: I'm going over the souls series of games running yolo builds and not checking guides, because if you have to read a guide to beat the game or understand the story, the game sucks. Regarding how much I played, I completed the main game and most of the Old Iron King DLC (everything but the last boss.

TL;DR: This sequel tries a bunch of new things. Some of these are great ideas that all newer souls games keep; others are bad ideas that will never be visited again. Overall, I enjoyed my time in this game more than the original, with some caveats. The main source of frustration for me was not the intended difficulty of the game, but the input lag issue for mouse & keyboard which led to a lot of swearing.
7.5/10 overall, 8.5/10 if you use a controller instead.
If DS2 is the one that ran when DS1 walked, I am so looking forward to what DS3 is like.

Graphics: Beautiful. I was hooked from the word go by the environments.

Gameplay: This game is faster than DS1, and most of the enemies reflect this. The downside is that most of the main game bosses play like it's still DS1, so a number of them feel like pushovers. Dying at the low levels feels bad because your max health decreases (capped at 50%) when that happens, but you can undo that easily about a quarter through the game.
The Iron King DLC though has the first souls boss that I've been waiting for in the series (Fume Knight). It was just bonfire right outside, pull that sucker and figure it out.

Some areas have way too much trash in my opinion, though. This mostly applies to the Iron King DLC where it felt like I had to kill a whole army to get back to one of the bosses, because fog walls aren't covered by iframes like they were in DS1.

Story: Souls games are more about vibe than they've ever been about story. For what it's worth though, DS2 feels more grounded, whereas in DS1 there were far too many NPCs who were just cryptic ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in the middle of nowhere.

Posted: May 9
I bought Dark Souls 2 years ago, seeking to play it with friends and only recently have I decided to pick it up and try to complete it to the extent my sanity allows.

I can say that I had a miserable time 80% of my time spent playing it.

Dark Souls 2 is a game plagued by stupid design decisions (gank bosses, flooding areas with enemies - staring at you, Crown of the Old Iron King, sluggish... everything, etc.)

I found myself cursing it every time I decided to play it.

Despite that, I had some fun in the time I played it, so let's start off from that.

Dark Souls 2 is relatively charming in how it presents itself, it has a lot of secrets and gimmicks that are, in some ways, interesting and sometimes fun (Bonfire Ascetics, Covenant system, rounding NPCs up in Majula) and it made the experience somewhat bearable when I found myself smiling at the small quirky bits of it - even majority of the areas are fun to explore and have varying degrees of eye-candy (Heide's Tower of Flame & Frozen Eleum Loyce were two of my favourites).

A lot of the equipment you get to wear is charming and makes for a fun time getting the best style while also allowing you to bring the armours up to speed through the method of upgrading them.
The weapons... I did like, but there's a caveat to them which made it so obnoxious to use the more interesting ones I wanted that I will address further down - All in all, I just had a mace from the beginning of the game and it worked for everything, much to my annoyance or relief, depending.
Then there were the summons, they could support you through entire areas rather than just individual bosses - it made the world feel more alive, despite how clearly bleak and empty it really is.


However, the grand design of Dark Souls 2 shoots all the good points in the foot in a grand show of terrible game design that makes the entire game experience complete rubbish.
A lot of bosses were solved simply through the simple matter of "rotate right/left around them and hit their rear when they're swinging at (and missing) you" and let me down in every possible manner just due to the sheer... boring or tedious way you had to play.

The few bosses that didn't follow this rule of "rotational-attack-force" were all... just flat-out annoying, hitboxes that'd hurt you when they start moving - combined with the horrible movement system that feels like you're dreaming and trying to run at the same time makes for awful experiences (looking at you, Aava and your kin).

Then there's the run back to the bosses. Oh gosh. I cannot state just how infuriating the design of some areas are, you get one bonfire and a sequence of corridors with enemies that will stick onto you like glue (Crown of the Old Iron King is the worst for this - Iron Passage and Memory of the Old Iron King made me lose my damned mind, let's not even get started on Frigid Outskirts or the path to the Darklurker).
I just hope whoever designed these areas never get to do so ever again.

Then there's the one mechanic that makes me hate Dark Souls 2 more than anything: the hollow mechanic.
The hollow mechanic is why we can't have nice things: "Oh you died trying to do that? Let us punish you some more by making you struggle even more on your next attempt. Also don't forget, you only get so many of the Human Effigies that you need to restore your humanity! Teehee!"
The hollow mechanic is a terribly implemented function with a nice idea behind it (or so I think), it punishes you for dying and doesn't do anything for you if you stay alive - it is nothing more than a way to screw you over should you break your stride.

Alongside all of this is the durability system - a system that lets your armour and weapons break, meaning you only have so many swings before you need to repair the weapons at a blacksmith; this made it hard for me to try testing out weapons from different bosses just due to the sheer speed at which some of them would break that I'd already upgraded.

I played through the game entirely and the ending literally took me by surprise, not due to any story element; just due to the fact that I was literally unaware I had reached the ending.
And so, in my desire to tick off Dark Souls 2 from the list of games I'd experienced - I decided to run through it and... well, I hated it. But to be fair to the game itself, I'll decide to list my numerical ratings of the base game and DLCs.

Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin - 6/10: It's fine, but there's a lot of jank found in the game itself that makes it feel more like a slog than anything. People keep parroting its difficulty, but all I can see is just garbage game mechanics that make it annoying and tedious - not hard.

Crown of the Sunken King - 7/10: It suffers from the same fate as the main game, but I did kind of enjoy my time in the first DLC - despite the boss giving me the worst RNG ever with the summons.

Crown of the Old Iron King - 0/10: Delete it, I absolutely hated my entire experience in this DLC and it was never fun, not past the first few minutes of experiencing something new. Spamming enemies that can lunge at you, then enemies that have ranged attacks and don't stagger easily; then giant enemies that just club you into the floor (3 at the same time????) then combining that with the slow-spell of the mages in the Iron Passage? Who designed this? Who greenlit this? Stop.

Crown of the Ivory King (Ignoring Frigid Outskirts) - 7/10: It was a fun experience, despite how annoying the first boss was to fight; but I only really learned after the fact that people were undertaking the fight with far more stats than I was, so I can only blame myself. I did actually enjoy exploring and trying to find the knights to allow myself an easier time and the ability to complete the last fight properly - it was a fun gimmick.

Crown of the Ivory King (Including Frigid Outskirts) - 2/10: While not as bad as the Crown of the Old Iron King, it was not a fun experience to constantly be beset upon by ice reindeers that have an endless supply, only to end up in a bossfight with two bosses that feel flat out annoying to fight.

Overall: 4/10, I wouldn't tell anyone to put themselves through this. The things that made it somewhat enjoyable to me are definitely not worth the mental toll it took on me to go through this game, it never felt like a hard game - it just felt like an exhausting, tedious and annoying game that never really challenged you other than your mental fortitude when you kept getting killed because the game's RNG decided your fate.
It was fun, until it wasn't; and then it just kept feeling more and more of a slog to me trying to go through and finish it.

Dark Souls II is like that one friend who invites you over for dinner and then throws your food on the floor while telling you it builds character. The game constantly asks: *"What if every hallway was a boss fight and every boss was legally allowed to cheat?"*

You start with a broken sword, no idea what's going on, and a lady who tells you you're basically already dead. Great pep talk. Then some guy in a hat laughs at you for 80 hours straight.

Enemies teleport behind you, backstab you through walls, and form synchronized dance mobs just to ruin your day. Gravity is the strongest boss. Ladders are lies. And don’t get me started on the hitboxes—I've seen ghosts with better accuracy.

Despite all this, I keep coming back like a Stockholm Syndrome-riddled masochist. 10/10 would cry in Majula again.

Dark Souls 2, where do I even begin... From the moment I started playing this game, it was nothing but perfect. From absolutely stunning graphics, rivaling that of Elden Ring, to the spectacular enemy placements, this game leaves nothing to be desired. People may try to tell you that this game is bad, but they are simply lying. They ignore the fact that Elden Ring is a near 1:1 copy of this game. Undeniably, the developers are geniuses; they solved the formula and delivered boss fights that not only trump Dark Souls 1, but continue to remain the best boss fights in gaming history. To do this, they found out that instead of making a well designed (albeit boring) solo boss fight, they can turn every boss into a duo, triple, or even horde fight. By doing this, they have the ability to create more unique and challenging boss fights, while retaining the grandeur of a boring old solo fight. On another note, the enemy placement is amazing. Just like an impossible burger perfectly replicates the flavor and feeling of an actual hamburger, this game manages to make each area feel real, as if they were actually populated cities. My final scoring of this game is 10/10. There is no reason you should not buy this game. It is the Super Mario Bros of our century.

Posted: May 8
This is a complicated game to write a review about. It does a lot right but a ton wrong. It can be fun for people who enjoy FromSoftware games while also feeling like a cheap knockoff of everything else they've created. I've attempted to play this game 4 different times across 3 different consoles and each time I get farther than before. Well, I think I can finally say that this game is a (mostly) enjoyable experience.

I love Dark Souls 1. I'd rank it as my 2nd favorite game of all time behind Fallout:New Vegas. The first time I beat Gwyn I jumped out of my chair and audibly cheered. And I love Elden Ring, DS3, and Bloodborne but nothing has come close to replicating that feeling. Dark Souls 2... also probably won't but compared to the other three examples, it feels the most refreshing.

This is a FromSoftware game but with a different games hat on. It's Walmart Dark Souls but that's part of its charm. Yeah, the artificial difficulty is annoying but the build variety is fun to experiment with. For every annoying location there's one that'll take your breath away. It's a game worth giving a chance.

However, I wouldn't buy it at full price. If you like other souls-like games, there's some fun to be had here. If you're looking for a game on the same level as DS1, DS3, or Elden Ring, you won't find it here but you will find a game that's lovable despite all its flaws.

Posted: May 5
Ah Dark Souls 2, the misunderstood middle child of the series. Remade several times by several developers with one of the most troubled dev cycles ever seen it's a miracle this game came out at all.
Some die hard fans will claim this is the best game in the series. Honestly I just think they're being contrarian hipsters who like having the minority opinion but whatever.

Dark Souls 2 is essentially Elden Ring 0.5, which is no coincidence as DS2's director went on to co-direct ER. It's by far the most innovative game in the series (shy of Demon's Souls for inventing the family) and takes many, many bold and daring risks, a few of which turned out not terribly. From bosses to mechanics to even just character movement, the game is janky and unintuitive and will more often than not leave you frustrated at perceived unfairness instead of at your lack of skill. The entire game is filled with questionable choices, most obvious of which is the infamous Earthen Peak elevator; in the middle of a poisonous swamp you climb to the very top of a decrepit windmill marking the land. After a boss fight at the top of the tower (where you can drain the poison in the arena by burning the windmill's turbine??????????(?????howdoesthatmakesense???)) you get onto an elevator. This elevator begins to go up and up and up and finally emerges in a castle surrounded by lava. Why the elevator doesn't just go down into hell instead of up into the sky is only for Yui Tanimura to know, but these kinds of nonsensical and bizarre design choices are littered throughout the entire game.

Having shat on it for a while I have to concede some good points. The world is very atmospheric and immersive (when there aren't stupid elevators) - Majula is easily the best hub world in any Souls game, and the sense of relief and safety you feel when inside it is unmatched by any other game. Power-stancing is an incredibly fun mechanic and adds significant variety to your arsenal that even Elden Ring only partially matches. There's a couple of good bosses and the game can be quite fun in coop, but overall the great moments are few and far between, spaced out by overwhelming quantities of mid and mediocre.

Overall DS2 doesn't live up to its siblings, and while Yui Tanimura certainly did an admirable job stitching together the broken pieces of a game in the short time he had, the end product very much reflects the poor dev cycle.
6.5/10, honestly just play the other games.

What I'm using
I'm really trying to make this an honest review, so for clarity's sake I will say that I am playing an int build with basically exclusively spells. If that just made you say "well you're not really playing the game", then yeah. You got the point. I don't actually want to play this game, I'm playing it for completions' sake because I love Dark souls 1 and 3.
I have tried 2 separate melee playthroughs, melee being what I play 99% of the time in Souls games, and I quit both of them after a couple hours because the game was so unfun.
Apparently sorcery is really good in this game, and it shows. This feels much easier and more natural than my two attempted (and not even halfway-through abandoned) melee playthrough attempts of this game. In general, ranged attacks seem a borderline necessity due to the often atrocious enemy placement.

Intro
This game is a bit like someone played gartic phone with Dark Souls 1's game design.

Gartic phone is a game where you draw something, someone else sees it and has to try to recreate it, usually not quite getting it right, making for some pretty funny moments.

This game feels like someone played Dark Souls 1, told another person what it's like. That other person then tried to make a game based off the description alone, and did not nail it.

"Uhm, well, it's hard, and there's these boss fights behind fog gates. Sometimes you have to run through a bunch of guys to fight the boss again if you die. The bosses are pretty hard. Theres also this healing item that refills at these things called bonfires. To dodge attacks, you kinda just roll through 'em. Did I mention that it's hard? That's like, the most important part."
And hard they made it. In the worst way possible.

Bosses in this game are actually quite laughably easy, boring, and forgettable, despite what I just said. I'm not quite through the game yet (I believe I'm about halfway done), and I know there's at least 2 harder bosses coming my way, but I haven't gotten to those yet. Every boss so far has been laughable, I've beaten around 10 of them, most on the first try. Half the melee attacks from bosses that aren't that much larger than the player can be dodged by walking backwards and to one side. Which isn't a bad choice of game design, considering how much more committal rolling is in this game than in other games. But it also makes for really boring boss fights.

In fact, any 1v1 in this game so far has been laughable, minus some NPC invaders. The game developers recognized this and made it so there are barely any 1v1s. When you play Dark Souls 2, ask not "Will I get jumped?" but "From where will I get jumped?" This in itself is not an issue, it's pretty standard for Souls Games, even if Dark Souls 2 turns it up to 11. But the enemy placement is, again, a Gartic Phone'd version of Dark Souls 1. It puts the emphasis on "How do we make this as unfair as possible for the player?" instead of making it hard, but fair.

Where other Souls games make you check your corners and not blindly rush in to avoid getting swarmed, Dark Souls 2 expects you to have the patience of a camouflaged real-life army sniper behind enemy lines, army crawling your way forward while spinning your camera around every 3 seconds like a schizophrenic on Adderall to find out if that step you just took triggered the aggro of an enemy 17 parallel universes behind you.

The enemy placement is responsible for unironically 95% of the game's difficulty.

The vast majority of the game is spent getting from one forgettable boss fight to another.
What makes the game hard is the fact that you will get jumped by enemies like there's no tomorrow.
And there a LOT of enemies between you and every boss fight.
You're able to run through them, sure, but animations like opening doors or going through fog gates aren't invincible in this game. That way, the developers force you to somehow take care of the absurd number of enemies between you and the boss that's actually required for progression.

If you kill the same enemy (not enemy type, actually the same enemy) often enough, they will stop spawning.
"So, this is to prevent farming?", you may ask. Perhaps that's part of it. I'm convinced it's the intended way to get to a boss fight, though.

"Well, we can't make any single enemy interesting to fight in this game, so let's just force the player to kill the same enemies 20 times to get them to stop spawning so they can make their way to the boss more easily. Their playtime will be higher this way, making this game seem harder."

FYI, running through enemies is still the better option 90% of the time as opposed to fighting them, from my experience. But I refuse to believe the respawn limit (which can be circumvented with a covenant which is supposed to "make the game harder", further proving my point that it's supposed to make the atrocious boss runbacks slightly more bearable if you do it enough) has any other reason to exist.

The world at large feels honestly very same-y compared to Dark Souls 1, though I like Majula as a hub area a lot.
The NPCs are largely uninspired and forgettable.
Some NPC's feel just straight up ripped out of Dark Souls 1 so hard that they left their whole personality in the first game.
The emerald herald is a new and welcome addition, though. Though she's still not really all that interesting in terms of personality in the grand scheme of things.

As for lore, it feels like someone mistook Dark Soul's 1 intentionally hard to find (and sometimes incomplete) lore as it being intentionally confusing.
Maybe it's me missing something this time around, but Dark Souls 2 feels like it just wants to seem mysterious and edgy above all. I'm somewhat of a lore fanatic for Dark Souls 1, and 3 to a lesser extent, but 2's lore leaves me largely uninterested.
The map design is alright. Large parts of the map are actually fairly interconnected, though it is confusing to find your way around, I found myself being pleasantly surprised at how the different regions can interconnect and have multiple possible entrances.

Fragrant branches of yore are kind of a double edged sword, because you rarely know what you're getting.
If you're struggling to find branches and you use the last one on some for your run uninteresting item or a different entrance to a place you've already cleared, then it feels awful.
But sometimes you find stuff like the shaded woods entrance from Majula, which offers new ways to progress that you may not have had, which is a cool concept.
Overall, I don't like the branches that much.

Some people really like Dark Souls 2. I think it might be an just alright game.
However, it's an absolutely terrible Souls Game that puts way more effort into looking like a souls game than feeling like a souls game.

Other Dark Souls have a lot of straight up iconic bosses. Dark Souls 2 doesn't.
Other Dark Souls are hard but fair. Dark souls 2 is just hard, at any cost.

Other Dark Souls are set in a dark world with NPCs with varying interesting personalities, despite the dark in-universe times. Other Dark Souls want to lift your spirit, they want to keep you going. Dark Souls makes you die, not to make you feel bad, but to let you learn from your mistakes.

Dark Souls 2 is set in a dark world filled with largely uninteresting NPCs that have little in terms of personality.
In the intro, Dark Souls 2 tells you it will make you die, and it keeps that promise.
Dark Souls 2 makes you die, because "It's Dark Souls. You're supposed to die."
And it will make you die. Over and over and over again.

A single NPC line in Dark Souls 1 can give me more motivation to finish that game than anything in Dark Souls 2 could so far.

Don't you dare go hollow.

EDIT: Finished the game. It did not get better. It was bad through the end.

This is probably the most difficult review I've written in my life. Ladies and gentlemen, this is Dark Souls 2 SotFS edition. It's the worst version of the worst From Software game out there (major titles anyway). However, I am glad DS2 happened. DS2 was the sacrificial lamb that gave us precious titles like DS3 and Bloodborne. Also, the circumstances of the game's development contributed heavily to many of its less enjoyable elements, so you cannot judge it too harshly. However, this is a From Software game, and the successor to the legendary Dark Souls, and will thus be judged as such.

I'm not going to dive into the specifics of why this game feels bad, because the reasons for that are very technical and will take up a huge chunk of this review. However there are some really talented youtubers out the who have done a wonderful job at analysing the game's flaws. Youtubers like MauLer, Ch0pper and hbomberguy are great if you want to watch some videos on the subject.

The reasons why this game is not liked to the same degree of its brothers can be boiled down to one thing: The developers made the game hard for the sake of being hard, and not to provide a fun challenge for the player to overcome. The difficulty of most things in this game feels artificial: from the almost comical way enemies gank you, to the almost complete lack of windups, parry windows and punish windows for a LOT of enemy encounters. Another big reason for the adversity is the handicapped ("slow") combat. You are weaker in almost EVERY single aspect in the game vs DS1: more stamina consumption, less hp gain per level, less damage, less poise damage, less poise, longer healing animation time (a LOT more), and less i-frames depending on apd stat. Then there's the hitboxes, level design, lack of f@cking i-frames for fog gates and even f@cking reposte animations, the janky lock-on, and just the Adaptability stat as a whole...

In closing, I always laughed at those guys who say that they like a game and then they downvote it, but now I kind of understand where some of them might be coming from. This is a decent game, however, its flaws were simply too great to ignore, and they killed the experience for me to a degree. There was a point in the game where I just felt complete apathy for everything and just sprinted my way to 100%. That's not how a game should ever make you feel. That being said, there are still a LOT of people who enjoy this game, even with all its flaws - for some it is even their favourite title in the trilogy - but I am sadly not one of those people.

This is my second playthrough, i had already finished it on my old Xbox one back in the day. I know it's not the best in the series, but still when i play it i always get lost in this world and i feel very nostalgic thinking about the memories of the time.

The gameplay is slower, and i find it easier to experiment with different builds in this (and DS1), as opposed to the newer entries. The bosses are not very difficult compared to the other games, and you can summon NPCs for help almost everytime. The exploration of the levels is the difficult part, with all the bulls**t traps and gangs of enemies, but i just think it's the quirk of the game and i personally like it for what it is.

I can only play during the weekend, when my GF is taking a nap, but still while on launch-break at work i like to watch youtubers playing and explaining the lore and the secrets so when i come back i know what to do and i don't feel overwhelmed. To some this can sound like heresy, but doing this plus (so knowing what to do) and planning the build makes it easier to enjoy the game in mini-sessions where i try to complete a single area at a time.

The Steam Deck makes this all possible, I can put it in sleep and come back later and the game is still on; I can dock it to the TV and play on the couch with the PS5 controller; the game runs at 60fps flawlessly and has that retro-style graphics that are not GPU heavy.

I still find other players that invade me from time to time for some PVP, which I'm not a big fan of, but still if you like it the population is not completely dead in 2025.

TLDR: it's the black sheep of the series but i still love it

As a long fan of the freedom enabled through most Fromsoft games, I've always been the most fond of how Dark Souls 2 and especially Elden Ring worked. If you're someone who tried the other games and didn't like the concept of a dodge and attack fencing play, DS2 will sit better with you in terms of mechanics and options. If you level ADP, you can still play it the dodge and attack playstyle, but the rest are perfectly able to strafe most opponents quite fine, look for certain scenarios to exploit our own way, etc. DS2 also adds a few more elements than 1 and 3 such as powerstancing like weapons with the button combo to 2-hand the side weapon to enable a double weapon use moveset for like swords and whips, torch interactions that permanently ignite certain things in the terrain as a mechanic and even tool of making decisions to further imagine how a game's world could operate, and even some of the most smartest and creative enemy behaviors that add so much life to things. Something the game did better than its counterparts especially was how a NG+ should work, adding new enemy spawns to areas and events, and even increasing obtainable items from NPCs in later game cycles. If you were struggling with enemies in an area enough, defeating them 12 times makes them not spawn in on returning, but you have the option to reset their appearance as well as every item in an area to a boss you've defeated onto the next cycle's difficulty level if you want to go through them all again or even farm them for their loot and boss loot. And if you go into the DLC areas, you'll experience some of the best map behaviors seen in any of the games to the point that my favorite Fromsoft platformer pieces are actually a thing and in them, and I should be clear that I usually disapprove of their platformer sections in games. But the last big thing I must point out of this topic is the extra purpose behind things such as armor and rings you obtain such as the rings that do damage to enemies.

With Scholar of the First Sin edition we are exposed to a new and more competent arrangement to the enemies and even better enemy interactions like enemies that want you to challenge their counterparts for a more honorably earned safe passage. If some of the more excessive enemy appearances aren't your forte, then by all means basic DS2 may be more your speed, but you'll struggle to get some of the fashion more in the basic game version that I've personally spent a silly amount of time trying to get by upgrading area difficulties to farm, or miss out on the easier experience of Dragon Shrine.

One possible drawback to DS2 experience may be durability degrading much faster than in other games, but the developers made it more accessible to not let that happen from resting restoring their durability completely, and much greater variety of weapons that you can use that use and upgrade easily enough into a reliable backup. Trying out different stuff is much easier to consider in DS2 than in DS3 or DS1. Once you feel like you have a solid weapon in 1 or 3, you don't feel inclined to swap from them ever, but in DS2, it's particularly provoking you to consider trying out the immense variety, and some of which coming with more fun of surprises.

Posted: May 3
Scholar of the First Sin is more than just a repackaged version of the original Dark Souls II; it’s a reimagined take that feels like a remix of pain and discovery. FromSoftware revisits their controversial middle child with technical polish, enemy reshuffles, and narrative additions that reshape the experience for both veterans and newcomers.

The most immediate improvement is visual fidelity. Lighting effects, texture quality, and frame stability are noticeably upgraded, giving the world of Drangleic a darker, more oppressive beauty. It still lacks the architectural awe of Dark Souls I or the gothic splendor of Bloodborne, but there’s a haunting charm in its desolation.

Gameplay-wise, Scholar doesn’t radically alter the core mechanics—combat remains methodical and brutally unforgiving—but the repositioning of enemies and items makes familiar areas feel unpredictable. That Heide Knight you remember? He’s somewhere else now, and probably more aggressive. This makes second runs feel less like rote memorization and more like genuine exploration again.

The addition of Aldia, the titular “Scholar,” helps clarify and deepen the game’s otherwise obscure lore, threading new philosophical questions about the cycle of light and dark. His cryptic appearances challenge your understanding of the world, and in classic Souls fashion, leave you wondering if you ever understood it to begin with.

Not everything hits the mark. Enemy aggro ranges can feel unfair, and the difficulty occasionally veers into artificial territory—more enemies rather than smarter ones. Also, while the story additions are welcome, the fragmented nature of Souls storytelling means you’ll still need to piece together a lot through item descriptions and environmental clues.

Still, Scholar of the First Sin stands as the definitive version of Dark Souls II—a flawed, fascinating gem that rewards patience, curiosity, and a masochistic sense of determination.

Pros:
- Enhanced visuals and performance
- Rearranged enemy placements add unpredictability
- Lore additions deepen narrative themes
- Tons of content including all DLC

Cons:
– Some difficulty spikes feel forced
– Still the weakest world design in the trilogy
– Storytelling remains deeply opaque

While not the pinnacle of the Souls series, Scholar of the First Sin turns Dark Souls II into a richer, more cohesive experience. It’s the best way to suffer beautifully in Drangleic.

Rating: 8/10

This recommendation is shaky. While I don't regret my time with this game at all, and I think some things are better than the original DS1, there's also a lot wrong with this game.

The build variety is great, but the constant feeling of being ganked makes the game not as fun to play through. I played a hex/sorcery build, and had a great time, but I wonder how difficult a primarily melee run of this game would be.

The variety of locations is great; it's not just castles & cathedrals. However, the enemy placement can seem annoying at times.

I thought I would dislike the limited respawning enemies as I couldn't farm them, but it wasn't that big of an issue at all. It does a good enough job balancing soul cost with the limited souls.

What I didn't like was the fact that you could be invaded even if you were hollow. I tend to avoid pvp, and having it thrust upon me wasn't that fun. That being said, the invaders did tend to not be too cruel, and some were even helpful before attacking me.

All in all, I have mixed feelings about this game, but I on the whole enjoyed it. I recommend it, with a massive asterisk that says "know that this is not like DS1 or DS3".

Replaying after finishing the trilogy, DS2 stands out in a few ways. It experiments with a couple of new, esoteric mechanics that require explanation from the fan base, which is questionable. It's also noticeably harder than the others, with a higher count of ganky boss fights and areas that'll trip up even veteran DS players (Frigid Outskirts...)

So it might be rough landing coming at it straight from DS1 (something about the movement, for me) but sticking with it was very rewarding. The story's every bit of mysterious as the original, adding to the lore with its own twist and setting up the events of DS3. But my biggest point in its favour over the others is the wonderfully diverse and pretty environments, which really bring the world alive and give you that sense of epic, personal journey.

Posted: April 29
Hi.

So, to start off, I wanna say that i started out as a Dark Souls 3 player that sunk over 400 hours into that game before I decided to stop playing it and start playing the previous two games, Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 2. Dark Souls 2 was the last entry in the series that I played, and it was a truly unique journey for me unlike anything Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 3 gave me. Here, I'll try listing the things I liked and didn't like about the game and you can come to your own conclusion on whether or not to buy it (duh). Also by the way, I do not recommend playing the trilogy in backwards order because DS3 can give you a lot of bad habits that'll make you suffer in the other two games.


Here's the things I liked:

The game has a LOT of content. One of my biggest complaints about DS1 and even DS3 was that it didn't have enough; I wanted more, and DS2 really did give me more.

- The game has a truly large number of locations.
- An absolutely fantastic variety in color palette across all the locations.
- The game gives you multiple paths that you go through right from the start just like in DS1.
- The levels are for the most part more well thought-out than its prequel.
- The game has a sequel-worthy number of armors, weapons, spells, and items.
- A FANTASTIC variety of VIABLE builds.
- A great deal of quality-of-life improvements over DS1 such as the ATN (attunement) stat granting increased casting speed, increased spell uses, and increased attunement slots- all in one stat finally!
- Many mechanics that actually innovate and make it an overall large improvement over DS1 (more than it seems).
- Hollowing is an actual mechanic that's surprisingly done pretty well and thankfully there's multiple ways of making it less annoying if the player wishes to do so.
- Durability is finally a proper mechanic that's more than just mere boss weapon ammo.
- DS1's issue of players being able to run past enemies largely without consequences is resolved by making enemies persistent.
- Great and interesting lore that's about drangleic and the various kingdoms that have come before it and the effects of hollowing for humans.
- Great story along with two of the most well-written characters in the trilogy who-shall-not-be-named-cus-it's-spoilers, and the best ending that one will ever see in the trilogy (it's the alternate ending).
- The game has a consistent level of quality throughout the vast majority of its content and doesn't fall off halfway like how it did for DS1.
- Contrary to popular belief, the game actually overall has the tightest and most well-done hitboxes in the trilogy; the bad hitboxes problem is largely a myth.
- You can invade people regardless if they're hollow or not, and can disable invasions by burning a human effigy if you want.
- The game has the most balanced PvP experience.
- No ♥♥♥♥♥♥ invasion priority system (♥♥♥♥ you for that, DS3!).
- The combat requires more thought from the player.
- Successive rolls have a greater stamina cost.
- The LEVELS LEADING UP TO THE BOSSES are more of a challenge than the vast majority bosses themselves, meaning the devs put a greater priority on the actual meat of the game.
- The game has among the best DLC's in the trilogy.
- The game has lots of subtle charm and detail that they absolutely did not need to go through the effort to make, but I'm glad they did, such as your character huffing when you're low on stamina.
- And finally the game ACTUALLY DOES A LOT TO ITS NG+ such as more items, new items, more enemies, new enemies, some bosses get some changes, a new boss encounter, and new NG+ exclusive lore.

Many people like praising the addition the powerstance mechanic but I think the addition that is even more praise-worthy is the inclusion of proper dual-wielding that is never seen ever again after DS2; being able to have a weapon's full moveset in both the right AND left hand is a wonderful thing and I don't understand why it never returned. Oh and your left hand no longer holds all left hand weapons as if they're straight swords.


Here's the things I didn't like:

Right away I will tell you that you will not see "ganks" and "a million enemies" in this list, because those are not flaws of this game; it's not as bad as people make it seem if you actually think things through.

- Too many healing items. I especially dislike that Melentia sells an unlimited number of lifegems at such a low cost so early into the game.
- ADP. I do like this stat for its ability to make heavy-weight builds more viable, and while it IS communicated as to what it does in the stats menu, I still believe that it was not communicated well enough to the player as to what it does because this singular stat ON ITS OWN is responsible for all the false "bad hitboxes" claims that this game has received.
- Illusory walls (excluding Pharros walls) requiring you to press the interact button to uncover. The game makes NO effort to tell you how to uncover such walls, and I believe that DS1 and DS3's method of uncovering illusory walls is far more practical for a new player to figure out on their own.
- No password matchmaking for co-op.
- Soul Memory as a matchmaking system while a pretty neat idea, is awfully executed.
- The world isn't interconnected like how it was in DS1.
- A lot of level transitions don't make sense, although you could try explaining them by saying that they accurately follow one of the game's core themes.
- Some of the really cool levels are too short (this is personal nitpick).
- The vast majority of the base game bosses SUCK!
- The easiest-to-get ending sucks.
- The first half of Iron Keep is terrible if you're a new player that's trying to do the boss runback for that part of the location. (tip: wear armor with actual poise and jump onto the platform just before the bridge for a shorter runback). I've no idea why they couldn't just rework that first half for Scholar of the First Sin's release!
- The lighting isn't as atmospheric as DS1, although this is a personal taste thing.


This game has been an incredible journey for me and despite its flaws I still really enjoy it. I'm super sad that we will most likely never get a game like this again, as it seems that FromSoftware and Soulslikes in general are more interested in making speedy games nowadays and also there being no sign that Yui Tanimura (the creator of DS2) is interested in making another game, which is sad because I believe he's capable of making great games based on the overall quality of DS2 and the DLC's he's directed for this game and the fact that he somehow saved the game from being an entirely unplayable mess in less than a year after taking over mid-development; I'm glad he's been Miyazaki's co-director ever since, though! Good for him!

Thank you for reading my review. If you're confused as to which version of DS2 to get, then just know that DS2 Scholar of the First Sin is in many ways objectively the better version of the game; more lore-accurate enemy placements and some improved item descriptions, more quality-of-life changes, DS2 Scholar's lighting makes the areas' atmosphere stand out more, and although DS2 Scholar has way more enemies that does not mean that it's worse; you'd be surprised to see how much more polished & well thought-out the enemy encounters are in DS2 Scholar compared to DS2 Vanilla, and how much more empty and barren DS2 Vanilla's locations are- there's even some missing important lore in DS2 Vanilla if you care about that. Also, DS2 Scholar's AI enemies have anti-gank behaviour programmed into them- go search it up if you want to learn more. This is the conclusion I've come to after playing both versions of the game multiple times each: DS2 Scholar is better than DS2 Vanilla.

Also, I've written some new player advice incase anyone reading this wants some; It's contained in a screenshot because I've ran out of writing space in this review.

Posted: April 29
The black sheep of the Souls Trilogy is my favorite first-party Soulslike.

Before I get into the meat of this review, I'd like to go over some of my history with this game... This was the first game I ever bought the deluxe edition for, way back on the 360, before SotFS, and is the only game I took part in a beta for, namely the network test in Huntsman's Copse on the PS3. (Yes, I owned both consoles.) The OG Dark Souls is one of my absolute favorite games of all time, so my excitement for this game was off the charts before it came out.

I will also mention 2 secret challenges that are important to the game design, so be forewarned.

2 major design decisions are very important to understanding Dark Souls 2. Those two design decisions are:

1. The engine and movement, which comes with the sometimes shoddy hitboxes and the need for the ADP stat, which dictates not only the amount of invincibility frames when rolling, but the speed you can use/recover from your Estus Flask.
2. The 2 secret challenge rings, the Illusory Ring of the Exalted and the Illusory Ring of the Conqueror, which are obtained from beating the game without resting at a bonfire or dying. This necessitates design around an absurd amount of consumable items, including life-restoring items, that is a MAJOR step back from the extremely tight design focus on the Estus Flask as the primary mode of healing in the other 2 mainline Souls games.

Now, the hitbox issues with the game don't make it unplayable, but they do basically make it so that, after you have a few points in your stats to use your weapon, you'll be pumping ADP up to at least 16. If you do not, your roll is pitifully bad. Also, I won't sugarcoat it... You'll die due to bad hitboxes every 2-4 hours or so, mostly due to being hit by a lingering hitbox from an enemy (level ADP more, scrub!) or by being teleported into a grab that you clearly avoided. And it'll suck. It's not gamebreaking. The game is not unplayable by any means, but it is sometimes VERY irritating, especially compared to how tight the gameplay and hit detection is in the other 2 Dark Souls games. Sometimes, it really does feel like a Soulslike instead of a first-party FromSoft title.

Now, touching also on the second point, Rock Candy Souls™ has a balance issue... and that is the consumable known as lifegems. They were touted as the out-of-combat option to heal while the Estus Flask was supposed to be for immediate health, but it's actually the opposite... Lifegems can be used WHILE WALKING, at half speed. I repeat: You can dance out of your enemies' range while popping your rock candy. Their use speed and recovery is constant throughout your journey and is NOT affected by the ADP stat, unlike your Flask. What's more, the gems will continue to heal you while you until they run out or you reach full HP... So, a good strategy is to pop 2-3 lifegems around half health and trade blows with your enemies while your gems continue to heal you. This flips the healing on its head... The lifegems are your in-combat option, more often than not, while the Flask is for when you're not in danger, your ADP stat be damned. In this way, the consumables spam (including one that restores spell uses) makes it feel more like its initial progenitor, Demon's Souls, than Dark Souls 1. It's not a dealbreaker, but it does feel like a step back, design-wise, but you may feel right at home if you liked Demon's Souls.

Now, from what you've read, you may conclude that this game is crap. It is true that it is the weakest of the 3 Souls games, from movement and balance standpoints... but the game is fun. And the best selling point of the game is that, it will be cheap to you... It will ambush you, stack a group of enemies against you, etc. etc. But... the game gives you all the tools you need to fight back, and then some. It encourages experimentation.

And I think that that is Dark Souls 2's greatest strength - it will beat the crap out of you, but it will arm you with all the tools that you need to beat it. A plethora of weapons, consumables and titanite... A number of souls and spells to try on enemies, new enemy locations and phantoms in New Game Plus as well as new boss souls for spells and boss weapons. (Right down the road!) The game has a large amount of replayability, maybe even more than the first game, as much as it may pain me to say it.

Also, I would be remiss to not mention the 4 DLCs that Scholar comes with, and they are great (even if a certain section of the ice DLC is.. irritating, to put it mildly). Their quality, both in general and their inclusion in the overall lore of the game is great, and they have the best boss fights in the game, imo. Beating them will also but you should just play them and find out what that is.

If you want more Souls content, with the above asterisks in mind, I DO recommend getting Dark Souls 2: SotFS, ESPECIALLY if you can pick it up for under 20 dollars. You'll get HUNDREDS of hours of gameplay, plenty of replayability, TONS of stuff to experiment with, and 4 high-quality DLCs, even if the ADP stat is.. well, dumb, and the hitboxes are occasionally questionable.

Of all the souls games I've played, this one is definitely my least favorite. Dark Souls 2 is a good game, but the areas where it lacks are pretty frustrating, and it definitely deserves a large amount of the criticism it gets. I played DS1 directly before starting this, and I was amazed at how much better it felt to play it's predecessor.

Things Dark Souls 2 does well:
- Majula and the Central Hub, music, ambiance, sound, etc. here are incredible. I loved coming back here. I also enjoyed seeing NPCs here as I progressed.
- NPCs and dialogue were awesome as per usual Fromsoft games. I felt pretty immersed in the world and story.
- Music is great... simple and effective.
- Environments and world detail are next level. The entire game was awesome to look at

Things Dark Souls 2 DIDN'T do well:
- The lock on mechanic is INFURIATING. What the hell went wrong here? Every time I lock on to an enemy, my game has a seizure trying to lock on to any enemy within a 300 mile radius. God forbid you're fighting more than one and are trying to poke them with a rapier. Weapons with sweeping attacks helped so I wouldn't have to lock but my lord did this make me rage. Seriously... it will lock on to anything besides the target you want. I thought maybe this was just me switching between targets accidentally, but nope. Even without any character movement, it will bounce all over the place.
- Combat in general felt clunky compared to DS 1. Not slow; clunky. I like slow, methodical combat, but things just didn't have a polished feel. Enemies would be able to hit you with frontal weapon swings while you are behind them, input commands are super slow, animations aren't correct (1hand to 2hand animation has you putting something in your pocket even if you weren't holding anything in your off hand), and weapon swings would miss for no reason sometimes when locked on. If you saw a titanite bug, good luck... even if you're locked on, your weapon will still probably swing over it. Your character's eyes go blind from the knees down apparently. It creates a whole new and unfun meta-game trying to kill those things.
- The game relied on gimmicks rather than difficult enemy encounters. Mechanically, nothing was too bad, but the amount of traps, got-yas, and ambushes took some of the fun out of it. These things are expected, but it was an overused tool. Bosses were by far the easiest part of the game.

I know it looks like I hated the game, but I really didn't. It was an awesome experience outside of the main draw of the game which is the combat. The exploration was awesome, the art is incredible, and the sense of awe when entering areas was nailed to me. That's why I kept going... the world they've created is something special. I'm going to take a souls break before DS3, but that's coming up shortly for me.

Short answer, buy it, play it, but go in with lower expectations and a patient mind.

Rating: 10/10

FromSoftwares Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin stands as the series most divisive entry, yet beneath its imperfections lies a world that continues the haunting legacy of its predecessor. While it may lack the perfect interconnectedness of Lordran, Drangleic offers its own poignant vision of decay, built on themes of identity, memory, and the inevitability of ruin.

Drangleic is a kingdom where the past repeats itself endlessly, a reflection of a world trying and failing to break free from the cycle of fire and dark. Its ruins are not just broken physically, but spiritually, hollowed by time itself.

Unlike Lordrans tightly woven geography, Drangleic’s lands feel more fragmented, almost dreamlike, emphasizing the games meditation on fading purpose. This structural shift mirrors the players own growing disorientation, as the search for truth leads only to more questions. Characters like King Vendrick, Nashandra, and Aldia embody different responses to this cycle from denial, to corruption, to reluctant understanding.

Though Dark Souls II sometimes stumbles under the weight of expectation, its lore remains rich and rewarding for those willing to listen carefully. It doesn't merely expand the universe; it deepens the tragedy, showing that even knowledge and power are powerless against times slow, inevitable erosion.

In the end, Scholar of the First Sin reminds us that in a dying world, the struggle to hold onto purpose, even in the face of oblivion, is itself an act of defiance. It may not shine as brightly as Lordran, but its fading echoes are no less haunting.
