It's a decent game, but only in a vacuum. Not as bad as the haters say, certainly not as good as the fanboys say, and most definitely not the best souls game.

If you can get used to its flaws it is fun, just like any other Dark Souls game, but that doesn't mean the flaws aren't there:
8-directional snap point movement, I-frameless interact animations and ADP all suck, and I really have no clue why they were implemented, and trying to justify them is stupid. I'm not even kidding when I say this, if ADP and I-frameless interact animations weren't a thing, I'd like this game maybe twice as much. The fog walls make runbacks extremely tedious.
Warping between bonfires, while convenient, makes shortcuts much less meaningful and the relief of finding a new bonfire isn't nearly as cathartic as the first game, because there's a lot of them.
Lifegems nullify a lot of the game's difficulty, and make healing pretty low-risk, as opposed to the first and third game's calculated decision making, where pulling off a successful heal is immensely satisfying.
The world is littered with desolate, prop-less corridors that just make it seem half-assed. Geographically, the world design makes no sense at multiple points as well. So many places exist where they physically cannot, like the infamous Windmill elevator, which could've easily been much less ridiculous if it simply went *down*, just saying...
The bossfights, even with a fair share of highlights, don't come close to the boss quality of the rest of the series. There's a lot of pushovers that can be obliterated just by circle-strafing. Dragonrider is a joke lol.

There's still good things about this game, but I can't think of many that don't also apply to the rest of the games. The build variety is as good as you'd expect. The combat is passable, but hindered by the movement and ADP. One seemingly cool thing this game added is power stancing, but I never used it because it never suited my playstyle. Bonfire ascetics are an amazing concept as well.

In this game's defense, I think the ganking, despite being awful at select points, is really not that bad for the most part. Also, the DLC has a lot more interesting level design compared to the base game.

I recommend this *only* to people who are somewhat experienced in souls games. It can be fun, but it's an inferior experience to the other Dark Souls games.

Posted: April 23
Frustrating, Still Good

Dark Souls 2 is a good game, just not a great Dark Souls game. It still features great combat, a vast world that's fun to explore and full of secrets, and a ton of fun weapons and builds to use. If you're new to the series, I don't recommend starting with this one as it is frustratingly difficult at parts, expecting the player to beat hoards of enemies like it's a warriors style game and not a Dark Souls game. It still features some good bosses but it's definitely the weakest of the series that I've played so far.

Combat

Dark Souls 2 features the same combat as the first one but the main difference is the enemy placement and boss runbacks. Some of these runbacks are absolutely horrendous and lead to you spending more time just trying to get back to bosses. The bosses themselves are kind of a let down, but there's still a couple great ones in the mix. While learning how to deal with multiple enemies does a while to nail down, once you figure it out it can make challenging parts a breeze and actually quite fun. Early on there's a boss with 3 enemies that seemed downright impossible at first but learning how to deal with them and create openings lead to a satisfying experience that I'll look back fondly on.

World Design

I don't want to talk about the enemy placements too much, as it's been talked about to death but it is a genuine issue with the game and a big one. Too many areas are just filled with enemies that are difficult to deal with and create what feels like artificial difficulty. The world itself though I enjoyed quite a bit. There's some truly great areas here including some really fun dungeons and castles to explore. I do wish the world was more interconnected like Dark Souls was but as a whole I think it's not bad and it has some really cool areas and set pieces.


In conclusion, Dark Souls 2 isn't a bad game at all, it's just a bad Dark Souls game. I had fun with it despite its frustrating parts and I do think if you're a fan of others in the series that it's worth checking out. Getting this on sale is still a very worthy purchase that you will get your moneys worth from. For more reviews, follow my

Posted: April 22
Dark Souls II is a game that both impresses and frustrates. While it preserves the soul of the series, it delivers it in a somewhat offbeat, occasionally awkward package.

More content – but is it better?
In terms of size and content, DS2 surpasses its predecessor. Dozens of locations, tons of weapons, armor sets, spells – it’s a treasure trove that can keep you busy for dozens of hours. But the sheer scale comes at a cost. The world design often lacks logic, and the map feels less interconnected and coherent than in Dark Souls.

Combat – a change in rhythm
Combat still revolves around patience and precision, but the pacing feels... off. Characters move more sluggishly, and animations have a strange “lag” to them – like intentional delay between input and action. It may turn off veterans of the first game.

Difficulty? Uneven.
Dark Souls II can definitely challenge you, but not always in a fair way. Instead of the signature “fair challenge,” it sometimes leans into cheap tricks: enemy spam, frustrating traps, and bosses that are... just a bunch of regular enemies thrown together. There are incredible fights (Fume Knight!), but they’re scattered among forgettable ones.

Visuals and atmosphere – hit or miss
The audiovisual presentation is inconsistent. Some areas are beautifully atmospheric (Drangleic Castle, Shrine of Amana), others feel flat or uninspired. The much-hyped lighting engine from early previews was scaled back – and it shows.

Is it worth playing?
Yes, if you're a fan of the series looking for a different Souls-like experience. No, if you're expecting the same level of polish as DS1 or Bloodborne. Dark Souls II is an experiment – bold, but not entirely successful. Still, it has a unique charm. That melancholic, forgotten-world vibe... it pulls you in.

Pros:

Huge world and tons of content

Some truly atmospheric zones

Great DLC (especially Crown of the Old Iron King)

Cons: − Uneven level and boss quality
− Sluggish animations and odd combat feel
− Less cohesive world design than other Souls titles

Recently came back to this after many years for a fresh look. My first time playing the Scholar edition. After a few rounds of Elden Ring i started replaying the older souls games for a more recent perspective. I found that a lot of the things this game is maligned for aren't actually particularly true. It's also not as difficult as i remember it being years ago.

The combat is faster than ds1 but still slower than later games. The AI reflects this also, as most enemies are pretty easy to trick or sidestep, with a few exceptions. Even if you aggro everything in the area, they'll typically take turns one at a time. Or you can easily lure from groups 1 at a time with arrows. Game also gives you a really expansive variety of equipment and spells.

The VAs, OSTs, characters and story are all what you'd expect from a FromSoft game, as well as the scenic environments.

For content the game is probably twice as long as Demons Souls and slightly longer than DS1, but half the size of Elden Ring.

The runbacks aren't really a big deal, most are under a minute. The exceptions i'd say are Darklurker and the DLC's 3 optional co-op section fights which can be kind of obnoxious. FWIW i beat more than half the bosses in 1 try anyway and a substantial portion of the rest on the 2nd.

With combat the 2 main issues i experienced were occasional jank with lock-on. That exists in all these games but in this one sometimes felt especially weird. Also with directional attacks, changing direction on the second swing or after a roll, it's not as precise as later games.

I got this game alongside the 2 other Dark Souls games after beating Elden Ring. I played DS1 and absolutely loved it, I then moved on to DS3 and loved it even more than DS1 and Elden Ring... It was all peaceful until my friend reminded me that I hadn't played DS2 and plunged into the Abyss and mentally I have not recovered.

When I say I hate this game I don't mean that I hate it as a standard video game, if this was its own thing and possibly made by another company i'd say valiant effort and good attempt at mimicking the soulsborne experience... But its not its own thing, its a part of the Darksouls franchise and therefore I have to judge it based on the other games and it simply doesn't hold up.

To put into basic perspective, every soulsborne game (And no i'm not including Sekiro) has multiplayer, if you're playing online you can summon other players to help you or you can get invaded by other players that want to kill you. Every game has the option to turn off online and play single player except for this game and the only way to turn it off completely is either playing without WIFI our using a firewall to block the online. And it's ironic because this is the only soulsborne game you cannot get banned on. Thats right, you heard me, you cannot get banned on DS2 because there is no anti-cheat and zero community support. I have come across so many cheaters on this game that I've nearly lost count, I'll never forget getting instantly killed by a guy with no weapon and no armor.

Asides from the multiplayer problems there also the actual gameplay issues, Scholar of the First Sin randomly places enemies in areas that weren't there in the original and the hitboxes can the jankiest I've ever seen in a game like this. You'll get hit from 3-4ft away and then be inches close to an enemy and miss your swings.

And this game IS connected to both DS1 and DS3, there are both blatant and subtle connections to both games, but when playing this game asides from a few nods here and there to the previous title it feels very secluded and like its own thing. I have to state that this is my opinion and I know many souls players who have dubbed this as their favorites but I did not have a good time playing this on the first go around and neither the second or the third time beating this game did I feel like I actually enjoyed the game I was playing.

This isn't an awful game nor is it a bad game, but it's not a good one either and its unfortunately just average and I can't really suggest it.

Posted: April 16
Overrated or Underrated? Just Rated* actually.

I actually played Dark Souls II Vanilla as my first souls game and loved it for how ambient the game felt; personally I feel it does ambiance the best with how diverse its settings and levels are. Especially the DLCs. The attention to detail in DS II is also more than I can say for any other for the trilogy. You can knock a rock down a well, and a body comes up; that's gold. Ivory King is also absolute cinema. A giant spider webbing up a dragon is both disturbing and so metal. So, having never played First Sin, tried it out to rekindle my love for the souls series to only be met with a few huge issues:

Hitbox Jank
I swing a mace right in front of an enemy and it misses. Hitting enemies will also knock them slightly back OUT OF YOUR RANGE and escape your combo. Range also feels very nerfed for the player. I swing a bastard sword and I still can't hit sh*; always by a needle's length too short. Meanwhile enemies seem to have plenty range since their attacks also often bring them forward and chase. Meanwhile your attacks you commit to locks you right where you stand. So while you knock enemies out of your own combos, THEY can keep wailing on you even if you get knocked back. With strafing an effective tactic in DS II, this wouldn't be as big of an issue as it is if it wasn't for enemy spawns...

First Sin Exclusive Enemy Spawns
First Sin's enemy spawns are actually... atrocious. Ambushes. Everywhere. Ganks. Everywhere. And you can't really know about a lot of these ambushes before-hand so you end up paying a death just to gather information. Earthen Peak Mannequins is what comes to mind for this; what a slog. Heide's Tower is also horrible with all the Heide knights now scattered everywhere. If you beat the DragonRider before the DragonSlayer... good luck getting to the draw-bridge. Also, there will be enemies put right in front of fog gates so you can't rush through a lot of the time. There are a bajillion Alonne knights before the Smelter Demon I don't remember existing in Vanilla. Interacting with the world is purposely slow and doesn't provide i-frames for the first half of the animations so you can't get away with even entering. Enemies aggro will also chase you down to the ends of the earth until you recreate the marathon scene from Forrest Gump. So you either try your luck rushing, or go through and clear the horde everytime which just slows down progress to a crawl. Yes. Enemies go extinct EVENTUALLY, but that's not good enough. Its intentionally despicable and utterly pathetic overhaul that wastes your time and ruins the game.

Combat Scam
Enemies are also just generally faster and can spontaneously speed up to land trades or cheap hits on you while you take an eternity to recover from staggering. I also find dealing damage in this game produce really unreliable and inconsistent numbers; sometimes I'll two-tap enemies but then other times I'll three-tap them... and often times they survive on a sliver of health so I am almost convinced their HP is programmed to just demand another swing from the player that we won't expect. That's a real pain when I have trouble even HITTING them half the time. Estus is also a suicide button since you have to stand completely still for... I timed it... 2.47 seconds. Heal only goes off the second half of the animation with healing speed so slow it won't make up with the 3 seconds, more or less, that enemies have to kill you. That's plenty of time since enemies, again, are just faster than you. Only life gems are a viable healing option since you can keep walking and use it. Just a lot of little, frustrating things that are exacerbated because of, again, horrible enemy spawns.

* TLDR: First Sin's enemy spawns ruin the game. Still, I recommend... Vanilla. Despite other flaws, there are a lot of good non-combatant things to experience from DS II.

Posted: April 16
My feelings on this game are complicated, but I DO recommend it if you're already a fan of FromSoftware's games and enjoy soulslikes. This probably isn't the best entry point to the series, so for anyone looking to get into the genre, try Dark Souls, Dark Souls 3, or Elden Ring.

There are a lot of things to criticize about the game, and a lot of people have covered them at length, just look up "Dark Souls 2 Critique" on YouTube and you'll get something. Given that in-depth breakdowns about the game's flaws are out there in abundance and anyone can find them, I actually want to highlight what I enjoy about this game that makes me recommend it, in spite of its numerous shortcomings.

- The game is gorgeous (most of the time), with the most vibrant and pleasant to look at locations in the entire trilogy (Majula, Heide's Tower of Flame, Brume Tower, Shrine of Amana, etc.)
- The developers clearly wanted to experiment with the established Souls formula, and while some experiments were failures (Hollowing, ADP), others were really cool, and either were or should have been included in future entries (Bonfire Ascetics, Estus progression)
- The lore of Drangleic is very interesting, and may be some of my favorite lore in the trilogy
- The DLCs (which are included in Scholar of the First Sin) are all very interesting, and have the best bosses in the game (and some of the best of the trilogy)

Now some gripes that plagued me personally:
- The lock-on system is done weirdly, as you can still attack in other directions, meaning I could (and frequently did) attack backwards or sideways while locked onto an enemy
- For all the areas that I like, some of them are just plain crummy, and there are some truly awful runbacks
- The world design is bizarre, consisting of multiple relatively linear paths branching out from the hub area. But don't follow any one of them to the end too quickly or the difficulty will ramp up too much. Instead, tackle the first area of each branch off, then the second, so on. World progression in this game is strange.
- Once you get past the initial hurdles and level ADP a bit, the base game is just easy, most of the time. The bosses are mostly pushovers, and the only real challenge usually comes from bad/excessive enemy placement

Okay so more complaints than I meant to include. Which definitely says something about the game. But at the same time, I've really enjoyed this playthrough (mostly) and do recommend it for those who like soulslikes if your sentiment, like mine, was: "I just want more Dark Souls." Basically, this game is weird, and bad in a lot of ways, and also I love it because Fume Ultra Greatsword. And really, what more does a man need?

Ah, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin. The creative peak of FromSoftware, a true gem among gravel. A game that boldly declared: “Interconnected levels? Nah. Let’s have a magic elevator connecting a windmill to a lava castle instead.”

Combat? As refined as a washing machine full of knives. Slow, clunky, and featuring hitboxes drawn by a blindfolded toddler.
Ever dreamed of a stat that saves your life but doesn’t tell you what it does? Welcome, adaptability.

Enemies are everywhere. Literally. Why create balanced encounters when you can just toss three giants into a broom closet?
And the bosses? Pure originality—except when they’re not. Because fighting the same one again, but this time with two friends? Genius.

The real magic? Scholar of the First Sin. It reshuffled enemy placements with all the logic of a fever dream.
Already learned the area? Too bad. Now it’s filled with invisible ogres with sniper rifles. (Almost.)

Level design? A dadaist poem. You’ll go from castle to poison cave to sky fortress with no rhyme or reason. And it’s beautiful. In a way.

But that’s the thing—DS2 isn’t just a game. It’s a spiritual trial.
An experience so absurd it makes you appreciate every other Souls game like it was hand-crafted by angels.

Score: 10/10. The best game ever made. Play it once in your life.

review for Dark Souls II on PC with keyboard and mouse after now just beating the game at level 205 with 102 hours played:

Absolutely fantastic. Amazing character progression, graphics, and boss designs (Wayyyyy more bosses than dark souls 1). The changed invasion system makes it so there is always an element of danger. Showing any keyboard keybinds in the UI: It just shows xbox controls in the UI no matter what you do. Half the functions of the UI like "Show item info" and "Help" I never knew what keybind it was, so I couldn't even press those buttons. I focused my build to limit the amount of time I spent in the menus due to this.
The UI once you rebind something: It just doesn't change, the UI will still show random xbox controls for something mapped to PC controls
The default binds provided for keyboard and mouse: The default binds are so bad to the point that I was attempting to refund my first hour in. The only thing that saved this game from refund was my steam auth being on an old phone that wasn't charged
Keybinds not being able to be bound to more than 1 output: In darksouls 1 and elden ring, both the "back" buttons and target lock buttons could both be bound to "Q". This isn't possible for dark souls 2 as it only lets you choose between target lock OR back, no two buttons
Disable Double Click bug: by default, the game allows for double clicking for light vs strong attacks. This introduces about a 200ms delay to guarding and attacking which is awful. You can disable this in the keybinds and remove the delay, but there is a bug that requires you do this every single time you launch the game.

I love Dark Souls 2. It's such a strangely controversial game. Mention an opinion about it anywhere and a fifty-comment argument is sure to follow.

Is Dark Souls 2 perfect? By no means. But it nails the sense of "adventure" as good as, if not better, than any other FromSoft title. If that's what you play these games for, don't you dare skip this one.

It's the most non-linear game after Demon's Souls, but unlike Demon's Souls, it's massive. The first half gives you four bosses to slay in any order, while the second half is a linear shot to the end... BUT with the three DLCs included in this Scholar edition, the completion of which is needed to fight the true final boss and get the best ending, suddenly you have four paths to clear in any order again. It's like Demon's Souls but twice as long, and that's a great thing! In many ways this is "Demon's Souls 2" as much as "Dark Souls 2", even if the lore is connected to that of Dark Souls.

Another similarity to (and improvement upon) Demon's Souls is the healing system. You rely on consumables to heal, but you also have an estus flask to rely upon in a pinch, so you never feel like you have to grind for healing before fighting bosses. It's the best consumable healing system From has ever done. Having the estus heal you quickly and the lifegems heal you gradually is just the icing on the cake.

The combat is a bit more sluggish than usual, but that fits the slow, deliberate pre-Bloodborne pace of FromSoft's fantasy epics. Unfortunately, the bosses do suffer as a result. Many are still fun, but they're neither as outlandishly creative as the games preceding DS2 nor as addictively responsive and challenging as the ones that came later. There's nothing on par with Artorias or Ornstein & Smough from DS1... but also nothing so frustrating as the Bed of Chaos. The bosses are just kind of there, outside of a few highlights.

As to enemy placement, there are a lot of enemies, but the same could be said for certain areas in the other FromSoft games and I think DS2 gets an unfairly bad rap for this. It's probably because enemies are harder to run away from than usual, chasing you much farther. But take everyone down, gradually chipping away at a level, and it's really no worse than the other From games. Plus, the enemies eventually despawn! No other Souls game grants you that mercy. The enemy placement is fine. I was also never bothered by not having as many interconnected levels or world design. Yeah, it would be impressive, but 99% of games don't do this and they're still fun. Same with Dark Souls 2 here.

That's the key. Dark Souls 2 doesn't reach the same heights as the most universally beloved FromSoft games, but neither do 99% of games that are still pretty good. And Dark Souls 2, with its addictive action-adventure formula, is better than "pretty good". It's great. Don't skip it.

As other reviews will attest this game isn't what most people make it out to be. Still, I find it difficult to give a glowing review, but also difficult to give it a terrible review. The game has issues with enemy placement, certain boss fights/designs, and combat that generally does not feel as good as DS1 or 3, yet it also has pretty interesting lore, some of the most beautiful environments in the series, and the majority of the areas are well thought out and varied. Because of those points and many more, my feelings toward the game teeter back and forth each time I play it, but even though I ultimately tell myself "I won't be playing this one again" after completing it, I find myself going through it again each time I replay the series and still having fun more often than not.

I do ultimately agree that it is the worst in the series, but it is still a good (sometimes great) souls game overall. If DS1 & 3 are 4.5-5 star games, I'd say DS2 is ~4 stars. So maybe being the worst in a series of essentially perfect titles isn't actually that bad... I'd decide for yourself though, pick it up on a sale if nothing else and give it a shot.

I originally marked this game as 'Not recommended'. However, after playing for a while longer I'd say it's a disappointing addition to the souls franchise although not unplayable.
Recommended 6/10.

The problem with DS2 is it feels like a SquareEnix spin off of a souls game.

My points below are just summing up why this is a disappointing sequel.

- They look worse in general. NPCs have mannerisms that twist their arms out their sockets like early PS2 characters. Environments feel block-y with copy paste assets everywhere.

- Cheap and generic. Previous titles are immersive and make you feel like you are exploring a real environment. DS2 feels very artificial and not particularly fun to look at or explore. There are shortcuts and the world links together, sort of, which is nice. But this doesn't redeem the bland scenery.

- A big regression. The camera now works against you, jumping is harder as a result. Part of the fun of previous titles is the ease of character control and how user skill can be expressed as well as influence outcomes throughout the game. DS2 requires that you level adaptability (and in general) before your character becomes easier to control and listen to your commands more accurately. Also everything feels much slower and clunkier in general. These changes add up to largely degraded experience when compared with DeS or DS1.

- It feels lifted from another IP with cheap sounds thrown in. I agree the menu system in previous games wasn't the best but this new menu system is busy and makes it hard to find what you are looking for.

- Difficult to hit even when locked on. It is almost impossible to hit enemies below you or close to the ground, which wasn't a problem in DeS or DS1.

- Generally there aren't really many patterns to learn other than in what order you should kill enemies. The difficulty of this game is dealing with many enemies at one time. Herding them somewhere they will have to file towards you and then take them out in a fairly simple way. This is much different to DeS and DS1 where the fun was in learning new enemies movesets and occasionally dealing with an ambush which would require employing that knowledge. Enemies are also souless automatons lacking character. Another negative when compared to immersion felt in previous titles.

- So far they seem to have had nothing to do with the environment around them or any of the NPCs. In DeS and DS1 the area boss would be very much on theme with either the geography, enemies or lore for the area. This knowledge would usually be imparted on the player well before meeting the boss, at the very least after. I'm also not struggling with them at all on my first playthrough. Boring.

I recently finished Dark Souls 2, and there are several aspects of the game that distinguish it from the original. Let me start with the positives: The game is much better optimized for PC compared to the first Dark Souls, which makes for a smoother experience. There’s also an impressive variety of weapons and combat mechanics, offering something for every playstyle. I really appreciated the ability to teleport between bonfires—something that wasn’t possible in the first game. Additionally, mechanics like the Fragrant Branches of Yore and Pharros Lockstones felt rewarding to find, as they unlocked new areas and secrets across the game. The leveling system in Dark Souls 2 is also solid, and while the grind for Titanite can sometimes be frustrating, it’s not overly punishing.

However, there are a few issues, especially toward the end of the game. The level design becomes incredibly confusing, with little direction on where to go next. Another issue is the placement of enemies in certain areas, which feels downright trollish. For example, in Dragon Aerie, there are explosive enemies hidden around tight corners, often in places where you can't see them in time. This leads to cheap deaths where you’re knocked off cliffs with no way to react. This kind of enemy placement feels unfair and frustrating and it gets worse through the game, especially when the game throws in previously-fought bosses or adds invaders and phantoms immediately after boss fights or during long stretches to bonfires.

Additionally, many of the bosses felt underwhelming and lacked the challenge that Dark Souls is known for. It almost felt like they were added at the last minute, rather than being carefully designed. I also encountered some issues with hit detection—sometimes my attacks wouldn’t register properly, while enemies’ attacks seemed to land no matter what. This was particularly noticeable with enemies like Mimics, Ogres, and certain bosses. This was also experienced with online invaders, and phantoms where they would somehow teleport behind me and back stab me.

Overall, I think the game was fun, just not as well designed as the first game. My scoring metric goes like this:
1 - God Awful
2 - Terrible
3 - Very Bad
4 - Bad
5 - Tolerable
6 - Fine / Average
7 - Good
8 - Very Good
9 - Great
10 - Masterpiece

And I would give the game a 7/10.

Posted: April 6
After finishing Dark Souls 1 and Elden Ring, I was genuinely hyped to jump into Dark Souls 2. I’d heard mixed things about it over the years, but I figured that if it came from FromSoftware, it had to have something worthwhile. Boy, was I wrong. What I got was one of the most frustrating, janky, and straight-up baffling entries in the entire Souls series.

Let’s get straight into the disaster that is the gameplay mechanics. Parrying in DS2 feels like a cruel joke. It’s slow, unresponsive, and wildly inconsistent. I thought maybe I was just rusty—until I realized that it’s actually tied to a stat. Yes, a literal stat—Adaptability—controls your i-frames and parry timing. Who in their right mind thought that was a good idea? They took the core mechanics of combat—rolling and parrying, the literal lifeblood of a Souls game—and slapped a progression wall on them. Want your dodge roll to not suck? Dump points into this weird stat that isn’t explained properly and sounds completely unrelated. Want your parries to not feel like laggy garbage? Better grind that ADP up. It’s honestly one of the dumbest design decisions I’ve seen in any game, and it actively punishes new players for not already knowing about this hidden system.

In DS1 and Elden Ring, parrying was about timing and practice. Here, it feels like rolling dice with one hand tied behind your back. And dodging? Forget about it. Early game dodging in DS2 is borderline useless unless you already know to pump Adaptability, and even then, it never quite feels right. It’s like trying to roll through molasses and hoping the game decides to give you i-frames out of pity. It’s not hard in a good way—it’s hard in a broken, poorly designed way. The idea that a basic combat function is tied to an obscure stat is just baffling. If they wanted to make it harder, they could’ve done literally anything else—smarter enemy design, more aggressive AI—not this.

And the movement? Stiff and clunky. My character feels like they’re moving underwater half the time. Even basic traversal feels off—jumps, rolls, directional changes—it all feels slightly delayed or just… wrong. It’s like they copied the mechanics of DS1 and poured glue into them.

The level design does no favors either. Where DS1 was interconnected, logical, and atmospheric, DS2 feels like a patchwork quilt of unrelated zones duct-taped together. There’s no organic flow, no “ah-ha” moments where areas loop back in brilliant ways—just a bunch of disconnected zones connected by fast travel. Speaking of which, yeah, you can fast travel from the start. That’s nice. But it also ends up being a crutch for how awkward the world layout is. It’s like they knew it was a mess and gave you the warp mechanic upfront as an apology.

I’ll give a few small props where they’re due. The health reduction mechanic when you die adds tension, and it makes you cautious in a way that fits the atmosphere. And the weapon durability system, while occasionally annoying, did get me to rotate weapons more. I actually tried out different weapons instead of steamrolling the game with just one weapon like I did with my Zweihander in DS1. So yeah, some ideas here had potential.

But they’re buried under layers of bad decisions. Enemy placement often feels cheap, not challenging. Hitboxes are wonky. Bosses feel less memorable and more like damage sponges. Even the atmosphere—which DS1 and Elden Ring nailed—feels inconsistent and flat in DS2. The whole game just screams “off.”

After a couple hours, I had to stop. I physically couldn’t keep pushing myself to play something that felt like such a slog. I’ll come back to it eventually, probably after finishing Dark Souls 3, but only out of pure stubbornness. There’s zero enjoyment here for me right now. I don’t hate tough games—I love tough games—but I do hate when they’re unfair, clunky, and seemingly designed to be “challenging” through poor design instead of thoughtful mechanics.

To sum it up, Dark Souls 2 feels like the awkward middle child of the series. It looks pretty, sure, but that’s where the praise ends. The gameplay is worse, the mechanics are broken or hidden behind dumb stat requirements, the world is less engaging, and overall it just feels like a massive misstep after the brilliance of DS1.

Right now, it’s a hard no. Maybe when I come back after DS3, I’ll be able to stomach it long enough to see what fans love about it. But for now? It’s an unfun, confusing mess that actively punishes you for not reading a guide. What a disappointment.

There’s a lot to pick apart in this game, and I mean A LOT. But weirdly enough, if you stop looking at it like it’s a Souls game and just see it as its own thing, it kinda has a charm to it.

Still—I hate this game with a passion.

I’d like to start this review by addressing the stigma attached to this game. When other games are rough around the edges, they’re flawed masterpieces but when it’s Dark Souls II, it’s the “black sheep of the franchise”. Much of the critique of this game is not objective but instead is the result of a consensus which emerged around the game’s launch as a result of how it differs from its predecessor. Pseudo-intellectual youtubers would make long and verbose videos decrying DS2 as a horribly irredeemable game and to this day people will trip over themselves thinking of more and more extreme hyperbolic ways of describing how bad the bad game is. As an example, just the other day I saw a post from a Twitch streamer with a sizeable audience stating how he finally beat DS2 and that he “hated every minute of it”... Really? Even the Lost Bastille? No Man’s Wharf? BRUME TOWER? Just imagine being the one person in your friend group who thought the bad game wasn’t in fact bad, how embarrassing would that be? (a glimpse into the mind of a normie) All of this is to say, much of the hatred for this game is memetic and a good review of this game needs to address this.

Entering Majula for the first time was a truly special moment as it’s just such an intoxicatingly beautiful area. That first vista upon exiting the Things Betwixt is like a painting in the tonalist style with a strong emphasis on the horizon, seemingly reaching out towards the infinite. Pure aesthetic maestria. It also has a relaxing and yet delightfully mysterious soundtrack too. To this day, this is the best hub area Fromsoft has made none of the others even come close. In the distance, ruins can be seen sinking into the sea. Just like in the first game, you are in a fallen world, afflicted with the undead curse and already losing your memories, not entirely certain even why you’ve arrived in Drangleic. The story of this game is told in a more direct manner than its predecessor from the opening cutscene alone, and I don’t think this is a bad thing at all, just different. The story to me also feels more personal than the other two souls games. Your character follows in the footsteps of the last monarch who sought to break the undead curse and as you progress through the world of Drangleic, learning the roots of the world, you are questioned: what is it that you truly want? Also throughout your journey, you come across others who bear the curse, some of which you can even bring back to Majula which I think is an awesome feature. You get to see the curse from their perspective through their dialogue and questlines, further adding to the deeply personal nature of this game’s story.

One of the more controversial changes in this game is the overhaul of the stats system, and I for one like the changes made, even the addition of the much maligned adaptability. There are no longer any useless stats like resistance and there are more important stats to level compared to DS1 but this isn’t an issue because the game is designed with this in mind and gives you an ample amount of souls to work with. Another superb addition this game made is the ability to finally be able to respec your character’s stats. To go along with this addition, smithing upgrades were made much more common than the first game, making you able to max out multiple weapons in a single playthrough in case you do change your build. I also really liked the major change made to the humanity system, with your health bar downsizing upon every death until you revert it by using humanity. The resource management of humanity in this game is satisfying and I like that there’s now an actual impetus to using it now besides summons. The coolest innovation this game made though was the creation of an item which allows you to accelerate a location’s new game plus cycle and even rechallenge bosses.

The level design of DS2 does not disappoint. The game starts off very strong with the Forest of the Fallen Giant’s which is able to be spotted a distance away from Majula. It’s a really stellar area with memorable encounters, creative shortcuts, cool bosses, and it also as an early location serves as a great primer for the worldbuilding of the game. My favourite area in the base game is the Gutter. It resides directly below Majula and it is absolutely horrifying. The main gimmick of the area is that it’s extremely dark and you’re expected to slowly navigate it lighting sconces with a torch along the way, slowly making your way to the end only to realise that it goes even deeper… The fact that it’s just right below comfy Majula makes its existence all the more disquieting. The game’s three DLCs exhibit some of the best level design in the franchise. From having to make your way through to the icy heart of the city of Eleum Loyce to melt all of the ice, expanding opportunities for exploration throughout the city, to navigating between the ruins of two gigantic ziggurats in the city of Shulva, and to descending carefully down Brume Tower before turning on the tower’s elevators to explore even more of it. Exploration in the DLCs is viscerally satisfying and the art direction in them is some of Fromsoft’s absolute best.

This game has some very memorable boss encounters. Getting to fight Ornstein solo is really awesome and the fact that you can do it right at the beginning of the game provides a nice challenge. The executioner’s chariot is also to this day my favourite gimmick fight that Fromsoft has ever made. The pure spectacle of the Looking Glass Knight fight is amazing and the fight also has a very unique mechanic where the boss is able to summon an NPC or potentially even a real player using its shield as a portal. Like with level design, the DLCs also have the very best boss encounters the game has to offer. The Crown of the Sunken King offers one of the best mage bosses in the franchise along with one of the franchise’s best dragon fights. The gimmick of the Aava fight in the Crown of the Ivory King DLC is cool and the Burnt Ivory King fight feels like proto-Radahn. The two main bosses of the Crown of the Old Iron King, Fume Knight and Sir Alonne provide the biggest challenges in the game and are easily my two favourite bosses in the game. Each DLC also added areas designed around co-op which I think is a neat addition and provide an insane challenge for those looking to complete them solo and without summons.

One of the best things about the first Dark Souls is how interconnected the world is and how if you see some place off in the distance that looks cool, you’ll most likely end up going there at some point. When I completed the first areas of DS2, I thought the world of Drangleic would be like the first Dark Souls in this regard. From Majula, you are able to see off in the distance the Forest of the Fallen Giants, Heide, and the Shaded Woods; but for most of the game, you won’t be getting scenic vistas of future areas to explore. Even worse than this, as you are exploring the world, the world does not feel at all believable and that is because it isn’t. The Iron Keep shouldn’t be on top of Earthen Peak and the Dragon Aerie shouldn’t be behind Aldia’s Keep and adjacent to Drangleic Castle and the Doors of Pharros. This is a big problem as it kills immersion and it’s especially jarring going from such a cohesive world like Lordran to the mess that is Drangleic. It’s like the developers just made a bunch of cool areas and slapped them together and went “just trust me bro”, it really is that bad and is indicative of the development hell the game went through. There are other areas in which the development hell shows. Had Yui Tanimura been made lead developer much earlier and the base game shared the same quality as the DLC, Dark Souls 2 could have gone from being a very good yet extremely flawed game to ascending to the heights that the other two Dark Souls games did.

Dark Souls II was my least favorite souls game when it first came out. It's still enjoyable, but its faults are a lot more apparent as I play this again on my PC, a decade or so later.

The combat is very much a souls game, but the system is also very sluggish. An absurd amount of enemies are almost always camped around a corner, and this make run backs very un-fun, especially during boss fights. The movement, after all of these years, feels both floaty and slippery. I have died slipping off an edge despite moving cautiously. And on the topic of movement, it appears that your characters movement is so sensitive, you'll be gently tapping the direction you want to go so that you don't zip off into the abyss.

Despite these issues I'm having with the game currently, it's still very fun, and enjoyable coming back to this game all of these years later. It's one of the more easier Souls game that you will find.

Setting and Story
Dark Souls II takes place in Drangleic, a kingdom plagued by the curse of the undead. While its story is not as directly connected to Dark Souls I, it builds on similar themes of despair and the cyclical nature of the world. The game’s lore is deeper and more fragmented, encouraging players to piece together its mysteries through exploration and dialogue.

Gameplay and Combat Enhancements
One major change is the addition of the Adaptability stat, which affects agility and invincibility frames during rolls. This change initially frustrated some players, but mastering it brings a rewarding sense of progression. Combat feels slightly slower but more deliberate, and weapon variety has been significantly expanded with dual-wielding and power stance mechanics, allowing for more complex combinations.

The New Game Plus (NG+) mode is another highlight, adding new enemy placements and changes that make subsequent playthroughs feel fresh and challenging. Bosses in Dark Souls II are numerous, with fights like The Pursuer, The Lost Sinner, and Sir Alonne being particularly memorable. Some bosses, however, feel repetitive, like the Royal Rat Authority, which is essentially a larger version of a standard enemy.

Level Design and World Structure
The world design, while not as interconnected as Dark Souls I, features sprawling environments that are visually diverse, from the eerie Lost Bastille to the majestic Drangleic Castle. Fast travel between bonfires is available from the start, which, while convenient, reduces the sense of connection between areas.

Multiplayer Improvements
The multiplayer mechanics are smoother, and the covenant system is more balanced, providing diverse ways to interact with other players. PvP is especially vibrant, with arenas dedicated to dueling and covenants that encourage both cooperation and competition.

Dark Souls 2 is a downgrade from Dark Souls 1 and, in my opinion, the weakest entry in FromSoftware’s Souls series. One of its most frustrating mechanics is Adaptability (ADP), which determines the number of invincibility frames (i-frames) while dodging. This stat either makes dodging feel unresponsive and unreliable or, once leveled, trivializes it completely. It’s an unnecessary mechanic that disrupts the core combat experience.

Boss design is another major disappointment. Many bosses have only two or three attack patterns, making fights feel repetitive and unchallenging. In addition, the game allows enemies to interrupt you while walking through fog gates, opening doors, or pulling levers. This doesn’t add meaningful difficulty—just frustration. Enemy placement is also a frequent issue, with too many encounters forcing players into cramped spaces surrounded by swarming enemies, leading to unfair deaths rather than skill-based challenges.

Perhaps the worst mechanic in Dark Souls 2 is the health reduction penalty upon death. Each time you die, your max HP decreases, down to a possible 50% cap. This directly punishes players for engaging with the game’s trial-and-error learning process—one of the core aspects of the Souls experience. While there are ways to mitigate it, such as a ring that reduces the penalty to 25% or using a limited Human Effigy, this system feels restrictive and unnecessary.

That said, Dark Souls 2 does introduce a few good ideas. The ability to warp between bonfires from the start is a welcome quality-of-life improvement over Dark Souls 1. Some enemy designs are genuinely creative, such as the pyromancers in the Undead Crypt and the giant knights from the DLCs. Speaking of DLC, the game’s three expansions are a highlight, featuring well-designed levels, better enemy encounters, and more engaging bosses than the base game. There are also plenty of unique and interesting weapons, though the lackluster combat mechanics hold them back.

Overall, Dark Souls 2 falls short of its predecessor in many ways. While it has a few bright spots—mainly in the DLC—the questionable mechanics, weaker boss design, and frustrating enemy placement make it a less enjoyable experience. If you’re playing through the Dark Souls trilogy, I’d recommend skipping this one and jumping straight to Dark Souls 3, which refines the combat and level design to near perfection. You won’t miss much—except for some unnecessary frustration.

I like Dark Souls 2, but it has several pretty glaring issues with it in comparison to other Dark Souls games. You probably have heard people complain about the same things all over the place, and they're probably all true, but still this game is a very competent albeit flawed game.

Just to echo those complaints... it has a more multiplayer focus than other Dark Souls games, meaning most areas suck ass to traverse. They try to remedy this by making enemies despawn if they kill you or you kill them enough times, but this feels like a band-aid, not a solution. In addition, the bosses in Dark Souls 2 are generally less interesting, despite the fact that there are quite a few more than other games in the series.

However, there is still something compelling about this game. It is the black sheep of the series and it feels a bit worse to play than other dark souls games, but there are some things that I feel it does really well. For instance, lifegems. Lifegems are a really cool mechanic in this game, where if you run out of estus flasks, you can use lifegems to heal slowly. Making it so you aren't ever feeling dead in water against a boss. There is always a chance to still beat the boss, even if it's made more slim.

The regions are also a bit more varied and interesting than Dark Souls 1 in my opinion. The environments and themes feel varied and interesting, and you never know what's coming next. In addition, they gave you a jump button in this one so you can traverse more of the world.

There are also quite a few more minor touches that I feel like really give this game a unique and cool flavor that feels different than the other Souls games.

However, the glaring issues in this game just make it really hard to enjoy sometimes. The constant onslaught of minor enemies with some of the worst runbacks in any souls game makes it really hard to want to enjoy the game. The hidden walls that give no indication that they are hidden, so you're running around dungeons spamming A until a wall moves. All of this makes the game feel, to me, like an unpolished fan game of Dark Souls. The setting is a bit different, the vibe is different, and there is just way less polish than Dark Souls 1, and the later souls games from Fromsoft.

In conclusion, I do recommend playing this game. Genuinely. I think this game is a great game that I do have a lot of fond memories playing and some really really interesting areas. But, you also need to be prepared to meet the game at its level, and not your own. The game is frustrating and annoying and punishing at times, but through that adversity, you find a great game that is genuinely fun and rewarding.

Dark Souls 2 is like waking up in a house you don’t recognize. It’s familiar, but something’s off. It’s the Souls equivalent of getting lost in a mall as a kid, except instead of a kind security guard finding you, you’re getting slapped around by 37 enemies at once. People love to dunk on this one, but honestly? I had a great time.

The real challenge isn’t the bosses—most of them are about as telegraphed as a slow-motion slap. The real pain? The marathon back to the boss room after every death. Dying isn’t the punishment; it’s the walk of shame past the same enemies who now know your every mistake. And then there’s Black Gulch—a place so aggressively unpleasant it feels like the developers lost a bet and had to make the worst level imaginable. It’s like walking barefoot through a LEGO factory while getting pelted with dodge balls full of bees. Every step is poison, every wall has something awful hiding in it, and by the time you escape, you feel like you’ve legally survived a war. But hey, finishing it is rewarding—kind of like escaping a burning building just to run back in for your hat.

Oh, and Adaptability? Turns out that little stat secretly controls whether your dodges are slick ninja maneuvers or the desperate flailing of a fish on land. I spent the entire game rolling like an old man trying to get out of a recliner, only to find out after beating the game that my i-frames were trash because I ignored ADP. Did I notice the difference? Not even slightly. But would it have been nice to know? Absolutely. Still, despite the weirdness, I enjoyed it and still loved every second. 9/10, would get lost in Majula again.

Feels like the game embodiment of a double-edged sword

The game gets way more hate than it deserves, but it's also far from perfect. I really wanted to like ds2, and it's solid, but for me personally it's my least favorite out of the trilogy. It has a lot of interesting ideas and then about an equal amount of frustrating ones that make me wanna chew on my controller like a possessed gerbil.

The lore is decent, the soundtrack is great to be expected, and it's fun to see the evolution throughout the games. The areas are often beautiful, and the immediate availability of warping between bonfires makes it no issue to backtrack and explore - although I did find myself missing the interconnectivity of ds1's map and the first half of that game where you had to navigate without warping.

Lastly, I unfortunately didn't enjoy the DLCs all that much, but some of the zones and bosses were cool. Personally would've been okay without the rest though.

If you're a fromsoft fan and never got around to this, give it a shot and try to stick with it so you can form your own opinion. It's painful at times, but memorable in all the rest.

Posted: March 31
Dark Souls II is a very divisive entry in the trilogy. Some people adore it, some people hate it. Either way, there is rarely ever any in-between. We FromSoftware fans are probably biased by the fact that this game was made on a new engine and not directed by Miyazaki, too.

Now, credit where credit is due: It isn't all bad. In fact, there are good ideas throughout this game, some of which have only gotten better with time (Elden Ring being the current epitome of these). And yes, Majula atmosphere and music are peak.

However, it just *feels* wrong to play. It feels bad once you have the controller in hand and control your character. So many things are exceedingly slow, and some are just goofy fast. The level design is just... meh, some really good things and some really bad things mixed up. Some of the mechanics in this game are absolutely stupid. The UI/UX is a pain to try and make sense of. The game is genuinely ugly pretty much all throughout. For a "definitive" version, it has a dire lack of settings, accessibility features, or simply the ability to play offline!

Now those are all just some of my opinions. I don't really dislike this game, I just dislike playing it. I am not calling anyone who disagrees stupid or anything, so please remain polite and courteous :)
All in all, you really just need to give it a try for yourself, and see if you like it. It's just not a game for everyone, and it was unfortunately a frustrating experience for me at best.

The game was fun until I got to the DLCs.

Dark Souls 2 has genuinely annoying rage-inducing DLCs.
Invincible gank-spank ghost knights that can't be damaged if your build is physical melee.
Frozen reindeers coming from out of nowhere.

On top of that people will keep invading these areas.

I never felt rage playing any of the other Fromsoft games. This one got me.

All the other stuff about Dark Souls 2 was forgivable.
* Linear damage calculation from armor invalidates it to a degree, but the bosses are easy enough to dodge.
* Some enemies don't really even give you enough time to recover stamina or to dodge in time (animation is too fast), but you have infinite heals and all enemies have blindspots.
* Adaptability as a stat felt like a social experiment gone wrong, but you can offset that by shield builds if you don't want invest in it for dodges.
* You lose max HP every death, but in lategame you gain the ability to restore humanity infinitely; there's also a ring that mitigates the hollowing.
* Soul memory is weird but overall doesn't matter much if you're not a PvP guy.
* Durability matters way too much and can be annoying, but it's usually not a big problem.

I was really about to say "Dark Souls 2 is better than Dark Souls 1" because I actually enjoyed the game up until Nashandra/Aldia. Dark Souls 1 felt too lonely. This game felt like the world was alive because of all the NPCs you meet.

Bonfire Ascetics is also a really good mechanic that should've made it to Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring.

But the DLCs are too stupid. I am going to give Dark Souls 2 a 6/10.
Just play the base game then uninstall it, and be done with it forever.

Play it. Despite its flaws, this game is truly amazing. In some ways, it stands as my favorite in the series.

I would argue that this entry contains some of the most unfair moments in the franchise—nothing outright impossible, just occasionally janky.

However, it also introduces unique and innovative mechanics that set it apart. The game experimented heavily with the Souls formula, and while not every change was a success, many should have been kept in later titles. Some features were, admittedly, a welcome goodbye.

One of its greatest strengths lies in its diverse movesets and weapon variety, unmatched by any other game in the series. While PvP activity has diminished over time, at its peak, this game offered some of the most memorable multiplayer experiences in the franchise.

How It Ranks in the Souls Series (Subjectively, of Course):

Dark Souls 1
Bloodborne
Sekiro
Dark Souls 2
Elden Ring
Dark Souls 3
Demon’s Souls

Breaking It Down:
Dark Souls 1 – Best atmosphere, story, world layout, and unforgettable bosses.

Bloodborne – Tied with DS1 but features superior mechanics. (Desperately needs a 60+ FPS version.)

Dark Souls 2 – Most unique combat and mechanics; highly creative.

Dark Souls 3 – The most polished but also the most linear.

Sekiro – The best combat experience in the series.

Elden Ring – Largest map and great QoL improvements. (Biggest drawback: it’s the only Souls game that feels like a chore to replay. This is an issue with all large open world games for me. It's just the best one.)

Demon’s Souls – Enjoyable but ultimately the most forgettable.

I'd like to add while I'm replaying the PC version for the first time currently, I had 300+ hrs in the PS3 version.

Overhated, underrated, but redeemed with time. YES, you should play this game

My review will be about SOTFS version only, as I didn’t play the OG DS2 fully and cannot compare them. I know that some fans weren’t satisfied with the update, but I saw zero issues with it both on PC and PS. Contrary to that, I found this game one of the best-optimized I’ve played – it performed with 60 FPS even on the worst laptop I had while being a poor student, and it’s the brightest and prettiest in the series after DS3. I’ve never encountered any bugs, glitches or FPS drops, this game ran perfectly ten years ago and still does now when I replayed it in 2025. So from the technical standpoint, DS2 is a gem. The other aspects of it though? Deserves the longer description.

It still boggles my mind what a hate-train this game got simply due to being not the direct successor of DS1 style. But ironically to me, the reasons DS1 hardcore stans dislike DS2 are actually the ones that elevate this game for me. DS2 is really unique in comparison to both DS1 and DS3, and I’m not going to lie, it did its rights and wrongs while trying to be original and new. And still this is one of my most replayed games which I enjoyed a lot. Here’s why.

1) The visual and sound design of DS2 SOTFS is amazing. Yes, there are lots of other stunning areas aside from Majula, forget the memes as they deceived you. The look on my face in 2015 when I firstly saw Dragon Aerie, Drangleic’s castle, Brume’s Tower entrance, Eleum Loyce and many other areas should have been studied. Visually the game aged incredibly well and it still gives me such an eyegasm to go through it. The details about DS2 sound design always made me happy too, because I am weak to this crap. Bow’s string drawing, sounds of different spells casted, going up ladders, stepping sounds in different landscapes, and damn the soundtrack. Once you learn the game so that it doesn’t stress you out anymore, playing it becomes almost a meditative experience to me just because how freaking beautiful it is all around.

2) Level design may be flawed… But this game actually teaches you to use other means of fighting against one sword to bonk through entire game (yep DS1, I’m looking at you). Trust me: once you actually notice the bow’s existence and that lil thing called 'Yearn' spell, the main complaining topic about DS2 oversaturation with enemies will disappear. Believe it or not, but even the infamous Amana Shrine becomes perfectly walkable once you’ll understand that the game rewards slower pacing, attention to your surroundings and bow’s usage. The arrows are cheap as hell too. The game also features tons of different spells, weapons and variety in playing. The game will only reward you for it. And in case you wonder what is there to know about the game's hidden gems of playstyle, check out Otzdarva's No Hit Run YT videos.

3) DS2 doesn’t limit you in trying something new. The upgrade materials are a sea you can drown in. Upgrade almost whatever weapon you want, you are even given more than enough soul vessels to experiment with your build and rearrange your perks several times in one game.

4) Fashion souls. No need to elaborate. Also the best character’s creation menu. My female character finally looks pretty for once.

5) The game introduces so much stuff that is simply absent from DS3. I loved gems, all the little consumables, the concept of spices, invisible bums dropping stones to exchange with the crow. Never used bonfire’s aesthetics though but appreciate the effort!

6) LOVED how NG+ actually changes the enemies’ position, adds new phantoms, gives you mimics instead of some chests that can literally give you a heart attack, and just keeps you on guard even though you’ve beaten the game. Never understood why wouldn’t DS3 try the same thing later.

7) Also appreciate how many varied locations DS2 has, even though it kind of became its own problem in the process of development (some parts were rushed and not polished enough, we all know Iron Keep’s sad story). The bosses of almost all of these areas are not great to put it mildly lol… But the actual levels are creative and focus-required as hell. DLCs also redeemed all of the main game’s past sins, as all three of them had both greatly designed arenas and one of the most iconic bosses in the entire series.

To sum it all up: basically the title. DS2 was clowned upon for being too different from DS1, even though underneath there always was an ambitious, probably a tiny bity overblown, but genuinely good game that still looks stunning and performs flawlessly after all these years. I still come back to it from time-to-time, and each time it grows on me even more. Thankfully, as time goes by more people begin to appreciate its forgotten glory.

This game is a terrible, loathsome experience. This game has developed a fanbase of it’s own, and I have a theory as to who those people might be which I’ll get into later. Let’s just talk about the game for now.

The music—save for Majula—is all bland and mediocre. It’s the audio equivalent of eating plain porridge made with water constantly. It becomes an annoying drone most of the time. The sound design, like all From Software games, is serviceable most of the time, but the decision to have no hit sounds on bosses makes the game feel even more cheap.

The artistic direction of the game is vile to look at, and becomes more and more derivative as you go. I’ve heard many a DS2 fan say “Dark Souls 3, more like Brown Souls 3 XDDDD”, but in response to that. At least that was an intentional artistic design, unlike this game, which looks like someone threw up into a 3D game engine and published it. The palettes are dull, and clash frequently, with way too many warm tones for the dark and brooding style it’s trying for. All the armour looks like cheap plastic toys, and the weapons certainly hit like them.

The map is a non-nonsensical mess, and can only be what it must be like for an ant to navigate a university students floordrobe. Progression is obscure and confusing, with barely any hints on where to go. You thought the graveyard next to Firelink was bad? Wait till you’re in the poison place, without even visiting the pirate port. I see DS2 fans constantly whine about the linearity of 1 and 3, but

The hardest part of the game is the runbacks, which are also extremely unfun. They suck. Not a single boss took me more than 4 attempts at max, which isn’t good. Half the point of these games are the boss fights, and they’re all unfun, gimmicky, trash. Usually with some annoying other enemies thrown in for good measure during the fight. The game’s difficulty comes from the fact that most systems you can’t “git gud” at, because they’re fundamentally flawed. It’s baked into the fabric of the game. You can’t “git gud” at fighting 4 enemies who all see you at the same damn time, while 2 have homing missile arrows sniping at you, while you are ganked and stun-locked by 2 katana wielding enemies. Everything in the game feels sluggish and unresponsive.

I want to put a stop to this myth. Far too many people placate the people who like this game by saying, “It’s still a good ga-“ NO. IT. ISN’T.

If I was stuck on a desert island with a PC and Dark Souls 2, I would throw it into the ocean. I would rather sit looking into the sea, than play this garbage again.

If you were interested in the story? Don’t be. It’s just a derivative of a much better game called DS1. Go play that instead.

This game is awful. I feel comfortable, and confident, in saying this is categorically the worst game I’ve ever had the displeasure of playing. If a fan of this game tells you to try it, assume that they are the lowest common denominator of gaming and ignore any of their gaming opinions from that point on.

Oh, and for the record, I had maxed out agility from the start and I hated it this much, so if you don’t, you’re in for a rough one.

The multiplayer is a mess with the matchmaking system being poorly designed, and never being safe from invasions even after clearing an area. Animations are poor, and when it makes certain attacks difficult to dodge or block it becomes a glaring issue.

Although 2 is absolutely my least favorite in the series, each time I play it I get the feeling "It's not THAT bad", and enjoy it a little more. However, had it not been the sequel to the first one, I'm not sure I would have stuck with it long enough to beat it in the first place.

In it's defense, it does have one of the more dedicated communities of any game in the series, with many returning to play it yearly even now. It's worth a try, but I would say if you don't actually like, don't really feel bad about refunding it.

Base Game: 6.9/10

DLC: 7.4/10

Man oh man where to begin with this one. Looking at this as just a game, this is not a bad game; it's pretty decent honestly. But if you were to look at this game from a FromSoftware perspective (I haven't played all of them but I've played 3 others besides this one (Sekiro, ER, DS1)), it's not very good. While it has some interesting ideas that it added to this game from DS1, it also has a lot of "interesting" ideas that probably should have had a second look through.

In terms of atmosphere, I didn't really care for this one compared to DS1. I felt the theme and chilling atmosphere of DS1 was more appealing and matched the game very well, while this game I felt it was sort of all over the place in terms of consistency. The bosses for this game were also pretty lacking, some of the movesets were super basic and repetitive (Ancient Dragon I'm looking at you). I'd say Darklurker was probably one of my favorites in the base but a pain in the ass to setup the 1st time, while Fume Knight was probably my favorite boss of the entire game. I felt they had some really cool movesets and were the most challenging of the bosses in the game.

The areas in this game were such a pain in the ass to get through let me tell you. They really tried to frustrate me (also Royal Rat Authority can DIRL) with some of the areas, and I felt there were some spots they tried to emphasize platforming a little too much (which is to say imo, the worst mechanic in these games). I can get behind some pain in the ass stuff, but poison spitting statues while my max HP goes down anytime I die was certainly not an enjoyable mechanic.

The DLC I will say had some of the coolest areas and bosses for sure, Old Iron King was my personal favorite. I will say I only bothered with the Frigid Outskirts 2 times because I was close to finishing this game and just didn't have it in me to go through that garbage area (so rip that optional boss). But I feel they really upped their game in general for the DLC, had a lot more fun and got wowed by some of the places and ideas that were done for these areas.

I think this game is a decent-to-good game, but as a souls like game, it's hard to recommend to someone. I definitely wouldn't tell someone to play this as their first souls like game either, but if you're like me and feel the need to play the full souls series from FromSoftware after finally getting into these styles of games, it's not as bad as everyone makes it sound.

Dark Souls II⋆. 𐙚 ˚

────୨ৎ────
Dark Souls III is the black sheep of the Dark Souls series — but for me, it's one of the most beautiful sheep.
────୨ৎ────
「 ✦ GAMEPLAY ✦ 」

-Good things-

Dark Souls II has the most original mechanics in the series — dual weapon movesets, for example, which were removed in Dark Souls III. Or the possibility of reviving bosses without having to go into New Game Plus.


The combat is good, but that’s true for all Dark Souls games, so there’s not much to say about it.


-Bad things-

What I’m about to say doesn’t affect me personally, but it’s something commonly mentioned by others. It doesn’t impact my gameplay, but it’s still something everyone should be aware of.

The amount of enemies can be frustrating for new players. Many of them could be described as “artificial difficulty.”

The hitboxes can be tricky, too.


「 ✦ LORE ✦ 」

I can’t say much about the lore because I don’t know it well. But I can say that the game doesn’t present its story directly like other games — you have to dig into it. Just playing normally won’t reveal the full lore.

「 ✦ ART ✦ 」

The landscapes and the design of enemies, characters, and environments are stunning — the aesthetics of the game are a true masterpiece. The designs are iconic, memorable, and full of personality. Majula is the best "home" in the entire Dark Souls series; it’s truly beautiful.

The music is amazing — every track during boss fights, cutscenes, or key moments is packed with a amazing soundtrack. Each song makes you tremble It's not just background music; it's a core part of the game's soul.

「 ✦ DIFFICULTY ✦ 」

The difficulty is tough, like in all Souls games. But with practice, it becomes manageable — there’s not much more to say.

「 ✦ SHOULD YOU BUY IT? ✦ 」

If you like challenging games, good combat, RPGs, or just good games in general — then yes, definitely.

Even though a lot of people criticize it, for me it’s one of my favorite games and a very good Souls game.

8.5/10
I must say that DS2 has its own unique and fascinating charm that sets it apart from the traditional Souls series; but on the other hand it also has some very cringe-worthy and disgusting gameplay mechanics. But all in all, flawed as it is, it's still a good game (in some ways).

Let's start with its strengths. As we all know, the lead developer of DS2 was not Hidetaka Miyazaki, as he was in charge of developing Bloodborne during that time, so the responsibility of development fell to Yui Tanimura (谷村 唯). Compared to DS1, DS2 has a more balanced and diverse weapon system, and the rather distinctive dual-wielding system allows for more varied gameplay. The map design is not as extensive as DS1, but it is more inclined to a level system design, which is also unique. Especially when you start the second round of the game, you can just parkour to the castle instead of fighting the Four Giants, and only challenge the necessary bosses, and you can pass the game in as little as 2.5 hours if you're faster. At this point you can feel the excellence of its map design, giving the player a variety of choices, many places can be visited or not, it is entirely up to the player to decide. As well, DS2's unique add-fire system also allows the player to collect the vast majority of items in the first round of the game, which adds to the game's playability and richness, and is also a nice design. Speaking of the plot, it's not as full of grandeur and epicness as DS1, but it does sort of add a lot of detail to the overall Dark Souls worldview, better depicting the various plots that come in all shapes and sizes under the DS worldview (mainly by focusing on the portrayal of people). And, compared to the original, Scholar of the First Sin edition optimizes some of the content, which does make it harder in places, but generally makes for a better overall experience, and of course the best part is the three DLCs, which are arguably the most distinctive content designed for this game.

Okay, next it's time to talk about its downsides. First of all the biggest downside of DS2 is that it has a lot of extremely cringe-worthy game design, it's not about difficulty, it's about sheer nastiness that makes the player feel like the producers developed this game with great malice. That alone dissuaded countless new players. Even for many of us who have 100% achievements and have cleared countless rounds of the game, we still feel this disgusting feeling from time to time. It's a feeling unlike Demon's Souls or DS1 or DS3 or Bloodborne or Elden Ring. It doesn't even have anything to do with “difficulty”, it's pure nausea, and it's a feeling of deep malice on the part of the developers, a feeling of wanting to disgust you. So while you're playing DS2, you're attracted to its unique gameplay elements, but you're also disgusted by the nausea that comes your way all the time. This mixed feeling of dissonance is DS2's most “unique” experience. As well, DS2 feels rushed, with a lot of areas lacking in detail, many scenes being forced to drag on in order to fill in the gaps in the game's content, and a plot that doesn't quite work as well as it could have. DS1 and DS3 I could play several rounds of the game in one go, but DS2, after playing the first round, I was a bit reluctant to start the second round, and at best I just quickly get through the second round by parkouring.

All in all, DS2 can be described as a game with very clear strengths and weaknesses. But overall, it's still an excellent piece of work. I'm glad, though, that From Software chose Hidetaka Miyazaki's style as the main tone of the Souls series, and because of that, games like Elden Ring are known all over the world. If the DS2 style had been continued, the Dark Souls series would have been destined to become only niche and never really fit into the wider gaming community market, exposing the series to a wider range of gamers.

At first, Dark Souls II felt like pure punishment. My first five hours were a frustrating blur of death, confusion, and the overwhelming urge to quit. I genuinely hated the game at that point — it felt unfair, cryptic, and cold.

But I stuck with it, and I’m so glad I did.

Now, after pushing through the early game and slowly improving both my character and my own understanding of the mechanics, I can honestly say this is a challenging masterpiece. The difficulty hasn’t gone away — it’s still very much a trial by fire — but it’s manageable now. Every victory feels earned, and every loss teaches you something.

It’s not an easy game to love, but once it hooks you, it really hooks you. Dark Souls II rewards patience, perseverance, and growth. I went from hating it to being completely in love with it.

If you’re on the fence, give it time. This game doesn’t open up to you — you have to fight your way into it. But when you do, the payoff is unforgettable.

Dark Souls II is not merely a game—it is a cathedral of suffering, a baroque tapestry woven with the threads of despair and triumph, a magnum opus that lesser minds might dare to call 'flawed.' To play it is to sip from the chalice of peak fiction, a vintage so refined it leaves the palate of modern gaming parched for anything remotely comparable. The clunky elegance of its world design? A deliberate stroke of genius, like a cubist painting smeared across the canvas of Lordran’s legacy. The hitboxes, you say, are imperfect? Nay, they are a philosophical challenge, a reminder that life itself is a dodge roll through an indifferent universe.

I chortled—yes, chortled—as I parried the Fume Knight with the grace of a Victorian dandy, my soul enriched by every bonfire’s flicker. The Shrine of Amana? A sonnet in blue, penned by a sadist poet. This is not a game for the faint of heart or the feeble of spirit; it is a pilgrimage for those who understand that true art lies in the grind. Five stars, for I have gazed into the abyss, and it offered me a +10 greatsword.

Dark Souls 2 iterates on Dark Souls 1's brutal level design in pretty awesome ways, while leaving the coherent interconnectedness behind. Certainly a loss, but not a crippling one, because damn if that level design isn't *good*. However- it is a test of patience. You WILL NOT enjoy this game if you do not have the patience for it.

The bosses in this game- save for the highlights in the DLCs- are unfortunately unremarkable or downright jokes, unfortunately. Even with that in mind, Looking Glass Knight is a genuinely creative boss, and Vendrick is a poignant and emotional fight. But uh... yeah, it's not winning any awards on the boss fight front; they're too easy, even for a game more focused on level design and having the bosses be areas' mid/endpoints.

The overall story in Dark Souls 2 is the easiest to follow in the trilogy. Where Dark Souls 1 is largely caught up in item descriptions, and Dark Souls 3 is almost impossible to grasp in isolation, Dark Souls 2 simply... lays it bare. If you pay attention to what the NPCs tell you, you'll have a full vision and grasp of what happened in the Kingdom of Drangleic, and what the true purpose of your quest within it is. This is especially apparent in the third DLC, which has honest-to-god the most straightforward story out of anything in the Dark Souls trilogy and it is really just incredible to experience, especially with it ending off with easily one of the coolest fights in the entire trilogy.

In conclusion: buy this game- if you think you've got the temperament for it.
