TLDR: After spending 100+ hours in DS1 remaster and enjoying it very much, I can say with clear conscious that this "definitive edition" is a garbage, especially when you look at the price tag. Description of the game in store says: "Gamers are in for a big surprise in DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin". But I didn't know it will be such a negative one.

Before you consider buying it, especially if you don't usually play with a controller and even if you played DS1 and/or Elden Ring, make some considerations.

All prompts in the game are for the controller, no matter what. Yes, the game is controllable with keyboard and mouse, but you will have to figure out most of it yourself as they are really different from previous game and your options to rebind them are more than limited. Even changing camera speed is a challenge, which you have to tackle because default camera speed would win a 100m sprint. There are two camera speeds in the settings, and you have to change (and confirm) the right one, which I even had to google out.

Playing offline is not an option. Admittedly, it was introduced in original DS3 and put into DS1 remaster, but it's a headscratcher for me, why it didn't make it into this definitive edition as well, especially when they decided to re-enable online features. So, either suffer through undesired PvP or block the game in your firewall.

It can be as well the best game of all time, but the first half an hour was disgusting and that's where it ends for me. My hopes now turns to DS3, although they are not high now

Playtime: 60 Hours

What I liked:
I appreciated being able to fast travel from the start, unlike in Dark Souls 1 — a welcome quality-of-life change. I also liked the introduction of the Adaptability stat, a new addition since DS1. It adds depth by affecting things like flask speed and dodge responsiveness, which I found to be an interesting and strategic mechanic.

What I didn’t like:
The Human Effigy system was a frustrating addition. Losing maximum health and gradually becoming hollow after each death felt unnecessarily punishing. On top of that, it increases the risk of invasions, which can seriously disrupt your playthrough — especially for solo players.

Overall:
For a Dark Souls game, there's a ton of content and replay value here. The slower-paced combat takes some adjustment, particularly early on, but the game offers a rewarding challenge once you get into the rhythm.
If you're looking for a tough but worthwhile experience, I recommend it — 6/10.

The game is pretty decent although it is not beginner friendly and even if you have come from playing other souls titles, it is not beginner friendly. The main reason for that being given one estus flask at the beginning of the game as opposed to ds1 with 5 and idk about ds3, although you can get 4 estus flasks before the first boss.

Secondly. the game penalizes you every time you die by limiting your HP. Lets say, you have 1000 health (Unhollowed) and you die. When you respawn again, you will have 900 health and so on (The health reduction percentage is an exaggeration here) Every time you die, it gets reduced by a little bit till you have 50% of your health bar. It will not go lower than 50% of your health.

There are items to prevent this like a human effigy, which although the game gives you a good amount, it is not infinite (Not to my knowledge), meaning you have to be moderately careful when using them. This item restores your health to 100%. There is a ring which lets you have a maximum health reduction of 75% at the cost of a ring slot. (You can have 4 ring slots but you can only equip one of each ring, ie the effects of the ring don't stack

Thirdly, there is a problem for adaptability. It isn't the biggest issue. Following is a short summary on how you want to play DS2

Google beginner weapons and see what you like
Get your stats to the point to where you can one hand the weapon
Get your adaptability to 25. Not only does this give more rolling I frames but also increases the speed of your actions (eg drink estus faster) and Vigor to 20. Do both these in any order but try to push these as hard and as fast as possible.

Personally I recommend reading up on what all the stats do in Ds2 and reading the soft and hard caps and then invest in the various stats depending on your build but ADP and VIG are very important.

Spoilers (personal problems)

Executioners Chariot run-back
Fire Spell in Iron Keep (100% completion requirement)
Dark Lurker boss fight requiring 2 human effigies per attempt.
Frozen outskirts.
Blue Smelter demon run-back
Ng+ Smelter Demon skip run-back "OIK"

Overall, if you are going in blind, then you are in for a rude awakening so I recommend going in with some kind of knowledge and the game will be more enjoyable. Except 100% completion/ all achievements which in that case, I highly recommend following a guide as there are things which are very easy to miss and you don't want to have to do those things in Ng+ whrere things hit much harder.

TLDR: A near-frustrating experience that constantly throws hordes of enemies at you making levels a much greater challenge than the bosses. Smoother combat makes DS2 a better modern experience than DS1.

PROS:
➕Fast travel
➕Generally great balance for builds
➕Much more smoother gameplay than DS1

NEUTRALS:
🔹Can be a big difference in feel from DS1 and DS3
🔹Durability can be punishing, requiring you to swap weapons frequently
🔹Art direction feels more dark fantasy-esque than DS1

CONS:
➖Overly punishing death mechanics (Max HP loss)
➖Constantly swarmed by multiple enemies
➖Enemies tend to be harder than bosses
➖Adaptability stat requires investment to make rolling good again
➖Non-consensual PVP at ANY time, which also contributes to Max HP loss
➖Dual-button functions Ex: parrying when you’re trying to attack

I don’t feel the game world is as good as DS1.

Much better than DS1. The combat feels a lot better. You can even aim in first person with a crossbow now! Much less clunky overall and new additions like dual wielding are nice.

I thought DS2 looked even better than the DS1 Remastered version.
i9-13900KF | RTX 4090 | 64GB RAM | 1440p @ 240hz | Windows 11 installed on SSD

Higher than DS1 because I encountered no audio issues and I enjoyed the ambiance of the levels. Everything is eerily quiet and if you listen close enough you can even hear the footsteps of a dozen enemies rushing at you from behind.

Small improvement over DS1 because you can get (only a few) re-specs.

Dark Souls 2 (DS2) has always been viewed as the black sheep of the DS family. To me though, I’ve considered it an improvement over DS1 in many ways. It’s still a large departure from the DS1/3 formulas, but I do think it brings some good worthwhile gameplay to the series.

The biggest negative to me in DS2 is more of a culmination of low points that each feed off one another. DS games in general can walk a fine line between fun and frustration. DS2 has a lot of frustrating enemy encounters where you are swarmed by multiple enemies. Frankly, I found the enemies/levels to be much harder than the bosses, which kinda seems backwards to me.
When you die in DS2, there’s a mechanic that you lose a portion of your max HP, thus making your next death come that much quicker. This stacks all the way until you’re around 50% of your max HP. Even non-consensual PVP makes you lose HP. At one point in Heide’s Tower, I was ganked 3x by the same player, the first time right after I used a humanity to restore my HP to max, which dying to PVP immediately negated. I opted to play offline for the majority of my run, because F that. Unfortunately, this did make the game feel a bit lonely.

As for the positives, I feel that the combat is much more smooth than DS1, but obviously still has ways to go before it reaches something like Elden Ring. Still, It's a huge leap in the right direction.
Another thing that makes a huge quality of life difference to me is fast travel. I hated having to trek through levels back and forth with bonfires in DS1. God forbid if I wanted to farm something off an enemy on the other side of the world, I’d have to walk all the way back there.

Overall, I do prefer DS2 over DS1 in perhaps the most important part - combat smoothness. While DS1 might have a more engrossing world (Nothing in DS2 matches the Blighttown or Anor Londo vibes), I think that DS2 takes the crown due to gameplay. Yes there are many times I have been more frustrated with DS2 than DS1, but nothing I’d go so far as to call unfair.

Why I would not recommend this game to other players:

1. The Hitboxes in this game are completely jank, for both the player and for enemies. You will teleport for attacks to hit you, attacks you swing will hit enemies behind you or outside of your range. Boss attack-hitboxes will not reflect the attack you witness.

2. Movement is not a true 360 degrees, but operates with a set number of hard points. This greatly detracts from the movement of DS1, and causes you to make wonky turns that will end up killing you during the platforming/exploration of the game. The inertia of the game is also wonky, and can especially be felt when trying to fall down to lower platforms.

3. A concerning amount of bosses may have a boss bar, but are just collections of base enemies that you would find outside of the door. Others can be easily cheesed by walking in circles around them. There may be more bosses than DS1, but they are all significantly lower in quality.

4. Exploration and story progression can be nightmarish. In DS1, where to progress was easy to ascertain due to the game's delivery of the story and the how cohesive the world/areas are. In DS2, there will be times where you will walk around aimlessly until you figure out some unclear task to progress, or find one of the 50 locked doors you passed while playing that has a new path to go forward, instead of a chest with a bobble or titanite.

Could go into my issues with durability, leveling, ADP (a broken stat that warps gameplay and RPG element of the game), Boss run backs, enemy saturation, and so on, but I think this will suffice.

Posted: March 10
All the disdain for this game that I have read and heard about over the years always seemed to be unjustified in my mind; I truly didn't think this game could be as bad as everyone made it out to be, but it is truly as bad as everyone says.

From the very moment you start playing, you can tell something is just inherently wrong with the game; the extremely bland colour palette along with the bizarre choice to reduce movement to eight directions opposed to the full 360 degree movement that was present in the first Dark Souls. This one thing alone makes doing fine movements basically impossible. If you try to line yourself up with a jump say for instance, or you're walking along a tight ledge, your character will just automatically snap to one of the 8 directions, throwing you off entirely.

Tying I-Frames to a stat is also just a bizarre choice as well; I can sort of understand where the developers were going with it, the more agile you are, the more I-Frames you get... but it just seems like a weird area to focus on while developing the game. Why not just leave it the same as the first Dark Souls where you get a set-amount of I-Frames depending on whatever roll you have instead of having two forces determining how many I-Frames you get; adaptability and equip load.

You also don't get I-Frames while doing actions like opening doors or pulling levers; what was the point of implementing this? Not like it makes the game inherently harder, it just makes things annoying. It wouldn't be the worst if most doors actually stayed open after returning to certain areas, but no.

Also, the fact that you have to upgrade your stats to drink Estus faster is dumb; at the start of the game, your character acts as if the Estus Flask weighs a ton and takes what feels like a solid ten seconds to finish the whole drinking animation.

The over-abundance of upgrade materials also just makes the game extremely easy; the player is no longer forced to spec into using one weapon, you can just have all the +10 and +5 weapons you like. Sure, it allows you to experiment more with weapons, but it also just makes the weapons feel less special than they are; in the first Dark Souls you would more often than not base an entire build around one weapon and stick with it till the end. Maybe you would upgrade other weapons, but due to the scarcity of upgrade materials you would, again, just focus on the one weapon you care about. The locations of these upgrade materials are also so weird; you'll just find corpses with Titanite Slabs, or Twinkling Titanite and not just one most of the time, they'll have three or so.

That along with Titanite Lizards being ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ everywhere makes maxing out ♥♥♥♥ even more easy, especially items that are "rare"; by the end of my play-through I had 80 Twinkling Titanite just sitting in my inventory and around 13 Titanite Slabs.

Speaking of making the game easy, basically all the bosses on this game you'll kill on your first try if you have some basic understanding of how to play a Souls-like. A lot of them have extremely slow move sets or very minimal ones that are extremely easy to read, and most of the time you can just find some way to insta-kill the boss (Dragonrider and Pursuer to just label two.) And then there are bosses that are just really awful to deal with, such as the Gank Squad in the Sunken King DLC, you have to run through an extremely awful area just to fight an extremely garbage boss, what fun.

Along with reusing bosses repeatedly AND just implementing bosses from the first Dark Souls for some reason; it's just extremely lazy. Not saying the first game didn't reuse the same bosses, but at least the first game didn't have 40+ bosses.

The world is also just too big for its own good. Most of the areas are extremely boring to explore due to both the boring colour palette, and the lack of stage-setting; getting invested in the world is a lot harder when everything is just empty rooms filled with the same annoying enemies. I would have much rather them make a smaller world with more time spent in making it interesting, opposed to having a big world that feels empty. And most of the places you don't have much of a reason to revisit, unless there are key items you missed or hidden boss fights that you feel like trying, but that's about it.

Also, no idea why they thought implementing the same invaders over and over and over again was a smart idea, it gets a little stale by the tenth time you have to fight Forlorn again, but maybe that's just me. These invaders are also just in the most annoying places possible; I don't mind developers trolling in games, but when it happens over and over again with the same boring invaders, it doesn't feel that special.

And finally but not least the absolutely bottom-tier DLCs; a lot of them have pretty neat worlds to explore, but again they get plagued with the same problem that the rest of the game faces; they're too boring and easy if you have any sort of basic understanding of Souls-like games, and then you reach sections that absolutely infuriating for all the wrong reasons. Not because they're hard, but because the developers thought it would be wise to fill a tiny room with 5+ enemies that all spam the same attacks. Why oh why couldn't they have just made the DLCs more interesting; it could have give this game the reputation of "the base game sucks, but the DLCs were good at least." The best of the worst in my eyes was Ivory King, minus the Frigid Outskirts, even people who actually like this game agree that area is complete garbage.

Now there are very few things that they did implement that were positive changes, such as Powerstancing, having four ring slots instead of two, being able to move while using items (even if very slow) making jumping feel not *as* bad and being able to warp to every single bonfire, opposed to ones that are deemed "key" ones like in the first Dark Souls.

But overall, I would say avoid this game. It has very little to offer and barely connects to the first game lore wise; minus a few items and some lazy fan service things. If you want to play another Souls game besides the first Dark Souls, play Dark Souls III. Way better in every regard compared to this game.

After doing a full 30+ hour playthrough of Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin (All bosses, not all achievements) I think I can finally write my review. Before I say anything negative about the game (I'm gonna say a lot), this is a very good game, better than a lot of games. However, it is not better than any of the other Soulsborne games I've played (DS1, DS3, Elden Ring, Sekiro).

THE GOOD

All of the characters are great, especially best guy Lenigrast. At least half of the bosses are good (A few in the DLC, a good handful in the base game) which I won't specify for spoilers. Weapons, even the Ultra Greatswords, are pretty fast. Warping between all bonfires. A LOT of bosses (there's 41, only 2 of which are repeats of earlier bosses). All of the animations are very good. Fast running and climbing ladders. Majula is not my personal favorite hub area, but I definitely see why it is for most. It's very nice and cozy. And that's about it.

THE BAD

Where to start? Max health degredation from Demon's Souls is back, though not as severe, still sucks. Animations may be good, but they are so unbelievably slow. Drinking Estus takes forever, and doesn't even instantly heal. Most of the levels have absolutely horrible design. It's like if Dark Souls 1 was all Blighttown. Adaptability is unacceptable. Rolling correctly shouldn't be locked behind a few thousand souls at the beginning of the game. Most of the games worst bosses are either gimmick fights or gank squads (Player-like NPC enemies). And by far the worst part of the entire game is the boss runbacks. 90% of the bosses have some of the worst boss runbacks in video game history, namely one in a DLC (one so bad there are memes about it). I can't think of anything else off the top of my head, but there's likely more.

THE UGLY

Hollowing is so ugly in this game. You don't look like beef jerky like DS1, you start slowly looking like a zombie. It's so unbelievably ugly, and I hate it.

VERDICT

DS2 is a great game, but far from a perfect game, and far from the best Soulsborne game. I would say I enjoyed the game, but felt angry or disappointed a lot too. Greatsword is nice though. Overall, I only think you should play this if you are a die-hard DS fan, and play it last too.

TL;DR

Good game, only play if you really love FromSoft

Posted: March 7
Dark Souls 2 is very experimental and it shows. It added a lot of good features such as powerstancing, but also added some features that had me scratching my head. After playing through the entirety of the game, including all of its DLCs, it's definitely not as terrible as most people make it out to be. However, its low points are some of the most frustrating and unfair moments in the entire franchise. I uninstalled and reinstalled this game numerous times going through the base game.

The agility mechanic feels cheap and I'm glad it never made its way into future FromSoftware games. The game feels alright after getting agility to around 105-110. Even with agility leveled up, everything feels slow, a lot slower than even Dark Souls 1. Whether it's going through mists, opening doors, or opening chests, you do these actions at a snail's pace.

Some areas and enemy spawns can also seem absurd. On the bright side, there are also some positives—the DLC bosses are quite fun, and the DLCs in general have some great areas which are way better than the base game. I do also like how much build variety there is. Despite not personally doing NG+ myself, I appreciate the thought the developers put into having so many unique weapons and playstyles that could be had with those weapons.

I think it's worth at least trying Dark Souls 2 if you're on the fence about playing it. This game definitely has more flaws than other FromSoftware titles, and does need to be played differently than other Souls games due to its mechanic changes, but I still think it's a unique experience you won't find anywhere else.

Just played for the 4th or 5th time coming back and decided to 100% the game's achievements. I wouldn't wish this upon my worst enemy, but it gave me more insight into the game and the design choices that made themselves apparent now that I've done everything. I was in love, but weirded out on my first playthrough. Stayed up all night at a friend's house and went absolutely ham and loved it. Ive had mixed feelings up until this last experience, and I've come to a final conclusion:

Dark Souls 2 is a miserable pile of ♥♥♥♥ from a butt. Its a series of frustrating scenarios where the game straight up trolls you, and not in a way that can be simply and/or consistently avoided like in the other titles. It gimps you, then hits you when you're down and laughs at you. Like the game prides itself on putting you at an insurmountable disadvantage because it just has to one-up its predecessor. The mechanics like SM and ADP are there set in stone to punish you for playing the game how you would like, and even when youre spending your precious soul memory on damage stats, they soft cap as soon as you get the requirements for mid game spells and weapons and most have crushingly diminishing returns. Its like the game wants to steer you toward a weak jack of all trades build regardless of your starting class, and punishes you for specializing.

Wanna use spells? Chaos Fireball and WotG were too easy to acquire in 1, lets make the player beat the game 2 and a half times or win 500 janky ass PVP fights in order to get them now. Genius nerf by B-Team. And many of these hard earned spells are ridiculously bad or useless. 90% of the sorceries are like a 5 second cast time spell that has an effect similar to something you would see at a water park with similar damage until you get 50 INT and 3 rings to amp the damage. And I don't mean like Homeward useless, I mean literally there is never a single reason to ever buy or use this spell in any scenario ever due to poor design or aggressively weak effects that also never hit anything. Favorite moment was launching an 80,000 soul "climax" at Fume Knight for a whopping 300 damage. Out ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ standing.

DS2 gets a bad reputation, but is still an excellent game by any reasonable standard.

The pros:

-Beautiful world design and rewarding exploration
-Pretty good balance of builds. Sorcery, strength, dex, any number of builds work pretty equally well

The cons:

I really don't have much negative to say about this game. I believe it holds its own with the others in the series, and is very much worth playing if you like the genre.

Posted: March 5
I highly recommend the video essay "A Decade of Dark Souls 2" by "Babbbs". It helped sway me into giving this misunderstood sequel another try after twenty years.

This game has so many brilliant answers for all the cheese and mechanical limitations of Dark Souls I (one of my favorite games of all time).

I hated this game at launch, but with all of the content that the SOTFS (PC) version offers, as well having recently replayed DSI for the first time since high school, I needed another fix.

The bonfire ascetics and simpleton's and skeptic's spices are BRILLIANT creations. DSIII is lesser for not having them.

DSII is a completionist's dream (or nightmare depending on your POV).

I also highly recommend the video"Odd and obscure things in Dark Souls 2" by "STAR RUN."

The amount of hidden features and easter eggs in this game is staggering.

For anyone to throw this game away as a more jank DSI with ♥♥♥♥♥♥ hit boxes is seriously missing out on some truly unique mechanics and courageous design choices.

Yes, the level design from a geometric standpoint makes no sense. Yes the hit reg APPEARS broken.
I highly recommend the video: "The Dark Souls 2 Hitbox Debate Ends Now" by "Babe1Babe2" for an in-depth look at exactly how the hitboxes work in this game.

The weapons, the amount of content, the build variety, the decision to redo the animations and backstabs, parry system, etc. it all combines to make something that truly stands apart from DSI, yet it compliments it so well.

It is not perfect, but neither is DSI (if only they had more development time).

I love this game.
I love Dark Souls I.

This game is amazing.
It is also far and away the easiest Dark Souls game.
Especially if you use summons and are not a "fromsoftware-sexual" try-hard.

Enjoy it. It is a rich sandbox with incredible replay value and deep customization options that neither DSI or DSIII can match.

And lastly: "Try tongue but hole."

Posted: March 2
Well, I just beat the and finished the main game + the DLC's and there is quite a lot to say. TL;DR People should get over themselves hating this game, cause it's not as terrible as people say, but there are a lot of terrible game designs that don't make me enjoy it as much as DS1.

Bad: Runbacks in these games are absolutely horrendous, while some bosses are within spitting distance from the bonfire. The consistency is just really weird. A few bosses are just reskins or they just make two of them cause sure why not? While some are a bit gimmicky while not necessarily terrible. The quantity of enemies within one area does not equal challenging, they just become annoying roadblocks to progression, even if you kill them enough times to despawn. And finally what I think is the worst thing that Dark Souls II has done is lowering your max HP each time you die. Uhhhh excuse me??? This is a franchise infamous for the single fact that you will die over and over again, and now you actively PUNISH ME for doing something that is guarantee to happen? No other Soulsborne game has that feature.

Good: I love the worldbuilding. Much more grand and ambitious, but at the cost of removing the interconnectivity that DS1 is memorable for, which isn't a bad thing. I loved the different boss designs, the enemy designs, and the lore was interesting. NPC's are also solid, and the lockstone and torch mechanic, while a bit gimmicky, were fun to utilize when needed, and I like the expansion of ring slots from 2 to 4.

I would give the game an 8/10, maybe a bit more, maybe a bit less, but it certainly shouldn't receive sooo much hate. Be constructive and analyze what *really* deserves the criticism in this game and what doesn't. Good luck Bearer of the Curse, try tongue but hole

Alr I've decided to edit this and make a fair review

The game isn't THAT bad it's just not THAT good either it's not like the game doesn't have any huge issues(it does) but the game does a lot of things exclusive to ds2 very good things like I genuinely believe if this game was more polished before released and had maybe 50% to 70% of it's flaws fixed it would be on par or even better than ds3 but tbh my main issue is just the excessive number of enemies as well as there aggro distance(the distance they detect you not the distance they'll chase you) the just has too many enemies crammed into various parts of the game and you just have to deal with it and I don't mind cutting down multiple enemies but they're always placed in frustrating areas forcing you to use a bow or sorcery just to pick off enemies before engaging which slows down the game a bit too much for me but still this game has potential I just wanna see a modder create a mod that reduces the numbers a bit(not too much) and reduce the aggro range a little though if the game is on sale and you happen to be a dark souls or any souls-like fan then this game could be worth your time I just can't recommend this game at full price.


Now for 3 pros and 3 cons(there are definitely more of both but I just wanted to do 3)

Pros:

Majula: arguably the best hub area in any souls-like.

Powerstance: even though elden ring allows powerstancing I genuinely believe it's better done in ds2.

Bonfire acetic/NG+: ds2 likely did NG+ the best due to making exclusive rewards available only in NG+(or higher) and doesn't even force you to beat the game to get them, that's where bonfire acetics come in but due note the some bosses on NG+ gain new mechanics that can make the boss feel more fresh... or more annoying(lookin' at you lost sinner).


Cons:

Obnoxious enemy placement: there everywhere, ok not literally but expect every doorway to have an enemy to the left, right, above or a mix of the bunch and just when you think you think you got 'em all BOOM stray projectile to the back and I swear ranged enemies aren't even that bad they just always screw with you at the worst times.

High ping players/Hackers(typically invaders): I've never ran into hackers but I hear they've tend to cause more game breaking problems, I recommend backing up your saves often however something I have run into a lot are players with high ping(expect to get facestabbed often if happen to run into one)

Map design: the maps are all really creative usually very unique from one-another however... you know how every souls-like has that one or two parts of the game that is typically frustrating to traverse by the majority?

yeah ds2 has a lot of 'em... the undead crypt still gives me nightmares(I can still here the bells!)

I tried to be un-biased whilst also still giving my opinion, just don't hate on the game to much the devs tried with what little time they had and it was a different team if I remember correctly but also stop meat riding the game it's definitely underrated but a lot of the criticisms are valid.

p.s. I don't think ADT is that bad, upgrade it early and I promise the rest of the game will much easier(around 100 to 110 AGL) also don't let some tryhard tell you ADT is for those who are bad at rolling, this game starts you off with much less iframes than ds1 and ds3(especially if mix it with the inconsistent hitboxes of ds2)

My favourite Dark Souls game.
While for me the DS1 has the best story and atmosphere, DS2 is like a beautiful adventure. You just go on and only need to think what kind of build you'd like to have, while the game takes you through an abundance of different unique areas and challenges.
It's also my favourite Soulslike PvP game. The build variety (stats, spells, items) and the restriction on rolling too much makes for every fight to be fun and interesting. I've spent at least 50 hours on that lava bridge.
While the story and the world might not be as unique and gripping as DS1, I still love it as a true sequel. This game focuses on the final solution to the endless cycles of life and death. You work towards exiting the cycles, by visiting the remnants of all those that tried to fight them before you.
I just wish they remaster this game in the future and add a constant co-op option, so me and my future son don't have to kill every boss twice :).

Honest Review for Dark Souls 2 after beating every boss and 100% the game

1. The Experience
Ds2 environments are genuinely nice to look at, the weather impacts the total vibe very nicely Drangleic Castle particularly and Heids Tower of Flame. Other than that most of the game is mostly unfun a straight or c u r v y line, every dungeon is designed nicely until reaching the next area. mostly every path follows: c u r v y line towards a boss, long ass elevator, the actual area that makes no sense to the environment before (LOOKING AT YOU DRAGON AERIE AND IRON KEEP). Most levels straight up show to a direct path to the boss to the next area and you dont have to play the game, at all, if you do you can either go through very ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ level design either walking through grass in which just has a hole with no hint at all, having the difficult curve depending if you do it or not. If you do run straight to the boss, every single encounter will be ass unless you do run the classical mace and rapier cheese, question is, is dark souls 2 softs or dark souls 2 rapier and mace edition? It doesnt make a for good level design, zero reason for natural progression in a curve for the basic player who isnt trying to do everything, another thing is the well ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ upon agility stat, the Smelter Demon isnt even that hard, just level ADPT, why does combat that feels not ass unlocked after going so far into the game? This mean you cannot try out that cool weapon you found because you dont have 50 adpt, its not fun to explore because you cant use it or else the whole experience fighting enemies and bosses will just feel like ♥♥♥♥. I understand the appeal of magic in this game after going through it with a melee weapon and spells in the following NG++++ to 100% the game, its night and day, you cant deal with adpt being a problem if you just avoid the probleml, so why bother do anything else, it make more sense than just melee. WHICH sucks because the melee system is actually really fun and cool, weapons are cool, dual wielding is cool, but its ass because you cant because most of you stats are into ADPT so why bother, use mace and rapier, the game is unfun to progress through because of the games design. The DLC on the other hand, are nice unless you havent leveled to a point where you are far past the end of the base game, the damage dealt the the sunken king DLC enemies are just extremely high, and thats running a havel monster build I used for that DLC, The Ivory King DLC was actually fun as a love letter towards how much people just run through the game, progress to make things reasonable, its a love letter to how most people played the game, running through everything. Brume Tower DLC was probably my favourite DLC, because of the boss battles i had the most fun, Alonne was the first boss in the game where i had to focus, it was fun and even more fun to use his gear in NG+. The Fume Knight on the other hand was not as interesting, i blame mostly to my build and level as it lasted 6 hits from the glorious guts greatsword lightning and sunlight blade. Despite that was is a big hitter is just finding and inserting the r o d, and reigniting the entire facility, absolutely fantastic. Note I did Brume Tower, Ivory, and Sunken last.

2. Getting achievements
This was the worst experience ive had 100% a game, someone in their ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mind thought it was a good idea to lock some spells AFTER 500 PVP WINS, WHY in general WHO WOULD GRIND 500 KILLS. Thank god they did add other ways of unlocking said spells..... BY BEATING THE GAME 4 TIMES. On the other hand the other spells that you cannot unlock this way is through ♥♥♥♥ ass drop rates. the berserker man in the belfry sol was not bad, BUT SUN LIGHT METALS ARE GENUINELY THE MOST WORST FARM EVER. WHY THE ♥♥♥♥ ARE THE DROP RATES ABYSMAL. 100% the game was learning how poorly designed the level design was, how much content didn't matter, boss completely skippable and irrelevant, ENTIRE LEVELS irrelevant because the boss is in a C U R V Y LINE.

2.5:
I SWEAR 50% of most enemies moves set are locked in NG+ beyond, ive seen MORE interesting combat in my NG++++ run than THE ENTIRE EXPERIENCE.

3. Final Note

Ds2 is a mixing pot of design because of its very poor production, from the cut content uncovered by the community this game had such cool concepts and designed that where stitched together in a incohesive mess that has random PEAK design in characters, yet such ass designs in combat and level design. the combat idea for the systems are SO ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ COOL but at the same time progression is cucked by AGILITY so good old mace and rapier and no other weapon. I would not recommend this game and im uninstalling after getting all the achievements and this review.

Since 2022 I greatly got an interest in Souls-borne games and still have a whole list of these games I want to experience. In this case, I decided to go for the highly acclaimed trilogy - "DARK SOULS". The first game was actually interesting even though outdated gameplay mechanics it still holds up pretty well, but I have mixed feelings about the second part. "DARK SOULS II: Scholar of the First Sin" was visually stunning but gameplay-wise rough game. NPCs were so disproportionately spawned throughout the map that it gave me a stroke during the playthrough... It's not just about difficulty level but also the balance between enjoying the game and taking a challenge. Visually, as I mentioned before, the game was stunning and the creators did a great job with art design alongside all 3 DLCs. Yet, the best part of the game still remains the lore and how Gwyn's selfish, ambitious, and ruthless actions disrupted the natural order that kept The Undead Curse flowing for hundreds of thousands of years. To conclude, if you are a Souls fan and enjoy this kind of dark fantasy, you should check out every single game in the franchise to know how it all came together and what changes these games went through to reach the level of perfection. Now... it's time for the final chapter!

Posted: February 21
I am a megafan of DS1. I think DS2 is hated on too much. The game tried to be a direct sequel to DS1. It does most of what DS1 did, but in a larger way. More enemy types, more areas, more bosses, more items and weapons, more uprgades, more secrets, but your goals and grind are the same. They tried to extend the length of the game, tried to create more boss-esq battles with a huge amount of mini bosses, it added more magic and spells. The game tried to simply grow off of the DS1 formula. They tried to keep the system very similar to DS1 but also added some quality of life changes like usable torches, 4 rings instead of 2, more online options, new humanity system, larger quick inventory, and so-on. In trying to do this, I think they lost their path. The game becomes very long, sometimes the mini bosses become tedious , sometimes unlocking areas becomes more of a drag than a excitement. It is essentially just a bigger and more dished out version of DS1. What DS1 had that felt so good, was a level of simplicity and order to it. Those go out the window with DS2 as they tried to make the game alittle more advanced and chaotic. Often there are moments where its unclear where you're supposed to go next, or what your overall goal even is. DS1 had more hand-held guidance as far as what to do next, it was more linear. There are points where the game seems to be outright copying material from DS1 like certain enemies and locations, or specific puzzles and gimmicks. While the game does well at copying that DS1/souls borne feeling, the same principle applies for some of DS1s downsides. There are many areas that are extremely annoying to deal with. They are not difficult in a skill-based way, but more in a routine/method way. Im talking about the classics, like poison swamps and double black knight archers. Areas that make you want to rage quit but dont offer anything for skillful play-style, like bosses or puzzles may. Theres new stuff, but it does feel alittle frustrating to see recycled content in a blatant way. The game looks and sounds beautiful, it plays well although maybe slightly alittle more clunky than DS1. Overall its a toss-up based on whos playing the game and why.

What DS2 does good -
The additions of new weapons, equipment and spells are fun
The quality of life improvements are solid
The diversity of enemy types are great
Good online balance
AMAZING visuals
AMAZING soundtrack/design
decently well done open world design
It delivers that souls-born play style on a larger scale

Where DS2 struggles :
Struggles with monotony
Mini bosses can feel pointless and irritating
Arguable resource grind for things like branches and keys
ALOT of dark invaders
It ups the scale on annoying difficulty
Sometimes its hard to understand your next objective
Slightly clunky feeling compared to DS1

Overall I think its an interesting continuation from DS1 and if you enjoyed the formula of DS1, you SHOULD play DS2. If not for enjoyment, then to actually understand why you liked DS1 and what about it makes it such a good game.

3.5/5

This game in the first half wasn't too terrible. The areas weren't as bad as people were saying, and while the bosses were bad i thought it would get better. It didn't, every boss in this game with a very select few (1 or 2) were terrible. People play this game for the bosses and almost all of them suck. Not only are they easy with the slowest most predictable attacks, most of them are just enemies you've fought before just with a health bar. This wouldn't be an issue if the areas were good. Except they aren't, each area felt bland and uninteresting with even more uninteresting enemies that would stack 15 types of statuses onto you. You sludge through the garbage areas, only to fight an even more garbage boss. You only play this to say you've beaten every dark souls game. If you enjoyed it, I'm envious, I struggled very hard to get any enjoyment out of this game.
The base game is around a 4/10: I still had some enjoyment playing this but getting around to the 2nd half was a challenge to want to play and have fun. Dreaded logging onto this game almost every night so i can finish it. The ending was pretty dissatisfying with Nashandra being completely boring to fight. You can't fight Aldia if you don't fight Vendrick who has disgusting resistances unless you keep the giant souls. Which most people eat away unless you looked up boss fights prior. The entire game just felt unrewarding at the end cuz your fat ass just gets locked away. Emerald Herald is a baddie tho so idm if she gives me some gawk.
DLC 1, 4/10: This DLC was genuinely so forgettable with even more forgettable bosses, the final boss Sinh lazily flies into the air and does ♥♥♥♥ all. Area's weren't too bad. Shulva was pretty alright and I had some fun exploring. Other than that this is the middle child.
DLC 2 7/10: This DLC was kinda fun, the areas weren't the worst and I enjoyed how they were set up to be a giant tower. The first boss however is just a rehashed version of a boss you've already fought and was disappointing. However the next 2 bosses were bangers. Fume knight was extremely fun in the first phase, but got a bit droll in the 2nd phase If i want to be honest. However Sir Alonne was amazing and definitely the best boss DS2 had to offer. The only critique is maybe the combo strings could've been a bit longer but still an enjoying fight nonetheless.
DLC 3 -400/10: This dlc was ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ terrible, I was so disappointed. The first fight with Avaa requires you to explore and find a key item since its invisible which I didn't mind. The fight was actually really fun. Then you discover the frigid outskirts, what a disappointment of an area. You're in the middle of nowhere in a bilzzard that blinds you and you have to traverse for like 20 minutes before finally finding the boss arena. The area is filled with ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ horses that follow you throughout the end of time and snipe you, and finally when you get past the horses and find the boss arena, guess what awaits you. A rehashed version of Avaa except its just 2 of them. I wouldn't bash on this area if the Bosses weren't so terribly lazy and gave us literally anything else. This area was such a failure and I would rather go through Blightown with 15x the snipers than ever play this part of the game again. Lastly the final boss of this DLC, the burny ivory king, I think he has an interesting idea but its translated pretty poorly. You want to ideally find the 4 knights which are around the maps and I think its very cool actually. However the AI is utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and instead of the knights helping you, they just run away while you get bent over and ♥♥♥♥♥♥ down by 25 knights while the 4 sit in the corner and watch you. The actual part of the boss fight was decently fun but not enough for me to like this DLC.
Final thoughts is that this game was very disappointing

EDIT: Have now beaten Dark Souls 3 in its entirety and if you're looking for a sequel to Dark Souls 1, I recommend you play Dark Souls 3 instead, as Dark Souls 2 clearly is not it. I'd also recommend watching a video on youtube by 'Matthewmatosis' called 'Dark Souls 2 Critique' if you'd like for more information on such.

Recently finished Dark Souls 1 and though-roughly enjoyed it and immediately started Dark Souls 2. Sadly this game constantly (and ramps up as such the deeper you get) appears to be a mockery of its previous title. The cons far outweigh the pros. A sea of intentionally bad designer and developer choices. I am amazed at how much they have dropped the ball on Dark Souls 2 in comparison to Dark Souls 1. I understand that this is the "B team" and not the "A team" (those who worked on Dark Souls 1; 3; and Bloodborne) but this is impressive work. Also having your director swapped-out mid-way through alone is a torpedo to any large project. Also doesn't help that coincidentally, Miyazaki was not a director at all for this yet was for all other Souls games, ER, and BB.

Die-hard Souls fans will be obtaining and playing this game regardless of reviews. Due to the nature of reviews, I see it is for those on the fence and as such I strongly suggest to skip this one. A very disappointing and let-down of a game, especially considering the previous entry in the series.

It's also rather telling that negative and critical reviews and videos on Dark Souls 2 are spammed with negative reactions (dislikes and clown awards), yet, you do not see this with any other From Soulslike, such as DS1; DS3; or ER. The playerbase of this game is seemingly rather spiteful and ugly.

Here's the thing, this game sucks. But it's actually amazing. Let me explain:

There's a lot of mechanical problems with this game. Certain features like agility or stamina recharge speed are vital to the combat but are never explained, dying reduces your max HP which only makes you die more, many enemies have erratic attacks that are tedious to avoid, and the learning curve is frustratingly steep. You definitely don't want to play this if it's your first souls game.

But all of these problems are solved by leveling one special stat: Patience.

Once you accept the game for what it is, and embrace its flaws as part of the challenge, DS2 is an excellent game. The environments and scenery are beautiful and diverse. The level design always feels hand-crafted, even when it's being evil. The sense of adventure and exploration is incredible. The lore is really interesting and there are some awesome characters.

Overall, I would highly recommend Dark Souls 2. Just make sure that you have the right mindset before you play it.

The Divorce Child People Sadly Had No Love For
The number two in the souls series is in my opinion the best Dark Souls overall. Speaking in its entirety: the scenery and zones, the characters, the diagolue and quests they have, the beautiful dragons, the unqiue graphics style and the one game they made that does not have a painfully cryptic story you will definitely have to watch a VaatiVidya video on to understand what is happening. It's a beautiful story that is much more forward than you're used to from them.

The Biggest Problem People Have With The Game
The stats work differently than what they're used to. I-frames are not only by default fixed to the 3 equip load statuses like in the other Dark Souls, but additionally modified through the new adaptibility stat. So you technically start out with a smaller and tighter dodge window than in DS1. The game is also a lot faster. You basically have to get your ADP and Agility up to max out your i-frames. Here is a detailed explanation how exactly it is different from DS1 and DS3.

A Well Rounded Game With A Few Weaknesses
Some zones and NPCs sure seem to be meant to piss you off, much more so then in the other titles. They really tried to test you and make you mad.
Some parts of the game seem a bit copy&paste or more vast than they need to be, like parts of the DLCs I found to be somewhat underwhelming.
If you know about how the stats work in DS2 I had literally no issue with hitboxes, combat or any of that for what it commonly gets criticized for. It's a very precise game in my opinion. If at all only marginally worse than the DS3 mechanics.

I can only speak on SOFTS, because that's the only thing I've played. As far as I know a lot of issues with the earlier version got fixed, but also some enemy placements got changed, which some people find weird that are used to the earlier version. I did not find anything weird with this version or that things seemed out of place. It is the definitive edition.

Having said all of that, DS3 is clearly superior in the overall gameplay mechanics, graphics and PVP. It's no surprise because it's the newest one and the most modern. Even though the gameplay is superior and more solid, I just felt less of a connection to it with a lot of the zones it had, the story and the vibe it put forward. It's very great, undoubtedly. Dark Souls 2 to me was just a more beautiful journey and seemed more mysterious. I love them all, but DS2 is first place for me, DS3 very very close right after on place two and DS1 on the third.

Dark Souls II: A Hollowed Experience

Dark Souls II is the black sheep of the Souls franchise, and for good reason. While it retains the core mechanics of its predecessors, it stumbles in almost every way that matters, feeling like an imitation rather than a true successor.
Level Design: Quantity Over Quality

The game is filled with uninspired, disconnected areas that lack the cohesion and environmental storytelling that made Dark Souls a masterpiece. Instead of a tightly interwoven world, we get a series of linear, often illogical locations that feel stitched together with no thought to spatial consistency. Why does an elevator in a windmill take me up to a castle surrounded by lava? Dark Souls was known for its intricate world design—Dark Souls II abandons that entirely.
Enemy Spam and Artificial Difficulty

Rather than offering well-crafted encounters, the game opts for overwhelming the player with sheer numbers. Fighting multiple enemies at once is frustrating, not because of deep combat mechanics, but because the game wasn't designed for it. The overuse of ambushes and cheap encounters make it clear that difficulty here isn't about skill—it's about enduring frustration. Enemies do not infinitely spawn unless you're in the Company of Champions covenant, which is also ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.
Clunky Combat and I-Frame Dependency

The controls feel noticeably worse than in other Souls games. Hitboxes are inconsistent, movement is sluggish, and the addition of Adaptability—a stat that determines how many I-frames you have when rolling—forces players to invest in leveling up just to make dodging function properly. This is a baffling design choice that punishes players for no reason other than padding stats.
Lackluster Bosses and Gimmick Fights

Dark Souls is famous for its unforgettable boss fights, yet Dark Souls II delivers some of the blandest, most forgettable encounters in the series. Many bosses feel like slightly modified versions of regular enemies, often fought in boring, empty arenas. Worse still, too many of them rely on gimmicks rather than genuine challenge—whether it's summoning multiple enemies, forcing players into awkward terrain, or relying on uninteresting mechanics rather than skillful combat design.
Souls Memory: A Broken Multiplayer System

The introduction of Souls Memory—a mechanic that matches players based on the total number of Souls they've earned—completely ruins PvP and co-op. Instead of being matched based on level and equipment, you're often thrown against opponents far beyond your power level, making invasions and cooperative play feel random and unfair.
A Hollowed Experience

In the end, Dark Souls II is a Souls game in name only. It lacks the careful world-building, satisfying combat, and masterful boss design that define the series. Instead, it leans on artificial difficulty, poor design choices, and a general sense of inconsistency. While some enjoy its variety and sheer amount of content, it ultimately feels like a shallow, frustrating imitation of what makes Dark Souls great.

Final Score: 2/10

DARK SOULS II: Scholar of the first sin (2014)

СЮЖЕТ: Даже с учётом того, что к игре Миядзаки и ко практически не притрагивались, все равно сюжет оформлен в нашем любимом стиле - "белые пятная на лице сценаристов".
Ничего напрямую не говорится и все нужно узнавать на Ютубе, единственное что может понять игрок проходя игру, что тут какие-то проклятые короли, сходящие с ума рыцари и тотальная депрессия. дефолт для соулс лайка.
Единственное что могу ответить для этой части, это ее постоянное заигрывание со сложностью игры(о которой позже) и с бесконечными смертями игрока.

ГЕЙМПЛЕЙ: Все очень плохо. Так уж вышло, что DS 2 я проходил после первой и третьей части, которые славились своими мелким деталям, тщательно проработанными локациями и геймдизайном, который с тобой говорит. Помним, да? Забудьте! Двойку делал не Миядзаки, а другие люди, про этом они абсолютно не поняли в чем вообще прикол оригинала. Локации блеклые и душные, местами очень сырые и забагованные(игре 11 лет). Реально могу похвалить ток одну локу - лес павших гигантов, она пускай и душновата, но хотя бы доделана. Вторая часть больше не то что не метроидвания, вторая часть в принципе больше не Дарк Солс. И оно не мудрено, изначально игра должна была стать опенворлдом(привет Elden Ring) и делалась всего неполных 1,5 года. Очевидно, что тщательно проработать каждую локу, хотя бы на уровне нормы - это очень тяжело. Поэтому мы получаем полное отсутствие бектрекинга, полное отсутствие в геймдизайне, малое количество секретов, Ctrl+c Ctrl+v вместо нормально расположения врагов, ну и вишенка на торте - в некоторых локациях невозможно вернуться обратно к костру без использования специального предмета.
Как говорится, два шага вперёд и один назад, поэтому DARK SOULS II привнес новую систему здоровья. Теперь за каждую смерть у игрока понижается маасимальный запас здоровья, что можно излечить с помощью специального предмета(который ок бесконечный и его нужно фармить). Здоровье так может понижаться вплоть до 50%
То есть вы понимаете, да?) Теперь если ты попадаешь в душную локу(вспоминаем про отсутствие баланса в расставление врагов(CTRL+C и CTRL+V)) или встречаешь душного босса, то ты ещё и хп теряешь. Ваааааау. Как будто бы игра недостаточно душная и нужно добавить духоты, и это ещё не всё между прочим! Если ты хочешь вернуть прежний показатель здоровья? Иди гринди специальный расходник. Что говоришь? Не выпал, ну так пацан, это только твои проблем, у нас тут хардкор, у нас тут СЛОЖНО! И так во всем, игра постоянно китчится какая она сложная и не для всех, а все почему? Ну типо, это DARK SOULS, тут типо про страдания да. И даже достижение "This is DARK SOULS"(выдаётся после первой смерти) не помогает. Игра всячески пытается тебя убить или заморить и убить, после чего ещё и наказывает за это.
С другой стороны мы получили систему прокачки фласок, которая нам знакома по третьей части, и которой так не хватало в оригинале. Да, на удивление эта фишка появилась именно в двойке(одно из немногих удачных решений лол). Теперь костры нельзя прокачивать, следственно и фласки тоже. Чтобы улучшить или увеличить число фласок, теперь нужно искать специальные осколки(вспоминаем про уровень проработки локации и понимаем, какое "увлекательное" приключение нас ждёт)

БОССЫ: И опять же спорно. С одной стороны, DS 2 явно выигрывает количеством хороших боссов по сравнению с DS 1, с другой стороны боссов в DS 2 в принципе очень много, и те что не входят в формат "хороших".. ну они... м.. мягко говоря слабые.. ХОТЯ НЕТ! ОНИ УЖАСНЫЕ!! Я не знаю в чем прикол ставить много врагов на арену и называть это боссом. Вау! Посмотрите! У меня есть полоска хп! Да я ж босс из DARK SOULS!!! (хотя по факту это просто противники из high-level локи). И да, многие скажут, что такое было и в DS 1, однако я возражу, что это было от силы раза два, в то время как в DS 2 это распространенная практика. При чем ладно бы это был честный бой 1 на 1. "Нет, мальчик, тут не будет этого"
На арену ставят минимум трёх ♥♥♥♥♥ кентиков, которые просто тебя во все дыры ♥♥♥♥. Отличный пример это - Ruin Sentinels. Представьте локацию из двух этажей. Первый этаж - это ровный пол и все, если туда падайте, то все гг, тогда сразу три стража агрятся и идут бить вам личико. Второй этаж - хотя правильнее сказать его остатки, это парочку висящих пластин бетонных на которых эти стражи сидят.
Что с боссом делать? Ну смотри. Ты появляешься на втором этаже на одной из пластин сразу с одним из трёх хранителей. Если ты случайно падаешь/тебя скидывают вниз - поздравляю гг, то есть нужно оставаться сверху Логично, да? Только проблема в том, что сами пластины очень маленькие и вам с боссом на ней в принципе тесно, так тебе ещё и кататься нужно, потому что он бьёт сериями размашистых атак. То есть вы поняли? Вас пытаются скинуть, ты сам можешь упасть пока катаешься, падаешь вниз - это сразу рестарт, при этом падаешь вниз ты скорее всего со 100% шансом. Отличный босс. Не забываем что у тебе ещё после каждой смерти хп отнимается, а ещё чтобы до босса от костра добраться тебе нужно убить 18 нпс(я считал), потому что у нас же тут СЛОООООЖНО. Браво. А потом ты узнаешь, что это был необязательный босс и его можно было обойти.

ВИЗУАЛ И МУЗЫКА. Только ленивый не поругал DS 2 за графон, поэтому не буду лить воду и скажу - Да, графон ужасный. Да, он хуже чем в первой части. Тащит лишь около Берсерковский визуал в некоторых локах и все ещё великолепная музыка(OST Манжулы это нечто)

ИТОГ: Я очень люблю soulslike'и и игры дедушки Миядзаки, но даже с учётом с этого, с учётом того что я наиграл в игры FROMSOFTWARE больше 300 часов, я все равно ненавижу DS 2. Этой игры не должно было быть. Я жалею, что тратил на нее время. Мне жалко разрабов, которые ее делали. Мне жалко игроков, которые в нее играли бесплатно(привет Папич). Мне жалко вас, которые купили/хотят купить и предварительно читают отзывы. Не покупайте! Первая и третья части в разы лучше(особенно третья)! Да, они дороже, но они того стоят, а это... это DARK SOULS II

A hard game, but generally fun. Compared to Dark Souls 1, DS2 has improved in many aspects regarding game design, making it a less frustrating and draining experience. On the other hand, DS2 is actually worse in other aspects of the game, and there are many design choices which feel awful, especially compared to Elden Ring. Still, to me, DS2 generally feels less infuriating and exhausting than DS1.

Pros:
1. The character and combat feel more fluid than in DS1, although it still can feel clunky at times.
2. The camera lock on has improved, allowing for rolling in any direction while locked on enemies.
3. The bosses are relatively fun and challenging, although easy compared to bosses in Elden Ring.
4. There is free teleportation between all of your bonfires from the start!
5. Unlike in DS1, you can see what you can use boss souls for, as long as you have found the right NPCs.
6. The areas are less frustrating and confusing than in DS1, as they are relatively easy to navigate, although some areas still feel very draining, being intentionally designed to be frustrating.
7. They are more generous with bonfires than in DS1, saving you a lot of time when running back after you die in different areas.
8. Most NPCs permanently gather in one area after finding them, making them easier to interact with and find.
9. After killing an enemy a certain amount of times, it dissapears, making it easier to traverse the area.
10. Good story, lore, and graphic design.
11. Pyromancy is useful and even good, unlike in DS1.

Cons:
1. Every time you die, you lose some of your max HP, eventuallly capping you at 50% HP, unless you use a certain ring which increases the cap to 75% HP. To reverse this effect, you have to use a consumable item. There are only a limited amount of this item, so you can't use them every time you die. Because of this, you often run around with reduced HP, increasing the difficulty of the game.
2. You can't level up at bonfires. Instead, you have travel to a specific NPC every time you want to level up, which is time-consuming.
3. There are still a lot of frustrating areas, like areas with way too many enemies, or way too dark areas. Some areas and boss arenas are very hard and frustrating, unless you perform some speficic hidden task, forcing you to use the wiki to help you.
4. Boss run-ups are long, although generally shorter than in DS1. Luckily many bosses are relatively easy.
5. Some NPCs are very hard to find, almost forcing you to use the wiki unless you want to be locked out of some of the content of the game.
6. NPC questlines can be very hard to do without a guide, like in all FromSoft games.
7. The story and lore is hard to figure out by yourself.
8. Weapons have low durability, sometimes forcing you to switch to another mid boss fight.
9. There are too many stats you have to level up. Unlike in DS1, you have to level up stamina and equip load separately in DS2. In addition, you have to level up adaptability to gain more invincibility frames during your dodge, and to increase the speed of the incredibly slow healing animation. With all the stats you have to level up, you miss out on several weapons and spells, since you never have the neccessary stats.

Btw, I may have missed some pros and cons.

All in all, although hard and occasionally very frustrating, Dark Souls 2 is a fun experience. There are a lot of intentionally exhausting game design choices, but it manages to keep it at a reasonable level. The game offers a lot of fun content and is mostly an upgrade from DS1 in my opinion.

Dark Souls 2 is often considered the worst in the series, but to me, DS2 is a better experience than Dark Souls 1.

Genuinely some of the worst game design I've ever seen in my entire life. I have no idea how this game even made it beyond the planning phase.

Every area is the same 2-3 enemies copy pasted into every single room. That wouldn't be too much of an issue, if the game had any sense of flow at all. The player movements are so sluggish and weighty, every fight is just "Whoops, I missed a swing. Guess I'll get stun locked to death by these 3 guys."

Don't even get me started on ADP. The most redundant, asinine stat to ever exist. ADP governs rolling and drinking from your flask. At the beginning of the game, your dodge roll has NO I-FRAMES and your flask has a solid 8 seconds of standing still before you can perform another action. I've put THIRTY SIX LEVEL UPS INTO ADP, and still it takes around 5 seconds of being totally still before I can move after drinking. This amazing feat of game design leads to most of your levels being put into this garbage but necessary stat, which literally adds nothing to the game but frustration.

Another great thing: Stamina consumption. It's high. Very high. Two sword swings and a roll at stamina level 28 and you are out. Yet another tack against the already slow feeling.

This feels like it should have been the first Dark Souls, unpolished and rocky on it's feet. PS1 game level of game design. Like playing resident evil 8 with Resident Evil 2 controls. The one Souls game I will recommend AGAINST playing.

Edit: Yes, I beat it. Yes, the last boss HURTS YOU if you stand too close to it. Have fun melee fans!

This review isn’t so much about the overall quality of the game itself, but I’ll still write about it and I’ll begin with that… Unlike most people in this community, I don’t need the elitism nor the mental gymnastics that some people need just to say that this game is good , Dark Souls 2 is a good game , end of story, is not a “underrated/misunderstood masterpiece” is not “a bad souls, but good game” neither, is just a good game, something that I think is worth your time, worth looking into and most importantly something that is worth your money, now which version? For the moment I’ll recommend SOTFS for your average player, and if you have a low-end PC the vanilla one (I think you already knew which one to choose) DO NOT BUY BOTH VERSIONS I’ll explain why throughout this review. But first an advice, ignore the overall screeching and nonsense surrounding this game from this community they are people desperate for attention or just attempting to rage bait you, believe me you’ll enjoy more this game if you ignore the people that worships, condemns and/or overthinks something that’s nothing more than a solid ARPG…

ABOUT SCHOLAR OF THE FIRST SIN...
My main goal with this review is to promote the idea of having the vanilla version unified with the SOTFS version, would you disagree with me if I say that this game could be better if it was sold as a complete package? And not this “Definitive edition.” Is it so hard for the publisher to unify these two versions and let people decide which version they want to play when opening the game? Low-end PC users can play the vanilla version, and users with more beefy PCs could choose either version. My main issue is that Bandai is selling both vanilla and SOTFS at full price, that is just greediness, and no, I don’t care if they let you buy this “Upgrade” for less, what kind of ♥♥♥♥♥♥ discount is that, you shouldn’t use that discount either way, that discount is the equivalent to spit on you in the face and then telling you “here’s a tissue”, if the publisher is really desperate to make a profit out of DS2 then why don’t they sell the SOTFS version as a DLC or bundle together with all DLCs.
Yes, to me SOTFS is just an update for the original game, and for context and so you can understand where I’m coming from.
I spend up to 480/500hrs in my vanilla copy of the game in old gen consoles on its last patch. This game is marketed as “The definitive edition of DARK SOULS™ II.” And the “Director’s Cut.” After playing SOTFS my experience didn’t change radically, I appreciate the QoL changes that this version made, new shaders, better lighting, DX11 instead of DX9 etc. But I can’t shake the feeling that this is just an TU for a 360 game, where those changes are nice, the new enemy placements are a mixed bag, I’m not talking about “spam” I didn’t have an issue with the higher enemy count honestly that higher count feels more like the developer trying to flex the power of the new consoles, where I took issue is in how they use these new enemy placements, it feels more like a cheap way of trying to surprise returning players, by putting an enemy that wasn’t there, and in consequence destroying some set pieces just for that cheap feeling, to reiterate, these changes are nice but they aren’t worth a full price game nor the stupid upgrade price.

You probably already know if Dark Souls games are your jam. If they are, this is the most bizarre of the FromSoft canon. While still providing the basics of a doomed world full of twisting paths towards corrupted bosses, and keeping a lot of what made the first Dark Souls such a smash. The game is also a garden of ideas that never made it out of the sophomore slump along with the early versions of concepts that wouldn't solidify fully until Elden Ring. It's even still sprinkled with that Prepare to Die snark that the genre has mostly grown out of. That inventiveness can pay off very well, or ultimately be a miss at times, but it keeps the experience fresh and interesting even for fans who've played a lot of the other titles.

The Scholar of the First Sin edition incorporates the DLC more naturally while also balancing the enemies in a much more difficult way. If you have played the base game, it can be fun and disorienting when the game surprises you, but it does change the game in one big way. Dark Souls 2 is the only entry where and with the increased enemy density and difficulty, it changes the usual Soulslike formula to something more attrition based than skill building. This is not bad, just different and will probably not be to everyone's taste, even if they like the other entries in the series. Still worth trying and appreciating for its own merits in my opinion.

Dark Souls II is a great time for any fan, but would probably serve as a poor first impression for FromSoft's catalog.

Dark Souls 2 is often considered the Black Sheep of the souls family, and while it probably is the weakest game in the series, it nevertheless remains a fantastic, harrowing trek through some of the best environments From Software has ever put on screen.

There are still two versions of the game available, the original or “vanilla” game, and the enhanced “Scholar of the First Sin” edition. Die-hard veterans will tell you the newer version is worse, as the rebalanced areas and rejuggling of item positions has made it too different from the original experience.

I have played both, many times, and that is absolutely not the case. I would in fact argue that the SOTFS is slightly more difficult, as a lot of the stronger items have been moved to later game areas making it harder to become overpowered early on. It also gates off certain locations with petrified hollows (think statues that you need to use an item on to move) which funnels you down a slightly different path.

But SOTFS is not just about enemy placements. SOTFS on PC has all the bells and whistles of the console versions, with even more graphical flourishes, and ran at a smooth 60fps on my 4K monitor.

It also adds more invasions, more NPC summons, more dialogue, a new final boss, new memories, and, best of all, the three pieces of DLC are now directly baked into the game in a much more natural way, with the keys needed to unlock them coming through natural progression rather than just being dumped into your inventory.

You will find mixed opinions on the DLC. I personally believe them to be one of the best parts of Dark Souls 2. They are hard. Properly hard. I was tearing through Castle Drangelic with my estus flask, weapons, and armour fully upgraded, and died within ten minutes of entering the first DLC area. I find that’s a good thing, however. They force you to play more cautiously. More methodical. You suddenly become afraid again : it’s like you are starting a whole new game. They all have some really interesting mechanics to boot. The Sunken King has structures that can be raised or lowered by hitting certain switches. The Iron King has lifts that are powered by a furnace that must be activated. Finally, the Ivory King (possibly my favourite of them all), has areas blocked off by snow that must be melted to proceed, and a quest to free some imprisoned knights that completely changes the final boss battle.

So why is it not spoken about with the same lofty acclaim as the other two games in the trilogy? I think perhaps it comes down more to the lore and environments than the gameplay or wealth of content. Being able to warp between bonfires from the get-go is great, but it means you lose a lot of the interconnected tissue that bound the first game together. It feels more like jet-setting around the world rather than walking through it. I also don’t find the story as interesting – in fact there is barely any story at all. A rehash of ideas from the previous game with an uninteresting antagonist – something the newly-added final boss does little to change.

It's still a fantastic game though, managing to create that feeling of dread and consequent accomplishment that few other sous-like have managed to mimic. If you’ve already dipped your toe into the souls universe, dive into this one head-first. If you’re coming here for the first time, Demon Souls or Dark Souls Remastered would be a better place to start.

Mixed feelings on the Scholar version of DS2. I played the original version to death on Xbox 360 and managed to finish it solo. This game, however, mixes up the enemy placement and numbers to a ridiculous level that made it quite frustrating to me. I said "to me". I get the appeal of the Souls series and the tough as nails, "get gud, son" mentality of many of the series fans. You have to study the game and the enemies just to best even common foes, not to mention the bosses. Learning to roll, parry, and block enemy attacks is absolutely a necessity in this game. I just found the increased enemies was overdone and done so simply to make the game harder to appease the hardcore Souls players out there. To each their own.

I will say I do not like a few game decisions in DS2 though. Dying and repeat deaths cause the health bar max to decrease, making an already tough game even more punishing and frustrating. Yeah, you can "un-hollow" yourself and gain the life bar back, but those items that do so are short in supply. Also, being able to out level (out-soul, actually) other players, locks you out of playing co-op with them, which is frustrating when you really need help with an area in the Scholar version. (I played solo in the original so I don't remember if that was a mechanic from the original). Just a few of the game design choices I thought were in poor taste.

Those aside, the game is pretty good and can be rewarding if the work is put in properly. I would say you could probably get away with playing DS Remastered and then jumping right into DS3 and feel at home, skipping DS2 entirely. I'd say (for Steam players) doing DSR, DS3, Eldin Ring, and then going back to DS2 may be the best way to play the series. Again, just my subjective opinion. Enjoy!

Posted: February 1
I cannot express how much I do not enjoy this game. 100% DS1, 100% DS3, 100% Elden Ring, yet a total Dark Souls 2 hater. As much as I like a secret challenge to run the game without bonfires like I did in Elden Ring, the mechanical inconsistencies of this game made me quit multiple times in a *standard* run. They are various and constant, they'll happen in the middle of an extended boss fight, they'll ruin run after run, & they make this iteration of Dark Souls miserable for all the wrong reasons. No other game fails to register my inputs as severely as this game does. When it does, your character's either facing the wrong direction or missing a target directly in front of him. [This was present in Dark Souls 1, but not nearly as severely] Boss fights felt the least iconic in this entire game series, and more like "Kinda strong Boss plus mob".

The achievement structure also sucks for this game. Certain miracles and pyromancies aren't available until NG++, and have a 3,000,000 soul requirement in that NG++ to open the door to obtain them. This wildly understates the difficulty and time investment of "obtain all pyromancies".

I honestly hesitated in buying it and even more in playing it while looking at all the hate this game gets and while a lot of the criticism that it gets is at least to a big extent true i am glad that i picked it up and played it.
The game itself has a huge failure in its core design and that being the Despawn mechanic, (enemies despawn after being killed at least 12 or 13 times). The problem this creates is that it is visible how the game design revolves around this. Areas are ganky, paths to bosses are filled with a huge amount of enemies that are stupidly put there, You end up having to be either the best dodger or having to go through the entire thing over and over in order to reach a boss while having some estus flasks. This is simply bad and is even more reinforced by the lack of i-frames on fog walls.
Ganks fill the game and it feels like a chore than gameplay at sometimes when going through areas. The game heavily relies on exaggerated numbers, huge detection and follow radius of enemies and last but not least ambushes. everything is so overdone that it becomes boring and tedious rather than a gameplay of fun.
The areas are disconnected logically and have no cohesion. whilst many are beautiful in their own right they do not maintain a feeling of one cohesive world but rather levels. this takes a lot of the immersion and fun out.
The Bosses are generally weak in their design and not interesting. I do not even remember the 4 bosses that dropped the main souls.... some of them i did not even know where big characters holding these souls....
Ganks are applied to bosses two. It seems that this mentality follows throughout the game and its DLCs.

The story is really good, the controls are meh, a lot of cool ideas exist but nothing is complete. It is a mishmash of everything and feels more like an experiment rather than a complete game. I never had the feeling of wanting to know whats next as i did in DS1 as i knew its a bunch of ganks and ambushes (boring). I did not fear or think a lot about most of the bosses, some i even fought without any effort and first try kill them.
Majula is beautiful. A lot of areas have smart traps and mechanisms to help go through (levers, pads like in IronKeep, barrels..etc) but this is an exception rather than norm.

The end boss is just a joke, Nashandra is one of the worst bosses i fought. pure garbage. Aldia is just hmmm not interesting as a boss at the end... Finishing the game was disappointing rather than awesome and intriguing as it was in DS1.

NPCs are a mix, some are interesting and have a good arc whilst most are just meh.

All in all it is not a bad game per say but it is also not the best of games. I am glad i played it (couldn't believe to finish it) as i will never come back to it again. in comparison i find myself longing to DS1 but disliking DS2.

Apparently DS3 has a lot of cool stuff regarding DS2 but yeah, I recommend going through it before continuing further.

Posted: January 30
Amazing game, not sure why people insist it’s so bad.

Highlights
—

Visual design: be sure to stop and look at the skybox, so many majestic and creative designs.

Enemy and encounter design: pay attention to the enemies in each area, their attacks often telegraph the attack of the area boss. The limited enemy respawns lend some inevitability to the bosses. IMPORTANT: fight the regular enemies, don’t run past them.

Stats and rpg elements: it is a lot easier to reach the soft cap in multiple stats, which is wonderfully forgiving and invites more experimentation. Ability to respec using single use items is so great, I never did it but knowing I could removed a lot of the stress picking stats.

Story: From games always have amazing deep stories. I appreciate that this game is so consistent with the theme of memory and identity loss. A consistent narrative theme helps give the story weight even if you don’t bother to read all the item descriptions.

Progression: this game is more non-linear than people want to admit. You can skip entire areas and like half the bosses and come back to them when you want. Ability to warp from the start is an excellent choice.

100% would recommend, just don’t go in comparing to DS3 or Elden Ring, it just plays very different.
