Posted: January 29
Dark Souls 2 is sometimes called the black sheep of the trilogy. Often considered the weakest of the three, but it still has a dedicated fanbase, and I personally had a great time playing it too. This review is mainly for those already invested in the series and unsure whether to try DS2. If you are new to Dark Souls, I’d recommend starting with DS1 or DS3 instead.

Exploring this game was a lot of fun. The world is quite large, with a wide variety of areas to discover, more so than in DS 1 or 3. However, exploration would have been much better if the world had more interconnectivity and better environmental cohesion. Instead of feeling like an interconnected world, it plays more like 5-6 long, linear paths that branch off slightly but never connect with each other in a meaningful way. With so many areas, it is no surprise that there are a ton of bosses. While very few fights are outright bad, most are pretty forgettable and relatively easy. The best fights mainly come from the DLC areas, but overall, the bosses aren’t the highlight of this game. To be fair, I felt similarly about Dark Souls 1’s bosses, just that they are less bosses in that game.

Some mechanics I really disliked include: having to level Adaptability for reasonable iframes (without it ever being explained), the high stamina cost of rolling, the large aggro and leash distance of enemies, and the temporary HP reduction when dying, which can lower max HP down to 50% (or 75% with a ring). And, of course, extra minus points for creating the worst area in all of gaming: the Frigid Outskirts. But none of them are enough to seriously destory my enjoyability once I got used to them.

Despite all the negatives, I had a ton of fun exploring the world and progressing my character into an absolute powerhouse. In the end, I’d say I had about as much fun as I did playing Dark Souls 1, which is a lot. However, Dark Souls 3 remains my favorite by a significant margin.

Posted: January 29
Dark Souls 2 is an interesting game. On one hand, it introduces many bizarre gameplay changes, but it also does a lot right.
Let me explain.
Dark Souls 2 is often regarded as FromSoftware's worst game. And while it's not the best game in the Souls series, it's not the terrible game as many claim it to be. I personally think it's one of their easiest, and most creative games.
Let's talk cons-
Some "cons" are subjective, but others are outright cons.
Firstly, spell uses are relegated to a set number instead of a magic bar. This is a subjective con, as I personally don't mind this mechanic. It leaves me with more flasks instead of dedicating a handful to blue flasks like in Ds3 and Elden Ring.
Second, the hitboxes are sketchy. This isn't new to the Souls Series. I always found much of Ds1-3's difficultly came from its janky hitboxes, but this game takes it to another notch. Enemies will stagger out of a swing but you'll still take full damage, grabs will yank you in from a mile away, and many hitboxes linger long enough for the player to still take damage even with a perfect roll.
Speaking of the roll. . .
Third, leveling the roll is not fun. Sure, once you pump a few levels into it, the issue becomes non-existent. And I'd argue that you'll have enough souls to level your roll comfortably while also completing your desired build. Without grinding, I was able to beat the game at level 183. The roll wasn't an issue for me--most people complain about it like it's a game ending design choice. Unless you acctively choose not to level it, it's not.
Fourth, the bosses (and game in general) is SUPER easy. This is another subjective con, but I'm aware most people play Souls FOR the challenge. You deal so much damage in this game, you'll be chunking 90% of bosses even while "under leveled."
I'm well aware there are more cons, but these are the ones I think matter most. Your attacks dealing less damaging per enemy in a single swing is meh, and the weapons being super weak on durability is also eh. I personally only broke my weapons a handful of times, but it's not a big issue. The flask slowly healing you instead of instantly healing you is also pretty annoying but can be played around.
Now for the pros--
The world of Ds2 is much more fantastical than Ds1 and Ds3. It's dark and depressing, but not in the same way as the other entries in the Souls series. It has the same storybook feel as Elden Ring and Demons Souls to me. This detail is also a minor reason why people dislike Ds2, as it doesn't have the same atmosphere as the other Souls games.
The bosses, as easy as they are, are fun. I actively look forward to fighting Sinh, The Rotten, and The Pursuer. The Flexile Sentry's gimmick of the sinking ship is so damn cool. Sure, bosses like Mytha are annoying unless you find their bizarre counter, and Royal Rats are just flat out bad, but bosses like that aren't all that common.
Next, the combat. While many feel the combat is boring, I found the combat fun. The addition of power stancing is great, the quick yet somewhat chunky feeling of the weapons make your attacks feel good (even though the impact sound is terrible and flaccid), and the magic variety is wonderful.
Lastly, and easily my favorite part of Ds2, is the charm. Small things like the "friendly" invader that back stabs you and the one that hides as a barrel add to that storybook feel I love so much. Majula, Dragon Shrine, and the entire memory gimmick add to that feeling immensely.

Dark Souls 2 is a great game. Not perfect by ANY means. But it opened up a lot and brought ideas into the franchise that would becoming recurring themes in later games. Are the other Souls games better technicality-wise? Oh 100%. But is Ds2 that horrible, rotten, unplayable lobotomite game so many souls fans want you to believe it is? No.
It may not be the best souls game, but it's a damn good fantasy game.

The only other Souls games to grab me this hard were Elden Ring, Bloodborne, and Lies of P.
That says alot about this old ass game that's supposedly the worst thing ever created.

You've got genuinely invisible enemies, unmanageable enemy hordes, enemies that ruin all your equipment, getting invaded by six red phantoms in the same instant, DLC keys hidden behind random doors that take entirely different (and equally hidden) keys, overstatted foes that appear to kill you once and leave forever, dragons that take ten minutes to kill, poison that gives you ten seconds to live, spiteful gotchas, level layouts that leave you feeling like Stevie Wonder trying to find a place to sit in a medieval dungeon, enemy placement designed to overwhelm, nearly enough stamina to dodge and attack upwards of thrice at a go—look, let's cut it short, it's pretty much the Dark Souls of Dark Souls. Any deranged, defamatory BS a guy who barely plays any games at all could make up about any other game in the franchise could be found for real in this one, just front and center like it's the most normal thing in the world.

For some reason, I don't particularly care - it has an exploratory, cozy, "homemade" feel that I can't help but turn back to when times get a bit tough. I have less hours on it that any other FROM titles short of Code Vein and AC6, but they've been fun, memorable hours in what subjectively feels like the delineating, exploratory voyage that made their later successes feasible in the first place. It's not my favorite, per se, but it might be the one I love the most, if you get what I'm saying. Hell, I won't even hold the hair choices in the character creator against them.

Oh, and it is good outside of that. Of every FROM game I've played, this one's felt the most like a genuine adventure through a genuinely strange land. Within a few hours of play, there are a good five directions you could feasibly be heading in, and in my own subjective experience it's wholly possible to take them in pretty much any order you'd like. You can even skip the lion's share of them altogether if you like - provided you take the path leading directly to the King, everything excepting and preceding that is essentially optional. You can buff pretty much any weapon pretty much any way, you can lower the stat reqs on spells until they slot seamlessly into an entirely unrelated build, and you can still get into matchmaking with stats as high as your own sense of decency allows. As far as personal agency goes, it's arguably the most permissive souls game - your character, your way, provided it's equipped to tackle the game's schizophrenic hodgepodge of pissed-off zombies. The levels themselves keep you on your toes pretty well, too, with an unprecedented quantity of traps, ambushes, invasions, and NG+ mix-ups throughout. I have to say, FROM really threw some babies out with the bathwater in their response to this one's reception.

Speaking of the character creator, launch the game from Big Picture mode if your character name's too long - you can pull almost twice as many letters that way before the game gets weird about it. IF you want to bake titles into your name like all the cool NPCs do, it's either that or searching for four-letter Old English names on Behind the Name, sorry to say - think 14 characters is the legitimate cap.

Dark Souls 2 is kind of hard to judge because it's unfortunately a sequel to one of the greatest games ever made. If not for the inevitable comparison to Dark Souls 1, I'd say that DS2 is a bold and challenging take on the ARPG genre that rewards exploration and doesn't pull its punches while offering the player a plethora of fun and viable character builds to play with, then leave it at that. And for the record, I really do mean those things when I say them.

Unfortunately though, I have to say that Dark Souls 2 is kind of a piss poor sequel in every single way. Worse level design, world design, boss design, story, and art direction, on top of having less polish and being dumbed down and streamlined in all the worst ways. Outside of a few small QoL improvements like better UI, pretty much everything about it is worse. Worst of all, the overuse of bonfire placements as well as the underuse of shortcuts, combined with the ability to warp from the start of the game, completely destroys the risk-reward scenarios from DS1 that were so effective in immersing you into its world. If that wasn't already bad enough, lifegems bury the tension even further.

Dark Souls 1 is a masterpiece in interactive fiction where every element of the game is built to pull you in and immerse you in a haunting dark fantasy universe. Meanwhile, Dark Souls 2 is simply a very good game. One that you should play if you like action RPGs, for what it's worth.

I do understand why people have split opinions about this game, there is an extra level of difficulty outside of combat. You lose health on death, durability is a different more punishing system, you only start with one estus flask use. These combined add to an experience that is really rough starting out. It can take hours to get to the first boss of the game and the main thing this game is trying to teach you is patience.

What I want to note for people who have never played this game before is, it is also the most forgiving dark souls. At a decently early point in the game you can send the merchant from the forest of giants to Majula, and while she's there she sells infinite lifegems. this means if you are okay not using all your souls for levelling you can heal after every fight to full. There's a system in place where if you kill an enemy 12 times (and are not in the covenant of champions which is basically hard mode) they will be despawned.

There may be systems in place that punish you more than other darksouls games, and it may make this game a lot more frustrating to start out with, but the world building is beautiful, the story is amazing, and as long as you have patience I promise you can get through it. I think this game deserves better than the reputation it has, when you take it as it is and not as dark souls 1 but on a different map, theres a lot to be enjoyed.

Some advice for anyone starting out.
- Your I-frames when rolling is determined by your agility stat. you want to reach 99 agility but it's not worth while to go higher than that, because the cap is 105 and you only get 1 extra frame going above 99. if you're a caster you can level agility via a mix of attunement and adaptability, if you're melee just do adp.
- There is a ring guarded by someone sitting in-front of a metal chest in hiede's tower of flame, you can get to this area pretty quickly and this ring will be the most important ring you find on your journey, this halves the amount of max HP you lose from dying. It's called the Ring of Binding.
- Do not join the covenant in majula at the giant beat up tombstone if you are struggling, this is the hard mode covenant called the covenant of champions. what this does is it makes every enemy deal 50% more damage, you deal 20% less, you cannot summon any allies but can get invaded still, and enemies no longer despawn after 12 kills.

While the systems introduced in this game are controversial it also adds a lot of systems that are really unique and fun to use, for instance powerstancing. ds2 doesn't tell you upfront about this but if you meet 150% of the requirements of a weapon, you can duel wield weapons of that type by holding Y. Going through this game powerstanced using manikin claws while not optimal was some of the most fun I've personally had while playing darksouls. Plus there are many more systems in place unique to darksouls 2 that makes it extremely interesting, like bonfire ascetics that make a specific area associated with a bonfire ng+

This game also has a really alive pvp scene if you are interested in that!

Overall i'd say give it a shot. the start of the game will be hard but once you get through that and learn how this game works, It's really enjoyable. at least to me.

Posted: January 25
This game gets a bad rep, and not without reason. There are some jarring design decisions when it comes to leveling and combat (e.g. camera lock on not aligning with the direction of your attacks is unforgivable).

It's very fortunate that with a little know-how, much of the initial jank of Dark Souls II can be overcome. Finding the right weapon moveset, leveling into ADP to have an acceptable amount of iFrames on your dodge roll, and understanding how carry weight impacts your roll will really open up the gameplay. There is some seriously good Souls content to be experienced here.

But it's not without its shortcomings. Despite some cool/satisfying level designs, most bosses are just brain dead easy. It probably took me 30 hours before I found bosses that really forced me to give them more than two attempts.

I did miss the stacked level design present in the first game. Constantly doubling back at the end of sections can leave you lost at times, and it didn't always feel as rewarding to open up the world as it did in the first. Thankfully, the game gets better and better the further you go along, a very welcome change from Dark Souls I's atrocious second half.

Mid to end game bosses start to get more challenging and dynamic. The areas you explore become much more compelling. The three DLC are also fantastic.

Overall, this game has more good than bad to offer. I thoroughly enjoyed it,

8/10

Finally I understand the Souls series. After playing through Bloodborne -> Dark Souls 1 -> Elden Ring -> Demon Souls Remake -> Dark Souls 3 -> Dark Souls 2.

It was a long journey, but very much worth it. I think combined I spent at least 800 hours in these games.

DS2 I played with the Lighting engine mode, the 4k texture mode, and the controller DeadZone fix, and let me tell you - It looks beautiful, imho better than DS3 on Playstation 5.

Pros:
1. The game is much more streamlined than DS1.
2. Has one of the best mechanics in the series (ability to upgrade bonfires to NG+ to reset bosses etc) (wish we had this in Elden Ring.)
3. Has good weapon balance, varied locations, good magic system
4. Good dialogues (the NPCs are very talkative for a souls game!). gives you a good chunk of Souls lore in a relatively approachable manner(which is rare!).
5. NG+ is different than NG! Many more enemies, placed strategically in different places, new loot, more challenging boss fights (with unique changes such as additional enemies in the Lost Sinner fight) -> Elden Ring could benefit significantly from such an approach.

These are significant pros, the game has quite the unique set of benefits compared to other games in the series and I didn't feel a complete immersion into the series until I played it to completion.

Cons:
1. The bosses are overall quite easy.
2. The "new balance" introduced in the DLCs, made any "caster" gameplay extremely unbalanced.
Case in point - in the base game any viable build feels extremely strong. But not in the DLCs. There, as a melee character you feel fine, the experience is similar to the base game, but there are more enemies, they are more aggressive, they act smarter, which is great. But if you are a caster.. it's an entirely different story. The enemies receive a plain defense against magic of any sort, and the defense is huge, they get around 3 times less damage than they should.
3. The DLCs are quite short.

These are, pretty much, the only cons of DS2 that mattered to me.

Highly recommended!

Posted: January 24
DS2 is not quite a direct sequel from the first game, taking place in the same world but being an undetermined amount of time in the future after countless civilizations have come and gone. It changed a lot of things from the first game, some good, and some bad. In no particular order:
- The story is a lot deeper than in the first game, and more of it is provided directly to the player, while still leaving some room for personal research and theorycrafting.
- While DS1's world design had lots of verticality and zones that were interlocked with each other like a maze, DS2 opted for having multiple branching paths from the hub world that each ended in a major world boss. After you finish exploring a branch, you return to the hub and find another path to go on.
- Enemy placements feel different from the first game. DS2 has many more situations where you get suddenly swarmed by many enemies out of nowhere, with no choice but to retreat and try to pick the enemies off one by one. Aggro range is a bit inconsistent as well so it's hard to try and fight the enemies one at a time from the start.
- Despite technically being about the same length as DS1, DS2 ends up feeling much much longer by comparison, almost dragging on a bit by the end. It's really hard to describe why but I'm definitely not the only ones that thinks this way.
- Bonfire Ascetics are a new item that allows you to advance a certain area to a New Game+ cycle. This is actually great for farming boss souls; in DS1 you had start the whole game over again in a NG+ cycle if you wanted to have a rematch with a boss, or if you wanted more copies of a certain pickup in an area. In DS2, you just burn an ascetic and that specific area is reset for you!
- Weapons have durability now and can break if you're not careful. Really this is only an issue very early in the game, as you very quickly get an item that automatically repairs your gear when you rest at a bonfire. After that you only have to worry about acid pools or attacks from enemies that drain your durability.
- More DLC areas! I personally think the quality of the DLCs in this game aren't quite to par with the one in DS1, but I still had a good time. As a plus, the end reward for each area can actually combine with an item you get in the basegame for a very powerful effect.

I personally don't get the sentiment of DS2 being "bad." I had just as good a time with it as I did the first game. There are some changes I didn't like, but they're just minor annoyances that didn't affect my enjoyment of the game that much.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is an absolute masterpiece of gaming perfection that transcends the medium itself. It’s not just a game; it’s a philosophical journey, a test of your mettle, and the ultimate love letter to masochists who revel in the beauty of despair.

From the very first step into Drangleic, you’ll feel the overwhelming sense of awe and dread that only a Souls game can deliver. The world is meticulously crafted, with every crumbling castle and poisonous swamp telling a story of ruin and forgotten glory. The interconnectedness of the levels, the foreboding atmosphere, and the sheer scale of the world make exploration an intoxicating thrill.

The combat? Oh, it’s divine. Every swing of your weapon, every perfectly-timed dodge, and every spell cast feels like a deliberate and meaningful act. The variety in builds is staggering—want to be a hulking tank with a giant club? Go for it. A nimble dual-wielding assassin? Do it. A pyromancer setting the world ablaze? The choice is yours.

The Scholar of the First Sin edition elevates the experience to god-tier status. The improved enemy placements, the enhanced visuals, and the sheer brilliance of its lore expansion make it the definitive way to experience this game. The DLCs—Crown of the Sunken King, Crown of the Old Iron King, and Crown of the Ivory King—are some of the best content in the entire series, delivering brutal challenges, epic boss fights, and lore so deep it might as well be a dark abyss.

Sure, some people complain about things like adaptability or enemy gank squads, but that’s just part of the experience! Dark Souls II isn’t here to hold your hand—it’s here to test your patience, skill, and willpower. The triumph of overcoming its challenges is unmatched in any other game.

If you love Soulsborne games, RPGs, or simply want to ascend to the pinnacle of gaming greatness, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is mandatory. Prepare to die, prepare to rage, and most importantly, prepare to fall in love.

Rating: 11/10 – A flawless gem of pain and beauty.

The Weakest Entry in the Series

DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin brings the franchise’s renowned obscurity & gripping gameplay to a new level. Join the dark journey and experience overwhelming enemy encounters, diabolical hazards, and unrelenting challenge.

Story
You are the Bearer of the Curse, seeking a cure in the ruined kingdom of Drangleic. King Vendrick fell to deception by Nashandra, who desires the Throne of Want. You claim the Great Souls, confront her, and choose to continue the cycle or reject it entirely.

Gameplay
Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is a third-person souls-like action role-playing game, including all DLCs. Combat is a core part of Dark Souls II, deliberate and punishing, requiring precise dodges, parries, and stamina management. It includes melee, ranged attacks, defensive maneuvers, and magic. Melee weapons include swords, spears, maces, greatswords, and other fantastical arms, while ranged options feature bows and crossbows. Defense involves shields, armor, dodging, backstepping, and parrying. Magic—sorceries, miracles, and pyromancies—uses limited casts replenished at bonfires rather than a mana system. All combat types have trade-offs and can be enhanced through leveling, smithing, consumables, rings, and new equipment.

Bonfires are central to the gameplay, serving as checkpoints throughout the world. Resting at a bonfire heals you, refills Estus Flasks, and allows leveling up with acquired souls, attuning magic, and repairing or upgrading equipment. However, resting also respawns all enemies, except bosses, mini-bosses, and defeated NPCs. Upon death, you respawn at the last bonfire rested at. Dying causes you to drop all collected souls at the death location, which are lost permanently if not recovered before dying again.

Another key mechanic is the hollowing system. You can be in human or undead form. Dark Souls II removes the “Humanity counter” and replaces it with a single-use consumable. Every death increases hollowing, which reduces max HP by a percentage, eventually caps at 50% reduction. You can restore humanity by using a Human Effigy item. While human, you can summon allies but are also vulnerable to invasions by other players or NPCs. Hollow form still allows you to be summoned (as a phantom), unlike DS1.

The game offers strong replayability. After finishing, you can start New Game+, where enemies and bosses have increased health and damage. This continues up to NG+6, after which scaling stops, though you can continue playing on NG+. NG+99 is the maximum for each character. Unlike other games in the series, this one actually changes in NG+, adding more and tougher enemies in some areas and even extra foes in a few boss arenas.

Dark Souls II features online multiplayer for both co-op and PvP. In co-op play, players place a summon sign that becomes visible to others using a matching item; interacting with it brings the summoner into their world until the area boss is defeated, the host dies, or the summoned player dies. In PvP, summon signs can be used to challenge others to duels, or players can use special items to invade other worlds.

Dark Souls II offers a wide variety of enemies and bosses, each with distinct attack patterns that require careful observation and strategy to dodge and counter. Every enemy has unique moves, gear, and specific weaknesses and resistances, keeping combat engaging and varied.

Bosses are the true test of the Dark Souls II experience. Bosses are powerful, challenging foes. Each boss is unique and has varying attacks, weaknesses, and strategies to defeat it. For the most part, the bosses are well-designed—it’s the game itself that’s poorly designed.

Set in the kingdom of Drangleic. Exploration is a core mechanic. You are encouraged to proceed cautiously, learn from mistakes, and discover alternative paths. Drangleic is somewhat connected, but the layout is more hub-and-spoke. Areas are often accessed through separate entrances, with fewer natural shortcuts linking regions. It’s expansive but feels more segmented.

The game has a good variety of environments, but compared to the first game, the level design feels annoying, forgettable, and not as well crafted. The layout feels off in many areas, with pointless rooms and paths containing no items or enemies. A few areas are well-designed, but they’re rare.

The visual design captures a world caught between faded glory and inevitable ruin. Ancient castles, flooded cities, and overgrown ruins evoke the memory of great civilizations long lost, while the varied environments—from the tranquil cliffs of Majula to poisonous swamps and icy palaces—create a sense of constant discovery.

Every enemy has a distinctive set of sounds—grunts, roars, weapon swings—that communicate their size, aggression, and attack patterns. Audio cues are essential for combat; a giant’s stomp, a dragon’s growl, or a sorcerer charging a spell signals danger and timing windows.

There’s a lot to discuss about this game. The three issues I had with the first game—story narration, co-op, and PvP—are still present, but they pale compared to the other flaws. Many new changes feel unnecessary and poorly thought out, adding difficulty in cheap and frustrating ways. Here are the worst cons:

The game feels very off and wonky in the way you run, jump, dodge and attack.The hitbox and targeting mechanics are also often problematic. A key stat, ADP (Adaptability), raises AGL (Agility), which affects dodge invincibility, item use speed (including Estus Flasks), switching between left/right-hand equipment, and guard raise speed while blocking. This is beyond stupid and pure madness. At the start of the game, you are slow, and drinking an Estus takes a long time to restore health.

Fog Walls now require a few seconds of channeling to pass through, and enemies can interrupt this process.

Illusion Walls now require pressing A on the Xbox controller to reveal them; attacking them no longer works.

Crystal Lizards no longer give loot automatically—you must collect it yourself. If they fall off the map, the loot is lost. They are also difficult to hit with many weapons.

Upgrade materials are unbalanced, with far more Titanite Slabs than Chunks, making the distribution feel excessive.

Weapons have low durability and break easily.

One major issue is the sheer number of enemies. You often get overwhelmed by multiple foes at once. Most enemies are easy to kill, but their numbers make encounters deadly.

Bosses are easy and boring. If you die, it’s likely due to poor game design rather than the boss’s difficulty. You often die on the way to the boss due to long, enemy-filled paths, which can be more frustrating than dying to the boss itself.

The hollowing system is stupid and annoying.

There’s a strange change to enemy respawns: after killing an enemy 12–15 times (depending on type), it stops respawning. Burning a Bonfire Ascetic at a bonfire resets enemy and boss spawns, but each use increases that area’s NG+ level by one. This is just stupid if you wanna farm specific items.

Dark Souls II is very far from flawless and is for sure the weakest entry in the series. Most deaths are your fault, but not all, since this game is more poorly designed than the first. When I played it for the first time over 10 years ago, I was not as blown away and immersed as the first game. If I had played this game for the first time today I would have been even less impressed. Even though Dark Souls II is 11 years old, I still find it fun when I haven't played it in a while, but mostly for nostalgia. I just played through the game again with a friend, marking my 25th completion (26 if you count his world too), if I count all the playthroughs, both Xbox and PC.

Dark Souls II is definitely a game. Is it a terrible game? I wouldn't say that, but is it a terrible Dark Souls game? Yes, it very much is. For every good idea this game has, there are about 100 bad ideas to go along with it. To start with what I like about the game, I think that the increased amount of weapons and armor sets is certainly a positive. Bonfire aesthetic is a neat mechanic, as well as the immediate access to the warp mechanic. But that's about where my appreciation for this game ends.

While the areas and environments certainly look cool, their layouts are sometimes extremely nonsensical. This isn't really a gameplay criticism, but more of a thematic one, which is another issue I have with the game. The themes, environments, and enemy designs feel all over the place, and I don't think they fit into the Dark Souls theme very well.

When it comes to mechanics in this game I don’t like oh boy, are there plenty. Soul Memory, ADP, Estus animation, fog wall animation, changes to how backstabbing works, and just how slow the animations are in general really make this game a slow, clunky mess—at least in terms of PvE. Enemy placement is another huge problem. You know what would make this game more challenging? Let’s fill a small room with five of the most annoying enemies in the game and make their aggro range seemingly infinite.

Most of the bosses are also just mind-bogglingly boring and reused multiple times throughout the game. "Quantity over quality" must have been the rule the developers followed when making this game. Oh boy, I love fighting the Pursuer for the 100th time within the first 30 minutes of the game.

My closing notes are that the Emerald Herald is a baddie, so Dark Souls II good game.

Enemy placement and quantity was too much for my preference. It made clearing areas take so much longer than you'd want to spend in them. Enemies will chase you seemingly forever. Despite having the most bosses out of the dark souls trilogy, bosses were often disappointing because the path to get to them was magnitudes harder than the fight itself. (Executioners Chariot, The Rotten, both Smelter Demons and Old Iron king, Demon of Song, Velstadt, Sir Alonne, The Kings Pets.)

The game is a deviation, not an evolution of ds1. Just like all souls games, you have to dig in to find what you enjoy about it.

I first tried this game four years ago and couldn't even figure out how to leave Majula. Now, having beaten the base game, I can say that this is a perfect continuation of the first game.

Dark Souls II introduces new game mechanics, some of them even are successfully adopted in the later games. Soul Memory, Aged Feather, instant Hollowing Reversal, Bonfire Ascetic, dual wielding weapons, to name a few.

It also improved some aspects of its predecessor while getting rid of some pain-in-the-ass moments like having to upgrade weapon to ascend it to a boss weapon (now boss weapons can be traded for Boss Souls), infusions are better than having millions of different kinds of titanites, locations design rewards killing bosses rather than soul-farming, PvP works better, etc.

To me, Dark Souls II is about standing out and being bold. It is not the same game. It's different. It's unique.

DS2 was an interesting playthrough. I enjoyed it, then I accidentally played through most of the game with the "Company of Companions" covenant active, which increases damage taken by 50% and reduces player damage output by 20%. I went through the game's toughest areas with those modifiers and I got really frustrated until I found out about it after some research.

Barring my own decision making, the rest of the game was very lackluster. The levels were weak and enemy placement was absurd. My biggest issue with the game was how lame the bosses were. There were two main archetypes: The gank fight and the slow guy with a big stick. The gank fights were just uncoordinated brawls where your character is the center of the mayhem. Each enemy stacked attacks to a point where you had to throw away all discipline and constantly gamble to get hits in. On the other hand, the slow, lone bosses were laughably easy and provided zero to no accomplishment upon seeing "Victory Achieved."

I have not done the DLCs and someday I might, but for now, I'm glad I experienced the game.. at least.

Dark Souls II is a polarizing entry in FromSoftware’s acclaimed Souls series. While it retains the challenging combat and intricate level design the franchise is known for, it lacks the soul, both figuratively and literally, that made its predecessor and successors so beloved. The absence of series creator Hidetaka Miyazaki is keenly felt, as Dark Souls II struggles to capture the magic that defines the series.

The most glaring issue is its tonal and structural departure from the rest of the Souls games. Drangleic, the game’s setting, feels disjointed compared to the tightly interconnected worlds of Dark Souls and Dark Souls III. The level transitions are often nonsensical, breaking immersion and making the world feel like a collection of disconnected zones rather than a cohesive, lived-in land.

Combat, while familiar, suffers from pacing and balance issues. Movement feels sluggish and less precise, making encounters frustrating for reasons that feel artificial rather than genuinely challenging. The enemy placement frequently prioritizes quantity over quality, leading to moments that feel unfair rather than rewarding.

The story, always a subtle and interpretive aspect of the series, also falters here. While Dark Souls II touches on intriguing themes of cycles and forgotten kingdoms, its narrative feels less compelling and lacks the emotional resonance found in other entries. Characters and NPCs, often a highlight of the series, feel underdeveloped or forgettable, with few leaving a lasting impact.

Even visually, Dark Souls II feels like a step backward. Despite its ambitious lighting engine (which was ultimately toned down in the final release), the environments lack the haunting beauty and intricate detail of other Souls games. Areas like Heide’s Tower of Flame and the Iron Keep, while visually striking in isolation, often feel disconnected and lack the atmospheric cohesion the series is known for.

That’s not to say Dark Souls II is without merit. The game’s willingness to experiment with new ideas, such as the increased focus on adaptability stats and new multiplayer mechanics, is commendable, even if the execution is divisive. Some boss fights, like the Fume Knight and Sir Alonne in the DLC, are genuinely memorable and rank among the series’ best.

Ultimately, Dark Souls II feels like a misstep in an otherwise exceptional series. It’s a game that tries to carry the weight of the Souls legacy but stumbles under the pressure, delivering an experience that feels hollow compared to the brilliance of Dark Souls and Dark Souls III.

For newcomers, Dark Souls II might still provide a challenging and rewarding adventure. But for fans of the series, its deviations from the formula and the lack of Miyazaki’s guiding hand make it a frustrating outlier in a franchise that typically achieves greatness.

Okay there's a lot of things to know before getting into this game.
- If you came here looking for the dark souls experience then you're in the wrong place. This game isn't Dark Souls. It's an imitation of it. Does that mean it's bad? No. It just means that it's imperfect. If you want the definitive Dark Souls experience play either Bloodborne, Dark Souls 1 or Dark Souls 3. But if you really wanna play this game here's everything you need to know. Many parts of this game will be purely there to piss you off. It's not even trying to hide it. And many parts are just poorly explained and terribly designed so instead of a review I'll teach you everything you need to know before you play this game.
1. Broken weapons like the broken longsword doesn't deal any damage. So try to find something else
2. The first area you are "supposed" to go through is behind the first bonfire in Majula. Try to find it.
3. You need to talk to the lady beside the tree near the bonfire in Majula in order to get your estus flask (your main healing item).
4. Do you know how in every souls game you can roll? And you get something called an I-frame short for invincibility frame that makes you invincible while you roll? Well in this game that i-frame is tied to a stat called AGL short for agility. And if you don't level up your ADP (short for adaptability) which increases your AGL (among other stats) you will not have fun playing this game so make sure your ADP is at least 20.
5. Keep an open mind. This game isn't fun on a first playthrough and it never will be. So make sure to keep yourself healthy. Have fun (And yes the strongest weapon in the entire game is right at the start of the game I won't spoil it so you're gonna have to find it yourself) (hint: it's thin, light and used for poking)

Dark Souls 2 is the equivalent of signing up for a marathon, only to find out the finish line is a pit of despair, and every step you take makes you want to throw your controller out the window. You’re cursed, and by the end, you’ll wonder if the real curse was the time you spent playing.

Combat:
It’s fine—if you enjoy slowly dying over and over in increasingly creative ways. You’ll master the art of rolling and dodging, only to get one-shotted by a giant turtle wielding a tree. Fun!

Level Design:
It’s like a maze designed by a sadistic god who really wants you to get lost. Backtracking? Oh, you’ll backtrack. A lot. By the end, you’ll forget what you were doing in the first place. Just like life.

Story:
You’re cursed. You’re always cursed. There’s no happy ending. It’s like reading a self-help book written by an emo teenager. At least it’s consistent.

Bosses:
Some are epic, others are frustrating, but all of them are here to remind you that you are insignificant. Nothing like dying to a giant lizard with a bad attitude to really make you question your life choices.

Conclusion:
Dark Souls 2 is a beautiful, brutal reminder that life is pointless and we’re all just one bad roll away from death. Will you finish it? Maybe. Will you regret it? Definitely. But hey, at least it’s fun in a "why am I doing this" kind of way. Play it if you hate yourself—it's an oddly satisfying kind of torture.

To be real, I kinda love the game. It is a pain in the ass though.

I just completed a week-long marathon replaying every souls-game.

Whilst Dark Souls I is my undisputed G.O.A.T for souls-borne games, Dark Souls II is an incredibly close second that would likely topple my love for Dark Souls I if it weren't for the countless memory filled hours I poured into the original Dark Souls in my early teens.

Dark Souls II is the franchises' most wildly misunderstood entry, its often scorned for its seemingly unfair difficulty and departure from the art style and story of Dark Souls I, but for me, all of these things make up what I love most about Dark Souls II. Dark Souls II is unafraid to fill rooms with enemies who seek you out forever, it's unafraid to fill lakes with poisonous sludge you have to trudge through, and most importantly it is unafraid to be entirely different. Without spoiling anything all I can say that this entries commitment to telling an entirely new story away from Dark Souls I is so refreshing and so fun to experience, time and time again.

TL;DR
If you've been avoiding Dark Souls II because forum dwellers or video essayists have told you it's "awful" or "a disgrace to the souls series", I implore you to set those notions aside and give this game a playthrough. It is none of the terrible things that some would have you believe.

I’ve 100% completed Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin (SOTFS), and while the game has its strengths, the flaws often overshadow them. Visually, the game looks great, the gameplay feels solid, and the bosses can be fun—though many are forgettable.

That said, there are notable issues. The hitboxes are inconsistent, which can make combat frustrating. The enemy placements in SOTFS, compared to the base Dark Souls 2, feel intentionally annoying rather than challenging, and the long, punishing boss runbacks only add to the frustration.

New Game Plus, instead of enhancing the experience, adds even more enemies, making it worse. While despawning enemies after killing them 12 times is an interesting mechanic, grinding to reach that point is tedious. The Adaptability stat, which increases dodge iframes, feels like a poorly designed addition that artificially inflates difficulty rather than adding meaningful depth.

On the bright side, the game has three excellent DLCs—far better than the base game—with standout moments like Brume Tower. They feature challenging but fair encounters, tighter level design, and memorable boss fights.

If you’ve played Dark Souls 1, I’d recommend giving Dark Souls 2 a try, but temper your expectations. While SOTFS changes enemy placements, the original Dark Souls 2 feels better and more balanced overall.

Haters? Maybe they just didn’t understand what they were dealing with. Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin isn’t just a “harder version” of the base game — it’s an ambitious reimagining that deliberately challenges the expectations of Souls fans. With remixed enemy placements, new mechanics, and subtle changes in level design, it creates an experience that forces even veterans to think and adapt.

One of the most debated elements is the Adaptability stat, which affects how fast you use items and determines the number of invincibility frames (i-frames) during rolls. For many, this was a shock — in previous games, your roll was reliable from the start. Here, you have to earn it by investing points. Not everyone liked this, but it wasn’t a random choice — the game rewards players who take the time to understand and work with its systems.

Yes, DS2 has a different rhythm than Bloodborne or Sekiro — more "chess-like" than "balletic." But that’s not a flaw; it’s a stylistic decision. The bosses may be less visually striking at times, but the game makes up for it with excellent world architecture, a massive amount of content, and many optional areas that are easy to miss.

What’s important is that DS2 isn’t a game you can fully judge from videos or others’ opinions. It’s something you have to experience yourself — especially in the Scholar of the First Sin version, which improves graphics, balance, and enemy AI. Its atmosphere — melancholic, foggy, and full of decay — is among the most unique in the entire series.

So if someone says it’s the “worst” entry, ask them: did you actually play it? Did you finish it, trying to understand its pace? Because for many who gave it a real chance, DS2 is an underrated gem — different, yes, but still brilliant.

A Flawed but Fascinating Journey...
Dark Souls 2 is a curious entry in the Souls series, a game that strives to recapture the magic of its predecessor but stumbles along the way.
While it has its moments of brilliance, it also has its share of missteps that make it feel like a step sideways rather than forward.
Let’s start with the positives.
The world of Dark Souls 2 is undeniably vast, offering a wide variety of locales to explore.
From the sun-drenched ruins of Heide’s Tower of Flame to the eerie darkness of The Gutter, there’s no shortage of atmospheric areas to delve into.
The game also introduces some genuinely memorable bosses, like the Fume Knight and the Abyss Watchers, as well as new mechanics, such as power-stancing, which allows for creative dual-wielding setups.
The game’s flexibility in character builds is another highlight.
With an abundance of weapons, spells, and armor, Dark Souls 2 encourages experimentation, and the reintroduction of Soul Vessels makes it easier to respec and try new approaches.
However, the game’s shortcomings are hard to ignore.
The interconnected world design that made Dark Souls feel so cohesive is largely absent here.
Instead, the areas in Dark Souls 2 often feel disjointed, connected by bonfire warping rather than natural pathways.
This lack of spatial logic detracts from the sense of immersion and discovery that the series is known for.
The combat, while still satisfying, feels slightly off.
Hitboxes can be inconsistent, and the movement has a sluggishness that makes dodging and timing more frustrating than it should be.
Additionally, the game’s overreliance on enemy swarms, placing multiple foes in tight spaces, feels more like artificial difficulty than genuine challenge.
Narratively, Dark Souls 2 explores intriguing themes of cycles, memory, and loss, but it lacks the subtlety and emotional impact of its predecessor.
The story feels less personal, and the absence of a central figure like Gwyn or Artorias leaves the narrative feeling a bit unfocused.
Despite its flaws, Dark Souls 2 is far from a bad game.
It’s ambitious, expansive, and packed with content, including some excellent DLCs that rank among the best experiences the series has to offer.
For fans of the Souls series, it’s worth playing, but it’s unlikely to leave the same lasting impression as the first game.
In the end, Dark Souls 2 is a mixed bag, a game with undeniable strengths and frustrating weaknesses.
It’s a journey worth taking, but it’s one that occasionally stumbles on the path.

I find this game to be a bit of an odd one amongst the other soulsborne games but I still appreciate it despite the flaws it presents. The character controlls are a bit jarring but you get used to em after a bit. The adp stat on paper was a cool concept but wasn't very good in execution. The bosses are... alright I'd say (definitely the easiest out of all the other games) if we're talking about the base game. The 3 DLCs offer much more challenging and fun fights (Sir Alonne and Fume Knight are definitely the best). The level design is very confusing but I had fun traversing through em and exploring each area and admiring the way they looked. The way this game does NG+ is the best in the entire series. It actually feels like you're going through a new experience, as it adds some new enemies and item placements, changing up the gameplay a bit rather than keeping everything the same and just going through the whole game again but just with the stuff you had when you 1st finished the game. Music's definitely one of the best parts of the game and it complements the environments and situations well. Majula is most definitely up there among the best hub areas.

This game is a 7.5/10. Not as great as the other titles but it still is a good game regardless.

Posted: January 4
Тёмные души 2, школяр первого греха.

По стандарту - начал играть, потому что давно уже надо было и закончились игры, которые хотелось проходить. Игра принесла удовольствие, но шедевром она точно не является, но и в одно и тоже время не оправдывает завышенного количества хейта от общей фан базы игр серии.

Оружие и броня- 9/10 - Большой выбор оружия и брони является очень приятным добавлением к геймплею и развитию своего персонажа за счёт того, что всегда будет возможность экспериментировать с разными билдами из разного оружия и брони. Я конечно хоть и использовал только одну рапиру, копьё Орнштейна и меч Арториаса, но нахождение уникального оружия всегда было интересным даже просто, чтобы увидеть мувсет.

Боссы - 7/10. Многочисленное появление Преследователя иногда бесит, но битвы с ним являются простым скилл-чеком для игрока, так как стоит понять его мувсет, как он становится простым. Финальные боссы не сказал бы, что зацепили, но сойдут. Есть любимый босс - дымный рыцарь. Бой с ним был как своего рода танец, к которому приятно возвращаться не смотря на высокое количество смертей.

Музыка - 5/10. Первая часть Дарк Соулса для меня выделилась тем, что там множество запоминающейся тем босс файтов и других ОСТов, которые по сей день слушаю, но тут как-то не особо. Из тем боссов для меня запомнилась только грустная тема Вендрика и тема Сэра Алонна, пока остальные не особо - даже мелодия не запомнилась. Но и есть свои алмазы - тема Маджулы, пение Мильфанито, тема огненных хранительниц из самого начала и конечно же мелодия из финальных титров, которая убирает желание их пропускать.

Геймплей - 7/10 - Введение стата адаптивности бесит. От уровня этого стата зависит скорость использования эстусов, как и количество кадров бессмертия при кувырках. Даже при рекомендуемом 20 уровне скилла, кувырки часто не спасают от получения урона. Также, механика, которая запрещает бегать пока стамина не будет полностью восстановлена является бесящей. Но, по сравнению с первой частью, боёвка стала плавнее. Новый способ находки скрытых дверей в стенах является любопытном, так как это просто жмякание кнопки Х на геймпаде, пока найдёшь скрытую дверь, Наличие старых стен, которые можно открыть при помощи удара или кувырка также есть, что мне всегда нравилось.

Персонажи - 7/10 - Сильно не запоминаются, но они вполне интересно сделаны. История Лукатиель была вполне интересной. Ну и хранительница огня - waifu material.

ДЛС - 6/10 - Сильный нехваток наратива и своего рода сюжетной составляющей в некоторых ДЛС. По сравнению с ДЛС бездны из первой части, дополнения из второй части больше чувствуются прогулкой до босс файтов без особого чувства того, что чего-то добился. Просто убил боссов и пошёл дальше. Да, у снежного дополнения (там, где король слоновой кости) был наратив, объясняющий историю локации и всего подобного, но немножко суховато. Но файты вполне приятные и локации было интересно исследовать.

Итог - игру стоит попробовать.

So here we stand, almost a decade past Sotf and i believe a decade after the initial release, DS2 stands as the most controversial souls game still.
I started out on DS2 back on the ps4. Then i played bloodborne, then 3, then 1, then sekiro and elden ring.
I still believe that DS2 is a good SOULS game and a good action style game. It only for one reason is that it actually tried to diversify and innovate on the formula, rather than just be Dark Souls 1 but again!
I appreciate that it tried to blend certain aspects from Demon Souls and Dark Souls.
I am ABSOLUTELY in love with power stancing, and I'm glad to have seen it returned in Elden Ring.
People say that Life Gems nullify the game's difficulty, but i always see this coupled with people then saying "But by the time I get to X or Y i am always low on healing"
DS2 is not an endurance run to see how many bonfires you can skip.
Anyway, I enjoy that they kept the gameplay slow and methodical. I think both the DS3 and DS1/2 style of combat have a place in souls likes. DS1 and 2 feel more deliberate, it's more like you are dueling each opponent. Meanwhile 3/BB/ER are meant to be more hack&slash like. They both have their ups and downs.
I think the crown DLC's are good (except for the boss Siph or whatever. I just hate that fight}
DS2 also has my favorite implementation of spellcasting. Spells actually hurt. Like, a lot. They hit like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ trucks, even in earlier gameplay. And if you want more of a spell, you need to find/buy more of it. Which also puts a limit and a tactical spin on it. I enjoy that more than the Mana system 3 and ER use.
Because saying you ONLY have 4 uses of CSS means that sure, it's gonna deal like 2K damage per, and it'll probably kill whatever it hits, but you got it SPARINGLY. Though yes i know herbs negate this to a certain aspect, later NG+ Cycles due to health scaling might require the use of an herb which is flawed imo but that is a gripe.
Weapons also FEEL like they respectively impact according to size. GREAT hammer. GREAT sword. You hit, big number, big reaction. It feels impactful.
Yes hollowing isnt great i know it is a bad take on the system and its good this variation didnt return.
Yes adaptability isn't great but also just get your agility to like 94 or 96 and then you're back to DS1 dodge rolls. Which is like rank 20 in adaptability. We all know depraved is best class bc 10 in all stats and SL1. So that's 10 levels in a stat to get fastest item usage, ds1 dodge frames, and minutely faster weapon swapping. I also know yall still using shields in this title so its not even that crazy to ask.
DS2 is still a good game imo and yall just hate it bc its popular to hate rather than thinking and forming an opinion of your own. I dont think ds2 ranks nearly as high as the other titles, but i still think its good. 6.8/10

As someone new to the Souls series, I decided to play the original Dark Souls before jumping into Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin, and I can confidently say that this sequel is a solid and enjoyable game in its own right. While it doesn’t quite capture the magic of the first game, I still had a fantastic time exploring its world, mastering its combat, and diving into its challenging yet rewarding gameplay.

Yes, some elements feel different—like the pacing, level design, and even the mechanics—but these changes didn’t take away from the overall experience for me. In fact, some of the differences, like the variety of weapons and builds, added a new layer of depth to my playthrough.

I honestly don’t understand all the hate this game gets. Sure, it’s not perfect, and comparisons to the first game are inevitable, but when taken on its own, Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin is a good game. It’s packed with memorable moments, okay bosses, and enough challenge to keep you engaged. For newcomers like me, it’s absolutely worth playing if you enjoy a good challenge and an intriguing dark fantasy setting.

Give it a shot—you might be surprised by how much you enjoy it!

It's a great game and I enjoyed it as much as the first dark souls. I haven't finished the DLC content, but as far as the main game content goes I found it easier than the first game.
Unfortunately this game didn't receive a remaster, so there are a couple of annoying technical problems:
- fullscreen doesn't work on modern monitors (it can be solved by using borderless with some game config tweaking)
- if you have ui scaling enabled on windows then the window resolution of the game will be scaled as well, which is annoying if you trying to fix the fullscreen problem (you'll have to disable scaling as well)
- there are some problems with game servers (every time I start the game there is a ~50% chance that game won't connect to the server. Servers for the DS1 work just fine)
- also default keybindings for keybord+mouse are awful (I also recommend disabling parry with double click. I died a lot before I realised this option even existed)

[README]
- This review contains opinions and possible falsities, I'm not always correct.
- I completed Bloodborne ages ago, and DS1 just before completing DS2.

[Things I liked]
- Boss quality was much better than DS1. There is more use of the environment to make fights interesting. I loved the aesthetic of many of the bosses, especially . There's so much to love about so many of them.
- Having to adjust your build and weapon/shield to specific types of enemies. It's rewarding when you find the best build for fighting a particular boss or enemy type.
- Good graphics and audio compared to DS1, don't expect Bloodborne-levels of audio quality.
- Game feels very fluid and smooth. I'm not sure why some people say otherwise, everything feels very responsive.
- Combat is more interesting than DS1, there's a lot more strategy in fighting enemies, there are many groups of enemies and you must always be on your toes. There are many different kinds of attacks you can do and they have their own advantages and disadvantages.
- Ability to .
- Ability to redo your build if you mess up horribly, I don't want to talk about it.
- Almost bugless, no stuttering or lag or game-ruining bugs, clearly well-developed.
- Adrenaline and sense of relief/accomplishment after beating tough enemies.
- Much better NG cycle than DS1, there's a lot of new content for you to explore if you're that sort of person
- Much more content than DS1.

- The girl in the starting area, .
- Free infinite salt generator.

[Things I didn't like]
- Two specific areas () make you feel guilty for bringing summons to help you, but if you don't you are in for a hellish and very tedious experience. .
- There's many interesting parts of the game locked behind hidden knowledge obtained from wiki-reading or being super perceptive (I'm not).
- So much keyhunting, so much exploration and yet 99% of found items were worthless to me.
- Crown of the Sunken King was just a really boring experience for me, all the bosses were extremely easy.
- Mouse controls are awkward, not terribly and actually it brings an interesting challenge, the game really feels made for a controller (but plays fine using KB/M after some practice).
- NPCs don't feel as interesting as DS1's NPCs do. I just don't care about any of them. I'll never forget that time in DS1 when .
- The world just doesn't feel as interesting as DS1's world does, everything from NPC interactions to the actual design of the levels. Levels feel very strangely connected and out of place compared to DS1.
- DS1's bosses actually felt like they really existed in the world; DS2's bosses felt random, like obstacles that only 'exist' for the player to slay and then forget about.
- 99% of armour (not including shields) is utterly useless, or in other words provides extremely marginal benefits that I just don't care to think about. Enemies aren't difficult enough to warrant thinking about defense stat bonuses.
- Dying not being punishment enough. No real incentive to try harder. Failing doesn't really mean anything.
- Bonfires are everywhere, like literally everywhere, it doesn't matter if you die, with the exception of very few specific areas there is no real challenge in reaching the next bonfire.

Posted: January 2
This isn't the Black Sheep of Souls, this is the underrepresented Queen of the series.


You really think DS2 is the best Souls game?


Yes, and I'm tired of pretending it isn't.

DS2, it's such a funny situation. Dark Souls players are the most fickle of things. They want challenege, but when it isn't the right kind of challenge that involves a one handed shield carrying you through the game, OP pyromancies, fishing for the easiest backstabs of your life, and tells you to stop crutching on the lock on button, everyone loses their minds! Dark Souls 2 is a beautiful menagerie of throwing most Souls-ism's DS1 and DeS created on their head. It remixes the formula in such a fresh take, with a STUPIDLY high amount of quality content, probably the most MEANINGFUL content (had to note for Elden Ring there) difference between 2 Souls games we've ever received.

DS1, first half hallowed by thy name, is...such a masterpiece in the 1st half, as we all know, and the 2nd half is really...well let's be frank it's pretty ass. This leaves you with scathingly little content to begin with, as the game is notably smaller (but not less mighty) than further Souls titles, which on its own isn't bad! But you realize this more and more each playthrough until the game feels just too small, the magic is lost, in a sense.

DS2 I believe dishes just the right amount of content for a Souls title, there is a HUGE breadth of areas and bosses to fight, which allows for even more gimmicky fights, yes. But that's not a bad thing on its own. DS2 is extremely experimental, by far the most bold title in the franchise asides DeS, of course. It tries the weirdest ♥♥♥♥ you can imagine, the game like your favourite band's bold experimental record. And while it turns off loyalists who just want more of the same. For people who love what the art is all about, you'll massively appreciate it only more and more as time goes on.

I think Miyazaki leaving his team to create a Souls game all on their own was the best decision you could've ever made. There are SO many unique and flavourful decisions, Lifegems being a great way to allow new players to get into Souls and allows you to set your own difficulty, on top of giving you a way out of statuses like Poison and Toxin...for a price of Souls. Warping to every bonfire, THE BEST Stancing by far in the series that has never come close to its peak here, the wistful and whimsical way each area completely turns the world shape on its head as a complete inversion of the connected Metroidvania DS1 was. It was purposefully not trying to just clone DS1, and it should be applauded for that. After all, a game that's all fanservice like DS3 shows why you shouldn't just repeat tricks you already performed. Why you need new ones (asides that beautiful Soul of Cinder boss fight, GOD why can't that fight be in a better game).

Don't listen to weird gatekeepy Souls ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that tell you otherwise, play the game for yourself, make up your own mind, I promise you if you drop the conventions and expectations of what a Souls game can do, and approach with a fresh mind, DS2 is the best unexplored cavern of treasure you'll venture down in this series. DO NOT LISTEN TO THE HATERS!!! THIS GAME IS AWESOME!!!

Dark Souls 2 has got to be one of the most over-hated games of all time. The vast majority of the haters can't even come up with coherent arguments for the game being bad. They use the same, tired, vague arguments such as "bad level design", "bad hitboxes", "copy/paste bosses", all of which are not true. It's just sad that a vocal minority of players who are simply bad at DS2 or simply don't like that it's clearly different from DS1 took to YouTube and tarnished this game's reputation, and once a reputation is tarnished, it unfortunately will most likely never recover, hence why to this day you see so many ignorant, dishonest people bashing this game.

Is this game different from DS1 and DS3? Yes. The combat is slower paced, healing is slower, and Adaptability (ADP) is a thing (Ah yes, the infamous ADP mechanic. Don't listen to the haters. ADP is not nearly as bad of a mechanic as the haters make it out to be. Get it to around 16-22 and you're good for the rest of the game, which should'nt even be a problem since DS2 is EXTREMELY generous with souls. In fact, at 22 ADP you have MORE iframes than DS1 light rolling, and you can go higher if you want!). Does that make DS2 a bad game? Certainly not.

That's my two cents. At the end of the day, you'll just have to play the game yourself and determine for yourself if this game is good or not. Don't let dishonest haters discourage you from getting this game.

I'm pretty mixed on this game. In the end I do recommend it if you are a committed Dark Souls fan. This probably should not be your first Dark Souls game. I tried to play this game two times before and dropped it really quickly. There are a lot of bad things about this game and I understand why a lot of people dislike it so much. I don't think it is that bad but I also think the positive reviews that call this the best game in the series are over correcting for the negativity. I enjoyed this game a lot more than DS3 but neither game comes even close to DS1.

The negatives:
- The default dead zones feel horrible (though this can be fixed with mods). Without this mod I would not play this game it just feels so bad to control otherwise.
- Agility is not intuitive and if you don't level it up you are going to have a worse time.
- There are so many times when you will take damage that feels like it came out of nowhere. I'm not sure if this is just the infamously bad hit boxes, these attacks lacking impact, or iframe weirdness. It feels bad and can get frustrating. For the most part the boss fights are pretty easy in this game so I was able to look past this.
- The DLC kind of sucks. Crown of the sunken king was enjoyable and I like most of the old iron king. I didn't like the ivory king much at all.
- Shrine of Amana is maybe the worst area in any of these games.
- The few boss fights that are difficult tend to be frustrating. They are either difficult because of annoying gimmicks or because of the aforementioned DS2 jank. I liked the Fume Knight but I feel like it would have worked better if the mechanics of this game were a bit more refined. At least the run back is not bad.
- Hearing about the original DS2 makes me regret playing this version first.

The positives:
- The level design is generally pretty good and interesting. The environments are some of the best in the series.
- No-man's Wharf is amazing. I first encountered it on the opposite side and the mystery of the ship was intriguing. It was exciting when I found myself on the other side and got to explore the area.
- Aside from one obtuse part towards the end and accessing the DLC, the progression of the game is pretty intuitive and can be figured out without a guide.
- I like the addition of life gems. Having alternate expendable healing sources is interesting. I think life gems plus Estus is a good system.
- Some of the run backs work. In general I think run backs are not fun. DS1 has a lot of really bad run backs (Artorias, Seath, Gwyn etc.) I do however think the Taurus Demon and The Bell Gargoyles work well with their run backs. They have challenging areas that enhance otherwise relatively easy boss fights. Most of the run backs in this game work as well if not better than the run backs in DS1.

That's a lot more negatives than positives... Maybe I actually don't like this game. Nah, this game is good. Maybe play the original instead.

Posted: January 1
Let me preface this review by saying that there is a lot of fearmongering about this game. I was reluctant to play it because of constantly hearing stuff like "the game is unfair" or "the difficulty isn't fun". After playing it became clear that most of the complaints about Dark Souls II come from people who haven't played the game or are too stubborn to engage with the game in a reasonable way. This is a flawed game, but the discourse around it is straight up misinformation. Do not fall trap to it. If it weren't for my friends giving me this advice, I probably wouldn't have experienced what Dark Souls II has to offer.

If you want a short review of this game in relation to Dark Souls 1, here it is: DS1 is an amazing game with some problems, while DS2 is a great game with a lot of problems. However, the areas where DS2 is great are completely different than where DS1. DS1 has a better interconnected map, memorable NPCs and a great variety of bosses, while DS2 has a better combat system and variety of weapons and gear, and both of them share the great style of storytelling that come with souls games. If you liked Dark Souls 1, this game is not it. You will not get an elevator from the undead parish to firelink. You will not see anor londo again. However, you will get a worthy experience.

If you want a long review... let's start!

This game takes place in Dragleic, a land where the undead go in order to try to release themselves from the "curse". The game is direct and tells you that this place is horrible and mocks you for even trying to go there. It is immediately obvious why. The game is clearly more difficult than Dark Souls 1. The number of enemies in the map is way higher (sometimes, way too high, lol), you start with a single estus flask and each time you die you lose a bit of your maximum health, having to use a consumable item to go back to normal. Meanwhile, the enemies are doing everything they can to kill you. They will ambush you, they have 20 million moves they can use and they aren't afraid of coming at you all at once.

This adversity is balanced both thematically (by giving you beautiful sceneries and companions between your travels) and mechanically (by giving you enough tools to deal with difficult situations). Not understanding how the adversity is balanced mechanically is a big point of frustration between players, as the game does not spell out what you have to do to avoid scenarios like being ganked by a bunch of enemies. This is by design, as the player is expected to figure out how to deal with every situation, and this is arguably the most unique and fun aspect about souls games. However, it does feel like hollowing can make the player a bit too fearful of dying and experimenting, pushing them in the wrong direction.

It is clear that the game really wants you to interact with it's mechanics, and this exacerbates the frustration mentioned before. For example, fog walls don't grant you invincibility for the whole duration of the animation anymore, and chests take super long to open. While a lot of people like to attribute this to bad design, there is an obvious reason why they made these changes: to force you to engage in combat with the enemies. Since they made the enemies stronger, it naturally occurs to the player that perhaps they should try simply ignoring combat if they can. To prevent this from being a viable option all of the time, you will have to clear enemies if they are particularly close to a fog wall or if you want items that are near them if you don't want to get killed.

You are also rewarded for clearing enemies, since they will stop spawning after dying 12 times (this also prevents you from farming infinitely and leveling up too much). This way, fighting is the most viable option during most of the game. However, relying solely on it is particularly difficult, given that the enemies are challenging some of them are way too strong for their respective area, and may be guarding a secret that you can unveil after you come back stronger. This is good game design, you are supposed to think what particular strategy is more effective for each particular situation, making sure that the player can't just do one thing the whole game.

When you are done with an area, you will be faced with a boss. The bosses in this game are... surprisingly easy. Sometimes I had more trouble with a single regular enemy in an area than in the actual boss fight. I believe this is a problem, since a lot of bosses feel completely forgettable due to not even needing to memorize their attack patterns before beating them. Most difficult bosses in the game (specially pre-DLC) are encounters with multiple enemies, which isn't bad per se but it really feels like they didn't get the balance right in single enemy encounters (again, pre-DLC). Because of this, many bosses feel pretty uninteresting.

However, for interesting battles, the game gives you all sorts of tools to engage with them. There is a gigantic array of weapons, armors and rings you can mix and match, along with re-specs in case you screwed up your build. In particular, weapons feel varied and they are amazing to use. The game also floods you with materials for you to upgrade said weapons, to the point where i had something like 10 fully upgraded weapons i could choose by the end of the game. Since the particular attack animation and stats of each weapon matter, it is a meaningful and strategic decision to choose your loadout for each particular battle.

We end up having a great system, but it is sadly bogged down by some really uninteresting areas, specially at the middle of the game. A lot of them are either too short or filled with annoying enemies. In part, this is because of the development hell this game suffered, which lead to a lot of areas being simplified and repurposed. This was frustrating for me, because I felt like there was something great beneath the surface that wasn't being explored for a big chunk of my playtime.

Still, the vision of the game becomes clear once you go to the DLC areas. They are 3 carefully constructed maps that become a playground for the amazing core of Dark Souls II, each of them with their own story which ties thematically with the rest of the game. They also "fix" some of the problems of the base game, for example the lack of challenging single target boss fights. These areas revitalized my love for the game and are as "Dark Souls" as you can get. They aren't without controversy though, as they also feature the most painful boss runbacks of the series. I don't particularly these, as they are locked to optional bosses, and I think it's fine for them to try to extend your playtime when you already finished all the mandatory content (not to mention that I enjoy fighting regular enemies in this game). Not judging people that hate these runbacks, but please remember that you are not forced to do them, they are an additional challenge.

Lastly, about the story. Like in Dark Souls 1, they don't really spell out what is happening to you, and you need to pay attention to item descriptions and environmental clues to understand what this game is trying to say. The themes brought up in the first game are expanded and even more of them are introduced. However, it is worth pointing out that the execution is lacking compared to DS1. In particular NPCs talk in a manner so direct and "to the nose" (for example, the old women at the beginning of the game saying that "you will die a lot") that it feels antithetical to philosophy of storytelling of souls games. However, I would be lying if I said I didn't take a liking for some of the characters, even if their writing is arguably questionable.

In general, great game, would recommend. . 8/10

PS: I ran out of characters in the making of this review, and after rephrasing and cutting some stuff, I was only left with exactly this much space : P

I dropped this game like a rock when I played the original at release on the PS3. However, I'm very glad I decided to beat this last year and I still play it randomly when I just want to mess around. It's my least favorite Soulsborne game, but it has a unique charm that could only have have been bred from a significant lack of involvement from Miyazaki. After the first director, Shibuya, was summarily dismissed and Tanimura brought in to right the ship, sacrifices had to be made and compromises struck. This is similar to how Miyazaki took over for Demon's Souls, and I believe Tanimura is to be praised for getting this released in the state it was. The Scholar version certainly improves on most things in my estimation and getting all three (mostly good) DLCs included is great. I would recommend playing this directly after the first Dark Souls if you are starting the series from scratch. It will allow you to appreciate what it brought to the series as a whole (even Sekiro and Elden Ring) that we just take for granted now.

Not everything in the following list is included in DS3, and this isn't an exhaustive list. Just some off-the-cuff thoughts I had after finishing.

• Weapon/armor degradation and repairing system overhauled for the better - has actual impact on gameplay now. Carried over to DS3.
• Ability to warp from the beginning (debatable if this is a good thing). Carried over to DS3.
• Curse system - initially frustrating but started to grow on me. Has a visual, gameplay, and lore reason that works well.
• Estus flask upgrades must be obtained one by one by the player.
• Multiple items that allow a player to modify the difficulty in-game to allow more players to beat the game but be even more challenging than DS1 if one so desires: bonfire ascetics, more summons, specific rings, early game armor and weapons, Covenant of Champions, etc.
• NG+ adds more enemies and items - every game with a NG+ should handle it similar to this
• POWER STANCE
• Wooden chests are breakable, leaving rubbish
• Mini-boss that follows you around, getting stronger each time
• Hex magic
• Ability to aim magic properly like a bow
• Enemies that cannot be backstabbed
• Ring that prevents backstabs
• Story and world are unique and don't copy DS1, but instead build from it. I think some of the lore and story are actually very good and present a more personal story while using themes we haven't seen previously.
