I started the game trying not to think about all the negatives that i heard about it and yet i can say with sadness that it's probably the only from's game i'll never play again. I finished the vanilla game and also all the dlc. The game it's not as disgusting as everyone says but it really does show how poorly designed it is and how it's a step down in almost every way from the predecessor. I played almost every game Fromsoftware has ever made and had planned to get all achievements and yet Ds2 managed to make me desist. There's a lot to say about the game, so i'll start with the good parts:
-The game has some mechanics that are most welcome (bonfire ascetics) and a variety of playstyles that I think is a good competition even with Ds3.
- The soundtrack as always is masterfully crafted and always made me stop for a moment to fully appreciate it (majula).
-The locations are amazing and always feel masterfully made (on a aesthetic point) and gave the world an incredible feeling. Majula is one of if not the best main hub of the series even tho i feel like some of its beauty is given by how punishing and overwhelming all the game feels.
The game up to this point is amazing and surely would have deserved another playthrough if not for what i'm going to talk about next. Sadly the game takes a plunge down from now. So let's discuss the bad by addressing each rotten piece that poisons this game:
-Adp: this stat must be one of the WORST ever design choice I have ever seen in a souls game. It increases you high frames and consumables speed. This is a gigantic problem as adp literally works as a difficulty bar for the game, based on how much you level up adp the game goes from being barely playable to a joke.
-Healing: the healing in this game is slow and most of the time will get you killed because of how forced and underwhelming it feels. Ds1 also forced you in a stationary position while using your estus and yet that feels 100 times better than what we have in Ds2. The lifegems are a cool addition but the fact that you can buy infinite stocks from the merchant makes the estus and all the upgrades related to it almost useless. The fact that healing also doesn't instantly take effect but slowly replenishes you health bar feels like a forced difficulty.
-Enemies: going through a simple area has never felt more tedious as before, as in Ds2 difficulty is not based on an intricate and ever evolving level design but is based on a x4 on every enemy on the map. The iron keep is complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ for how many enemies are thrown to the player and how much damage they deal and it's far from being the worst. The fact that also every enemy has the ability to stun lock you makes moving through the map a nightmare, as getting hit once by certain enemies means an almost guaranteed game over.
-animations: the animations for each action is incredibly slow and most of the time will get you killed since, in contrary to the other souls, you have no protection whatsoever while performing them. The enemies animation are also junk, especially their recovery. This is because while doing so they are completely immune to any sort of damage and most of the times is incredibly difficult to punish them. Add on top of it that the moment they get back up they are immediately ganking you again and you'll find yourself stuck in an attack animation that didn't connect and most surely will get you killed. Also some enemies have completely broken animation as one second they are standing still and then they instantly hit you (i'm looking at you heide knights).
-Bosses: Most of the bosses are boring and uninspired if not directly reused bosses. I can count the good bosses on maybe one hand (fume knight, looking glass knight and alonne on top), and there are 41 present dlc included. Most of them are also stupid gank fights that are only difficult for how stupidly balanced they are as in the belfry gargoyles. If you also add that most of them are easily exploitable or can be bamboozled by simply walking around them it makes the experience even worse. Overall i fell like the average boss in Ds2 is worse than the bed of chaos. This is a big let down as bosses that deserved so much more as the burnt ivory king become get penalized by the pre-boss. The final boss in so bad. I understand that Gwin may be hated by some for how stupid easy is to parry him but Nashandra can't even hold a candle to him; its boring, slow and uneventful. Some of the boss are also so gimmiky that is laughable. Some will simply damage you for being close to them and some will simply break your weapons over and over. I know these are on levels so different that it feels like staring in an abyss, but take Gael as an example. Dying to him never feels frustrating or undeserved for how incredible his moveset is and how balanced the fight feels. There's been maybe 4/5 bosses where i felt like my death were fair and never felt as good.
-targeting and detection: the player's targeting sometimes feels like it's held together with hopes and dream as I've lost count of how many time my character just decided to make a 90° turn while attacking an enemy. Even worse is the target lock when multiple enemies are present on screen as sometimes it locks on the closest enemy and sometimes it locks on an enemy behind you on a ledge 10 meters above, you screwing you over completely as you watch your character swing in the air while your lifebar quickly drops to 0. The enemies on the other side are capable of drifiting on your location even mid animation making impossible to dodge certain attacks if not with the power of the incredible hitboxes that plague this game (more on that soon enough). Not to mention that some enemies are capable to detect you from so far away that you sometime don't even have the time to realize they are there before they swarm you.
-Level design: two words, Iron keep. Jokes apart the world feels so disconnected and unidirectional that I found myself more than once wondering where I was or how the hell was i supposed to proceed in the game. Take Dark souls 1. From the first moment you get to Lordran you can se almost every single zone you can explore up to Anor londo, here you are lucky if you understand where you are headed to. I feel that completing the game sometimes is relatable to doing all Elden ring's npcs quests in one playthrough without a guide, a punishment out of hell.
-Hitboxes. oh boy, what a way to end this list. Never have i seen such badly implemented hitboxes as in this game (ok taurus demon and centipede demon, you can join the Ds2 family and stay in the corner of shame as well). Almost every thrust attack made by enemies will connect just if you are near the target . I've been skewered by the pursuer while being behind him so many times that at a certain point i just stopped counting. Not to mention how many times my zweihander simply faded out of existence while attacking an enemy. I just can't even start to describe how horrendous it feels to die because an enemy simply fazed through your weapon and oneshotted you.
So, it's time to wrap up this insanely long review of a game i wished with all my heart i could enjoy even with all the problems it has. Sadly, after over 50h (i know it says 49 on my profile but i played around 10h in offline mode) of playing it i can't say I'd ever recommend it. I know that a lot of people love this game form the bottom of their hearts and i understand it but i believe that its also correct to admit that the game is not even close to being the best of the series. I've seen a lot of comments saying how the game is more of a strategy game and as such cannot be approached like its brothers, but to this I say that there is a big difference between having a strategic game and a poorly design one. So if anyone ever asked me to describe Dark souls 2 in a single world, with a bitter taste in my mouth I'd say this: tedious.

Dark Souls II is the best worst game I've ever played. It's BRUTALLY difficult and "unfair" when you start this game. Compared to other Souls games, the game is excruciatingly stingy with stamina, and stamina management is a constant must. But once you get some estus shards, level up your Agility (the actual stat that controls i-frames for rolling, and flask drinking speed, controlled by both Adaptability and Attunement), and start to understand the rhythm of the game, most of it becomes an addictive, masochistic joy.

The level variety, build variety, combat are all very fun. However, most of the levels are basically a series of one booby trap after another. The game is constantly try to "get" you. If you see an item out in the open, you KNOW it's an ambush. Everything is an ambush, everything is a trap. In fact if I can summarize the game in a single sentence, it's "ha ha, got you again, gamer-kun!" However once you learn where the "traps" are, it's sickeningly satisfying to "defeat" the traps and get through unscathed.

However the DLCs in particular have some of the most ridiculous, un-fun boss run-backs in video game history (Sir Alonne and Blue Smelter Demon, I'm looking at you). The bosses themselves are mostly pretty fun to fight. I died a record 114 times to the Fume Knight before finally besting him, but learning his moveset and then killing him the first try on NG+ was exilirhating, seeing how far I'd come in terms of skill.

All in all, the game is a deeply flawed masterpiece. The highs are high, the lows are low.. But if you are a fan of Fromsoft/Souls games you should have a blast. You will curse at the game but then wonder why you put in 130 hours into it. One last thing. This game is definitely for Souls veterans though. If you have never played a Souls game, DO NOT start with this one, you will regret it and never play any of these "acquired taste" games. Otherwise enjoy, seek Souls, seek the King. Lest this land swallow you whole.

By the end of Dark Souls II I had become the most powerful creature in the world. I had overcome the challenges between me and my throne, and gone beyond the bounds of my crown and put down the ambitious embers of my rival monarchs. I had charted the deep and learned all manner of magics and swordplay, and forged myself into an unstoppable force of will.

To me Dark Souls II is a perfect sequel to Dark Souls. While my quest through Dark Souls Remastered certainly had a greater impact on me from the start, being my first foray into the dark world of fire and souls, I never reached the level of investment in my own characters progression in DS1, that I did in DS2.

The quest of Dark Souls II starts out feeling quite meaningless, intentionally building and building and building in tension, stakes, visual and narrative splendour and complexity and gameplay quality until it reaches a point I never found in the first Dark Souls.

The game starts out relatively slow and clunky both in story and gameplay, but that is part of THE POINT to me. More than the other games in this series I really suggest sticking it out through this one, the payoff is well worth the struggle.

Posted: December 28, 2024
Dark Souls II: SotFS is a game that lacks in what makes Dark Souls games truly great: balanced areas, good enemies, and memorable and engaging bosses. It is as clunky as Dark Souls I, but without the charm; as expansive as Dark Souls III but lacking in quality. Most of the game is unmemorable, yet traumatizes your mind in a certain way that makes me think of it as "The Dark Souls of Dark Souls", meant only for the most masochistic of gamers. There are bosses like the Ancient Dragon or Lud and Zallen which the mere thought of fighting and likely dying to sends deep tremors throughout my soul.

The bosses are a huge issue for me personally. Not only are they harder than Dark Souls I's bosses but there are more of them and they sport similarly nightmarish run backs (Blue Smelter Demon, Sir Alonne). And some of the hit boxes are so outrageously out-of-depth it's ridiculous. The game's lock-on mechanics (or more accurately, lack there-of) rear their ugly head here.

Areas like the Shrine of Amana give me cancer. Bonfire placement can be very frustrating as you can die for so many reasons and be forced back so incredibly far. In every area there is a ♥♥♥♥-ton of enemies that need to be methodically defeated in order for you to have another chance to die to a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boss attack (Old Iron King); I have no doubt OG Dark Souls II was far better about this.

Is it an atrocious game? No. There are undoubtedly good moments and it delivers upon much the same notes of satisfaction like in other FromSoftware titles. The DLCs are FromSoftware DLCs, that is to say: they are good for the most part. But there are serious internal flaws that just simply cannot be ignored. I understand if you like this game, as I can see a lot of good concepts and innovations in it. For me though? All these ideas are mired in a cascade of BS that has turned me off from touching this again for the foreseeable future, or at least until I have enough time on my hands to truly savor Tanimura's """genius""" design decisions.

Quite a few years ago, I put 2 hours into DS2 sofs, got frustrated, and quit. So earlier this month with the goal of the 100% achievement, I went into this thinking it would be the worst experience of my life.
91.2 hours later with the 100% achievement in hand, I am so glad to say I was wrong, and ds2 sofs ended up being one of the easiest, and most fun 100% completions to date.
I went into this game “mostly” blind. And by mostly, I had no idea about any of the bosses, story, etc, but I knew about stuff like I frames scaling with ADP. So I poured my first 30 levels into ADP. And given my experience with SL1 runs in DS3 and ER, and given how slow moving and telegraphed a lot of the bosses were, this lead to a lot of first or second try boss completions, regardless of my lacklustre damage.
DS2 has a lot of really cool, unique, and gimmicky (in a good way!) bosses, even though it has quite a few fairly ordinary ones.
The real difficulty of DS2 comes in the way it tests patience and tactical abilities with its level design. DS2 punishes the impatient and unobservant, which most of the time, so it’s a game for those of whom have a meticulous approach to games.
Would I recommend DS2 SOFS? 100%. But maybe not as a first Fromsoftware title. Start with ER or DS3, then go to DS1, and then only after completing those three, try DS2. DS2 has a massive skill check that can (for the most part) be circumvented with adequate experience in other fromsoftware titles.
To conclude, whilst DS2 has quite a few issues, it is still an amazing title of which I would recommend to most.

- Combat is slower and more methodical. Timing your attacks is key to successfully defeating enemies.

- You are going to die. The game is meant to be challenging, so have patience.
- Read item descriptions. They will tell you if a weapon or piece of armor has a special effect.
- Remember, have fun when you play. Take breaks when you need them.

(Minor spoilers)

What a frustrating game. Mostly because of how it had good ideas, great ideas even, but was let down by the overall package. The long and short of it is that I would recommend playing it, especially if you like other similar titles, but curb your expectations.

Starting with the good...

Power stancing (or dual wielding) is good. It needs a lot of stamina with the weapons I tried, but it's just nice to have the option and variety. The way the character holds some weapons while power stancing is goofy (like two Greatswords), but it is what it is. I think it's a nice addition.

While it's arguable how good the level design is (magma above the elevator, yes), I've honestly come to appreciate it, especially because it has elements that other souls games don't. Hidden switches to open doorways and disable traps, lots of secret doorways, burning the windmill to make the boss fight easier, melting the ice to unlock the new parts/aspects of the level, activating doorways and statues with souls, etc.

Some of the enemies were also cool in how you had to deal with them, like destroying statues to make them vulnerable to damage, collecting souls to lower the damage reduction of a boss, and so on.

Overall, the game does feel unique and interesting in a lot of ways.

Onto the bad...

The graphics feel off somehow. I don't know if it's the shaders, the textures themselves, or what, but the game feels overly contrasty and jarring a lot of the time, even if there are a bunch of nice areas, like Majula or Shrine of Amana.

when dying makes sense from a lore perspective, but a dumb way to punish players for failing, especially when you already lose your souls.

. The Black Gulch and The Gutter are two examples, but the absolute worst one, without a damn shred of contest, is the Frigid Outskirts. Just... no. Vision so bad you can't see your own ♥♥♥♥, zero bonfires (actually zero), massive lightning goats with horrible hitboxes coming at you from any and all directions to skewer your ass, and a boss fight with multiple enemies to top it off, if you actually make it to the end.

That said, the game throws multiple enemies, even bosses, at you so often, that you just kind of learn to live with it by the end, which is something that's true about the game overall, to me anyway.

A lot of the , slow, had very similar attacks to one another. There are exceptions, like Sir Alonne or the Darklurker, but for the most part, they felt lackluster.

While I like the game's creativity overall, and I have no idea how you'd find them without looking them up on the wiki. "Just play the game" would be a good argument here, if it wasn't so frustrating overall, because of...

. This is my number one gripe with the game, by a landslide. Absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I don't know how else to say it. How they tested it at Fromsoft and were like "yeeees, this is ripe for release" is beyond me. Did they not sit down and play it with a controller? It feels so much worse than the other titles simply because of how the sensitivity works (and how janky jumping is, but to be fair, the other two DS titles aren't much better in this regard), and no, not even the mod fixes it. Can you live with it? Sure. You get used to it eventually. But it makes the other parts of the game more frustrating. Have lots of enemies to deal with or projectiles coming at you and want to quickly run in the other direction? Your character will first mini stun itself, even if you were standing still, before running in the other direction. Fighting on narrow pathways, platforming? Just more frustration. Controls are super important in of these kinds of games, and it's a miss in this one, because the game just doesn't good to play. Add to this that certain weapons disregard the fact that you're locked on if you hold a direction with the left analog while attacking, plus the often , and you got yourself a recipe for frustration.

I hope they add DS3 character controls to it one day as a quality of life update.

Basic rundown of my souls journey: played Dark Souls 1 at launch, bounced off. Tried again a few years later, bounced off. Tried Bloodborne, bounced off. Played Elden Ring, had it click, put 300 hours into it. Went back and played Dark Souls 1, enjoyed it. Played Bloodborne, loved it. Then I played Dark Souls 2, and while it isn't as high as Bloodborne in my power ranking, it's definitely up there.

I had a blast with this game. Exploring every new zone felt like a total adventure, I never knew what was going to be around the next corner. The story notes and bits of lore made the world genuinely interesting, and some of the enemies got genuine belly laughs out of me when they beat me in entertaining ways. I found some of the bosses to be disappointingly easy or simple, but there were equally many bosses that I enjoyed thoroughly, and a handful that will live on in my head as "best souls bosses ever."

I'm going to get another run in on this as soon as I get through DS3, then it's on to Sekiro. For someone who's trying to keep score, my power ranking is currently Elden > Bloodborne > DS2 > DS1. Of them all, I still find DS1 to be the least approachable and most frustrating, but they all have things I find to be worth replaying whenever the mood strikes.

Dark Souls II
— is a masterpiece that continues the tradition of its legendary series, offering a unique gaming experience that combines the complexity of locations and gives you a challenge.

Every corner here is steeped in history, which can be revealed through environmental details, NPC dialogues and found items.

The variety of builds deserves special mention. A huge selection of weapons, spells and equipment allows you to experiment and find a style of play. Of course, the combat system is not sharpened to the ideal, but it was a very interesting experience.

The multiplayer mode, unfortunately, is almost dead, but during my playthrough I came across a couple of summoning signs, which means the community's dedication to this game.



This DLC takes us to underground ruins surrounded by darkness and ancient magic. Smart puzzles and difficult enemies make this DLC an unforgettable challenge. True, the bosses were not difficult, but rather stuffy, which leaves a certain mark that changes the picture of the entire DLC.


This DLC is a real test for daredevils who are not afraid of heat and hot metal. You are given a challenge to collect all the fragments of the "Soul of Nadalia" that are encountered along the entire journey. This DLC has enough puzzles and many hidden details, like a secret room on the elevator platform. The bosses of this add-on are extremely pleasing, because "Smoke Knight" and "Sir Alonne" offer you a challenge that not everyone can overcome, which brings a sea of emotions after their victory. Although the copy of the boss "Demon Smelter" in this add-on was disappointing, because it left only sad emotions after the Iron Citadel.


The snowy kingdom of Eleum Loyce, filled with ice storms and hidden dangers, is the most atmospheric of all three DLCs. This add-on introduces the mechanics of collecting allies, which will be necessary in the battle with the "Ivory King". And the king's pets "Aava", "Lud" and "Zallen" provide a real challenge that can bring a lot of emotions. Regarding the stuffiness in this DLC, I was pissed off by the hit boxes of the king's pets, as well as the stuffy battle with the final boss. After all, it was not interesting to clear the arena of enemies for 5 minutes over and over again, and then fight the boss himself. But still, this will be the most beloved DLC of all three masterpieces.


— это шедевр, который продолжает традиции своей легендарной серии, предлагая уникальный игровой опыт, сочетающий в себе сложность локаций и давая тебе челлендж.

Каждый уголок здесь пропитан историей, которую можно раскрыть через детали окружения, диалоги NPC и найденные предметы.

Особого упоминания заслуживают разнообразие билдов. Огромный выбор оружия, заклинаний и экипировки позволяет экспериментировать и находить стиль игры. Конечно, боевая система не заточена под идеал, но это был очень интересный опыт.

Многопользовательский режим, к сожалению, практически мертв, но за свое прохождения я встречал пару знаков призыва, что означает преданность комьюнити к данной игре.



Данное DLC переносит нас в подземные руины, окружённые мраком и древней магией. Умные головоломки и сложные враги делают это DLC незабываемым испытанием. Правда, боссы оказались не сложным, а больше душными, что оставляет определенный след, который меняет картину о всем DLC.


Данное DLC — это настоящее испытание для смельчаков, которые не боятся жара и раскалённого металла. Перед тобой ставят испытание собрать все осколки «Души Надалии», которые встречаются по пути всего путешествия. В это DLC достаточно головоломок и множество сокрытых деталий, как секретная комната на платформе лифта. Боссы данного дополнения крайне порадовали, ведь «Дымный Рыцарь» и «Сэр Алонн» предлагают тебе челлендж, который не все могут преодолеть, что доставляет море эмоций после их победы. Хотя копия босса “Демон из Плавильни” в данном дополнении разочаровало, ведь оставлял он лишь печальные эмоции после Железной Цитадели.


Заснеженное королевство Элеум Лойс, наполненное ледяными бурями и скрытыми опасностями, является самым атмосферным из всех трех DLC. В этом дополнении представлена механика сбора союзников, которые будут необходимы при битве с «Королем Слоновой кости». А питомцы короля «Аава» , «Луд» и «Заллен» предоставляют реальный челлендж, который может доставить кучу эмоций. Касательно духоты в этом DLC, то меня выбесили хит-боксы питомцев короля, а также душная битва с финальным боссом. Ведь, было не интересно раз за разом 5 минут зачищать арену от врагов, а после сражаться с самим боссом. Но все же это будет самым любимым DLC из всех трех шедевров.

Posted: December 21, 2024
i don’t really know why i’m writing this, it’s like 3 in the morning, i just noticed my coffee is cold again, i think this was cup five… or maybe six? i cant really tell, i remember making one and then i blinked and it was gone and now there’s another one, it just keeps respawning, like some sort of cursed ritual, every time i die, i drink, and i die a lot,
also yeah “call me maybe” is playing in the background and i dont even have the energy to laugh at how stupidly perfect that is anymore, this game does something to me, like it reached deep inside me and unplugged something.

i’ve played dark souls 2 many times, before i was on steam, when i had this old ps3 or maybe it was the 360, cant even remember, i used to think i understood this game, but the more i play, the less it makes sense, like i think it’s changing when i’m not looking or maybe i’m the one changing and the game is staying exactly the same, which is worse

and then you get to majula... again, god, majula, always always majula, even if i don’t want to, i always end up here, it’s hard to explain why, the moment you walk in and the wind starts blowing and that music starts everything just stops, not dramatically, just a quiet pause in my brain, i sit by that cliff and stare at the sun or whatever that is, it’s stuck, it never moved, is it rising? or setting? has it ever moved? i dont know, sometimes i just end up staring for a long time, i look down at the ocean and it feels like my soul left me and drowned down there, very deep, i stop being a person for a while,
the firekeeper keeps telling me “bearer of the curse” and yeah, same, every time i hear it, i feel like i’ve gone back in time, to the first time i ever played this game.

there’s something so off about this game, not in a bad way, more like emotionally off, like when you walk into a room that belonged to someone you lost, everything is still there untouched, but it feels wrong, everyone in this world talks like they forgot their own name and are trying to remember it without letting on, they keep saying things, sad things, cryptic things,
it’s weird because i’ve seen people online say thr same thing, dark souls 2 feels like a dream, a dream you don’t remember having, and yeah it does, it’s like this shared feeling most players had, but we all agree, it felt like a home in a way it really shouldn’t, the enemies, the architecture, the music, even the menus, it all feels like a memory, a fake one, but still real somehow.

i keep finding myself walking into areas I know by heart, I have done this many times, but i don’t know what’s around the corner anymore, my hands remember but my brain doesn’t, it feels like doing it for the first time and the tenth time at once.
the enemy placements sometimes i try to understand them but you cant, not in a “haha funny” way, more like “haha sadistic”, like the game is mad at you, or maybe it’s trying to teach you something, or maybe it’s just... reflecting you, ever think about that? maybe this game isn’t hard in a “git gud” way, but in a “mirror held up to your soul” kinda way.

it feels like the game is asking: why are you here again? what are you hoping to find? you walk into an area where tons of enemies attack at once, you start rolling, trying to run away but no matter what, you die, and then again you die, and keep dying, but then out of nowhere, you walk through a forest and the trees are leaning in just slightly too much and the air is thick with nothing, and suddenly you remember being 14, feeling like the world was ending, and that it meant something.

there’s that poison place, god i hate that place, not like game hate, but more like emotional hatred, i think i lose a part of myself every time I go there, it’s so dark, the air is heavy, everything wants to kill you slowly, even the walls feel like they’re staring, and those damn statues spitting endlessly, they never stop, i keep dying and lighting my torch, dying and running, dying and running, dying and running....... and at some point i just looked at the screen and realized I wasn’t human anymore, like literally in the game i went hollow, but also metaphorically felt it, and maybe that was fine.

I think a lot of people playing dark souls 2 start to feel the same way, like you’re not playing a game anymore, you’re just there, wandering inside it, looking for something you dont remember the name of, and the game never gives you anything, no reward, no understanding, just more silence, more ruins, more broken people.

And the world...... it feels wrong in a way that's hard to explain, not ruined wrong, but more like no one’s been here for a long time, and everything settled in the wrong way, quiet but not peaceful, still but not safe, it's hard to explain, like walking through someone’s half remembered dream of a kingdom, with the details fuzzy at the edges, you'll find a tower where there shouldn’t be one, a fortress above the clouds, a dock at the bottom of a castle, snow after fire, and after some time you will stop questioning it, you just keep going.

people complain about the world design, say it’s not like the others, but i think that’s what makes it what it is, the world itself feels hollow, and you’re just passing through its remains, the sky never changes, it just hangs there, pale and faded, like a backdrop no one bothered to replace, even majula which should feel safe, feels… tired, like it’s been watching people come and go for ages and already knows how this all ends.

there are so many weapons, so many different builds, it’s overwhelming, it feels like staring at a wardrobe after a breakup, nothing fits anymore, but you keep trying on different things hoping one will feel like you, and none of them do, or maybe they all do, and that’s the problem, i keep playing with new builds like i can never find mine, there’s this urge to keep changing, i dont know if that’s because of the game, or because of me.

and then there’s magic, specifically hexes, i think this is the only game that really lets you fall into them fully, they’re not just dark sorceries, they’re heavier, some spells come with a cost, not just in stats, but in the way they draw from both faith and intelligence, like you’re trying to believe in something and understand it at the same time, some spells even cost the souls you carry, not usage limits, hexes are strong yeah but they feel wrong to use, like borrowing something you shouldn’t have touched.

The bosses… there’s a lot, some feel like just fog and a wall you need to pass, others are extremely hard not because the boss itself is hard, no, the way to the boss is the real fight, sometimes you die ten times before even reaching the door, those paths feel like hell. but the good ones stick with you forever, not just because they’re hard, but because they mattered, like they were saying something even if you’re not sure what.

some i’ll never forget, sir alonne, fume knight, king vendrick, and you can fight them again in the same playthrough, bonfire ascetics let you burn the past and relive it, harder this time, most games would make that feel like grinding but here it feels like trying to understand, like maybe if you fight them enough times something will change, or you will.

i don’t think this is a review anymore, it’s just something i needed to write down,
i think dark souls 2 is about decay, about repetition, about the long slow burn of existing, it’s not about hope, really, it’s about stubbornness, you keep going not because it gets better, but because you can, and somehow, that’s enough

Review after recently playing all 3 Dark Souls games in order.

At first, I was thinking of skipping this one because of the reviews and general reception of the game. Oh boy, I am glad I did not!
It hooked me the most out of the three - I just could not stop playing it! But it is a love-and-hate relationship. :D

The Good:
It's weirdly beautiful. At first glance, the graphics seem ugly, but the game is beautiful. It has a wide range of different areas, and you just never know where you'll end up next. The whole game feels like a big journey - not always a very logical one - but very interesting nonetheless. It makes you want to discover more.

And it's not just the traveling; you just never know what you're going to find around the corner. Enemy designs are interesting and different from other FromSoftware games. Also, this game has lots of quirky ideas to troll the player. The devs experimented a lot, and it shows. That's probably what hooked me the most.

I also love that this game requires a different combat approach than other Souls games. It makes it a breath of fresh air. Here, you don't just run past enemies—most of the time, there’s an army of enemies in an area, and if you try to run past them, they’ll chase you forever (to your grave). And I think that's a good thing. It makes the game a bit more strategic. You need to bait enemies, think about your position, and use all the tools the game gives you. For example, in some areas, a bow is your best friend, or you can play with enemy ai and make them fall to their deaths.

Also, the game has a huge variety of weapons and movesets!

The lore is arguably the best out of the trilogy.

The DLCs are also arguably the best DLCs in the trilogy.

The Bad:
You start the game incredibly weak, and this design choice definitely adds to the games reputation. Some people will start it, get frustrated, and just drop it. Dark Souls 2 gets easier the more you play it.

On top of that, there are a lot of changes from the standard FromSoftware formula.

For example: LEVEL UP YOUR ADAPTABILITY! This stupid stat will add more i-frames to your roll. You need 96 agility to get the equivalent of a DS1 mid-roll. If you're doing a mage build, Attunement also gives you agility. Of course, you get a lot of levels in the game, but this again contributes to a harsh start - you need to invest in a stupid stat that shouldn't exist, instead of your health or main damage stat.

As much as I liked the exploration, there’s no logical natural progression through the maps. For example, you can accidentally finish the game without even realizing you’re heading toward the last boss.

The Ugly:
Combat. For some reason, the devs decided to change the character movement from 360 degrees (or something close to it) in Dark Souls 1 to an 8-directional movement. On top of that, the lock-on doesn’t work as a full lock-on. This basically means you’ll miss your swings, and it feels horribly clunky. The only tip I can give you is to learn to play without locking on.

All in all, don’t skip this game. It has very high highs and very low lows, but it’s a journey you’ll never forget!

Don’t go hollow, skeleton!

P.S. Ran perfectly on a Steam Deck OLED.

Posted: December 17, 2024
Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is the game that truly tested my persistence and patience. While I did enjoy certain parts of it, I cannot overlook the flaws that mark this as the weakest entry in the Dark Souls series and one of FromSoftware's most divisive releases. If you're considering buying this game, I feel compelled to offer a realistic perspective: unless you're a dedicated Souls fan with an iron will, this might not be the experience you're hoping for.

The Early Game: A Rough Start
I consider the beginning of DS2 to be the hardest among all Souls games, though not for the right reasons. The keyboard and mouse controls are poorly optimized and lack customization (for example, you can't even set E to interact solely). Enemy placements and frustrating gank fights feel less like thoughtful challenges and more like attempts to artificially slow down your progress. This leads to many encounters feeling repetitive and, frankly, boring.

The Last Giant, likely your first boss, sets the tone for many others in the game: bosses often have underwhelming movesets, questionable hitboxes, or lack the pacing and respect for player skill seen in other Souls entries. Despite this, there are standout fights that reminded me of FromSoftware's brilliance—Lost Sinner, The Rotten, Darklurker, and Fume Knight were genuinely enjoyable and well-designed.

World and Game Design: Missed Opportunities
One of the greatest strengths of Dark Souls has always been its interconnected world design. Unfortunately, DS2 takes a more linear approach, which feels like a step backward. The lack of cohesion between areas is disappointing, and later games like DS3 didn’t improve upon this either.

Boss runs are another significant issue. Some are so tedious that they feel like a punishment rather than an engaging prelude to a challenge. The Frigid Outskirts epitomize this problem: a barren, frustrating area plagued by random snowstorms and relentless Frozen Reindeers that spam magic projectiles and spot the player from ridiculous distances. It’s the peak of poor design, where the developers seemed to misunderstand what makes a Souls game enjoyable.

Many times, the area leading to the boss is harder than the boss itself (Shrine of Amana and Iron Keep come to mind). This diminishes the satisfaction of overcoming a fight, as the buildup feels more tedious than rewarding. For me, the joy of Souls games comes from battling masterfully designed bosses, learning their movesets, and earning that triumphant dopamine rush. DS2 often undermines this.

Lore and Worldbuilding
The lore has its moments, particularly with Aldia and the ending of Scholar of the First Sin, which is intriguing and worth experiencing. However, while Aldia's role in the story is interesting, the developers completely ruined his boss fight. It feels uninspired, and defeating him gives you nothing of real value, which is a disappointing payoff for what could have been a memorable encounter. However, interactions with the old Firekeepers are far less engaging. Their dialogue, intended to immerse you in the world, comes across as pointless rambling. Mocking the player by constantly reminding you of your inevitable deaths feels more condescending than immersive, especially when anyone buying Dark Souls II knows exactly what they’re getting into.

Gameplay Mechanics: Flawed but Addictive
Healing in this game is poorly balanced. Lifegems, while useful, contribute to the overreliance on gank fights, making many encounters feel cheap rather than challenging. On the bright side, the weapon variety is excellent, offering plenty of options for different builds. I found myself rotating between at least three distinct playstyles, which helped keep the gameplay fresh and engaging as the game progressed.

Highlights: Beauty in the Flaws
Despite its shortcomings, Dark Souls II does have moments of brilliance. The hub area, Majula, is breathtaking, both visually and sonically. The soundtrack here is a masterpiece and provides a rare sense of peace amidst the chaos. Areas like Heide's Tower of Flame and Sir Alonne's boss arena are stunning and left me in awe when I first encountered them.

Final Thoughts: A Flawed Yet Compelling Journey
Ultimately, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is a game that gets better the longer you play. The beginning is undoubtedly its weakest point, and it takes considerable patience to push past its frustrating design choices. Over time, though, the experience builds into something more decent, even enjoyable—provided you're willing to overlook its glaring flaws.

Still, I can’t recommend this game to most players.I glanced over some issues like ADP stat and paying Human Effigy to fight Darklurker for the sake of keeping this review shorter. As someone who loves Souls games for their masterful bosses, interconnected worlds, and fair challenges, DS2 left me disappointed and dissatisfied with how FromSoftware handled its development. For die-hard fans, there’s still enjoyment to be found here. For everyone else, proceed with caution.

Dark Souls 2 doesn't deserve the hate that it gets. After grinding both Dark Souls 1 and 2, I can feel that they are completely different games. Since Dark Souls 2 was made by somebody different, it feels like a different game and you can tell that's what they were going for with all the new mechanics and combat changes such as the gradual heal over time from the flask and permanent loss of health after death. These features definitely don't belong in the Souls games as it ruins the flow of combat and punishes the player, as if dying and restarting from square one isn't punishment enough, Though mid to late game pretty much removes these issues as you should have a stockpile of items to help by that point. Finding the right time to heal in fights yet still being hit and dying because your flask hasn't actually healed you yet, is heartbreaking, and not to mention having to level up your dodge rolls invincibility frames, Yuck. Much like Dark Souls 1, this game is hardly enjoyable as its own game. If you've played other Souls like games then go ahead and play it, see what it's like. However, like i said in my last review, if you haven't played any Souls like games before but want to try them out, don't start with Dark Souls 1 or 2. That being said I do believe that Dark Souls 2 is better then Dark Souls 1 purely because of the fact that it has all the same issues as 1, just with more modern graphics and less glitches in the terrain.

I understand some of the complaints people have about this game. Most of the bosses are quite dull, the default controls are bad, and theres bugs here and there.

But other than this, most complaints boil down to people not planning ahead and running past a bunch of enemies, and then complaining when they have a lead of 20 hollows following them. This game requires you to strategize, and in this game you can never run past all enemies to get to a bonfire. Take your time, and you will be rewarded.

Another thing I have noticed is that many people who complain about this game refuse to change their playstyle. For example, a lot of people use melee-based build their first time around. I did that too when I first played it. What I am realizing on my second playthrough is that this game has an incredible amount of different builds you can make, thanks to the wide array of weapons, spells and charms, and being able to hold these in either hand which gives them different properties. Ignoring this variety and sticking to the safest option that they usually do in all souls game (im talking about the greatsword) is a recipe for disaster. The spellcasting alternatives can make this game a lot less frustrating in many areas. You dont even have to fully commit to a spellcasting playstyle, you can do a half-caster build to give you the best of both worlds. There is so much to be done with this vast collection of weapons and tools.

I really think most the complaints about this game are about people being stubborn, not that the game is inherently bad. There is valid criticism of the game as well of course, and it does have its issues.

Do I think this is a good game? Of course, its amazing if you play your cards right. Does it hold up to the other souls games? Debatable, but I think its an experience of its own, in a good way. If you are struggling to have fun with this game, I highly reccomend you change the way you play the game. It made me fall in love with the game again.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin - A Challenging, Imperfect Gem

When it comes to the Dark Souls series, fans are often split between which entry stands above the rest. While the first and third games in the series have rightfully earned their place as fan favorites, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin exists in a somewhat strange place. It’s often regarded as the black sheep of the Soulsborne family, and for good reason—out of all the Dark Souls games, this one feels like the least polished and, at times, the most frustrating. But despite its rough edges, it's still a remarkably compelling and rewarding experience. If you love punishing, methodical combat, intricate world-building, and a dark, melancholic atmosphere, this game is undeniably worth your time.

Story and Atmosphere

Dark Souls II is a game where the narrative is more about what you don’t know than what you do. The story is shrouded in mystery, and while that’s a hallmark of the series, here it feels more obscure than ever. The setting is the kingdom of Drangleic, a land cursed with an ever-deepening cycle of death and rebirth. While this concept isn’t inherently bad, the story’s structure feels fragmented. Unlike Dark Souls I, where the lore and story were more tightly woven into the gameplay, Dark Souls II leaves too many loose ends and abstract pieces for players to connect on their own. The heavy use of NPCs with cryptic dialogue doesn't help either—these characters don’t feel as fleshed out, and their motivations are often unclear, which can make the world feel less engaging.

But despite these narrative shortcomings, Scholar of the First Sin does an exceptional job with its atmosphere. Drangleic’s decaying castles, haunted forests, and cursed swamps are rendered in gorgeous detail, bringing the bleak, oppressive world to life. The art direction remains top-notch, and the environments are still some of the best in the series, filled with subtle, often eerie details that encourage exploration.

Gameplay and Mechanics

Where Dark Souls II falters most is in its gameplay mechanics. While the core of the Souls formula remains intact—challenging combat, exploration, and brutal difficulty—this game introduces several changes that feel more experimental and less refined. One of the most significant departures is the way it handles stamina and movement. While stamina management is crucial in all Souls games, Dark Souls II introduces a "stamina penalty" system that affects your character's stamina regeneration when wearing heavy armor. This can be a source of frustration for players, as it forces a greater reliance on lighter builds, which might not appeal to those who enjoy tankier characters.

Combat, while still satisfying, feels a little clunkier than the tight, responsive fighting in Dark Souls or Dark Souls III. The character animations sometimes lack the fluidity and polish of the other games, especially when it comes to weapon swings and dodging. There’s a certain awkwardness to the movement that never fully clicks for some players. The introduction of "hyper armor" on certain attacks also means that enemies can often trade hits with you in a way that feels unfair, particularly in the game's notoriously difficult boss encounters.

Dialogue and NPC Interactions

The dialogue and interactions with NPCs in Dark Souls II are often cryptic and less emotionally resonant compared to the other games in the series. While this fits with the game’s bleak tone, it also creates a sense of distance from the characters. Unlike Dark Souls I, where the characters felt like they were bound to the world’s tragedy, here, NPCs feel more like puzzle pieces scattered across the world, providing bits of lore without ever forming a deeper connection.

Combat

The combat system in Dark Souls II is undeniably one of the most challenging in the series, and in some ways, that’s a positive. The game throws some of the most punishing enemies and bosses at you, and success requires patience, precision, and a deep understanding of the game’s mechanics. However, it’s also where the game can become frustrating. The inconsistent hit detection, clunky animations, and difficulty spikes often lead to moments of pure rage. While Dark Souls II is by no means an unplayable game, it’s often the least enjoyable to play due to its sometimes chaotic combat mechanics.

Art Style

Where Dark Souls II shines is in its art style. The visual design is magnificent in terms of world-building and location variety. From the haunting ruins of Drangleic Castle to the unsettling atmosphere of the Shaded Woods, each area has its unique identity. The game’s attention to environmental storytelling is evident—every corner holds a piece of history, and the atmosphere consistently evokes a sense of despair and hopelessness. However, this beauty is often overshadowed by the game’s technical issues. There’s a certain murkiness to the visuals that feels inconsistent with the more polished worlds of its predecessors. Still, the art direction remains one of the game’s strongest points, even if the execution falters at times.

Conclusion: An Amazing Game, But the Weakest of the Souls Games

In the end, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin stands as a testament to the potential of the Souls series, but it is undeniably the weakest entry in the franchise. The game is rough around the edges—frustrating in its mechanics, clunky in its combat, and lacking the narrative cohesion of its predecessors. However, it still delivers the core Souls experience with relentless difficulty, deep world-building, and a palpable sense of loss and tragedy.

While it may be the most frustrating and imperfect of the Souls games, Dark Souls II is still an excellent game in its own right. The challenge it offers is unique, and its world is vast and filled with hidden secrets. For those who are willing to look past its flaws and embrace its difficulty, it can be an immensely rewarding journey. However, if you’re looking for the best Souls experience, you’re better off starting with Dark Souls or Dark Souls III, as they provide a more polished and refined version of what Dark Souls II aims to achieve.

Ultimately, Dark Souls II might not be the pinnacle of the series, but it’s still a remarkable game—imperfect, yet brilliant in its own way.

Please note that if you are trying to unlock all of Dark Souls 2 Scholar of the First Sin's achievements on steam you must ensure that your steam is in online mode, you do not need to play in the games own online mode only steam needs to be online.

I think this may be the weakest entry in the 'SoulsBorne' line of games.

Is it fine? Sure. Is it a GOOD game? Ehh...at best the answer is 'Yes, but it hasn't aged great'. Even the official screenshots above look more like something from an old Turok game. It has some design issues that its kin simply don't...To wit:

Combat feels rather clunky compared to other entries, regardless of your weapon or build. Enemy hitboxes and hurtboxes seem imprecise, sometimes to the point of jank. Frame data doesn't feel smooth, whether yours or theirs. Animations don't feel crisp compared to the first game, because they're not.

Difficulty, one of the big draws of the series, feels inconsistently applied here (usually in a broad sense, not within single areas). The DLCs are included, and are much, MUCH harder than the vanilla endgame content- To the point where it feels like it's trying to compensate. Ironically, doing any of the DLC before the vanilla finale will therefore make the latter feel comically, anticlimactically easy. However, there's also no real reward for the DLC otherwise- And it's also fully self-contained and feels quite separate from the rest of the experience. So you'll potentially feel unsatisfied regardless of whether or not you choose to do it.

It's a common criticism of the game but worth mentioning: Where the heck is all the enemy variety? Effectively every foe in the game is simply a humanoid, as opposed to the plentiful monster or 'unique' enemy designs from the other games. But more importantly than that, the bosses (humanoid or not) are all mechanically similar. You'll spend multiple entire areas fighting only humanoids, interpersed with bosses that are also highly interchangable. Welcome to 'Keep Your Distance Until The Boss Makes A Really Obvious Opening Simulator'.

Honestly, if you've not played the game already, you probably shouldn't. You're not missing anything meaningful, and the game lacks the polish, the variety or the satisfying combat dynamics that make the other entries in the series impactful.

Skip this one, play some Elden Ring with randomiser mods instead. You'll have a whole estus flask of fun.

I wish I could leave a mixed review.

This game does a bunch of things right. An interesting world and storyline. Great atmosphere. Faster more fluid combat than DS1.

It also has so many ideas that are poorly executed or just not fun. Mixing up the healing system so you have options in a fight is cool. Making healing take forever is not.

Having multiple enemies to fight brings the challenge up. The lockon and camera then become your enemies and absolutely screw you over at any possible opportunity.

Also this game has so much more of the worst aspects of dark souls. Poorly explained mechanics? Yep. Annoying weapon durability? Yep. Tricky puzzles that reward you with locking you in a cramped room with a mimic that drops garbage? Yep. Annoying areas full of poison or practically unavoidable damage? Welcome to the entire midgame.

The game has weak bosses, areas rivaling the most annoying areas of ds1, and half baked poorly executed mechanics. But despite that the world is still fun to explore. The combat still has that sense of dancing around enemies that leaves you feeling you could perfect any boss fight that would go on to be perfected in DS3 and Elden Ring.

Also annoying fromsoft quirks that don't make any sense being in a game from 2014. Why can I not rebind my controls if I'm using a controller? Why do I have to use my firewall to block the online servers if I want to play offline and still get steam achievements? WHY ARE THERE NO STEAM CLOUD SAVES TO SYNC BETWEEN MY STEAMDECK AND PC??? (It pulls up steam deck's keyboard on character creation so why only half support it??)

If all you want is more souls then this is worth a pickup. If you're here for the story/lore watch a long play on youtube and save yourself the $40

Posted: December 9, 2024
I don't know, man.
It has to be said from the outset that it's the weakest part of the series, the masses aren't wrong, even if a lot of it is a matter of taste.
DS II has some really good ideas, mechanics and aspects that are unique and really good. The first 60% of the game, at least, is also really good.
But after 80 hours and getting all the achievements, for the second time mind you after I had already done everything on Xbox, I just feel exhausted and am glad to be able to uninstall the game. This feeling wasn't as strong with the other parts. Just as there are many good things, there are also many bad things.
Pros:
- Incredible number of weapons, armour and items
- Well-structured world, varied environments and therefore many different enemy types
- Many different bosses
- Bonfire Ascetics mechanic
- 3 fat DLCs
- Good NPCs with good quests that are not absolutely absurd to solve and very good voiceacting & dialogue
- Magic is limited to use and there is no mana
- Good PVP
Cons:
- Many of the bosses are humanoid and design-wise not on the usual S class level
- Adaptility mechanics (better read up on this yourself)
- The game feels incredibly long
- Worldbuilding, enemy placement and boss run ups are sometimes extremely brutal and poorly designed
- DLCs are packed with content but often just surreally stupid and poorly built, not to mention unnecessarily unfair
- Hitboxes and delayed hit feedback are not as perfectly worked out as in other parts, often resulting in an unfair combat experience
- At a certain point in the story, the game feels unnecessarily drawn out
- Very disappointing final boss fight that makes little sense in terms of content structure
- I can only emphasise how disturbingly difficult, unfair and stupid many parts of the DLCs are, it just won't get into my head

All in all, it was still fun for the most part, it's Dark Souls. You get a hell of a lot for your money, especially in the sale. If you have the patience to bite your way through it, you're sure to feel great afterwards because the unfairness simply takes it to a different level of difficulty than the other parts, where you ‘just’ have to get better. If you want more Dark Souls and have already devoured the other parts, go for it.

The most hated, yet unique souls game out of 3 of them. Dark Souls 2 were made without supervision of Miyazaki, thus giving the impression of a bad game. Don't get me wrong, I acknowledge all the issues of the game, all the problems. It's not a bad game, it's just a different(I could say that it's even a broken game). I never played original, so I can't compare enemy placements between those two. Although, I can agree that Iron Keep and other places(Yeah, I'm looking at you Shrine of Amana) are infested with a bad enemy placements. But, this game has a unique way of telling you about the curse of undead, and how the one's who were affected by it were trying to solve the problem. I even surprised that this theme wasn't that persistent in othere games. Let's talk about bossfights! Alot of them aren't unique, and mostly just done bad and for the numbers. But technically they fixed that in dlc. The most unique things in this game? Bonfire Ascetic. I don't even understand why Miyazaki and Fromsoftware decided to left out the idea of reviving the bosses and returning items into the zone of bonfire that way. Maybe balance issues? In other games you just need to die to boss, to fight it again. But it isn't the same. Because when you revive the boss from ascetic it counts like a new game+. And if you underleveled, it's definitely going to be a hard fight because of incoming damage from the boss. Second thing is definitely weapons variety (Yeah, I know Elden Ring exist already, but mainly I'm talking about the trilogy). Powerstance is the most unique thing here. The fact that you can combo a two greatswords that deplete your stamina in few hits, but let you do a ton of damage is fun, yet risky. Let's talk about the negative side of the game again, shall we? Adaptability! Who in the right mind decided that your Iframes are dependable on the stat that you upgrade? Most of the times you see people complaining about weird hitboxes because of that issue and because of the weird animations (Another issue). Animations...Ah, the most confusing thing when people playing this game. You can see ton of examples like Sir Alonne grab. Basically what happens that people roll early or they don't have enough adp. You can clearly see that blade touched the character, but the animation plays it in different way like you rolled, but it's teleported your character right into the blade. Well... I can talk about this game negative and positive sides more and more. But do I recommend this game? Yes and no. If it's your fisrt souls game, you better to start from the 1 one because it's going to be unbelievably hard for you and could turn you away from the whole genre. If It's your second souls after the first one technically yes. But you will compare, and you will be angered by the decisions that were made in this game(Although the 2 part of the dark souls 1 exist, and I would even say that's it's even worse than whole ds2). But for me it's my favorite souls game out of trilogy. And I really recommend people to give it a chance!

Personal Rating: 9/10
Objective Rating: 5/10
I played DS1, DS3, and Elden Ring before DS2, and this one felt completely different. My first playthrough was honestly hell. The game doesn’t explain its systems well at all — I didn’t level up ADP because the game never tells you what it does, and that made everything feel sluggish. Estus drinking was painfully slow, dodges felt useless, and I died countless times to things that could’ve been avoided.

But once I learned how the mechanics actually work — with the help of guides — my second and third playthroughs were a completely different experience. DS2 hides a lot of secret systems and odd mechanics, and once you understand them, the game opens up beautifully. The death penalty mechanic was interesting at first, but got more annoying the more I played.

The bosses are mostly easy, though a few of the DLC ones stand out as real challenges. The level design can feel unfair in some spots, relying more on quantity than quality, but the atmosphere more than makes up for it. The art style and music blend perfectly — the world feels melancholic and haunting in a way even DS3 didn’t always achieve. Every location feels distinct and memorable.

After three playthroughs, I’ve come to really appreciate Dark Souls II. I now enjoy it more than DS1, but I still can’t recommend it to new players — it demands too much outside knowledge to be fun blind.

Not a bad game and the hate is undeserved, and the writing and story are great.

DS2 is experimental for a Dark Souls game and that comes with positives and negatives. The ADP stat was unnecessary, and enemy infinite aggro range can be difficult.. and some of the level design is sometimes questionable (earthen peak elevator), but the overall experience is positive and fun. There are also some great aspects for it being experimental that you don't get from other Dark Souls games; and after you get a feel for it, it plays really well and is satisfying without having to min/max: the combat is impactful and deliberate which is the point of a soulsborne. Plus, unless you join the champions covenant, enemies stop respawning after 7 kills which makes it far more accessible for first time/low skill players or if you're having difficulty with an area. Same with the lifegem items, which let you take your time with an area and save your estus flasks for the boss fight. All of these things tell you to take your time with an area. Unfortunately, this slower pace is not well communicated if you're coming from a faster Dark Souls, but when it came out, I think it would've been fine.

Also Majula is the best soulsborne hub

It is a slower game and if you're coming at it from just playing DS3 or Elden Ring, you'll feel underwhelmed until you get a better feel of the pace, but once you do, it's a lot of fun.

Posted: December 4, 2024
I've played DS1 ad nauseum, DS3 a few times, Elden Ring, loved them all. I decided to try DS2 despite everything I've read about it, and oh man do I ever understand the hate. I'm sorry DS2 fans, but yes, the bad is terrible.

The worst and most unforgivable aspect of this game is its hitboxes. With or without rolling, the hitboxes in this game for enemy weapons are HUGE and will hit you when it appears you're so very clear if them. You can be standing beside or even behind some enemies, but when they go to swing, the hitbox from their swords handle turns on, and if it's within a few feet of you, guess what, yeah, it hit you.

I could honestly look past everything else if it wasn't for the hitbox issue, but OMG its terrible.

Other brutal aspects of this game that might turn you off:
- Boss runs. You can't really dodge the enemies to get back to the boss. They chase you (actually forever) and then stun lock you in a very long fog wall animation. You have to kill them, each time. W... T... F...
- Your max health decreases every time you die. So every time you die, the boss gets even HARDER. There's objects you can farm to restore it, but who wants to go kill 100 BS enemies just so they can have a normal boss fight. It sucks.

Stuff that sucks but you can work with:
- They definitely go overboard on placing enemies in places that will jank you, but once you realize that's what they're going to do EVERYWHERE, you get used to it.
- Rolling. They nerfed your rolling until you put enough stats into it. It's not the end of the world and I can work with it, but since DS combat is designed around rolling, you notice it. Honestly once the stat gets up enough, rolling doesn't really save you either because you're perpetually inside the enemies MASSIVE F'N WEAPON HITBOX.
- The animations are SSSSLLLLLOOOO Yes, you can put points into a skill to make them normal, but again, why?

Anyway. I gave this one a go to see for myself.

So, I originally played this game back in 2017 and hated it more than anything in the world. I would constantly tell people that if you're going to play all the souls games, play DS2 last, then burn your copy. Flash forward to two weeks ago, and I was browsing social media when I came across a DS2 forum. Someone was complaining about how hard the game was, and how bullsh*t the healing and invicibility frames were. Most were the average get gud comment but a couple people said "Level up your adaptability to at least 24 then play through the game." I thought back on my original playthrough and realized I have NEVER leveled up my adaptability once. This had me thinking about doing another playthough, because I have already beat all 7 soulsborne games and got a Hunter Mark tattoo as tribute to the series and my experience playing the franchise, so what's one more go around? I purchased the game, farmed in Heide's Tower until my adaptability was 24 and played on the normal route. Now, I started playing the souls games when I was 15 back in 2016, so I've been a fan for almost 10 years. And with that, comes maturity as you grow with each game. Is beating Sekiro maturing? Yes. Is beating Elden Ring maturing? Yes. But you wanna know what above all is maturity?

Finding out Dark Souls 2 is a f*****g MASTERPIECE of a game after absolutely dunking on it for years of my life.

I cannot stress to you how phenonemal of a souls game DS2 really is. There's so much breath-taking scenery in the various locations of the game (except for like Black Gulch and ig Grave of Saints), the weapons/combat is insanely well-put together, albeit some frames being janky but hey thats souls for ya, and some of the coolest bosses in the souls line-up. Playing through recently, I forgot how cool Looking Glass Knight's fight actually is. Burnt Ivory King is such a cinematic sick looking fight and inspired some boss entrances down the line(Yes, I know Four Kings from DS1 has the same drop down entrace). The lore is so cool when you read item descriptions, talk to the npcs, I mean I didnt even know Tark or Vengarl existed until this playthrough. The DLC worlds are so sick as they're like extensions of other areas like Brume Tower. The soundtrack. MY GOD THE SOUNDTRACK. Nashandra's theme got me praisin' the lord with that holy choir.

Now, there are still some grips I have with the game but its ALOT less than I had before. My biggest one and most commonly amongst the community is the boss run backs are atrocious, but some aren't bad at all. Lud and Zallen come to mind immediately as a garbage run back. Another for me was how heavy the fall damage is in the game. You could drop from like dresser height and break your f*****g ankles. There are a few others but I'm not trying to write a college paper here.

All in all, I'm glad I came back to DS2. It was like playing a whole new souls game and I'm always down for that. The spark of joy I got from going "Oh I remember that!" and remeniscing back to when I played before has given me a deep sense of nostagia towards DS2 that the others just can't replicate for me. My order of favorites use to be: Bloodborne, Elden Ring, Sekiro, DS3, DS1, DS, then DS2. But now, I can confidently say that DS2 has over thrown the #4 spot and the spot for my favorite souls game specifically. It really feels amazing to now have a series so close to my heart where I can feel that not a single one of the games are bad.

Anyways, I've rambled for long enough. If you ever see it on sale, pick it up. It's definitely a challenge compared to DS1 and DS3(LKSS go brrrrrr), but its a unique one that will stick with you after you look back at the journey you took. I've gone from a Dark Souls 2 hater to a Dark Souls 2 glazer and no game has made me do that before.

I love you, Fromsoft. Never change.

ds2 is a very well designed game, with some obvious flaws with experiments with their game design. this is seen in some areas late game, plus the dlcs. the agility stat being a horrible addition to the game, where you pretty much NEED to level it up or else you'll get hit by every attack.

most areas in ds2 were actually designed really well, i only really got stuck on one for hours (no it's not shrine of armana) it was iron keep! seriously fix enemy placements im begging you, the run to smelter demon was absolutely cancer as i found a way of getting there without getting hit. but it only worked like 20% of the time, so i found myself doing runbacks for an hour, dying around 20 times to the lava while reaching the boss maybe 15 times. (for some reason i'm horrible at dodging his attacks)

the dlcs in ds2 were really horrible, i know loads of people love ds2 for the dlcs and most people say they have the best bosses in the game. which is partially true, fighting fume knight was very very fun! but i found myself not wanting to do the optional bosses in them or even the dlc at all, just because of the horrible runbacks and horrible design in some of these areas. using a memory to get to sir alonne was a horrible idea, i wish it just placed me in the arena rather than doing a runback with those fire lizard and iron keep knights. the final boss of the crown of the ivory king dlc was a horrible design too, (i didn't finish the boss because of the horrible fight before it) being forced to fight a ton of enemies before the final boss was a horrible horrible idea, i'd rather have a 4 minute runback than be forced to fight the final boss with 4 flasks.

the thing this game did really well (once getting used to it), was the combat! i loved fighting enemies in this game, the dodging when you get enough agility was really nice. the speed of the combat in ds2 was insanely good! it's got the style of ds1 combat where you need to read your enemies to dodge properly, but quicker which was really really nice. i love the quicker combat in the souls games, but this felt really nice to fight in!

i love how this game sort of plays like ds1, but with a more linear style like ds3. It's so nice being allowed to fight tons of different bosses at any one point in the game while not getting lost like i always did in ds1.

i would DEF recommend this game to anybody who enjoys the souls series, coming from someone who came into this game expecting to hate it i was pleasantly surprised with how good ds2 was.

Dark Souls™ II: Scholar of the First Sin – A Deeper Dive into Darkness

Dark Souls™ II: Scholar of the First Sin takes the original Dark Souls II experience and elevates it with refined mechanics, a reimagined world layout, and expanded content, solidifying its place as a worthy installment in the Souls series. It’s a challenging, atmospheric, and deeply rewarding adventure that rewards patience, curiosity, and skill.

Enhanced Visuals and Performance
The Scholar of the First Sin edition boasts significant graphical improvements, with enhanced lighting and textures that bring the kingdom of Drangleic to life. From the foreboding majesty of Majula to the haunting beauty of the Shaded Woods, every locale feels richer and more immersive. The game runs at a steady 60 FPS, making combat smoother and the world more vibrant on modern systems.

Expanded Content and Tweaked World Design
One of the most notable changes is the rearrangement of enemy placements and item locations, creating a fresh experience even for those familiar with the original. This reimagining increases the challenge and forces players to rethink strategies, adding layers of tension and surprise to exploration. The inclusion of all three DLC packs—Crown of the Sunken King, Crown of the Old Iron King, and Crown of the Ivory King—provides a massive amount of additional content, each offering unique environments, lore, and brutal boss fights.

Refined Gameplay
Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin retains the hallmark gameplay mechanics of the series: deliberate, methodical combat that rewards patience and adaptability. The stamina management, weapon variety, and spell diversity offer countless ways to approach the game, encouraging creativity in builds.

While some combat animations and hitboxes have a slightly different feel compared to the first and third entries, the tweaks to enemy AI and the addition of new challenges keep the experience engaging. The increased enemy aggressiveness and clever ambushes amplify the sense of danger, ensuring players remain on their toes.

A Richer Multiplayer Experience
Multiplayer shines in Scholar of the First Sin with enhanced online connectivity, allowing up to six players in a single session. Whether you’re summoning allies to tackle a tough boss, battling invaders in tense PvP, or joining covenants to gain unique perks, the multiplayer aspect is more robust and dynamic than ever.

Atmosphere and Storytelling
Drangleic’s crumbling world is steeped in melancholic beauty, with a deeper focus on themes of loss, ambition, and the cyclical nature of power. The cryptic storytelling, conveyed through item descriptions, environmental details, and fragmented NPC dialogue, invites players to piece together its mysteries. Combined with a hauntingly evocative soundtrack, the game’s atmosphere lingers long after the controller is set down.

A Challenge Worth Undertaking
Dark Souls™ II: Scholar of the First Sin isn’t just a remaster—it’s a reimagining that enhances and expands the original while maintaining its punishing difficulty and sense of wonder. It rewards those who are willing to persevere through its labyrinthine world and demanding foes.

Rating: 9/10
Scholar of the First Sin takes an already ambitious title and makes it richer, darker, and more rewarding. Whether you’re a Souls veteran or new to the series, this is a journey you don’t want to miss. Prepare to die… again and again.

Posted: November 30, 2024
Dork souls 2 is the hardest game of them all in franchise when it comes to clearing areas of enemies. Unfortunately volume of difficulty is high as ever.

I have enough and I give up on playing it so here are reasons why I hate DS2 with passion.

-Early game player must fight 3 Ruin Sentinels at once, this is unbelievable, not just there are 3 bosses at once the space to fight them is not big enough and they tend to spin which result in getting caught in that spin.

-Too many enemies too fight in one space, you barely killed one guy there are two others coming out of nowhere.

-Lock on actually manages to kill the player so playing with it is more dangerous than playing without, because it keeps switching enemies not from the one you just killed to closest but for some reason to the one that is more far behind, the fok?

-Which leads to combat, - It is slow Jesus Christ! Can't even tell if the enemy can even be parried or not because how slow it gets when they attack, most of enemies tend to hit through shield and take away all your stamina, seriously? What is the point of shields then?

-Backstabs no longer works like in DS1, even if the player is behind the enemy it somehow does not let you to backstab it. Also backstabing no longer grants player I-frames so just in case you got lucky with backstab you most likely is going to be killed by rest of the enemies that you fight.

-There are also 2 enemy types (one of them is from DLC) which cannot be locked on and you must whiff your hits trying to attack it and enemies that can be locked on but are completely invulnerable.

-Iron Keep area is rage inducing place and player must defeat around 20 black knights just to get to the boss in fact distance from bonfire to the Smelter Demon (Boss) is about less than minute, but because of these enemies are so close one to each other you will spend about 30 minutes or so just to get cleared area before you can safely run to boss area, which leads to:

-Entering fog wall no longer provides invulnerability, thats right, if you think running pass enemies and then entering fog wall will solve this issue, think again.

-Dying in DS2 is a cruel punishment because once your character has died, your max hp gets reduced and if you say use effigies then i have to tell you, effigies are very limited, once they all are used you cant get them out of thin air.

-Hitboxes, everything has been reduced to its minimum, you know that in DS1 or DS3 once you know you will hit a titanite lizard then it will receive damage? Here is no such thing, 75% off the time your weapon will whiff and lizard will just run away or if they cant run away the will teleport out, usually hitting the lizard it would delay the time for it to disappear but not here. And if the lizard somehow falls off the cliff into abyss it does not count as kill and player does not receive the loot.

-Boss that can potentially summon another Boss from a base game.

-Fighting Boss can actually break your weapon which is insane because this was never an issue in DS1 or DS3.

-Exploding enemies that will break your gear including rings (yeah, rings).

-Confusing level designs, how on earth casual player can know that DLC key is located at the beginning of game behind closed door. Place that have been forgotten long time ago since player is already in end game? What were they thinking?

Fromsoft will make the game unforgiving for player while making game extremely forgiving for the enemies.
This is what happens when Miyazaki did not work on this game. DS2 is a game with horrible designs and I hate it.
