Continuing on with my first time ever all Souls games playthrough, we are coming to the "black sheep" of the franchise, as most say.

And honestly... I don't get it. I mean, I kinda do get it, but still don't. In most ways, it actually improves a lot from the first game. But there are some major issues in my opinion that really drags the game and the whole experience down. Lets go through it.

The good:

- Visually, it's a good looking game, even all these years later.
- Majula and the concept of the "main hub". It's visually beautiful and so calming, the music is just so relaxing and depressing at the same time, I just love it.
- Game is huge, and some of the zones are awesome designed (Earthen Peak Iron Keep, Black Gulch, Shrine of Amana, Heide, Lost Bastille, Drangleic Castle few examples). Some of the vistas are breathtaking.
- Weapons, armor (fashion mostly), spells, gameplay in that context and variety of choices.
- Power stancing.
- Some of the bosses are just awesome (The Pursuer, Looking Glass Knight, Flexile Sentry, Mytha, Smelter Demon, Velstadt, Vendrick, Darklurker). Both the fights and even some of their OST's (just listening to Velstadt OST as of writing this review).
- DLC's are for the most part so good and well made, having one of the best bosses (including the best one in the whole game, Sir Alonne, that fight is amazing and I wish I could replay it over and over without starting the game again).
- I love the story and history behind some bosses. Vendrick is depressing for me. Whole vibe feels like descent into madness, especially some of the npc storylines.
- NG+ being actually different and bonfire ascetics are genius idea!

So you would think, judging by the review so far, where are the issues? Well:

- Game is huge, but at the same time it feels bloated and overstays its welcome sometimes, because some of the zones are crap design vise and make no sense placement vise, it feels like they just put in as much as possible to make the game "bigger". It doesn't have near as much connectivity and the superb level design as first game, which is like peak for me.
- It's sad that armor is almost useless, might as well go naked, it will be easier. It's purely cosmetic. It barely made any difference when I took damage with or without it.
- Movement. This one is for me the biggest reason why game sucks. When I first started to play, I immediately felt something was wrong. My character was moving like it was floating, it was super janky at first. I got used to it eventually, but yeah, it's not good.
- ADP as stat is just pure garbage and total waste of time and souls. I won't talk about it because there are youtube essays about it and why it sucks so feel free to check those out.
- Hit boxes are sometimes horrendous, even if they help you out sometimes, it's still stupid.
- Some of the bosses are just ass: Executioner's Chariot, The Skeleton Lords, Belfry Gargoyles (literal repeat from the first game, just add them up to 4 at the same time!!!), Royal Rat Authority, Old Dragonslayer (lol), Covetous Demon (lmao), Guardian Dragon (mob acting as "boss"), Demon of Song (cool design at least), both Dragonriders, Prowling Magus (mob trash fight), Giant Lord, and final boss Nashandra sucks so hard it's not even funny. The others I didn't mentioned are ok.
- Crown of the Ivory King DLC has one of the worst bosses and run backs I've ever seen in a video game. It's so bad that I never actually played it, I heard stories about it, saw the video and said "f*** that!". Ain't wasting my time on that. While we are at it, 2 more runbacks in DLC's are almost as horrible (Smelter Blue and Alonne). You can add there Elana run back IF you don't find the nowayinhellyoucanfindthathiddenbonfirewithoutguideorwrittenmessage. Absolute bonkers.
- Now this is regarded to SOTFS edition, but they added BUNCH of enemies in all of the zones, and some of them make ZERO sense why are they there at all. Zero thought put into the design, just make it "more = harder" I guess.
- Lifegem. I know, don't use it then. But why put it in the game as unlimited item to buy? I mean, yeah I have no problem with it being in the game as limited item, but to add npc that can sell it for unlimited amount for dirt cheap + you can stack them up to 99, is nuts and makes the game super easy.
- If you are coming from the first game, you will not like the Estus flask overall system (need shards to have them more, slow HoT instead of instant heal, can have up to 12 max). Was off put at first, but I like it now, it adds to the challenge.
- Actually, I take back the controls... The worst, I mean the absolute worst part about this game? The insufferable, annoying and awful Dark Souls II "defenders". When you say you don't like something, and they'll write an essay of actually why you're wrong, it's the best game ever and say in the end "git gud". I'm lurking subreddits daily and I see them often, cancer.

Yet again, I had fun... crazy, right? I actually had so much fun, that I bought the ORIGINAL version and completed all the achievements AGAIN (this time much faster because I was experienced). This game just has something in it, I can't explain it. It has problems, but as an overall package, it delivers in my humble opinion and I recommend it to everyone!

Despite being quite underwhelming when compared to its predecessor, this game still shines due to the huge build diversity it offers, in addition to the whole Bonfire Ascetic mechanic and NG+ changes well explored by the developers.

However, if asked 10 times which one to get between this game and DS1, 11 times DS1 would be answered. DS2's (and especially SOTFS') combat feels extremely clunky when compared to the original Dark Souls, despite being released some 3 years later, and this gets exacerbated even more in the SOTFS version, where the developers opted to drastically amp up the number of enemies encountered (compared to the original DS2) instead of cleverly re-designing these encounters.

As a result, DS2: SOTFS brings extremely mixed feelings every single time it gets started. On the one hand, there is some kind of amazement due to the aforementioned diversity and uniqueness it transpires, though on the other hand actually playing the game usually kills this amazement due to all the problems previously detailed, irrespective of which version of the game is being played. Still, playing this game can remain a valuable experience for the sake of series' completion, though it is commonly accepted that the previous and subsequent entries in the series deliver a far superior experience.

Posted: November 27, 2024
I landed on not recommending this game, but I went back and forth on it alot. The first time I played Dark Souls 2 was in 2021. I played it for 2 4 hour sittings and never wanted to touch it again. But after enjoying Elden Ring so much I decided to commit to playing the souls trilogy (plus Bloodborne), I then finally gave this game a fair chance, played to the credits and even finished the DLC.

I simultaneously feel glad I did play this while also regretting doing so.

This game hits some highs but it also hits some of this series lowest lows.The issue lies in the fact that this game feels overcooked and undercooked at the same time. This game experiments with a lot of interesting changes, but it also changes some mechanics for what feels like just for the sake of it. There’s a lot of the obvious problems that people have pointed out here thousands of times. ADP requirement, enemy aggro range, inconsistent boss quality, the list goes on and on. For everything it does wrong, however, I feel like it does something right. Graphics and presentation are just as good as Dark Souls 1, power-stancing is a lasting feature that you can still do in Elden Ring even, magic was heavily expanded, again, the list goes on and on.

I look at this game as necessary growth. The absurd amount of mechanics changed did allow the Fromsoft team to learn what works and what doesn’t. Plus the quality of the DLCs is consistently good outside of one key area in the third expansion. I can definitely understand people who still love this game despite all of its problems, while also completely agreeing with people who absolutely despise this game. When it’s good, It’s REALLY GOOD, the quality you would expect from Dark Souls. But when it’s bad, it’s REALLY BAD, incomprehensibly so.

If you want to see the evolution of this series, or want the gamer status of surviving through this rollercoaster ride, give it a try. You might be the one of many who see past its flaws, even. But if you’re looking for high quality gameplay and something to scratch that Soulslike itch after playing all of Elden Ring, don’t bother. You won’t find it here.

I was playing this game with the hope that eventually it will all click in together. When that hope came crushing down by the undeniable reality of how irrelevant this lore is, I was so bitter that none of the story was enjoyable any more. I can not look upon it in a favorable way no matter what. It seems absolutely nonesensical.
The only part of the story I enjoyed was the DLCs, every single one of them were great. That is mainly because they are mostly disconnected from the main game.

Combat is a clear downgrade from Dark Souls 1 in most ways, alas, power-stance
I couldn't get over how they ruinned parry for a long while, instead of momentarily throwing enemies in an open stance after parrying their attack and throwing them off guard to deliver a riposte, you have enemies lose their balance and fall backwards, waiting into a birth-giving pose for you deliver to a downwards diagonal stab in their crotch(?!).
Most weapons are chunkier and feel as if you hit play on a standard video and just watch it play for a while.
Evade frames are based on a stat now, if you don't level ADP you're doomed.
Estus-drinking speed is based on a stat now, if you don't level ADP you're doomed.
Even if you do level ADP, you drink your estus SO SLOWLY and, worst part, your HP is periodically gained in about 1.2 seconds. That means you can die after drinking Estus and gaining only a portion of the HP by the time an attack connects. Even if 100% of the Estus would push you above one-shot levels.
Was so excited by Dark Sorcery only to be let down by how stupid their system is and how weak they are.

It's not all negative, though.
First of all, . Loved this addition.

Spells still work with charges.
New spells and pyromancies kinda dope.

The DLCs were awesome in setting and story. Navigating through them felt unique. Very hard too.
It's another Dark Souls, theoretically, so if you loved Dark Souls 1/3 I guess it's worth going through it just to say you've seen 'em all. That's why I 100% it.

I have played the Dark Souls games on other platforms, which is why it says there's only 45 minutes on PC. I did not play on PC because DS2 will require some configuration of files and settings to get the controls to feel good, but if you are willing to do a little configuration playing on PC is great.

Dark Souls 2 is a maligned entry in the Fromsoft ouvre, but I think that that reputation is undeserved. Dark Souls 2 deviates from the previous and subsequent entries in interesting ways, has a combat system that feels good, and a expansive scope that feels wider than any of the other games in the series. There are valid criticisms, but that's what makes this series feel like an intimate dialog with the world and the designers. There is that special Fromsoft-Sauce here, but it's a unique flavor that you have to develop a palette for. If you can set aside preconceptions about the mechanics of the other two Dark Souls and enjoy what's on the plate for what it is, Dark Souls 2 is one of the best games of all time. If, however, you expect it to be in continuity with Dark Souls 1 or 3, you will be working against your own best interest.

Posted: November 25, 2024
Dark souls 2, Let me tell you. This....
is not your typical dark souls game. Everyone else will tell you it's not even dark souls at all and they would be right. In fact, this was my first dark souls game in which I played on the Xbox 360 before I eventually had to return it to the library out of anger. Dark souls 2 is a game in which you play as the chosen undead, ready to go ahead and save the world! (or desert it.) Except if you've played dark souls games before, you know that you'll probably be struggling for a while. The game itself is very hard and if you aren't accustomed to soul-likes/souls-borne you'll probably find this game to be hair-tearing. Not only that but this dark souls game in particular is a walk off the path of the typical dark souls play book. Instead of being held by your hand to teach you how the game is played, you're actually given options to how you actually want to play. Don't need a tutorial? You can do that. Need a refresher? The game rewards you for going through all of that. Dark souls 2 is an open experience in which you aren't locked into a singular path to becoming the chosen undead, instead of being locked into one singular area or boss in the beginning, you can choose which path you wish to travel in order to suit your needs and skill level. Not only that, your character can be any class you wish to choose, aside from a few classes that are..quite frankly useless. There's also a simple yet interesting feature added to this dark souls game, life gems. A resource that allows you for the price of one or a few, be the key factor to beating a difficult boss or rush through a difficult section without needing a checkpoint or a bonfire in order to recharge your "health potions". The bosses are challenging, often unfairly if you're unsure of what you're doing but the game does have ways to allow you to improve to better allow you to proceed. A tough enemy ruining your perfect run? If you defeat that enemy a certain number of times, they disappear entirely. The game would be easier with a walkthrough or a guide for beginners as this game is a far cry from the first and third games. Another thing I should mention is that upgrading your weapons will make the game easier, don't waste your time hording ores as you progress as it'll only make the enemies and the bosses much harder to beat. Most people will tell you to skip this game but once you play through it, (after a long break for the rage to settle down.) you'll want to come back and try a new build, go a new path or even play alongside some friends or duel them to the death. Dark souls 2 isn't everyone's favorite game but it's mine.

Posted: November 24, 2024
Dark Souls II was my first experience into the Soulsborne franchise back in 2014, I'd sunk countless hundreds of hours into it on the PS3. When Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First sin released in 2015 on the PS4 I went and sunk countless of thousands of more hours into it before I moved to PC in 2018 where I've sunk another few thousand hours into this game, needless to say it's one of my favourite games of all time.

The hub area known as Majula has got to be one of the best and most atmospheric hubs in the whole of the Soulsborne Franchise. It has a warm and welcoming feel, with the music in the background being slow, calm and relaxing, really reinforcing how much of a safe space the area truly is in comparison to the other hostile areas throughout the game. The Hub is also home to our favourite Waifu: The Emerald Herald, Shanalotte.

The levelling system while introducing a new mechanic called Adaptability was not all that bad, unlike Resistance in Dark Souls I, Adaptability has an actual use. That being that it increases the speed that you use consumables like your Estus Flasks but it also increases your IMMUNITY FRAMES, a thing that most new players tend to not know and complain that the game is too hard because the dodges feel impossible to do which in the early game is rough, but spending a few points into Adaptability fixes the dodging issue entirely.

Another controversial mechanic introduced is Soul Memory, matchmaking based on how many souls you've collected along your journey, I assume this was intended to stop twinking (The act of making a low level character intended for competitive PVP or COOP.) but this is also counteracted by the Agape ring, a ring that prevents souls from being earned by any means, unfortunately twinking is still common, at the cost of a ring slot.

The story is much like the other Soulsborne titles, where you can follow it at a baseline and if you're truly invested in it you can talk to NPC's, explore areas or even look into the item's descriptions that you collect for a deeper understanding of areas, enemies and bosses. You do not need to be an expert to understand and enjoy these games by any means.

Fashion Souls is an important part of any Soulsborne title and this one certainly delivers on that front, a lot of armors working well together, colours of fabrics, tints of metals, weapons complimenting said outfits, a personal favourite combination of mine being:







And







PVP is honestly some of the best in the entire franchise in my opinion, with plenty of variety in weapons that you can choose to powerstance, armor combinations to pick from and mechanics to compliment your playstyle; ranging from simply backstepping through attacks to animation cancelling and animation recovery through certain actions. There are also plenty of ways to parry from shields to weapons to tools like shields and the sound from achieving a parry being like music to your ears. A personal favourite parry to achieve is the great sword parry.

In conclusion, Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin holds a special place in my heart and I cannot recommend it enough, I'd rate it a 10/10 and advocate that any fan of the genre who hasn't tried it yet to give it a chance.

Posted: November 22, 2024
Dark Souls II is a great game covered in ♥♥♥♥. The game has many interesting or cool ideas, but most of the time they're executed poorly. That's the most frustrating part about this game.

The very first thing I noticed was how weird or off the combat and movement felt when I played, and that persisted the entire way through. The 8-direction movement in the game is too restricting, it makes controlling the player character feel stiff. There is no impact or weight behind anything other than firebombs. Many attacks just lack a wind-up both in bosses and enemies like those spear guys. Many, many bosses have bugged out animations or attacks e.g. the Pursuer model bugs out when you get too close to him, making his wind up wonky.

The areas are huge, filled with many enemies but still feel empty somehow. And in the areas that are narrow or tight, it genuinely becomes annoying. It's fine on initial runs, going and killing all the enemies, but many of the boss runs are very long and have many enemies in the way making it just tedious. Also making the bosses feel worse than they are. This is my main issue with the game's level design. The enemy placements are awful. Enemies also have Superman vision. They can see you from waaaay too far away and then bumrush you immediately since their running speed is insanely fast. Hell, it's faster than their leg animations. Enemies also have aim lock for some reason. They aim one place then slide their feet like the floor is ice at the last second and hit exactly where the player is. It's pretty dumb. To add to level design issues, a couple of areas have infuriating designs. The two biggest for me were Black Gulch and Shrine of Amana. I don't think a game has made me this frustrated with its design choices for at least half a decade. Those two areas made me want to bash my head into my monitor.

Of course, one of the most common complaints about Dark Souls 2 are the hitboxes. I'm mixed on it personally, there are times when it has good hitboxes, times it feels a little weird or wonky, and then many times when it's just straight-up bad. This can mainly be seen with larger bosses like the awful, awful Rotten fight, but I've seen it happen with Looking Glass Knight, Velstadt, and Old Dragonslayer to name a few. They tend to have lingering or inflated hitboxes.

Some things I liked were the overall lore and story. I found it interesting for the most part but wish the flavour text for items were better. The music was also pretty good. The best thing about the game however is the number of great builds as well as the genuinely impressive weapon variety. I had the most fun with this part of the game. The thing that bums me out about them though is that they don't particularly mesh well with the overall game (areas, bosses, etc). Making the system fall short of its full potential.

Some other weird things I noticed were the kinda silly cutscene direction and the ambience/music cutting out when any other sound effect plays like boss death sound, player death sound, and weapon switching.

Most of the things I mentioned were negatives or complaints but I really feel this game has a lot of potential. If any Souls game could use a remake, it's this one. Because I feel it could genuinely be amazing if they change and fix its many issues. It's needed much more than the absolutely useless Demon's Souls "remake".

5.5/10

This game is hated by many, but it’s definitely my favorite Souls game of all time. I know it has many flaws, but what makes me love this game is its overall artistic direction. Everything, from the aesthetics to the level design, creates a unique experience in the world of gaming.

It’s as if this game is, for me, a valuable lesson in how to deal with depression. I’ve lived with depression for many years, and Dark Souls 2 taught me that life is like that—we’ll fall, we’ll get up, and we’ll keep fighting. We don’t need an easy mode, we don’t need life to get easier. All we need is to learn to keep pushing forward, to be resilient, and to believe that one day we’ll overcome all the challenges life throws at us.

This game is incredibly beautiful, even if many don’t see the beauty beyond the graphics, it’s there. A work of art that sparks deep reflections in those who persist on the journey and teaches us a lot through our mistakes.

More than recommended, it’s a necessary experience for building character.

TLDR: It's better than you think.

I think this game gets waaay too much hate for what it's worth, the art is still really cool and cruising through the world is mesmerizing as any other FromSoft game.

The controls are sometimes clunky, but you get used to them in the first hour of gameplay.

The story is cool, and the enemies are intriguing. Actually, normal enemies hit even harder compared to other titles in the saga, so i found this one way more challenging than DS1 and DS3. Luckily, they stop respawning after the 12th death, and it's kinda cool to observe and traverse the empty map.

The only negative note that i have to give, since we are being completely honest, is that... the bosses are kinda bad. Some are reeeeally bad. Like, "stomp stomp wait"-moveset bad.
But what can you do, we all know it's not the best Souls game, but it's not nearly as disastrous as some friends painted it to me before i played it.

I found it part of of the journey, if you will. And in case you are playing the Souls saga in order, finishing this will make you love DS3 even more. Just enjoy the ride!

Posted: November 16, 2024
This review is written only after a single playthrough. I have not played on ng+, and can't speak on anything past normal ng.

I can say without a single doubt in my head that this game is incredibly overhated. DARK SOULS II™: Scholar of the First Sin is not a bad game, and takes bold steps for the series that are 100 percent in the right direction. Does the game miss the mark sometimes? Yes, absolutely, I think its level design is a pristine example of that (and really my only major gripe for the game) but I don't think its flaws are too overbearing on its overall value.

Compared to the original DARK SOULS™, the game's combat and combat system feels much smoother and nicer to play with. I have no issues saying DARK SOULS™' combat felt slow, clunky and kinda bad. Not to rag on the first Dark Souls, I think it's a masterpiece with its flaws, but only being able to roll in 4 directions and run in maybe 2 (iirc) while locked on made fighting things feel very difficult (and that's if you were even using lock on lol)
In Its sequel, however, you're given a glimpse as to what combat in the following games would look like. Attack animations feel less floaty in DARK SOULS II™ compared to its predecessor. DARK SOULS™ felt almost turn-based with its combat, and DARK SOULS II™ certainly takes off in the right direction when it comes to making fights flow and feel better.

There's a lot more freedom when it comes to building your character in this game. In the original DARK SOULS™, you had to be meticulous as to what stats you put your levels into. There was no way of changing your stat allocation, and in general souls were harder to come by. In DARK SOULS II™ there are many options when it comes to gaining and farming souls and equipment (mainly via bonfire ascetics, a feature I am sorely disappointed did not return in future installments of the series) and reallocating stat points is almost available from the get-go.

This plays in nicely with its wider build variety. All in all, there's more toys in DARK SOULS II™, and lots for many different and general builds and types of play. It feels hard to lock yourself out of cool new options when finding something new, especially with how forgiving leveling is in this game, not mentioning being able to powerstance weapons (A fun new addition to the game I unfortunately never got to play around with much). The amount of content this game has is insane, a first playthrough easily nets you ~80 hrs of playtime across most of the main game and all of its DLCs. With how the game is structured (being twice as linear as the original DARK SOULS™) you would figure that the game would be shorter, but compared to the roughly 50 hrs or so spent playing my first run of DARK SOULS™ I have roughly 90 hours on its sequel.

I think it's time to get to my issues with the game, though. DARK SOULS II™ is far from perfect.

I won't say more content an entirely good thing. This game overstays its welcome just a little bit, and the last, I'd say, quarter of the game felt like a drag to go through, but I feel like that's a byproduct of being more or less done with the game's character progression at that point, and finding new ways to better your character becomes less attainable and the time between finding something new and exciting becomes longer.

This isn't helped by my main issue with DARK SOULS II™, It's level design and worldbuilding. DARK SOULS™ felt like a world you inhabited, finding new and exciting things was the rule, not an exception, and it was incredibly easy to immerse yourself in the game's exploration with how interconnected the levels were, and how for probably 70% of the game you didn't have access to fast travel, which made you feel like your location relative to other things was actually important to take not of.

DARK SOULS II™ has none of that. Levels aren't woven together like the first game, and they feel like just that. Levels. There isn't really a need for immersion, the game's setpieces feel more like setpieces and less like actual places with real significance. I think the Lord Souls are the best example of this, having a linear path straight to one big baddie that you fight and at the end you're rewarded with a bonfire that just warps you back to the main area, and the game does this 4 times. There's a lot of warping in this game, and while it's convenient it doesn't give you any sense that you're traversing an actual world. And really that's my only huge problem with DARK SOULS II™, in the first installment of the series I felt a deep desire to go back and find some new thing. Be it a place, a weapon, a new set of armor or a ring or some other upgrade I always had the feeling I'd stumble onto something new, and in the process of doing that I'd stumble into something different as well. I always WANTED to explore DARK SOULS™' game world, but that drive was sorely missed in its sequel.

That being said, a lot of people complain about things that are non-issues or just overblown.

The enemy placement is, weird, but not irredeemable. Critics of the game love to point out just how "ganky" the experience is, but taking an actual thoughtful approach to enemy encounters 99% of the time will net you a normal experience and the results you expect. If you plan on running through all of the encounters, you are going to get mobbed plain and simple. That is how the game is designed, and with this in knowledge I want to say that the runbacks in this game also aren't as bad as people say they are (with exceptions, of course).There's just the right amount of bonfires, too. With DARK SOULS™ having too little and DARK SOULS III™ having too many, I think the placement of checkpoints is spaced out just fine enough to not be too forgiving but not strain the player out too much at the same time. With the addition of life gems, you can save your estus for important encounters like bossfights, a change I welcome.

As a lover of the fromsoft souls genre, I think people should give this game a fair shot and play it with an open mind. This game, to me, is a solid 8/10 and my enjoyment was at 7/10 pretty much the whole time until the end, and doesn't at all deserve the sheer amount of hate and stigma it currently has.

If you're looking to get into DARK SOULS™ as a franchise, I actually recommend starting with this one first. This game is good, but it isnt the original DARK SOULS™ and I think playing it with the mindset that its supposed to be like the first game leaves a lot of people with the wrong impression. DARK SOULS II™, mechanically, has aged better than the first game. You won't get hit with the "Oh this definitely feels like it was made in 2011" with the control scheme and general feel of the game. Don't worry, you're not figuring anything out about the story unless you have youtube video essays describe it to you, so major story beats will make no sense regardless. Tis' the fromsoft tradition.

Overall, this game is overhated and only marginally underrated. This game is good, and after playing it I can safely say most of the stigmas people have of this game come from other people who thought of it poorly before them. I think more people need to give this game a chance, because it definitely is NOT as bad as so many people make it out to be.

Bearer of the Curse... Let me say, i did go into this game negative af. Seen a lot of vids on yt on why this is the worse game in the series. I'll say this, while some if not a lot of criticism it true ESPECIALLY how many gank squads in here and some awful hitboxes, and of course leveling ADP, the biggest gripe is if you play on k+m (and i changed all binds to be more like the controls in ds1-3 and Elden ring respectfully) the option for "double click mouse disabled" is a must. It will make attack instant with no delay. The only problem is every time you close app and re-launch it you will manually have to switch off the double click disable and turn it on again because some reason it reverts back to being off even when it still says its activated. Beside this the game was still fun. Majula is probably my second favorite hub in the series. The story is loosely its own thing although there are clear call backs to DS1. There is a lot of cheap deaths but that didnt really make it unbearable or too annoying. The areas are not as well woven and sometimes confusing af, often asking yourself where tf to even go but the environments themselves are pretty cool and the art direction is more dark fantasy this time around imo. The Bosses are hit and misses, but nothing i would call bad except for maybe three of them. The DLC'S are bangers, but the frigid outskirts is dogwater lol. The weapons/Spells/Hexes/Miracles and armor are bountiful and the covenants are still a grind. Its simultaneously the easiest Dark souls and the hardest do to the punishment of having your health taken each death as a hollow, but how many life gems you got can make you invincible non stop healing which makes it kinda cheapened, however flask now take time to heal you and you stand still as opposed to life gems let you walk while using one. Last new game+ is handled a little different along with bonfires and humanities.

Finally if you played elden ring and dark souls 1-3 and avoided this one because of the negatives surrounding it, take it with a grain of salt. It is different and has flaws, but its definitely NOT a bad game at all. This definitely had very fun moments and good levels and enemy designs, the darker fantasy vibe was well done and i had FUN. Give Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin 8.5/10

Dark Souls 2 is the Hawaiian Pizza of Dark Souls

A polarizing game whose fans will defend ad nauseum, and haters will hate on. And, like Hawaiian pizza, the debate as to whether Dark Souls 2 is peak Dark Souls or janky trash rages on ad infinitum.

I never eat Hawaiian pizza unless my wife orders it. I 100%'d this game to finish 100%'ing the Dark Souls trilogy. I'll never play this game again unless someone invites me, but it's still pizza, and I'll take bad pizza over no pizza. FromSoft said they're done making Dark Souls games, so you gotta take what you can get.

So, I recommend playing through the game if you are a fan of Dark Souls like me. Then you can at least say you had a "no thank you bite" of Dark Souls 2. After all, it's not about whether or not Hawaiian pizza is objectively goated or blasphemy that never should've been created, it's about personal preference. To each their own, I say.

One last note: I hear a lot that DS2 PvP is the best of any Dark Souls game, but at least here on Steam, the PvP was dead throughout my >70 hours of playing. If you want to make friends (PvP adversaries) who also enjoy Hawaiian pizza, you have to make some effort to do so.

Posted: November 13, 2024
*Dark Souls II* is definitely a divisive entry in the *Souls* series, and I get why—it’s got a mix of great and frustrating elements that create a love/hate experience. First off, the quality of life in this game is trash. I’d recommend grabbing a mod to smooth out things; otherwise, playing can feel like a grind. The lock-on system, in particular, is a nightmare. Too often, I’d try to lock onto an enemy in front, only to suddenly be targeting a random boss or enemy behind me. It drove me crazy until I just disabled lock-on altogether, which, surprisingly, made most boss fights way easier to manage.

The bosses themselves are decent but feel easier than those in *Dark Souls I*, especially without relying on the lock-on. The graphics are fine, though different—due to they used a different game engine, along with the timing changes in controls, which feel noticeably off if you’re coming straight from *DS1*. That said, this game is massive! With the DLC merged into the base game, there's a ton to explore, and I found myself constantly hooked, wanting to see every area and defeat every boss.

Despite its flaws, *Dark Souls II* is an addictive experience, and once you get past the rough edges, it’s genuinely fun. There’s a lot here for anyone who loves the *Souls* formula and doesn’t mind working around some quirky mechanics.

Posted: November 11, 2024
If you are reading reviews for this game, you most likely are a fan of the Souls series but is unsure if you should play this game due to its reputation and controversy. If not, you are probably detached from the Souls community and is just trying to find a new game to play. Either way, it's very important to highlight some curiosities about the development of this game.

FromSoftware is known for their souls games, but there is generally a person that is ALWAYS behind the development of this games, which is the infamous Hidetaka Miyazaki. He's the only person at the time of this review that can bring a feeling to a game such as Dark Souls or Elden Ring, it's really a very unique experience. If you already played and finished one of the Souls games, I'm sure you know what I'm talking about.

I consider this fact important because making a good Souls game is not a task that FromSoftware can achieve on its own, but instead Miyazaki is the genius behind these creations. And on top of that, you should know that Miyazaki DID NOT PARTICIPATE in the development of Dark Souls 2, which makes this game EXTREMELY distanced from the rest of the Souls series.

Now that this is out of the way, all I'm gonna say is that, if you can play Dark Souls 2 differently from other souls games, completely ignoring the game being called "Dark Souls" and instead just play it like a Dark Fantasy RPG action/adventure game, you are probably gonna have a lot of fun or at least be amused by the game.

This game is very unique and one of my favorite games ever (I still like DS1 more but not for a lot), but before I could enjoy and have fun with this game I had to learn to play and love it for what it is, and not for being a Dark Souls game.

If you are open-minded enough to play this game like this, or just don't care if it's similar enough to Dark Souls 1 or 3, go ahead! This game is a masterpiece, it's beautiful, captivating, fun, challenging and so many other things.

Big yes from me

Posted: November 11, 2024
Product received for free

I truly believe this game has a laundered reputation and we forgot how bad the original console release was for DS2. Borderline mandela effect. It is not befitting of being in the same series as the other two souls titles. Had this been released with a different IP, it would have flopped. 100% boss completion run, including DLC by the way. Truly gave this "enhanced version" of the game a chance despite vividly remembering a subpar at best experience on console. Skip to the final point at the bottom for the tl;dr

My experience was 54 hours of 100% blind gameplay, 100% boss completion including the DLC. PURE strength build as I have always done with the deprived start.

Good things about ds2:
-Weapon variety and absurdity
-A more precise fast travel system
-respec'ing is much easier to accomplish
-dual wielding improvements including unique movesets with two weapons of the same type
-bonfire ascetics to "restart" the area for unique pickups / redo the boss encounters
-more toys to play with in all different builds (spells/miracles/hexes), just a little more things they tried
-armor types and styles are much more varied, with some giving stats, although the way they implement it isn't exactly clear, it does make for low level challenge runners having more resources.

Bad things about ds2:
-weapons and items are all made out of aluminum foil for some reason. Repairing on the go with repair powder is much more crucial, but the logic as to WHY is... nonexistent. Makes exploration much more painstaking. I didn't mind having multiple weapons to use, but a good chunk of the weapon variety is just inferior to some of the hallmark weapons people use.
-the level designs are mediocre on average with barely any notable high marks. Claustrophobic, monochromatic for the most part. Some are purely experimental but not playtested, and some are just outright AWFUL to try and traverse and end up making you run past the optional side passages that would be worth exploring, if the zone wasn't terrible. The high marks are at the start of the game, conveniently, where you should also consider stopping your playthrough and playing a better game. A lot of the late game levels feel very rushed, with little to nothing to offer, and some of the exploration feels insulting. Expanding upon this in its own bullet point.
-Lockstones used to access secret areas or hidden treasures are far and few between, and a huge chunk of the lockstone doors simply dont do ANYTHING at all. Same with fragrant branches. The branches are a less egregious system, but ultimately they're a VERY finite resource that limit your exploration opportunities in a way that's quite insulting, since you have NO indication what the ramifications of your choices are even from a hunch. The hunch instead is to simply never use them unless you look up a guide on what the "useful" locations are. Stonesword keys in elden ring is the polished version of this system, but we're not giving lockstones/branches credit for this because we had unique keys in DS1, and illusory paths are an existing system.
-character movement/feel, and ADP. A stat that has a placebo effect until you go to reddit and read where the break points are for its effectiveness in playing the game, is bad. Period. "skill issue" shouters did the same thing to find out when to use this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ stat.
-bosses are pretty subpar at best. Some of the worst offenders are Sir Alonne with the vacuum stab and a copy/paste moveset of the lost sinner, despite being hyped up as this badass warrior king, demon of song with the worst grab hitbox, dragonrider duo being REQUIRED to pass through drangleic, an END GAME location, royal rat vanguard/authority, the giant lord in the memory sequence, vendrick being an actual punching bag lord soul (veldstat 200 feet in front of him was a better boss), nashandra and aldia being the worst final bossses in souls' history. The king's pets at the end of the frigid outskirts is an insult to a grievous injury for even conceptualizing that zone, nevertheless running through it, and the list will go on.
-Hollowing when you die to lose your max hp by up to 40% or so unless wearing a ring that lowers this effect is especially egregious for challenge runners, but just bad in general, especially when you're compounded by other bad gameplay systems uncharacteristic of even the first souls title.
-Soul memory killed a good chunk of the PVP in DS2 involving low level pvp (twinking). I'm not a pvp oriented player, but a system designed to be counter-intuitive to its own built-in player interaction is not a net positive.
-Story is some half-assed fan fiction and the NPCs are especially ignore-able compared to previous titles. questlines are especially more convoluted than in DS1.
-Torch time.... yeah we're grateful we have a torch, but why is it that collecting more torches, somehow makes a torch last longer, but the torches still have a time limit? who actually thought this through? This is the least scalding negative as the torch has been improved upon since then in subsequent titles
-Combat feels worse than an old PS1/PS2 title. Bad hitboxes. your character is extremely lackadaisical when trying to do anything with a sense of urgency. Anything that has a charge has a hitbox that would be borderline unavoidable if you didn't level enough ADP. This game could have been passed off as turn-based at the rate the combat goes.
-Boss runbacks from dying are THE worst and it's not even close. Even though I first or second tried most of the bosses in the game, the environment when you fail just sucks. Period.

FINAL POINT

-The expectation vs reality overall is much more mundane and underwhelming despite having so much feedback given from the first title. That's the worst part. They had a good formula, but flew too close to the sun in the development time they had, and almost UNDID what made the first game so much better. The community in this game in particular is particularly insufferable when you try to point out this review is only PART of the complaints some people will have, because they just write it all off as the players' fault, citing no fault to the developers.

Posted: November 10, 2024
Dont play this complete and utter atrocity of a game. Here is just 10 of the innumerable reasons why I hate this game and wish I could punch it in its stupid face repeatedly:

1. Player actions feel like youre constantly stuck in quick sand where everything you do locks you in place for a bit. Also it is often hard to see when you are free to give an input again and if you do it too fast it won't register which feels makes you want to rip out your own tongue and eat it.
2. Every input feels like you're playing the game at 300 ms latency at all times. Need I point out how TERRIBLE and unenjoyable that is.
3. EVERY SINGLE AREA SUCKS. And I mean horribly. There are zero engaging features or anything cool whatsoever anywhere. Everywhere you go is entirely bland, boring, lame, ugly, and lacks the imagination that made every other souls game so cool. Every area I can think of makes me cringe with how bad it is.
4. All the boss fights are unfun and not all memorable either. There is no 'wow' factor, no visual spectacle, no thrilling music, no tension due to the lore, no flow feeling where it feels like you're dancing with a boss. They just plain giga suck and there is nothing good about any of them.
5. The game wastes your time with unbearably long paths from the bonfires to the bosses. It is tedious and a hassle that adds up to hours of game time.
6. Exploration is unrewarding. Most items in this game are supremely useless and you know it. It doesn't feel good to have finally made your way to that item that was hard to get to because you just know it's gonna be a freaking torch or a stupid consumable you'll never use. And sure enough, it always is.
7. Everything is a trap. You walk into that alley to see which useless trash consumable they put there for you to waste your time picking it up and you better expect there to be 500 enemies jumping on you from every direction and body blocking the only exit way. Wall? Enemy can and will hit you through it. Door? Enemy behind it that blasts it open as you approach, oneshotting you. Chest? Trapped or mimic. Lever? Will open a way for extra enemies to come get you.
8. Hitboxes make no sense and stuff constantly hits you that has absolutely no right to, for example when you're out of attack range, behind the enemy and mid roll all at the same time. There's tons of stupid stuff and bugs such as getting suspended in an endless falling animation while 1 meter above the ground and dying.
9. Enemy placement and timings are deliberately chosen in a way that makes them aggro on you with some carefully tuned lag so that you will just barely not be able to open a chest, move through a fog gate, get through a door, move past another enemy, or do anything really because at the last moment you will get hit by that arrow, smacked by that enemy, knocked off the platform, and so on. This makes it so that you'll generally take ages and ages to draw out the sometimes dozens of enemies individually to defuse these traps. It's dull and boring and takes forever and is frustrating if you die because you'll have to do it all over again. But you also can't not do it because if you want to explore an area there's really no way to deal with all these threats at the same time.
10. The graphics are horrible and have not aged well at all.

Some bonus reasons because they just came to mind: The PC port is horrendous and without community fixes or tools it is nigh unplayable with keyboard and mouse which was absolutely okay in DS1, DS3, and Elden Ring. The game has no borderless full screen which makes it a pain in the behind to tab out. When you close the game you need to wait for it to log onto the DS2 servers again which gets you stuck in the menu. You need to travel to the main fire keeper AND spam through four lines of dialogue every time you want to level up.

Rereading my own review makes me angry because I hate this game so much and my review doesn't do this hate any justice. There is no redeeming quality about this game and no fun to be had. I have suffered through other ordeals before and usually had at least something positive to say about it afterwards. But not about DS2. I only recommend this abominable, awful, despicable, disgusting, repulsive, and straight up vile waste of hard drive space to people who truly and passionately loathe themselves.

Posted: November 8, 2024
Dark Souls II is a great game, no doubt. It's beautiful, challenging, and has its own unique charm. However, it's far from perfect and can be pretty frustrating at times. The hitboxes often feel off, leading to unfair hits, and the gank situations—where enemies team up to stunlock you to death—can feel cheap and unforgiving. Compared to Dark Souls 1, the environments, bosses, and story don’t have quite the same magic or allure.

At first, the game feels clunky; the movement and controls can take some getting used to. But if you stick with it, there's a truly beautiful game waiting beneath the rough edges. It's actually amazing when you realize that the developers only had around a year to put this together. With patience, you find yourself adjusting, and the game becomes a memorable experience you won’t soon forget.

One thing that might disappoint some players is how the game’s difficulty curve flattens out as you go along. The bosses become somewhat repetitive, often just big guys in armor or humanoids with predictable movesets. I never really felt "stuck" in the same way I did in other Souls games, which meant that some victories felt less rewarding. However, New Game Plus flipped that on its head and gave me a real challenge that humbled me fast.

Then there’s the ending, and wow—that’s where it truly hit me. The theme that plays during the ending is beautiful, and it’s something I still listen to even outside of the game. It was the best reward I could’ve asked for after pushing through all the frustrations, struggles, and victories. Dark Souls II may have its flaws, but it’s a game worth playing, and one that stays with you.

Dark Souls 2. The pinnacle. The zenith. The apex of FromSoftware’s magnum opus. A game so unparalleled, it renders all others obsolete. Why bother with the rest? Why waste time on lesser titles? Dark Souls 2 is the alpha and the omega. The beginning and the end.

Consider the hub area, Majula. A serene haven. A tranquil respite. The sun sets eternally. A metaphor for the player’s journey. A symbol of hope amidst despair. Who needs Firelink Shrine? Who needs the Hunter’s Dream? Majula is all you need.

The enemies. The foes. The adversaries. Each one a puzzle. Each one a challenge. The Pursuer. A relentless hunter. The Mirror Knight. A reflection of your own struggle. The Rotten. A grotesque reminder of decay. Why face the Nameless King? Why confront Lady Maria? Dark Souls 2’s rogues’ gallery is unmatched.

The lore. The story. The narrative. A tapestry woven with threads of mystery. A labyrinth of intrigue. The curse of the undead. The cycle of light and dark. The quest for the crowns. A journey of self-discovery. Who needs the Age of Fire? Who needs the Great Ones? Dark Souls 2’s tale is the one to tell.

FromSoftware fans, heed this call. Embrace the truth. Accept the reality. Dark Souls 2 is the one. The only. The ultimate experience. Forsake the others. Abandon the rest. Your journey begins and ends here. In Drangleic. In Dark Souls 2.

Posted: November 6, 2024
Dark Souls II is a game that, for me, misses the mark in almost every way that made its predecessor a masterpiece. Despite its reputation within the Soulsborne series, I found it to be a frustrating, disjointed, and often poorly designed experience.

The combat, while still retaining the core mechanics of the series, feels sluggish and unresponsive at times. Hitboxes are often questionable, leading to cheap deaths that feel unfair rather than earned. The enemies, instead of offering unique, thoughtful challenges, often come in large, repetitive groups, making some encounters feel like artificial difficulty rather than the skill-based challenge Dark Souls is known for.

Then there’s the infamous adaptability stat, which feels like an unnecessary and frustrating barrier to the smooth, responsive combat of the first game. It artificially hinders dodge rolls and recovery time, forcing players to invest heavily in a stat just to make the controls feel as they should have from the start.

The boss fights, usually a high point in the Souls series, are mostly forgettable. Many are just reskinned enemies or overly simplistic designs that lack the creativity and intensity of the first game’s iconic encounters. It's hard to feel excited when you're up against yet another humanoid with a big sword, and some bosses feel like they were designed to be unfair rather than difficult.

The game’s story and atmosphere also feel weaker. NPCs are less memorable, and the narrative lacks the deep, haunting mystique that made the first game so captivating. Instead of feeling like a cohesive world with rich lore, Dark Souls II comes off as a hollow shell that tries to replicate its predecessor's success but falls short.

Overall, Dark Souls II feels like a step backward in nearly every aspect. It lacks the polish, challenge, and depth that define the series. While some fans might appreciate the changes, for me, this game fails to live up to the high standards set by Dark Souls 3 and Dark Souls 1. Disappointing, to say the least.

This is my favorite souls-like game.

It has plenty of questionable design decisions (IE: Adaptability) but it has far more redeeming and endearing qualities.

In my opinion, it has many of the best looking armor sets in the series, the most memorable environments, and the single best boss fight in all the FromSoftware games. And while many of the characters aren't as charming or interesting as others in the series, it all contributes to a cohesive experience and a compelling storyline that reflects the core values of the Dark Souls franchise. The gameplay may take a little getting used to, but it is rarely (but occasionally!) unfair.

Negatives include a poorly designed online matchmaking system (known as soul memory), the aforementioned adaptability stat that affects the player's rolling invincibility frames, and occasionally questionable hitboxes (although its predecessor wasn't known for its fair hitboxes either). The hollowing mechanic can be annoying to interface with, especially for newer players. Level design is consistently appealing, but there are certainly a few areas that are a little obnoxious and many areas that are downright confusing. And many boss fights have external factors (occurring outside the boss room) that affect the difficulty of the fight in question, which might make the fight seem unfair if overlooked.

Overall, I believe this is the most rewarding experience out of all the FromSoftware souls-like games! But I do concede that its design can appear hostile to many players, so while I would recommend this game it would be with the caveat that it's an experience that improves with understanding of the game. Veterans of the series should feel at home here, and new players should be prepared to face adversity, confusion, and the possibility of making decisions that have unforeseen possibly negative repercussions.

Posted: November 5, 2024
The black sheep of the trilogy, DS2 is a flawed masterpiece.

It's a VERY different game than the other two. If you're looking for fast and reactive combat, look elsewhere. DS2 is a very ethereal and dreamlike experience based around cautious exploration and engagements in slow and deliberate combat against groups of enemies that must be kited one by one.

A very frustrating first experience, no matter how you play, DS2 is initially quite unfriendly. The world is hostile and alien, enemies lurk beyond every corner, and death is punishing, but faint glimmers of hope linger still. More involved lore tells the story of a decaying kingdom and the overall feel is much closer to high fantasy while still maintaining the Souls series trademark dark fantasy, mixing the grim and the fantastic in imaginative ways. The story, largely divorced of the direct symbolism around fire and the lineage of Gwyn, explores freedom of choice and a fate for the world beyond mere light and Dark. DS2 contains some very interesting NPCs even outside its major story, some real memorable characters in this one (my favorite is Gilligan).

Build variety is insane here, rivaled probably only by Elden Ring; power stancing creates hundreds of different weapon combinations, hexing is a dedicated dark magic system unique to this game, and soul vessels and bonfire ascetics make for easy re-speccing and grinding/farming. DS2 is incredibly ambitious with the freedom it gives the player, though its general jank can take getting used to, and the Adaptability stat tends to confuse new players. While it is incredibly ridiculous to tie i-frames to a stat, DS2 gives out level ups like halloween candy and for a decent dodge roll ADP only needs to hit about 18 or 20, it's really not that much of an investment. In addition, this is the only game in the entire Soulsborne canon with backstep i-frames, highly recommend taking advantage of them.

It's long. Longer than any other Soulsborne sans Elden Ring. While nowhere near as massive, the originally intended open world experience is still present in world layout design artifacts. It's content rich and has insane replay value, with a NG+ cycle that actually changes things around. While it's not a Metroidvania like the first game, a knowledgable player can still sequence break like crazy, and the first half is incredibly loose with progression before streamlining in the latter half.

The DLC levels are incredible, often cited as the best content in the game. They are. Base game still has great levels and jaw dropping scenery regardless. You may hate Shrine of Amana, but you won't forget its beauty. The weakest front here is bosses; DS2 is lousy with boss fights, but most are unimpressive, with only a few true standouts in the base game. The DLC bosses fare better, but this is much more like Demon's Souls in that the emphasis is on challenging levels that end with fairly trivial boss encounters.

Online is weird. Soul Memory is the worst mechanic From have ever put in one of these games and I stand by that. It's far too easy to accidentally level into another tier and lose potential online summons, interrupt co-op campaigning, and invite high level invaders. Invasions are thankfully uncommon due to a lack of any reusable item for it, but DS2 also contains a plethora of NPC phantoms - both as summons and invaders. You'll never have to fight a boss solo, even offline, but you'll also rarely go through a level without being accosted by a dark spirit (looking at you, Forlorn). The Agape Ring is meant to remedy the problem by allowing user-set capping, but it ironically invites possible twinking in a system specifically meant to prevent it. It's bad through and through. Co-op in this game is wonderful, and PVP tends to be less cancerous than other titles due to the niche dedicated fanbase this game has, but do yourself a favor and look up a guide to SM tiers so you can actually cater your online experience.

All its flaws considered, DS2 still stands as an exceptional and unique entry in the Soulsborne canon, completely disconnected from the rest of its series and only scarcely referenced in its succeeding games. To some degree, if this game were not called "Dark Souls 2" I feel it would fare much better, as the unintentional baggage of carrying the title Dark Souls leads to mountains of expectations, very few of which this game even intends to meet.

Go into it fresh and with an open mind, divorced of any other Soulsborne preconceptions. Ignore the hate. Be patient, be cautious, and be creative. At some point, it'll click. Even if it takes 8 different attempts, like it did for me. It just might even become one of your favorite games, the B Team really did alright here.

"Bearer of the curse. I will always be at your side. Until hope has fully withered."

**A Solid Entry with Its Own Charm**

Dark Souls II is an impressive and worthy addition to the legendary Souls series, though it may not quite reach the lofty heights of its siblings, Dark Souls and Dark Souls III. While it doesn't have the same level of cohesion and polish as the first and third entries, it still offers a rich, rewarding experience with plenty of the challenging combat and deep lore that fans of the franchise love.

The world of Dark Souls II is expansive, diverse, and packed with secrets. From the misty marshes of the Forest of Fallen Giants to the eerie, haunting Drangleic Castle, each area is beautifully designed and filled with its own unique atmosphere. The interconnected world is present, though perhaps not as tightly woven as in the first Dark Souls, where every corner seemed to connect in unexpected ways. Nonetheless, the sense of discovery and exploration is still very much alive, and there’s a real joy in uncovering hidden paths, unlocking shortcuts, and finding all the game’s myriad secrets.

The combat in Dark Souls II is still solid but feels slightly different from the fluidity found in Dark Souls and Dark Souls III. The game introduces some tweaks to mechanics, like the stamina system and weapon variety, which can be hit or miss depending on your playstyle. It may take a little while to adjust, but once you do, the combat feels satisfying. The bosses, while numerous and creative, may not pack the same punch as the iconic encounters in the first or third games, but they still offer a fair challenge and often require careful strategy and timing.

One of the standout features of Dark Souls II is its level of difficulty. It's tough, no doubt about it, but it's also extremely fair. Every death feels like a learning experience, and unlike the more punishing nature of Dark Souls, the challenge here is often about trial and error, making it feel like there's always a chance for success with patience and persistence. For many, this may feel like a more accessible entry in the series, though it's still a brutal test of skill and willpower.

The lore and storytelling, while rich and immersive, doesn’t quite have the same impact as the first game. Dark Souls II has a more fragmented narrative approach, leaving much of its backstory for the player to piece together through exploration and item descriptions. While it’s still fascinating, it lacks the same sense of mystery and world-building found in Dark Souls, or the tighter narrative focus seen in Dark Souls III. That said, there’s still a lot to enjoy in the story if you're willing to dig into it.

In summary, while Dark Souls II may not surpass the monumental achievements of the original or the refined brilliance of Dark Souls III, it’s still an incredibly good game. It offers its own unique challenges, intriguing environments, and deep combat mechanics. For anyone who loved the series, it's definitely worth playing, even if it occasionally feels like it's playing second fiddle to its predecessors.

The few drawbacks in my view was the level design was a bit all over the place. At points I didn't know where to go. So a few times I had to look up a guide. But overall a very good game. Not better but also not worse than any of the dark souls game in my view. 9/10

There is no doubt in my mind that if I played this solo I would've rated it negative. Because I played with friends, I had a good time and was laughing a lot. Also laughing because the game is seriously just so bad at times I can't help but laugh. But everyone who said this game was bad was 100% correct. But, there's also so many good ideas and additions and I genuinely believe that if it got remade and removed some of the awful things it COULD be seriously good. I feel like a good way to describe this game is 2 steps forward, but 10 steps back...

But I can't talk all trash about DS2. This game really did start some of the best QoL features of the souls games like teleporting between fires, the estus flask upgrade system, and powerstancing. They also implemented some other stuff that I seriously wish were in future games like lifegems, bonfire ascetics, pharros lockstones/branches of yore, weapon-unique backstab animations, being able to get some covenant items outside of covenants, small soapstones which allow co-op after the boss is dead, and max heals while in co-op. As well, the level design is fun, the included DLCs are the best part of the game, and NG+ actually changes up some stuff which is very nice.

But, unfortunately, there are way more things that I really don't like. A lot of these are the reason why so many people trash on 2 compared to 1 & 3. I seriously believe that if they fix/remove a lot of this stuff DS2 could've been one of the best souls games:
The amount of enemies is ridiculous. You will walk into a room and face like 5-8 enemies at once. They also can see you from so far away and will follow you forever.
The movement overall feels awful. Jumping feels decent though. As well, the controls are not great and the fact there's a bug where if you use KBM, every time you launch the game you have to go back into settings and enable double clicking. If you don't, you won't be able to spam click left click to attack.











And after all that, I still did have a good time, but the issues are still hard to ignore. I'm rating it positive because I genuinely did learn to enjoy it and I can totally see others enjoying it as well, but I can't help but recommend you play every single other souls-like before DS2.

This is by far the most divisive Dark Souls game. There's plenty to hate, but there's also a lot to love and I think DS2 isn't given enough credit for many, many reasons. I usually start with the positives, but I will break that rule and get the most obvious negatives out of the way first so we can then have a look what DS2 does right.

There is a stat called Adaptability that everybody hates and for good reason - it governs how many i-frames you have and how fast you complete certain actions like drinking your flask. If that sounds like a dumb idea, that's because it is. All it really does is make you waste souls on it because the game just feels "off" without ADP on 25, which brings it in line with the other two games. However... I felt DS2 was the easiest to become overleveled in, because the soul requirements at lower levels are so low you can just blast through the first 10 or so levels like nothing and you'll hit ADP 25 easy enough so you can then ignore it and play DS2 like a "normal" Souls game.

The boss runbacks in this game are among the worst in the series, there's no two ways about it. They're long, they're tedious and they're cheap. They're not ALL like that, mind you, but the ones that are bad are really atrocious. This was one thing future FromSoft games changed for the better.

Without going into a breakdown of every area, I can also say that compared to DS1's masterfully interwoven world and DS3's linear, but still satisfying map design, DS2 is a weird game in terms of level design. Majula is hands down the best hub in any Souls game but unlike DS1, which gave you a fairly good hit at where to go and where NOT to go, paths and progression in this game are far less cleverly signaled and getting lost is a way more common occurrence. That said, it set a strong precedent for the "go somewhere else and come back later" style of world exploration that Elden Ring later capitalized on. But overall, I think the world of DS2 is still as beautiful, grandiose and mysterious as a Dark Souls world should be and I would argue that despite some quesitonable design choices, the aesthetic and atmosphere is spot on and there's something soothing, even fairy-tale-like about DS2's world. I also enjoyed the story - it's not connected to DS1 or DS3, and it's told in that familiar "read the item descriptions and observe the environment" way which makes you feel like an archaeologist dropped into a world into which you arrived around 500 years too late for all the big action. There's also a lot more disparity between the nicer areas like Majula and its adjacent forest(s) and the really gloomy areas like the underground tombs and Drangleic castle. All in all, the world is definitely an interesting place to inhabit and it feels a little less confined than either DS1 or DS3.

Which neatly brings us to DS2's strong points. I've touched on the aesthetic and the world, but I would say DS2 gets a bad rap in the gameplay department as well. Once you solve the Adaptability problem, it feels like it should and there is an even wider variety of weapons and especially sorceries, hexes, miracles and pyromancies than DS1. I didn't touch magic on my first playthrough, but I feel I would have a blast with it. It feels a lot less like DS1 where some weapons were just flat out better than others, whereas here, a lot more weapons feel like a viable choice if for no other reason than sheer number alone - there's just much more to choose from, so a lot more can do the job well. There's also the introduction of proper powerstancing for even more moveset variety.

One aspect which gets praised a lot for good reason is the "Bonfire Ascetic" mechanic. DS2 has a strange anti-farming mechanic where if you kill an enemy too many times (10-12 or so), it stops respawning. To counter that, you can burn an item called a "Bonfire Ascetic" to essentially reset that counter to zero - but it also raises that area (and only that area) to NG+, then NG+2 etc. It's a really neat way of increasing challenge without having to complete the entire game to do so, and you get to choose which areas to boost for various purposes. It turns bonfire management into a game on its own and it's a nice addition. I wish future FS games had something like this.

On the topic of NG+, this game also changes on NG+ runs a lot more than just "enemies have more health and hit harder". The spawns are different, you get better versions of existing items, it's a different experience than the first time.

There are also a few QoL additions like being able to warp to any bonfire right from the start (though I will ALWAYS insist that the way DS1 did it was better, it only had to give you this option a bit earlier than after completing basically 3/4 of the game), Life Gems which are small consumables that regen your health without needing to waste a flask. It sadly also has the infuriatingly tedious mechanic of forcing you to level up at Majula, meaning that every time you want to level up, you have to port to Majula, level up and port back.

Brief word about the DLCs. They are pretty neat because they connect into one coherent goal, but short of reading a guide it's not very clear how to realize that goal. Lastly, one of the DLCs includes what I would consider the absolute worst area in any FromSoft game ever. But if you want more DS2 after finishing the base game, it scratches that itch just fine.

All in all, I think some of the glaring issues with this game are often overstated. "Level Adaptability", for all its meme status, is a legit piece of advice and once you do it, the game becomes a lot more in line with what you'd be expecting. The level design, while not nearly as tight as DS1 or DS3, has its own merits in its scale and openness. There are plenty of new mechanics that make the game stand out from its predecessor and successor, and for all its flaws, it's still a unique and captivating game. A lot of people recommend skipping DS2 and going straight to DS3 from DS1 - I would say you're doing yourself a disservice if you enjoyed either of those games. If DS1 is "the prim and proper one" and DS3 is "the fun one", then DS2 is definitely "the special one", for better or for worse.

Posted: October 31, 2024
I played many hours of DS2 on PS4, but unfortunately, it didn't quite live up to the legacy of its predecessor. Here are a few reasons why I found it disappointing:

Inconsistent Level Design: One of the biggest strengths of Dark Souls was its interconnected world and clever level design. Dark Souls 2, however, feels disjointed with areas that don't logically connect, both thematically and geographically. The sense of exploration and discovery is diminished when the world feels like a patchwork of unrelated zones.

Uninspired Boss Fights: While there are a few standout encounters, many of the bosses in Dark Souls 2 lack the creativity that made bosses in the first game memorable. Several bosses feel like larger versions of regular enemies or rely too heavily on gimmicks rather than skill.

Questionable Mechanics: The introduction of the Adaptability stat, which affects your ability to dodge effectively, feels like an unnecessary complication. It forces players to invest in a stat just to make basic mechanics feel responsive, which can be frustrating.

Hitbox Issues: There are noticeable hitbox issues that can lead to unfair deaths. Getting hit by attacks that visibly miss you is a common occurrence, breaking immersion and adding to the frustration.

Lack of Atmosphere: Compared to the haunting and atmospheric environments of Dark Souls, the settings in Dark Souls 2 often feel bland and uninspired. The game lacks the same level of detail and environmental storytelling that made the original so captivating.

In conclusion, while Dark Souls 2 isn't a terrible game by any means, it's a step down from the brilliance of Dark Souls. It lacks the polish and coherence that made its predecessor a masterpiece. If you're new to the series, I recommend starting with the original (remastered) or Dark Souls 3 for a better experience.

6/10

There’s so much to say about what this game does right and wrong compared to DS1, so I’ll summarize some of the best and worst features:

Good:
-Fast travel is available from the start, with the ability to warp to any bonfire
-Upgrading your Estus quantity with shards encourages exploration and offers a sense of power scaling
-Bonfire Ascetic; a consumable that lets you respawn all enemies (including bosses) and loot in an area
-Stat respec is possible, so you’re no longer limited to one build per character; it's also easy to achieve

Bad:
-Each time you die, you lose about 5% of max HP (up to 50%), recoverable by using humanities
-Some of the worst runbacks in Soulsborne history; the longest I recorded was 3:45 in the Ivory King DLC
-ADP: a stat that affects the number of iframes on your dodge roll
-The stamina system had several reworks and feels clunky, leading to slower, less fluid fights
-Many doors you open do not stay open (reset area = closed door); opening doors/chests is also very slow
-No iframes when entering fog gates, opening doors, opening chests, or operating levers
-The lock-on system has limited swing directions (kind of like a unit circle), causing many attacks to miss
-Enemies will chase you for miles before finally de-aggroing
-Overall map and environmental design is worse

Again, these are just what I consider the best and worst features. Though it's objectively the worst Soulsborne game, I don't think it's unplayable.

Posted: October 30, 2024
I played this game immediately after Dark Souls 1 and by the end have enjoyed my experience. The controls feel weird right from the start when you pick up the game and it took many hours for me to get used to, which I believe is the main reason this is the worse of the Dark Souls games that no one talks more about. Attacks after rolling have little tracking and manually aiming your attacks against enemies feels jank. They also increased the animation time for opening chests for some reason which gets annoying when you need to open 4 in a row. And if you didn't already hear, hitboxes and hurtboxes are jank where grab attacks would teleport you to the enemies, backstabs rotate the enemies, certain attacks can hit you without touching you, and the game adjusts the brightness when you enter a "dark" room feel artificial.

When I first played this, it felt like the team who made this didn't understand what made the first game work and made the difficulty more artificial instead of natural with the mechanic of decreasing your health after every death. But don't worry, if you die too much, enemies eventually stop respawning if they're defeated enough times to make going through the levels easier. Not only did this break my immersion, but it felt like I was being babied as I played. I wanted to learn how to go through this area on my own, but the game said it was too hard and took it away before I could learn. And with those enemies gone, you now lost a potential source of farming, but in practice didn't matter since the bosses felt like the easiest in the franchise.

I'd still recommend playing this to experience the black sheep of the series. Especially since playing through the 3 DLCs was a better experience playing through compared to most other areas in this trilogy. My only problem with the DLCs is how to get access to them. In the Scholar of the First Sin edition, you need to find keys to enter each of the DLCs, but 2 of the 3 keys are in really obtuse locations.

scholar is great. if you wanna play dark souls 2, this is the one. the only thing worse in this version is iron keep, but even then it sucks in og as well, just a bit less. the rest of the changes just make the game more interesting and less boring (although with a side effect that a lot of areas are slightly harder).

the downsides of dark souls 2 are it's troubled development making a lot of levels and bosses kinda bad and the whole "this game is really hard" buzz ds1 had affecting how they built this game (this is probably the meanest souls game tbh). also covenant requirements suck, which functionally locks a lot of good spells behind new game plus 2 (essentially you will never see them online). the upsides are a ton of very interesting mechanical changes from dark souls 1, the general slowing down of game & bringing it more in line with older from soft titles like kings field, and it's just chock full of secrets. also the dlc's are the best they've ever made and a reflection of how the levels in the base game should have been if they didn't make the decision to try to make this game open world and with crazy lighting on a ps3 and had to rush the game a ton. pretty much only like lost bastille & no man's wharf in the base game feels like a well put together level with everything beyond the shrine of winter feeling like fake dark souls levels.

still though, in spite of all that, this game is peak. just did a ng+ playthrough of og and I'm gonna do halfway to ng++ on this version (on ng+ now) to hundred percent it. online play still works, it's just that soul memory kinda sucks, so dont equip silver serpent ring or jester gloves if you wanna online play. also seems like saturdays are the only reliable day to get multiplayer in my experience. not a ton of invasions, but getting summoned or summoning isn't the worst.
