Posted: October 22, 2024
Dark souls 2 gets way too much hate. This game It’s not just good, it’s got a totally different vibe from DS1 and DS3, and that’s exactly why it works. It does its own thing, and once you’re in, you’re hooked.

Mechanics wise, it’s packed with gems. The power stance system is way underrated. Hit the right stats, and dual wielding with unique movesets is game changing. Fromsoft really went, “Let’s make combat even crazier.” No other Souls game gives you this kind of freedom to mix and match weapon combos, and experimenting with it is a blast.

And yeah, Adaptability (ADP) gets a lot of flak, but it’s honestly not the nightmare people make it out to be. Get it to 25, and dodging smooths out. Sure, people complain, but ADP just adds depth, giving Dark Souls 2 its own unique feel. Once it clicks, it really clicks.

The world design is massive, and exploring feels rewarding the whole way through. And let’s talk about the DLC the Fume Knight, Crown of the Old Iron King Some of the best in the series.

Plus, the build variety here is unmatched. Every run can feel new, keeping it fresh each time you play. This game wants you to experiment, and it pays off big time.

So, if you’ve skipped this one because of the criticism, give it a shot. You might find yourself completely hooked.

Posted: October 22, 2024
to start off, ADP didn't bother me much, it gave me a motive to earn souls to level up instead of steamrolling through each area which is usually how i play souls games.

most bosses were pretty easy with a move-set no larger than 3-4 moves so the difficulty factor for this game wasn't in the fights (with the exception of 1 or 2) but more so traversing the areas. I was disappointed in the areas of this game, as dark souls 1 left some pretty big shoes to fill and unfortunately dark souls 2 just didn't do it for me. i found myself dreading most of the late game areas like iron keep or shrine of amana. the enemy variety was bland and stale with most enemies being carbon copies of the ones prior, the shortcuts were a pain to achieve and most of the time weren't worth it honestly. there was a few areas i did enjoy like forest of the fallen giants and heides tower of flame but that's the most of it.

there's not many other negative things i have to say about dark souls 2, other than weapons and armour having a durability bar but that was manageable most of the time. the story was fantastic and was what was really motivating me to finish the game, the music was awesome and weapon variety was pretty dope (even though i found the rapier in my hands about 80% of my playthrough).

i think dark souls 2 is still fantastic, but just not as great overall as the others, with dark souls 2's flaws being really hard to push aside unlike the others. i'd recommend it to anyone who enjoys the other games in the catalogue but just take the bad stuff with a grain of salt and you'll get through it semi-satisfied.

this game can be summarized as a combination of minor inconveniences

- ties invincibility frames into a stat, forcing players to spend 20+ levels and delaying access to str, dex, etc..

- enemies have a comically large aggro range and will follow the player to another country if they decided to immigrate

- has some of the worst run backs out of any game FromSoftware has released

- constantly reusing bosses, and the mainline bosses are quite forgettable

- the DLC has significantly higher quality in term of boss fights, it makes the base game seem unfinished,
(fume knight and sir alonne are the very few bosses that actually mix up their moves, while the other bosses are easily predictable)

- the difficulty is artificial, and mostly derives from the devs coming up with the right number of enemies to place in an area, and then doubling that

when this game executes its original ideas flawlessly, like the ivory king fight, it can actually exceed past the two other titles, but unfortunately the mainline content in this game is unremarkable in terms of boss design

Posted: October 20, 2024
Insanely overhated game (for fair reasons). If you exclude one or two boses and areas this game is very good. Don't get me wrong, I don't mean to say that this game is better than the other souls games -even though i prefered it from ds1- nor that it doesn't have any problems, because as you probably already know... it has. The movement is clunky (although you can somewhat fix it by tweaking some settings in steam controller), a lot of hitboxes are ♥♥♥♥ (mainly on grab attacks and overhead attacks), and a lot of bosses -including the final one- are extremely weak and anticlimactic. Furthermore, the fragrant branches of yor and the pharros' lockstones mechanic is not needed. Moreover the runbacks for some bosses (like the smoulder demon, old iron king, sir alone and more importantly the twin pets in the dlc) are absolutely atrocious, and the health loss after death mechanic is a pretty major inconvenience in the early game, and many other things that I haven't listed.
On the other hand, the game also has a lot of interesting and welcome mechanics/changes. For instance, you can now freely teleport to every bonfire, you can revive npcs that might have accidentaly died, you are not only limited to estus for healing (and sometimes the fact the the healing isn't instant can come in clutch), Bonfire ascetics, Increased weapon and armor quantity and quality and (not that) many more nice inovations from the first game.
All things considered, the cons do in fact outweigh the potential pros that this game might have, but even though that might be the case, it is still a pretty good game in my opinion (or at least I enjoyed it) with great replayability that kept getting better the more you kept playing.

P.S the dlcs of this game are peak (except for that one boss runback)

I originally gave this a postive review, lol nope not anymore. I got to the bs for sure.

For context, in the 10 or so hours I've played I've died to an enemy maybe like 3 times? The other deaths were all on me for trying to make a few of the ridiculously placed jumps they put in the game for items.

I've played and loved every other FS (and other devs) soulsborne games. The normal enemies and bosses in DS2 (at least the ones I got to in the time it took to get to the tower of flame and wharf) are stupidly easy if you've played these games a lot. Slow, dumb, wide openings. Cool, right? WRONG

The horror stories about janky ass hitboxes, enemy density, and really weird AI pathfinding were true. I've never seen enemies spaz out like this in another souls game. The white knights in the flame tower area just started to breakdown and move in seemingly random directions and attack nothing.

I knew about the BS that is adaptability going in so I powered leveled it because honestly I didn't even feel like I needed points in my other stats with the Knight class because I was barley dying and doing great damage throughout the first areas. So I got my ADP to 100 or whatever which is supposed to be equal to the mid roll in DS1 in regards to Iframes. Yet...I was still regularly taking damage mid roll despite spending 20 levels on ADP lol It probably means the hit boxes were done poorly and extended past the enemy's weapon and was catching me at the end of my roll or something, but in a game like this? That's just ass. You're already at a disadvantage, but now you also need to account for bs like this due to shoddy design and programming. Pass.

But, holy sh*t the enemy density is actually abysmal. In that same area I was getting mobbed literally 5ft away from the bonfire by 1 giant enemy and 2 super aggressive tanky ones, one of which aggroed me as soon as I stood up from literally over 150 ft away, WHAT? How did he even see me?! Normally in these games, enemies are programmed with "leashes". Leashes ensure they don't do this bs and/or follow the player throughout THE ENTIRE LEVEL. I swear to god, I don't think leashes are in this game despite them being in both DS1/3 and literally every other FS Soulsborne game. You will literally have like 15 guys of varying sizes chasing you if you try to skip them for a boss/corpse run.

The level design itself, isn't bad (enemy density aside), but all areas just kinda...end. Which is confusing for a first playthrough because normally you'd find a shortcut or something to telegraph to the player that you need to go all the way back and try another way. Not in DS2, plus a lot of level transitions just are laughably bad.

Why am I just randomly sailing away on a ghost pirate ship in a Dark Souls game? F*ck it! Because it's DS2 you IDIOT LMAO- Love FS Devs

There is some good here, but there's A LOT of bad. It just reeks of a trouble development and why would you play this when pretty much every other FS game is a perfect well-oiled machine?

I may return at some point, but it just doesn't feel finished or polished which is insane for a FS game. Honestly, everything DS2 tries to do and fails at was successfully done in Elden Ring so...just play ER lol

It's also insane this game is still 40 bucks for this

Posted: October 16, 2024
If you're just starting DS2, and have the money, I would recommend checking out base DS2 first. If not, understandable, but be aware that this is a harder version of an already hard game.

Dark Souls 2 (and DS2 SoTFS) is one of, if not the best action-RPGs you will ever experience. It builds on Dark Souls 1's mechanics without taking away any of the strategic aspect of that game as Dark Souls 3 did (not to bash that game, it's good in its own right), it expands upon both the RPG and the action elements as it introduces new items, weapon types and combat styles. It asks you to take a cautious approach to each encounter, to be wary of your surroundings and it rewards you again and again for engaging with its mechanics. Crowd-control is a skill many souls fans detest but DS2's enemy design accounts for it so perfectly and allows a calculated player to both slash his way through those encounters or avoid them outright. It handles the sort of semi-linear aspect of Dark Souls 1 in an amazing way too. Boss fights are an extremely mixed bag, but overall, this game is on par with one. Yes, you have frustrating bs sometimes, but you also get bosses that challenge you in a unique way or are just incredibly fun to fight. The infamous "dudes in armor" are the best boss fights the series has to offer and Dark Souls 2 revels in that aspect of the souls games. Few bosses come close to DS1's highs, but this game has plenty of highs too, especially the DLCs. Overall it's just a consistent, extremely enjoyable experience, and its lore and world take the established and extremely subversive fantasy of Dark Souls and subvert it again, toppling its kings, burying its gods, raising new ones who are just as meaningless and asking the player to make a truly terrifying decision regarding the fate of the world. What an incredible game.

Posted: October 15, 2024
A thoroughly beautiful mess that I love.
By now many people know the backstory of DS2 and how messy it was, but in the end, it is a game that deserves far better than it gets. It experimented in ways we didn’t see again until Elden Ring. I jokingly say DS2 stumbled so Elden Ring could run.
Pros:
+Graphics are quite nice, the varied environments keep things feeling relatively fresh.
+Tons of customizability, even 3 struggled to keep up with it.
+Its story is legit interesting and is to me a very introspective tale, adding more depth to the already rich lore beyond Lordran and Anor Londo.
+Some of the best expansion content period, I’ll die on this hill but the crowns are second only to Shadow of the Erdtree.
+Some of the best PvP hands down.
+Oodles of secrets to find.
+While some Bosses are meh, there are some very amazing boss fights to have (especially in the Crowns content).
+Different covenants actually impact gameplay, not just summons.
+Bonfire Aesthetics are a ton of fun.
+Majula is just a very nice hub.
+Origin of fashion souls!
Cons:
-Final boss leaves a lot to be desired.
-Finite respawns of enemies can be annoying.
-Soul memory is a double edged sword.
-The meh boss battles are exceptionally mid and lack a level of oomph that all other games give to bosses.
-The disjointed level structures/transitions can be jarring at times.
-Adp is one of the worst ideas ever!
Final thoughts:
DS2 boldly tries a large number of ideas, some hit and others miss. But the whole package is in my humble opinion still a very excellent game that is very much worth playing and if you have finished Elden Ring and crave more, this may be more satisfying than DS 1&3 in terms of vibe and feel. It’s a great game with a crummy reputation it is well worth the time.

Before playing this game myself, I used to agree with people who curse and mock this game for being the worst Souls game that Fromsoftware has ever made. I began to appreciate the environments and the world-building of this game, they are very different than the other titles which makes the exploration feel interesting and fun. The bosses are very unique but a lot of them are very frustrating due to their hitboxes being straight bad. The combat doesn't feel that bad, you just need to get along with it and it will be fine.

The conclusion is, that this game is worth buying only if you consider yourself a true Soulsborne fan like myself. Other than that, I can't guarantee you to enjoy this game as much as I do.

The neglected Middle child of the Souls series and the last Souls game I played before ED and BB.

An interesting entry to the franchise, it strays far from the path set by the first game and takes us to the land of Drangleic, a delapidated kingdom now in crumbling ruins looking for someone who can rekindle the fire and start the cycle anew.

It has some HORSESH*T level design. Some of the armour looks hideous. Repeated boss fights and "Oh Look, The Normal Enemy is a Boss now! how quirky!" happen more than i'd like and there's a lot of bosses with out of arena gimmicks you need to basically pull off before even attempting the boss because they just add layers of difficulty.

However, the story, the setting, the aesthetic, the general aura the game gives off, they're all very unique, a feeling you wont find in any other souls game. The difficulty is sort of ridiculous in this, think Bloodborne with DS1's movement. Of course, the upside is this game has really good hitboxes and even better swing patterns for weapons.

An excellent game at its core, overshadowed by its even more excellent brothers. Highly recommend using this one for your challenge runs, the difficulty is off the chain in some sections. It will have you crying for a higher power to help you eventually.

Posted: October 13, 2024
i cannot put into words how much i despise this game and its alterations to the core gameplay mechanics of dark souls. every moment was misery exploring baffling level designs with so many enemies i didn't even bother to actually look around. any intended environment mechanics i came across were pure happenstance or fleeting moments of respite from the incessant hordes. exploration in this game was so miserable that i ended up getting only around three quarters of all the bonfires in this game. it feels intended for you to have a guide at all times that tells you exactly where to go and what to do, which completely ruins the point of exploring for yourself, not that i addressed guides or asked for help unless i had gotten so confused that i felt i had no choice.

the bosses in this game are some of the worst in the series, most of them feeling so slow and simple to overcome that i ended up beating the vast majority of them on my first attempt. there are a few standout exceptions, such as looking-glass knight and fume knight, but this game's mechanical changes are so utterly moronic that i just wish they had been put into different games. not even the final bosses are safe from being easy as all hell, making up for their shoddy difficulty by incorporating stupid gimmicks which make the fights take longer as you work around them. standard enemy encounters are more dangerous than most of this game's bosses, the game would not change in difficulty if you were to cut out every single boss fight.

changing the way estus flasks and dodge rolls worked from the original was essentially a cardinal sin, especially with how pathetically slow using estus feels. you have to commit so hard to using your estus that by the time you've actually restored health, you're having marital problems with it. and i almost feel as if this is where many of the problems with the game design stem from, the absolute slowness and clunk of your own character. the bosses should be for the most part built around how your character plays, and if controlling your character is flawed, that would no doubt lead to numerous issues when it comes to design. they sacrificed something you learn and made it something you earn. the only way to get more dodge roll i-frames and healing use speed is to level adaptability, which is an entirely strange decision to make considering the consistency of the dodge roll and estus use made them learning mechanics that stuck with you even through different characters. the timings of some of this game's central mechanics are not even set in stone, which makes it harder to judge when you should do the things you want to do. (not that you'll have a problem against most of these bosses, given how long it takes them to attack.)

the most fun i've derived out of this game is invading unsuspecting players. i've literally never wanted to do soulsborne pvp until this game sucked the life out of me and left me searching for anything fun to do. i tried the pvp arenas when i ran out of cracked red eye orbs, but everyone there was way stronger than me, all i'd really do is lose after buzzing around like a lunatic fly with my fragile build for a minute. still more fun than most of the bosses, though.


in short, most of the bad things you hear about this game are true. the hate had to come from somewhere, after all. and if you're looking for a souls game to play, pick this one if you're a masochist for bad game design.


also, i wouldn't consider this totally relevant to my thoughts on the game's design, but it straight up corrupted after randomly freezing one time. no idea why it happened, but i really should've taken it as a sign to stop playing.

The short & sweet:

Without even having finished all the content yet, I can confidently say, even as the "Black Sheep" of the trilogy, this is an awesome title and deserves more praise. I will be sinking plenty more hours in. Can recommend.



The long explanation:

DS2 has a reputation among the souls community that maligns it, and that is upsetting having enjoyed it as much as I have. I came fresh off of a replay of DS1 to finish it, and the first 10-15 hours did feel a bit frustrating, until I stopped trying to play this title like its predecessor and listened to what it was teaching (As you should in any souls title).

It operates quite a bit differently, most areas aren't built for you to speed through avoiding all the enemies, grabbing loot and finding bonfires/bosses. This title wants you to fight those battles a lot more, but rewards you for doing so. You can actually "Clear" areas, as in, no more enemies will spawn (Unless you use an item to make them). Doing this becomes extremely useful for leveling up, gearing up, and getting loot, all the while slowly carving a path towards the boss. So you can legitimately just make any run backs a nice leisurely uninterrupted walk if you'd like. At the end of the day, I felt far more prepared and pretty strong in a lot of the bossfights for DS2 than I did in most of them for DS1. In fact, this method of farming your level up feels more reliable stage-to-stage and less gimmicky than many methods used in other Fromsoft titles.

There are more stats, some of which are highly important regardless of build (ADP), but as such you pick up levels more quickly. The hub area has an awesome vibe that borders on a restless melancholy, and this game has a less gritty more colorful cast of characters to interact with while still feeling like a souls title and hitting you with the dark fantasy tropes you might expect from time to time.

Finally, this is far less linear even from the start as once you make it to the hub you get this sort of branching outward in all directions feel, with a general goal given to you. You can immediately travel from any one bonfire to another, and I prefer DS2's inventory interface to DS1 by a large margin.

All of the above points combine to make a game that is very unique even when compared to the other 2 of the same trilogy and not just all fromsoft games. However, I would posit, that it is the difference that truly makes this title great and worth your play.

Posted: October 10, 2024
There is something about this game that brings me a lot of nostalgia every time I replay it. Not sure if it's because it was my first souls game I beat or the whole atmosphere of it. The game, while being most commonly known as the black sheep of the souls family, is a masterpiece on its own, if you give it a chance. It's packed with content and areas to explore and a huge arsenal of weapons and spells to fit your build. On top of that, it allows for powerstancing to mix up your playstyle. The story, while being more disconnected from the rest of the series, is still amazing and worth going through. NPCs are well written, DLCs are hell of a experience and let's not forget about probably the most beautiful hub out of all the games in the series - Majula. A gorgeous small village by the sea, where you truly feel at ease and relaxed when returning to it to level up.

Some areas can be very challenging, but not impossible to overcome. Bosses for me are personally on the easier scale and could be improved here and there to make it more interesting. What I'd also like to see is no soul memory, or at least not tied for pvp or co-op, to allow for easier matchmaking if pvp and co-op are your things. It still works, but it could be something like in the other games in the series.

The game is by any means not perfect, but that doesn't mean it doesn't deserve a chance.

Dark Souls 2

After loving Dark Souls 1, I jumped into Dark Souls 2 expecting the same level of challenge and excitement, but my experience was a bit different. Don’t get me wrong, I enjoyed the game overall, but it felt like a different kind of challenge—one that didn’t always land right.

Boss Fights – Surprisingly Easy
The bosses in Dark Souls 2 were... well, kind of easy. Almost every boss I fought, I either beat on the first try or after a couple of attempts. It wasn’t like in Dark Souls 1 where every boss was an uphill battle, pushing me to my limits. Here, I didn’t feel the same sense of accomplishment when I defeated a boss, and by the time I got to Nashandra, the final boss, it was honestly disappointing. I was expecting a grand, memorable fight to close the game, but it ended up being way too simple and underwhelming.

Enemies and Areas – Frustratingly Unfair
While the bosses were easy, the regular enemies and areas were a different story. Some of the enemy placements felt really unfair, almost like the game was trying to cheap-shot me. Ambushes around every corner, enemies spamming attacks, and some areas like Shrine of Amana were just plain brutal for the wrong reasons. It felt less like a fair challenge and more like the game was throwing obstacles at me to frustrate me rather than test my skill.

Exploration – Still Fun, but Lacking the Magic
The world design in Dark Souls 2 didn’t have the same interconnected magic that I loved in the first game. Don’t get me wrong, there were still cool locations to explore, and I enjoyed wandering through the areas, but it lacked that feeling of discovering something truly special. It felt more segmented and less cohesive. The atmosphere was still strong, but the world itself didn’t pull me in the same way.

Final Thoughts
Dark Souls 2 was a fun experience overall, but it was a bit of a letdown compared to Dark Souls 1. The bosses were way too easy for the most part, and the real challenge came from frustrating enemy encounters and unfair area design. That being said, I still enjoyed playing through it—it’s got some cool moments, and I’d still recommend it for fans of the series. Just don’t expect the same level of epic boss fights or world design.

7/10 – fun but frustrating in the wrong ways.

It's a bit hard to recommend this game, as it didn't live up to the expectations set by DS1, and also introduced a slew of problems that weren't present in the original.

My problems with DS2 are the bad hitboxes (♥♥♥♥-boxes) as sometimes dodging out of an enemy's attack is not enough, and you can get some cheap deaths (ogres are a prime example of this and are found early on in the game). It really feels like the enemies can track your movement with great accuracy at any time. The Soul Memory system tried to fix invasions to some extent, offering different Soul Memory ranges to even the playing field, but this system was easily exploited by veteran players of the series, making it more of a hassle than a fix. The reused bosses (dudes in armor) are less memorable than the bosses found in DS1 (there are some that I enjoyed, like The Darklurker, Sir Alonne), and the NPCs are a bit less memorable (still, there are some standouts, like the Emerald Herald, Gavlan, Vendrick). I also kinda dislike that dying now also reduces max HP (you get used to it, but I don't feel it added much). Also, the level design is less interconnected, and the way some sections of the world are connected feels very random and less cohesive, and there is no real sense of place in the world. Another annoyance is the downgrade to darkness, as the game was more moody when first revealed, the torches played a major role in exploration, but in the release version, the game's much brighter, and I almost never used a torch.

Scholar of the First Sin, is the best way to play DS2, as it offers all the previous DLCs, makes some changes to enemy placement (some for the better and others for the worse), and adds a new NPC to the game, which adds some story bits here and there. The graphics are somewhat improved (minimal changes), but the game looks a bit better.

The build variety is still great, and there are a lot of viable options to play DS2. The places you explore are pretty interesting and visually distinct for the most part. The game's also playable out of the box with mouse and keyboard, and the port doesn't have as many problems as the DS1 one (personally, I still recommend using a controller, as the game's more enjoyable that way).

Overall, an OK addition to the series, frustrating at times and can be entirely skipped, as it pales in comparison to the first Dark Souls.

I did it. I ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ did it. Every Fromsoftware game on pc finished, and this was the game i saved for last. It all started with buying Dark Souls: Remastered in 2019 and rage quitting at asylum demon because i thought you had to kill him. Five years later and after finishing dark souls 2: SOTFS I actually would recommend any fan of the series play this. But play it last. Have the knowledge and experience of their other games which are much more polished and defined. Systems like adaptability will make no sense if you don't know what i-frames are and that this stat, for whatever reason, boosts agility which is basically i-frame count. Play other choices like elden ring or dark souls: remastered first, so when you get stabbed by the pursuer when you were fully 90 degrees away from his sword, you laugh it off.

To be honest, I'm not sure if it was from my experience in other souls games or what, but I found this one to be the "easiest" after the first 15 hours of pure frustration. But once your stats settle and you get the hell out of the Lost Bastille, the game feels much better. I ended up one shotting, or if not less than 5 attempts, most bosses. The atmosphere and ambiance, tho, is superb. To be honest, if this game got a TRUE remaster and it had the mechanical standards of DS3 or Elden Ring (goodbye ADP), I think this game would go up by leagues. I still need to finish the 3 DLC areas, but this game is absolutely worth playing.

I'd liken it in a way to the Majora's Mask of the series: strange, new mechanics that sometimes truly work and others which didn't make a comeback to the series again. Even down to its odd, surreal tone and feelings of pure hopelessness. Lost in the cycle of rekindling the flame.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is a definitive edition that not only enhances the original experience but also deepens the lore and atmosphere of this beloved title. FromSoftware’s signature blend of challenging gameplay and intricate world design is on full display, making it a must-play for both veterans and newcomers to the series.

The core gameplay remains true to the Dark Souls formula, delivering a challenging yet rewarding experience. Combat is fluid and responsive, requiring precise timing and strategy. The adjustments to enemy placements and AI in Scholar of the First Sin elevate the difficulty, ensuring that even seasoned players will face new challenges. Each encounter feels intense, with the ever-present threat of death looming around every corner.

The multiplayer component is seamlessly integrated, allowing for cooperative play and competitive invasions. The ability to summon friends or face off against other players adds a unique dynamic to the experience, fostering a sense of camaraderie and rivalry that enhances the overall gameplay. The atmospheric sound design, combined with a haunting score, immerses players in the dark, brooding world of Dark Souls II. Each sound, from the clanging of weapons to the distant howls of foes, contributes to the overall tension and suspense. The ambiance creates a palpable sense of isolation, making victories feel even more rewarding.

My first experience with a Souls-like game and it was the most fun I've had in years.
In little to no time I got immersed into these beautiful scenarios and decaying world fighting my way through to get to the throne.

Now that I am playing DS Remastered so I can get to DS3, I fully appreciate all the risks Fromsoftware took while making this, risking their butts with some ideas that make people think this is the worst Dark Souls of the series without actually realizing that all those small decisions made all their Souls Like games, even Elden Ring, better on the long run.
The best example for this is the focus on consumables like the Bonfire Ascetic for NG+ areas in your first playthrough or the cool odd weapons/builds that exists in this game, and I mean, stances?. I fell in love with the Santiers Spear, that thing is fun as ♥♥♥♥; sadly it is the only game where this weapon is into the souls saga. But you get the idea, they really tried to improve and create new ways for this game to be even more awesome and challenging in the right ways, even by failing in some aspects on the way making the experience for some areas harder or more frustrating than it could had been.

I died a total of 450-ish times playing the whole thing including DLCs, so even if myself, sucking that much and struggling like a chimp with a controller, enjoyed the game this much, I am positive anybody that put themselves into embracing the struggle could do it as well.

I saw the differences between Vanilla and SotFS editions, this one takes the point for me, it is better and more enjoyable. Even more challenging in some aspects.

Give it a try, endure the pain, make it through. You won't regret what you are capable of.

Posted: October 4, 2024
This game... is so peak its actually outrageous. I went into this game aware of its reputation, determined to complete it and fearing the worst. And it is very flawed of course. But I've never had so much fun in a game. DS2 really feels like you and Fromsoft are at that point in the friendship when you're such good friends you can make fun of each other. The game feels like a mischievous little brother: unpredictable, chaotic and far more energetic than the other games.
You can just be opening a chest and then you spot a barrel moving towards you (like something out of prop hunt) only for an NPC invader to jump out and try to backstab you.
Or you open a chest and a crossbow comes out and starts blasting you.
Or this one NPC invader who disguised himself as and pretended to be friendly only to try backstabbing me when I turned around. Then after we fought for a bit he literally just ran away and I lost him behind a corner. Then like 30 minutes later, I'm fighting the area's boss like 10 miles away and this guy from earlier just jumps at me from behind and now I've gotta fight him at the same time
There was so many moments that just made my jaw drop of disbelief, both from the game's mischief, beauty and badassery.
The only outright bad things in the game are Agility and Soul Memory. All the other things people complained about I either really enjoyed or barely noticed. Hollowing and durability are both really neat mechanics that keep you on your toes. Being able to be invaded while hollow is a genius choice which I wish stayed for the future games. The covenants are really cool, Rat King for the win of course. NPCs have some interesting lore and questlines (although admittedly some do feel half-baked). The areas are all extremely distinctive and cool. The bosses, while not as memorable as the iconic bosses of DS1 nor as challenging and fast-paced as DS3, are still awesome and there's some really outstanding boss fights in there. And just so many awesome ideas. Again the game is clearly unpolished and loads of parts of it feel underutilised and rushed but to me that sort of adds to the charm and it all still made for some great gameplay.
I could gush about this game all day. It's good. Give it a chance.

Posted: October 1, 2024
Writing this review after finishing DS3 (played the DS series in order).
Putting the review here because DS2 is my favorite. DS1 is a very close second.
Played all using mouse + keyboard.

These games feel like Metroid Prime trilogy (not in difficulty!!!):
Prime 1 makes you soil your pants cause it was so unique, banging soundtrack and awesome pixel worlds.
Prime 2 is edgy Prime 1.
Prime 3 is the culmination of Retro's learning. It's nice but it's not as memorable as 1 or 2. Same thing for DS3, it feels like a remaster of DS1 Remaster. Kind of like Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom to a Zelda: Breath of the Wild.

--DS3 has the "best" bosses. Soul of Cinder felt like Manus on meth (to me) and that's a good thing. Anor Londo is really ugly in this game and there aren't many areas that made me feel "WOW THIS IS SO SICK."
--DS2 is basically a 3D Zelda game. Has the best character art style, most charm and possesses elements of comfort (Majula) not found in DS1 or 3. Plays the best on mouse + keyboard out of the 3 games. The map in this game is an absolute pain in the ass. Finding Drangleic is like finding Narnia. The Old Chaos is hands down the most hyped I got throughout the entire series. You gonna tell me the boys roll out to hell and you're like "ok cool", please control yourself. Beating the snake lady in her own poison because you didn't know about the windmill? Peak.
--DS1 is DS1 come on man. Legendary game. I still think about DS1 map layout from time to time. HURRAH FOR ANOR LONDO!!! Artorias and Manus are peak. Having to perfectly roll ceaseless discharge because you didn't know about the cheese, yea it's an excellent game.

All in all I would recommend the entire trilogy. The struggle you experience in these games is very real and unique to your playstyle. Try to learn for yourself and avoid looking at too many YouTube videos/guides if you can. What you will find is a way to play that works for YOU!

NOTE: Consider playing these games on a controller.

Thank You FromSoft :)

Posted: September 27, 2024
I never played the original Dark Souls 2. Of the mainline souls games, this is probably my favorite.

I like the plot, especially the parts related to the queen and the giants, although it would have made less sense without the addition of Aldia in this version. Its relationship to DS 1 is also fairly tangential and the game does complicate any attempt at a series through line or timeline.

The character quests are significantly expanded from DS 1 and bringing people to the starting area feels more impactful.

The addition of hex magic is appreciated. You can see the influence of this a bit in later titles with things like dark sorceries or pyromancies.

A lot of my love for the game is due to the DLCs, which add a lot of items and more challenging/interesting enemies and bosses along with much improved level design.

NG+ also adds extra enemies throughout the world to make it more difficult, something I didn't notice in DS 1 or 3. Granted, they're just about impossible to miss in this one due to being bright red.

It took a long time to 100% due to the need to reach Ng++ in order to buy the last couple spells without engaging in the extremely dead multiplayer covenants.

It's a very divisive game, generally considered the worst in the series, but something about it is just very approachable and it's the first one I reach for when the mood hits.

Notice to all new players of the DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin. Even if the game is almost over ten years old. It still has an active hacker issue, as I was able to play approx. 12 hours before hacker invaded my world, force-injecting my inventory with “illegal items” of 1200 poison binoculars and killed me. Once respawning and opening the inventory, scrolling to these items my game would always crash every time.

There was suggestions on DS2 reddit to use mod or play in offline. Offline mode means going to Steam > Go Offline > Open the game.

There is an mention in mod page that using the mod won’t get you soft banned, but there isn’t really 100% proof from developers side about it.

If you are already a victim of injection hack, there are couple of NPCs whom you can sold items to. For me it took approx. 25 minutes to sell 1200 items > Save > Quit > Sync the save to Steam > Start the game in offline mode.

Got to say, , as hackers are not in any danger getting banned, but the victims can and will. Only way to find who invaded your game world is open Steam > View > Players.




+ Completely own vision of other DS games
+ Multiple mid-game soul farms
+ Unique bosses (and other boss mechanics)

+/- Level complexity design, getting lost eventually
+/- Adaptability + Poise, just what the f.. happened
+/- Weapon & Armor degrade system

- Guaranteed few “and how the f*ck should I have known that” moments
- Few zones can get bugged (enemies standing still, unkillable, but skippable)
- Hacker danger in online mode without mods or offline mode

Is it as bad as people say? No. Not even close. Most of the issues in this game are recurring in previous titles and even all the way to Elden RIng. It's things that are pretty much in every of these games. Adaptation, although kind of a bad design, is NOWHERE near ruining the game like so many people say. Group bosses horrible? No. Are they all good, then? Absolutely not. Most of them are fine, you just got to stop relying on your lock on and you'll be able to do group bosses super well. However... PvP? It's really good and very fun. BUT, the servers are old and... hum... well... Hope you like being hit 5 seconds later by nothing after the host you're invading swings at the air. Yeaaah... lag can be.... VERY BAD.

So in short: Game is good. People are mostly mad at this game because it ain't a carbon copy of DS1.

Posted: September 16, 2024
Having recently finished the original Dark Souls, it was only natural that I would jump into the contentious sequel that is Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin.

The visual fidelity is hugely improved over the original. There were many times I found myself awestruck by some of the vistas and immense structures towering over my head.

Unfortunately, there are also a lot of things this game does worse than the last, and I can't write a review in good faith without mentioning them. Firstly, the level design and balancing in DS2 is not good. Many areas feel very obtuse and confusingly constructed. Areas that aren't confusing are poorly balanced. I found myself having a pretty tough time at the beginning of the game but soon there was almost no challenge at all save for one or two bosses. The controls also feel unresponsive and I had to rely on Steam's controller overlay to fix the square dead zones that for some reason were chosen for this title. Certain choices like allowing the player to swing in the completely wrong direction after a roll (while being locked on), and not being able to queue up items after an action feel poorly thought out and clunky.

Despite the flaws of DS2, the game still earns a recommendation from me. Plain and simple: the game is fun, the world is huge, and the sheer amount of content is well worth the money. If you like what made Dark Souls such a beloved franchise, you will have fun. The reason the shortcomings of this game spark so much frustration from me is because I hold it to the very high standard that the first game set.

Posted: September 15, 2024
By far my favorite of the series and one of my top 5 favorite games ever.

First of all, difficulty is ON POINT, but only if you are willing to look for creative solutions in situations that seem to be annoyingly hard. Yes, you should carry and use a bow with different types of ammo here in addition to a melee weapon and, optionally, magic.

The game has a special atmosphere that is supported by the genius soundtrack which is maybe the best music FROM has ever done, the dream-like world that does not fit together with castles in the sky and caves in the ocean, giving you the feeling of… “perhaps you have seen it, maybe in a dream”. The game feels spooky, maybe in a way as if Burton has run by, and its world is simply lovely.

The slow combat pacing is great, combat feels really exact and precise once you understood how it is meant, the rasterized movement directions are an improvement over DS1 and the game is never unfair unlike its predecessor.

The game wants to teach you, so sit and learn. Learn about greed, about anger, about satisfaction, about addiction, about solution-oriented thinking and much more. And enjoy while doing that, and have yourself sucked in a world full of joy.

Only a few bugs and glitches, hitboxes are less of a problem than people tell you, only minor downsides like weapon durability and by far less bu**sh*t than you find in Dark Souls 1 (like being hit through walls and stuff). And yes, agility is cool!

285 hours on the record, multiple playthroughs with almost every kind of character build, including all bosses in new game+ with hex witch, and so on.

Honestly, the hate this game gets is ENTIRELY UNDESERVED. On my first playthrough, i had a rough experience- generally siding with the public about DS2 being a "bad game". However, now that ive come back for a 2nd playthrough and have returned with an open mind and new perspective, Dark Souls 2 has quickly become one of my favorite in the FromSoft repertoire.
Super diverse level design, awesome armor and weapons, super cool side quests, unique enemy design... Its all so great.
That being said, would i recommend Dark Souls 2 to someone new to the Souls-Like genre/As a first game to try in the series? Absolutely not- Simply because of how overwhelming/confusing it can be at times, along with some shoddy hitboxes and the like.
Would i recommend giving it a shot to anyone else who HAS played other souls games? 1000x YES!!!

All in all, Dark Souls 2 is a fantastic game and addition to the SoulsBorne series, and id HIGHLY recommend picking it up and playing it through- and please DO NOT let the general public's opinion of the game sway or affect your outlook/opinion on the game itself before you've even tried it for yourself.

The combat is also really good. I don't understand the talk of every level being a gank fight. The same mfs will run past every single enemy, get to a door, and then complain that they got ganked and ran out of estus (even though this was intentional because the game adds consumable heals and also makes the enemies in an area disappear over time). It also has the most replayability and build variety out of the trilogy thanks to the addition of things like power stancing and the mundane infusion. The combat and movement is not nearly as bad as people say, and it's kind of grown on me; It feels like the character has actual weight that I am controlling around and personally I like that. DS2 strikes a nice balance between the fast paced dodge heavy combat of ds3 and the slow and methodical combat of ds1. I love this game and I think people just don't like it because they are coming into the game with the wrong mindset and that it's different.

Posted: September 7, 2024
Dark Souls 2 is probably the most controversial game that I ever played. The community is split in half, many people would say it's the best while the others will say it's bad. I'll try to give it a fair review based on my personal experience. Fair warning: the game doesn't have steam cloud saves so you should back up your files if you take a break to prevent data loss. It also took me some time and a youtube guide to configure the buttons to be usable with MnK similar to DS3, so it has the worst PC support, but it was worth the time. NO, you don't have to listen to the fanbois telling you to play with a controller, the best experience is using the input you're most comfortable with.

I'll start with the negatives and I will say that Dark Souls 2 it's objectively the worst received FromSoftware game. This is the last souls entry I tried and I intentionally changed the order I played them based on some recommendations from friends. It has so many flaws in the design that only a true souls enjoyer will be able to have fun and not rage quit. It is considered harder than the others in the series, but I think that this statement is not true, at least in the grand scheme of things. I feel like the "difficulty" is artificially inflated by the unfair game mechanics such as bigger hitboxes, slow combat, slow healing, no immunity while passing through fog walls, the enemy placement, souls memory, the addition of adaptability that affects i-frames and other weird choices. If you take into consideration the fact that you can have hundreds of healing items, the only challenge is knowing when to find a window to heal yourself, because you'll get stuck in a long animation that most of the times will result in a guaranteed hit. In reality, the bosses are poorly designed (with a few exceptions) and don't stand much of a fight, but the runbacks is what makes them "memorable": Blue and normal Smelter Demon, Ancient Dragon, Dark Lurker, Lud&Zallen and probably the list can go on depending on your own experience. While some areas are incredibly beautiful, they are painful to traverse even on consecutive runs, the worst of them for me being Shrine of Amana, mostly because if you don't use a bow you need to close the gap to the ranged enemies that can hit like a truck, while also having non efficient dodges in the water and being careful where you step because the visibility is reduced and you can fall to your death in no time. Speaking of low visibility, the Frigid Outskirts is absolutely terrible, coming in the second place in the bad designed zones category.

This game feels more like an amalgamation of ideas, so there are definitely some interesting ones. In fact, I strongly believe that Elden Ring could not run if Dark Souls 2 didn't walk in the first place. This is the first time where the dual power stance was introduced and for some reason it wasn't reused until the monumental success of the 2022 GOTY winner. This gives the player a lot more options for different builds, resulting in more playstyles and a higher value of replayability. This mechanic alone might be the best thing that FromSoftware could add to their games and I hope to see it as a baseline of their future products. Another good mechanic that was recycled is the Pharros' Lockstones (Sword Keys) that allows the user to enter optional/hidden areas using a limited item. While the lore doesn't have too many connections with the Dark Souls universe presented in DS1 and DS3, it's interesting enough and it had a big influence over the tarnished story, making it a good experience for those curious to find out how the Bearer of the Curse became a Monarch. I mentioned that the game has some beautiful environment and it would be a disgrace to not make a point about Majula that is the best hub area in any souls game, it's beautiful, it's visually stunning, the atmosphere is amazing and it's really the place where anyone can agree that you can spend hours feeling safe from the dangerous foes that lurk the lands.

While I consider the bosses to be lackluster in the difficulty department, those from the 3 DLCs were very challenging and I had a good time defeating them. In fact, if we disregard the annoying runbacks, they are very close to Dark Souls 3 design, which I'm a big fan of. Some of my favorites are the Fume Knight, Sir Alonne, Sinh the Slumbering Dragon and Burnt Ivory King + Aava the King's Pet for having the requirement of exploring the map and finding an item to make it visible to the human eye. Another underrated mechanic that was never reused is the bonfire ascetic that allows the player to respawn any item and boss in an area, but on a higher NG+ difficulty. This can trivialize the entire game by farming specific powerful rings that can only be found in consecutive runs or by farming boss souls in order to overlevel the current world. Speaking of which, another good idea was the addition of different enemies in NG+ and beyond. However, the game already had too much density of monsters by default, but I really think this can work with other titles and result in a unique experience for those who are willing to take the challenge after beating the game once.

Overall, Dark Souls 2 is better than most of the RPG games on the market, it receives a solid 7/10 from me. I can only recommend it to those who already are experienced fans and want more souls content.

Posted: September 6, 2024
The immersion in this game is incredible. It's like I can actually feel myself losing my sanity and going hollow

EDIT: Finished the game with close to 100% completion, and I would absolutely recommend it to any fans of the series, but maybe not as an introductory title. First of all, the areas in this game are absolutely gorgeous, definitely my favorite game visually. The fashion souls potential is on point and many of the weapon designs are very unique and cool. Certain items like the spider fang, smelter swords, and FUGS are some of my favorites. I really admire this game's inventiveness and creativity. It's a shame we didn't get powerstancing back until Elden Ring, and lifegems/bonfire ascetics should have been a permanent staple in the series. This game also has my favorite story out of the 3 dark souls games. Many of the bosses and characters feel more fleshed out than the other games. I'd also like to especially mention the DLC areas. They're all visually stunning, and have some of the best bosses in the whole game. I also love that the community for this game has its own distinct sense of humor, with "skeleton," "horse," and "left/right" being some of my favorite bits while playing through the game.

Now, as much as I can sing this game's praises, it definitely isn't without flaw. First of all, people will go back and forth on whether the game is a "gank-fest" or whether there are "too many enemies," and I tend to lean towards there being so. Certain areas like iron keep, brume tower, and no man's wharf can be just painful to traverse, and although luring enemies out one-by-one or using bows can make this significantly easier, it takes so long to the point where it can sometimes feel like a grind. Huntsman's copse was also pretty bad in this regard. Also, I would say your character just genuinely feels worse than DS1. Your roll often feels less effective, and while leveling ADP isn't as bad as some people say, even with it maxes you still often feel like your roll isn't protecting you at all. Also, armor is way less useful than DS1, and your overall lack of poise in this game can pair particularly nastily with the large amounts of enemies in some of this game's areas. I wish estus healed you more quickly like DS1. Being invaded while hollow is no fun either, especially mid-way through Shrine of Amana.

Overall, Dark Souls 2 is somewhat of a flawed masterpiece in my opinion, with a ton of fresh ideas, some of which were executed a bit better than others. The truly beautiful moments in this game are somewhat held back by certain moments where the game is just genuinely not fun to play. Gank notwithstanding, this game is absolutely worth every penny.

TLDR: game gud, get gud, don't give up, skeleton!

Posted: September 5, 2024
EDITED AFTER COMING BACK TO FINISH IT AFTER DS3: Everything stated below is still absolutely true for me. The gameplay feels as if it's made to frustrate you at times, and SOTFS has specifically added a ton of enemies not present in DS2 originally to make it "harder". It relies far too much on outnumbering you. The boss fights are often comically easy compared to getting around in the game world. It's also even more opaque about quests and what you need to do than any other Dark Souls game, surprisingly.

For the good, the story is actually REALLY good. It is possibly better than Dark Souls 1, with a very fulfilling ending added by SOTFS. I do think DS3 is better in every way, still. The locations are sometimes very nice visually, with some really enjoyable level design, and the progression of the game, after the first few hours, is actually totally not terrible. However, this may be because I shifted from "wretch into dex build" to "bonk", which is my go-to for souls games in general. A lil poise and the ability to force downtime was critical to making things less frustrating.

It's a real shame the game seems to be committed to making you hate it. Do NOT play this game after Dark Souls 1, come back to it after DS3 to perhaps get some context on "how did we get here", or something, instead. The positioning and responsiveness you're asked for is closer to DS3, with the responsiveness by the game being about the same as DS1 (or worse), and you'll be much better adapted to handle it immediately after DS3 or Elden Ring.

ORIGINAL REVIEW:
Coming from Elden Ring and Dark Souls 1, while there is a lot of matching artistic vision (despite not being a Miyazaki-led effort, or so I hear) and the game has a lot going for it visually and thematically, it is just... not fun. Mechanics have been implemented which only make it more tedious and frustrating: longer delays on actions, only being able to level in one place (which, while a nice idea, makes early levels a menu-fest), and rolling is tied to a stat now. Everything in this game takes so long, and the controls' responsiveness is so bad that it's incomprehensible - I queue in motions with a dagger in 5 business days, but only sometimes, and sprinting only works sometimes as well, regardless of how much stamina I have. The combat seems... fine, I guess, but feeling like I am not in control of my character and being roll-caught constantly by barely-telegraphed moves is not it.

Honestly, I can believe this being a fun game for people who commit to working through it, but I have better things to do than being purposefully frustrated. This is NOT Happy Souls.

IF YOU CARE ABOUT LORE, READ THIS:



this franchise does not offer you proper codices where every lore bit is properly annotated and cataloged. You are expected to keep tabs of everything if you want to understand what little you are told about the world and its characters.

You will solely rely on dialogues and the descriptions of armor, weapons and other objects in your inventory to learn 95% of the lore.

You are shown some of these on the loading screens, but these last like two seconds, so have prepared your screenshot shortcut to review them on the Steam Overlay.

no matter how much you try to find every character and complete every possible unknown quest line, you will most certainly miss stuff. so once you finish the game you will need to go to YouTube and listen to all dialogues from all the characters to make sure you lisen what you missed and remember what you forgot. and then check the wiki and read the in-game description of every object, armor, weapon, spell, and trinket the game has. some descriptions were changed with the release of the Scholar Of The First Sin Edition, so the old ones either were retcnoned or reveled too much? who knows.

There is an official book guide of the game with descriptions of most places, enemies and NPCs in the game which will interest you, so make sure to read these after you finish the game.




My review of the game: cool world, but I am never replaying this again unless it gets a remake. There is little joy and too much frustration.



NON-SPOILER TIPS:

-write on a paper a detailed description of the location of a misterious thing, device or locked door you may find in your travels. so you know you still have to check stuff up before you finish the game. don't expect the game to offer you any help with that.

-the town is on the northern coastline of the land, so you know what direction people mean when they tell you to go East or whatever else.

-if you are going to sell stuff and consume souls of specific characters, first screenshot their descriptions if they offer some lore in them, so you can check stuff up when you get to overwelmed with information.

-if a player-message tells you to jump, 99% sure it will lead you to your death. if it tells you there is a hidden passage throuhg a wall, 80% sure it will be a lie.

Posted: September 3, 2024
Take the recommendation with a grain of salt. Dark Souls 2 (or at least SotFS) is a game that very quickly flip flops from being an actual enjoyable experience to some of the most dull and frustrating things I have ever played. There's a lot of small details and QoL changes in DS2 that are genuinely worth giving credit to, such as power stancing, introducing the boss soul mechanic, immediate fast travel, and other aspects that would eventually become mainstays of subsequent titles Fromsoft has made since, as well as other small things most people would never see. However, at the same time, it introduces many mechanics that are needless and frustrating, such as everyone's favourite new stat of Agility. But if you turn away from all that, the game has a big issue with quantity over quality. Some areas and bosses are legitimately enjoyable to progress through and fight, but the overwhelming majority of them are ugly to look at and poorly designed (for levels) or extremely simple and overly easy (for bosses). There is no challenging part of the game that is also fun and engaging, as all difficult sections are only hard due to cheap difficulty boosts, like flooding an area with status effects and more enemies than you could see, or having so many boss fights outnumbering you. Overall, if you aren't dedicated to playing every Fromsoft title, I'd probably skip this one and instead play one of the more polished games that they've come out with.

Posted: August 27, 2024
Spoilers @ the end, caution. Onto the review...

Absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ incredible. 10/10, and I'm not even finished with the game yet. 42 Hours in as of this review, with what appears to be around 85% complete. I am using a Steam community guide by DrummerBoy, just as I did with DS1. His guides are top notch. Few more details. I am also using some mods - Lighting Engine Mod (more advanced renderer than in DS3), exceeding ER in many areas graphically (check out my screen shots) and lastly a deadzone controller mod which basically takes the square deadzone and makes it just like ER, or in DS1/DS3 being circular. With those updates out of the way what did I think of the game overall? Here are some quick bits...

It does many unique things and I'd say is the most experimental souls game. Some things it does never to be seen again in a FromSoft game. There are a ton of npcs, although less memorable ones than in DS1 by weight. Build variety is the best only equaled by ER. Online pvp has been the best I have encountered. It has the best NG+ Mode per the community although I have yet to try it. Prepare for a slower more committed experience. Enemies are more aggressive and will chase you madly, in many cases making the rush tactic a stupid one. I literally saw some enemies that will respect you if you fight fairly and not run away like a coward. If you've completed DS1 then you know about making choices. It's no different here. Kill an npc, that's it. Do this or that, that's it. It is a lot more hardcore than in ER with choices. Like I said prior, you are more committed. A choice by design, some will like this some will not. Can I respec my character? Where's my larval tears. Go back to ER if you wanna be more hand holdy. I loved ER btw, just know what you're getting into. Your choices matter more here and I love it for that. Graphically its a step up from DS1 even without a graphics mod installed and the controls are also improved. It also has some very unique areas to explore, like a pirate themed level(?), which on the onset doesn't fit, then you realize the area was used to ship the undead out of Drangleic. I could literally make a long list about the things it does that make it a huge upgrade from DS1 but I won't from a gameplay spoiler perspective.

CAUTION: STORY SPOILER TIME!

1. YOU...

2. HAVE BEEN...

3. WARNED!

I won't spoil it all, but what I have figured out, is enough to get the general story. You could call it a twisted love story in a land far removed from Lordran even. Drangleic, a kingdom built upon generations by what would appear to be a good king. A king falls in love with his future queen, she's not from Drangleic. This queen tells him, there is trouble brewing beyond the seas. She convinced him there is a looming threat from the Giants. He decides to take her beyond the seas and go to war with the Giants. In doing so he brings back something of theirs, a piece of technology? Magic? We really don't know (its the mcguffin of the game). With this new power, the king is able to make golems. Said golems help create an even better kingdom. Constructing castles, defenses and so on. With the help of his brother, they are able to perform many advanced experiments. Creating golems, ancient dragons even from these old Giants as the catalyst. After some time, the Giants come to the shores of Drangleic and wage war. What turns into a generations long war cripples the kingdom. Both sides suffer large losses. Drangleic more so, but the Giants are all but gone because of this war as well. With the kingdom in shambles, now an undead curse hits causing things to further spiral out of control. The king and his brother look to experiments for curing this curse, not fully knowing what it is, where did it come from etc.

Here's the twist, the queen is actually a shard from a boss in DS1 that we destroyed and has taken the queens shape (looks a lot like a DS1 npc btw, hint hint) knowing all to well about the light and dark cycles. I won't name the character she really is, but its a DS1 DLC boss character. So, basically she looks like an DS1 npc, but is actually a DS1 boss remnant. This connects the two games. The king unable to kill her gives up and goes hollow (I won't forget seeing him just walking around aimlessly hollow when I expected a boss...I wanted to cry), but before decides to stonewall her every advance in claiming the throne of want, igniting the flame. These are the obstacles as a player we remove for her to claim the throne at the end. People complain she's one of the easiest bosses in the souls series, well no ♥♥♥♥. There's the reasons. The king blocked her from becoming powerful. To her, we are the final boss. I love this twist. Although I haven't beat her yet, I was aware of her as a boss and that people moaned about her gameplay wise. So, that explains that. I love the story and I would easily rate it up there with DS1.

10/10.

Edit:

Just finished the game @ 91 hours. You can actually respec in DS2, in DS1 you couldn't.

Dark Souls 2 sucks... is probably what you expect me to be starting with. And as a Dark Souls game it is questionable whether it fits with 1 and 3.

In my opinion, it is an excellent game, but not an excellent Dark Souls game. Think of it in terms of pudding. Yorkshire puddings are not good puddings, but they are awesome food none the less.

There are some tenuous links to DS1 and it is referenced occasionally in DS3, but it does not need to be. It does feel more like a sequel to Demon's Souls than DS1 ever did.

I once heard a You Tuber (yes this old boy does watch the tube like the young uns) say that "DS1 tried to challenge you, tried to make you better, and rewarded your skill, but DS2 is just trying to kill you". And it does feel like that. No matter how good you think you are, always watch you back, because she would rather stab you than anything else, and bring stabby, bitey friends along for the ride.

Now onto OG or Scholar. Well, if you have OG, play it. However, online is gone. Saying that, OG is slightly easier than Scholar, apart from getting the bastille key.

Scholar also throws some changes your way which make the game better and worse in equal measure. New shades/summons, new invasions and some enemy placement make it a more interesting game, but some changes in enemy and item placement does the opposite. And the extra petrified statues thrown in just make no sense other than to artificially restrict player movement. But it does have all the DLCs which is where this game truly shines.

So while it has its "challenges", it is still a game worthy of anyone's time. Buy it. Challenge yourself, punish yourself, and I am sure I will see you around Drangleic.

A lot of great new ideas and mechanics, some of the best areas, weapons, armors and spells' designs I've seen in any Souls game, and I'd dare say even more freedom than it's predecesor when it comes to exploration and builds, specially the latter.

On the other side, we have even clunkier controls than DS1, no attention at how the areas connect to each other (ily Iron Keep in the sky), and some very, very bad decisions (seriously, who thought making the estus flask regenerate health instead of instantly healing was a good idea).

The game got a rather bad reputation early on that has maintained ever since, but it's really not as bad as many people make it out to be, if at all. It can be frustrating at times, specially the early game, but eventually you learn to enjoy the good, laugh at the bad, and embrace the chaos the game offers. A very fun experience overall.

Is it the best Souls game ever made? Hell no, not by a long shot. But it isn't a bad game either. It has it's strengths and it's very clear flaws, but what game isn't flawed anyway.

I deffinitely recommend to give the game a chance. Specially If you're like me, and avoided it for a long time because of the bad reputation, trust me, you won't be disappointed. One way or another.

john dark souls, maybe the true scholar were the first sins we made along the way. moneybags ahead

frankly, idk how to feel. i 100%ed the achievements on elden ring and DS1/3 before playing this, and i was skeptical due to the reputation this game has. it was kinda warranted. level and sound design in this game are lacking, few of the places are cool and combat feels weightless due to the sounds not hitting as hard as they should. not only that, the game just lacks responsiveness in general. idk why, but this is the only game in existence where you can get hit and not even realise it. the character doesn't grunt, doesn't move when they get hit so after every attack you just kinda look at the HP bar to see if you got striked. the early game in this game is stupid and frustrating. the other games felt like the world was hard, this game felt like the devs wanted to make me listen to the "jumping ahead" messages. you have a singular estus flask (why??) and there are two HUGE noob traps when you start (heide's tower and champion covenant). heide's tower seems like the place you have to go, until you realise that after every 3 enemies you have to waddle your ass back to the bonfire due to the devs thought that lifegems made it ok to give you a single estus flask charge and the champion's covenant just makes the game harder because it's funny. i knew about the covenant, but heide's tower boosted me because i ended up just farming the one guy at the first bonfire so i was high enough level to fight the next guy, and the next guy, etc. but whatever, the early game sucks in this game, but once you know where you have to go and get a few cool weapons, the game gets enjoyable. DS2 has stiff combat, but the weapon variety in this game is phenomenal and i'd argue it's 2nd to elden ring. there are so many weapon classes and different choices in said classes, you really can't go wrong with any of them (altho thrusting weapons are pretty strong). not only that, but DS2 has an update on the NG+ mechanics, where if you wanna do an area all over again, you can burn a bonfire esthetic to level it up a NG cycle and respawn the bosses. this was really cool, because it adds new loot, enemies and makes some changes to the bosses themselves and even sometimes adding new environmental hazards, making NG+ it's own experience and not the campaign but with slightly stronger enemies. i haven't even mentioned the best part of this game yet, the DLCs. all the DLCs in this game are MILES better then the base game. interesting bosses (except gank squad), cool new enemies and phenomenal level design, reminiscent of DS1. playing through the DLCs was the only part of the experience where i was consistently having fun.

overall, subpar fromsoft experience. bad sound design, mediocre level design, combat feels very off but the builds are very fun, the actually good areas (no-man's wharf, lost bastille, dragleic castle, undead crypt) made me forget the flaws and the DLCs are very enjoyable (elena is sick). 6/10

Posted: August 25, 2024
I wanted to like this game, I wanted to like it so hard.

Admittedly, this game has a lot of good. Great build variety, amazing gimmicky level design that is usually fun to explore, there is awesome spell variety and a lot of unique items to get, and the much needed bonfire ascetic system. But these are overshadowed by some GLARING flaws.

I have played every single other souls game, and "gitting gud" was always a vital part of the experience, overcoming obstacles and improving had you feel *rewarded* for finally pulling through and triumphing over whatever boss lay ahead.

But good god, this game does not want to let you get better...

First, the hollowing mechanic makes is so that dying just straight up lowers your max HP, experimenting around is no longer as risk-free as other games.

Second, I-frames, which are an essential mechanic are locked behind a stat now, a STAT. So you can do practically everything right and still die simply my the detriment of not having a high enough ADP stat.

From a coding standpoint even the controls have very odd square deadzones that lead to more rigid movement. And hurt/hitboxes are very unoptimized, the typical souls "jank" is just not goofy anymore.

The Pharros Lockstones and Fragrant Branches make it so that you can just miss whole areas and content because you used your stone/branch on the wrong spot, oops!

And there are many enemies whose combat is objectively bugged, like the falconers whose models will literally twist in on themselves as they fight. Also, the lock-on is befuddligly bad, not allowing you to lock onto obvious things like a dragon's head, something all other games in the series have. And usually the lock on will have you just swing the wrong way anyways unless you actively work to swing directly at the target.

Every other souls game has me go "Oh, that was rewarding, what's next?" While DS2 has me go "Well, that was miserable."

Posted: August 25, 2024
Dark Souls II, whilst being a sequel to Dark Souls, is simply not the same game. If you look it up you can find videos relating to the production cycle of Dark Souls II which explain why the game is so different.

The great thing about this is all of these decisions shaped the game into what it became. Dark Souls II does not take the original formula and run with it, instead it toys with ideas and introduces new mechanics, pushing the envelop of what a Dark Souls game can and should be.

Is Dark Souls II perfect? far from it, some of the changes present in the game are downright frustrating, but other changed and additions are welcomed. Dark Souls II is the most Marmite game of the series, players love or hate it, there is no in between.

I certainly have my reservations about parts of the game, but they did not stop me from appreciating everything Drangleic has to offer.

If you liked the first game, you may find it difficult to adjust to Dark Souls II, you may even find you actively dislike it. However I would implore you to stick with it and let the game take you on a journey and tell you its story.

Posted: August 24, 2024
Foreword: I am biased, I'm largely basing my opinion off comparison to the first game, and my opinion is colored by marketing, however, I will not be discussing that here.

Pros:
--starting with fast-travel makes exploration a lot less scary and traversing the world simpler
--bonfires outside boss rooms (except when there's not)
--boss weapons are acquired simply by turning boss souls into the appropriate NPC as opposed to the truly archaic system that was present in Ds1 and DeS, not to mention upgrading in general is a lot more straightforward
--speaking of archaic systems: covenants have better conveyance and are a lot simpler to quit, join, rejoin, etc.
--tons more weapons and enemies having significant weaknesses and resistances actually encourages you to try out different weapons
--tons more armor, peak fashion souls
--lots of fun interactions with the environment, which I've always wanted more of in Fromsoft games cuz they have this weird habit of only ever using a fun gimmick or mechanic ONCE
--NPCs (including invaders and phantoms) have a lot of personality
--NG+ and beyond add tons of new stuff and is honestly probably best in series for NG+
--POWER STANCE

Cons:
--starting with fast-travel reduces risk and makes the world feel smaller
--mob encounters are a mess and no "they stop respawning after you kill them a dozen times" does not justify anything

--stats are kind of a mess... I'm not gonna beat a dead horse by talking about agility (ADP), however there's some truly baffling choices like pyromancy having no requirements yet "fire bonus" scales with both faith and intelligence for some reason--the "dark bonus" situation is similar but different, this time having requirements and again scaling with both faith and int, but not in the same way--not to mention power stancing requires you to have 1.5x the str and dex requirements but doesn't count as two-handing so you can't cheese the str requirement, which, honestly, idk why I'm complaining about that cuz if it DID let you cheese the str requirement then I'd be complaining about dex falling by the wayside instead

*catches breath*

Suffice to say, if any of this is confusing to you or you didn't take the time to read the above, then you see the problem

and I don't like it.
Though, to be fair this problem exists in all Souls games and to some degree all rpg


--Speaking of stats: endurance & vitality--stamina and equip burden were governed by the same stat in Ds1, but Ds2 decided to split them up. Just why? I guess it wouldn't bother me so much if I didn't have to worry about ADP as well--but, again, not touching that
--They still haven't moved that f***ing shadow

Unexpectedly good, except for 1-2 annoying af locations in end game (no, not the usual swamp, watery place this time).
Bosses however were underwhelming.
There were 2 that were a bit harder in particular for me but the rest died in few attempts max.

I know I missed one worth to remember and haven't played DLCs but 2 areas I entered were just too annoying to handle. Just like in DS3, I didn't feel any reason to go into DLCs (ik, ik, profanity, but if it does not give me new ending, I don't really care anymore). Maybe one day I will go back to DLCs.
I died way more to mobs but it's always the "trash" and way from bonfires to bosses that set the difficulty level in From soft games (maybe except for Elden Ring).

Quests/achievements were the easiest out of all 3 Dark Souls - the only hard part was Lucatile which I f*d up at first - god bless backing up save files. At first I didn't like that bosses gave me none of them but in the end it is better system than DS3 f*d up covenant farm in PvE and NG+ only rings. Talking about NGs -> Bonfire ascetics are great feature if you don't want to go fully into NGs.

In the end I am surprised how big and open this world is and I don't know where all the hate towards DS2 comes from. For me it is still better than DS3 which had better bosses but worse world exploration (way, way worse).

Ah, a big minus for me is I could not finish a game with Guts's sword this time as its' damage output was not big enough compared to how slow and stamina draining it was...
