Posted: August 23, 2024
Dark Souls II stands as a complex and often misunderstood entry in the renowned series. While it may not consistently match the quality of its predecessor, particularly in terms of gameplay, it remains an impressive artistic achievement that deserves recognition. The game's difficulty curve feels steeper and more unforgiving, especially in the early stages and DLC content, leading to moments of frustration that some players find excessive.
However, it's important to note that difficulty in the Dark Souls series is inherently subjective, varying based on playstyle, character build, and even luck. Criticizing the game solely on its challenging nature overlooks similar difficult moments in the first game. Many of the issues players cite become less problematic as they progress, often transforming into a sense of satisfaction upon overcoming obstacles.
Dark Souls II rewards patience, a core lesson from the original game. Players who approach it with this mindset often find a rich, engaging experience. Unfortunately, the game suffers from an undeserved stigma, with some dismissing it without even playing. While it does have some repetitive elements, particularly in boss encounters, the overall experience remains enjoyable and worthwhile for most players.
What truly sets Dark Souls II apart is its narrative approach. Rather than attempting to recreate the grand, world-shaping story of the first game, it focuses on exploring the fundamentals of the Dark Souls universe on a smaller scale. Players find themselves in Drangleic, one of many kingdoms in a world already broken and devoid of hope. This setting allows for a deeper exploration of previously untouched aspects of the lore.
Unlike the first game, where the player is central to a creation myth and tasked with deciding the world's fate, Dark Souls II places the player in a more ambiguous role. The game constantly encourages questioning - of the cycle, of oneself, of the world, and of the very nature of choice within this universe. It repeatedly asks the player what they truly want, pushing them to consider their actions and motivations more deeply.
Despite its differences, Dark Souls II successfully captures the essence of the series. It provides just as much philosophical and narrative depth to contemplate once the journey is complete. For these reasons, it remains a highly recommended experience for fans of the series and newcomers alike, offering a unique perspective on the Dark Souls universe and challenging players to think beyond the conventional notions of light and dark.

As of writing this I have completed the game in terms of story. I beat all the bosses of the DLC and base game and have just defeated the final boss sitting atop my throne. First and foremost, this game was great. I do not see where people get the idea this is a bad game by any means. It took me a hot second, pretty much all the way past the Last Giant to grow used to the graphics style and the feel of the game. It is much less rigid than DS1 and the rolling feels much smoother. The graphics I also much prefer as I was not staring a gritty landscapes the whole time. DS2 had quite the wide range of visuals and was captivating from start to finish. Secondly, to address the elephant in the room, yes ADP sucks. Did I invest all of my souls at the start into ADP to get it to 20? Yes. Did it make the game so much more enjoyable past that point? Yes. It is a necessary evil to erase the one clunky part of combat DS2 presents. The bosses were not as memorable for me as they were in DS1 solely for the fact that they were a breeze. The run backs and normal ads were definitely the harder part to this game, but I did enjoy that each area seemingly had a gimmick to it that made the final bossfight easier. Thirdly, the point that has not entirely won me over is the level design. DS1, when playing through it for the first time, had this beautiful way of bringing you right back to the start. DS2 was just incredibly windy and twisty and very "you can go here if you want to!" The saving grace was that the lordvessel is in the game from the start, you can warp to bonfires freely. If not for this, I do not know if I could have played through this game. All in all a beautiful time and a must play for anyone who has touched a souls game. I will update this with my time to see how long the final 7 achievements take of the game! You can get all of them easily on a first play through in sub 40 hours like I did (including DLC!)!

Edit: Finally got the 100% (Addition of about 20 hours), in terms of raw time played, it was easier than DS1. However, I messed up in getting some of the spells from the DLC so I had to play through all the DLCs up until when I can get the spells. Once I was able to just farm souls to bypass the Shrine of Winter with fighting The Rotten, that was reminiscent of DS1. The farming part of the first makes it feel like the remastered first one is easier than the SOTFS of DS2. It was still a very fun and nice challenge but it is interesting to see how much of this game you do not need to interact with to get to 100%.

Posted: August 21, 2024
+ Deep, tactical combat like no other
+ Certain zones, specially on the DLC's, with masterful architecture and lovely shortcuts
+ It has some very unique mechanics (torch usage, lockstones, some environmental puzzles, collectibles that unlock essential areas for the game to advance - if you consume them somewhere else or by accident, you'll be stuck forever)
+ Hollow mechanics are controversial but, in the end, not much different from embers on DS3

- Lore is incredibly obtuse, with some NPCs scattered and hidden in some very strange places
- Dodge iframes tied to a specific stat (that must be leveled up for the game to be minimally fair, otherwise hitboxes can get quite erratic)
- Graphically very basic (and downright ugly in certain areas)
- Some mobs (and some paths to the bosses) are downright insane, specially on the DLCs
- Design-wise, some areas are ridiculous (e.g. Black Gulch)
- It has some very unique mechanics that will annoy the hell out of you (like weapons breaking when hitting dragons)
- Hollowing takes away chunks of the healthbar

This may be considered the ugly duck of the Souls series.
However, it was also my first Souls game. After 30 hours of my first run dying and dying again, suddenly something clicked. Something made sense. Suddenly, I understood my purpose on that world and what should I do to succeed. After finishing the base game, I went for Dark Souls 3. Then Bloodborne. Then Elden Ring and devoured them all.

Coming back to Dark Souls 2 to finish the DLCs (that were left pending) was cathartic, a rememberance of how a game that is so different from his successors can still be so captivating to play. Beware, however, about the way you play, because your decisions can have dire consequences here.

Posted: August 21, 2024
Honestly, it’s not as bad as everyone says it is, though the game definitely has its problems.

There's a big difficulty spike in the beginning of the game and an excessive amount of enemy placement in some areas.
Bosses are also a hit or miss.
Some of them are actually pretty cool, and have interesting mechanics (specially the DLC ones), but others are kinda meh, bad, or just straight-up reused bosses.
The hitboxes also seemed to be 'off' quite often. Enemies would either hit me when they weren't supposed to or miss me when they shouldn't have. The same thing happened to me sometimes when I would hit them.

That said, the game has its share of great moments.

Many areas in the game are stunning, making exploration feel good, with loot everywhere for you to grab (some pretty well hidden).
The interconnectivity between each map is very well designed, making everything feel cohesive while still allowing for distinct changes in the area's theme (Except for the transition between Earthen Peak and Iron Keep, that was very out of nowhere but still a nice surprise).
I also liked the fact that the game makes some small changes to some boss fights when you go into NG+, though I wish they had done it to more bosses and not just 3 of them.

Overall, it's still an enjoyable game. It can be very frustrating at times, but... you know... it's Dark Souls.

Throughout my life I heard a lot of things about the souls series, good and bad. But the one constant across many of them was that DS2 was the weakest of the trilogy, I started the game with low expectations a result of it's rep online and I was blown away by a game that was a huge improvement in many ways over it's predecessor.

The game is difficult, it does not beat around the bush, but it also rewards you in many ways for experimenting with the tools you have available or for utilizing the environment to your advantage. Many encounters can be made much easier with proper strategizing or by taking it slower and paying attention to your surroundings. Enemy hoards can be thinned using environmental hazards, shortcuts around ambushes can be uncovered, bosses can be made easier with the right set up, etc.

The game has it's flaws but I feel like it unfairly gets a bad rap, as many the issues I've seen people talk about DS1 was as guilty of them. And this game gives you many ways of approaching a problem and gives you a lot of ways of changing your approach if you find your current methods not working for you.

This is a fantastic game and I feel like it should be given a chance with an open mind, it's quite experimental and has a few odd changes that wouldn't make sense if you're coming here from the other games in the trilogy but it's only better for it.

Okay, so... after completing all Dark Souls Remastered achievements in around 107 hours (I don't exactly remember, but it was almost 3 full playthroughs), I have moved onto Dark Souls 2: SotFS. I am 12,5 hours in so far, and I have come to a brief, early conclusion:

First, let's look at the pros:
Okay. First things first, fast travel from the start of the game is priceless and better inventory system and additional ring slots are great as well, compared to DS1. DS2 also makes the fat rolling threshold higher, which is a nice change. The game feels smooth in rolling action, imo more than DS1, and also afaik it does not give you the invulnerable frames when you roll into an enemy attack, which I think makes the game harder (or I just can't time it right lol). In DS1, those invulnerability frames were kind of easy to time. The game also introduces interesting healing mechanics that force you to think in advance and use your healing carefully. It also introduced that, while using some healing items, you can walk, which is imo probably the best improvement since DS1. The fall damage reducing items that you need to access certain areas is also kind of a cool feature, but nothing really that groundbreaking. Last but not least, the new Hollow system is, in my opinion, better and worse at the same time than in DS1. If you run to a boss room unexpectedly and want to use summon, you don't need to go all the way back to the bonfire and clear the area again to reverse hollowing, you can do it quickly and on the spot, then heal up with some lesser lifegems you don't need and you are ready to go. BUT the fact that you lose your max health alongside humanity with each death and you need to use a consumable that is fairly hard to come by (at least early on) to reverse it can cause some serious troubles, esepcially for new players. In DS1, the only downside of being hollowed was that you could not summon, but also could not get invaded, so it really did not matter if you reversed hollowing or not. In DS2, you are slowly being forced to do so (not that it isn't a really cool mechanic lore wise, slowly turning hollow as your health goes down with each death and your irl mental state worsens as well).

Now we move onto the cons:
Beside the Hollow system downside that I've mentioned together with the pro aspect of it, I really, really hate the fact that you can't level up at bonfires and instead have to travel to the starting location, wasting a ton of time in loading screens and additional dialogues and animations before and after leveling up (not to mention you need to leave the coversation menu to use some souls from your inventory if you can't afford the last level up by like 150 souls, and then you need to go through the conversation and animations again, which takes a lot of time again). The area design is, imo nowhere close to the DS1 design, especially when it comes to area layouts and shortcuts. The areas in DS2 are just sort of blending together, not really feeling that special at all, compared to DS1. The NPCs are also less memorable and unique than those in DS1. The combat system, compared to DS1 Remastered, is clumsy sometimes, but that might be because I don't use a controller. Also, even though I appreciate the option to bind my own keyboard and mouse bindings, it was sort of hard in the start to understand which key was supposed to be X on controller, Y on controller etc., because the controller tooltips stay the same for mouse and keyboard users, which is shame. I have also spent quite a while figuring out how to progress, which wasn't an issue in DS1, but that could be contributed to the inferior level design and layout of DS2. With all that being said, let's move to the greatest con I have noticed about DS2 - like FROM, how many enemy desings do you have? 10 models for the entire game? The repetetiveness of enemies is insane. I am talking about normal mobs, liek the hollow infantry and soldiers make 70% of what I have encountered and they are basically in almost every zone I went to so far. For reference, DS1 had a ton of unique mobs for almost every area, with the only exception being Undead Burg, Undead Parish and partially the Depths, which makes sense since it's basically still one area. Eversince then, every area had a unique and level-themed set of monsters. Blighttown? Great design of toxic abominations that live below the sewers. Anor Londo? Fancy guards, Silver Knights and Demons that are seen nowhere else. Duke Archives? Except for some golems that could be seen in Darkroot Basin and for Channelers that are minibosses in some prior areas, the area offered like three unique and new mobs and an interesting boss encounter. Crystal Cave had repetetive enemies, that's true, but still it wasn't an area to explore, but rather to fight the boss and implement the invisible path mechanic, which was cool; and the list just goes on. In DS2, I kid you not, the amount of the same enemies in like 4 areas so far is insane. There are no unique or area-themed enemies in the first 12,5 hours, which makes the areas look even duller than the weak level design. And not to mention that the second boss you kill comes back as a regular enemy like two minutes later, and I have fought him like 5 times already. This is super annoying, as the monster is basically the bossfight again with sliglty less HP. You can't kill him in a few hits before your stamina runs out and you don't get much souls from him either. You just have to deal with him over and over again. Invasions in this game are also fairly difficult and inevitable. Armored Dennis is the first invade I enountered, and for the love of God, before I've learned how to cheese his spells, I died like 8 times to him. You can't really escape him, he is super tanky and he casts really heavy hitting magic that is hard to predict and dodge. That's not even fun, it's just bad imho.

Lastly, I don't know if it's good or bad, but enemies stop spawning after being killed for like 15th time or so. This makes the progression less frustrating, but also makes the farming for items/souls very limited. I know you can use Bonfire Aestethic to respawn all enemies in the subarea around bonfire, but like why? DS1 made it way better. Hard enemies need to be killed only once and they are gone for good (which is better I think) and the weak enemies can be farmed if needed and are not that hard to get through in case of boss runback or whatever. In DS2, if you want to kill a tough enemy for good, you need to do it 15 damned times. If it wasn't for better bonfire placement and easier boss runbacks compared to DS1, I would put this in the con category, but I put it in neutral for now.

The final thoughts:
I would recommend the game, even if it has its flaws. Do you research before buying it though, as it is pretty different from the first one. I can't compare it to DS3 and other souls games yet, I am only about to play them after finishing DS2, but if you only want to play DS2 because you played other souls games, I really, really recommend doing some research beforehand. Also, keep in mind that I have spent 12,5 hours on this game when I was writing this summary, so some of the points I've made (especially the repetetiveness) could change later on and therefore could not really be valid for the full playthrough.

Posted: August 18, 2024
This game has some problems.
I enjoyed playing Dark Souls, but I did not enjoy Dark Souls 2. Coming from the keyboard and mouse mechanics, they changed the whole format in order to support double click. This, however, present a bigger issue which results in a substantial input delay. It would take 1-2 seconds of delay to attack with a quick attack and the same with a strong attack. The keyboard binds are different from Dark Souls so it felt weird coming from a game that requires muscle memory, but one can change the keybinds. I changed the keybinds however, whenever you log back into the game, you have to redo canceling the double click option. This is not the best experience with mouse and keyboard. So this is good for players who play with controllers.

The second reason is the wonky movement the player character makes. The character has a long attack animation so there is additional delay. This is also the same for Dark Soul 1, however in Dark Souls 1 you can aim your next attack to the opponent accurately. For some reason, this game requires you to attack based on movement rather than simply facing the general direction of the player character. So when you are attacking you are essentially circling around the enemy whiffing around the enemy. Another way the player character can be wonky is that when attacking some attacks can seemingly miss when it looks like I can hit the enemy. I'm not sure if it is that the enemy hitbox are small or if they had iframes. For example when the enemy character is getting back up or when you use an axe it looks like you hit the enemy but for some reason it did not register as a hit. Another change from Dark Souls 1 to 2 is general direction in which you can move your character in a 360 degree format to a sharp directional format. This game requires you to get used to a sharp directional format, which can lead to slipping off the platform. I fell off of platforms more here than i did in Dark Souls.

The third reason is that the game disincentivizes you to continue the game. The more deaths you accumulate the less health you start off with. I think as a beginner, you have to learn with death. This mechanic is incredibly frustrating and it should have been a new game+ feature not something the game starts off with. The only way to get back all your health is a human effigy, which does not come very often when you start off. One option that was possible in Dark Souls and not Dark Souls 2, is the fact that you can run away from enemies and go straight to the boss. In this game you cannot run away, you have to fight the normal enemies before you go into the boss. Let say you died once by the boss. Now you have to run back to where your souls are, which is in the boss room, but you have to deal with the enemies in the way. In Dark Souls 1, if you know the route and enemy placement, then you can simply weave through enemies or deal with them. In Dark Souls 2, you have no choice but to deal with them, because the AI in this game once they see you, will be hellbent in killing you, even to the point of running faster than you. You also don't get iframes when you enter the fog door and once you beat the boss, the normal enemies will be waiting for you. The aggro from the enemies can be hit or miss at times where some enemies will fail to recognize you and 1-2 enemies have laser focused vision.
The enemy tracking is perfectly locked on to you for this game. The enemies have perfect response times, and have perfect aim when it comes to shooting arrows. This is very similar to Dark Souls you might say, however, you get stun locked when shot with an arrow for 1-3 seconds. Adding on to another frustrating part of this game is the stun lock. When enemies attack you with a combo, there are times you cannot get away especially when you cannot maintain distance from the enemies. Another thing about the enemies is that there are times where you might have to deal with 1-2 enemies or 5-7 enemies all at once. This was poorly designed because if you play a certain build, there is simply no way to deal with all the enemies at once because of collision physics. The collision physics can act as a detriment to your player character as some enemies have long reaching attacks, in which an enemy in the back can attack and reach you and for some reason there is no friendly fire and enemy fall damage.
The only thing that I like about this game is that they learned from this game and made the other games better and that it can be very adrenaline inducing due to its difficult nature. I will only recommend this to people who love pain and suffering, who play with controllers, who wants to play a sequel to Dark Souls and who love frustrating and unpolished games. I think if you have the money, you should just give this a chance, but know that experiences can vary. You might like the clunky nature of this game. While it is one of the slowest Dark Souls game trilogy, you can still accomplish great things and come out on top of this difficult game. It just doesn't help when you are told to get good, or you're doing something wrong. If this game is too difficult and stress inducing, then it might be better to quit. But learning from what I learned from Dark Souls, passing the hurdle of something that may seem impossible requires great strength and courage. You feel great when you do end up accomplishing the next step. It's just this game is not for me, but it can be for you.

Posted: August 16, 2024
Great game that Dark Souls enjoyers will probably like. Not as elegant in world design (which is hard to do considering DS1 has the best world design of all time), but still similarly fun and atmospheric. One of the DLCs included with SOTFS has the best and most unique boss in the entire game, but I won't say which as not to spoil it.

The main problem with Dark Souls 2 are the input mechanics. First of all, they're using a square outer limit on the analog control zone that makes camera movement feel clunky. I couldn't make it circular like DS1 even with mods. There's also less fine control for analog movement compared to DS1.

While I understand the game difficulty is balanced to account for this, certain mechanics like slow flask and consumables speed, iframes being tied to the agility stat, and some minor things like the wonky turnaround animation when running away from a locked on target and the weird run stamina interaction do make DS2 feel like the most frustrating, mechanics wise, in the entire Soulsborne collection.

It isn't that DS2 is more difficult than DS1, but that the difficulty feels more frustrating due to some of the input mechanics, especially if you're used to DS3 or ER. DS1 also has a slower paced feel to the combat, but it feels more finely crafted and intentional. It just feels right. It has a weight and deliberateness to it without feeling clunky. DS2's mechanics really do just feel clunky, just not as far as to make the game completely unfun.

SOTFS also has some changes to enemy placement that imo weren't necessary and only make the game worse. It isn't drastic, but it increases difficulty in some areas for no good reason. Otherwise, the lighting is slightly more contrasty and bright compared to the original DS2.

The game still has cool and atmospheric levels and fun boss fights. There's a lot of choice for the player and developer polish. I would not really understand people saying it's bad while DS1 and 3 are good unless they want to have a scapegoat to feel better about their favorites.

9/10

So, there's a reason why fans of the soulsborne games consider this the worst, or the red headed step child of the genre. In a game series that is known for being difficult, there are plenty of times this one sets up as just being unfair. Hidden Ganks, Invader NPCs having entirely to much health, forced pvp, and a level design that has plenty of parkour without a dedicated jump button. For those coming from Elden Ring to play Dark Souls, I really can't recommend this one... and this is from someone coming from Bloodborne. These are the dark souls 2 experience. But for those wanting the best and worst...

Plenty of weapons and armor
Plenty of ways to farm
Foes can be despawned if you defeat them enough times

This game does have upsides, such as the massive item variety, a couple cool bosses, quality of life features, and a great hub area. However, to me the downsides really outweigh them; adaptability, being a stat that controls the i-frames on your roll, is probably the most mentioned one, but it honestly isn't too bad. You don't even notice it once you get your adp past 20, so its just an inconvenience in early game. The average boss quality is pretty low, with most bosses having about 2-3 moves and dying pretty quickly, though where I am there have already been exceptions (I liked smelter demon and old dragonslayer, especially smelter demon). The movement can feel pretty janky sometimes, but that's nothing new; the same happens in Dark Souls 1, though maybe not as bad. However, by far my biggest complaint for this game is the level traversal. Though the puzzles and world design can be pretty engaging, the developers mindset when it comes to placing enemies is just "lets have about 2 unique enemies per entire area, and make it hard by putting twenty of them back to back. This combined with some horrendous boss runbacks (SMELTER DEMON) makes progress really tedious, and made me often feel like I was just wasting my time. I will be finishing this game, cause I really enjoy this style of game, and out of respect for FromSoft, but I definitely wouldn't recommend it to most people. As someone who has gotten all achievements for their other games (at least on steam), I would just point anyone in the direction of Dark Souls 1 or 3, or of course Elden Ring, all much easier games to get into.

Posted: August 9, 2024
What a wild ride—yes, it's still worth picking up Dark Souls 2 in 2024.

This game gets a bad rep (compared to other Souls games) for some of its more frustrating elements, but honestly there is so much to enjoy about it, including some innovative design elements that were removed from later FromSoft titles.

Pros

• A lot to do, in a fairly non-linear fashion. The game also lets you skip optional content and stick to the main story if you feel like it. Most of the parts of this game people usually complain about are entirely optional, and can be more easily approached after getting more valuable gear through the mandatory quests.

• Large list of bosses, with a surprising amount of variety. I've played DS 1,2,3 and Elden Ring and DS2 bosses were by far my favorite, with even some of the most challenging fights being fun without seeming impossible. Most bosses allow you to observe and react to their movements, avoiding the player having to memorize complex attack patterns.

• Great build variety; leveling up is much faster in this game compared to the others, and upgrade materials are in plenty. I played a melee run but was able to switch around using swords, rapiers, hammers, whips and great clubs as needed, which made combat always fresh.

• An actually worthwhile NG+ experience; even if you don't begin an NG+ system, an in-game item (Bonfire Ascetic) allows for bumping individual areas to NG+ difficulty, re-fight bosses, respawn most item rewards, and in many cases, provide upgraded items normally unavailable.

• The ability to despawn (and respawn) regular enemies; Souls games can be stressful. Repeatedly dying while exploring can be discouraging, but knowing that every enemy kill is closer to making the area permanently safer is comforting compared to the dread of having to navigate treacherous areas repeatedly.

• Fashion souls! Armor matters less in this game than others, allowing you to mix and match pieces to let your character look just as you like.

• Fun NPC questlines and interactions, and questlines are generally easy to follow, especially if you're playing online and can see player messages. The game has a (sometimes twisted) sense of humor and tries to troll you every now and then. I can't even be mad at some of the gotcha moments.


• The hollowing system; your max HP slightly reduces per death (capped at a certain percentage), until you use an item to replenish it (and these items can be hard to come by early.) This seems especially punishing early on, but there's a ring that limits this loss to 25%. I played most of the game with it on and still had a good time.

• Some mechanics are obscure. This game is best played if you're willing to occasionally look up what certain things mean (e.g. the Adaptability stat). I can't recommend a fully blind playthrough, though (and worse, offline without being able to see player messages).

• Boss runbacks can sometimes be brutal. Most of the annoying ones are for optional bosses, but especially towards the later half, there's plenty of room to experiment with fun builds (and switch them out on the fly).

• Graphics; by default this game looks pretty gross (somehow even worse than Dark Souls 1). Still, with just a single easy-to-install mod (Dark Souls 2 Lighting Engine) which also works for online play, the visuals transform to absolutely gorgeous, even better than DS3 / Elden Ring in my subjective opinion.

Don't let what others say about this game stop you from giving it a shot, especially if you'd like the full FromSoft catalog experience and don't want to skip a Souls title 😄

Well, that was a blast.

Ran through Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin (DS II) with a friend during the 'rona times and it was an absolute blast. Dont expect to hear anything new if you've already played this, if not ~you should~ but only with a buddy.

Souls games are known for three things: Difficulty, Environments, PvP. I can attest that this game...doesnt follow any of those. The game is hard at first, and that's it. Playing solo is a slog in the initial areas, and its off to the races afterwards. Couple of boss hiccups along the way, but you wont be tested after hour 4. Playing with a friend, well lets just say its hilarious fun. Get ready to blast through everything, kiting and taunt bosses, and have an all around great time.

As to environments, expect a mixed bag! supposedly there is a story told via places and names and such, but who cares! this game didnt. You'll see grand vistas, hordes of drakes and towers, and a melancholy moon over a seaside fort. You'll also get bland stone caves, dimly lit or uninspired windmills and forests, or just darkness shrouded wicker platforms. Its very random. Theres barely any music and the sound design is decent.

Concerning PvP it was probably awesome ages ago, but this game is OLD and means it either has no one fighting, no one invading, or if you do happen across a random player they are likely cheating. Kinda sad, but what are you gonna do.

Overall, this game is very long, there is a lot of content, but it really stops being different halfway through, and the DLC always grants two awesome bosses and a dud of one. You'll always know which is the dud. Lotta bang for your buck otherwise, and playing with a friend is a must!

Summary: Overall I think it is a really bad game. Biggest contribution to making it really bad is the DLC. It is unfair instead of challenging. And constant gotcha moments with teleporting enemies and enemies that spawn behind you. And just waves and waves of enemies in small constrained spaces that are mostly unlit. I have played and enjoyed many other souls titles and this misses the mark.

They seem to have littered places purposely with enemies so you have to fight them over and over in order to despawn them.
Farming is the least fun part of any souls game and they deliberately designed the levels and game mechanics so you can't progress without doing it.

User AoiYozora created a mod named "Bearer of the Curse Edition".

The mod fixes some of the most glaring gameplay problems and also ties the map together and allows for a lot more freedom at the start of the game. It also makes NPCs and PCs look really good. In vanilla DS 2 the NPCs skin color is molded cheese sandwich and the PC skin looks like the skin of an orange.

Thanks to the mod combat now feels fair (in the main game, DLC is a different story) and I find myself actually enjoying the game.
I highly recommend the mod in combination with the lighting engine mod.

Fromsoft should hire the modder to fix DS 2 for them.

Here are my highlights of DS 2 with this mod:
- Woman calls me bear and lets me level up
- Manjula
- Increadible amounts of exploration
- Pyromancies are still there and easy to access
- Play dress up with an NPC
- fast animations for everything
- crestfallen warrior actually has helpful tips
- uga buga stick to bonk enemies
- attacks can be chained different combos lead to different attacks
- a lot of NPCs with interesting dialogue
- NPC quests are easy to follow/hard to glitch
- So many bosses
- Many covenants (most focus on PVP but the mod should have the covenant items in game since it is offline only)
- Sadly it all falls apart as soon as you get to the DLC

- why is everything tinted green

- textures like a wallpaper pattern
- wtf is this model design and art direction

Posted: August 7, 2024
Despite being a fan of the FromSoft games for several years now, with the original Dark Souls being my introduction to their games, I have always avoided playing Dark Souls II. For several years I had been content knowing that I’ve completed all of their other Souls games from Demon Souls to Elden Ring, and I had purposefully avoided touching Dark Souls II due to the negative reception the game generally has within the community. Well, following the hype of the new Elden Ring DLC, I finally decided it was time that I really play all of the Souls games, and stop leaving Dark Souls II behind.

I went into this game knowing about its reputation; however, I did not go into it seeking a negative experience. In fact, I went into this game really hoping that I would like it, and you know what… I kind of did. I finally decided to buckle down, give the game an honest try, and I found that as the final cutscene played I had overall enjoyed my experience with Dark Souls II.
So, what did I think about Dark Souls II? I think that Dark Souls II is a very ambitious title that was sadly hurt by a poor development schedule. As I was playing the game I realized that there were so many things about it that I genuinely enjoyed and might even say are real improvements over the first game. It really seems like the developers had a lot of great ideas that were hampered by the poor development. The first thing that you will notice about Dark Souls II compared to the first game is the new movement system. Dark Souls II uses an 8-directional movement system with dead-zones, this is something that DS1 does not do. DS1 uses a full 360 movement system. Within the Dark Souls community there appears to be a debate about which system is better, and while during my initial starting of the game I was nervous about this noticeable change in movement, I found that by the end of my 16 hour playthrough I had grown to really like it. I believe that the DS2 movement system sort of makes certain things easier than they would be in DS1. Like walking in a straight line and chaining attacks together. It is easier.

I also didn’t mind all the ways they attempted to make the game more challenging, I enjoyed the harsher penalty for dying, I didn’t mind only being able to level up at one bonfire, and I actually really liked that they tied iFrames to the ADP skill in this game. Once again, that is a change that is very controversial and will confuse you if you’re a new player coming in from one of the other games, I didn’t like it at first either. However, when I really thought about it, I found that tying iFrames to a levelable skill like ADP instead of Equip Load was a really good change that allows the player to try out different clothes and armor sets without having to worry about the irony of becoming more vulnerable.

Also, I generally really like how Dark Souls II looks compared to the first game. Majula as the introductory area is really nice and relaxing. The game carries its own unique atmosphere that sets it apart from the rest of the FromSoft games honestly even to this day. I really like how the implemented the UI design, and I really like the lighting in the game too. I know that the lighting seems to be decisive in the community, but in my subjective opinion I like it. In regard to the art direction the thing that I dislike the most is really just the player character. The NPCs in the game all look somewhat realistic for the graphics of the game, however, your player character has a weird cartoon look in comparison that I can’t help but notice. Sure, you can edit the character to your heart's content and make them look more realistic, make the eyes smaller, the face more proportional, but the player character doesn’t seem to escape this slightly stylized look and once again in my subjective opinion I honestly believe the player looked better graphically in Dark Souls 1.

During my playthrough of Dark Souls II, I really enjoyed most of the boss fights in the game. The weird part is that I didn’t find many of them to be super memorable, there were no standout bosses in this game like Ornstein & Smough or The Abyss Watchers from the other Dark Souls titles, however, I distinctly remember having fun while fighting them. The most interesting boss encounter in Dark Souls II to me is the Looking-Glass Knight. This goes back to what I said near the beginning of the review, about the game having a lot of good ideas on paper. The Looking-Glass Knight is a boss that has a big shield with a mirror on it. The boss is able to use the mirror to summon real-life other players to help him fight you. I found this idea to be really cool and was surprised when it actually worked to this day, honestly, I was surprised by the number of online players I found still enjoying this game. Those interactions were fun.

So, I seem to have said quite a lot of positive things about Dark Souls II, what did I dislike about it? Well, DS2 suffers from three issues that really hold the game back and I believe these issues are what led to the games negative reputation. The three issues I have with Dark Souls II are as follows: Level Design, Enemy Placement, Poor Programming. The game has so much good to offer, but these three things really hamper the experience and for me it felt like I had to learn to get used to these quirks in order to enjoy the game as much as I did. The level design can be decent in many places and there were a few areas like Dragon Aerie that I found to be pretty cool looking, however, there are many other places that seem a lot less inspired like the Shaded Woods. I also felt that the interconnected level design found in the first game was missing here, however, you could argue that because the game allows you to Fast-Travel from the very beginning, the developers weren’t as concerned about creating shortcuts back to Majula.

Now, enemy placement, this is a controversial one. Let me start off by saying, I do not think that the game is totally unfair and I think that most of the areas in the game actually have fine enemy placement. I did not attempt to speedrun the game, it took me 16 hours so clearly not, I took my time and killed things and made my way through most of the game just fine. But one has to admit that there are some places, like Iron Keep, where the enemy placement just gets a little too nutty. I did not make it past Iron Keep and the Smelter Demon boss until I had reset so many times that the enemies simply stopped spawning. Maybe that is a skill issue on my part, but putting 10 Alonne Knights in those tight spaces I thought was kind of bogus.

The final of the three points, Poor Programming. Now, obviously I’m not going to pretend that I have the skills of the people who programmed this game. They are clearly more experienced than me, however, I am willing to still make observations about a couple of things which I believe were better in the previous game, DS1. When playing Dark Souls II initially I was really bothered by the lack of responsiveness compared to the first game. There are many times where I would take damage, but my character would have absolutely no reaction to it. The enemies would slide through me like butter and the health bar would drain, no flinch, no grunt, nothing. This is one of those things that I simply got used to while playing, and leveling up my ADP certainly helped too, nonetheless I feel that in some aspects Dark Souls II feels weirdly unresponsive compared to the first game which was praised for its responsive, polished, and fair combat.

At the end of the day, I’m happy to say that I have finally sat down and completed Dark Souls II. It was a game that I avoided for many years, but now I’ve done it. I’ve completed all the Soulsborne games. I was nervous about Dark Souls II, but thankfully, it seems that this is a game I would definitely enjoy giving a second attempt.

Coming from DS1 remaster, this game definitely feels different. Honestly not a huge fan, here is a rundown of what i don't like.

Graphics - 3/10

In a lot of ways it doesn't look much different from DS1 remaster. That is good and bad because I would expect the graphics to get better with time. I don't like some of the art direction either, with the zombie green look hollows have now. It's very ugly and almost cartoonish. The U.I. is also very bad, and desperately needs cleaned up.

Controls - 3/10

The controls are awful and i hate them. They made a ton of really poor design choices when it came to mechanics like double-clicking being its own input, making something as simple as attacking difficult. the default controls are VERY unintuitive. I think this is a byproduct of the game being lazily ported from Xbox. The only reason I don't give it a 1/10 here is because with a lot of tweaking you can somewhat fix the controls so that they are manageable. BE AWARE!!! though that to save any changes that you make in the settings, you MUST use your cursor to select the green Xbox A button, then back out with the red Xbox B button. If you hit ESC it will not save your changes. extremely annoying.

Story - ?/10

I'm not nearly far enough to judge this yet. May come back and edit if i ever finish the game.

Gameplay - 5/10

Souls games aren't known for holding hands, I get that. However, you should be introduced to and well informed of the games more basic mechanics in the starting levels. I almost missed getting something as basic as the Estus Flasks because i didn't see the woman in the starting town. For whatever reason she also is the only way you can level up. I don't know if that changes later, but if you accidentally aggro her or kill her you're completely screwed in the early stages.

Conclusion -

If you're a fan of the Souls games, Dark Souls 2 is playable but not without first changing the settings. Personally I'm hoping for a remaster that will fix the ugly graphics/U.I. and be a true PC made game rather than a port.

Posted: August 5, 2024
Dark Souls II is an over-hated gem.

There's a lot to love about DS2. Its soundtrack, its level designs, far more varied move sets over the original, the power stance mechanic. I've heard many times from the people who like this game that it's a vibe. And they're absolutely right. Sure, you'll get ganked, especially in this version, and unlike DS1 and other FromSoftware games that followed, you can't wholly rely on i-frame rolling your way out of everything without eating damage if you're not light rolling or going the right way. And I don't mind that too much. Maybe it's because I started off with Sekiro, but I still think rolling THROUGH an attack that should have caught you square in your undead beef jerky-looking face looks ridiculous and doesn't make sense. Still, it's a different enough experience that I see why it turns some DS1 fans off. But while I can agree with the folks who understand that this system works differently from DS1 and how it's just not for them, I can still completely disagree with the people who outright say this is a bad game.

There's aspects of it that are worth criticizing: its overpopulated sections; the runbacks that feel unreasonable because so many nearby bonfires are either hidden away or easy to miss; the lack of an offline mode, which wasn't added onto Scholar of the First Sin, despite the fact that even Dark Souls: Remastered added it; healing animations for both the Estus Flask and the Life Gems that take AEONS, shifting from DS1's instant healing to a heal-over-time, a huge focus on proper stamina management that isn't thoroughly explained anywhere (I found out about the inability to sprint during stamina recovery when it's going up from empty only after finishing the final boss), or the fact that there can be a point where you clear out an entire area of enemies because the game stops respawning them after a certain amount of times killed to discourage farming, but you might still not get every possible drop because rare drop rates are still a thing. That last one is probably the one that kinda bothered me the most, because while I like the fact that I can essentially clear an area like a more traditional game and make my runbacks to bosses easier, I also like the collection aspect in FromSoftware's games, especially with how cool the outfits, armor sets, and weapons they make tend to look. And the fact that the only way to get any missed drops from enemies you've cleared is to use an item to make an area around your bonfire of choice bumped up by one NG+ cycle permanently is kinda wild.

Outside of all of that, I enjoyed the hell out of this game. Despite its aged graphics, its art design is just gorgeous. There's a ton of both beautiful and disturbing imagery to discover that got me feelin' feelings on multiple occasions throughout the Bearer of Curse's journey. It doesn't always have the layered, interconnected design between areas like DS1 did for its earlier sections, but most of the levels aren't so linear as to detract from making navigating them engaging. There's lore here that expands upon what went down in DS1 in a way I genuinely enjoyed. It does it on a larger scale over a longer time period, so there's not too many in the way of blatant callbacks to DS1. But it's showing how the cycle has simply continued after DS1's ending. Drangleic is somehow a brighter-looking, more colorful, and varied world than Lordran was, without taking away from that same feeling it being a doomed and dying world, cursed to repeat a cycle that only ever leads to undeath, something that DS1 already did so well, and DS2 has only improved upon.

I'm gonna say it. I had fun with Dark Souls II. More than I expected. I'll admit: part of that likely has to do with the fact that I didn't wanna get sweaty with this like other folks might. I went STR/DEX to be able to try out as many different melee weapons as possible, hid behind greatshields if I had to, didn't forget to level ADP, and didn't hesitate to use summons (because if a boss is gonna gank you, you have the right to gank them back). So I'm sure taking what some might call an "easy" route kept this game from being as frustrating as it might have been for others. There's enough tools here to mitigate any frustration that can be had from dealing with a system that's a lot slower and more dangerous than Dark Souls 1 was. And there's so much to it to sink your teeth into that's so good that doesn't have much to do with the mechanical minutiae that people argue about, to a point where I can't fathom calling this a "skippable" game in the series like so many do. It isn't.

This is what people call "the worst" FromSoftware game? This is their idea of a "bad" game? Because man, it's still better than a lot of the AAA slop that's out there, by a country mile. I'm very excited for DS3, but I'm very glad I didn't take anyone's advice and skipped this, because I would've missed out on something beautiful.

+Great visuals. Dark Souls 1 has its own visual identity but the direction itself leaves a little to be desired as far as lighting goes. Dark Souls 2 does a LOT with shadows and lighting effects, as well as the rendering techniques used for vistas & horizons. It’s a very unique and gorgeous looking game even 10 years later
+Mechanically very sound - Feels like a strong evolution of DS1 mechanics.
--- Making healing with Estus gradual and harder to ‘pull off’ and making lifegems your go-to for healing between encounters is really smart because it gives you more healing options (letting the game throw more, harder challenges at you) while also making you choose between healing options responding to specific situations.
--- Making respawns finite is smart because you can’t farm souls and you are granted a reasonable appreciated leniency on sections that are completely walling you.
--- Invasions while Hollowed are good because invasions should’ve always been a consistent threat and not something you accept as a tradeoff of another benefit like full humanity (or God forbid, having a white summon - Invasions being effectively ‘opt in’ or giving the player a massive advantage by default like Elden Ring is a disaster)
--- Incremental Hollowing is good because it eases the player into playing Hollowed and lets you space out human effigies vs. being hard cut upwards of 50% of your health after a death and feeling effectively robbed of your fighting odds all at once.
--- Shouldn’t have to explain why NG+ map changes are great
--- Powerstancing is just one more layer to build variety and it provides a lot of reward for a lot of risk
--- More crowd-control centric bosses give the player a greater variety of fights to expect, and more reason to learn other weapons and keep backup options
+Pretty good story that builds on DS1 in a really unexpected alternative way. It’s a compelling exploration of the original game’s themes that turn them on their head a little bit. I’m still not really a fan of Souls story presentation but what’s there is good.
-A couple of rough levels, same as any other Souls game. The runback sucks, and the runback sucks for the same reason - aggro from very far away, do high impact mid-range damage and they gank the ♥♥♥♥ out of you. It's just irritating.
-The game doesn’t feel super balanced, in the player's favor. Because of the open structure of the game you’re going to be pretty advantaged for the latter half of the game no matter what order you do levels in, with the exception of .
- DLC sucks. The levels are very twisty and windy in a forgettable way and the final boss is just completely garbage (Awful range, awkwardly paced fight thanks to the RNG on his flights, just terrible). I think all the DLCs overcompensate on difficulty but does so by putting you through a lot of misery.
-Soul Memory, a total-souls count for your player, makes getting consistent invasions a challenge, and consistent summons ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ impossible. I like it conceptually, it just can’t be the core MMR/ELO system because my multiplayer pool was VERY dried up by the end of the game.
+Music was generally good, though not much stands out
-Another Souls game where you have no reason to, and are arguably punished FOR changing weapons from one you upgraded to one that is not, because your invested starter weapon will be better than a new weapon in the same class dropped at endgame. This is so silly. Cmon.
+Favourite bosses:
-Worst bosses: [/spoiler]SINH THE SLUMBERING DRAGON, Sir Allone’s Grab[/spoiler]

Run Time: 48h01m42s

Great game. It has a lot to say about the themes of the previous game and it has a lot that it evolved mechanically from it. The sad part is how many great decisions and lessons were discarded from Dark Souls 2 as the series moved forward

8/10

**A Masterclass in Game Design: Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin.**

In a world where mediocrity often reigns supreme, Dark Souls 2 stands tall as a beacon of excellence, a testament to the unparalleled genius of Hidetaka Miyazaki and his team at FromSoftware. This game is not just a masterpiece; it's an epiphany, a transformative experience that will recalibrate your very perception of what a video game can achieve.

Miyazaki's magnum opus is a symphony of interconnected design elements, each one meticulously crafted to create an immersive, hauntingly beautiful world that will leave you awestruck and, occasionally, terrified. The level design is a work of art, a labyrinthine tapestry woven from threads of exploration, combat, and narrative. Every area, from the mist-shrouded forests of Brightstone Cove to the crumbling grandeur of Drangleic Castle, is a masterclass in atmosphere and tension.

But it's not just the environments that make Dark Souls 2 a standout – it's the way the game flows, the way each section builds upon the last to create an almost unbearable sense of momentum. The combat, oh the combat, is a beautiful ballet of steel and strategy, where every enemy encounter is a puzzle waiting to be solved, and every victory is a hard-won triumph. The controls are precise, responsive, and intuitive, making even the most grueling battles a joy to engage in.

Posted: August 2, 2024
It's not the most beloved Fromsoft game but it has some unique features the others could have used. Yes, it is flawed but it's worth the price on sale. Power-stancing is a great feature that significantly improved combat. The character creator is pretty good (could use more hair options). While it never gets credit for this, it did improve healing at least. No more needing a ridiculous amount of faith and still only having 2 healing spells that affect allies. Caressing prayer clearing the poison condition in a small AoE makes all the difference in a few zones.

Fragrant branches aren't as bad as some reviews make them seem but I understand some of the frustration. The story is nothing amazing. The Demon Souls mechanic of losing maximum HP while hollow (until human again), is a bit of a downside.

Nonetheless, it's a blast with a friend or two.
Pros:
The weapon variety and power-stancing makes combat more fun.
The DLCs are tough so people who like the difficulty of dark souls have something to look forward to.
You can use bonfire ascetics to make a zone more difficult and get stuff from new game + without entering new game plus and getting all the way back to that zone.
Better system overall for faith casters. Guiding that one friend through Dark Souls made easier with healing.
Early leveling goes by fast and getting upgrading materials feels like less of a grind than DS 1 .
The option to just slap an element on a weapon for 2,000 souls and an elemental titanite is a game changer.

Cons:
Soul Memory: While I understand where they were coming from with the Soul Memory based system of connectivity, I preffered standard character level. When grabbing a small amount of souls to buy some more arrows can decide you can't connect to your friend that is a problem. Yes, the name engraved ring helps with that a little bit the issue remains.
DS1 Remastered and DS3 have the password system to connect to friends. Adding that system as a free patch would fix the issue listed above and there's no reason not to add it.
Gank Squads: you are constantly outnumbered and pulling enemies out one by one with arrows does get exhausting.
Maximum Health Reduction: While hollow your maximum health is reduced by a bit progressively more and more each time you die again while hollow. However, this is undone when you use a human effigy. There is also a ring to reduce the amount of this effect.

Overall though, it's pretty solid. If playing by yourself without the agape ring: 7/10. If playing by yourself with the agape ring: 6/10. If playing with a friend 8/10.

Is it amazing? Not particularly. Is it as terrible as some of the fandom make it out to be? No.

Posted: August 2, 2024
This game is rather infamous as being the "black sheep" of the Fromsoft family, and because of that I only played this recently after having played all of the other Fromsoft titles. That being said, I believe that this game is worth a playthrough or two. It is bashed I think by the community too much, as it does a lot right, but at the same time is a bit deserving of its status. Let me explain:

For starters, this game is very different, and highly experimental. This is probably its greatest strength and weakness. Like powerstancing in Elden Ring? You can thank this game for bringing it to the series. Do you prefer a more open approach to progression as opposed to the linear nature of DS3 or Sekiro? This game gives you a surprising amount of freedom once you figure out the layout of the map. Enjoy build variety? There are so many different pieces of gear, armor, weapons, and spells to grind for. Whether its for a min/max PVP build or fashion souls there's something for everyone. Did you enjoy Int/Faith casting in another game? Again, thank DS2 for making it more mainstream (in the form of Hexes mainly). And as for Bonfire Ascetics, I really wish they had a similar mechanic to this in other games (Sekiro is the only one that came close with allowing you to re-fight bosses in a gauntlet).

On the downside, this experimental approach can also lead to some very awful areas or encounters, easily some of the worst in the series, up there with Bed of Chaos. Black Gulch? I know that Miyazaki loves poison swamps, but this area is cancer. Snowfield in the 3rd DLC? Good luck and try not to lose your sanity; if you enjoy it then I'll certainly question yours. And as for boss design? There are some good ones, such as the community fan favorites of Sir Alonne or Fume Knight, and every Souls game has its fair share of weaker bosses, but ♥♥♥♥ me there are a lot of fairly subpar boss encounters in this game (Skeleton Lords, Rat Vanguard, and Covetous Demon come to mind first).

Overall, its not a bad game, is underrated and I would recommend it. I had genuine fun trying out different builds and some of the unique spells and weapons are fun to play with. However, I wouldn't fault someone for disliking it and believe that you need to lower your expectations a bit when you go into this game, or you'll just end up getting frustrated and disappointed.

I went into the game thinking "I just want to try it so I can validate the notion that it sucks" like I did for Devil May Cry 2. Unlike Devil May Cry 2, this game is actually really good despite its flaws. DMC2 is irredeemably bad.

Adaptability is the game's biggest flaw, so getting to enough iframes (96 AGL for 11 iFrames, which is the same as the DS1 medium roll), will make the experience much better. A lot of the bosses are mechanically simple and disappointing to fight.

The game is very slow paced, and often unfair. However, I think of it as this game's "flavor". If you go in expecting it to be trying to f you at every turn and are okay with that type of challenge, it is very fun. It's clearly not as polished or fair as the other entries in the series, but it's shortcomings are a refreshing change of pace. You'll often get spammed with mobs, killed by traps that seem very unfair, and the game seems designed by a troll.

Where the game really starts to shine is the DLCs, which aside from the co-op areas (blue smelter demon, frigid outskirts, and the gank squad are terrible areas), are actually very well designed and usually quite fair.

TLDR I expected to hate this game but have completed all boss runs on it twice now (I typically only replay games if I really like them), and I'm sure i'll do another run in the future.

The negatives of Dark Souls 2 are vastly overstated, especially compared to its multitude of positives. Not only was it innovative in some aspects (e.g. bonfire ascetics, power-stance), it also has an enormous quantity of content (weapons, regions, enemies, bosses etc.) relative to the other titles, some beautiful sections all throughout the entire game (ranging from Heide's Tower to Dragon Aerie), a great variety of choice regarding how you want to progress, and to top it all the masterfully written story (and correspondent themes) - the latter alone having turned this into my favorite Soulslike.
7.75 / 10 - Plagued by similar issues Dark Souls 1 was hindered by (a flawed production), Dark Souls 2 has a tremendous amount of lost potential. Despite this, it would be a plain mistake to consider this a bad game.

Generally, I did not enjoy the combat, and sometimes going through specific levels after already exploring it and dealing with the enemy placement was frustrating and unfun. However, as someone who has played the other Souls games, I imagine that my distaste for the gameplay likely stems from it being a "different flavor" compared to the others in the series.
Other than hitboxes, some of which are inexcusably bad (mimic), I can't think of much that is inherently wrong with the game. I LOVED the variety of weapons and armor, the atmosphere of the game feels cozy and nostalgic, the hub as well as the NPCs were lovely, and most importantly to me, my character looked and felt badass.
I think that DS2 did a lot of things that I wish carried on into the later games, and I am glad a lot of the things in DS2 did not carry over, but overall, I do think it is a good game.

And just because I feel like adding this; I started using a greatsword, which evolved into me dual-wielding great swords, which evolved into me dual-wielding ultra great swords, which led to me beating the DLCs with both smelter demon swords. By the end I had the alva helm, heide knight chest and hands, and dark leggings. I felt and looked awesome, and while I was glad to uninstall and never play this game again, I was a little sad that I would never be able to go on this journey again.

Posted: July 29, 2024
This game is greatest evidence towards the fact that Hidetaka Miyazaki is the only one capable of making the best ‘soulsborne’ games. This is a dark souls game in name only; it’s a pale imitation. It is endlessly obsessed with its predecessor but fails to capture anything about Dark Souls 1 that made it so universally beloved in the first place.

Interconnected, believable world? Not here, the kingdom of Drangleic is a physically incoherent mess that doesn’t even try to present a facsimile of Dark Souls 1’s timeless, intricate, purposeful world design. Areas are connected haphazardly, without careful consideration, in ways that only make sense if you ignore the very principles of logic and consistency. No-Man’s Wharf is a harbor that somehow exists DIRECTLY BELOW Heide’s Tower of Flame, which is an area that very clearly exists at sea level, given the fact that the titular Tower of Flame is sinking into the ocean. The Iron Keep is a castle nestled between multiple volcanoes, surrounded by an expanse of seemingly endless flat earth with rivers of lava, that is inexplicably accessed by ascending an elevator at the top of a tower that overlooks a quarry atop a mountain, an area with a wide open sky called Earthen Peak. So, a hellish landscape of lava and volcanoes exists ABOVE another mountain, from which you can stare up at the sky and see no such thing? Other areas are accessed essentially via teleportation or flight, and so are unburdened from even pretending to be connected in interesting, logical ways like in Dark Souls 1, 3, Bloodborne, Sekiro, and Elden Ring.

Challenging yet fair gameplay that rewards smart reactions, and well-designed enemies that have tough but balanced movesets? No. Instead, Dark Souls 2’s gameplay is largely comedically easy or artificially difficult. Seemingly a big focus of this game is to kill the player at any expense, hence the cringeworthy achievement ‘this is dark souls’ which is received upon a player’s first death, as well as the introductory sequence in which a coven of old women laugh at the player while they explain how many times you will lose your souls, over and over again. Not only is this a crucial misunderstanding of the original intention of Miyazaki when he invented this subgenre with Demon’s Souls, (who has stated in interviews that the point of these games is NOT difficulty, or punishment, but rather narrative cohesion with the gameplay) but this isn’t even an accurate assessment of this game’s level of challenge. The game is largely quite easy, due to numerous factors. The addition of infinite healing with lifegems, the poor enemy AI, pitifully undercooked and easily exploitable bosses like The Rotten, Dragonrider, Covetous Demon, etc, Adaptability giving every build, including defensive tanks, the ability to gain as many i-frames as you want, and the sheer amount of souls and therefore levels the game showers you with leading to quite overleveled gameplay even in the midgame. The game’s attempt to live up to this espoused ideal of ‘difficulty’ and ‘death’ leads to an immense amount of encounters with overwhelming mobs of enemies that chainpull all at once, as if souls games are a good fit for Dynasty Warriors type enemy encounters. This isn’t hard, it’s just tedious, and that is an incredibly important distinction that the directors of DS2 failed to grasp. Everything about the game is designed to induce tedium. The new hollowing system, a perverse copy of Demon’s Souls’ Human/Soul form mechanic that decreases your max hp each time you die? Pure tedium. Adaptability as a stat, which requires you to level it before anything else due to its universal importance for every build? Pure tedium. Soul Memory, which irreparably destroyed this game’s online, both on the original release AND this re-release? More tedium, forcing players to continuously make new characters in order to cooperate or invade in specific total soul acquisition ranges rather than allowing players to simply choose not to level up like in literally any of the other games in this series.

I won't say much of the story, since much of the Souls games stories are open to interpretation. I won't spoil any plot elements of any of the games aside from their intros. I WILL say that DS2 (as well as DS3) reference the first game far too much, and thus do not stand on their own in terms of lore and plot. Rehashes of previous bosses, NPCs, and tropes abound. The inciting incident and subsequent plot of DS2 is far weaker in terms of player motivation than DS1, the game directly preceding this. The game actually lampshades the bad plot by having a woman in the intro cutscene say, flat out, that the player character is traveling to Drangleic 'without really knowing why'. This obviously is a comically poor inciting incident compared to DS1, in which the player is saved by Oscar of Astora then asked to listen to him recount the prophecy of the fate of the undead as his dying wish. Hell, even compared to DeS, this is bad, and that all that game gives you is the bare context for the world and the important characters within it - DS2 can't even manage to do that.

Side note but the enemy placements of the vanilla game were far better than SOTFS, but unfortunately SOTFS is the only way to play the game in dx11 at 60fps so you have to choose whether you want good enemy placement or the bare minimum 60fps (not even high frame rates, just 60) for this game. Also, my time played on steam is low but I played the game at release on 360, and SOTFS on PS4 numerous times. Got it on here recently to reluctantly include it as part of my full-series runs.

Is Dark Souls 2 a good game? Sure, I think it’s far better than most ‘soulslikes’ out there, and certainly for the time it released. As many flaws as it has, it stands head and shoulders above empty-headed imitators like Lords of the Fallen (the old one, AND the new one) and Mortal Shell. I was very, VERY hyped leading up to its release, I remember rewatching footage of the network test that took place in Huntsman's Copse so many times back in the day.

Is it a good Dark Souls game? No. It had some interesting ideas, such as unique NG+ enemies and Powerstancing, and it also happens to have a LOT of good armor + weapon design, but the bar for quality with Fromsoft is just too high to excuse this game. I wanted to love this game, but even after over a decade of playing this series, and nearly a decade with this game, I just can't. It just failed to grasp the core elements of what makes this series special. Apparently this game was headed by two amateur directors and had a very troubled development, and all of that really does shine through in the final product. It’s janky, overly ambitious, inconsistent, and full of purposeless decisions that either add nothing of value or actively detract from the foundation that Demons Souls, Dark Souls, and Miyazaki have laid out for not only this franchise, but the entire genre of Action RPGs.

Posted: July 28, 2024
First of all, I both love and hate this game.

I just finished my first playthrough in around 65 hours, I'm positive I've found most secrets, I've found all covenants, done all bosses, the only thing that remains is farming Loyce Souls in the Burnt Ivory King DLC for the armors and weapon,

It's probably my favorite game in the souls trilogy; it feels like a much more complete, and wayyy longer game than the others. DS1 has too many glaring issues for it to be enjoyable all the way through; DS3 to me felt extremely linear and way too short of a game for it to warrant it's price-tag.

DS2 on the other hand, has a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ of content, an unbelievable amount of secrets and hidden paths you can take; many areas have amazing interconnectivity (especially the DLC areas like Sunken King and Burnt Ivory King). Of course, it goes without saying this game also has it's flaws; mainly the spammy enemy placements, and mostly consisting of below-average bosses.

Personally speaking, this game's lore was enticing to me; the plot twists when you discover were top-notch.

The armor sets in this game are the best, they're so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ cool. It's better than any other game in the souls trilogy and including Elden Ring. the weapon variety and special effects are awesome too, I love how many melee weapon catalysts in this game are.

I just wish there were some areas that had less enemy spam (specifically Earthen Peak, Iron Keep, Old Iron King DLC, Shrine of Amana), ADP not existing, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ boss runbacks not existing, and if the bosses were just a tad bit better it'd objectively be a much better game with an overall better experience for first-timers.

Fume Knight, Sir Alonne (I liked him so much I did the no-hit Seppuku secret), Burnt Ivory King, Sinh, Ruin Sentinels, Looking Glass Knight and Velstadt were my favorites of the bunch. I love how much content there is in this game, and every single area has a vastly different and gorgeous (or grimey) looking environment; walking on the chains in ORK DLC, Eleum Loyce, Iron Keep, Shrine of Amana, Dragon Shrine, Drangleic Castle are all amazing areas in terms of environmental design. the lore to me was really enticing to me personally, all the reveals and lore descriptions were really cool.

The PVP that I've done for a couple hours was really cool also! The most fun I had while doing Souls PVP.

Please, do give this game a chance, It's wonderful. It's a great game, one of the best games I've played, personally, despite my gripes with it, I still love it, a lot.

Posted: July 28, 2024
Spoilers ahead, you have been warned.

I have done an entire playthrough of this game, including the DLC sections, until I hit credits. Most of that playthrough was done while the game's online functionality was disabled by FromSoft to patch the netcode against hacking, so I had no player help during that playthrough (this is why my hours that Steam reports is so low). I used no mods.

I am on a second playthrough right now in which I am using a mod that fixes the controller deadzones and another mod that improves the lighting. Both of these are online compliant and do not give me any sort of advantage that would result in violations of the TOS.

I have zero nostalgia for Dark Souls 1 and am capable of making up my mind on my own, so I didn't come to my conclusions about the game just because some YouTubers made some review videos. I have seen videos that recommend this game and videos that do the opposite.

The story, characters, lore, atmosphere, and soundtrack range from decent to great, aside from Aldia who is a yammering pretentious jerk. None of it is as good as Bloodborne or Elden Ring, but it's serviceable.

The level design ranges from not great to great. There has been endless debate on the fact that the level placement makes no sense in certain places, specifically a lava fortress being right above a windmill connected by an elevator or a dragon fortress being above another fortress and the skyboxes not reflecting any of this. This doesn't make me mad or anything, but it does count against the game from a logic standpoint.

The gameplay itself is the worst in the entire series. The way that character movement, controller input, lock-on, stamina recovery, weapon aiming, and everything else that is essential to you interacting with the world of the game is inferior and backwards in comparison to DS1, DS3, Bloodborne, and Elden Ring (I've not played DeS but presumably DeS plays like DS1). SOTFS' grunt enemy spam is annoying and if you are an experienced player you will find the grunts more of a challenge than the bosses. The controller deadzone fix I am currently using makes this game less painful to play, but that's a compliment to the modder, and it only fixes the worst of many problems this game has.

Weapon durability is pointlessly low. All that this mechanic does is force you to buy and use resources to solve a problem that didn't need to exist, or go repair items that didn't need to break. It's no wonder that FromSoft abandoned this mechanic altogether by Elden Ring. It adds nothing.

Arrows fired by grunts have tracking ability. These are not magic arrows, just arrows fired by ordinary grunts. It's just there to make your experience worse.

Some areas require the use of torches to be able to navigate. This is a cool mechanic that is underused.

Bonfire Aesthetics, items that reset area enemies and bosses and upgrade their difficulty to the next stage of NG+ without NG+, is a great concept that should be present in Elden Ring and is wasted on a lesser game like this.

Adaptability stat is not the worst thing in the world if you are aware of it and farm levels to fix your roll and Estus drink speed. It's still pointless and adds nothing, but at least you can fix it unlike weapon durability.

The Hollowing mechanic is okayish. It's not great that the game punishes you for failure by reducing your health, but there is a ring that mitigates the problem and you can reverse Hollowing with a consumable. What makes this mechanic not a waste is that there is a thematic payoff. If you claim the four crowns found in the main game and DLC areas and bring them to a specific place, you can become immune to the Hollowing mechanic so long as you wear one of the four crowns. This also makes you immune to the curse effect of one of the two final bosses of the main game, which is very satisfying. If this payoff was not there, I would consider this mechanic pointless as well.

The opening cutscene's message of "You will lose your Souls over and over again" is god awful and reflects this game's design choice of difficulty for the sake of difficulty, instead of difficulty for the sake of challenge that is present in every other title.

The one big advantage that DS2 has over DS1 is that at least DS2 is a finished game. DS1's Lost Izalith area, even in the Remastered version, is an unfinished mess.

I cannot in good conscience recommend that anyone play DS2 when all of the other FromSoft games exist and Lies of P and Code Vein are around. Here are the only justifications for playing DS2:
1) If you want to earn the right to make up your own opinion on the game, you should play DS2 for yourself.
2) If you are desperate to play everything made by FromSoft, you might as well put up with DS2.
3) If you install the controller deadzone fix mod, it will make the game somewhat less horrible.
4) If you are willing to stay offline, you can find a bunch of mods that fix various problems mentioned above that will get you banned from online play if you go online. I have not done this, but it's out there.
5) If you enjoy difficult for the sake of difficulty and hyperpatient to a fault, you will probably enjoy DS2 more than most people. Most defenders of DS2 self-identify as having these traits.
6) If you really want to experience the story and lore of this game and can't stand watching a let's play, your only real option is to play DS2 and put up with it.
7) If you are "addicted" to Soulsborne and Soulslikes and can't get into any other game type because you need to play this type of game, DS2 is available.

None of the above options is sufficient justification for me to recommend Dark Souls 2. FromSoft is, at the moment, the best video game developer out there, and making DS2 helped them learn and improve so they could make the masterpieces that came after this lump of coal. It's still a lump of coal, and no amount of resentment by its defenders about YouTube videos affecting the reputation of the game is going to suddenly turn it into a diamond. A video game has to feel fun to play to be a good video game. DS2 lacks this.
