Dark Souls II is a game saddled with the daunting task of following up on one of the most acclaimed games of all time, and while it doesn't quite reach those heights, it's a very strong effort in its own right. Between changes to the enemy spawn system, the placement of enemies, and the layout of environments, this is a game that encourages brute forcing enemies until they stop respawning over more conventionally skilled play. The world itself is less interconnected than the first game, and indeed, the game expects you to rely on the expanded warp system rather than navigating previously-conquered areas, giving the player that much less incentive to become accustomed to their environment. Visually, the environments themselves are mixed bag, with some being visually stunning and others incredibly bland. In regards to combat, healing is a much riskier endeavor, as it leaves you open considerably longer and enemies seem to be better at closing gaps than the first game.

But this is still a Dark Souls game. The tension of risking your souls is still wholly intact, the combat is still generally methodical, and discovering new areas and secrets is still an absolute delight. And indeed, some of these changes force the player to rethink their approach in positive ways. It's just a bit more uneven. Don't go in expecting something as good as the first game, and you'll still easily come out having had a good time.

It has a different engine, slightly altered game mechanics, different attack/parry timings, wacky enemy placement... and it completely threw me off my game when I transitioned from 1. Although, vice versa, it was fluid, with a significantly different combat pace than the rest, and leaves little room for missteps. BIG experimentation was done in this game.

Graphics? They're gorgeous, and a step up from 1. A lot of cool sceneries to get to, a lot of sight seeing to look forward to.

Base game bosses? Quite manageable, a few good in an abundance, but lacking in terms of quality. Even coming back, the bosses never felt that memorable and I hate saying that, as they are cool, original concepts that just couldn't live up to their own expectations through FromSoft's execution. It just makes me feel like 32 base game bosses is a bit much (which is 10 more than DS1s). Maybe cutting it to 26 would've helped..?

DLC bosses? As someone who just got around to fighting them in my 2nd run for the first time, I enjoyed it. This is the quality in boss fights I enjoy the most.

The convolution of co-op is... weird. Having to meet the criteria of being close in Soul Memory (AKA, total souls collected), and wearing the Name-engraved Ring (which you can buy at the beginning of the game) to even find your friend's sign is awkward. I came from Dark Souls Remastered and its password system, which was very simple and user friendly.

A friend of mine mentioned this game encourages the player to co-op, which seems pretty on the money. For example, the Small White Soapstone enables a shade (AKA, a lesser phantom) to roam in an area the player has already completed, and allowing 8 min, 20s (or 12 min, 30s) of helping. As you kill more difficult enemies, the timer goes down significantly, and once it hits 0, you get a Smooth & Silky Stone upon completion, and a restoration of health, and , allowing you to co-op again.

The multiplayer side is your usual P2P (peer to peer). I had constant disconnects every 3-5 minutes, rendering multiplayer to be unplayable for me. All because my ISP has strict NAT type. :/

I have no idea if others have had the same problem, even after scouring the internet like I did, but I ended up using a VPN called for playing, which works phenomenally.

On the modding side, I'd say the most essential mod is . If you plan on playing multiplayer to invade others, or just to simply play with friends, use it. It is essentially a community patch, with some QOL improvements, automatic backups of your saves after every multiplayer instance, AND an anti-cheat helper.

Does it deserve the hate it gets from the community? . The extreme exasperation of this game from hardcore fans is... relatively justified. The hate for the enemy placement/quantity, the likelihood of getting ganked, and tying your iframes to a stat (ADP) does suck. But once you've established a build... it's so fun.

What happened in development? As an ambitious project, the creator himself, Hidetaka Miyazaki, already started development over Bloodborne as the director, and in turn, co-director's Tomohiro Shibuya and Yui Tanimura took charge over Dark Souls II's development, with Miyazaki taking a 'supervisory' role. Although, Miyazaki insisted on not being affiliated, intending to not stifle the directors' creativity. Later on, only one of the directors stayed (Tanimura), and had to stitch the game together because of Shibuya's wrong creative direction, and with time constraints in mind, he had to salvage together what he could and cohesively create a game from it. This also explains why the DLC has A LOT more quality put into it... since Tanimura had complete control, and better time prospects.

But why is this review positive? Even knowing all these objective negatives, , and proceeds to teach you patience. Every time I've conquered a difficult area, Majula always reminds me of the little instances of adversity I had at the very beginning, and makes me reminisce and take in this... wonderful view.

What can you take away from this review? Even though the lows and highs are REALLY apparent, Dark Souls II is a , and . If you do not enjoy a challenge of endurance, or a different direction from the original creator's work, do not play this game.

Posted: July 25, 2024
It must be said that playing this game after the first Dark Souls feels amazing as the graphics and movement are noticeably higher quality than those of its predecessor. The developers added so much including a few quality-of-life improvements that should have been in the first game. But I feel with every improvement they made two more problems sprung up in their place. As it is I recommend this game to people who care about this series on a religious level but not for new-coming casual players. To those, I suggest skipping this entry or sticking to just the first game. You aren't losing out on much as the lore can be gained threw a quick web search and connective lore is scant and hypothetical in many cases.

Either way, I'll try to keep it brief and stick to just my main issues.

If you are new to Fromsoft games you going to have to get used to phrases like "get gud" and "skill issue" directed at you if you are having trouble getting past a particularly hard area or boss. Fromsoft games often may feel like they are unfair. The difference between games like Elden Ring which feels unfair due to skill issues on the Player's part is that Dark Souls 2 is unfair because it's the game's fault. This isn't to say the game is unbeatable but it allows very little wiggle room to do so. Often times I found myself in situations where my only skill issue was where I allocated my skill points up to the point of the concerning boss or area. Thankfully, I'm glad to say, I have the skills to make it to the end of the game. (Skills being, looking up guides and game mechanics to figure out how the game works. (Mechanics like the new Adaptability stat that gives no context on how it works. (It controls your dodge roll i-frames and how fast you heal from your flasks. (Of which the community agrees you need at least a 26 to be on par with Dark Souls 1. (For me that was 20 levels of just getting back to normal from Dark Souls 1.))))). Then let's say you screwed up and you need higher stats. You can still grind of course but there is no spot to do that intentionally. As there is a cap for how many times a foe can be killed before it disappears... forever. Of course, the community has found a way to get the enemies to re-spawn, so forever isn't forever, But the method feels more like a lack of thought on the developer's part and not a game mechanic.

It also hides key items that are necessary to make it through certain areas in the game in dark nooks and crannies that you would never find on your own unless you are the type to forgo a left-hand item in place of a torch to see things in the distance... faintly. There is a whole section that is dark, filled with statues that spit poison at the player (Poisoning near-instantly if you are highly poison resistant or not.), enemies that command grab on entrance, two NPC dark invader spirits, and a major boss fight at the very end. Also, the only bonfire in the area after the entrance is near the end and blocked by a wall that can only be passed if you have a rare item you most likely won't have.

All in all it certainly tempers the spirit I just wish that my success wasn't mostly luck. I really could go on and there is a lot of advice I would give to those who choose to subject themselves to this experience, but I would suggest just looking up guides and the wiki on the many, many, MANY different things you need to know to complete the game that the game just fails to tell you. I will give you one. Grow your hatred of WINDMILLS. BURN them all ANY CHANCE YOU GET.

Dark Souls II (especially SotFS) is the most divisive FromSoftware game in the SoulsborneKiroRing "series", and for good reason. After the mainstream success of Dark Souls, FromSoftware once again experimented with the Souls formula, much like they did with Demon's Souls. While DS2 certainly has its flaws, if you can look past them, or at least tolerate them, you will have the pleasure of experiencing one of the most atmospheric games in the series.

Playing at a very casual pace, I finished 100% achievements on Steam with a strength build just shy of 100 hours. I have probably beat the game 10+ times with DLC between vanilla on PS3, SotFS on PS4, and now SotFS on PC. Each experience has been different, but memorable. After my most recent run through NG++, here are my thoughts on DS2:

- Ability to clearly understand which bonfires are located at which locations, displaying the bonfires you are missing (after a certain in-game event), and displaying which NG each bonfire is currently at
- Ability to Powerstance; Bonk Brothers rejoice!
- Ability to heal via limited rechargeable Estus Flasks or finite consumables
- Bonfire Ascetic to take specific sub-locations within the world to greater NG levels
- Ability to clearly understand the covenants, which covenant you currently belong to, and which rank you are in each covenant
- Fashion souls! No seriously, there are SO many different armor sets, weapons, and spells to try out
- Soul vessels giving you the ability to respec your character points a limited number of times each playthrough
- While strange, the ability to change gender mid-playthrough
- Unlimited "homeword bone" uses after acquiring a certain key item
- Limited respawning enemies, with workarounds like Covenant of Champions and Bonfire Ascetics
- New enemies in NG+
- One of the best DLC trilogies in ARPG history
- Ability to farm covenant rank items offline
- Ability to open a certain key door with the intended method and a Soul Memory method
- Stone Trader Chloanne best maiden
- Majula GOAT hub world

- Soul Memory and Agape Ring
- Penalty to maximum overall health with each death, until an Effigy is consumed; although I prefer it, perhaps this could have been a feature unique to the Covenant of Champions

- A few areas are punishingly difficult for certain build types, or just mostly unfair overall; specifically, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Valley and Shrine of Amana (still annoying to traverse through post-mage nerf)

Posted: July 23, 2024
TL;DR: not so great, Save your money for Ds1, Ds3, or Elden Ring.

Dark Souls 2:(SOTFS) presents hard challenges for the player to overcome, from combat to exploration, preparing for bosses, and even platforming. The inventory system becomes fully fledged with a huge addition of items and equipment for the player to use against the battles ahead. The quantity of choices in the player's hands is abundant, from swords and shields to axes, hammers, spells, hexes, miracles, and pyromancies. You are not short of means of combat.

However, the game is a parody of its predecessor. The level design is very lackluster; enemies come in hordes, almost as if there were 25% fewer enemies and they added more to make the game "harder." Areas go to nonsensical places, creating a sense of disbelief, or they end too abruptly, not being fully utilized to their full potential. The PvP was supposed to be the best of the franchise; however, many times enemies or I were so lagged that attacks were connecting when they were absolutely not supposed to, and I had cases of being invaded when hollowed.

The movement doesn't feel good, and the over-reliance of the game on multiplayer aspects narrowed the view of the developers on its single-player parts. Covenants are heavily reliant on the connection with other players, and the acquirement of necessary items is tedious and hard. The easy access to lifegems trivialized healing. Equipment condition, while an additional way for devs to balance the game, overstays its unwanted welcome since equipment degradation is too much in this game.

The game punishes players to an extreme with the addition of cutting a percentage of HP per death and soul memory. After a while, the combat becomes tedious and repetitive since you have too many enemies in any given area.

In conclusion: This game is a failed attempt at creating a new Dark Souls that tried to put an honestly good story on top of a mechanical disaster of a game, which tries to be "hard" by overwhelming the players with number of enemies instead of quality. I honestly loved this game, but after a repeat playthrough, the flaws were too many to handle.

Posted: July 22, 2024
Honestly, this game is far from bad, but it has aged the worst due to the direction that the series -and its adjacent games like Sekiro, Elden Ring and Bloodborne- have taken. Only with the perspective of the older FromSoft games, you can start to understand this game.

Miyazaki has been a blessing for the company, no doubt about it. And Elden Ring is one of my favorite games of the past decade. But increasingly, FromSoft, misguidedly pushed by their players, challenge-runners and speedrunners, among other tryhards, has been steering their games toward spectacular boss fights where the only puzzle left to solve is the combat in itself, which ultimately leads to a vicious cycle where with every game the movesets of both player and bosses -and especially bosses- becomes even more ridiculous. The general impressions of difficulty of their latest Eleden Ring DLC shows that the general public might have reached their limit in that aspect - and I did like the DLC a lot!.

Older FromSoft games, especially those that came before Miyazaki, were extremely janky, difficult, obtuse, but with very simple combat. Bosses could be difficult, but most of the time it was due to a lack of knowledge of the puzzle that the player was supposed to solve, and never due to attack spam, summersaults and other ♥♥♥♥ that we have been used to lately. And even then, most of the times bosses were actually not hard and easily cheeseable. The overall difficulty of the game came from the unforgiving gameplay, level design and enemy placement. Being trapped with four enemies in a tight room could be more challenging than beating a boss, for example. Sounds familiar? That's Dark Souls 2.

It's really easy to see this game as the black sheep of the family in hindsight, if you are returning from the later, less janky and more action-oriented games. Even coming from Dark Souls 1 it seems like an odd side-step instead of a progression. But go play Demon's Souls (you can do so in PC with RPCS3, easily). Or try emulating the old King's Field games and its adjacent games like Shadow Tower. I recently played King's Field IV: The Ancient City for the first time (PS2, emulated on PC) and now I understand what they were going for in Dark Souls 2. Iron Keep is not badly designed, indeed it was purposedly designed like a puzzle where the pieces to solve are the enemy kill order, player positioning, awareness of the environment.

If you take it slowly and accept the game's ruleset and design philosophy, there's a lot to see and enjoy in Dark Souls 2. If you want a slick action-game, with memorable boss fights and overpowered player-first combat, this ain't it.

And even then, as a regular souls-like, DS2 still has plenty to show to its better received siblings. Its PVP is some of the series' best, the bonfire ascetic mechanic is a great idea that I wish could be modded into Elden Ring, and build diversity for PvE is extremely wide. But playing it as a regular souls-like is probably not the best way to play it.

Remember all the discussions and praise that surrounded Demon's Souls and Dark Souls on release, due to their "novel" difficulty, in a time where most western games were taking away player agency in favor of more cinematic games? It's exactly that. A little more unfair perhaps, but it never treats the player like an idiot.

Summary:
This game, and specifically this version of this game, get a lot of hate in the Dark Souls community for being a significant departure from the standard the original set. That hate I think is largely overblown, as this game is a fantastic experience, with a wide variety of game play styles, and it heavily rewards specific sub-aspects of play, like patience, clearing, and farming. The boss design is consistently good, the DLC is great, and the fights got progressively more challenging in the NG+ and NG++ experience, which I enjoyed greatly.

Pros:
- Boss Design is great, and very fulfilling
- The story is convoluted in the way all FromSoftware titles are, it rewards you if you look, it seems empty if you dont.
- The music is perfection
- I ran multiple builds throughout my play throughs, and each felt like they had a good balance of strength and weakness.
- The level design is at its best in terms of promoting an exploratory mindset.
- Significantly improved covenant system
- Visuals are stunning beyond explanation

- Adaptability, Atunement and Vitality as stats are not good additions,
- The game suffers from being the oldest in the modern adaptations of the series, and has some jank because of it

This game plays like someone is shouting sekiro mechanics into the dark souls franchise, and if you embrace that feel, its very good. I had a lot of fun playing this game.

For the 3rd time im changing my review, this time to Positive.
Overall the game is a net good. I really enjoyed the world exploration and the sheer amount of bosses. But theres some things to consider

**Pros**
- Exploration is fun, levels are overall well designed
- Sense of accomplishment after completing bosses/hard area is on par/better than the other souls games
- With the DS2 texture mod and lighting engine mod, the game looks incredible. Sucks that you need a mod to do that though
- So much content and arguably the best NG+ in the entire series
- Souls are easy to get which means more levels. Doesn't feel broken and imo you would have to be incredibly negligent to over level.
- Enemy and boss design is actually really cool

**Cons**
- Enemies are spammed. Really bad at and past Iron Keep. That area SUCKS
- Optimization in some areas is wack
- Too many gimmick bosses
- Movement is very punishing, specifically when locking on. Character will take a long pause when changing directions
- Illusionary walls aren't hittable. You have to push the Talk button and hold it. Literally didn't know that until after I completed the game.
- The experience really sucks at times. It goes from being fun and fair to incredibly unfair out of nowhere. Iron Keep took me like 2 days (roughly like 4-5 hours) to get through because of how many ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ enemies there are
- Hitboxes are really janky. Sometimes its because large sword attacks have really big lingering hitboxes and other times it is just bad and rushed hitboxes.

With this many cons why do I rank it a Positive? Because overall im happy I completed the game. The challenge even though super unfair at times left me with an even stronger sense of joy then after Dark Souls. Personally, I recommend completing the other games first. Don't let DS2 keep you from playing DS3 or any other of the souls, this game is kind of like an optional challenge.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is amazing.

I am fully redoing this review after playing this game for...a few hours. Scholar of the First Sin is the definitive way to play Dark Souls II. The difficulty is actually significantly reduced in plenty of areas in comparison to the original game and there are accessible summons in many areas there hadn't been before for those who are struggling. Adjusted enemy placements are almost always used to encourage a cautious playstyle in the player. This game on having the player adopt a very cautious and tentative playstyle; one where you have to take encounters enemy-by-enemy, step-by-step. It's not that this game is necessarily hard; it's that the game has a very different approach to the nature of combat from the other games in the Soulsborne franchise from FromSoftware. It doesn't sit well with some people and that's okay. You don't have to like the way that the game plays. That's part of the territory for being a highly skill-based, intentionally-difficult game. However, that doesn't mean that it's a bad game. The Shrine of Amana can be really easily gotten through by taking enemies down slowly and methodically, one-by-one, instead of running through the area trying to get to the Demon of Song. This is just the way that it's designed.

At the end of the day, this game does have plenty of flaws, and it is a genuine fact that its development was very rushed. However, it's also true that DS1 had a very rushed dev cycle as well and most people find it to be a masterpiece. I just feel most people have to come back and approach DS2 with a different lens and many many more people would enjoy it for what it is. It is a very well-crafted experience that genuinely makes me have some of the most fun I've ever had with a video game.

Dark Souls II is a polarizing title for those that are fans of souls. There are primarily two camps: one which insists this game is unplayable garbage and another that thinks it is the greatest thing the series has produced. I'm more inclined to be somewhere in between. This is not a terrible game by any means, I thoroughly enjoyed it all the way through. Personally, I found it to be probably my least favorite, but I still liked the majority of the game. Miyazaki was not involved with development, and the game definitely shows this. However, it is in both positive and negative ways.

On a gameplay level, things are changed up from Dark Souls. For some reason I still don't understand, they decided to bring the Demon's Souls death system. So everytime the player dies, your max health gets lower until it caps halfway down the healthbar. Estus flasks are no longer the only source of healing, having added lifestones (basically crescent moon grass)which heal you while moving passively. The gameplay changes are a little jarring at first, but it takes little time to get used to it. My biggest gripe is the abandoning of DS1 world design. From the first two zones, it becomes apparent that the game is MUCH more linear and the world is no longer interconnected. Instead, most levels exist chained in clusters that have to be travelled between through teleports. While exploration still feels rewarding in the levels thanks to estus flask shards and branchs of yore, that overall feeling of world building and Metroidvania design is glaringly missing.

Despite the issues, I found the game to be endearing and charming in its own way, especially upon replay. The world feels kind of dreamy and strange. Kind of like an old fairytale. This captures it's own unique style and atmosphere. All the design choices along with the brilliant music and visuals, allow for an immersive experience that is only slightly worse than its brothers. Recommend for all souls fans on sale.

Posted: July 19, 2024
Dark souls 2 is considered by many to be the black sheep of the souls franchise and with good reason, it's not very good and it feels like a different game tonally.

This game reminded me a lot of devil may cry 2, maybe not as much as a cluster ♥♥♥♥ as that game but a similar troubled development cycle, with DS2 being run into the ground by a director who was replaced last minute by Yui Tanimura. Yui is often blammed for the failing of dark souls 2 much like Hideaki Itsuno was for DMC 2 and much like how Hideaki was able to redeem himself with devil may cry 3 Yui would go on to co-direct elden ring.

It's in Elden ring that you can see a lot of dark souls 2's DNA, from story beats to game mechanics like powerstance and Pharros' Lockstones to cut DS2 bosses coming back in Elden ring, there is a reason elden ring is often referred to as dark souls 2 2.

So whilst I don't really like dark souls 2 it is still an important game for fromsoft and before we talk about the bad let's acknowledge the good.

DS2 introduced a large number of quality of life fixes that carried on into the newer games. Consuming multiple souls in one go, remapping the jump button, better ui etc.

Build variety and weapon armour variety is very strong with plenty of unique ways to play and tons of cool armour sets and weapons, many are truly unique with cool move sets and some are hidden in new game plus. You can do a keyblade build or a ladel build and it's all viable, also powerstance is such an incredible feature it's a shame DS3 fumbled it and elden ring's version still wasn't as good.

New game plus is the best in this game, with an actual reason to keep playing from new enemy placement to new items only avaliable in ng+, such a shame no game past ds2 has incorporated this. Also Bonfire aesthetics are a fantastic addition allowing you push areas into a ng+ state so you can farm bosses.

Pvp is supposedly the best, cant confirm as I've never cared for ds2 pvp.

The dlcs are alright.

Dragon form looks sick in this game.

And lastly the majula theme is one of the best bits of music in any of the fromsoft games.

That's all the positives I can think of, I'll try not to go too overboard on the negatives as I still think this game is worth playing and I still enjoy playing it but the other games in the series are just so much better.

This game just feels off, from the intro cutscene to the way the old ladies talk about losing your souls, it's so on the nose it's embarrassing and it loses all of that mystique that the previous game had.

Also the whole tone of the game just feels different, like it's not part of the same series, almost like this is a fantasy game set in a different universe, this isn't an issue until they start really badly implementing call backs to dark souls 1 in the most hamfisted attempt to build on the lore, it's contradictory at best and lazy at worse. Nothing they introduce improves what was set out in DS1 and I really hate the half ass plot in this game.

Level design is a joke, the game starts off fairly well when you notice you could potentially have 4 paths to chose from but once you realise that most levels are long corridors with a boss at the end with no interconnected levels like DS1 you start to realise how weak the level design is, you can see why fast travel was introduced from the get go because you can't just loop back round like before. What's worse is most levels are extremely bland or nonsensical or both. The amount of empty rooms with a chest in them is laughable and most item placements are done with little or no context. I always loved the twin humanities in the great hollow to signify two illusory walls in dark souls 1 but nothing like that is present in ds2.

One thing that I loved about dark souls 1 was the fact you could see other areas from other points in the game, from anor londo you can see the dukes archives and from the tomb of giants you can see ash lake/lost izalith. In dark souls 2 however you see a windmill and after riding an elevator up the windmill you end up in bowers lava castle, it's this massive leap in logic that totally shatters immersion and makes me take Ds2 less seriously than any other game in fromsofts library

Dark souls 2 really just feels too game-y, areas are just big levels and it feels like mario or something, look at the dlc you have the fire level the snow level and whilst they are fun to play they make no sense, like where is the brume tower located? Mordor?

This game also just feels so janky, I know adaptability has been meme'd to death but combat and enemy animations are just so clunky, don't talk to me about the hit boxes either. This also extends to the difficulty, this game is both super easy and annoyingly difficulty, the game gives you unlimited healing items which breaks the improvements the estus system introduces in dark souls making it too easy but then the game throws ridiculous gank sqauds at you in front of boss doors which aren't hard but are just boring to grind each time you die to a boss, running past them is risky as you take damage when interacting with the environment/fog doors, a needless change which was made possibly to make the game harder.

I know it's a well known fact that the B-team at fromsoft worked on this whilst miyazaki was too busy cranking out a legit masterpiece with bloodborne but you can tell the team didn't understand the nuance that made both Demon's and dark souls so popular, it took the absolute core mechanics of combat but made it worse, it failed to understand dark souls' dark fantasy world and made every npc state the obvious in round about ways, it completely ruined bosses by having some of the worst bosses in the series, sure bed of chaos sucks so bad but at least it was a memorable design in a cool arena with satisfying lore. When you've fought man with sword 7 in dark souls 2 it starts getting stale.

Basically this game is like if you let an AI generator make a dark souls game.

Years after completing it and its DLCs on console, I decided to give it another shot because The Shadow of the Erdtree left me craving more Souls-like. I thought it was bad when I first played it too, but since it was the only one I hadn't played, I saw it through to the end back then.

Replaying it now, I find it even worse than I remember.

This is just a bad game, plain and simple. It's not just a bad Souls game, but a bad game overall, despite what some might say. It's abysmal. Its flaws are especially jarring after experiencing SotE. It's hard to believe these two games came from the same developer.

The level design is poor and, on top of that, it's visually unappealing. It's full of empty corridors and rooms that look like they belong in a PS1 title.

The bosses are somehow even worse than those in Demon's Souls; I wouldn't be able to differentiate them from regular mobs if it weren't for the health bars and cutscenes. The controls are unresponsive, and there's a certain jankiness to the player character's animations. Combined with the poorly implemented Adaptability mechanic, it feels like you're fighting against the gamepad, not the enemy. The lack of visual or auditory feedback makes it hard to tell if you've hit an enemy or been hit yourself.

Overall, it's not worth the time spent. If you've beaten all the other FromSoftware Souls-likes and still want more, go play Lies of P. It feels more like a FromSoftware game than this one, despite not being developed by them.

To Quote the Act Man:

"They want difficult, we'll give them difficult: Pump in as much artificial challenge as you can, tell them they're going to die, make them suffer in pain, oversaturate the game with terrible boss fights, double no TRIPLE the amount of enemies and that connected level design, thats gotta go"

The Good:

-Mejula is an interesting hub world where you can have most of the important NPC merchants in one area rather than going around the whole map for a specific NPS (for the most part)
-fairer bonfire placement for certain boss fights (with some exceptions)
-Powerstancing debuting for the first time, allowing to use 2 weapons be it the same type or different
-better scaling for spell/miracle uses
-Tons more weapons and armor with a much simpler approach to upgrading/enchanting them
-boss weapons/armor/spells is rewarded much better via talking to 2 different NPCs, trading the boss souls and a few small souls for the prize
-bonfire warping at the start of the game
-better indication of stats for durability and equipment load
+much more

The Bad
-too many enemies to fight in certain areas creating unfair scenarios (Old Iron King DLC suffers from this a lot)
-most runbacks are just outright terrible (again for the boss Sir Alonne)
-adaptability existing and having skill be tied to a number leading to some times where you dodge and you still get hit on screen.
-lackluster locations with terrible interconnectivity or lack there of
-bosses that could pass off for elite like enemies rather than there own boss, lack of variety of unique bosses.
-on top of enemies to fight, a lot of artificial difficulty such as traps that cause status inflictions that become more of an annoyance than a challenge to overcome (Black Gulch is a perfect example of this)
-death is very unforgiving as you will have your health cut in half if you die too many times (plus human effigys are rare early on but can become less annoying with time)

Overall, I wouldn't be recommending this to someone who wants to get into dark souls or even the souls series in general as a game more modern and with better mechanics is more worth someones time, that title being Elden Ring. It's not a bad game by any means necessary, I just find personally the flaws outweight the good. Hell, I would say play the Dark Souls 1 just on the story/environment alone.

My fourth play-through on different systems, and while I cannot comment yet on the Steam version, I can offer an awesome dinner recipe to consider on this rainy Friday night.

Ingredients

300g raw king prawns
2 tbsp soy sauce
40g cornflour
sunflower oil, for deep-frying
handful of coriander leaves, roughly chopped
cooked rice, to serve
For the sauce
1 red onion, roughly chopped
2 red peppers, deseeded and roughly chopped
2 garlic cloves, finely grated
thumb-sized piece of ginger, finely grated
1 tbsp golden caster sugar
100ml malt vinegar
2 tbsp soy sauce
2 tbsp tomato ketchup

STEP 1
Tip the prawns in a bowl and mix with the soy, then set aside for a few minutes. Stir in the cornflour.

STEP 2
Heat the oil in a wok over a high heat to a depth of 3-4cm. When it’s shimmering or a cooking thermometer reads 170C, fry the prawns in batches for about 2 mins until crisp and golden, turning them with a slotted spoon halfway through. Carefully lift onto a plate using the slotted spoon. Turn off the heat. Leave the pan for a few minutes to cool down.

STEP 3
Carefully pour all but 1 tbsp oil from the wok – keep the remainder for use in another recipe or discard.

STEP 4
Put the wok back on a high heat. When the oil is shimmering, carefully add the onion and peppers, and stir-fry for 1 min. Add the garlic and ginger, and continue to cook for 30 seconds, then scatter over the sugar and pour in the vinegar. Turn up the heat and boil for 2 mins. Stir in the soy and ketchup to make a thick, glossy sauce. When you’re ready to serve, tip the prawns back into the pan and stir to coat in the sauce. Scatter over the chopped coriander and serve with cooked rice on the side.

Posted: July 12, 2024
If you're here to play Dark Souls, just play the 1st or 3rd one. Devs made some quality of life changes like, being able to fast climb ladders unlike DS1, fast travel being a base mechanic in this game instead of unlocking it in DS1. As someone that has beaten DS1, DS3, Elden Ring, Lies of P, (if you count Ninja Gaiden a "Soulslike" in very loose terms then also the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd of that series) several times each, DS2 is the least favorite. It seems like the devs made an awesome game with DS1 and thought "let's remove everything good with DS1" and DS2 was born. Definitely the middle child game. Coming into the game as a "veteran" souls player and having to learn that the first thing you should be leveling up is a useless stat that only increases your iFrames and losing the first 8+ levels to just that stat and not into anything else so I can get normal iFrames. How is it I am on the side of a basic mob with a spear and the hitbox is so broke that get hit *through a dodge* to get behind him for a backstab? The spear point was literally half a screen away from my character. Or when I dodge away from an attack yet I get damaged before the enemy's animation even gets half way through.

I'm not really hating on the game itself. I would be way more into it if some of the mechanics changes they made from DS1 into DS2 were left out. I.E. the adaptability stat, and if the controls didn't feel so clunky. I will say being able to dodge in more than the basic 4 cardinal directions when locked on is better than DS1. Its a playable game, but if you've played other Souldborne/Soulslike games beforehand, do read more about the mechanics and things you should lvl up (adaptability) before starting a world and dumping lvls into any other stat. I don't foresee myself replaying this game many times over like I can with DS1, DS3. Heck, just a couple years ago (circa 2022) I was replaying Ninja Gaiden even though I was playing that game on the OG OG XBOX back in 2009 and every generation of XBOX since then. But because its Fromsoft and I'm a (S)ierra (L)ima (U)niform (T)ango for their games I'll probably beat it at least a couple of times

Personal opinion alert: The art style is also completely different from what anyone thinks when they think DS and all in all I didnt get very Dark Souls feeling from playing. It looked like a DS shaped world met Fable and Twilight princess color scheme, lighting, and character models. But thats not truly that important in the grand scheme of things.

I recommend playing DS3 first to get sucked in, DS1 to get a taste of something new and to appreciate DS3 more for its handling ability, and then DS2 if you really just want to complete the trilogy like I do.

The hate this game gets is mostly undeserved. It approves alot upon the first game while having some jank in of itself

Positives:
-More Weapons and Spells (POWER-STANCING)
-Of course amazing Soundtrack
-Areas look amazing and are unique to each other

-Some cool bosses in here (don't wanna spoil)

Negatives:
-Adaptability stat was a silly idea from the get go, it makes hitboxes so inconsistent and janky at times
-Hitboxes in general can be absolutely ridiculous
-There are some terrible runbacks in this game
-Durability and weight on rings is a little bit silly
-Recycled bosses here and there, occasionally some sort of meh bosses
-Not being able to level up at bonfires can be a little bit annoying

OVERALL
I did really really like this game. People like to ♥♥♥♥ on it alot but all in all it's on equal footing to DS1 for me personally. It added stuff to the formula which were welcome additions but also came with it's own jank. I'd honestly give Dark Souls 2 an 8/10. After playing through the DLCs in this game, I can say they are even worse.

I will not comment on the base game because I had fun with it (the base game is probably 6 or 7 out of 10). The DLCs, on the other hand, are disappointing.

The first DLC, "Sunken King," has a good aesthetic, but the bosses, enemies and level design are really bad. I will comment on the bosses specifically because for me they are the biggest part of the problem. All of the bosses have something in common: THEY ARE NOT FUN (actually, this applies to all DLC bosses except Fume Knight). Elana is probably the second most fun boss in all the DLCs, but she is still mediocre. The fight feels kind of fast and offers zero satisfaction after defeating her. The trio is actually an easy fight but is really slow and feels like it was designed to be that way. Fromsoft made the boss feel like a real PvP encounter just a chaotic mix of random builds and things happening. To kill them, you need to waste so much time just to get the most worthless rewards. Sinh the worst ai dragon, yeah the name says its all this ai is ♥♥♥♥.

Old Iron King is actually really fun, not going to lie. Probably the only bad thing is the poor mob placements and Sir Alonne's runback filled with not unique enemies(boss fight feels kinda cringe but fine ig).

The "Ivory King" DLC is probably the worst DLC FromSoft has ever made. Let's start with the area and mobs, which are the ugliest mobs and map design. The runbacks for bosses (except Aava) are really long, boring, and mindless. The Ivory King runback sounds cool on paper, but if you die, you are forced to kill the black knights over and over again (I died only three times on this boss fight, but it felt like 30 because of this). Posted: July 8, 2024
Dark souls 2 the ugliest duckling of any trilogy, From my 50 hours playing I feel this title is unfairly given.

Just briefly covering mechanics that I despised, the decreasing health until you restore humanity using one of your prized human effigy’s feels criminal. Having to upgrade with one person rather than the bonfires is a terrible change. On the good side having access to quick travel at the beginning of the game was a well needed change.

This game out of all FromSoft games has some of the most horrendous, tedious and frivolous run backs. Having to deal with the same unpleasant area just to get cheesed by a boss will forever haunt me. Playing this following DS remastered it just makes that games run backs look simple in comparison.

The boss design and underlying story is top notch and not lacking at all compared to other games they’ve produced. A remastering of this game and tidying up some of the janky mechanics would do this game justice.

Weapons available in this game are significantly better, creative and more fun to use in many instances. The DLC bosses are a whole different breed, each of them being extremely hard and the run backs even worse, Sir Alonne & Lud and Zellen were both war crimes.

In summary I wouldn’t suggest this game if tedious run backs will grate you, but beneath all its issues I had a lot of fun. Being sandwiched by DS1 and DS3 this game catches hate I don’t feel justified.

7.5/10

Posted: July 5, 2024
Dark Souls is an dark adventure RPG. The objective is dual choiced; Continue and succeed the Throne of Want or Ignore it. You are the Bearer of the Curse. Your back story is disjointed and untold because of political issues at FROM Software.

Gameplay-wise, best with controller.

Refer to comments for hypothesis.

The Lore. It's good but they didn't perfect it, most likely due to replacing directors. This is sad because the lore seemed like the first team put effort into the lore, but it got cut by the 2nd director.

Conclusion, try Dark Souls 2 with a guide. There is very low value in self-exploration, mostly frustration. I played it almost 10 years after its release and there are still players. Important tip: Learn how to use the bow and arrow (Power stance + L1)

For me, the frustration was too much. So, I didn't want NG+. I prefer to just move on.

PROS
1. Lore
Really nice to read the story and worldbuilding. Inconsistencies do exist but overall it can be read if you selectively ignore the contradictions.

2. Rotten
Nice boss design. The human eyeballs were a nice touch. The story of Gryth to Rotten was interesting.

3. Heide's Tower of Flame
It's a trick. Heide is actually a place to help you level up fast. The enemies look big and scary but they are actually the easiest to farm and everything there can be cheesed with arrows e.g. DragonRider, Knights and Hellkite. Or you can time your attacks.

4, Multiple Ways To Win - Cheesing
Hard to figure out cheeses on your own. Online, people have figured out cheeses to kill bosses. e.g. Alluring Skulls, bow and arrow, enemies can't jump. Meta knowledge makes the game enjoyable e.g. Pursuer ballista, Dragonrider arrows, Hellkite arrows, etc.

Instead of making you feel like you want to wrangle a certain someone.

5. Multiple Ways To Win - Optional Boss Prep
How do you optionally make the boss easier without cheesing? Boss prep e.g. Lost Sinner lights, Ashen Idols, Eleum Noyce Knights. I like this quite a bit as it fits into lore and lowers the difficulty for normal players. Thematically, boss prep makes sense. The Witcher 2 has a similar good one with preparing Kraken Traps for the Kraken.

Being optional allows the hardcore players to do it on hard mode.

6. Dragon Aerie
Wow... This was fun to be in. Seeing all the draongs fly around, the swaying bridges and the one that collapses. The Dragon Eggs.

7. Locations
About ~30 locations. It's a nice numbers

1. Frustrating
The AI is specifically designed to make you fail and it is frustrating. e.g. If the Last Giant is on low health, its foot can magically twist out of place to avoid your attack.

2. Incomplete, Incoherency and Inconsistency in Lore
Why isn't Shulva covered in poison if Sinh released all the poison? Why is all the poison in Harvest Valley/Earthen Peak?

3. Controls
Made overly difficult, on purpose. Power stancing is not taught to you. Arrows have to be loaded in your equipment. Aiming your bow is not taught to you. This did not give me joy in any form. Pure frustration and knowing someone did this on purpose makes me wonder if this was necessary.

1. Bugs and Glitches
Glitches either benefit or harm the player. e.g. I ran into a bug where Cale never respawns. I never opened the house in Majula because bonfire ascetics did not revive him, nor was there a grave site.

2. Locations - The Gutter
I played The Gutter, with the original low lights and no map knowledge, just to understand the original game design. Boy, was it demanding. If you go early, you don't have Flame Butterflies. Frustrating to slowly light the sconces 1 by 1. The enemies weren't hard but the poison and whoops-a-daisy really raised my blood pressure.

After I completed it the hard way, I changed the gamma to max and found the various jump shortcuts. In this case, I was neither frustrated nor happy.

3. Sunken City Connected to The Gutter - "Old Shulva City/Dsuke City"
A pity it wasn't added into the game. In Dark Souls Dissected, a few exiled Gyrm were supposed to be there, including Gavlan. It was supposed to explain the Sunken City, Sunken King/Rotten, exiled Gyrms, what Sinh the dragon destroyed, the Emerald Herald, poison/miasma from Sinh, alcohol distillation and why the Gutter inhabitants are actually climbing upwards.

4. McDuff Secret Opening With Barrel
This could not be done with self-exploration. The 1st time I tried, the barrel didn't explode. McDuff requires a guide to tell you that the barrel will explode to reveal the entrance.

1. Mastodon Warriors / Primal Knights Lore
I think Primal Knights were wasted as mutations by the Duke (Lore). If Primal Knights were hired mercenaries from a land faraway, you would have a lot of lore to expand i.e. An Elephant empire. That would have been cool.

Are the Primal Knights allied with Drangleic or Gyrm/Rat King? Inconsistent.

If the Primal Knights were mercenaries, it would have removed any inconsistency in the Door of Pharros. The Gyrm/Rat King could have hired the Mastodon Warriors/Primal Knights as protectors. Drangleic could also hire neutral Mastodon mercenaries as guards.

No inconsistency + new lore + new Mastodon units?

2. Ship on the Sea DLC
Could have made a ship that sailed out from No Man's Wharf to a ghost boat zone -> big ghost ship -> island -> underwater zone (drowning). The Flexile Sentry was not that interesting.

3. Strange NPC's and Ring of Whispers
The Bug Queen guarded by shrooms, the Pagan Tree, the Lion Men, the Spiders, Primal Knights, big pig in Majula, etc. All of these things could have worked with the Ring of Whispers. In the current version, Ring of Whispers was only for the ManScorpion.

Thematically, it would have been interesting to hear the thoughts of non-speaking NPC's to expand the lore. Something akin to the Heart in Dishonored.

4. Pharros The Gyrm? Sadly Human.
It would have been nice to meet Pharros the Vagabond as a Gyrm. Sadly, the lore states that Pharros is a human.

5. NPC
They look like they would have nice backstory and support items e.g. Steel-willed Lorrie, Drifter Swordsman Aidel. Maybe they could sell you skills? It feels limited if the most of the talking NPC's only sell you things.

6. Mastodon Warriors
Indian/African? Good story for Great- Weapons e.g. Great Bow, Great Sword, Great Shield. All the stupidly large weapons that humans shouldn't normally carry.

DS2 is my favorite title in the series.

A Screenshot of DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
By: noseblunt


Especially now after having played Elden Ring you recognize the importance this game had.
In my opinion, DS2 was an important shot by the development team to try out what works well and what doesn't: there are many parallels to be drawn to elden ring. Power stancing, non-linear map exploration aswell as some story and game design aspects (outdoor and indoor dungeon designs; The Crumbling "Farum" Azula being a big throwback to the nameless king; Imp statues and Swordstone keys and the Petrified Statues and Branches of Yore, etc.)

Posted: July 3, 2024
Dark Souls 2, the highly anticipated sequel to its renowned predecessor, arrived amidst both excitement and scrutiny. As a devoted fan of challenging RPGs, I eagerly delved into its world of Drangleic, eager to experience its unique take on the Souls formula. While it faced the daunting task of following in the footsteps of a beloved classic, Dark Souls 2 sought to carve its own path with new mechanics, expanded lore, and a hauntingly atmospheric world.

Visuals and Atmosphere:
Dark Souls 2 presents a diverse array of environments, from crumbling castles and sunken cities to mist-covered forests and lava-filled caverns. While the visual fidelity may not reach the heights of contemporary games, the art direction shines through with its evocative landscapes and attention to detail. Atmospheric effects such as lighting and weather contribute to the game's ambiance, creating a sense of immersion amidst its dark and foreboding setting.

Combat System:
The combat system in Dark Souls 2 builds upon its predecessor's foundation while introducing refinements and adjustments. It retains the deliberate pacing and strategic depth that define the series, emphasizing stamina management, precise timing, and skillful dodging. Weapon variety remains robust, allowing players to experiment with different playstyles, from nimble swordsmen to powerful spellcasters. While some mechanics received mixed reception among fans, such as adaptability affecting invincibility frames, the overall combat experience remains challenging and rewarding.

Character Development:
Character development in Dark Souls 2 offers a wealth of customization options, allowing players to tailor their builds to suit their preferred playstyle. The introduction of soul memory for matchmaking and the ability to reallocate stats with a specific item added new dimensions to character progression. The game's interconnected world encourages exploration and discovery of new equipment and abilities, fostering a sense of growth and evolution as players traverse the treacherous lands of Drangleic.

Story and Lore:
Dark Souls 2's narrative unfolds through fragments of lore scattered throughout its world, revealing a tapestry of kingdoms risen and fallen. The overarching story explores themes of fate, memory, and the consequences of ambition, set against a backdrop of ancient curses and forgotten monarchs. While the narrative may lack the tightly woven cohesion of its predecessor, it offers ample opportunity for speculation and interpretation, inviting players to piece together its mysteries through dialogue, item descriptions, and environmental cues.

Replayability:
Dark Souls 2 excels in replayability, offering multiple paths, endings, and new game plus variations that encourage exploration and experimentation. The inclusion of downloadable content (DLC) expands the game's scope with additional areas, bosses, and challenges, enriching the overall experience for dedicated players. Cooperative and competitive multiplayer elements, including covenant mechanics and PvP arenas, further enhance replay value, fostering community interaction and memorable encounters.

Conclusion:
In conclusion, Dark Souls 2 is a worthy successor to its acclaimed predecessor, offering a distinct yet familiar experience within the Souls series. While it may not surpass the lofty expectations set by Dark Souls, it stands as a compelling journey through a dark and intricate world filled with formidable foes and haunting beauty. As a player immersed in its challenges and discoveries, I appreciate its ambition and the depth of its lore, despite its occasional departures from established mechanics.

Dark Souls 2 remains a testament to the enduring appeal of challenging gameplay and immersive storytelling in the RPG genre. Its expansive world and nuanced mechanics invite players to embrace its trials and tribulations, forging their own path through the enigmatic lands of Drangleic. For those seeking a gripping adventure that rewards perseverance and exploration, Dark Souls 2 offers an experience that is both daunting and deeply satisfying.

Overall Rating: 9/10

Игра хороший пример того, как куча разработчиков с "хорошими" идеями собрались вместе, чтобы обосраться.
Худшая из всех частей.
1) Геймдизайн уровней полный отстой по сравнению с предыдущей частью. Если раньше вы бегали по локациям в поисках коротких путей и вам было интересно сражаться с каждым противником, а локации менялись и привносили, что-то новое, то тут вы бегаете по безжизненым коридорам в которые даже вглядываться не охота.
2) Урон мобов, некоторые противники специально сделаны так, чтобы не научить вас чему-то новому, а просто заебать и не дать вам нормально пройти дальше по локации. Идёшь 15 минут по локе убивая сотни врагов, появляется моб который гига надутый и тратишь ещё 15 минут только на этого моба.
3) Некоторые противники прсто куклы, которые плевали на какой либо вес и инерцию. Никогда не забуду огненных ящериц которые крутятся вокруг со скоростью света.
4) Боссы, есть пара действительно интересных, но большинство с хреновыми патернами атак и областью удара, что чаще всего ты не понимаешь как эта херня могла тебя убить.
5) Игра убивает тебя не потому, что ты допускаешь ошибку, а потому, что разработчик плевал на баланс и доведение до ума игровых механик.

По итогу игра хороший пример, как можно из DS1 деградировать до DS2 если поставить некомпетентных людей на разработку.
Хорошо, что в DS3 и Elden Ring всё стало лучше и они отказались от большинства механик DS2.

Posted: July 2, 2024
By itself, Dark Souls II is an incredibly frustrating, yet still rewarding and enjoyable game. When compared to the other Dark Souls games, it is probably worse, yet still manages to be my favourite to play with friends.

Dark Souls II is an oddity in the souls franchise. It doesn't really follow the story of the others, though it is reminiscent of them. The world design is similar, but somehow seems to follow a slightly different philosophy. The bosses, one of the main aspects of a souls game, are more numerous, but also less intricate.

In terms of gameplay, the game is full of puzzles and mazes, but none of them are really explained to the player. This, combined with the many systems that the player has to either look up or somehow intuit, makes the game extremely difficult to start. Enemy placement seems unfair, in a way unlike any souls game that followed. And the obstacles areas bad or worse than anything found in Elden Ring.

Despite all this, and the frustration I describe, Dark Souls II contains some of my fondest memories of the franchise, and I cannot recommend it enough.

Fun but flawed dark souls game. Its not as bad as people say. It does not have totally busted levels like Dark Souls 1, just annoying ones, with horrible run backs. I think this game has a very bad reputation for the initial release and even if it was a decent game it was a downgrade from Dark Souls 1 which was flawed as well.

:

- Good graphics in the remaster. The main hub music is good and the main hub has its own style, not better than the Hub in DS1 or DS3 but good, its like a dramatic Irish cliff side, has it's charm.
- Decent/good zones, better than most people say especially after the remaster.
- Good monsters/soldiers/knights design. They are cool to fight and kill.
- Bosses are maybe simpler than Dark Souls 1 bosses but are still fun and raw to kill.
- Actually good bosses in the expansions.

:

- The biggest negative are the satanic run backs in some levels like the Iron Keep and the DLCs, you will have to reclear the whole level to try a hard boss once.
- Shielding is kinda slow so makes the shield + sword gameplay harder to play than in DS1 or DS3.

I approached Dark Souls II with skepticism, expecting to dislike it based on its reputation. However, I was pleasantly surprised by how much I enjoyed the game. The intricate world design and atmospheric environments are immersive and rewarding to explore, offering a satisfying sense of discovery. The combat system, while familiar, introduces unique mechanics like the adaptability stat, adding strategic depth. Despite some minor flaws, such as inconsistent hit detection and uneven level design, the overall experience is deeply satisfying and engaging.

Initially, I considered skipping Dark Souls II and going straight to the third installment due to negative reviews, but I am glad I didn't. This game is a hidden gem in the series, providing a rich and challenging experience. Dark Souls II is undeserving of the hate it often receives, as it offers hours of rewarding gameplay and exploration. If you're on the fence about playing this game, take the plunge – you might find yourself as pleasantly surprised as I was.

Posted: June 26, 2024
I bought this game after having played it for years on Xbox One. I have to say any positive thing I could say about this game was all nostalgia or Stockholm syndrome. If you want a good Dark Souls game you're better off overpaying for DS: Remastered.
If you're looking for an RPG with interesting mechanics, however, you have come to the right place. Dark Souls 2 takes everything you like about Dark Souls and adds a twist for better or for worse. If you like the combat of Dark Souls you may like this entry's slower take on it. If you like fashion souls you'll love the fact that most armor ends up being completely irrelevant outside of some pieces providing passives or stat boosts. If you like the world design of the original Dark Souls but hated running back and forth to places until mid-game, DS2 has you covered. You get the ability to teleport right from the start as long as you have one other bonfire discovered. Sure this means that the world design creativity suffers heavily, but no more annoying run backs to Andre to upgrade (even though the design of the map always made that quite easy, but I digress). If you enjoyed picking and choosing which stats you want to level for that cool sword you picked up earlier, there are a ton of options available, even without infusions, which adds for quite a bit of variety in builds.However, you also need to burn a couple levels into adaptability so that rolling can feel half as good as it does in any other souls game. The only thing DS2 has that beats out the other entries, hands down, is power-stancing, which we wouldn't see again until Elden Ring.
If you enjoy a challenge that is fun and fair, I would not recommend DS2: SotFS, there's a laundry list of flaws that keep this game from being as good as the original release of DS2. If you haven't played Dark Souls or Dark Souls 3 yet I strongly recommend skipping this entry until it goes on sale because paying $40 for a game that feels this awful to play is a waste of time and money.
Also, Miyazaki didn't even direct this game why are there like four poison areas?

Posted: June 20, 2024
Dark Souls II is a magnificent yet flawed work of art that only gained its unjustified infamy due to not being able to top its predecessor (and possibly successor) in terms of consistent quality, especially from a gameplay perspective. it's not necessarily a harder game than Dark Souls I, but it certainly feels a lot more unforgiving, ruthless, and exasperating just for the sake of provoking the player at times, especially in the early game and the DLCs. I could list a bunch of questionable changes that can turn off a lot of players, but I find it hypocritical for most people to whine about this game when the first game also had its fair share of moments that could give any sane person violent tendencies. in my opinion, criticizing areas and bosses based on their difficulty and how fun they are in these games is pointless because they have many variables such as play style and character build and sometimes even luck - it's all subjective. despite this, what makes "ds2 sucks just skip it" an invalid opinion in most cases is the fact that one important thing Dark Souls I teaches you is to be patient, and as long as you play Dark Souls II patiently there is nothing intolerable about this game, and you'll see that most of the "issues" people list about this game are non-issues that merely leave you frustrated until you manage to finish the area and then you're left with that sweet feeling of satisfaction again. not to mention there is an odd stigma around this game where people just decide that this game is bad even without playing it themselves. for me, the only tedious part of this game was it had a lot of repetition, especially with the bosses, but for the most part, I've had a lot of fun playing it. that's why I believe this game is definitely a must play for anyone who is getting into Dark Souls.
what makes Dark Souls II special for me is that it does not try to replicate what Dark Souls I did. instead, it focuses on the fundamentals of Dark Souls I on a much smaller scale, allowing the narrative to explore some untouched parts of the lore in more detail. in Dark Souls I, you're a significant part of a grand creation tale, tasked with deciding the fate of this world, and meeting all the legends behind its origin during your adventure. in Dark Souls II, however, you're in one of the many kingdoms of an already broken and hopeless world. all over Drangleic, you see the aftermath of the grand events of Dark Souls I and prior. your mission is still breaking the curse, but this time, you're rarely encouraged by anyone to play your part and do the "right" thing. instead, you're persistently encouraged to question. question the cycle, question the conceited ideas planted in your head by others, question yourself, and question the rest of the world. you're asked again and again, what do YOU want to do? after all is done, do you have the courage to take a step towards the unknown - beyond the light and the Dark?
despite being widely different from it, this game truly carries the soul of Dark Souls I and it will give you just as much to contemplate once you're done. for this reason I highly recommend Dark Souls II.

Posted: June 19, 2024
I loved the original Dark Souls II!
I can't, in good conscience, recommend a game I wish I'd refunded, though
This version of the game has a lot of problematic changes from the original

Pros:
- Improved graphics
- Online is still functional

Cons:
- Moves many encounters around so that every area is basically a nonstop chain of gank squad enemies. Can't recall a single place where you don't fight 3+ enemies at once in an area for this version, where most of the original game didn't go above 2
- Moves many items around to hide key build items later in the game or outright remove the ability of the player to farm items they want. Also, a particular DLC item is just made worse for... no discernible reason. I mean, the DLCs for both versions released at the same time, so why is the Iron Crown worse in this version..?
- Waaaaay more statues blocking progression areas, and it's really annoying to get past them. The routes you can take through this game are significantly more limited than the original, and require a lot of backtracking or picking up specific items in out of the way areas just to go the way you want to go
- The Scholar Updates for the game actually broke both games' physical defense values. Hitting the cap for defense only reduces damage by a measly 10% at the very most, so there's literally no reason to wear armor except for elemental defense. One could argue for poise as well, but you'll take so much damage that voluntarily tanking any hit is madness

I can't recommend this version of the game for these reasons; I've seen a lot of people come away sour because of the gank design alone, and it feels disingenuous to recommend the version of the game that made that happen. There were absolutely ganks in the original, but nowhere near this many; the design choices of this game are honestly wild

Posted: June 19, 2024
This is a weird gem of a game. It doesn't take long for you to understand all the hate it receives, I would even argue it deserves all the hate it gets. But at the same time, I will also argue that it's easy to fall onto the hate train and just accept the game partially for what it is - an absolute mess.

The level design is just plainly bad, most of the times without a coherent logic to it when it comes to lore, and you'll find yourself frustrated with the enemy placement, which brings forward the obvious conclusion that the team behind development didn't truly grasp that the true enjoyment in Dark Souls difficulty is not on the hordes of enemies waiting to kill you while you hopelessly try to kill most of them while staying alive, but rather this idea that even though something might be difficult, if you're patient enough you will eventually understand the intricate way you need to act to solve the challenge ahead. Indeed, it's not a game where you should blindly and stupidly run around with a big sword slashing your way through boss fights, but it forces you to either think carefully about what you're doing, or go hollow.

Dark Souls ll takes a different approach, and if that's not your cup of tea, I totally get it, it's not mine either. Time and time again, I found myself losing patience and even interest in exploring the world this game has to offer because it purely is too much to have hordes and hordes of enemies constantly around you, and a lot of them with ranged attacks that make it seem impossible to play the game even if you're the most patient of people. But if you don't go hollow, if by chance you find a way to keep going, to suffer through, to dedicate yourself to the game and search for what it has to offer, you'll be extremely rewarded with I would say is one of the most beautiful, intricate, complex stories in the entire trilogy.

DS2 is a tribute to dedication, to preserverance, and it does indeed pay back with one of the most interesting stories which are not told in an obvious way at all - and I do realize this is saying a lot given how the trilogy is told - while still feeling fresh and interesting. I fell in love with all the very thin and almost transparent ties it has to its predecessor and how these become evident the more you progress through the game. It's a beautiful, unforgiving, but also unforgettable and necessary bridge between DS1 and what would be the perfect ending to this trilogy, DS3.

I cannot recommend this game enough to everyone who actually, truly cares about the story of Dark Souls, and to those who're patient enough to dig deeper to get a truly rewarding experience.

DS2 defenders will of course tell me to git gud, and tell you what, I dislike the game not because how difficult but how TEDIOUS it is. It is NOT difficult, not in the sense it demands mastery of skills. It's difficulties are what a toddler would offer when they go out all on making the hardest ever super mario maker level. Most bosses are laughable, and there's barely anything memorable about the enemies aside from their density and quantity. Level design is mediocre at best, aesthetics included. The only thing you need to get through the game is repetition. Of course all other souls games need a ton of repetition and patience, but for this one I don't feel like I am upgrading my skills as I make progress, I don't get amazed by recognizing the pattern and figuring out the intention of the designer, and I don't even feel excited after clearing out an area. I just sigh and the only excitement comes from another chapter is finally closed. The same problems about the game have been analyzed trillions of times. Of course it does have some shinning moments, but there's no way I can remotely consider DS2 even a 'good' game. Don't listen to the gameplay hours on Steam, I've played this for around 100, and from everything I've seen, it's a fantastic game! It gets a lot of crap for some things, like making you level up a stat to increase your roll speed or having a lot of similar-looking bosses. Though these things are present, there are so many positives to outweigh these things. For instance, the roll frames can be increased because of the ludicrous amount of Souls you get for everything. It would be crazy to say that the Ruin Sentinels are anything like the Old Ivory King, and this goes for every boss. This is one of the best Souls-Likes out there, as to be expected of FromSoft. 10/10

Even after playing Dark Souls 1, 3, Sekiro and Elden Ring, this is the only Fromsoftware game that I find myself coming back to, time and time again. I must have beaten it at least 10 times by now. I may 'only' have 100 hours on it on Steam, but I also played it a lot on console. There's something deeply special about this game, its lore, atmosphere, setting and general vibe. As troubled as the development was, you can tell that they set out to make something truly special from the start. The build variety is immense, all regions of the map are very distinct from one another (something that bothered me in Dark Souls 3, where every single area, save for a couple of them, had the same colour palette).


It has some very unforgettable moments, such as seeing Vendrick for the first time, after hearing countless times about what he is like, this nearly mythologycal ruler turned hollow in the depths of a crypt. On the subject of hollowing, this game's entire story is centered around it, on a smaller, more personal scope, and that is perhaps why this game really clicked for me. It felt more like a journey than all the other games in the franchise. This journey you embarked on, without really knowing why.

Posted: June 13, 2024
I've got very mixed feelings on this game.

On one hand, I can definitely see the potential of this game. It has a lot of unique stuff going for it when compared to its predecessor, and I can appreciate trying to go in a new direction with the sequel. Sadly, Dark Souls 2 is an infamous game for a reason. Sluggish controls, extremely difficult runbacks to laughably easy bosses, the non-existent story that barely connects with the original (if at all) and the nonsensical world design that's held together by duct tape (or rather elevators that go up into the sky), weird animations, insanely aggressive enemy AI, et cetera. Every bit you've heard about the game is unfortunately true.

My biggest disappointment with the game is the fact that if you make it so the enemies don't constantly gang up on you and space them out a bit and have less of them, the game becomes a laughably easy snorefest. It's an extremely easy game made artificially difficult by the developers who saw the enemy placement of Lost Izalith in Dark Souls 1 and thought they could one-up themselves by having that kind of enemy placement throughout the entire game.

It's at a point where getting to the boss arena is legitimately 20 times harder than the bosses themselves. Not to mention most bosses that don't get reused as basic enemies later in the game only have one or two moves, are extremely slow and just unfun to fight. Fumey, Sir Alonne and BIK are legitimately great fights that are up there with Artorias and Manus from DS1, but all of the other bosses are pushovers, save for a few like Smelter Demon.

The fact the final boss is a stationary target that barely attacks you and just throws 5 sprites in the arena that curse you non-stop is honestly embarassing, which is how I would describe the majority of this game. From the story to the gameplay to the atmosphere, from the beginning to the very end, I was waiting for this game to finally click with me, but sadly that never happened. And I really did give it a fair chance, even going as far as getting all the achievements.

Many people say how the game's main problems are how the number of invincibility frames you get by rolling is tied to agility which is increased by leveling the adaptability stat (and attunement for some reason), or how the enemies despawn permanently after you kill them 12 times, and you genuinely can't tell if that mechanic is there in order to make it so you can't farm souls or because developers wanted to make the game easier by having a way to get rid of the horrendous enemy spam.

But personally, I would say the biggest problem with this game is how it has so many cool ideas, but they're just that: ideas, ones that aren't fully realized and feel like they're all forcefully shoved into the game. Quantity over quality. And while the game had a troubled development, that doesn't change the final product that we got.

While it has moments that truly do show that there is a good game here somewhere, I cannot honestly say that Dark Souls 2: Scholar Of The First Sin is a good game because of all the insanely crappy stuff that's present in this game, and for that I cannot recommend it to anyone other than those who genuinely want to either experience or 100% every Soulsborne game.

Excluding a few rare moments, it's a fundementally poorly crafted and awfully designed experience that, contrary to the first game, which made me want to overcome its challenges and break the curse of the undead, instead left me disappointed and, ironically, very much hollowed by the end.

One of those on the fence reviews where I wish there was a neutral option or something.

More often than not I have fun playing this game, but I wouldn't start with this one if you're new to the souls series, and I straight up wouldn't recommend this at all to someone who doesn't have at least a passing familiarity with souls-likes.

There are so many "gotcha" moments that feel like they exist to frustrate rather than challenge the player: inactive enemies that hang out outside boss arenas often become active after you beat the boss to sneak up from behind and attack you, no i-frames when passing thru fog gates, ADAPTABILITY, crystal lizards designed to be easily missed by your weapon swings, illusory walls requiring you to interact rather than hit them, ADAPTABILITY, the... curiously designed hit boxes that accompany certain enemy attacks and weapons (looking at you, guts greatsword), not regaining your HP when you homeward bone, ADAPTABILITY...

Also many of the areas have horribly drab color schemes and frankly give me a headache like huntsman's copse, earthen peak, and yes, even forest of fallen giants. These areas are also designed in such a way that their transitions from one to another feel much less seamless and elegant than those found in this game's predecessor; they make you feel like you're slogging through video-gamey areas with heavily telegraphed paths forward rather than living, breathing zones that feel as if they're part of a greater, organic whole world.

All that said if you're a fan of dark souls 1's weighty and methodical combat you'll likely feel right at home here, where combat demands that you pay attention to spacing and stamina management and weapon ranges more than ever. Also, powerstancing two craftsman's hammers is the ♥♥♥♥.

tl;dr Solid 7/10 as someone who loves the souls games and souls-like genre, avoid as your first souls game or if you're hoping for something that's like elden ring.

Dark Souls II is about learning to dance. It's a world you stumble into, feel blindingly at the controls in desperate hope of staying alive; you trip, fall, and roll on your feet as the blows swing around you; but eventually you begin to perceive a rhythm in the madness. There are hidden beats here, throbbing just below the surface, and as your awareness expands, you begin to tap into the beat. You stop fighting the monsters, rushing ahead mindlessly; you *dance* with them. Roll. Parry. Strike. Beat. Roll-roll, parry, strike-strike. Beat. You become fluid motion, a whirlwind not fighting the elements but at one with them. Once you start understanding the dance, of course the beat shifts, but you're going to dance with it, given enough time and practice. Eventually you'll be able to add your own artistic flair and flourish to the dance, and perhaps soon, one day, you'll be Hollow no more...

THIS IS NOT DARK SOULS 1

DS2 deviates in a lot of ways from DS1. If you come here expecting to play the same thing as DS1 but with a new story, it's not gonna happen. DS2 is special in its own way. The weapon combat is fantastic (shout out power-stancing), the armor outfits look great, and the game is home to a beautiful hub area in the form of Majula. Plus, the voice acting is top-tier, so it's easy to get invested into the story.

Now, you should know two things to make this game easier. First, LEVEL ADP. A big critique of this game is you have to level your invincibility frames via ADP. I don't like it, but it's also not that big of a deal. I found that once I had ADP to level 12 or so it played fine, with level 20 being the max level you need to dodge like a leaf in the wind. Second: explore as much of an area as humanly possible, including hunting for invisible walls (press the interact key, don't hit them, the community will cover you on where they are for the most part). The game was miles easier for me once I started maxing out what I could do in each area since I got loaded with souls and items. Oh, also, get your hands on the ring of binding ASAP :)

DS2 is a good game. In fact, the DLC for DS2 that's included in Scholar is super fantastic. Do I think that DS1 is better? Yes. But I think too many people compare the two and focus on the deviations from DS1 instead of the positives of DS2. This game is 100% worth your money, I had a great time playing.
