DS2... I've played for 60 hours, so I think I can offer my opinion. First off, don't play this if it's your first souls game. Start with 1 AND 3 first before trying 2. Two is a flawed game in many ways, but it does have the most content and bosses. Half the bosses are recycled though, and the combat is like 3, but nerfed. For example, adaptability is a separate skill that gives you I-frames from dodging. Without levels in that skill, enemies will hit you when you dodge/roll. Very annoying.

The locations are very cool, but are congested with enemies. Using a bow to lure single enemies is the norm. Some enemies are fun and challenging, others are straight up broken. Companions for bosses are definitely helpful, since you DO NOT want to have to run through the area to get the boss again. The weapons are the best though, with lots of variety.

PVP is still awful, since the invader will be many levels above you and likely one shot you while you deal with the numerous enemies in the area. Healing is frustrating and I recommend buying as many healing gems as you can, since you start the game with 1 estus lol

First time players, I recommend agility/strength builds, since magic is a lot tougher in this game due to the spam of enemies and ADAPT stat, and using light armor is a death sentence.

Probably my least favorite game of the series. Get it on sale (though it never does go on sale, thanks FROM!)

Posted: June 7, 2024
This game makes me feel like a conservative for the first time in my life. While i lovelovelove experimentation, this game proves that no amount of experimentation can make consistent principles unimportant. Rather than building on the ideas of the first game, DS2 appeals to many of the revolutionary aspects of the franchise, such as its difficulty, and unique world-building. The resulting mess of a game closer resembles a gimmicky parody of the first game than a faithful sequel, or standalone successor.

This game has some of the coolest items, skills, stats, enemies, and general gameplay mechanics of the franchise, yet struggles to connect them meaningfully. This compounds with the gimmicky enemies and traps, (which pale in comparison to the enticing dilemnas of DS1,) making for a hefty game with very little room for true diversity of play-style.

Some more general complaints:
tonnes of bugs,
really ♥♥♥♥♥♥ map design (which is unusual for FromSoft),
the worst Mouse and Keyboard support (shockingly) of any FromSoft game,
and honestly a lot more....


....If you've played the other games, give it a shake. You can clearly see what makses DS1 so great when playing DS2, and it really makes DS3 feel like a perfect sequel.

(EDIT: i take it all back. ng+ makes it all worth it. This game gave me a humiliation + gank + pain kink)

Posted: June 6, 2024
Having played a full 100% walkthrough now after I did a run through the original DS2.

Dark Souls 2 offers the absolute best and worst of fromsoft. The game is absolutely huge and the exploring aspect is probably the best in the soulsborne serie. The game has some amazing designed areas like Dragon Shrine, Harvest Valley, Brume Tower and Drangleic Castle while also having some pure ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ like Shrine of Amana (Its pretty, but fcking irritating) and Frigid Outskirts (probably worst area in gaming ever, which also is an entire bossrun).

A huge problem with the game are the bossruns. My least favourite ones are Lud and Zallen, Blue smelter demon, gank squad and executioners chariot. Darklurker isnt too bad but the fact that you have to use an effigy everytime to go back is just awful. With bossruns like that they are just begging to be cheesed, luckily for most of the bosses you can summon and are pretty easy.

The bosses in the game are pretty fun though. Darklurker, Fumeknight, Sir Alonne are great. My favorite one is Burnt Ivory King, although gimmicky, the fight and arena are just so well made and doing that boss with friends is so much fun. The game does have a ton of bosses and not every boss is great, most are super easy and are kinda pointless tbf.

SOTFS didnt really change much, some items and enemies here and there are replaced or moved. But nothing too crazy.

Soulmemory, adaptability, agility and less health everytime when hollowed are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ stupid still.

All in all, the game is amazing, but has some problems. It is still one of my favorite games ever. No game can beat the feeling of returning to Majula to level up after exploring and fighting through an area for an hour.

9/10

Some people dislike DS2 because they compare it to DS1 and DS3. It's different in a lot of ways, but that's part of what I love about it. It's a truly unique and wonderful game but probably best viewed as it's own thing. The frustrating parts are probably the most frustrating in the series, but the rewarding parts are also some of the best. If you're the kind of person who focuses on the best parts of a game, this might end up being one of your favorite games of all time. If you can't handle frustrating parts of games then you might hate it.

Posted: June 3, 2024
Disclaimer: Know that the hackers may be bit more serious than just in-game trolls, and I have no clue how safe the anticheat softwares actually are. Thus, after a certain point, I decided it safer to play offline, than risk malware. Which is a pity, since until that point, the multiplayer was a fun addition in this game, even with the occasional immortal trolls.

That having been said, I think this is one of the better Souls games, especially suitable for old geezers with lacking in reaction speed department. I like the difficult gank levels, as they play nice with patience: each room, each corner, you play until the monsters stop spawning at all: you get loads of souls, and lots of gametime. The lure is not the amazing loud bossfight with light effects and symphonic orchestra, but every single room and corner turning into many challenging battles for a long playtime. This also tricks you to pay attention to each nook and cranny, for many times, which is good for stumbling on to the really many secrets and details. This game is not to be charged into like a cavalry, but carefully and patiently hacked and slashed into with thought. For a zombie and monster mashing game the pace is surprisingly patient and alert.

Basically you are slow moving ironclad zombie who has to think carefully about each coming hack, slash, poke, dodge and sip from healing jar, for almost every minor violent encounter and gank. If you are not playing as armored ironclad, and instead wear some magic infused gown or such fancywear, you still move like ironclad zombie, so might as well think of yourself as one and plan accordingly.

Tips to get started:
Adaptability: get that ADP to about 20, and everything becomes doable, no matter are you wizard, priest, brute or sneak, If your adaptability is much lower than that, you will die from everything, all the time, and not have much fun at all.
Killemall: Kill the trashmonsters and leets of a location, reload the area at the bonfire to respawn them, and keep killing them again and again by reloading the area, until they they stop respawning and stay dead for good. This is the secret to the uninterrupted boss runs. This ancient knowledge will turn Sir Alonne into one of the best bossfights ever, instead of a series of infuriating endless gank deaths. Go slow and patient and kill them so dead that they are not even undead anymore. Also, you will be so massive tank of some sort from all that killemall-xp through the game, that in the very end of the DLC areas, it does not matter if you have much reaction speed at all. Suitable for us old people.
Read everything: carefully. Every item, loot, dialogue and especially, environment. That is basically where the game instructions are; in tiny snippets of item descriptions. Otherwise you will get very lost and very clueless very fast.

Looks bit clunky and several bits old, but feels really moody and fresh like undead breath of the ice queen of Eleum Loyce. Really, really, good game. I wish they would make Dark Souls II 2, the sequel.

Posted: June 3, 2024
7/10
Dark Souls 2 is easily the black sheep of the souls titles from fromsoftware, and it earns that reputation easily. However, it is still, a fromsoftware souls game. Even with all its flaws, it is still a good game... BUT, it is still, a bad souls game.

Story
A surprisingly good story for how panned this game is. While dark souls 1 is the beginning of the age of fire and ds3 is the end, DS2 takes place in the very middle of the age of fire. Dark Souls 2 is easily much smaller in scale compared to the other 2 souls game, as you are set in a castle that has no bearings to the era, with a time difference so large from ds1 that everyone forget gwymns name, the first cinder lord. It is also much more personal, with your character and the undead curse with how hopeless everything is, even if you try your best. Nobody believes you apart from 1, and it is your struggle that overcomes everyone's expectations. All of the characters struggle to accept the reality that they can't change what is going to occur, with death so close at the door, the only thing they believe they can do is wait, as trying is worse than just accepting fate. Apart from that, the story is a bit messy compared to DS1, and isn't as rich as future titles like ER or DS3, however it has the best personal journey for your character with the struggle to cure the curse that nobody has ever accomplished curing. Of course talking about the whole story would take too long, but the tldr is the war against giants that vendrick caused, even if they were probably weren't going to attack to begin with. Curse gets to Vendrick, vendrick is now a sad, sad, wondering cursed giant.

Characters
DS2's characters is SUPER mixed compared to other titles. For the non hostile NPC, they either range from interesting to downright bland with their story. Aldia was added in scholar, and I can't see who well this game would had done without him cause he had the more interesting dialogue out of the other NPCs in this game by far. Emerald herald, who takes your firekeeper role, is very interesting just like all the other firekeeper. What seperates her is the fact she wasn't ORIGINALLY a firekeeper, and only became one to test you out and see the journey you would take. Vendrick's story and inevitable depressing confrontation was also handled really well. Vengarl was a interesting dialogue as he is stuck beneath stone that he will never escape, and lastly Lucatiel, who had the best of the questlines surrounding a talkable NPC. The other characters suffer from having either super disappointing questlines, or none at all and turn into more bland shopkeepers who sell you ♥♥♥♥. I can't tell if they were just not given the thought to have interesting characters, or if they didn't realize the time it takes to add their lore, so only focused on a few NPC and gave the rest just a "want to buy something?" plot. For the boss lores, they are very interesting like all other boss lores. While the story is much more confusing for what happened with all the bosses that weren't vendrick, they were still captivating.

Gameplay
Now here is where it gets super controversial and what causes the label of "worst souls game" comes from. The gameplay of DS2 is very... mixed? Saying that EVERY aspect of DS2 is worse is just plain wrong. A ton of new mechanics in this game become staples in future titles. Power stance, SLIGHTLY moving with drinking (you still slow down to a stop mid way thru tho), the MUCH larger build variety in this game, respeccing character, SO many new mechanics in this game were just pretty good and are used in future titles to be improved upon, like power stance of course, and moving while drinking. However, this was a very experimental souls game, which means there were a ton of new mechanics introduced that were just plain ass. The most obvious one is roll I-frames being tied to agility stat, which is just a terrible mechanic that thankfully does not return, as it forces you to invest into a stat you didn't need to before just to match I-frames of previous titles. Animations like going thru fog doors, opening levers or doors no longer give you invulnerability frames for the first 2-3 seconds, which means areas that spam you with enemies are now REQUIRED to kill, as running past means getting killed before you can even get past the door. Going back to that, the runback in this game was just INSANELY terrible. This game has the WORST enemy placement in the series, with many areas forcing you to fight 3-4 enemies at the same time, and punishing you for even THINKING of running past them. The game forces you into a playstyle of pulling them one at a time to handle them, which quite honestly wouldn't be that bad if they weren't in your way to ACTUAL BOSSES. Runback is the worst mechanic in any souls game, and DS2 makes runback the worst I have ever experienced with far distances, forced encounters just to have another chance. the only mechanic that I truly believe they did not intend, but makes these 3-4 enemy jumping you bearable was that after killing them around 15 times, they stop spawning in for the rest of the run, which I believe they did to prevent farming, but in reality makes you not stuck on some runbacks for actual days, especially in areas like Iron Keep that throws 15 enemies in front of you, with a fog door that punishes you for running past them at all. I was able to get past this area purely cause they stopped spawning in period, as the boss was already hard to begin with. For the bosses in general, they were overall worse in DS2 compared to future titles. Many of the bosses were either too easy, too bland, or have terrible runback. There were some really good bosses, primary from the DLC, which had not only interesting stories, but had some of the best DLC in the game and were overall MUCH better compared to base. Most of the DLC felt higher quality then base, but they also introduce optional bosses in 2 of the dlc that quite frankly, were trash. The other bosses were probably the best in the game though.
Music
Still really good, just like other souls games. A ton of the songs were really good, but some were a bit generic for my taste (or were too easy to hear their full song). Songs like what played after finding Vendrick, Majula, Skeleton Lord theme (even if his fight was ass), Aldia's theme, Allone's them and the ruin sentinels' theme.

Last Positives/Nitpicks
-The PC port is ASS. no steam cloud save, streaming the game was terrible, terrible kb+m controls with a crap double click you have to constantly reset to fix, no PC overlay, so many aspects of this pc port lacks a ton of the modern features the other titles and even DS : remastered have.
- You get jumped by dark spirits... alot. You even get attacked by actual players even if you are turning hollowed.
- losing max hp after death is cruel and I am glad it doesn't return at all later titles, as running out of ways to restore humanity would had made segments of this game near impossible without grinding for more.
- ♥♥♥♥ Frigid Outskirts
- Some areas in this game was BEAUTIFUL. But some were also really ugly, which with the level design, was really inconsistent.

Conclusion
This game is still a pretty good game, as even when I tested out some other indie game souls game, I craved for how good DS2 is compared to those titles. However, comparing DS2 to other formsoftware titles shows just how weak this game is compared to the other souls games. The experimental aspect of this game and sloppy feeling of this game makes way more sense once you realize that Miyazaki did not direct this game, and barely had his input of the development of this game. You can easily tell, as tons of aspects of this game lacks the polish that the other titles contain. Even with that, I like this game a ton, and to call it a bad game is laughable. But would I recommend it as a first title compared to the other souls game? probably not.
7/10

When I started off Dark Souls 2, I was very pleased by how much more forgiving most boss battles were, and how mysterious the setting was, but over time, the storytelling became way to vague to be enjoyable. The objective kept shifting, your character seem to have no motivation for doing half the things you made em do, and unless you explored every area prior to going to a certain castle, you would have no idea why you're even there.

Directions given by NPCs were also very vague, and once you actually found the places you were "meant to go to", you ended up confused as hell. Sometimes you just got items, and were supposed to piece together your objective.

Overall:

Pros:
-Less linear than Dark Souls 3 (have yet to play 1)
-Less rage-worthy bosses
-Fresh feeling due to the obscure nature of the land

Cons:
-Story loses all logic half way through the game
-Story assumes you've visited all locations and know what's going on even if you haven't.
-Soul Memory prevents pvp and co op matching after a certain amount of souls have been acquired
-Locations don't even seem to be physically linked.

Overall the premise of the story is good, but it's told so poorly in certain spots that it just degrades the whole experience.

Honestly, I hate to be that guy, but having played it from start to finish 3 times I can definitively say that this game misses the point of what makes Dark Souls, Dark Souls. The games aren't difficult because they drown you in enemies, force you to grapple with poor hitboxes and leveling the i-frames on your rolls, they're difficult because they offer engaging combat between the boss and enemy encounters, but at the same time a fair experience because every death in 1 and 3 are your own fault. When I open a chest and I'm ambushed by a boss, when I'm jumpscared and murdered by a troll bursting through a wall, or ganked by 6 enemies after pulling a lever, those aren't deaths that you can learn and grow from to become a better player, they're frustrating wastes of time that you can't prepare for because they're unintuitive and cheap traps designed to piss you off rather than providing a challenge. Case in point, my second playthrough of this game was simple and boring with mundane boss encounters that typically didn't take more than one of my lives, as the developers clearly put most of their effort into crafting obnoxious troll traps and removing various quality of life features in the first game because they didn't know how to give the player a proper difficult experience. There are some interesting ideas, it was a fun time infusing weapons and I think some bosses and levels are well designed here and there (Smelter Demon, Veldstadt, Forest of Fallen Giants, etc.). Overall though, far and away the worst installment in the series and a massive waste of my time and money. Majula's my favorite hub though

Scholar feels... iffy. I've played this on PS4 from start to end and I felt uncertain. I have tried to replay this on PC a few times but... as you can see, I only have 13 hours on this. And in the original PS3 release of DS2, I threw something like 200 hours. How can that be?
Well, on one hand, I played the original 100% co-op, from start to end, with another friend, which made it MUCH easier... and the original is easier by default already. It's THE easiest Souls game by a long shot even with the whole forsaken Adaptability stat being added (thank God it was removed in DS3 and ER). On the other hand... Yeah, as I said, original DS2 was easier than SotFS. There are so many more enemies and placed in such annoying, frustrating ways that you HAVE to rely on leveling up adaptability. So many more road blocks placed just to slow you down...
Might be a ME issue, but on PS4, I COULD NOT defeat one DLC boss without leveling up ADP. Having it without a single point invested got me through the whole base game, but couldn't suffice for that DLC (ice DLC, can't recall the name; Ivory King, I think? Anyways, I'm talking about the tiger boss fight).

Dark Souls 2, even Scholar, isn't a bad game, but it's not a good Souls game. Well, not to the level of Dark Souls 1 and 3. It's not terrible, it's not trash... And honestly, it even had some great ideas (story wise, the whole king lore, especially with the DLCs, is actually rather great, maybe better than DS1; too bad it tries to connect to DS1 in stupid ways that undermine everything else; and ascetic bonfires are the greatest thing ever in the genre, I want them back in future Souls games, or in Elden Ring 2).

However... I can't say I recommend this. Maybe to a new player, sure; but this probably caters more to who played original DS2. And to those, nah, base DS2 is better, all in all. Yes, this fixes a bunch of things, but it also creates a lot more frustrating situations that... makes me feel like not recommending it.

Funny thing is DS2 was my first Souls game. I managed to get my hands on DS2 before DS1, on the PS3, so, at the time, it was my absolute favorite... then I kept replaying 1 and once I grabbed SotFS on PS4... whew, my opinion made a whole 180 degrees turn.

But yeah, I can't recommend it. I don't enjoy it nearly as much as all other Souls games. Slow in a boring, tedious way, enemies feel often unfair and way too numerous in places, the changes are often a detriment... So, not recommended to me. Base DS2 seems to still be sold even here on Steam, go buy that one instead-

Posted: May 29, 2024
This entry offers the best playground of them all, where you can try all kinds of crazy things and fail spectacularly in combat. I picked it up again on PC in 2020 after many years and although I did the others too, it was DS2 that impressed me the most.
NG+ cycling and "special runs" in particular are tremendous in this game and I think for at least 3 main reasons: traversing Drangleic, although as difficult as ever, is mostly fluid and quite compact which helps boost your motivation and morale. Additionally, the availability, variety and flexibility of weapons greatly expand your scope of operation, although I would say this is less important. I also suspect that the underwhelming base game bosses have to do with this as well...a lot in fact. Ultimately though, the game offers so much opportunity for experimentation...If you have experience with this game, increasing your handicap whether by foregoing the improvement of your stats or the ascension of your weapons (or both), is strongly recommended and nothing to be afraid of. You can do this in a multitude of ways and at your own pace. My SL1 run completely revitalised the game and embarked me on a roller coaster of emotions, on par with my first playthrough. I learned, laughed, cried, raged, tripped, fell, dodged, dipped, ducked and dodged more than ever. The DLC's were particularly marking and went from fantastic to absolutely monumental...

If you do not have experience with this series though, I cannot possibly imagine recommending this game.
If you want to indulge in the Souls experience, DS3 is probably your best bet. It is the most polished of them all, featuring beautiful aesthetics and more variety and fluidity in combat than ever before. You can safely stay away from the other 2 if you start with DS3.
DS1 is also a good choice but for other reasons: it's the first iteration so why not start there if you're really interested...it is easier, at least in terms of combat but more importantly, it's truly magical and mystical...by far the most dreamy and enchanting. It will always remain closer to my heart and imagination than the other 2.

But in the end, as a gaming experience, DS2 tops them all with it's incredible playground, wherein can be found an inexhaustible source of quality entertainment and challenge. Just remember to be safe friend, and not dare go hollow...

Posted: May 27, 2024
Objectively the worst ds game, but my personal favorite.

First off, ill start off the bad.
ADP: the worst mechanic in any souls game, but that doesnt make it awful. yes it can be annoying at first but invest 20 points into at the start of the game and you'll be fine.

hollowing: my personal most disliked mechanic of ds2, it chips away at your health each time you die and wont come back unless you use an effigy. Interesting in theory but ends up just being a tedious grind for human effigies.

Bosses: Lackluster. Many bosses either share the same moveset, are uninteresting, or are gimmicks. This does come with a benefit though, this makes you only need to run to the boss 1-3 times, making some runbacks slight annoyances at most.

Now to what most people are wrong about.
The runbacks aren't that bad. In fact, id go as far as saying ds1 has more annoying runbacks. Iron keep isn't that bad at all. It's easy to reach the fog gate, the only reason people complain is because they arent routing correctly. The worst runback would come from an optional boss that gives access to a covenant. Runback difficulties are overcomplained.

The good:
The world is beautiful. The area variety is better than ds1 and 3. While ds1 and 3 mainly looks like piss yellow , crap brown, and ash gray, ds2 has very unique areas that will surprise you on common occasion. The areas are well connected, with bonfires never feeling too far or too close like most would claim.

The story is bold. While 1 and 3 talk about an endless cycle of light and dark, ds2 talks about a way to break the cycle. The characters and npcs are some of the best in the game. everything about the world is so enthralling.

build variety while not as good as 3, is still extremely enjoyable to do with lots of variety.

music. man the music is just perfect. fits the atmosphere so well.

Majula. enough said.

【English & Spanish】


➥ English review:

"Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" is often considered the black sheep of the Souls saga, and we can't deny that it has its flaws. This title is characterized by some areas and bosses of considerably lower quality compared to other entries. Additionally, it features a difficulty that can feel artificial, especially accentuated in New Game+ modes, where areas become overrun with enemies, rather than offering more organic and strategic challenges.

Another negative point is the ludonarrative dissonance that occurs in some transitional sequences between areas, which can break player immersion. Likewise, the leveling and stat system is confusing and complicated, which can frustrate both newcomers and veterans of the series.

However, "Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" also has numerous virtues. The game's soundtrack has truly outstanding moments, perfectly accompanying the dark and oppressive atmosphere of Drangleic. The class system is exceptional, possibly the best in the entire saga, offering a variety of play styles and customization that encourage multiple playthroughs.

The DLCs of "Scholar of the First Sin" are extraordinary, adding high-quality content that expands and enriches the base game experience. The DLC stories are especially interesting and unique, providing additional backstory that complements and deepens the main narrative. Additionally, the online system is very polished, surpassing even the later entries in terms of stability and functionality, facilitating both cooperation and PvP.

Finally, the Bonfire Ascetic system adds an interesting mechanic, allowing players to increase the difficulty in certain areas at will, offering an additional challenge for those who seek it.

Personal Conclusion: Despite its flaws and weaknesses, "Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" has many mechanical and design virtues that make it a valuable and worthy experience in the Souls saga. I cannot fail to mention how beautiful Majula feels, a small corner of peace in a hostile world. It feels like home. "Dark Souls II" deserves an 8.4/10. Although it may be considered the black sheep, it is still a game that can provide hours of enjoyment to genre enthusiasts.

➥ Reseña en español:

"Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" es a menudo considerada la oveja negra de la saga Souls, y no podemos refutar que cuenta con sus fallos. Este título se caracteriza por algunas zonas y jefes cuya calidad es considerablemente baja en comparación con otras entregas. Además, presenta una dificultad que puede parecer artificial, especialmente acentuada en los modos New Game+, donde las áreas se infestan de enemigos hasta la saciedad, en lugar de ofrecer desafíos más orgánicos y estratégicos.

Otro punto negativo es la disonancia ludonarrativa que se produce en algunas secuencias de transición entre zonas, lo que puede romper la inmersión del jugador. Asimismo, el sistema de niveles y estadísticas resulta confuso y complicado, lo que puede frustrar tanto a novatos como a veteranos de la serie.

Sin embargo, "Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" también posee numerosas virtudes. La banda sonora del juego tiene momentos realmente destacados, acompañando perfectamente la atmósfera oscura y opresiva del mundo de Drangleic. El sistema de clases es excepcional, posiblemente el mejor de toda la saga, ofreciendo una variedad de estilos de juego y personalización que incentivan múltiples partidas.

Los DLCs de "Scholar of the First Sin" son extraordinarios, agregando contenido de alta calidad que amplía y enriquece la experiencia del juego base. La historia de los DLCs es especialmente interesante y única, proporcionando un trasfondo adicional que complementa y profundiza la narrativa principal. Además, el sistema online se encuentra muy pulido, superando incluso a las entregas posteriores en términos de estabilidad y funcionalidad, facilitando la cooperación y el PvP.

Finalmente, el sistema de las Ascuas de la Adversidad añade una mecánica interesante, permitiendo a los jugadores aumentar la dificultad en ciertas áreas a voluntad, ofreciendo un reto adicional para aquellos que lo buscan.

Conclusión personal: A pesar de sus errores y sus puntos débiles, "Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin" posee muchas virtudes mecánicas y de diseño que lo convierten en una experiencia valiosa y digna de la saga Souls. No puedo no mencionar lo hermoso que se siente Majula, un pequeño rincón de paz en un mundo hostil. Se siente como un hogar. "Dark Souls II" merece un 8.4/10. Aunque pueda ser considerada la oveja negra, sigue siendo un juego que puede proporcionar horas de disfrute a los aficionados del género.

To sum it up I love all the Dark Souls games, but this is my least favorite one in the series. It is by no means a bad game and is actually really good. If you liked another game in the series or maybe even Elden Ring and want to see what we experienced years ago make sure to pick this up. This entry adds many new quality of life features from its predecessor and the weapon gameplay feels awesome. The reason it’s my least favorite is because it has the highest level of artificial difficulty scaling later in the game when you get to the DLCs as well as somehow being the jankiest game in the series.

The jank is mostly attributed to a terrible stamina system, poor hitboxes, lock-on that either doesn’t work or makes you miss the enemy half the time, and movement control. It is often times even worse than Dark Souls 1 in these departments which is pretty sad considering that game is known for its annoying shenanigans. Maybe I am just spoiled with Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring being much better with these problems but back when I played on console around release time, I still remember having a similar experience.

The difficulty feel later in the game feels super artificial. In the DLCs, instead of adding new and complex enemies and mechanics you need to overcome and learn they just throw many basic enemies who often times have scaled up damage, health, and resistances. Almost all your fights will be fighting 3-5 enemies at once. On top of this they are difficult to stagger, making it hard to deal damage without taking some yourself. This gets super obvious when they pack all these enemies in dangerous areas such as pits, small rooms where you can’t make space, and also add unreachable range enemies. They also made it so there is a huge gauntlet you have to go through to even get to some bosses making you waste time and resources that doesn’t even feel difficult it just feels tedious. Keep in mind this is only my experience with the DLCs the base game isn’t this bad in this department.

I know I pointed out a lot of negatives, but this is still a good Dark Souls entry. Scholar of the First Sin is the recommended one to buy as it has all three DLCs built in. There are only a few minor changes to item locations and the layout of enemies in this version vs the normal version but it’s not that big of a difference. You will get your money’s worth at full price, but it has gone on sale for 75% off in the past and that’s a steal.

Posted: May 20, 2024
Dark Souls II isn’t a bad game — it’s actually pretty solid. There are some great ideas here, cool locations, and a ton of content. But compared to the rest of the Souls series? Yeah… it’s kinda mid.

Let’s start with the good:
The game still has that signature Souls feel — punishing but fair(ish) combat, cryptic worldbuilding, and plenty of “oh god what is that?” boss moments. There’s a ton of gear variety and build freedom, and Scholar of the First Sin adds a bunch of enemy and item reshuffling that makes replaying interesting. Also, the sheer number of bosses is wild — some are genuinely great.

But the flaws are hard to ignore.
The world design lacks the tight, interconnected feel that made Dark Souls 1 so memorable. Instead of a clever, looping world, you get zones that feel stitched together with loading screens and ladders. The combat animations are a little floaty, hitboxes feel off more often than they should, and the enemy tracking (especially in Scholar) is absurd sometimes.

The bosses are hit or miss — and yeah, there’s a lot of dudes-in-armor. Not terrible, just forgettable. You’ll remember a few, but a lot blend together after a while.

Also, the vibe is different. It’s still bleak, but not quite in the same haunting, mysterious way the others nailed. It feels more like it’s trying to look like Dark Souls than feel like it.

Still, I wouldn’t say skip it. If you’re a fan of the series, it’s worth playing. There’s enough cool stuff here to enjoy — and once you adapt to its weird quirks, it can be genuinely fun. But if you’re coming off DS1 or DS3, be ready to temper expectations a bit. This one’s the “experimental phase” of the series — not bad, just not elite.

Dark souls 1 I loved because even though it may put insurmountable odds in front of you, and a trap here and there; you could reliably prepare for and play through or around such challenges. The game rewarded you either for getting better at the game, finding ways around the threat, or finding the tools or weapons which would assist you in dealing with the issue.

Dark Souls 2 is the sequel for the now classic Dark Souls. The game tries to do different things from its predecessor and most of them clearly angry the fans.

In my case, as someone who has finished most of the Souls games from Miyazaki, I can say that the game feels easier than other installments, and that is a good thing. Not every Souls game has to feel like you're walking in shattered glass while feet naked.

The only complain that I have to agree with is the crazy hitboxes that the bosses have. Most of them can be beating very easy, but others just feel cheat. Nevertheless, this is something that can be applied to every Souls game in some degree.

In conclusion, Dark Souls 2 is a different "Soul" experience that feels easier than the other games, but I think it can be enjoyable eitherway.

7/10

I remember taking my PS4 copy of this game to work to let a co-worker borrow it. It was after a shift, and we were all sitting around having a few beers. Before i pulled the disc out of my backpack, I asked everyone at the table "Hey, y'all want to see a $40 piece of ♥♥♥♥?".

Well, after having played this game across numerous platforms for thousands of hours total(mostly ps4, played through the original, pre-Lightning Spear nerf on ps3 as well) I can admit that i was wrong.

This game is flawed, sure. The gotcha moments are well documented. The Gank Squad is unforgivable. But this game has such a wild style that i can't help but love. The astounding variety of different locations and environments, the absolutely buck wild amount of weapons and armor, the build variety. It feels more like a Shadow Tower game to me than it does a sequel to Dark Souls. This is like Shadow Tower Abyss 2.0, with a bit more jank thrown in there. Or a lot more, depending on how you look at it.

I'm not here to tell you that Dark Souls 2 is the best souls game, but i have played it for so much longer than either DS1 or DS3, and boot it up from time to time just to try out a new build or do something dumb like a naked, dagger-only playthrough. You can still dunk on the clown-ass elements this game definitely does possess, but you can enjoy it and for damn sure get your money's worth.

After my 8th playthrough of this game (the first 6 on ps4), I can say that it is indeed pretty good. While many people dislike the slow pace of combat in this game, for me as someone who sucks at these games, it makes it so much more enjoyable. While the enemy placements in the levels are very unbalanced, I would say that this game is probably the most balanced of the trilogy simply because just about every weapon, armorset, or playstyle you can invest into is viable. Wanna be a dark mage and shoot everything to death from a safe distance, broken as hell. Wanna do a faith build with a badass lightning weapon and supplemental healing, broken as hell. Wanna have access to the most diverse magic arsenal in the game almost for free, be a pyromancer, it's broken as hell. Wanna duel wield two big swords and not even touch magic, broken as hell. Everything is just so much fun and the freedom of choice in progression helps prevent difficulty roadblocks for new players. Peak Souls.
Edit: duel wielding whips is extremely funny, but very bad with knight fights

I have a love/hate relationship with DS2. While playing it's so easy to be frustrated or call bull for certain attack patterns, hotboxes, or enemy placements, but whenever you stop playing the only thing you can think about is hopping right back in. It's a game that's definitely frustrating in the moment, but looking back is a fun time (I know, I know, I'm stockholmed). My biggest complaints would have to be that the vibe just isn't there. It's distinctively different from both dark souls 1 and 3, not only by it's unique game design decisions but also just in atmosphere. Everything is a bit too human, animations are a bit too smooth, while also kind of clunky, it's truly bizarre. There's still so much good here, and lots of unique ideas that sadly stay locked to this entry.
If you've played another souls game I think this is a fine one to jump into, but don't have this be your starting point.

Look at footage of the iron keep or shrine of amana and really, really think about if you want to play this game. There are so many good Souls and Souls-like games out there, don't waste your time with this one. The level design ranges from okay to abhorrent, the enemy placement is ,in the later game, mostly abysmal (it feeels more like it's intentionally designed to annoy and slow you down, rather than being a fun challenge) and the movement feels sluggish and laggy (imagine the first Lords Of The Fallen game).

The only saving grace for this game are the DLC, which are all good and show what could have been. But to reach them, you have to drag yourself through nearly the whole game.

If you really insist on playing this one instead of 1, 3 or Demon's Souls, then at least bring a friend and have some coop fun.

"Seeker of fire, coveter of the throne. Seek strength. The rest will follow..."

As I set off in my mission to play every entry in the Dark Souls franchise, I went into this game expecting to have the most horrible gaming experience of my life. You hear it everywhere online, how Dark Souls II is awful, how it failed to live up to expectations, how it was a mistake and even a bad sequel, but I wholeheartedly disagree. In fact, I had a lot of fun in my adventures in the kingdom of Drangleic.

Is the Iron Keep a horribly designed area? Without a doubt.
Is the Sir Alonne runback the worst in the series? You bet.

But aside from those annoyances, this game tried a lot of new things and many of them worked great. If I'm being honest, I actually wish we'd gotten more of it in its sequel. I genuinely believe this is the most innovative entry in the Dark Souls series and we can see this by how much of it was utilized in the creation of Elden Ring.

The story of Dark Souls II is an element I don't see enough people discussing. It was very captivating to uncover what had happened to Drangleic and, as you probably could see from the quote at the beginning of this review, King Vendrick is one of my favorite characters in any FromSoftware product.

Scholar of the First Sin also comes with the DLCs included and I have to say... They're great additions to the base game. With the exception of Crown of the Sunken King, I had a lot of fun exploring the new areas and the bosses in them (Fume Knight, Ivory King and Sir Alonne, especially, are amazing. I exclude Crown of the Sunken King because it was overall disappointing, with a particular focus on the bosses. There's a dragon fight which, to this day, FromSoftware still hasn't managed to get the hang out of, a lazy gank squad trio and what is essentially a reskin of the base game's final boss. The other two DLCs are much more impressive in terms of content and quality and definitely worth your time.

As a whole, this game definitely deserves a playthrough. You'll get mad, you'll want to go back to the past and kill the baby versions of the devs, but in the end I believe you'll have a good time. Dark Souls just doesn't seem to be able to miss.

A 7/10 from me. It does have its issues, but if you're willing to overlook them you'll find a solid game that is another great entry in this series.

It's different to the first part in the souls series.
Cons: Many foes at once, enemy tracking not as good as in the first part, hp restriction mechanic, hitboxes not as good as in the first part, pyromancy spells require stamina, parrying more difficult than in the first part.

Pros: larger world than in the first part, great weapon variety, many cool outfits, bonfire ascetics (unique for ds2!), wonderful world design!(especially the hub - Majula), interesting lore, more bosses than in ds1, dead npcs can be brought back to the game in exchange for souls

Neutral aspects that differ from Dark Souls 1: ADP stat: Enables player to get more invincibility frames during rolls and therefore makes the game easier the more you level it, bonfire travel mechanics: available at the start of the game, areas are not as interconnected as in ds2, gemstones (and derivates): Common item that lets you restore hp over time and can be held up to 99 (?) pieces in one slot, torches (unique for ds2!): Enables player to see in dark places and can scare some foes

Posted: May 8, 2024
"Dark Souls II" is a worthy successor of the first legendary game. The world is once again gorgeous with various locations, filled with unique creatures and enemies. Level design creates a fantastic exploration feeling and the atmosphere remains both epic and weird. Battles are rewarding, while weapons, armors, spells are masterfully designed to drastically affect your character's behavior. Yet, compared to the first game, writing is mediocre with shallow lore and one dimensional NPCs that you'll struggle to love. Bosses lack versatility, sometimes use similar move sets and the battles lack the epicenes of the other episodes of the series. Also it has the same control problems as the first game with unclear hit boxes and hits through walls. Despite the issues it's still an epic game that honors its title and feels great for the fans of the genre.


Manometer: 8/10


The good
- Gorgeous, immersive world with a variety of different locations. The atmosphere has something weird, mythical and magical. It's on the darker side compared to the first game and feels less poetic though.
- Complex and realistic character customization that drastically affects the gameplay. Equipment affects you weight, movement and agility. Each weapon has different handling depending on its kind, size and weight. There are so many assets and abilities to take care of and everything has an impact. There are so many beautifully designed armors, weapons, spells to try. You can built your character in so many different ways to match your preferred playstyle.
- Great multilevel open world design. It doesn't follow well worn approaches that you acquire silly errands from NPCs and mechanically follow dots on a map. Instead, it organically pushes you to explore and gradually reveal the world. That feeling of finding a new location or a new bonfire is so rewarding. There are so many hidden chests, easter eggs, mysteries to discover. Nothing is put there by luck and everything has something to add to the lore.
- Addictive battles that are based more on tactics than a quick hack and slash approach. You need to be observant and memorize enemies' movements in order to avoid hits and find openings to attack. It feels realistic and most importantly you need to have the right character setup that suits each enemy. There aren't complex combos and your character's movements are rather simplistic but It has more to do with preparation and observation than actual skills during the battle.
- It feels rewarding. At the beginning it's harsh. You are weak and you can die pretty easily so the leveling up procedure, finding better weapons and gaining power gives you some short of satisfaction. Killing hard to beat enemies is fulfilling.
- Well balanced. It is challenging but not too difficult. If you haven't played other FromSoftware games you'll need time to get used of the controls and the battle's system but after the first few hours you won't really get stuck somewhere. Also, you can always flee in order to level up your character or just summon an ally to help you during a difficult battle.
- Good variety of nicely designed enemies with various sizes and move sets. Bosses are cool but lack the creativity and versatility of the first game.
- Music is subtle and you mostly won't notice it much, but is masterfully intergraded to the game. It plays only in specific places, enhancing specific moments and letting the environmental sound design do the rest of the job.
- Great online part with very active online community. It makes the world feel even more alive, like a huge arena. You can call a friend to help you against a boss or fight against players of other clans.
- Good voice acting performances and great post processing. Some of them are atmospheric and other really creepy.
- It has some parts that you can explore the world like a big platform puzzle by moving walls, pulling levers, changing levels' orientations etc. It isn't anything complex or difficult but they are playful and interesting and give the game yet another dimension.

The bad
- Same problematic controls as the first game. Both yours and enemies' hit boxes aren't clear. Especially against bosses with huge weapons or body parts you are never sure whether they touched you or not and you just get an idea by loosing several times. At narrow spaces enemies will hit you through walls. Cameras inside narrow spaces and against big bosses are problematic.
- Mediocre writing. Lore and storytelling are more clear and easy to understand than the first game, but also much more simplistic and shallow. The first game establishes a majestic world with all its epic primordial mythology and ancient forces that battle for their existence. This looks like a side mission during a parallel, not so interesting timeline that less things are in stake. Also, NPCs look one dimensional, being almost clearly evil or good. There are not tragic figures like Solaire or Siegmeyer that you'll deeply love. Overall it's much more straight forward and less poetic.
- Bosses, especially those of the base game, look nice but it feels sometimes that are randomly placed, they lack backstory and versatility using a lot of times similar move sets. Also many of them are pretty unchallenging and the battle lacks the direction and epicenes of the rest series. Fortunately the DLCs' bosses save the day but I expected something more from a Dark Souls game.
- Level design looks a lot like the first game and it's really great, but at some points the game falls short of giving you the needed information to proceed. There are times that you're not sure what to do next and guidance toward the ending is a little bit anticlimactic too.
- Managing your equipment could be better. Menus are mediocre without many options. It would be nice if you could save armor presets or if it had the tools to manually categorize your assets instead of this chaos.
- I would really love some bonfires closer to boss battles in order to prevent long repetitive boring walks. Shortcuts usually aren't very helpful too.

Dark Souls 2 has a door where when you open it and walk through, you'll fall off to your death because there was no balcony in front of it. But after you revive, you can fall off a nearby platform through that door as a shortcut to a boss that lets you evade all the enemies. It sounds fair now when I tell you in this review, but it's not very common knowledge as far as I know and people (myself included) called it a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ trap. Similarly, people endlessly debate about whether the first mimic in Dark Souls 1 is a fair trap, given it's location and the placement of objects in the room. DS2 has these kinds of debatably fair obstacles in spades, but aside from that, it's the easiest and most forgiving game in the series by a good margin. If you're the type of person who likes to use all your resources in an RPG, want a cozy time where you button mash bosses to death, and/or are willing to laugh at some comically stacked deaths every once in awhile. I highly recommend this game.

Dark Souls 2, often considered the weaker link in the revered trilogy, does face some criticism when compared to its predecessors. The game diverges slightly with its world design and boss encounters, not quite capturing the same level of intricacy and interconnectedness that fans admire in the first and third installments. This, coupled with what some players see as inconsistent enemy placement and certain questionable design choices, might lead to the feeling that it doesn't fully live up to the high standards set by the other games in the series.

Despite these criticisms, Dark Souls 2 shines in its own right, especially when played on the Steam Deck. The game performs admirably on the portable platform, offering a robust and immersive experience that leverages the Deck's capabilities to deliver Dark Souls' signature challenging gameplay on the go. I'd heard a lot of mixed things about Dark Souls II before jumping in, but I was happy to go in with an open mind. After all, I love the rest of Fromsoft's catalogue, from what I've played. After completing it however, I can't see it justifying its existence or a replay from me.

The combat feels floaty and just... a little off? The hitboxes are notably scuffed and moving your character just feels kinda clunky. It feels even slower than DS1 combat. The enemy placement feels lazy and is more interested in frustrating the player rather than teaching them. The boss runbacks though, are easily the most annoying aspect of this game. Long, spammed enemies everywhere, lack of shortcuts. It's got the lot! I want to fight the boss not the 100 enemies swarming me every time. Dark Souls 1 had the occasionally frustrating or long runback, but no where the level of the Smelter Demons or Sir Alonne.

In terms of good stuff: Majula is absolutely stunning, there's some great bosses (Like Sir Alonne) and the way you explore the different areas in whichever way you want is cool (though DS1 map exploration has it beat). Overall though, I highly doubt I'll ever play this again. It's missing the level design and art direction of DS1, and the boss quality and smoother moment-to-moment gameplay of DS3.

I played Dark Souls II way back on release, but dropped it relatively early, at Lost Bastille. Something just felt off about the game. I returned to it ten years later, having beaten Dark Souls III, Sekiro, and Elden Ring in the meantime and fully knowing this game's notoriety, to see if something truly is off with it. Turns out, yes, yes there is. That said, I'm not going to bore you with stuff you've already heard everywhere else, like iframes tied to a stat and a magma castle floating on top of windmills. The game has a larger problem, that being the complete lack of structure.
Yes, it has levels that change. Yes, it has beginning, middle, and end. Yes, you (mostly) understand what you're supposed to do. Yet the very core of what we call "structure", that is, cause and effect chains, interconnectivity on physical (or rather, polygonal) and mental levels, simply do not exist. The story does not support the level flow, the levels themselves don't flow sensibly or towards any understandable goal, the features and gimmicks get introduced only to be barely fleshed out and forgotten.
It's not just an unfinished game, it's a game that feels like it lacked a common design stage, a mishmash of bits and pieces taken from the cutting room floor. Are some of those bits and pieces pretty good? Absolutely. Does that mean it's an unfinished masterpiece? Absolutely not.
On top of that, it doesn't even have a creative spark that might still make the game worth playing against all odds, the true vision. It's a sequel to a well-received and well-sold and much better game, and it shows.
Compare it to something like Vampire - The Masquerade: Bloodlines, another infamous experience. While the entire last act of the game is a terrible experience, we know that there was supposed to be a last act, we know how exactly it was supposed to play out, and we know the overall story together with the amazing spoiler regardless. That's what structure is, that's what Dark Souls II lacks.
To put it simply, it's just not a game worth playing in this day and age. The good things it did were done far better in Dark Souls III and Elden Ring, and the bad things it does are extremely aggravating.
Besides, even if you're a gameplay purist, it's a very dull and easy experience aside from several infamous group fights. Bosses are terrible by and large with the exception of a single DLC boss, too.

Posted: April 29, 2024
The Dark Souls series is fun, but it's something I only ever really loved in its prime.

See, these games are fun no matter how you play, but they can be extremely frustrating and unforgiving. That's no surprise, they've built their reputation on that, but that isn't why they're fun and don't let anyone fool you. They're fun because they're well-developed RPGs with deep customization such as a variety of weapons, armors and spell types to choose from, decent progression mechanics that make you want to fully explore levels so you know where to go when you finally get that key from a boss you met later in the game or whatever else.

The problem is that the difficulty, which in many ways accentuates the game's skillful design, also really accentuates its worst aspects as well. I mean in the first Dark Souls, dodging was hot garbage. Dark Souls 2 had a different creative director and people like to crap on it for that, but I actually think it stands out more than the first game in a lot of ways and has a kind of unique, almost smoother feel to its gameplay. Maybe a bit too smooth, it does feel kind of awkward in ways, but I still love it.

Now why am I not recommending it, then? Well, it's simple. The online community is pretty much dead. Too many games, too many years have passed. Yeah, you can still find one or two people or just play alone, but this game was always fun for me, personally, because I could summon other people to play with. It mitigated the frustration of the difficulty, sure, and that's one thing I liked, but most of all I just enjoyed meeting new people and working with them to clear areas. It was honestly the highlight of the game. Whether I was summoning them or being summoned, I almost always had fun.

But even in its prime, the series has always had connectivity issues. FromSoftware is no different from any Japanese developer in that their netcode is a steaming pile of ♥♥♥♥. I remember struggling to summon even friends because I would constantly get a failure message or sometimes I just wouldn't see their signs at all. Later games really improved the latter with a password system, but Dark Souls 2 does not have that and even if it did, it doesn't fix the fact that the online is shaky and unreliable at best, and that's assuming anyone even shows up.

I think what this series really needs is a complete, from-the-ground-up remake of the three games put together into a single collection with actual crossplay between PC and other systems. It's the only way we can definitively be sure the community can last... but I don't see that being in the cards and even if they did do this, they'll probably mess up the netcode again.

Maybe one day we'll get something like that and it'll be great, but for the here and now? I do not recommend you buy this game. You'll probably encounter a bunch of hackers anyway, ruining what's left of the online community. Did you know that Dark Souls 3 took its online services down for 7 months because of a vulnerability? It took them 7 months to fix the problem. Ineptitude? Or maybe they just don't care about their community that much.

Whatever the reason, FromSoftware is becoming a company I have a harder and harder time recommending you support. That's to say nothing of my opinions on Elden Ring and its many design flaws.

I suppose the soulslike genre is moving on without me. I can only hope Nioh 3 is anywhere as good as Nioh 2 was or I just don't know if I'll ever play another new soulslike again. Hard enough to play the old ones when their online community is dying off more and more every year.

You want a recommendation? Go play Nioh 2. It's amazingly fun even when entirely solo.

People rag on this game because its different from the 1st game. But its good, just in a different way.

The 1st dark souls had smaller area that were interconnected in a bunch of places. You had few bonfires (checkpoints) but by advancing through a level you will regularly find shortcuts back to one of the bonfires so if you die you do not lose too much progress.

In this game bonfires are more like traditional checkpoints. There aren't that many shortcuts back to bonfires, but from a single bonfire you either progress in one direction until the next bonfire, or there are multiple smaller secondary areas that are reached from the same bonfire. It's less exciting and interesting that the 1st game and there is less sense of wonder at all the shortcuts. Shortcuts only become prevalent in the expansions, after you have completed the game.

Aside from that, in the 1st game the challenge were the bosses. Here the challenge are the areas. There are more enemies, tougher enemies, faster enemies. The bosses are easier but the enemies are designed to drain your resources before you get to the boss. However, the enemies only respawn 12-15 times so worst case scenario, after 15 attempts at a boss, the 16th will have no enemies standing in your way.

The quality of this game is a contentious issue in the community.
If you like Souls games, this is one of them.
It has all of the things that you like about Souls games (I mean, it IS part of the genre-defining series),
but is also includes many questionable and likely experimental features that hugely detract from the experience.
It also has some great innovations, but for me personally they pale against the negatives.

I would only suggest you play this game if you have prior experience with the Souls series and have to come enjoy the Souls games you have played thus far. If you want to play some more of its kind, then this game is a natural choice.
Just be aware that it suffers from unintuitively large hitboxes, iframe-durations tied to a character stat, further punishment for dying in the form of progressive hollowing and much more.

You can find loads of justified negative critique for this game.
It truly is as they say: "Dark Souls 2 is not a bad game, it is a bad souls game."
Still, its faults are bad enough that I decided to not recommend it.

Better than Ds2 Vanilla, more enemy variety and a lot more features, better item placement and most enemy placement is better.

Even tho i disliked this version of the game from everything i saw, i have to admit that is better, now that i played the game i realized that most of the hate comes from players who tried to play this game (and vanilla) like if it was Dark Souls 3 or Elden Ring, rushing every enemy without even thinking, greatsword here and there, dying, and then blaming the game. In this game you actually need to think just like players did in other games like DeS and Ds1, there is a lot of strategy involved by using consumables and many interactable objects in the areas (like exploding barrels, artillery, moving platforms, fire spitting bulls, etc) before you aproach enemies.

Some areas that have a different enemy placement or more enemies like Iron Keep and Shrine of Amana which can be easily taken out by just being careful and playing at a proper pace, for example, when i got to Iron Keep i realized that you can actually hold one of the pillars in the bridge section and wait for every knight to come in line. Another example is Shrine of Amana, when i got to this area i though it was going to be unfair, but when i got to the sorcerers section, i realized that they moved and added some of them in places where you can use pillars, rubble and structures to not get absolutely molested and also lure the knights one by one unlike many not so good players, this was the first and one of the only area where i had to use a bow (which you can get at the very start of the game by using the Blacksmith Key in Majula) just to kill 3 enemies and aggro 1 enemy on the final section, which later when doing a melee only Shrine of Amana i realized that the whole area is perfectly viable with a melee only build. The area is well crafted especially in the last encounter before the boss, since it really asks you to be careful and make good use of the aggro range so the enemies in the middle wont activate, and the hardest parts of this area didn't come from the game itself but from the LightingMod since i had to be more aware of falling to the void rather than the enemies.

Since i mention the LightingMod, i have to say that another great thing from this version is the amount of mods it has compared to vanilla.

The only time i got badly ganked was on Soldier's Rest bonfire in Forest of the Fallen Giants where the 3 turtles of top of the building aggro suddenly and then got invaded by an NPC, which could've avoided if i cleaned that part before but still that felt kinda meh, aside from that i didn't even die so i wasn't annoyed that much really.

No matter where somebody thinks this game lies compared to other in the Souls series, it is still a great game and could end up as your favorite in the series. It honestly plays and feels closer to Demon's Souls rather than Dark Souls, so I'd leave any preconceived notions about this series behind when starting DS2 up and let it speak for itself.

The story takes you to a mysterious world full of enigmatic characters. It’s far removed from the rest of the Dark Souls series, but the links are absolutely there for those with the desire to look. In fact, a 100% playthrough has some of the most interesting narrative experiences I've personally played in a souls game. If DS1 is about the major actors who set this world in motion, and DS3 is about your role in becoming one of those actors, then DS2 is about everyone else. You are dropped into the middle of everything and it's up to you as a player to make sense of things. You see how these grand events affect people of all kinds. I tend to think of it like this, DS1 and 3 are about the gods of this world, while DS2 is about the people that live in the wake of their actions.

As for the gameplay, DS2 demands patience and rewards thinking about how to approach fights. You are able to build a wide arsenal of tools and use them accordingly. Use weapons with sweeping attacks to deal with groups, strike type weapons to deal with armor, elemental resins to deal with damage resistances, and even something as simple as a torch to deal with darkness. I jokingly refer to DS2 as, "the torch game" for that reason, but my point here is that DS2 is a globe trotting adventure and you benefit from changing how you play to suit the situation at hand.

There is some contention in the community about whether you should play DS2 or DS2:Scholar edition. I like both but a lot of what was changed in Scholar was actually for the better of both new and old players alike. Both versions of DS2 want you to move through areas slowly your first time and Scholar is often more fun than base game if you do so. If you are accustomed to sprinting around DS1 while avoiding combat, just understand that this strategy will not serve you well here.

My general purpose info to know:







Finally, if this game clicks for you or if it sounds interesting but you don't really like how it plays then consider picking up Demon's Souls. Yep, tricked you, this is actually a Demon's Souls review! Dark Souls 2 feels like an attempt at iterating on Demon's Souls gameplay MUCH more than an attempt at iterating on Dark Souls gameplay. I love all these games, but Demon's Souls might be the concise and refined experience that a DS2 hopeful feel DS2 lacks. I think the best way to play that game is on PC via RPCS3 on The Archstones private server. You could also play the remake, but I think that loses some of the quiet dark fantasy charm in favor of bombastic artstyle choices.

Very highly recommended! Especially, if you liked Dark Souls 1 or Elden Ring.

If you haven't played Elden Ring or Dark Souls 1 first, I recommend those more than this. Then come back to this. This is very much worth playing after!

There are some notable differences to Dark Souls 1:
The start is slower and more difficult in Dark Souls 2. Your starting character is way more underpowered than in Dark Souls 1 or Elden Ring. It doesn't even roll properly until you invest stats in rolling.

The stats are changed from Dark Souls 1. In Dark Souls 1, you can do just VIG + END + STR (or whatever your main stat is). In Dark Souls 2, END is split into END + VIT + ADP. So, you need to level those three to get to the same place as in Dark Souls 1.

By level 40 or 50 you'll get to the starting point (level 1) of Dark Souls 1. On the other hand, by the time you finish this game you'll be about level 200 vs about level 100 in Dark Souls 1. So, you get a lot more levels, but there are also more stats to level up. Mid to late game plays very similarly to Dark Souls 1.

It seems like this version of the game was pushed out the door without any real consideration for enemy placement within the levels, or how well the enemies pair together in combat encounters. What was a decent, if a bit empty and confusing, entry into the souls series has become a distorted vision of a Souls game where the ball-breaking difficulty touted by the marketing was the sole aspect of a Souls game. This game does not feel tight enough to warrant the level of precision this game expects. I feel like the base game understood this, but Scholar and the DLCs really lost the plot when it came to how to populate the game to make it feel less empty. Smarter enemy placement is the way to go, not just vomiting enemies into every nook and cranny.
I really wanted to give it a fair chance, as Scholar and the DLCs are the only pieces of souls content I have yet to complete. It seems they will remain that way, its not worth putting up with the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ this game makes you deal with.

It's the fourth season of community. Some people like it as much or even prefer it. But the intention and tone I fell in love with from the original creator is just missing. That combined with some annoyances lead me to not enjoy my time with Ds2.

-The tone, lore, story, voice acting, characters, art direction are all poor imitations from its predecessor
-Nothing cohesive about the world design
-"if we can't make it interesting or challenging, just multiply the amount of enemies by 3"
-Weapon degradation turned up to 11 making some weapons worthless
-So many nothing boss fights in the base game
-Solutions for fighting against the annoying bosses (not hard, just annoying) boil down to "uSe ThE riGhT BuiLd"
-Healing items are so cheap you'll never run out of healing, killing the risk/reward dynamic of exploring a new area with limited estus' like in DS1/3
-The best boss in the base game is locked behind an item that isn't infinitely purchasable
-Too many boring NPC invasions
-Forcing online play unless you launch in offline mode
-Medusa? Chariot? Quelaag but now a scorpion? Brilliant.
-Wasted potential with bosses with genuinely cool designs that you'll only see once because they're way too easy
